VC2S2H01.w: We are learning that scientists find out about the world by looking, listening, touching, smelling and using special tools.
y: This helps us understand that we can discover things by paying close attention to the world around us.
People have always observed the world carefully — Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have watched Country for thousands of years to understand seasons, animals and weather.
When we practise looking and listening like a scientist, we learn to notice things other people might miss.
eg: Using a magnifying glass to look closely at a leaf.
Listening to the sounds birds make in the schoolyard.
Feeling different rocks to notice which ones are smooth or rough.
Smelling flowers in the garden to find out which ones have a scent.
Using a thermometer to measure how hot or cold the water is.
Watching ants carry food and drawing what we see.

VC2S2H02.w: We are learning that people use science every day — like watching the weather or noticing patterns in nature.
y: This helps us see that science is not just something in a lab — it is part of our everyday life.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have used knowledge of seasons, stars and animal behaviour to guide daily life for tens of thousands of years.
Noticing patterns helps us make better choices, like knowing to wear a raincoat when clouds are dark.
eg: Checking the weather before deciding what to wear.
Noticing that the days get shorter in winter and longer in summer.
Watching a plant grow taller each week.
Seeing that puddles dry up faster on sunny days.
Knowing which fruits are ready to eat by their colour.
Noticing that some birds come to the schoolyard at certain times of day.

VC2S2U01.w: We are learning that plants and animals have features we can see, and we can sort them into groups.
y: Sorting living things into groups helps us understand the amazing variety of life on Earth.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have detailed knowledge of local plants and animals, grouping them by how they are used and where they live.
Learning to sort and classify is a thinking skill we use in science, maths and everyday life.
eg: Sorting animals into groups — those with fur, feathers or scales.
Grouping plants by the shape of their leaves.
Noticing that a frog has smooth, wet skin and a lizard has dry, scaly skin.
Sorting toy animals into groups that fly, swim or walk.
Looking at flowers and sorting them by how many petals they have.
Comparing insects — some have wings and some don't.

VC2S2U02.w: We are learning that all plants and animals need things like air, water, food and a safe place to live.
y: Understanding what living things need helps us take care of pets, plants and our environment.
All people depend on healthy plants and animals — looking after them means looking after ourselves.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have cared for Country and its living things sustainably for thousands of generations.
eg: Watering our class plant and watching it grow.
Feeding the school fish and noticing what happens if we forget.
Learning that frogs need water to lay their eggs.
Talking about what our pet needs to be healthy and happy.
Noticing that plants near the window grow better because they get sunlight.
Discussing why birds build nests — to have a safe home for their babies.

VC2S2U03.w: We are learning that parts of plants and animals do special jobs to help them stay alive.
y: Understanding how body parts work helps us appreciate how cleverly living things are designed.
Knowing what different parts do helps us look after plants and animals better.
This is the start of understanding how our own bodies work too.
eg: Learning that roots take water from the soil up into the plant.
Noticing that a bird's beak shape matches the food it eats.
Seeing that a duck's webbed feet help it swim.
Understanding that thorns on a rose protect it from being eaten.
Knowing that our lungs help us breathe in air.
Noticing that a kangaroo's strong legs help it hop very fast.

VC2S2U04.w: We are learning that things around us are made from different materials and each material has its own features.
y: Knowing about materials helps us understand why different things are made from different stuff.
People choose materials for a reason — like using glass for windows because we can see through it.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have always selected the right natural materials for tools, shelter and art.
eg: Feeling that metal is cold and smooth while wood is warm and rough.
Noticing that glass is see-through but wood is not.
Sorting classroom objects by what they are made from — plastic, metal, wood, fabric.
Discovering that rubber is stretchy and paper tears easily.
Talking about why raincoats are made from waterproof material.
Comparing how a wooden block and a sponge feel different.

VC2S2U05.w: We are learning that when we mix materials together, the mix can be different from the materials on their own.
y: This helps us understand how cooking, building and making things work.
Mixing and combining things is one of the most important ideas in science.
When we understand how materials change, we can make useful things and solve problems.
eg: Mixing flour, water and salt to make playdough — it feels different from any one ingredient.
Stirring paint colours together to make a new colour.
Mixing sand and water to make wet sand we can build with.
Adding milk to cereal — the cereal gets soft over time.
Mixing dirt and water to make mud.
Noticing that mixing vinegar and baking soda makes bubbles.

VC2S2U06.w: We are learning that we can change materials by bending, twisting, stretching or breaking them — but they are still the same material.
y: This helps us understand the difference between changing the shape of something and changing what it is made of.
Knowing how materials behave helps us choose the right material for a job.
We use this kind of knowledge every day — when we fold paper, squash clay or snap a stick.
eg: Bending a pipe cleaner into a new shape — it is still a pipe cleaner.
Stretching a rubber band — it goes back when we let go.
Squashing clay into a flat disc — it is still clay.
Twisting a towel to squeeze water out.
Tearing paper into strips — each strip is still paper.
Folding aluminium foil into a boat shape.

VC2S2U07.w: We are learning that the weather changes from day to day and season to season, and this affects what we do.
y: Understanding weather helps us plan what to wear, what to do and how to stay safe.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples use detailed seasonal knowledge — such as the Kulin Nation's seven seasons — to guide activities like food gathering and ceremony.
Weather patterns are connected to bigger ideas about how the Earth and Sun work together.
eg: Checking if it is sunny or rainy before going outside.
Noticing that we wear jumpers in winter and shorts in summer.
Recording the weather each day on a class chart.
Talking about how farmers need rain for their crops to grow.
Observing that some trees lose their leaves in autumn.
Noticing that puddles form on rainy days and dry up when the sun comes out.

VC2S2U08.w: We are learning that Earth is one of 8 planets that go around the Sun, and we can see patterns in the sky.
y: Learning about space helps us understand our place in the universe.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have observed and named star patterns for tens of thousands of years, using them for navigation, storytelling and seasonal knowledge.
Watching the sky teaches us about patterns — like why we have day and night, and why the Moon looks different each week.
eg: Learning the names of the 8 planets in our solar system.
Noticing that the Sun rises in the east and sets in the west.
Watching the Moon change shape over a few weeks.
Learning that Earth spins, which gives us day and night.
Looking at the night sky and finding bright stars or planets.
Talking about how the Kulin peoples use the stars to know when seasons change.

VC2S2U09.w: We are learning that we need to look after Earth's water, land and air by reducing, re-using and recycling.
y: Taking care of the environment means we help keep the planet healthy for everyone — people, animals and plants.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have practised sustainability and cared for Country since time immemorial.
Small actions we take every day — like turning off taps and recycling — add up to make a big difference.
eg: Putting rubbish in the right bin — recycling, compost or landfill.
Turning off the tap while we brush our teeth to save water.
Using both sides of a piece of paper before recycling it.
Picking up litter in the schoolyard to keep our environment clean.
Bringing a reusable drink bottle instead of buying plastic ones.
Talking about why it is important not to waste food.

VC2S2U10.w: We are learning that how things move depends on their size, shape and what they are made of.
y: Understanding movement helps us play, build and solve problems in everyday life.
Knowing why things move differently helps us design things that work better — like choosing the right ball for a game.
This is the beginning of understanding physics — the science of how things move and work.
eg: Rolling different balls down a ramp and seeing which one goes fastest.
Noticing that a heavy rock is harder to push than a light ball.
Seeing that a flat piece of paper falls slowly but a scrunched-up ball falls fast.
Comparing how a toy car moves on carpet versus a smooth floor.
Dropping a feather and a marble and watching them fall differently.
Sliding different objects on ice and seeing which goes furthest.

VC2S2U11.w: We are learning that pushes and pulls are forces, and they can make things start, stop or change direction.
y: Understanding pushes and pulls helps us know how to make things move the way we want.
We use forces every single day — opening doors, riding bikes, throwing balls.
This helps us start thinking like engineers and designers who need to know how forces work.
eg: Pushing a swing to make it go higher.
Pulling a wagon with a handle.
Kicking a ball to make it start moving.
Catching a ball to make it stop.
Using a magnet to pull a paperclip without touching it.
Noticing that the wind can push leaves along the ground.

VC2S2U12.w: We are learning that sounds are made when things vibrate, and we can change how loud or high a sound is.
y: Understanding sound helps us with music, communication and noticing the world around us.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have a deep understanding of sound — the didgeridoo (yidaki) uses vibrations to create one of the world's oldest musical sounds.
Knowing how sound works helps us control noise and protect our hearing.
eg: Plucking a rubber band and watching it shake as it makes a sound.
Tapping a drum softly and then loudly to hear the difference.
Putting our fingers on our throat and feeling the vibration when we hum.
Filling bottles with different amounts of water and blowing across the top to make different notes.
Noticing that a short string on a guitar makes a higher sound than a long one.
Covering our ears to see how sound gets quieter.

VC2S2I01.w: We are learning to ask questions about things we notice and make guesses about what might happen.
y: Asking questions is how we start finding things out — curious people make great discoveries.
Making guesses helps us think about what we already know and test our ideas.
Scientists, explorers and community knowledge keepers all start by wondering 'why' and 'what if'.
eg: Asking 'Why do leaves change colour in autumn?'
Guessing that ice will melt faster in the sun than in the shade.
Wondering why some rocks float in water and others sink.
Predicting which ball will roll further on the grass.
Asking 'What happens if we don't water the plant?'
Guessing that a feather will fall more slowly than a stone.

VC2S2I02.w: We are learning to follow steps safely when we do a science investigation.
y: Following steps keeps us and our classmates safe so we can enjoy our experiments.
Learning to work carefully and in order helps us get better results.
Being responsible with materials shows respect for our classroom and the people around us.
eg: Wearing safety goggles when we mix things together.
Washing our hands after touching soil or pond water.
Following the teacher's instructions before starting an experiment.
Putting lids back on containers so nothing spills.
Taking turns with shared equipment.
Telling the teacher if something breaks or spills.

VC2S2I03.w: We are learning to use our senses and simple tools to watch carefully and write down what we see.
y: Recording what we observe helps us remember and share our discoveries with others.
Using tools like magnifying glasses lets us see things our eyes alone might miss.
Good observation is a skill we use everywhere — noticing details helps us in all parts of life.
eg: Using a magnifying glass to look at the veins in a leaf.
Drawing a picture of a caterpillar and labelling its body parts.
Measuring how tall a sunflower has grown using a ruler.
Using our ears to count how many different bird calls we hear.
Recording the colour and shape of different shells we collected.
Taking photos of clouds and sorting them by shape.

VC2S2I04.w: We are learning to put information into tables or charts so we can spot patterns.
y: Organising information makes it easier to see what is happening and find patterns.
Tables and charts help us share what we found out so others can understand too.
Learning to sort and organise information is a skill we use in science, maths and everyday life.
eg: Making a chart of the weather each day for a week.
Sorting rocks into groups by colour and putting them in a table.
Drawing a bar chart showing our class's favourite animals.
Making a tally chart of how many birds visit the feeder each day.
Recording the height of a bean plant each week in a table.
Sorting leaves by size and recording small, medium and large.

VC2S2I05.w: We are learning to check if what happened matches what we guessed, and to compare with what others found.
y: Checking our guesses helps us learn whether our ideas were right or if we need to think again.
Comparing with classmates teaches us that different people can notice different things.
Real scientists always check their results too — this is how they make sure their findings are trustworthy.
eg: Checking if the ice melted faster in the sun like we predicted.
Comparing our plant growth chart with a friend's to see if they got the same result.
Talking about why our group got a different answer from another group.
Looking back at our prediction and saying 'I was right because...' or 'I was wrong because...'
Asking 'Did everyone's magnet pick up the same things?'
Noticing that our shadow measurements were different at different times of day.

VC2S2I06.w: We are learning to share what we found out using words, drawings and simple science language.
y: Sharing our discoveries helps everyone in our class learn from each other.
Using science words helps us explain things clearly and precisely.
Presenting our findings — through talking, drawing or showing — builds our confidence.
eg: Drawing a picture of our experiment and explaining it to the class.
Using words like 'observe', 'predict' and 'compare' when talking about science.
Making a poster to show what we found out about magnets.
Telling a partner what happened when we mixed vinegar and baking soda.
Writing two sentences about what we learned from our weather chart.
Showing our chart to the class and pointing out the patterns we found.

VC2S4H01.w: We are learning that scientists collect information through experiments and observations to explain how nature works.
y: This helps us understand that scientific knowledge comes from careful investigation, not just guessing.
Knowing how scientists work helps us think critically about claims people make about the world.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have gathered knowledge through careful observation of Country over thousands of generations.
eg: Scientists observing how tadpoles change into frogs over many weeks.
A scientist measuring how fast different plants grow in sun versus shade.
Researchers counting the number of birds in a wetland each year to track changes.
Scientists using thermometers to record ocean temperatures over time.
Marie Curie carrying out experiments to discover new elements.
Ecologists observing how fire helps some Australian plants grow new shoots.

VC2S4H02.w: We are learning that people use scientific knowledge and skills to solve everyday problems.
y: This shows us that science is not just something in a laboratory — it helps real people fix real problems.
Understanding how science connects to daily life helps us make smarter choices at home and at school.
Communities around the world, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, use scientific thinking to manage resources and care for the environment.
eg: Using knowledge about germs to understand why we wash our hands.
A farmer using weather data to decide when to plant crops.
An engineer designing a bridge that is strong enough to carry heavy loads.
A doctor using knowledge about the body to treat an illness.
Using our understanding of heat to know not to leave chocolate in the sun.
Aboriginal peoples using knowledge of fire behaviour to do cool burns that protect the bush.

VC2S4U01.w: We are learning the difference between living things, non-living things and things that were once alive.
y: Understanding what makes something 'alive' is one of the most fundamental ideas in science.
This helps us think about how to classify and care for the world around us.
Recognising things that were once alive — like wood, fossils and wool — helps us see connections across nature.
eg: Sorting objects into three groups: living (a plant), non-living (a rock), once living (a seashell).
Discussing whether fire is alive — it grows and needs oxygen, but it doesn't reproduce.
Identifying that a wooden desk was once part of a living tree.
Recognising that a fossil is evidence of something that was once alive.
Explaining why a robot is non-living even though it can move.
Debating whether seeds are alive — they can grow, but only under the right conditions.

VC2S4U02.w: We are learning that plants and animals go through different stages of life, and babies look similar to — but not exactly like — their parents.
y: Understanding life cycles helps us see patterns in how all living things grow and change.
Knowing that offspring resemble but aren't identical to parents is the beginning of understanding genetics.
This knowledge connects to farming, gardening and caring for animals — all important life skills.
eg: Watching a caterpillar change into a chrysalis and then into a butterfly.
Growing a bean seed and observing the stages: seed, sprout, seedling, plant with flowers.
Comparing a puppy to its mother — similar breed but different markings.
Looking at baby photos and noticing we look like our parents but not exactly the same.
Learning that a tadpole looks completely different from an adult frog.
Comparing a chick to a hen and noticing similarities in beak shape and feather colour.

VC2S4U03.w: We are learning that living things in a habitat have different jobs — some make food, some eat it, and some break down dead material.
y: Understanding food webs helps us see how all living things depend on each other.
If one part of a food web is damaged, it can affect everything else in the habitat.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples understand these connections deeply and manage Country to keep ecosystems in balance.
eg: Drawing a food chain: grass → grasshopper → frog → snake → eagle.
Learning that plants are producers because they make their own food using sunlight.
Discussing what would happen to other animals if all the insects disappeared.
Observing mushrooms on a log and learning they are decomposers that break down dead wood.
Creating a food web for a local pond habitat.
Talking about how worms help soil by breaking down fallen leaves.

VC2S4U04.w: We are learning about solids, liquids and gases, and how adding or removing heat can change one into another.
y: Understanding states of matter helps us explain everyday things like ice melting and water boiling.
This knowledge is used in cooking, weather, medicine and many industries.
Learning how heat causes changes is the start of understanding energy and how it moves.
eg: Watching ice cream melt on a hot day — solid changing to liquid.
Boiling water in a kettle and seeing steam — liquid changing to gas.
Putting water in the freezer and seeing it become ice — liquid changing to solid.
Breathing on a cold window and seeing condensation form — gas changing to liquid.
Melting chocolate in a bowl over warm water and watching it become liquid.
Noticing that wet clothes on a line dry because the water evaporates into the air.

VC2S4U05.w: We are learning that natural and manufactured materials have different properties, and these properties affect how we use them.
y: Choosing the right material for a job depends on understanding its properties — is it strong, waterproof, flexible?
This is how engineers and designers think when they create things people use every day.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have deep knowledge of natural materials — choosing the right wood for tools, bark for shelters and fibres for weaving.
eg: Testing materials to see which is the most waterproof for making a raincoat.
Comparing the strength of paper, cardboard and plastic by putting weights on a bridge made from each.
Discussing why glass is used for windows (transparent) but not for shoes (brittle).
Sorting materials into natural (wood, cotton, stone) and manufactured (plastic, nylon, steel).
Choosing the best material to insulate a cup of hot water.
Investigating which fabrics are the most absorbent.

VC2S4U06.w: We are learning that rocks, minerals and soils are important resources with features we can observe and use in different ways.
y: Rocks and soil are the foundation of our landscapes, farms and buildings — understanding them helps us appreciate their value.
Learning to observe and classify Earth materials develops our scientific observation skills.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have used specific rocks and minerals for tools, art and ceremony for tens of thousands of years.
eg: Sorting rocks by colour, hardness, texture and grain size.
Testing whether a rock is hard or soft by trying to scratch it with a nail.
Looking at soil through a magnifying glass to see sand, clay, organic matter and tiny organisms.
Learning that granite is used in buildings because it is very hard and durable.
Comparing sandy soil and clay soil by adding water and seeing how they behave differently.
Discussing how Aboriginal peoples used specific hard stones to make cutting tools.

VC2S4U07.w: We are learning that water moves through the environment in a cycle — falling as rain, flowing through rivers, evaporating and forming clouds again.
y: The water cycle explains where our drinking water comes from and why we need to look after it.
Understanding this cycle helps us understand weather, floods, droughts and how ecosystems work.
Every drop of water on Earth has been cycling for billions of years — the water we drink today was once rain long ago.
eg: Building a mini water cycle in a sealed container with water, plastic wrap and a lamp as the sun.
Tracing the journey of a raindrop from cloud to river to ocean and back.
Watching condensation form on a cold glass of water on a warm day.
Drawing and labelling the water cycle: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, collection.
Discussing where our school's water supply comes from.
Observing a puddle over a few days and seeing it slowly evaporate.

VC2S4U08.w: We are learning that weather and climate affect the land and living things, and that human activity can change the climate.
y: Understanding the difference between weather and climate helps us make sense of changing conditions.
Knowing how humans affect the climate helps us think about what we can do to reduce our impact.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have observed climate patterns for thousands of generations and have seen recent changes affecting Country.
eg: Comparing the weather today with the climate for this time of year.
Discussing how drought affects farmers, animals and waterways.
Learning that burning fossil fuels adds greenhouse gases to the atmosphere.
Looking at how flooding changes the shape of a river or creek.
Researching how rising temperatures affect coral reefs.
Talking about actions we can take — like using less electricity — to reduce climate impact.

VC2S4U09.w: We are learning that heat can come from different sources, and temperature changes when heat moves between objects.
y: Understanding heat transfer helps us keep warm in winter, stay cool in summer and cook safely.
This knowledge is used in designing houses, clothing and technology.
Learning about heat energy is the start of understanding how energy moves through the world.
eg: Holding a mug of hot chocolate and feeling the heat move from the mug to our hands.
Measuring the temperature of water before and after adding ice cubes.
Wrapping a thermometer in different materials to see which keeps heat in best.
Discussing why metal spoons feel colder than wooden spoons even at the same temperature.
Noticing that dark surfaces get hotter in the sun than light surfaces.
Investigating how a thermos flask keeps drinks hot by slowing down heat transfer.

VC2S4U10.w: We are learning about different forces — like friction, gravity, magnetism and static electricity — and how they affect the way things move.
y: Understanding forces helps us explain why things move, stop, speed up or slow down.
Forces are everywhere — we use them when we walk, ride bikes, play sport and even when we sit still.
Engineers use their knowledge of forces to design safe buildings, bridges, vehicles and machines.
eg: Sliding a book across different surfaces and feeling how friction changes on carpet versus a desk.
Dropping objects and seeing that gravity pulls everything downward.
Testing which magnets are strongest by seeing how many paperclips they can hold.
Rubbing a balloon on our hair and watching it stick to a wall because of static electricity.
Designing a parachute and testing how air resistance slows it down.
Investigating how changing the slope of a ramp affects how fast a ball rolls.

VC2S4I01.w: We are learning to use what we observe to ask good questions and predict what might happen in our investigations.
y: Asking clear questions helps us focus our investigation so we can find useful answers.
Making predictions based on what we already know helps us think scientifically.
Good questions lead to discoveries — every major scientific breakthrough started with someone asking 'why?' or 'what if?'
eg: Noticing that plants near the window are taller and asking 'Does sunlight help plants grow faster?'
Predicting that heavier objects will make a bigger splash when dropped in water.
Asking 'What will happen to the ice if we wrap it in fabric?' based on our knowledge of insulation.
Observing a snail and wondering 'Do snails prefer wet or dry places?'
Predicting which paper aeroplane design will fly the furthest.
Asking 'Why do some rocks sink and others float?' after testing them in a tub of water.

VC2S4I02.w: We are learning to plan and carry out fair tests using a step-by-step guide, while staying safe.
y: Fair tests give us results we can trust because we change only one thing at a time.
Planning investigations step by step helps us be organised and avoid mistakes.
Staying safe protects us and our classmates so everyone can enjoy learning through experiments.
eg: Testing which surface a toy car rolls fastest on — changing only the surface, not the car.
Writing out our method before starting so we know what to do at each step.
Keeping everything the same except one thing — like the amount of water given to each plant.
Wearing safety glasses when testing what happens when we mix substances.
Repeating our test three times to make sure the results are reliable.
Cleaning up our workspace carefully after an experiment.

VC2S4I03.w: We are learning to make careful observations and measurements using proper tools like thermometers and rulers.
y: Accurate measurements help us get results we can trust and compare with others.
Using the right tools for the job is an important skill in science, cooking, building and many other areas.
Careful observation helps us notice things that are easy to miss, leading to deeper understanding.
eg: Using a thermometer to measure water temperature before and after adding ice.
Measuring the length of a shadow at different times of day with a ruler.
Using a balance scale to compare the mass of different objects.
Observing and recording the colour, texture and size of different soil samples.
Timing how long it takes an ice cube to melt using a stopwatch.
Using a rain gauge to measure how much rain fell during a week.

VC2S4I04.w: We are learning to organise our data using tables, graphs and models so we can find patterns.
y: Organising data helps us see patterns and trends that would be hard to spot in a list of numbers.
Graphs and tables are a visual language — they help us communicate information clearly to others.
Scientists, mathematicians and many workers use data organisation every day to make decisions.
eg: Drawing a bar graph to show how far different toy cars rolled on different surfaces.
Making a table recording the temperature at 9 am, 12 pm and 3 pm each day for a week.
Creating a pie chart showing the types of minibeasts we found in the garden.
Using a column graph to display the results of a class seed-growing experiment.
Building a model of the water cycle using a plastic container, water and cling film.
Drawing a food chain to show how energy moves from plants to animals.

VC2S4I05.w: We are learning to compare our results with others, check if our test was fair, and think about what to investigate next.
y: Comparing results helps us see if our findings are reliable or if something went wrong.
Thinking about fairness helps us improve our investigations for next time.
Real scientists always review their work — asking 'What could I do better?' is a sign of strong thinking.
eg: Comparing our plant growth data with another group and discussing differences.
Asking 'Was our test fair? Did we change only one thing?'
Noticing that our results were different from another group and trying to work out why.
Thinking about whether we measured accurately enough or need to try again.
Suggesting a follow-up question like 'What if we tested a different type of soil?'
Discussing whether our sample size was big enough to draw a conclusion.

VC2S4I06.w: We are learning to share our findings clearly, using science words, for a particular audience.
y: Communicating clearly helps other people understand and learn from our discoveries.
Using precise science vocabulary shows that we understand the ideas, not just the results.
Thinking about our audience helps us explain things in a way that makes sense to them.
eg: Writing a report about our experiment using words like 'hypothesis', 'variable' and 'conclusion'.
Creating a poster showing our method, results and what we learned.
Presenting our investigation to the class using diagrams and graphs.
Explaining our findings to a younger class using simpler language and pictures.
Writing a conclusion that answers our original question with evidence from our data.
Making a short video explaining what we discovered about magnets.

VC2S6H01.w: We are learning that scientific knowledge develops over time, often because scientists work together and build on each other's discoveries.
y: This helps us understand that science is a human process — knowledge grows as different people contribute ideas and evidence over many years.
Knowing that scientific understanding can change encourages us to stay curious and open-minded.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander knowledge systems also develop and are shared across generations, combining observation, practice and collective wisdom.
eg: Learning how our understanding of the solar system changed from Earth-centred to Sun-centred over centuries.
Discovering that many scientists contributed to understanding electricity — not just one person.
Seeing how modern medicines were developed by building on earlier discoveries about germs and the body.
Learning that Aboriginal peoples developed sophisticated ecological knowledge passed down for millennia.
Discussing how international teams of scientists work together on climate research.
Exploring how the discovery of DNA built on earlier work about inheritance by Mendel.

VC2S6H02.w: We are learning that communities use scientific knowledge to identify problems and decide on actions that affect the environment and society.
y: This shows us that science isn't just about discovery — it's about making informed decisions that affect real people and places.
Understanding how communities use science helps us participate in important conversations about our future.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities combine scientific and cultural knowledge to manage Country sustainably, including through practices like fire management.
eg: A town using water quality data to decide whether a local river is safe for swimming.
Communities debating whether to build a wind farm based on environmental impact studies.
Scientists and Aboriginal rangers working together to manage invasive species in a national park.
A school using data about waste to design a recycling program.
Local government using flood maps based on scientific modelling to plan building zones.
Farmers using soil testing results to choose which crops to plant.

VC2S6U01.w: We are learning that habitats have specific physical conditions, and changing those conditions — including through human activity — can affect the living things there.
y: Understanding habitats helps us see why protecting ecosystems matters for all life, including humans.
Human activities like clearing land, polluting water or introducing invasive species can disrupt the balance of a habitat.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have managed habitats sustainably for thousands of generations through practices like seasonal burning and selective harvesting.
eg: Investigating how clearing trees for farmland affects the animals that lived in the forest.
Researching how pollution from a factory changed the water quality and organisms in a nearby creek.
Studying how the introduction of foxes and cats has affected native Australian wildlife.
Comparing the biodiversity of a natural bushland area with a nearby urban park.
Learning how coral bleaching happens when ocean temperatures rise.
Discussing how Aboriginal fire management creates diverse habitat conditions for different species.

VC2S6U02.w: We are learning that living things have changed over time, as shown by fossils, and that their features and behaviours help them survive in their environment.
y: Understanding adaptation helps us see how living things are shaped by their environment over long periods.
Fossils give us evidence of life forms that no longer exist, helping us piece together Earth's history.
Understanding how organisms adapt helps us predict how they might respond to environmental changes like climate change.
eg: Examining fossils and comparing ancient organisms to living ones.
Studying how a platypus has features adapted for both land and water — webbed feet, waterproof fur and a bill for sensing prey.
Learning how the peppered moth changed colour over generations in response to pollution.
Discussing why Australian marsupials evolved differently from mammals on other continents.
Researching how cactus plants have adapted to survive in deserts with very little water.
Exploring how behavioural adaptations — like migration — help animals survive seasonal changes.

VC2S6U03.w: We are learning that the properties of matter — solids, liquids and gases — can be explained by how tiny particles are arranged and moving, and that different substances can be mixed together.
y: The particle model helps us explain everyday phenomena like melting, boiling and dissolving.
Understanding how particles behave gives us a framework for thinking about all matter in the universe.
This knowledge is applied in cooking, manufacturing, medicine and environmental science.
eg: Using the particle model to explain why ice (tightly packed particles) takes up less space than water vapour (spread-out particles).
Dissolving salt in water and discussing where the salt particles went.
Separating a mixture of sand and water using a filter.
Explaining why a balloon expands when heated — gas particles move faster and push outward.
Investigating whether sugar dissolves faster in hot or cold water.
Using evaporation to recover dissolved salt from salty water.

VC2S6U04.w: We are learning that some changes to materials can be undone (reversible) and some cannot (irreversible), and this matters for how we use materials.
y: Knowing the difference between reversible and irreversible changes helps us understand everyday processes like cooking and recycling.
This knowledge is important for environmental decisions — some damage to materials and ecosystems cannot be undone.
Industries rely on this understanding to manufacture products and manage waste.
eg: Melting chocolate (reversible — it can solidify again) versus baking a cake (irreversible — you can't un-bake it).
Dissolving salt in water (reversible by evaporation) versus burning wood (irreversible).
Freezing orange juice into ice blocks and then letting them melt back into juice.
Discussing why recycling aluminium cans is possible but recycling burnt paper is not.
Observing rust forming on iron and learning that this is an irreversible chemical change.
Investigating which kitchen processes are reversible and which are not.

VC2S6U05.w: We are learning that natural processes like weathering and erosion slowly or quickly change Earth's surface by breaking down and moving rock and soil.
y: Understanding weathering and erosion helps us explain why landscapes look the way they do.
This knowledge helps communities plan for natural changes like coastal erosion and landslides.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have deep knowledge of how Country changes over time, reflected in oral histories and cultural narratives.
eg: Observing how water running over soil in the schoolyard creates small channels — just like rivers carve valleys.
Examining how tree roots can crack and split rocks over time.
Comparing photos of a coastline taken years apart to see how waves have eroded the cliffs.
Modelling erosion by pouring water over a tray of sand and soil at different angles.
Learning how wind erosion shapes sand dunes in desert environments.
Discussing how frost can break apart rocks when water freezes in cracks and expands.

VC2S6U06.w: We are learning that sudden events like earthquakes, volcanic eruptions and floods can change Earth's surface, and that people can take actions to reduce their impact.
y: Understanding natural hazards helps communities prepare for and respond to dangerous events.
Knowing why these events happen connects to understanding how the Earth works beneath the surface.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander oral histories contain records of geological events like volcanic eruptions and sea level changes spanning thousands of years.
eg: Researching how an earthquake is caused by tectonic plates moving against each other.
Building a model volcano and discussing how eruptions change the landscape around them.
Investigating how communities prepare for floods — building levees, creating warning systems.
Learning about the tsunami warning system and how it helps people evacuate.
Studying how the eruption of a volcano can create new land but also destroy habitats.
Discussing the Budj Bim cultural landscape in Victoria, where Gunditjmara peoples witnessed volcanic activity thousands of years ago.

VC2S6U07.w: We are learning that gravity keeps the planets orbiting the Sun, and that Earth's tilt and rotation cause patterns like seasons and changing day lengths.
y: Understanding how Earth moves in space helps us explain everyday experiences like seasons, day length and time zones.
Gravity is one of the fundamental forces of the universe — understanding it helps us think about how everything from apples to galaxies behaves.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander astronomical knowledge — one of the oldest in the world — includes sophisticated understanding of seasonal sky patterns.
eg: Using a torch and a tilted globe to show why Australia has summer when Europe has winter.
Modelling the Earth orbiting the Sun while spinning on its tilted axis.
Measuring the length of day (sunrise to sunset) at different times of year and graphing the results.
Learning that gravity is what keeps us on Earth and keeps the Moon orbiting around us.
Discussing how different time zones exist because Earth rotates and different places face the Sun at different times.
Learning how Kulin Nation peoples used star positions to mark seasonal changes for food gathering.

VC2S6U08.w: We are learning that light comes from many sources, travels in straight lines, and can be absorbed, reflected or bent by different materials.
y: Understanding light helps us explain everyday things like shadows, reflections, rainbows and colour.
This knowledge is applied in technology we use every day — from cameras and glasses to fibre optic internet.
Learning about light is the beginning of understanding a whole branch of physics called optics.
eg: Shining a torch through a slit to show that light travels in straight lines.
Using a mirror to reflect light onto a wall and changing the angle to move the spot.
Placing a pencil in a glass of water and observing how it looks bent — showing refraction.
Using a prism to split white light into the colours of the rainbow.
Investigating why shadows are created when light is blocked by an opaque object.
Testing different materials to see which absorb, reflect or transmit light.

VC2S6U09.w: We are learning that some materials conduct electricity and some do not, and that electrical circuits have parts that each play a role in making the circuit work.
y: Understanding circuits helps us know how the electrical devices we use every day actually work.
Knowing about conductors and insulators helps us stay safe around electricity.
This knowledge is essential for careers in engineering, electronics, renewable energy and many trades.
eg: Building a simple circuit with a battery, wires, a switch and a light bulb.
Testing different materials — metal, plastic, wood, rubber — to see which conduct electricity.
Adding a second light bulb to a circuit and noticing that both bulbs are dimmer.
Drawing a circuit diagram using standard symbols for battery, switch and lamp.
Investigating the difference between series and parallel circuits.
Discussing why electricians wear rubber-soled boots and use insulated tools.

VC2S6I01.w: We are learning to develop questions and predictions that can be investigated to find patterns and test relationships.
y: Strong investigable questions are the foundation of good science — they lead to meaningful discoveries.
Learning to predict based on evidence helps us think logically and build arguments.
This skill transfers beyond science — forming clear questions and testing ideas is useful in every subject and in daily life.
eg: Turning 'I wonder about plants' into 'Does the amount of fertiliser affect how tall a bean plant grows?'
Predicting that darker-coloured surfaces will absorb more heat than lighter ones, and designing a test.
Asking 'Is there a relationship between the length of a pendulum and the time of its swing?'
Forming a hypothesis: 'If we increase the angle of the ramp, the ball will roll faster.'
Developing a question about whether exercise affects heart rate.
Predicting which insulation material will keep water hot the longest and explaining our reasoning.

VC2S6I02.w: We are learning to plan and carry out investigations where we control variables for a fair test, think about risks, and get permission to access special places.
y: Controlling variables ensures our results are valid — if we change too many things at once, we can't tell what caused the outcome.
Thinking about risks shows responsibility and helps keep everyone safe during investigations.
Seeking permission to access places — especially significant sites — shows respect for community and environment.
eg: Planning a fair test by identifying the independent, dependent and controlled variables.
Writing a risk assessment before conducting an experiment with hot water.
Asking permission before collecting plant samples from a local reserve.
Keeping all conditions the same except the one variable we are testing.
Checking with Traditional Owners before conducting investigations on culturally significant land.
Designing a controlled experiment to test which brand of paper towel is most absorbent.

VC2S6I03.w: We are learning to use equipment to observe, measure and record data precisely, repeating measurements to improve accuracy.
y: Precise measurements make our data more reliable and our conclusions more trustworthy.
Repeating measurements helps us identify errors and increases our confidence in the results.
These skills are used by scientists, engineers, healthcare workers and tradespeople every day.
eg: Using a digital thermometer to measure temperature to one decimal place.
Measuring the volume of liquid in a measuring cylinder by reading at the bottom of the meniscus.
Taking three measurements of the same distance and calculating the average.
Using a stopwatch to time how long a substance takes to dissolve, repeating three times.
Recording observations in a detailed table with units and appropriate precision.
Using a spring scale to measure force in newtons accurately.

VC2S6I04.w: We are learning to organise data and create representations — like tables, graphs and models — that reveal patterns and relationships.
y: Well-organised data helps us see connections and patterns that aren't obvious in raw numbers.
Choosing the right type of graph or model makes information easier for others to understand.
Data literacy — being able to read and create data displays — is an essential skill in today's world.
eg: Creating a line graph to show how temperature changed over the course of a day.
Using a scatter plot to show the relationship between plant height and amount of sunlight.
Building a 3D model of the layers of the Earth using clay.
Constructing a table with clear headings, units and averages from an experiment.
Drawing a labelled diagram to explain how a circuit works.
Using a pie chart to display the proportions of different materials in a soil sample.

VC2S6I05.w: We are learning to compare our methods and findings with others, identify errors, and develop further questions.
y: Comparing findings helps us evaluate whether our results are consistent and reliable.
Identifying sources of error makes us better investigators — it shows we understand what could have gone wrong.
Developing further questions shows that science is ongoing — every answer leads to new questions.
eg: Comparing our group's results with another group's and discussing why they might differ.
Identifying that a measurement error occurred because the ruler wasn't aligned correctly.
Discussing whether our sample size was large enough to draw reliable conclusions.
Suggesting improvements to our method, like using a more precise measuring tool.
Proposing a follow-up investigation based on an unexpected result.
Evaluating whether our control variables really stayed the same during the experiment.

VC2S6I06.w: We are learning to communicate our scientific ideas and findings clearly for a specific audience, using the right vocabulary and format.
y: Clear scientific communication ensures our discoveries can be understood, checked and built upon by others.
Adapting our language and format for different audiences is an important real-world skill.
Using correct scientific terminology shows we understand the concepts, not just the facts.
eg: Writing a formal scientific report with sections for aim, method, results, discussion and conclusion.
Creating an infographic about renewable energy for display in the school library.
Presenting investigation findings to the class using a slideshow with graphs and diagrams.
Writing a simplified explanation of our experiment for a younger year level.
Using terms like 'hypothesis', 'variable', 'evidence' and 'conclusion' accurately.
Making a short documentary video explaining a science concept to a community audience.

VC2S8H01.w: We are learning that scientific knowledge — including models and theories — can change when new evidence comes to light.
y: This helps us understand that science is not a fixed set of facts — it is a living process that improves as we learn more.
Recognising that knowledge can change builds our ability to think critically and evaluate claims rather than accepting them blindly.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander knowledge systems also evolve through ongoing observation and dialogue across generations, showing that adaptive knowledge is a strength, not a weakness.
eg: Scientists once believed the atom was the smallest particle, until experiments revealed protons, neutrons and electrons inside it.
The discovery of tectonic plates replaced earlier explanations for why continents seem to fit together like puzzle pieces.
Medical understanding of stomach ulcers changed dramatically when researchers proved bacteria — not just stress — could cause them.
Climate models have been refined over decades as new data from ice cores and satellites becomes available.
The reclassification of Pluto from planet to dwarf planet in 2006 showed how definitions shift as our understanding deepens.
Aboriginal astronomical knowledge has been continually refined through millennia of observation, recording changes in star positions and seasonal patterns.

VC2S8H02.w: We are learning that advancing scientific knowledge involves people from different backgrounds, perspectives and worldviews working together.
y: Diverse teams bring different ways of seeing problems, which leads to more creative and robust scientific solutions.
Understanding this challenges the myth of the lone genius and shows that science is a collaborative, community-driven process.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have contributed ecological, botanical and astronomical knowledge for tens of thousands of years — recognising this enriches our understanding of how science works globally.
eg: The Human Genome Project involved thousands of scientists from over 20 countries working together to map human DNA.
Aboriginal rangers contribute deep ecological knowledge to conservation programs that Western science alone could not achieve.
Marie Curie, a Polish woman working in France, overcame gender barriers to make groundbreaking discoveries about radioactivity.
International teams at CERN collaborate across cultures and languages to investigate the fundamental particles of the universe.
Citizen science programs invite everyday people — including students — to collect data that professional scientists analyse.
Traditional ecological knowledge from Indigenous communities worldwide is now informing modern approaches to fire management and biodiversity conservation.

VC2S8H03.w: We are learning that scientific responses to real-world issues can involve ethical, environmental, social and economic trade-offs.
y: Understanding trade-offs helps us see that scientific decisions are rarely simple — they involve balancing competing values and priorities.
This develops our capacity to engage in informed debate about issues like genetic modification, energy policy and medical ethics.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives remind us that decisions about Country and resources must consider long-term environmental and cultural consequences, not just short-term economic gain.
eg: Building a new dam provides water and electricity but can flood habitats and displace communities, including culturally significant sites.
Genetic modification of crops can increase food production but raises ethical questions about biodiversity and long-term ecological effects.
Mining lithium for electric car batteries reduces fossil fuel dependence but creates environmental damage at extraction sites.
Animal testing has contributed to medical breakthroughs but raises serious ethical concerns about animal welfare.
Vaccinations protect communities but require balancing individual choice with public health responsibility.
Uranium mining in Australia involves weighing economic benefits against environmental risks and the wishes of Traditional Owners whose Country is affected.

VC2S8H04.w: We are learning that communicating scientific knowledge to the public plays an important role in shaping people's views and government policies.
y: Understanding how science is communicated helps us recognise when information is accurate, misleading or oversimplified in the media.
This builds our media literacy — a critical skill in an age when scientific claims are used to influence public opinion and political decisions.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities have always communicated knowledge through story, art and ceremony — these methods carry scientific understanding across generations in ways that complement written publication.
eg: Climate scientists communicate findings through reports that directly influence government emissions targets and environmental policies.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, how scientific information was communicated affected public trust in vaccines and health measures.
Misleading graphs or statistics in news articles can distort people's understanding of issues like crime rates or health risks.
David Attenborough documentaries have shaped public awareness of biodiversity loss and ocean pollution worldwide.
Social media can spread both accurate scientific findings and dangerous misinformation at equal speed.
Aboriginal communities use cultural narratives to communicate ecological knowledge — for example, stories about seasonal changes that encode sophisticated environmental science.

VC2S8U01.w: We are learning that organisms can be classified into groups based on similarities and differences, and tools like dichotomous keys help us organise this diversity.
y: Classification systems help us make sense of the enormous diversity of life on Earth by organising millions of species into manageable groups.
Learning to use dichotomous keys develops our logical thinking — each decision point requires careful observation and systematic reasoning.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have their own sophisticated classification systems for plants and animals, based on ecological relationships, behaviour and cultural significance.
eg: Using a dichotomous key to identify invertebrates collected from a local waterway based on observable features.
Classifying organisms into kingdoms — animals, plants, fungi, protists and bacteria — based on cell structure and how they obtain energy.
Comparing vertebrate classes (fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, mammals) by examining skeletal structure and reproduction methods.
Creating our own dichotomous key for a collection of leaves based on features like shape, margin and venation pattern.
Discussing why classification systems change as new species are discovered or DNA evidence reveals unexpected relationships.
Learning how Gunditjmara peoples classify eels and fish based on seasonal behaviour and habitat, reflecting deep ecological understanding.

VC2S8U02.w: We are learning that cells are the basic building blocks of all life, and different types of cells have specialised structures that carry out specific functions.
y: Understanding cells gives us a foundation for understanding how all living things grow, function, reproduce and respond to their environment.
Cell biology connects directly to real-world applications in medicine, agriculture and biotechnology that affect our daily lives.
Appreciating the complexity within a single cell helps us understand why even microscopic organisms play critical roles in ecosystems.
eg: Comparing plant and animal cells under a microscope and identifying structures like the cell wall, chloroplasts and nucleus.
Examining red blood cells and understanding how their disc shape maximises oxygen transport through the body.
Learning that nerve cells have long extensions (axons) that allow electrical signals to travel quickly across the body.
Observing how root hair cells have elongated projections that increase surface area for water absorption.
Discussing why bacteria are single-celled organisms that lack a nucleus, and how this differs from our own cells.
Investigating how white blood cells change shape to engulf and destroy pathogens, protecting us from disease.

VC2S8U03.w: We are learning that the structure of cells, tissues and organs is directly related to their function, and that organ systems keep organisms alive.
y: The structure-function relationship is one of the most powerful ideas in biology — once we understand it, we can predict how unfamiliar structures might work.
This knowledge underpins our understanding of human health, disease and how medical treatments target specific body systems.
Seeing how organ systems work together helps us appreciate the interconnected complexity of living things, from single cells to whole organisms.
eg: Examining how the alveoli in our lungs have thin walls and a large surface area to maximise gas exchange.
Tracing how the digestive system breaks food down progressively — from mouth to stomach to small intestine — with each organ contributing differently.
Learning how the structure of villi in the small intestine increases surface area for nutrient absorption.
Investigating how the heart's four chambers and valves ensure blood flows in one direction through the circulatory system.
Comparing the muscular walls of arteries (thick, elastic) with veins (thinner, with valves) and explaining how structure relates to function.
Discussing how the skeletal and muscular systems work together to produce movement, with bones acting as levers and muscles providing force.

VC2S8U04.w: We are learning that matter and energy flow through ecosystems via food webs and food pyramids, and that changes to any part of the system can affect the whole ecosystem.
y: Understanding energy flow helps us see why ecosystems are fragile — removing or adding one species can cascade through the entire food web.
This knowledge is critical for making informed decisions about conservation, land management and the environmental impact of human activity.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have managed ecosystems sustainably for millennia by understanding these interconnections — practices like cultural burning maintain biodiversity by supporting energy flow through habitats.
eg: Constructing a food web for a local ecosystem and tracing what would happen if a key predator were removed.
Using an energy pyramid to explain why there are fewer top predators than primary consumers in any ecosystem.
Investigating how the introduction of cane toads disrupted Australian food webs by poisoning native predators.
Calculating the approximate energy transfer between trophic levels, recognising that about 90% is lost as heat at each step.
Analysing how overfishing one species can cause population explosions in another and collapse in a third.
Discussing how Aboriginal fire management practices create a mosaic of habitats that support diverse food webs across the landscape.

VC2S8U05.w: We are learning that the particle and kinetic theories explain how the arrangement and movement of particles determine a substance's properties and behaviour.
y: The particle model gives us a powerful framework for explaining everyday phenomena like melting, evaporation, dissolving and gas pressure.
Understanding how particles behave at the microscopic level helps us predict and control what happens at the macroscopic level — in cooking, manufacturing and engineering.
This theoretical thinking — using an invisible model to explain visible behaviour — is a core scientific skill that we apply across chemistry and physics.
eg: Using the particle model to explain why gases can be compressed but solids and liquids cannot.
Explaining why a balloon expands when heated — particles move faster, collide more frequently with the walls and exert greater pressure.
Modelling how particles in a solid vibrate in fixed positions, while particles in a liquid slide past each other.
Explaining diffusion — why we can smell perfume across a room — in terms of particle movement from high to low concentration.
Predicting that increasing temperature will increase the rate of dissolving because particles move faster and collide more often.
Using the kinetic theory to explain why evaporation causes cooling — the fastest-moving particles escape, lowering the average kinetic energy of those remaining.

VC2S8U06.w: We are learning that matter can be classified as pure substances or mixtures, and mixtures can be separated using techniques based on their components' properties.
y: Understanding how to classify and separate matter is fundamental to chemistry and has practical applications in water treatment, recycling and forensic science.
Learning separation techniques develops our ability to think about the physical properties of substances and how those properties can be exploited.
This knowledge connects to sustainability — understanding how materials can be separated and recovered helps us reduce waste and manage resources more responsibly.
eg: Classifying salt water as a mixture and pure water as a substance, then separating them by distillation.
Using filtration to separate sand from water and evaporation to recover dissolved salt.
Separating a mixture of iron filings and sulfur powder using a magnet, exploiting the magnetic property of iron.
Using chromatography to separate the pigments in black ink and identify the individual colours.
Distinguishing between a pure substance (like distilled water with a sharp boiling point) and a mixture (like salt water with a range).
Investigating how water treatment plants use a combination of sedimentation, filtration and chemical treatment to purify drinking water.

VC2S8U07.w: We are learning that atomic theory explains the difference between elements, compounds and mixtures, and that we represent them using symbols and chemical formulas.
y: Atomic theory provides a universal language for understanding what everything in the universe is made of — from stars to living cells.
Chemical symbols and formulas are the shorthand of science — learning to read and write them opens the door to understanding reactions, materials and biological processes.
This knowledge connects to real-world chemistry in medicine, agriculture, manufacturing and environmental science.
eg: Learning that water (H₂O) is a compound made of two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom, while O₂ is an element in molecular form.
Using the periodic table to find the symbol, atomic number and properties of common elements like carbon, oxygen and iron.
Distinguishing between a mixture of iron and sulfur (which can be separated physically) and the compound iron sulfide (which cannot).
Writing simple chemical formulas and understanding what the subscript numbers represent in terms of atom ratios.
Modelling the difference between an element (atoms of one type), a compound (atoms of different types bonded) and a mixture (different substances not bonded).
Discussing how understanding atomic composition helps forensic scientists identify unknown substances found at a crime scene.

VC2S8U08.w: We are learning to distinguish physical changes from chemical changes by looking for signs like colour change, gas production, temperature change or precipitate formation.
y: Knowing the difference between physical and chemical changes helps us understand and predict what happens when substances interact in cooking, industry and nature.
Recognising the signs of chemical change is a practical skill used by chemists, environmental scientists and anyone working with materials.
This distinction is foundational — it prepares us to understand chemical reactions, energy changes and the conservation of matter in more depth.
eg: Observing that dissolving sugar in water is a physical change (reversible by evaporation), while burning sugar produces new substances (irreversible).
Mixing vinegar and baking soda and identifying the fizzing as gas production — a sign of chemical change.
Noting that rusting iron changes colour and produces a new substance (iron oxide), indicating a chemical change.
Comparing melting ice (physical — same substance, different state) with cooking an egg (chemical — new substances formed).
Mixing two clear solutions and observing a precipitate form at the bottom of the beaker.
Investigating whether the temperature change when acids and metals react indicates an exothermic chemical change.

VC2S8U09.w: We are learning that Earth's resources can be renewable or non-renewable, and that how we extract and use them involves both benefits and risks to sustainability.
y: Understanding resource sustainability helps us make informed choices about energy, consumption and environmental responsibility.
This topic connects science to economics and ethics — resource decisions affect communities, ecosystems and future generations.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have practised sustainable resource management for tens of thousands of years, offering models of long-term thinking that modern society is beginning to adopt.
eg: Comparing the environmental impact of mining coal (non-renewable, high emissions) with installing solar panels (renewable, lower ongoing impact).
Investigating how groundwater extraction can deplete aquifers faster than they are replenished, affecting communities and ecosystems.
Analysing the life cycle of a mobile phone — from mining rare earth minerals to manufacturing, use and disposal.
Discussing how wind farms provide renewable energy but can affect bird populations and landscape values.
Researching how Aboriginal peoples managed forests and waterways sustainably, ensuring resources were available for future generations.
Evaluating the sustainability of fishing practices — comparing overfishing with catch limits and marine protected areas.

VC2S8U10.w: We are learning that Earth's tectonic plates move, creating geological features at plate boundaries, and that evidence supports the theory of plate tectonics.
y: Plate tectonics explains some of Earth's most dramatic features — mountains, ocean trenches, volcanoes and earthquake zones — as part of a single unifying theory.
Understanding plate movement helps communities prepare for natural hazards and explains why certain regions are more geologically active than others.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander oral histories contain accounts of geological events — including volcanic eruptions and sea level changes — that align with and sometimes predate Western scientific records.
eg: Mapping the locations of earthquakes and volcanoes worldwide and noticing that they cluster along tectonic plate boundaries.
Examining fossil evidence showing that identical species existed on continents now separated by oceans, supporting the idea that they were once joined.
Modelling the three types of plate boundaries — convergent, divergent and transform — and the features each creates.
Investigating how the Himalayan mountains formed from the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates.
Analysing how seafloor spreading at mid-ocean ridges provides evidence for the movement of tectonic plates.

Discussing the Budj Bim cultural landscape in Victoria, where Gunditjmara peoples witnessed volcanic activity approximately 30,000 years ago and engineered aquaculture systems in the resulting landscape.

VC2S8U11.w: We are learning that the rock cycle transforms rocks between sedimentary, igneous and metamorphic types over different timescales, and their properties reflect how they formed.
y: The rock cycle shows us that Earth's surface is constantly being recycled and renewed — rocks we see today may have been through multiple transformations over millions of years.
Understanding rock types and their formation helps us in practical applications from construction and mining to understanding geological history.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples have deep knowledge of rock types and their properties, selecting specific stones for tools, grinding, art and ceremonial purposes for tens of thousands of years.
eg: Examining samples of sandstone (sedimentary), granite (igneous) and marble (metamorphic) and linking their visible properties to how they formed.
Modelling the rock cycle by showing how igneous rock weathers into sediment, compacts into sedimentary rock, and transforms under heat and pressure into metamorphic rock.
Investigating why granite is used in buildings (hard, durable igneous rock) while slate is used for roofing (flat, layered metamorphic rock).
Observing fossils in sedimentary rock and explaining why fossils are rarely found in igneous or metamorphic rocks.
Comparing the crystal sizes in basalt (small, cooled quickly on the surface) and granite (large, cooled slowly underground) to infer their formation conditions.
Discussing how Aboriginal peoples identified and traded specific rock types — like greenstone for axe heads — based on their hardness and fracture properties.

VC2S8U12.w: We are learning that the changing positions of Earth, the Sun and the Moon cause predictable cycles including seasons, eclipses and tides.
y: Understanding these celestial cycles helps us explain everyday experiences — why seasons change, why the Moon looks different each night and why tides rise and fall.
These predictable patterns have practical significance for agriculture, navigation, fishing and coastal communities around the world.

Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander astronomical knowledge — among the oldest in the world — includes sophisticated understanding of eclipses, tidal patterns and seasonal star movements used for navigation and resource management.
eg: Using a model of the Earth-Sun-Moon system to explain why solar and lunar eclipses occur at specific times.
Explaining how Earth's 23.5° axial tilt causes seasons by changing the angle and duration of sunlight received at different latitudes.
Investigating tidal patterns in a local coastal area and linking them to the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun.
Graphing day length throughout the year and relating the pattern to Earth's orbit and axial tilt.
Modelling the phases of the Moon by showing how the illuminated portion we see changes as the Moon orbits Earth.
Learning how Torres Strait Islander peoples used tidal knowledge and star positions to navigate between islands and time their fishing activities.

VC2S8U13.w: We are learning that simple machines — like levers, pulleys, wedges and screws — change the direction or size of forces to make work easier.
y: Understanding simple machines helps us see the physics behind everyday tools — from scissors and ramps to wheelbarrows and bicycle gears.
These principles are the foundation of engineering and mechanical design, used in everything from construction cranes to medical devices.

People across all cultures have invented and refined simple machines — Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples developed sophisticated tools including levers, wedges and throwing devices that demonstrate deep understanding of mechanical advantage.
eg: Using a lever (like a crowbar) to lift a heavy object and investigating how changing the fulcrum position affects the effort required.
Comparing the force needed to push a load directly upward versus rolling it up an inclined plane (ramp).
Investigating how a pulley system allows us to lift a heavy weight with less effort by changing the direction and magnitude of the force.
Examining a screw and recognising it as an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder, which converts rotational force into linear movement.
Calculating the mechanical advantage of a wheelbarrow by comparing the input force to the output force.
Discussing how the woomera (spear-thrower) acts as a lever, extending the arm and increasing the speed and distance a spear can travel.

VC2S8U14.w: We are learning that balanced and unbalanced forces determine whether an object stays still, speeds up, slows down or changes direction, and force diagrams help us represent this.
y: Understanding forces and motion explains why objects behave the way they do — from a ball rolling to a stop to a rocket launching into space.
Force diagrams are a powerful tool for visualising and predicting motion, developing our ability to think abstractly about physical systems.
These concepts are essential for engineering, sports science, vehicle safety design and understanding how the physical world operates.
eg: Drawing a force diagram for a book resting on a table, showing that gravity pulling down is balanced by the normal force pushing up.
Investigating what happens when we push a trolley with increasing force — the unbalanced force causes it to accelerate.
Explaining why a skydiver reaches terminal velocity — air resistance increases until it balances the force of gravity.
Analysing the forces on a car braking on a wet road versus a dry road, and how friction affects stopping distance.
Using force arrows to represent the thrust, drag, lift and weight acting on an aircraft in flight.
Investigating how the mass of an object affects its acceleration when the same unbalanced force is applied, connecting to Newton's second law.

VC2S8U15.w: We are learning that energy exists in different forms, can be transferred and transformed, and that we can analyse how efficiently energy moves through a system.
y: Energy transformation is one of the most fundamental concepts in physics — understanding it helps us explain everything from how our bodies use food to how power stations generate electricity.
Analysing energy efficiency helps us identify waste and find ways to use energy more sustainably in homes, industry and transport.
This understanding connects to global challenges like climate change, where improving energy efficiency is one of the most practical steps communities can take.
eg: Tracing the energy transformations in a coal power station: chemical energy → thermal energy → kinetic energy → electrical energy.
Calculating the efficiency of a light bulb by comparing the useful light energy output to the total electrical energy input.
Investigating how a pendulum transforms potential energy to kinetic energy and back, with some energy lost as heat due to friction.
Comparing the energy efficiency of LED bulbs, fluorescent tubes and incandescent bulbs.
Analysing energy transformations in the human body: chemical energy in food → thermal energy (body heat) + kinetic energy (movement).
Discussing why no energy transformation is 100% efficient — some energy is always 'lost' as heat, sound or light that we do not intend to use.

VC2S8U16.w: We are learning that household energy use can be measured through an energy audit and is affected by the appliances we choose, building design, season and climate.
y: Understanding our energy use helps us make practical decisions that reduce costs and environmental impact at home and school.
Energy auditing develops real-world numeracy — reading meters, calculating usage and comparing efficiency ratings are skills used in everyday life.
This topic connects personal choices to global sustainability, showing that individual actions contribute to broader environmental outcomes.
eg: Conducting an energy audit of our classroom by listing all electrical devices, their wattage and how long they are used each day.
Calculating the energy cost of running a heater for a month and comparing it to the cost of better insulation.
Comparing the energy star ratings of different appliances and discussing how choosing efficient models reduces long-term energy consumption.
Investigating how building orientation and window placement affect heating and cooling needs in different seasons.
Analysing a household electricity bill and identifying which appliances consume the most energy.
Researching how passive solar design principles — used in both modern architecture and traditional Aboriginal shelters — reduce the need for artificial heating and cooling.

VC2S8U17.w: We are learning that electrical circuits transfer energy when current flows, and they can be designed using different components for different purposes, described using voltage and current.
y: Understanding circuits gives us the knowledge to design, build and troubleshoot electrical systems — from simple torches to complex electronic devices.
Learning about voltage and current helps us understand electrical safety, why fuses blow and how different components control energy flow.
Electrical knowledge is foundational for careers in engineering, renewable energy, electronics, robotics and many trades.
eg: Building series and parallel circuits and measuring how voltage and current differ across each component.
Using a multimeter to measure voltage across a battery and current through a circuit, recording values in a table.
Investigating how adding resistors in series and parallel affects the total resistance and brightness of globes in a circuit.
Drawing circuit diagrams using standard symbols for batteries, switches, resistors, globes and ammeters.
Designing a circuit for a specific purpose — such as a burglar alarm — that includes a switch, buzzer and power source.
Discussing how household circuits use parallel wiring so that each device receives the full supply voltage and can operate independently.

VC2S8I01.w: We are learning to develop investigable questions, predictions and hypotheses that guide our investigations and help us identify patterns and test relationships.
y: A well-formed hypothesis gives an investigation clear direction and makes our results meaningful rather than random.
Learning to distinguish between a question that can be tested and one that cannot is a foundational scientific thinking skill we use in every subject.
This process of questioning and predicting mirrors how knowledge has always been built — through curiosity, observation and systematic testing across all cultures.
eg: Turning a broad curiosity about rust into a testable hypothesis: 'Iron will corrode faster when exposed to salt water than fresh water.'
Predicting that increasing the concentration of a solution will increase the rate of a chemical reaction, then designing an experiment to test it.
Formulating a question about ecosystems: 'Is there a relationship between plant diversity and insect population in our school grounds?'
Developing a hypothesis about forces: 'If the mass of an object increases, the force needed to accelerate it will also increase.'
Refining a vague question like 'Why do things float?' into 'Does the density of an object determine whether it floats in water?'
