Asking 'Is this shape a circle? Yes or no?'
Sorting objects into 'floats' and 'sinks'.
Recording whether each classmate walks or drives to school.

VC2MFDN01.w: We are learning to say, write and put numbers in order from 0 to at least 10.
y: Knowing numbers to 10 is a big step — it opens the door to all the maths ahead.
Writing numbers helps us record and share what we have counted.
Putting numbers in order shows us how they get bigger one step at a time.
eg: Counting from 0 to 10 out loud.
Writing the numbers 0 to 10 on a whiteboard.
Putting number cards in order from smallest to biggest.
Pointing to each number on a number line as we count.
Finding the missing number: 1, 2, ?, 4, 5.
Tracing numbers in sand or with finger paint.

VC2MFDN02.w: We are learning to quickly see how many things are in a small group, up to 4.
y: Subitising — seeing how many without counting — makes our number thinking faster.
This skill helps us recognise amounts quickly in games and everyday life.
Quickly seeing small numbers builds a strong foundation for bigger maths later.
eg: Looking at 3 dots and saying 'three' straight away.
Recognising 4 on a dice without counting each dot.
Seeing 2 birds and knowing it is two immediately.
Looking at 4 fingers held up and saying the number.
Playing a quick-flash card game where we say the number we see.
Seeing 3 items on a plate and knowing the amount at a glance.

VC2MFDN03.w: We are learning to count and compare groups up to 10, and explain how we know.
y: Counting up to 10 helps us work with numbers in everyday situations.
Comparing and explaining builds our confidence to think and talk about maths.
Being able to explain our thinking helps us check our own ideas.
eg: Counting 7 shells and 5 shells, then saying '7 is more than 5'.
Lining up two groups of blocks and comparing their lengths.
Saying 'I know 8 is more than 6 because 8 comes after 6 when we count'.
Counting both groups and using a number line to compare.
Explaining 'this group has fewer because it only reaches 4'.
Comparing the number of boys and girls in our class.

VC2MFDN04.w: We are learning to break apart and combine groups up to 10.
y: Seeing that 10 can be made from different pairs helps us add and subtract with confidence.
Breaking numbers into parts is one of the most useful strategies in maths.
Understanding how numbers fit together makes problem-solving much easier.
eg: Showing that 10 can be 5 and 5, or 7 and 3.
Breaking 8 counters into two groups and counting each part.
Using a ten-frame to see how 6 and 4 make 10.
Splitting 9 teddies into groups and recording the pairs.
Using two colours of cubes to show all the ways to make 7.
Putting 3 blocks and 4 blocks together to make 7.

VC2MFDN05.w: We are learning to use objects to solve adding and subtracting problems with numbers up to 10.
y: Using objects to solve problems makes the maths visible and hands-on.
This helps us understand what adding and subtracting really mean.
Solving real problems shows us that maths is useful in everyday life.
eg: Using counters to work out '5 + 3'.
Taking 2 blocks away from 8 and counting what is left.
Acting out: 'There were 6 birds, 2 flew away — how many are left?'
Using fingers to add 4 and 3.
Solving a sharing story: '10 lollies between 2 children — how many each?'
Using a ten-frame to show 7 take away 4.

VC2MFDN06.w: We are learning to share up to 10 things equally between people.
y: Fair sharing helps us learn about dividing things into equal groups.
This connects maths to real life — we share food, toys and materials every day.
Sharing equally builds our understanding of division and fractions.
eg: Sharing 10 counters equally between 2 children — 5 each.
Dealing out 9 cards among 3 friends — 3 each.
Sharing 8 strawberries between 4 people.
Checking that everyone has the same amount after sharing.
Solving: 'If we have 6 pencils for 2 tables, how many does each table get?'
Using objects to share 10 things between 5 people and counting each group.

VC2MFDA01.w: We are learning to spot, copy and continue repeating patterns.
y: Patterns help us predict what comes next — this is powerful thinking.
Finding patterns in the world helps us understand how things are organised.
Patterns are found in nature, music, art and maths — they connect everything.
eg: Continuing a bead pattern: red, yellow, red, yellow, red, ...?
Spotting the pattern in a clapping game and joining in.
Copying a pattern of shapes drawn by the teacher.
Making our own repeating pattern with stamps.
Finding the pattern in a row of tiles on the floor.
Predicting the next colour in a pattern and checking if we were right.

VC2MFDM01.w: We are learning to compare objects by how long, heavy or full they are.
y: Comparing helps us describe things and decide which is bigger, longer or heavier.
This is the start of learning to measure — a skill we use every day.
Using measurement words helps us talk clearly about what we notice.
eg: Placing two sticks end to end to see which is longer.
Holding a block in each hand to feel which is heavier.
Filling two cups with sand to see which holds more.
Comparing two pieces of string by stretching them out.
Saying 'this bag is heavier than that one'.
Checking which container is full and which is empty.

VC2MFDM02.w: We are learning to put daily events in order and connect them to morning, afternoon or night.
y: Putting events in order helps us understand how time moves through the day.
Connecting events to morning, afternoon and night helps us talk about when things happen.
Understanding daily rhythms helps us feel ready and know what to expect.
eg: Putting picture cards in order: breakfast, school, dinner, bed.
Saying 'we go to the playground in the afternoon'.
Drawing three pictures: what we do in the morning, afternoon and at night.
Telling a friend what we did first today and what we did next.
Sorting events: 'getting dressed' goes in the morning, 'reading a story' goes at bedtime.
Talking about what happens after lunch at school.

VC2MFDSP01.w: We are learning to sort and name shapes, and find shapes in things around us.
y: Finding shapes in the real world helps us see that maths is everywhere.
Naming shapes helps us describe and communicate about what we see.
Sorting shapes develops our ability to notice what is the same and what is different.
eg: Pointing to a door and saying 'that is a rectangle'.
Sorting shapes into groups: circles, triangles, squares.
Going on a shape hunt around the classroom.
Finding circles in wheels, plates and buttons.
Building a picture from shapes and naming each one.
Noticing that a slice of pizza looks like a triangle.

VC2MFDSP02.w: We are learning to describe where things are compared to ourselves or other objects.
y: Describing position helps us give and follow directions.
This builds our spatial language — words like above, below, beside, in front and behind.
Understanding where things are helps us navigate the world around us.
eg: Saying 'the book is on top of the desk'.
Describing a toy as 'behind the box'.
Following an instruction: 'stand in front of your chair'.
Playing a game: 'Put the teddy under the table'.
Telling a friend 'I am next to you'.
Drawing a picture and saying where each thing is.

VC2MFDST01.w: We are learning to sort and compare information that has two possible answers.
y: Sorting into two groups helps us organise information and see patterns.
This is our first step in learning about data — collecting and comparing answers.
Data helps us answer questions about our class and our world.
eg: Sorting our class into 'has a brother' and 'does not have a brother'.
Making a chart: 'Do you like bananas? Yes or no?'
Sorting objects into 'red' and 'not red'.
Comparing two groups and saying which has more.
Recording whether each child walked or was driven to school.
Counting how many said 'yes' and how many said 'no' and comparing.

VC2MFN01.w: We are learning to say, write and order numbers from 0 to at least 20.
y: Knowing numbers to 20 gives us a strong start for all the maths we will do at school.
Writing and ordering numbers helps us see how counting works and how numbers grow.
Numbers are used everywhere — at home, at the shop and in our games.
eg: Counting from 0 to 20 out loud without missing a number.
Writing numbers 0 to 20 in a number line.
Putting shuffled number cards from 0 to 20 in the right order.
Reading numbers we see around the room: calendars, books, posters.
Counting backwards from 20 to 0 during a game.
Finding the number that comes just before or just after a given number.

VC2MFN02.w: We are learning to look at a small group and quickly see how many, up to 5.
y: Seeing amounts quickly (subitising) makes counting faster and builds number sense.
This skill helps us recognise number patterns on dice, cards and fingers.
Quickly knowing small amounts is a foundation for mental maths.
eg: Looking at 5 dots arranged on a dice face and saying 'five'.
Seeing 4 fingers held up and naming the number instantly.
Recognising 3 items on a plate without counting one by one.
Playing a flash card game where we name the number of dots we see.
Seeing 5 tally marks and knowing it is five straight away.
Using ten-frames to quickly see amounts up to 5.

VC2MFN03.w: We are learning to count and compare groups up to 20, and explain our thinking.
y: Counting and comparing bigger groups helps us use numbers in the real world.
Explaining our thinking out loud helps us understand our own ideas better.
Comparing amounts is a skill we use when shopping, cooking and sharing.
eg: Counting 14 blocks and 18 blocks, then saying '18 is more because it comes after 14'.
Using a number line to compare 11 and 15.
Explaining 'I know 9 is less than 12 because I counted on from 9 to 12'.
Comparing two groups of counters and ordering them from fewest to most.
Saying 'these two groups are equal because they both have 10'.
Counting objects in two containers and explaining which has more and how we know.

VC2MFN04.w: We are learning to break numbers up to 10 into parts and see how those parts fit together.
y: Knowing the parts of numbers helps us add and subtract much more easily.
This is called 'part-part-whole' thinking and it is one of the biggest ideas in early maths.
When we know that 7 is 5 and 2, we can solve problems quickly without counting every object.
eg: Using two colours of cubes to show all the ways to make 8.
Showing that 10 = 6 + 4 and 10 = 7 + 3 using a ten-frame.
Breaking 9 into 5 and 4 using counters.
Recording part-part-whole combinations in a simple table.
Using a number bond diagram to show 6 = 2 + 4.
Playing a game: hiding some counters and working out how many are hidden.

VC2MFN05.w: We are learning to solve real-life adding and subtracting problems using objects and counting.
y: Solving real problems shows us that maths is useful — not just numbers on a page.
Using objects to solve problems helps us see what is happening and check our answers.
This builds our confidence to tackle problems we meet every day.
eg: Solving: 'We had 8 apples and ate 3. How many are left?'
Working out: 'There are 5 children and 4 more come. How many now?'
Using counters to find the answer to '6 + 7'.
Acting out: 'The bus had 10 people, 4 got off. How many are still on?'
Solving a sharing problem: 'If 12 stickers are shared between 3 children...'
Using a number line to solve '9 – 5'.

VC2MFN06.w: We are learning to share and group things equally using real objects.
y: Equal sharing and grouping are the foundation of multiplication and division.
These skills help us solve real problems — like sharing food or sorting materials.
Using real objects makes equal groups visible and easy to understand.
eg: Sharing 12 counters equally among 4 cups — 3 in each.
Grouping 15 pencils into bundles of 5.
Sharing 20 stickers equally between 5 children.
Making equal groups: 'How many groups of 2 can we make from 10?'
Using objects to solve: '18 biscuits shared between 6 plates'.
Putting 16 blocks into groups of 4 and counting the groups.

VC2MFA01.w: We are learning to follow instructions in order, and to spot, copy, continue and make repeating patterns.
y: Patterns are everywhere — finding them helps us make sense of the world around us.
Following steps in order helps us learn to plan and organise our thinking.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art is full of beautiful patterns that carry meaning and stories.
eg: Clapping a pattern: clap-clap-stomp, clap-clap-stomp.
Stringing beads in a colour pattern: red, blue, red, blue.
Noticing the pattern of day and night that keeps repeating.
Copying a pattern a friend made with blocks.
Continuing a pattern: circle, square, circle, square, ... what comes next?
Looking at Aboriginal dot paintings and finding the repeating patterns.

VC2MFM01.w: We are learning to compare how long, heavy or full things are and explain our thinking.
y: Explaining our thinking helps others understand us and helps us check our own ideas.
Comparing teaches us to use measurement words like 'longer than' and 'heavier than'.
Measurement is something we use every day — cooking, building and choosing the right container.
eg: Comparing two ribbons and saying 'this one is longer because it reaches past the table'.
Holding two bags and explaining 'this one is heavier because it pulls my arm down more'.
Filling two jugs with water and saying which holds more.
Using blocks to measure a table and a desk and comparing.
Saying 'I know the bucket is fuller because the water is near the top'.
Comparing two books by weight and explaining how we can tell.

VC2MFM02.w: We are learning the days of the week and times of day like morning, lunchtime and night.
y: Knowing the days of the week helps us understand how time is organised.
Connecting events to days and times helps us plan and remember.
Every culture organises time — days and routines help us share our lives with others.
eg: Saying the days of the week in order.
Knowing that Saturday and Sunday are the weekend.
Saying 'we have sport on Wednesday'.
Sorting events into morning, lunchtime, afternoon and night.
Singing a days-of-the-week song.
Talking about what day it is today and what day it will be tomorrow.

VC2MFSP01.w: We are learning to sort, name and make shapes, and find shapes in objects around us.
y: Finding shapes in real objects helps us see that geometry is part of our world.
Making shapes helps us understand their features — how many sides, corners and curves.
Shape knowledge is used in art, building, design and everyday problem-solving.
eg: Building a square from four sticks of the same length.
Finding the shapes in a house — rectangle door, square windows, triangle roof.
Sorting 3D objects into groups: things that roll and things that stack.
Using playdough to make spheres, cubes and cylinders.
Drawing circles, triangles and rectangles and talking about their features.
Going on a shape walk and photographing shapes we find.

VC2MFSP02.w: We are learning to describe where we are and where things are, compared to other people and objects.
y: Position words help us give and follow directions clearly.
This is the beginning of spatial thinking — understanding how objects relate to each other in space.
Describing position helps us in games, building, drawing and finding our way around.
eg: Saying 'I am standing between the table and the door'.
Following directions: 'Walk forward three steps, then turn left'.
Describing: 'The clock is above the whiteboard'.
Playing a treasure hunt using words like near, far, under, behind.
Giving a friend instructions to find a hidden object.
Drawing a map of our classroom showing where things are.

VC2MFST01.w: We are learning to collect, sort and compare information to answer simple questions.
y: Collecting and sorting information helps us answer questions about our world.
This is the beginning of understanding data — one of the most important skills in modern life.
Comparing results helps us see what the information is telling us.
eg: Asking 'What is our favourite fruit?' and recording everyone's answer.
Sorting leaves by colour and counting how many of each we found.
Making a picture graph of our class's favourite animals.
Collecting data about the weather each day for a week.
Comparing the groups and saying 'more people chose strawberry'.
Sorting shoes by type and making a chart to show what we found.

VC2M1N01.w: We are learning to read, write and put numbers in order up to at least 120.
y: Knowing numbers beyond 100 helps us count and work with bigger amounts.
Reading and writing numbers is a skill we use every day — in maths, money and measurement.
Ordering numbers shows us how the number system works and grows.
eg: Reading numbers on a hundred chart and extending past 100.
Writing numbers from 1 to 120.
Putting number cards from 95 to 115 in the right order.
Finding missing numbers in a sequence: 108, 109, ?, 111.
Reading numbers on price tags up to $120.
Counting a collection of objects beyond 100 and recording the number.

VC2M1N02.w: We are learning to break numbers into parts, like splitting a two-digit number into tens and ones.
y: Understanding tens and ones helps us see how our number system is built.
Breaking numbers into parts is the key to adding and subtracting bigger numbers.
Place value is one of the most important ideas in all of maths.
eg: Showing that 34 means 3 tens and 4 ones using bundles of sticks.
Breaking 56 into 50 and 6.
Using base-ten blocks to build a number and name its parts.
Explaining that 42 has 4 in the tens place and 2 in the ones place.
Playing a game where we trade 10 ones for 1 ten.
Writing a number in expanded form: 73 = 70 + 3.

VC2M1N03.w: We are learning to count large groups by breaking them into equal parts and skip counting.
y: Breaking groups into equal parts makes counting faster and more accurate.
This connects counting to multiplication — skip counting IS counting equal groups.
In real life, we often count large amounts by grouping — like counting coins or eggs.
eg: Grouping a jar of buttons into groups of 10 and skip counting to find the total.
Counting a collection of 50 shells by making groups of 5.
Skip counting by 2s to count a pile of shoes.
Arranging counters into rows of 10 and counting by tens.
Counting a large set of objects by 5s and keeping track on a number line.
Grouping pencils into bundles of 10 to count the class set.

VC2M1N04.w: We are learning to add and subtract numbers up to 20 using different strategies.
y: Having different strategies means we can choose the best way to solve each problem.
Adding and subtracting up to 20 fluently gives us confidence with bigger numbers later.
These skills help us in daily life — sharing, shopping and solving problems.
eg: Using 'counting on' to solve 8 + 5 by starting at 8 and counting 5 more.
Using 'doubles' to solve 7 + 7 = 14.
Using 'near doubles' to solve 6 + 7 by thinking '6 + 6 + 1'.
Using a number line to work out 15 – 8.
Making 10 first: solving 9 + 4 by thinking '9 + 1 + 3 = 13'.
Using fact families: knowing that 8 + 5 = 13 means 13 – 5 = 8.

VC2M1N05.w: We are learning to solve real-life adding problems, including simple money questions.
y: Solving real problems shows us that maths is useful outside the classroom.
Working with money connects maths to everyday shopping and saving.
Learning to solve word problems builds our thinking and reasoning skills.
eg: Working out the total cost of a $5 toy and a $3 book.
Solving: 'We had 12 oranges and picked 7 more. How many now?'
Adding coins: 'I have a 10c coin and two 5c coins. How much is that?'
Solving: 'There are 8 girls and 9 boys in our class. How many children?'
Working out how much change from 20c after buying something for 15c.
Solving a two-step problem: 'I had 10 stickers, got 5 more, then gave away 3.'

VC2M1N06.w: We are learning to solve sharing and grouping problems using objects and diagrams.
y: Sharing equally and making groups are the building blocks of multiplication and division.
Drawing diagrams helps us see the maths and explain our thinking to others.
These skills help us share fairly in real life — with food, toys and materials.
eg: Sharing 12 stickers equally among 3 friends and finding each gets 4.
Drawing circles to show groups: 'How many groups of 4 in 20?'
Using counters to solve: '15 shared between 5 plates.'
Drawing equal groups to show 3 groups of 4 makes 12.
Solving: 'We have 18 pencils for 6 tables. How many per table?'
Using arrays (rows and columns) to show equal groups.

VC2M1A01.w: We are learning to make and continue number patterns by skip counting — by 2s, 5s and 10s.
y: Skip counting helps us count quickly and is the beginning of learning multiplication.
Finding number patterns helps us predict what comes next, which is powerful thinking.
Skip counting is used in real life — counting coins, telling the time and organising groups.
eg: Counting by 5s using a hundred chart and colouring the pattern.
Skip counting by 10s to count a pile of 10-cent coins.
Continuing a pattern: 5, 10, 15, 20 — what comes next?
Counting pairs of socks by 2s to find the total.
Using a number line to show jumps of 10.

VC2M1A02.w: We are learning to find the repeating unit in a pattern and keep the pattern going.
y: Spotting the part that repeats helps us understand how the whole pattern works.
Pattern thinking is one of the most important skills in maths — it helps us make predictions.
Patterns are found everywhere — in music, nature, art and language.
eg: Finding the repeating part in: red, blue, red, blue — it is 'red, blue'.
Identifying that a clap-snap-clap-snap pattern repeats 'clap-snap'.
Continuing a shape pattern: circle, triangle, circle, triangle, ...?
Making our own repeating pattern and asking a friend to find the unit.
Finding the pattern in a row of tiles and predicting what comes next.
Building a repeating pattern with blocks and describing the repeating part.

VC2M1M01.w: We are learning to compare and order objects and events by length, weight, capacity and how long they take.
y: Comparing helps us describe the world — which is longer, heavier or holds more.
Ordering objects builds our understanding of measurement and size.
We compare and order things every day when cooking, building and choosing.
eg: Lining up three ribbons from shortest to longest.
Holding objects and ordering them from lightest to heaviest.
Ordering three containers by how much water they hold.
Comparing how long it takes to walk to the library versus the playground.
Ordering classmates by height from shortest to tallest.
Saying which activity takes longer — brushing teeth or eating lunch.

VC2M1M02.w: We are learning to measure how long things are using informal units like paper clips or blocks.
y: Using informal units teaches us the idea of measurement before we use rulers.
It helps us understand that we need to use the same size unit to measure fairly.
Measuring is a skill we use in building, cooking, art and science.
eg: Measuring a desk using hand spans and recording the number.
Lining up paper clips end to end to measure the length of a book.
Using blocks to measure how tall a chair is.
Comparing two objects by measuring them both with the same unit.
Discovering that using different-sized units gives different answers.
Recording our measurements: 'The table is 12 blocks long'.

VC2M1M03.w: We are learning to describe how long things take using words like days, weeks and months.
y: Understanding units of time helps us talk about when things happen and how long they last.
This helps us plan ahead — knowing that a week has 7 days helps us count to an event.
Time is part of every culture — all people measure and talk about time.
eg: Counting how many days until a class excursion.
Learning that there are 7 days in a week and about 4 weeks in a month.
Saying 'my birthday is in 2 months'.
Using a calendar to count the days between two events.
Talking about how long the school holidays last in weeks.
Sequencing the months of the year and finding our birthday month.

VC2M1SP01.w: We are learning to make, compare and sort shapes, and notice how they are the same or different.
y: Comparing shapes helps us notice features like sides, corners and curves.
This builds our ability to think about space and geometry.
Shape knowledge is used in art, building, design and everyday problem-solving.
eg: Comparing a triangle and a square — both have straight sides, but different numbers.
Sorting shapes into groups: 'shapes with curves' and 'shapes with straight sides'.
Making shapes using sticks and comparing them.
Folding paper to create shapes and describing what we made.
Finding the difference between a cube and a rectangular prism.
Building 3D shapes from nets and comparing their faces.

VC2M1SP02.w: We are learning to give and follow directions to move people or things to different places.
y: Giving clear directions helps others understand where to go.
Following directions builds our spatial awareness and listening skills.
We give and follow directions every day — finding places, playing games and helping others.
eg: Giving a friend directions to walk from one end of the classroom to the other.
Following instructions: 'Go forward 5 steps, turn right, go 3 steps.'
Directing a friend through an obstacle course using words.
Drawing a simple map and giving directions to follow it.
Programming a bee-bot or similar toy to reach a target on a mat.
Playing a blindfold game where one person gives directions.

VC2M1ST01.w: We are learning to collect and record information using drawings, lists, tally marks and digital tools.
y: Recording data helps us remember what we found out and share it with others.
Using tally marks and lists is a neat way to keep track of what we count.
These are the first steps in learning how to work with data.
eg: Making tally marks to record how many of each colour car passes the school.
Drawing pictures to record the types of birds we saw at lunchtime.
Using a list to record everyone's favourite animal.
Recording the weather each day using symbols.
Counting and recording the types of trees in the school grounds.
Using a tablet to photograph and record what we see on a nature walk.

VC2M1ST02.w: We are learning to show our data in a chart where one picture stands for one thing, and talk about what we see.
y: Picture graphs help us compare information at a glance.
Talking about what our data shows builds our reasoning and communication skills.
Data is everywhere — learning to read and make simple charts is an important life skill.
eg: Making a picture graph of favourite fruits, with one picture for each vote.
Looking at a chart and saying 'more people chose dogs than cats'.
Creating a chart of how we travel to school — walking, car, bus, bike.
Answering questions about a picture graph: 'How many more people chose red?'
Comparing two columns in a graph and discussing the difference.
Drawing a picture graph showing the number of sunny, cloudy and rainy days this week.

VC2M2N01.w: We are learning to read, write and order numbers up to at least 1000.
y: Working with bigger numbers helps us count, measure and understand the world around us.
Ordering numbers to 1000 builds our understanding of how the number system grows.
We see numbers over 100 every day — in prices, distances, scores and population counts.
eg: Reading the number 347 and saying 'three hundred and forty-seven'.
Writing numbers to 1000 in words and numerals.
Ordering numbers like 256, 518, 103 from smallest to largest.
Placing numbers on a number line up to 1000.
Counting forwards and backwards by 1s, 10s and 100s from any starting point.
Comparing numbers: 'Is 489 bigger or smaller than 502?'

VC2M2N02.w: We are learning to break two- and three-digit numbers into hundreds, tens and ones, and regroup them.
y: Place value is how our number system works — each digit's position tells us its value.
Regrouping (trading tens for ones or hundreds for tens) is essential for adding and subtracting bigger numbers.
Understanding place value helps us work with money, measurement and large numbers.
eg: Showing 365 as 3 hundreds, 6 tens and 5 ones using base-ten blocks.
Regrouping: 2 hundreds and 14 tens is the same as 3 hundreds and 4 tens.
Writing 248 in expanded form: 200 + 40 + 8.
Trading 10 ones for 1 ten when adding numbers together.
Explaining why the 5 in 53 is worth 50, not 5.
Using a place value chart to show how digits move when we multiply by 10.

VC2M2N03.w: We are learning about halves, quarters and eighths by cutting things into equal parts.
y: Fractions describe parts of a whole — they are everywhere in our daily lives.
Cutting into equal parts teaches us that fractions must be fair and even.
This understanding prepares us for more complex fraction work in later years.
eg: Cutting a piece of paper into 2 equal halves.
Folding a strip into 4 equal parts and labelling each quarter.
Sharing a cake equally among 8 people — each person gets one eighth.
Recognising that two quarters make one half.
Drawing a circle and dividing it into halves, then quarters.
Cutting a ribbon into eighths and counting how many pieces we have.

VC2M2N04.w: We are learning to add and subtract one- and two-digit numbers using different strategies.
y: Using different strategies helps us choose the most efficient way to solve each problem.
This builds our flexibility with numbers — an important part of being a confident mathematician.
Adding and subtracting are skills we use constantly in everyday life.
eg: Using a number line to add 38 + 25 by jumping in tens and ones.
Splitting numbers to add: 47 + 36 = 47 + 30 + 6 = 83.
Subtracting by counting back: 63 – 28 by jumping back 20, then 8.
Using the relationship between addition and subtraction to check answers.
Solving: 'I had 54 stickers and gave 19 away. How many left?'
Using place value to add: 45 + 37 = 40 + 30 + 5 + 7 = 82.

VC2M2N05.w: We are learning to multiply and divide using repeated addition, equal groups and arrays.
y: Arrays (rows and columns) help us see multiplication as a picture, making it easier to understand.
Connecting multiplication to repeated addition shows us what the operation really means.
These strategies build a strong foundation for learning times tables.
eg: Drawing an array of 3 rows and 4 columns to show 3 × 4 = 12.
Using repeated addition: 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 20, so 4 × 5 = 20.
Sharing 24 counters equally into 4 groups to find 24 ÷ 4 = 6.
Using an array to show that 3 × 5 = 5 × 3.
Solving: 'There are 6 bags with 3 apples each. How many apples?'
Making groups of 2 from 16 objects and counting how many groups.

VC2M2N06.w: We are learning to use maths to solve real-life problems, including those with money.
y: Solving real problems shows us why maths matters — it helps us make decisions every day.
Working with money connects our number skills to something we all use.
This builds our ability to think through multi-step problems.
eg: Working out the total cost of 3 items at a school canteen.
Solving: 'I had $20, spent $13. How much change?'
Calculating how many $2 coins make $10.
Working out how to share $15 equally among 3 people.
Solving a two-step problem: 'Buy 2 books at $8 each. How much from $20 is left?'
Deciding which combination of coins makes 50 cents.

VC2M2A01.w: We are learning to describe and create number patterns that go up or down by the same amount each time.
y: Growing and shrinking patterns help us see how numbers change in regular, predictable ways.
This is the beginning of learning about rules and relationships between numbers.
Pattern-finding is used in music, science, coding and many areas of maths.
eg: Creating a pattern that goes up by 3: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15...
Describing a pattern: 'It goes down by 2 each time: 20, 18, 16, 14...'
Using a hundred chart to colour a pattern and see what shape it makes.
Making a decreasing pattern: 50, 45, 40, 35...
Finding the rule in a number pattern and predicting the next three numbers.
Creating our own pattern, writing the rule and sharing with a partner.

VC2M2A02.w: We are learning our addition facts to 20 really well, and using them to work out subtraction facts.
y: Knowing addition facts by heart makes solving problems much faster.
Understanding that addition and subtraction are connected helps us use what we know to find new answers.
Fluent number facts are the building blocks for harder maths later on.
eg: Knowing straight away that 8 + 7 = 15.
Using 8 + 7 = 15 to work out that 15 – 7 = 8.
Practising number facts with flash cards or a partner.
Playing a game where we match addition and subtraction fact families.
Using known doubles (like 8 + 8 = 16) to solve near-doubles (8 + 9 = 17).

VC2M2A03.w: We are learning our 2 times table really well, and using doubling and halving to divide too.
y: The 2 times table is one of the first we learn — it helps us think about equal groups and pairs.
Doubling and halving are strategies we use in everyday life, like sharing food equally.
Knowing one operation well (like doubling) helps us work out the opposite (halving).
eg: Reciting the 2 times table: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12...
Doubling a number: double 7 is 14.
Halving a number: half of 14 is 7.
Using doubles to solve 2 × 9 = 18, so 18 ÷ 2 = 9.
Sharing 16 stickers between 2 people by halving.
Using doubling to check skip-counting by 2s.

VC2M2A04.w: We are learning that multiplication is the same as adding equal groups, and division is the same as sharing into equal groups.
y: Understanding what multiplication and division really mean helps us solve problems, not just memorise answers.
Connecting operations to real actions — like grouping and sharing — makes maths meaningful.
This understanding is the foundation for all the multiplication and division work we will do.
eg: Showing that 3 × 4 means 3 groups of 4: 4 + 4 + 4 = 12.
Using counters to show that 12 ÷ 3 means sharing 12 into 3 equal groups.
Drawing an array of 4 rows and 5 columns to show 4 × 5 = 20.
Acting out: '4 friends each bring 3 cupcakes. How many cupcakes altogether?'
Solving: '20 stickers shared equally among 4 children' using division.
Showing that 5 × 2 and 2 × 5 both give 10 — multiplication can be done in any order.

VC2M2M01.w: We are learning to measure and compare length, capacity and mass using the same kind of unit each time.
y: Using the same unit each time makes our measurements fair and accurate.
This teaches us the important idea of standard measurement.
Measuring is a life skill — we use it in cooking, building, sport and science.
eg: Measuring a desk in hand spans and recording the number.
Using cups to measure how much water a container holds.
Comparing the mass of objects using a balance scale and uniform blocks.
Discovering that we get different answers if we use different-sized units.
Measuring the length of the classroom in footsteps, everyone using the same shoe.
Using a standard container to compare how much sand different buckets hold.

VC2M2M02.w: We are learning to recognise and use halves, quarters and eighths in shapes, objects and events.
y: Fractions help us describe parts of things — half a pizza, a quarter of an hour.
Understanding equal parts is the foundation of all fraction work.
We use fractions every day — sharing food, telling time and measuring.
eg: Folding a piece of paper in half and seeing two equal parts.
Cutting a sandwich into quarters and counting 4 equal pieces.
Folding a strip of paper into eighths.
Saying 'half past 2' when reading a clock.
Sharing a pizza equally among 4 people — each gets a quarter.
Colouring half, a quarter or an eighth of a shape.

VC2M2M03.w: We are learning to read a calendar and work out how many days are between events.
y: Reading a calendar helps us plan ahead and understand how time is organised.
Counting days between events builds our understanding of duration.
Calendars are tools we use every day — for school, family events and holidays.
eg: Looking at a calendar and finding today's date.
Counting how many days until a school excursion.
Working out how many days are in each month.
Finding the day of the week for a friend's birthday.
Counting how many school days are left in the term.
Using a calendar to plan a class event and mark important dates.

VC2M2M04.w: We are learning to read an analog clock to the hour, half hour and quarter hour.
y: Telling time on a clock helps us manage our day and be on time.
Understanding clocks builds our knowledge of fractions — half past, quarter past, quarter to.
Telling time is a skill we use every single day.
eg: Reading a clock that shows 3 o'clock.
Showing half past 10 on a clock with moving hands.
Reading quarter past 2 on an analog clock.
Drawing the hands on a clock to show quarter to 5.
Matching analog clock faces with digital times.
Talking about what time different activities happen during the school day.

VC2M2M05.w: We are learning about turns — quarter turns, half turns, three-quarter turns and full turns.
y: Understanding turns helps us with directions, angles and how things rotate.
Turns are used in dance, sport, maps and everyday movement.
This is the beginning of learning about angles in geometry.
eg: Standing up and making a quarter turn to face a different wall.
Making a half turn and seeing that we face the opposite direction.
Turning a full turn and ending up facing the same way we started.
Using a clock to show a quarter turn — from 12 to 3.
Following directions: 'Make a quarter turn to the right, then walk forward.'
Drawing arrows to show quarter, half and full turns.

VC2M2SP01.w: We are learning to compare and sort shapes by their sides, using words like 'parallel', 'curved' and 'straight'.
y: Using shape words helps us describe and compare what we see clearly.
Learning about sides and edges builds the foundation for geometry.
Shapes are part of everything around us — buildings, art, nature and design.
eg: Sorting shapes into those with curved sides and those with straight sides.
Comparing a square and a rectangle — both have parallel sides and right angles.
Finding shapes with parallel sides and shapes without.
Describing a triangle: 'It has 3 straight sides and 3 corners.'
Sorting 3D objects by how many flat faces they have.
Identifying shapes in real objects and describing their features.

VC2M2SP02.w: We are learning to find positions on a simple map and follow directions to move.
y: Maps help us understand where things are and how to get from one place to another.
Following directions on a map builds our spatial thinking and reasoning.
Map skills are useful in everyday life — navigating, travelling and understanding our world.
eg: Finding a location on a simple grid map using coordinates like A3.
Following directions on a treasure map: 'Go 3 squares east, then 2 squares north.'
Describing the path from the classroom to the library on a school map.
Placing objects on a grid map at given positions.
Drawing a simple map of our classroom and labelling key features.
Using compass directions (north, south, east, west) to describe movement.

VC2M2ST01.w: We are learning to collect data through surveys and experiments, and display it in lists and tables.
y: Collecting data ourselves teaches us how to ask good questions and record carefully.
Lists and tables help us organise information so it is easy to read and use.
Data collection is used in science, sport, business and everyday life.
eg: Surveying our class about favourite sports and recording the results in a tally chart.
Collecting data about the types of vehicles in the school car park.
Recording the temperature each day for a week in a table.
Making a list of the different colours of cars we see from the window.
Conducting an experiment to see which paper plane design flies furthest.
Creating a table showing how many of each type of pet our classmates have.

VC2M2ST02.w: We are learning to make different types of graphs from our data and compare what they show.
y: Different graphs suit different data — learning to choose the right one is an important skill.
Comparing graphs helps us see information from different angles.
Reading and making graphs is a skill we use in school, work and everyday life.
eg: Making a bar graph and a picture graph from the same set of data and comparing them.
Creating a bar graph showing the most popular lunch choices in our class.
Reading a graph and answering questions: 'Which was the most popular?'
Comparing a tally chart with a bar graph and discussing which is easier to read.
Making a column graph showing how many students are in each year level.
Discussing which type of graph best shows our data and explaining why.

VC2M3N01.w: We are learning about odd and even numbers and how to use their properties.
y: Knowing odd and even helps us with grouping, sharing equally and recognising number patterns.
Even numbers can always be split into two equal groups — odd numbers cannot — this is a useful idea.
Odd and even patterns are found throughout mathematics and help us make predictions.
eg: Sorting numbers into odd and even: 14 is even, 23 is odd.
Explaining that even numbers end in 0, 2, 4, 6 or 8.
Discovering that adding two even numbers always gives an even answer.
Sharing 15 counters between 2 people and noticing there is always 1 left over (odd).
Colouring a hundred chart to show the pattern of odd and even numbers.
Predicting whether the answer to an addition will be odd or even.

VC2M3N02.w: We are learning to read, write and order numbers beyond 10,000.
y: Working with bigger numbers helps us understand place value and how our number system extends.
We see large numbers in real life — populations, distances, prices — so reading them matters.
Ordering large numbers builds our comparison skills and number sense.
eg: Reading the number 12,450 and writing it in words.
Ordering numbers like 8,320 and 9,105 and 7,896 from smallest to largest.
Placing large numbers on a number line.
Comparing two numbers: 'Which is larger — 15,200 or 14,800?'
Writing numbers in expanded form: 23,456 = 20,000 + 3,000 + 400 + 50 + 6.
Rounding numbers to the nearest thousand.

VC2M3N03.w: We are learning about unit fractions like ½, ⅓, ¼, ⅕ and 1/10, and how parts combine to make a whole.
y: Fractions describe parts of things — understanding them helps us share, measure and compare.
Knowing that fractions are equal parts of a whole prevents common mistakes.
Fractions are used everywhere — in cooking, time, money and measurement.
eg: Folding a strip of paper into 5 equal parts and labelling each as one fifth.
Showing that 4 quarters make 1 whole.
Comparing: 'Is one third bigger or smaller than one half?'
Cutting a cake into 10 equal slices — each slice is one tenth.
Placing fractions on a number line between 0 and 1.
Showing that 3 thirds = 1 whole and 2 halves = 1 whole.

VC2M3N04.w: We are learning to add and subtract two- and three-digit numbers using place value to help.
y: Using place value strategies makes adding and subtracting bigger numbers manageable.
Having more than one strategy means we can choose the best one for each problem.
These skills are needed for money calculations, measurement problems and everyday situations.
eg: Adding 347 + 285 by first adding hundreds, then tens, then ones.
Subtracting 503 – 278 using a written method with regrouping.
Using a number line to add 168 + 240 by jumping in hundreds and tens.
Solving: 'We collected 456 cans last month and 389 this month. How many altogether?'
Checking our subtraction by adding the answer to the smaller number.
Estimating first by rounding, then calculating the exact answer.

VC2M3N05.w: We are learning to multiply and divide using number sentences, diagrams and arrays.
y: Seeing multiplication and division in different ways helps us understand what they really mean.
Arrays are a powerful tool — they show us how multiplication connects to area and equal groups.
These skills prepare us for more advanced maths and real-world problem-solving.
eg: Writing: 4 × 6 = 24 and drawing an array to match.
Solving 28 ÷ 4 by thinking 'how many groups of 4 make 28?'
Drawing a diagram to show 5 groups of 3.
Using an array to show that 3 × 7 = 7 × 3.
Solving: 'How many rows of 6 chairs can we make from 30 chairs?'
Writing a division number sentence from a sharing problem.

VC2M3N06.w: We are learning to estimate how many things are in a collection and check if our answers make sense.
y: Estimating helps us get a quick, rough answer before we calculate the exact one.
Checking if answers make sense is one of the most important habits in maths.
Estimation is a skill we use every day — when shopping, cooking and planning.
eg: Looking at a jar of marbles and estimating there are about 50.
Checking: '234 + 198 should be about 430 — is our answer close?'
Estimating the number of people in a photo by grouping and counting.
Rounding to estimate: 48 × 5 is about 50 × 5 = 250.
Judging whether an answer of 12,000 makes sense for 30 × 40.
Estimating the total cost of several items before adding them up.

VC2M3N07.w: We are learning how dollars and cents are related, and how to show money amounts in different ways.
y: Understanding money is an essential life skill that helps us shop, save and budget.
Knowing that 100 cents make $1 connects to our understanding of place value and decimals.
Being able to show the same amount in different ways builds flexible thinking.
eg: Knowing that $3.50 means 3 dollars and 50 cents.
Showing $2.75 using different combinations of coins.
Working out the total when buying items: $4.20 + $3.80.
Converting: 350 cents = $3.50.
Giving change from $10 when something costs $6.35.
Writing money amounts correctly using the dollar sign and decimal point.

VC2M3N08.w: We are learning to solve real-world maths problems by choosing the right operation and strategy.
y: Choosing the right operation shows we understand the problem, not just the numbers.
Real-world problems are what maths is for — they connect our skills to life outside school.
Learning to select strategies builds independence and problem-solving confidence.
eg: Deciding whether a problem needs addition, subtraction, multiplication or division.
Solving: 'There are 4 teams with 7 players each. How many players altogether?'
Working out: 'We have $50 and each book costs $8. How many can we buy?'
Solving a two-step problem: 'Buy 3 drinks at $2 each and a sandwich for $5. Total?'
Choosing to draw a diagram when the problem involves equal groups.
Checking our answer by estimating first and comparing.

VC2M3N09.w: We are learning to follow and create step-by-step instructions (algorithms) to investigate number patterns.
y: Following steps carefully helps us get accurate results and builds logical thinking.
Creating our own algorithms is the beginning of computational thinking — a skill used in coding and problem-solving.
Algorithms help us break big problems into smaller, manageable steps.
eg: Following steps: 'Start at 2, add 3 each time — what pattern do you see?'
Creating an algorithm to test if a number is odd or even.
Writing step-by-step instructions for someone to skip count by 4.
Using a flowchart to decide which operation to use in a problem.
Following an algorithm to sort numbers from smallest to largest.
Creating instructions that another student can follow to make a number pattern.

VC2M3A01.w: We are learning that addition and subtraction are opposites, and we can use this to find missing numbers.
y: Knowing that addition and subtraction undo each other helps us solve equations and check our work.
This understanding makes us more flexible problem solvers — we can work forwards and backwards.
Seeing connections between operations is the beginning of algebraic thinking.
eg: Solving 15 + ? = 23 by thinking '23 – 15 = 8'.
Checking 47 + 36 = 83 by working backwards: 83 – 36 = 47.
Finding the missing number: ? – 12 = 25.
Using a number line to show that adding 8 and subtracting 8 get us back to where we started.
Solving a word problem by deciding whether to add or subtract.
Writing a fact family for 7, 5 and 12.

VC2M3A02.w: We are learning to use addition and subtraction facts to 20 to work with bigger numbers in our heads.
y: When we know small number facts really well, we can use them to solve much bigger problems.
Mental maths saves time and builds confidence with numbers.
Strong number facts are the foundation for all the maths we do — they make everything easier.
eg: Using 7 + 8 = 15 to work out 70 + 80 = 150.
Adding 45 + 9 mentally by thinking '45 + 10 – 1 = 54'.
Using doubles: if 8 + 8 = 16, then 80 + 80 = 160.
Subtracting 99 by taking away 100 and adding 1 back.
Solving 250 + 300 by thinking '25 + 30 = 55, so 550'.

VC2M3A03.w: We are learning our 3, 4, 5 and 10 times tables, and using them for division too.
y: Times tables are one of the most useful things we learn in primary school — they speed up all our maths.
Knowing multiplication facts helps us solve division problems automatically.
These facts are used in real life for shopping, cooking, sharing and measuring.
eg: Reciting the 4 times table: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20...
Using 5 × 6 = 30 to work out 30 ÷ 5 = 6.
Solving a problem: 'How many weeks in 28 days?' using the 7 times table isn't needed — use 4 × 7 = 28.
Playing a times table game to build speed and confidence.
Using the 10 times table to estimate and check answers.
Solving: 'If 3 bags have 8 apples each, how many apples in total?'

VC2M3M01.w: We are learning which metric units we use for measuring everyday things, and how to estimate measurements.
y: Knowing which unit to use — millimetres, centimetres, metres, grams, kilograms, litres — helps us communicate clearly about size.
Estimating before measuring builds our number sense and helps us check if our answers are reasonable.
Metric units are used around the world, making measurement a shared language.
eg: Estimating that a desk is about 1 metre long, then measuring to check.
Choosing to measure a pencil in centimetres, not metres.
Estimating the mass of a school bag in kilograms.
Knowing that we measure water in millilitres or litres.
Choosing the right unit: a swimming pool holds litres, a cup holds millilitres.
Estimating the length of the classroom and comparing our estimate with the actual measurement.

VC2M3M02.w: We are learning to measure and compare length, mass and capacity using standard metric units.
y: Using standard units means everyone gets the same answer — it makes our measurements reliable.
Comparing measurements helps us make decisions and solve problems.
These practical skills are used by builders, cooks, scientists and many other people every day.
eg: Measuring the length of the playground in metres using a tape measure.
Comparing the mass of two objects using a kitchen scale in grams.
Measuring how much water a jug holds in millilitres.
Recording and comparing the heights of class plants over several weeks.
Measuring ingredients for a recipe in grams and millilitres.
Comparing the capacity of different containers by measuring each in litres.

VC2M3M03.w: We are learning how days, hours, minutes and seconds are related, and using them to compare how long things take.
y: Understanding time units and their relationships helps us manage our time and plan activities.
Comparing durations helps us solve problems like 'How long until lunch?' or 'Which activity takes longer?'
Time is one of the most practical types of measurement we use every day.
eg: Knowing there are 60 minutes in an hour and 60 seconds in a minute.
Converting: 'How many minutes in 2 hours?' — 120 minutes.
Comparing: 'Reading took 25 minutes, maths took 40 minutes — which was longer?'
Timing how long it takes to run around the oval and recording in minutes and seconds.
Working out how many hours are in a school day.
Solving: 'If the movie starts at 2:00 pm and goes for 90 minutes, what time does it end?'

VC2M3M04.w: We are learning to read both analog and digital clocks to the nearest minute.
y: Reading clocks accurately helps us be on time and manage our day.
Understanding both analog and digital clocks means we can read any clock we see.
Telling time connects our number skills to real-world situations.
eg: Reading an analog clock that shows 10:47.
Matching an analog time to its digital display.
Writing the time shown on a digital clock: 3:15 pm.
Drawing clock hands to show a given time.
Working out how many minutes until the next hour.
Recording the time at the start and end of an activity and working out how long it took.

VC2M3M05.w: We are learning that angles measure how much something has turned, and we can compare them to right angles.
y: Understanding angles helps us describe turns, corners and directions precisely.
Right angles are everywhere — in doors, books, windows and buildings — so they make a great reference point.
Angle knowledge is used in construction, design, navigation and sport.
eg: Identifying right angles in the classroom — corners of desks, books and windows.
Comparing angles to a right angle and saying 'bigger than', 'smaller than' or 'equal to'.
Making a right-angle tester from folded paper.
Turning a quarter turn and recognising it as a right angle.
Finding angles in shapes and saying whether they are right angles or not.
Sorting angles into groups: less than a right angle, right angle, more than a right angle.

VC2M3SP01.w: We are learning to compare objects, identify key features and explain why they are suited to their purpose.
y: Understanding shape features helps us explain why things are designed the way they are.
Comparing objects develops our ability to notice details and think critically.
This connects maths to the real world — designers choose shapes and structures for a reason.
eg: Comparing a sphere and a cylinder and explaining why a ball rolls but a can stacks.
Identifying that a triangular roof beam is strong because triangles are rigid shapes.
Explaining why a box is a rectangular prism — it stacks easily and holds things efficiently.
Comparing a cone and a pyramid by counting faces, edges and vertices.
Discussing why wheels are circular — they roll smoothly.
Describing the features of a hexagonal shape found in a honeycomb.

VC2M3SP02.w: We are learning to make and read simple maps showing key landmarks in their correct positions.
y: Making maps helps us represent real places and share information about where things are.
Reading maps builds our spatial understanding and navigation skills.
Maps are used everywhere — in travel, emergency planning, history and environmental science.
eg: Drawing a map of our school showing the library, playground, classrooms and office.
Reading a map to find the shortest path between two locations.
Placing landmarks on a grid map at correct positions.
Using compass directions to describe where things are on a map.
Making a treasure map with a key explaining the symbols.
Comparing our map with a partner's and discussing differences.

VC2M3ST01.w: We are learning to collect data to answer a question, and record it in tables and spreadsheets.
y: Collecting our own data teaches us to plan carefully and record accurately.
Tables and spreadsheets help us organise information so we can analyse it.
Data collection is used in science, health, sport, business and many other fields.
eg: Surveying classmates about their favourite fruit and recording in a table.
Collecting data about the types of birds in the school grounds over a week.
Entering data into a simple spreadsheet and sorting it.
Recording the temperature at the same time each day for two weeks.
Counting the types of vehicles that pass the school in 10 minutes.
Creating a data table with clear headings and categories.

VC2M3ST02.w: We are learning to make different types of graphs and compare what they show.
y: Different graphs tell different stories — choosing the right one helps communicate our findings clearly.
Comparing displays helps us see which one best shows the patterns in our data.
Graph literacy is an essential skill for reading news, reports and research.
eg: Making a bar graph and a pictograph from the same data and comparing them.
Creating a column graph showing how many students chose each sport.
Comparing two different graphs and saying which is easier to read.
Discussing why a pie chart might not be the best choice for this data.
Reading a graph and answering questions about the data it shows.
Making a dot plot to show the number of siblings in our class.

VC2M3ST03.w: We are learning to do a full investigation — ask a question, collect data, show it and explain what we found.
y: Planning a full investigation builds independence and connects all our data skills together.
This is how real researchers work — they ask, investigate, display and explain.
Learning to draw conclusions from data is one of the most important skills we develop in school.
eg: Investigating: 'What is the most common eye colour in our class?'
Planning what data to collect, how to collect it and how to display it.
Drawing conclusions: 'Brown eyes are the most common because 12 out of 25 students have brown eyes.'
Presenting our investigation findings to the class using a poster and graph.
Reflecting on whether our question was a good one and what we would change next time.
Comparing our findings with another class and discussing the differences.

VC2M3P01.w: We are learning to describe everyday events as 'likely', 'unlikely', 'certain' or 'impossible'.
y: Understanding chance helps us make predictions about what might happen in everyday situations.
Using probability words builds our ability to describe uncertainty precisely.
Chance and probability are used in weather forecasting, games, sport and decision-making.
eg: Saying 'It is certain that the sun will rise tomorrow.'
Describing: 'It is unlikely to snow in Melbourne in summer.'
Sorting events: 'Rolling a 7 on a normal dice is impossible.'
Ordering events from impossible to certain on a number line.
Discussing: 'Is it likely or unlikely we will have a fire drill today?'
Describing: 'Getting heads when flipping a coin is equally likely as tails.'

VC2M3P02.w: We are learning to do chance experiments, record results and notice that results can vary.
y: Doing experiments ourselves helps us see that chance events are unpredictable in the short term.
Recording results builds our data collection skills and shows us that patterns emerge over many trials.
Understanding variation is important — it prevents us from jumping to conclusions from small samples.
eg: Flipping a coin 20 times and recording the number of heads and tails.
Rolling a dice 30 times and graphing how often each number comes up.
Comparing our results with another group's and noticing the differences.
Spinning a spinner 50 times and recording the outcomes in a tally chart.
Discussing why we did not get exactly equal results even though the outcomes are equally likely.
Predicting what will happen, doing the experiment, then comparing our prediction with the actual result.

VC2M4N01.w: We are learning about tenths and hundredths, and how to read and write decimal numbers.
y: Decimals are another way to write fractions — understanding them helps us work with money, measurement and data.
Place value extends beyond whole numbers — tenths and hundredths work the same way as tens and hundreds, just on the other side of the decimal point.
Decimal understanding is essential for measurement, science, money and many real-world calculations.
eg: Writing 3 tenths as 0.3 and reading it correctly.
Showing 0.45 on a hundredths grid.
Reading $4.75 as 4 dollars and 75 hundredths of a dollar.
Placing decimals like 0.3, 0.7 and 0.5 on a number line.
Connecting fractions and decimals: ½ = 0.5, ¼ = 0.25.
Ordering decimals from smallest to largest: 0.2, 0.15, 0.8.

VC2M4N02.w: We are learning the patterns in multiples of 3, 4, 6, 7, 8 and 9.
y: Recognising patterns in multiples helps us recall and predict multiplication facts.
Patterns make large calculations easier — they give us shortcuts and checking strategies.
Pattern recognition is a core mathematical skill used in algebra, coding and problem-solving.
eg: Noticing that multiples of 9 have digit sums that equal 9 (18: 1+8=9, 27: 2+7=9).
Colouring multiples of 6 on a hundred chart and describing the pattern.
Using the pattern that multiples of 4 are always even.
Finding that multiples of 3 have digits that add up to a multiple of 3.
Discovering that multiples of 8 are double the multiples of 4.
Using skip counting patterns to quickly find whether a number is a multiple.

VC2M4N03.w: We are learning to find equivalent fractions and connect fractions to decimals.
y: Equivalent fractions show us that the same amount can be written in different ways.
Connecting fractions and decimals helps us move between two systems for describing parts.
These connections make comparing, adding and working with fractions much easier.
eg: Showing that ½ = 2/4 = 4/8 using fraction strips.
Converting ½ to 0.5 and ¼ to 0.25.
Finding equivalent fractions by multiplying the numerator and denominator by the same number.
Placing fractions and their decimal equivalents on the same number line.
Using a fraction wall to find fractions that are equal.
Explaining why 3/6 is the same amount as 1/2.

VC2M4N04.w: We are learning to count in quarters, halves and thirds, including mixed numbers, and place them on a number line.
y: Counting in fractions shows us that numbers exist between whole numbers.
Understanding mixed numbers (like 2½) helps us work with real quantities in cooking, measurement and time.
Number lines make fractions visual and help us compare and order them.
eg: Counting: 0, ½, 1, 1½, 2, 2½, 3.
Placing 1¼, 1½, 1¾ and 2 on a number line.
Counting by thirds: 0, ⅓, ⅔, 1, 1⅓, 1⅔, 2.
Identifying where 2¾ belongs on a number line.
Writing 5 quarters as the mixed number 1¼.
Ordering fractions and mixed numbers from smallest to largest.

VC2M4N05.w: We are learning to multiply and divide by 10, 100 and 1000 by understanding how digits shift in place value.
y: This pattern is one of the most useful in maths — digits move left when multiplying and right when dividing.
Understanding why this works (not just how) builds deep place value knowledge.
Multiplying and dividing by powers of 10 is used in metric conversions, money and scientific notation.
eg: Seeing that 34 × 10 = 340 — the digits moved one place to the left.
Dividing 5600 by 100 to get 56.
Explaining why multiplying by 10 makes a number ten times bigger.
Converting between millimetres and centimetres by dividing by 10.
Multiplying 2.5 by 100 to get 250.
Using this pattern to convert between metric units: 3 km = 3000 m.

VC2M4N06.w: We are learning efficient ways to add, subtract, multiply and divide, both in our heads and on paper.
y: Having efficient strategies saves time and reduces mistakes.
Being able to calculate mentally gives us independence — we do not always need a calculator.
Written methods give us a reliable process for solving harder problems step by step.
eg: Using the split strategy to add 456 + 278 mentally.
Using the traditional written method to subtract 803 – 467.
Multiplying 23 × 4 by breaking it into (20 × 4) + (3 × 4).
Using short division to solve 96 ÷ 8.
Estimating the answer first, then calculating to check.
Choosing the best strategy — mental, written or a combination — for each problem.

VC2M4N07.w: We are learning to use estimation and rounding to check if our answers make sense.
y: Estimation is our safety net — it helps us catch mistakes before they become problems.
Rounding to the nearest 10, 100 or 1000 makes calculations easier to do in our heads.
In real life, we often need a rough answer quickly — estimation gives us that.
eg: Rounding 487 to 500 and 312 to 300 to estimate 487 + 312 ≈ 800.
Checking: 'We calculated 38 × 7 = 266. Estimate: 40 × 7 = 280. Close enough!'
Estimating the total cost of a shopping list by rounding each price.
Rounding distances to estimate total travel: 45 km + 67 km ≈ 50 + 70 = 120 km.
Checking a subtraction: '1000 – 387 should be about 600. Our answer of 613 seems right.'
Discussing when an estimate is good enough and when we need an exact answer.

VC2M4N08.w: We are learning to work out change when buying things, rounding to the nearest 5 cents.
y: Calculating change is a practical skill we use every time we buy something with cash.
Rounding to 5 cents reflects real Australian rounding rules for cash transactions.
This connects our number skills to real-world money management.
eg: Working out change from $10 when the item costs $6.35 (rounded to $6.35 → pay $6.35).
Rounding $3.42 to $3.40 and $3.43 to $3.45 using Australian rounding rules.
Calculating change from $20 when buying items totalling $13.70.
Playing a shop game where we give and receive correct change.
Solving: 'I pay with a $50 note. My shopping costs $37.85. How much change?'
Checking our change by adding the change to the cost to see if it equals what we paid.

VC2M4N09.w: We are learning to solve real-life maths problems by choosing the right operation and checking our answer.
y: Choosing the right operation shows we truly understand what the problem is asking.
Checking answers with estimation builds the habit of self-correction — a skill that makes us stronger learners.
Real-world problems prepare us for the maths we use outside the classroom.
eg: Solving: 'We need 8 tables for 48 guests. How many guests per table?'
Choosing multiplication to solve: '6 packets of 12 biscuits = how many biscuits?'
Checking: 'We think the answer is 372. Let's estimate — 50 × 7 = 350. Close!'
Solving a two-step problem: 'Buy 4 books at $12 each. Pay with $50. Change?'
Explaining why we chose subtraction rather than division for a particular problem.
Solving a real problem: 'The bus holds 45 people. There are 130 students. How many buses?'

VC2M4N10.w: We are learning to follow and create algorithms using steps and decisions to explore number patterns.
y: Algorithms break problems into clear steps — a skill used in coding, maths and science.
Including decisions (if-then) in algorithms makes them more powerful and flexible.
Creating algorithms develops logical thinking and prepares us for computational problem-solving.
eg: Following: 'Start at 1. If even, divide by 2. If odd, multiply by 3 and add 1. Repeat.'
Creating a flowchart to decide if a number is a multiple of 3.
Writing step-by-step instructions for rounding a number to the nearest 100.
Using an algorithm to generate a number pattern and predict future terms.
Designing an algorithm that sorts a list of numbers from smallest to largest.
Testing a classmate's algorithm and checking if it produces the correct result.

VC2M4A01.w: We are learning to find missing numbers in equations using what we know about how numbers work.
y: Finding unknown values is the beginning of algebra — one of the most powerful branches of maths.
This develops our reasoning — we use what we know to work out what we do not know.
Solving for unknowns is used in science, engineering, finance and everyday problem-solving.
eg: Solving: ? × 6 = 42 by using our times tables.
Finding the missing number: 125 + ? = 200.
Using inverse operations: if 8 × ? = 56, then ? = 56 ÷ 8.
Solving: 'A number plus 35 equals 100. What is the number?'
Using a balance model to represent and solve equations.
Solving two-step problems: ? × 4 + 3 = 19.

VC2M4A02.w: We are learning all our times tables up to 10 × 10 really well, and using them for division too.
y: Knowing times tables fluently makes almost every part of maths easier and faster.
Using multiplication facts for division shows us how the operations are connected.
These facts are tools we carry with us for life — in shopping, cooking, building and working.
eg: Instantly knowing that 7 × 8 = 56.
Using 9 × 6 = 54 to solve 54 ÷ 9 = 6.
Playing speed games to practise recall of all facts to 10 × 10.
Using the commutative property: 4 × 7 = 7 × 4 = 28.
Solving: 'How many weeks in 63 days?' using 63 ÷ 7 = 9.
Using times tables to check division answers.

VC2M4M01.w: We are learning to use scaled instruments like thermometers and measuring jugs, including reading between the marks.
y: Reading scales accurately helps us measure precisely in science, cooking and everyday tasks.
Reading between the marks builds our understanding of fractions and estimation.
This is a practical skill used by scientists, cooks, nurses, builders and engineers.
eg: Reading a thermometer that shows a temperature between two marked lines.
Measuring liquid in a jug and reading the level between 200 mL and 300 mL.
Using a kitchen scale where each small mark represents 20 grams.
Reading a ruler to the nearest millimetre.
Measuring ingredients using a scaled measuring cup.
Interpreting a rain gauge reading between marked intervals.

VC2M4M02.w: We are learning different ways to measure perimeter and area of shapes.
y: Perimeter and area help us answer questions like 'how far around?' and 'how much space?'
Understanding both concepts prevents us from confusing them — perimeter is distance, area is surface.
These measurements are used in gardening, building, interior design and land management.
eg: Measuring the perimeter of our desk by adding the lengths of all four sides.
Finding the area of a shape by counting how many square units fit inside.
Comparing the perimeter and area of two different rectangles.
Calculating: 'We need to put a fence around a garden. How much fencing do we need?'
Using grid paper to draw shapes with a given area.
Discovering that shapes with the same perimeter can have different areas.

VC2M4M03.w: We are learning to solve time problems, including am and pm, and convert between time units.
y: Solving time problems helps us plan our day, catch buses and manage schedules.
Converting between units (hours to minutes, days to hours) builds our understanding of how time works.
Time calculations are used in travel planning, cooking, project management and sport.
eg: Converting 3 hours into 180 minutes.
Solving: 'If the movie starts at 2:45 pm and goes for 1 hour and 35 minutes, when does it end?'
Working out how long between 9:15 am and 11:40 am.
Understanding the difference between 3:00 am and 3:00 pm.
Converting 2 days into hours (48 hours).
Planning a schedule and working out how much time is available for each activity.

VC2M4M04.w: We are learning the names for different types of angles and comparing them to a right angle.
y: Knowing angle types (acute, right, obtuse, straight, reflex) helps us describe and classify shapes precisely.
Angles are fundamental to geometry and are used in construction, design and navigation.
Comparing angles to a right angle gives us a reference point for estimating and measuring.
eg: Identifying an acute angle as smaller than a right angle.
Recognising that an obtuse angle is bigger than a right angle but less than a straight angle.
Finding and naming different types of angles in classroom objects.
Using a right-angle tester to check angles in shapes.
Drawing examples of acute, right, obtuse and straight angles.
Classifying the angles in a triangle as acute, right or obtuse.

VC2M4SP01.w: We are learning to explain and compare the features of 2D shapes and 3D objects.
y: Knowing the features of shapes — sides, angles, faces, edges, vertices — helps us describe and classify them precisely.
Comparing shapes develops our ability to notice similarities and differences in the world.
Shape knowledge is used in architecture, art, engineering and design.
eg: Comparing a cube (6 faces, 12 edges, 8 vertices) with a rectangular prism.
Explaining why a triangle is the most rigid 2D shape.
Identifying that a cylinder has 2 circular faces and 1 curved surface.
Comparing regular and irregular polygons.
Describing the features of a hexagonal prism.
Finding 2D shapes in the faces of 3D objects.

VC2M4SP02.w: We are learning to represent real objects using combinations of simpler shapes.
y: Breaking complex objects into simple shapes helps us draw, build and design.
This skill is used in engineering, architecture and computer graphics.
Composing and decomposing shapes deepens our understanding of spatial relationships.
eg: Drawing a house using rectangles for the walls, a triangle for the roof and a circle for the window.
Building a model car from rectangular prisms and cylinders.
Identifying the shapes that make up a table: rectangular prism top and cylindrical legs.
Using tangram pieces to create pictures of animals or objects.
Designing a robot using a combination of 3D shapes.
Tracing around 3D objects to find the 2D shapes that make up their faces.

VC2M4SP03.w: We are learning to use grid references and directions to describe locations and paths.
y: Grid references help us pinpoint exact locations — a skill used in maps, games and navigation.
Describing paths using directions builds spatial reasoning and communication.
These skills connect to geography, orienteering, coding and everyday wayfinding.
eg: Finding a location on a grid map using coordinates like C4.
Describing a path: 'Start at A1, move 3 right to D1, then 2 up to D3.'
Programming a robot to follow a path using directions and distances.
Giving directions from the classroom to the library using grid references.
Playing Battleship and using grid coordinates to locate targets.
Creating a map with a grid and giving a friend directions to follow.

VC2M4SP04.w: We are learning about line symmetry and rotational symmetry, and creating symmetrical patterns.
y: Symmetry is found throughout nature — in butterfly wings, snowflakes and flowers — understanding it helps us appreciate patterns in the world.
Creating symmetrical designs develops precision and spatial awareness.
Symmetry is important in art, architecture, engineering and mathematics.
eg: Folding shapes in half to check if they have a line of symmetry.
Drawing the other half of a symmetrical picture on grid paper.
Identifying multiple lines of symmetry in a regular hexagon.
Creating a design with rotational symmetry using pattern blocks.
Finding symmetry in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art patterns.
Testing whether letters of the alphabet have line symmetry.

VC2M4ST01.w: We are learning to collect data and display it using pictographs, column graphs and other representations.
y: Choosing the right graph helps us communicate our findings clearly and convincingly.
Collecting our own data teaches us to be careful, fair and systematic.
Data display skills are used in science reports, news articles, business presentations and school projects.
eg: Creating a pictograph where each picture represents 2 items.
Making a column graph showing the favourite sports of students in our year level.
Choosing between a bar graph and a pictograph for our data.
Adding a title, axis labels and a key to our graph.
Interpreting: 'If each symbol represents 5 students, how many chose soccer?'
Displaying data collected from a survey in the most appropriate type of graph.

VC2M4ST02.w: We are learning to compare different ways of displaying the same data and identify what each one shows well.
y: Different displays can tell different stories from the same data — understanding this makes us better data readers.
Being able to critique data displays helps us spot when information is presented misleadingly.
This builds our critical thinking about how data is used in media, advertising and public communication.
eg: Comparing a bar graph and a pie chart made from the same data and discussing which is clearer.
Identifying that a pictograph makes it easy to count, while a column graph makes it easy to compare heights.
Discussing why a graph with a broken axis might be misleading.
Looking at two graphs of the same data with different scales and discussing how they give different impressions.
Choosing the best display for our investigation and explaining our choice.
Comparing how the same survey results look as a table versus a graph.

VC2M4ST03.w: We are learning to plan and carry out a full statistical investigation from start to finish.
y: Conducting a full investigation from question to conclusion builds independence and connects all our data skills.
This mirrors how real researchers work — they plan, collect, display, analyse and report.
Learning to draw valid conclusions from data is one of the most important thinking skills we develop.
eg: Investigating: 'Do students in our class prefer reading or playing sport at lunchtime?'
Writing a clear question, planning how to collect data, choosing a display and drawing conclusions.
Presenting our investigation to the class and answering questions about our findings.
Discussing whether our data collection was fair and what we would change next time.
Comparing our conclusions with another group who investigated a similar question.
Writing a report that explains our question, method, findings and what we learned.

VC2M4P01.w: We are learning to describe how likely events are, put them in order, and tell if events are independent or related.
y: Ordering events by likelihood helps us make better predictions and decisions.
Understanding independent events helps us see that one outcome does not affect the next — a coin flip does not 'remember' its last result.
Probability thinking protects us from common mistakes and myths about chance.
eg: Ordering events on a probability line from impossible (0) to certain (1).
Explaining that getting heads on the second coin flip does not depend on the first flip.
Placing 'it will rain tomorrow' and 'a pig will fly' on a likelihood scale.
Discussing why the chance of rolling a 6 is the same every time, no matter what happened before.
Deciding whether events are dependent or independent: drawing a card and putting it back vs. not.
Assigning simple probability values: 'The chance of rolling an even number is ½.'

VC2M4P02.w: We are learning to repeat chance experiments and observe how results can vary each time.
y: Repeating experiments shows us that short-term results can be surprising, but long-term patterns emerge.
Understanding variation helps us know that getting an unusual result does not mean something is wrong.
This builds our understanding that more trials give us more reliable information about probability.
eg: Flipping a coin 50 times and comparing our results with the expected 50/50 split.
Rolling two dice 100 times and recording the sums to find which totals appear most often.
Comparing the results of 10 trials with 100 trials and discussing the difference.
Spinning a spinner many times and seeing if the results match the expected probabilities.
Discussing why our class got 60% heads and another class got 45% — variation is normal.
Pooling results from the whole class to get a larger dataset and comparing with theoretical probability.

VC2M5N01.w: We are learning to compare, order and place decimal numbers with more than 2 decimal places on a number line.
y: Working confidently with decimals is essential for measurement, money and data in science and maths.
Placing decimals on a number line builds our sense of where numbers sit relative to each other.
Ordering decimals correctly prevents mistakes in real situations like comparing prices or measurements.
eg: Ordering 3.45, 3.405, 3.5 and 3.045 from smallest to largest.
Placing 0.375 on a number line between 0 and 1.
Comparing: 'Is 2.09 greater or less than 2.1?'
Ordering athlete race times to three decimal places: 10.234 s, 10.24 s, 10.198 s.
Placing benchmark decimals (0.25, 0.5, 0.75) on a number line and locating other decimals relative to them.
Explaining why 0.60 and 0.6 are equal even though one has more digits.

VC2M5N02.w: We are learning to find the factors of a number, identify multiples, and check divisibility.
