We learn that all feelings are OK — it is what we do with them that matters.

VC2CPFCS02.w: We learn our own strategies for calming down when our feelings are strong.
y: Having our own calming strategies helps us become more independent.
Everyone is different — finding what works for us is important.
These strategies help us move from feeling overwhelmed to feeling OK.
eg: We find that deep breathing helps us calm down.
We learn that squeezing a stress ball helps when we are angry.
We use a quiet corner when we need time to settle.
We listen to calming music using headphones.
We use a favourite comfort item to help us feel better.
We learn to count slowly when our emotions feel big.

VC2CPFCS03.w: We learn about our interests and the things that make good outcomes happen.
y: Knowing our interests helps us feel happy and motivated.
Understanding what leads to good outcomes helps us make better choices.
Recognising our strengths builds confidence and self-esteem.
eg: We try different activities and notice which ones we enjoy most.
We learn that practising something makes us better at it.
We notice what actions lead to praise or success and do more of them.
We share our favourite activity or topic with the class.
We try a new hobby and discover something we love.
We celebrate achievements and connect them to the effort we put in.

VC2CPFCS04.w: We learn ways to ask for help when something is hard.
y: Asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness.
Knowing how to seek support means we never have to struggle alone.
These help-seeking skills are useful throughout our lives.
eg: We learn to raise our hand or approach our teacher when we need help.
We ask a friend for help with a tricky task.
We use words like 'Can you help me?' or 'I do not understand.'.
We learn which adults we can go to for different kinds of help.
We practise asking for help before getting too frustrated.
We learn that everyone needs help sometimes.

VC2CPFCS05.w: We practise doing things we might not choose to do ourselves.
y: Not everything we need to do is fun — learning to handle this is important.
Trying non-preferred activities helps us discover new interests.
This flexibility helps us succeed in school and life.
eg: We participate in a group activity even though it is not our favourite.
We try eating a food we would not normally choose.
We practise a skill that feels hard because it is important for our learning.
We join in a class task even when we would rather play.
We complete a non-preferred activity and feel proud afterwards.
We learn that some things we did not like at first become enjoyable.

VC2CPFCS06.w: We learn about personal boundaries and asking for permission.
y: Understanding boundaries keeps us and others safe and respected.
Consent means that both people agree — learning this early is important.
Respecting boundaries builds trust in all our relationships.
eg: We learn to ask 'Can I have a hug?' before hugging someone.
We practise saying 'Stop, I don't like that' when a boundary is crossed.
We learn that everyone has the right to say no.
We discuss what personal space means and practise respecting it.
We learn that asking first shows respect for others.
We talk about what to do if someone does not respect our boundaries.

VC2CPFCO01.w: We learn about the different people in our everyday world.
y: Knowing about different people helps us feel confident in our surroundings.
Understanding who is in our world builds our social awareness.
Recognising the people around us helps us feel safe and connected.
eg: We learn the names and roles of people in our school — teachers, aides, office staff.
We recognise family members and other important people in our life.
We notice that people look different from one another and that is OK.
We learn about community helpers like firefighters and doctors.
We identify friends and adults we trust in our environment.
We talk about the different people we see at school and in our community.

VC2CPFCO02.w: We learn to notice what other people need and like.
y: Noticing others' needs is the beginning of empathy and kindness.
Responding to others' preferences builds positive relationships.
Being aware of differences helps us include everyone.
eg: We notice when a friend looks sad and offer comfort.
We learn that some people like quiet while others like noise.
We share something we know a friend likes.
We wait for a friend who needs more time to finish.
We adjust our behaviour when someone asks us to be quieter or gentler.
We help a classmate who is struggling with a task.

VC2CPFCO03.w: We play together with other people, sharing and taking turns.
y: Playing together teaches us to cooperate and share.
Associative play is where we learn the basics of friendship.
Interacting through play builds communication and social skills.
eg: We share materials during a group activity.
We take turns on the swing or with a popular toy.
We join in a group game and follow the simple rules.
We play a role in a pretend game with other children.
We build something together using blocks or construction materials.
We include someone who is playing alone.

VC2CPFCO04.w: We learn words and actions for interacting with other people.
y: The right words and actions help us make and keep friends.
Social language and behaviour are skills we can learn and practise.
Good interaction skills help us navigate social situations confidently.
eg: We learn to say 'hello', 'please', 'thank you' and 'sorry'.
We practise making eye contact when talking to someone.
We learn to wait our turn to speak during a conversation.
We use kind words to compliment or encourage a friend.
We learn body language that shows we are listening — facing someone, nodding.
We practise inviting someone to play using friendly words.

VC2CPFDS01.w: We learn different ways to show our feelings in everyday situations.
y: Expressing emotions appropriately is a skill that helps us every day.
When we can show our feelings in healthy ways, others can support us better.
Managing how we express emotions is key to feeling good and getting along.
eg: We use words to say 'I feel angry' instead of screaming.
We take a deep breath when we feel frustrated.
We show happiness by smiling and telling someone what made us glad.
We use a calm voice to express disappointment.
We draw a picture to show how we feel when words are hard.
We recognise when we need a break and ask for one.

VC2CPFDS02.w: We learn and use strategies to manage our emotions in everyday life.
y: Self-regulation is one of the most important skills for success in school and life.
Having strategies ready means we can handle difficult moments better.
These skills grow stronger the more we practise them.
eg: We use deep breathing when we feel overwhelmed.
We count to ten before reacting when we are upset.
We take ourselves to a calm-down area when we need space.
We use positive self-talk — 'I can do this' — when things are hard.
We identify our triggers — things that make big feelings happen.
We practise our regulation strategies during calm times so they are ready for tough times.

VC2CPFDS03.w: We learn about our personal strengths and celebrate what we achieve.
y: Knowing our strengths builds confidence and self-esteem.
Celebrating achievements motivates us to keep trying.
Everyone has strengths — recognising ours helps us contribute to our community.
eg: We share something we are good at with the class.
We celebrate when we achieve a goal we have been working towards.
We notice when we improve at something and feel proud.
We learn that strengths come in many forms — kindness, creativity, persistence.
We create a 'strengths shield' showing what makes us special.
We tell a friend about something they are good at.

VC2CPFDS04.w: We learn that challenges are part of learning and discover strategies to manage them.
y: Challenges are not problems — they are opportunities to learn and grow.
Having strategies for tough times means challenges do not feel so scary.
Learning to manage challenges builds resilience that lasts a lifetime.
eg: We talk about times when learning felt hard and how we got through it.
We learn the phrase 'I can't do it yet' instead of 'I can't do it.'.
We break a big challenge into smaller, more manageable steps.
We ask for help when a challenge feels too big to manage alone.
We celebrate the effort we put in, not just the outcome.
We share strategies that helped us overcome a challenge.

VC2CPFDS05.w: We learn ways to do things more independently.
y: Independence helps us feel capable and confident.
Learning to do things on our own is an important part of growing up.
Each new skill we master independently makes the next one easier.
eg: We practise getting dressed, eating or packing up by ourselves.
We follow a visual schedule to manage our own daily tasks.
We try to solve a problem before asking for help.
We organise our own belongings at school.
We complete a familiar activity from start to finish on our own.
We feel proud when we manage something independently for the first time.

VC2CPFDS06.w: We learn about keeping ourselves and others safe, including understanding consent.
y: Safety awareness protects us in many different situations.
Understanding consent means we respect ourselves and others.
These skills help us build trusting, respectful relationships.
eg: We learn about safe and unsafe situations and what to do in each.
We practise asking for consent — 'Is it OK if I...?'.
We learn to tell a trusted adult if we feel unsafe.
We understand that 'no' means stop — both when we say it and when others say it.
We learn safety rules for different environments — classroom, playground, road.
We practise identifying trusted adults we can go to for help.

VC2CPFDO01.w: We learn about different people in our community.
y: Knowing about the people in our community helps us feel part of something bigger.
Understanding our community builds respect for all the people in it.
Community awareness is the foundation for active participation in society.
eg: We learn about people who work in our community — shopkeepers, bus drivers, librarians.
We notice that our community includes people of different ages, abilities and backgrounds.
We visit a community place and learn about the people there.
We talk about who we might see on our way to school.
We learn about how different people contribute to our community.
We share stories about the special people in our own community.

VC2CPFDO02.w: We learn how to show care for other people.
y: Caring for others makes us good friends and kind community members.
Small acts of care can make a big difference to someone's day.
Learning to care for others builds empathy and compassion.
eg: We help a friend who has fallen over.
We share our snack with someone who forgot theirs.
We include someone who is playing alone.
We draw a picture or make a card for someone who is sad.
We help carry things or hold the door for someone.
We say kind words to someone who is having a bad day.

VC2CPFDO03.w: We learn to cooperate with other people.
y: Cooperation helps us achieve things we cannot do alone.
Working together builds our social skills and friendships.
Cooperative people are valued in every community and workplace.
eg: We take turns during a group activity.
We share materials fairly with our partner.
We listen to someone else's idea and try it out.
We help a classmate with their part of a group project.
We follow agreed rules during a cooperative game.
We work together to tidy up our classroom.

VC2CPFDO04.w: We learn different ways to show our feelings when we interact with others.
y: Expressing emotions appropriately helps our relationships go smoothly.
Others understand us better when we can show how we feel.
Emotional expression is a key part of healthy communication.
eg: We use our face and body to show a friend we are happy to see them.
We tell someone in words when we feel frustrated instead of hitting.
We show disappointment without yelling or throwing things.
We express excitement by sharing what we are excited about.
We show care by gently patting a friend who is upset.
We practise using calm words when we disagree with someone.

VC2CPFS01.w: We learn to recognise and name our feelings and the situations that cause them.
y: Naming our feelings is the first step in understanding and managing them.
Connecting feelings to situations helps us predict and prepare for emotional responses.
Emotional awareness is the foundation of all emotional intelligence.
eg: We use an emotions chart to identify how we are feeling.
We connect feelings to events — 'I feel happy when we play outside.'.
We learn that the same situation can cause different feelings in different people.
We describe a time we felt proud, nervous, excited or disappointed.
We use a check-in at the start of the day to share how we feel.
We recognise that feelings can change throughout the day.

VC2CPFS02.w: We learn strategies for dealing with difficult feelings and thinking about how others feel.
y: Managing negative emotions helps us stay happy and focused.
Thinking about others' feelings is the heart of empathy.
These coping skills protect our mental health and strengthen relationships.
eg: We practise deep breathing when we feel angry or worried.
We talk to a trusted adult when our feelings are too big to handle alone.
We imagine how a friend might feel and adjust our behaviour.
We use positive self-talk to manage disappointment.
We learn that it is OK to feel sad and that the feeling will pass.
We practise showing empathy by saying 'That must be hard' or 'I understand'.

VC2CPFS03.w: We learn about our personal strengths and interests and how to use them in school and life.
y: Knowing our strengths helps us feel confident about who we are.
Using our strengths in different situations helps us succeed.
Exploring interests opens doors to new possibilities.
eg: We create a list of things we are good at and things we enjoy.
We notice when we use a strength — like being a good listener or being creative.
We try new activities to discover new interests.
We talk about how different strengths are useful in different situations.
We help others using our strengths — like reading to a younger student.
We set a goal related to an interest and work towards it.

VC2CPFS04.w: We learn why it is important to keep trying when things are hard or new, including the value of trying new things.
y: Effort and persistence are more important than natural talent for long-term success.
Trying new things is how we discover abilities we did not know we had.
Building a 'keep trying' attitude prepares us for challenges throughout life.
eg: We share a time when we kept going even though something was hard.
We try a new activity and talk about how it felt — scary, exciting, interesting.
We learn the word 'resilience' and what it means.
We celebrate classmates who showed persistence, not just those who succeeded.
We discuss characters in stories who kept trying and what happened.
We practise positive self-talk: 'This is hard, but I can keep going.'.

VC2CPFS05.w: We learn behaviours that help us work safely and independently.
y: Working safely protects us and everyone around us.
Independence builds confidence and prepares us for new challenges.
Safe, independent habits are the foundation of responsible behaviour.
eg: We follow safety rules when using equipment or materials.
We manage our own belongings — putting things away, looking after our bag.
We complete tasks without being constantly reminded.
We make safe choices on the playground and in the classroom.
We ask for help when we need it but try on our own first.
We learn to manage our time — knowing when to start and stop an activity.

VC2CPFO01.w: We learn about the groups we, our family and our friends belong to.
y: Belonging to groups makes us feel safe and connected.
Understanding our groups helps us see who we are.
Learning about groups builds our sense of community.
eg: We talk about the groups we belong to — family, class, sports team, cultural group.
We explore how families come in many different shapes and sizes.
We share about groups our friends belong to and notice similarities and differences.
We draw a picture of the people in our family or class group.
We learn that we can belong to many groups at the same time.
We discuss what makes our groups special and why they matter to us.

VC2CPFO02.w: We learn how to include and care for others and make friends.
y: Including everyone makes our class a happier and safer place.
Learning to make friends is one of the most important life skills.
Caring for others builds strong communities.
eg: We invite someone sitting alone to join our game.
We learn to introduce ourselves to someone new.
We show care by asking 'Are you OK?' when someone seems upset.
We share toys and take turns to keep friendships strong.
We learn that being kind and honest helps us make friends.
We practise including people who are different from us.

VC2CPFO03.w: We learn basic skills for working together with our classmates.
y: Collaboration skills help us achieve more together than we can alone.
Learning to work in a team prepares us for school and future work.
Good teamwork makes activities more fun and more successful.
eg: We practise taking turns when working with a partner.
We listen to our partner's ideas before sharing our own.
We share materials fairly during a group task.
We help a classmate who is having trouble with their part.
We learn to agree on a plan before starting a group activity.
We celebrate together when our team finishes a project.

VC2CPFO04.w: We learn to use words and body language to understand what is happening during interactions, including disagreements.
y: Reading social cues helps us understand how others are feeling.
Understanding conflict situations helps us respond appropriately.
These skills help us navigate relationships more successfully.
eg: We learn that crossed arms might mean someone is upset or closed off.
We notice when a friend's voice gets louder and that might mean they are frustrated.
We practise using 'I feel' statements during disagreements.
We look at facial expressions to understand how someone is feeling.
We learn calming words to use when a conflict is starting.
We role-play how to handle disagreements calmly and respectfully.

VC2CP2S01.w: We are learning to notice and name our feelings, and to spot what is happening around us when those feelings come up.
y: When we can name a feeling, it becomes easier to talk about it and ask for help if we need it.
Noticing what makes us feel certain ways helps us understand ourselves better and feel more in control.
Being able to describe our emotions helps other people understand us and support us when things are tricky.
eg: We might notice our tummy feels tight when we are nervous about something new at school.
We could draw faces showing different feelings — happy, sad, worried, excited — and talk about when we feel each one.
We might practise saying 'I feel frustrated because...' instead of just saying 'I'm angry.'
We could share a time when we felt really proud and describe what was happening that made us feel that way.
We might watch a character in a story and guess how they are feeling from their face and body.
We could make a feelings chart for the classroom and point to how we're feeling at the start of each day.

VC2CP2S02.w: We are learning simple ways to ask for help when we need it, and how to show we understand other people's feelings.
y: Knowing how to ask for help means we don't have to struggle on our own when things are hard.
Understanding other people's feelings helps us be kind friends and classmates.
Learning ways to cope with tricky feelings now gives us skills we'll use for our whole lives.
eg: We might practise raising our hand and saying 'I need help with this part' during class time.
We could role-play comforting a friend who is upset by saying something kind or sitting with them quietly.
We might take three deep breaths when we feel overwhelmed to help ourselves calm down.
We could talk about what to do if we feel left out at lunchtime, like joining another group or talking to a teacher.
We might notice a classmate looks sad and ask them 'Are you okay?' to show we care.
We could make a list of trusted people we can go to when we need help — like a teacher, parent, or older buddy.

VC2CP2S03.w: We are learning to notice what we are good at and what we enjoy, and how these strengths help us at school and beyond.
y: Knowing our strengths helps us feel confident and proud of who we are.
Understanding what we enjoy helps us make choices about activities, hobbies, and learning.
Recognising our strengths means we can use them to help others and contribute to our classroom community.
eg: We might make a 'Things I'm good at' poster showing our skills — like drawing, running, reading, or being kind.
We could share something we enjoy doing at home and explain why it makes us happy.
We might try a new activity and notice if it's something we'd like to do again.
We could ask a friend or family member what they think we're good at and compare it with our own ideas.
We might set a small goal based on something we enjoy, like reading one more book this week.
We could talk about how different people in our class have different strengths that all matter.

VC2CP2S04.w: We are learning why it's important to keep trying when things are hard or new, and how effort helps us grow.
y: Keeping going when things are tough helps our brains learn and get stronger.
Trying new things can feel scary, but it's how we discover what we're capable of.
Learning to push through challenges now builds the courage we need for bigger challenges later.
eg: We might try a tricky maths problem more than once and notice that it gets easier each time.
We could share a story about a time we couldn't do something at first but kept practising until we could.
We might cheer on a classmate who is finding something difficult, saying 'You can do it, keep going!'
We could talk about how learning to ride a bike or swim took lots of practice before it felt easy.
We might try a new food or game we've never tried before and talk about how it felt to give it a go.
We could draw a 'before and after' picture showing something we couldn't do at the start of the year but can do now.

VC2CP2S05.w: We are learning ways to work safely and look after ourselves when we are doing things on our own.
y: Being able to work independently means we can get things done even when a teacher isn't right beside us.
Knowing how to stay safe helps us take care of ourselves and the people around us.
Working safely builds trust so we can take on more responsibility as we grow.
eg: We might practise putting away scissors carefully after we finish cutting.
We could follow classroom rules for moving around safely, like walking instead of running inside.
We might learn how to pack up our own workspace neatly after finishing an activity.
We could practise following a set of instructions step by step without needing a reminder.
We might talk about what to do if something feels unsafe, like telling a teacher straight away.
We could learn to use classroom tools like glue and rulers properly so nobody gets hurt.

VC2CP2O01.w: We are learning that families come in many different shapes and forms, and every family is special.
y: Understanding that families look different helps us respect and include everyone in our class.
Learning about different family types helps us see that love and care are what make a family, not what it looks like.
Knowing about diverse families connects us to the real world, where communities are made up of all kinds of people.
eg: We might share about the people in our family — like parents, grandparents, aunties, uncles, or foster carers.
We could read stories about children who live in different types of families and talk about what's the same and different.
We might draw our family and compare it with a friend's, noticing that every family is unique.
We could talk about how in some cultures, like Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, family includes a wide network of kin.
We might discuss how some children live with one parent, two parents, grandparents, or other family members.
We could make a class display celebrating all the different families in our room.

VC2CP2O02.w: We are learning that listening carefully helps us understand what other people think and feel, even when it's different from us.
y: Good listening helps us learn new things from other people's experiences and ideas.
When we listen properly, people feel respected and are more likely to listen to us too.
Understanding different perspectives helps us solve problems together and avoid misunderstandings.
eg: We might practise looking at the speaker and waiting for them to finish before we talk.
We could play a listening game where we repeat back what a partner said to show we understood.
We might hear a classmate's idea that's different from ours and say 'I hadn't thought of that' instead of 'You're wrong.'
We could listen to a story from another culture and talk about what we learned that was new to us.
We might practise nodding and using our face to show we're paying attention without interrupting.
We could take turns sharing something about our weekend and asking each other questions about it.

VC2CP2O03.w: We are learning how to make and keep friends, and how our words and actions can help or hurt the people around us.
y: Friendships make school more fun and help us feel like we belong.
Understanding how words and actions affect others helps us be the kind of person people want to be around.
Learning to change our behaviour when it's hurting someone shows maturity and kindness.
eg: We might practise inviting someone new to play with us at lunchtime.
We could role-play saying sorry when we've accidentally hurt someone's feelings.
We might talk about the difference between teasing that's fun and teasing that hurts.
We could make a list of friendly actions — like sharing, taking turns, and using kind words.
We might discuss what to do if a friend says something that makes us feel bad.
We could practise thinking before we speak by asking ourselves 'Will this help or hurt?'

VC2CP2O04.w: We are learning how to work well with others in a group and make sure everyone gets a fair turn to contribute.
y: Working together helps us achieve things we couldn't do alone.
Learning to collaborate now prepares us for teamwork in sports, projects, and future jobs.
Checking that everyone contributes makes the group fairer and helps everyone feel valued.
eg: We might take turns being the leader, the recorder, and the presenter in a group project.
We could practise sharing materials and waiting for our turn to use something.
We might check in with our group by asking 'Does everyone agree?' or 'What do you think?'
We could talk about what to do if one person is doing all the work and others aren't helping.
We might give each group member a specific job so everyone knows what they need to do.
We could reflect after a group task on what went well and what we could do better next time.

VC2CP2O05.w: We are learning about situations where disagreements can happen, and the difference between okay and not-okay ways to handle them.
y: Conflicts are a normal part of life, and learning to handle them well makes our friendships stronger.
Knowing the difference between helpful and hurtful behaviour keeps us and others safe.
Practising good conflict skills now means we'll be better at sorting out problems as we get older.
eg: We might role-play what to do when two people want the same toy or piece of equipment.
We could talk about the difference between telling a teacher to get help and 'dobbing' to get someone in trouble.
We might practise using 'I feel... when you...' sentences to explain how someone's actions affected us.
We could discuss why hitting, pushing, or name-calling are not okay ways to solve a problem.
We might brainstorm fair solutions when two friends disagree about what game to play.
We could watch a short video about a conflict and discuss what each person could have done differently.

VC2CP4S01.w: We are learning how being around other people — in different social situations — can change the way we feel and behave.
y: When we understand what triggers our emotions, we can prepare for tricky situations and handle them better.
Knowing that social situations affect our feelings helps us be more understanding of how others are feeling too.
This helps us feel more confident in new or unfamiliar situations, like meeting new people or starting at a new school.
eg: We talk about how meeting someone new might make us feel nervous, excited or shy — and that all of those feelings are normal.
We discuss how a disagreement in the playground can make us feel angry or upset, and how that changes the way we act.
We draw faces showing how we might feel in different situations — like a birthday party, the first day of school, or being picked last for a team.
We share examples of times we felt differently about the same situation as someone else, like one person feeling excited about a presentation while another felt scared.
We role-play social situations and notice what emotions come up and how they might change our behaviour.
We create a feelings thermometer showing how our emotions can go up or down depending on what is happening around us.

VC2CP4S02.w: We are learning ways to help our friends when they are struggling, different ways to cope when things are hard for us, and how to show others that we understand their feelings.
y: Having good coping strategies means we can bounce back more quickly when something difficult happens.
When we learn to support our friends, we build stronger and more caring relationships.
Understanding empathy — really feeling what someone else feels — helps us be the kind of person others can trust and rely on.
eg: We practise noticing how someone is feeling by looking at their body language — like crossed arms, a smile or tears.
We make a list of things we can do when we feel overwhelmed, like taking deep breaths, talking to a trusted adult, or going for a walk.
We role-play being a supportive friend — listening carefully, asking how we can help, and not trying to fix everything straight away.
We identify small behaviours that can change how others feel, like smiling at someone who looks sad or sharing a kind note.
We create our own 'coping toolkit' by drawing or writing the strategies that work best for us personally.
We watch a video of children helping each other and discuss what made their support helpful.

VC2CP4S03.w: We are learning how to recognise and use our personal strengths to help ourselves and others, and how to develop interests that help us grow.
y: Knowing our strengths gives us confidence to try new things and helps us support the people around us.
Developing personal interests makes life more enjoyable and can lead to discovering hidden talents.
Understanding what we are good at helps us know how to contribute when working with others.
eg: We list our personal strengths — like being a good listener, being brave, or being creative — and share examples of when we have used them.
We think of a time we helped someone by using one of our strengths, like cheering up a friend because we are funny.
We choose a personal interest we want to develop further and explain why it matters to us.
We interview a classmate about their strengths and interests and present what we learned to the class.
We draw a 'strength shield' with pictures of the things we are good at and display them on the wall.
We set a small goal related to an interest — like learning a new song or reading a new book — and track our progress.

VC2CP4S04.w: We are learning ways to keep going when things get hard and how to adapt when situations change unexpectedly.
y: Life often throws us challenges — learning to persist and adapt means we do not give up when things get tough.
Being adaptable helps us cope with change, whether it is a new school, a new teacher, or a change in our routine.
Practising persistence now builds the mental strength we need to tackle bigger challenges as we grow.
eg: We talk about the saying 'If at first you don't succeed, try and try again' and share times when trying again worked for us.
We think about a time something changed unexpectedly and how we adapted — like a sport being cancelled and finding something else to do.
We practise a difficult skill, like tying knots or solving a tricky maths problem, and notice how persistence helps us improve.
We read a story about a character who faced setbacks but kept going and discuss what kept them motivated.
We list the different actions we could take when stuck on a hard task, like asking for help, taking a break, or trying a different approach.
We share examples of athletes or musicians who had to practise many times before getting something right.

VC2CP4S05.w: We are learning ways to work well on our own and how to make good decisions when we need to be responsible for ourselves.
y: Being able to work independently helps us feel proud of what we can achieve by ourselves.
Making responsible decisions is a skill we use every day — from choosing how to spend our time to knowing when to ask for help.
Learning independence now prepares us for more responsibility as we grow older.
eg: We brainstorm strategies for staying focused when working on a task by ourselves, like removing distractions and breaking the task into smaller steps.
We discuss why asking for help is actually a smart strategy when working alone, not a sign of weakness.
We practise making a simple decision — like choosing which task to do first — and explain our reasoning.
We set up a work plan for an independent project, listing the steps we need to follow and ticking them off as we go.
We reflect on a task we completed independently and write about what went well and what we would do differently.
We role-play situations where we have to decide between doing what is easy and doing what is right, and talk about our choices.

VC2CP4O01.w: We are learning about what makes people similar to each other and what makes us different — things like our age, what we can do, the languages we speak, our culture and our beliefs.
y: When we notice both similarities and differences, we realise that every person brings something special to our classroom and community.
Understanding differences helps us be kind and welcoming to people whose lives look different from ours.
Learning about diversity now helps us build friendships with all kinds of people throughout our lives.
eg: We survey our class to find out how many different languages are spoken at home and make a colourful chart showing all of them.
We describe a friend and find one thing we have in common and one thing that is different between us.
We read a story about children from different cultural backgrounds and talk about what they share and what is unique to each.
We draw self-portraits showing the things that make us who we are — our family, hobbies, foods we love and traditions we celebrate.
We play a game where we stand up if something is true for us, and see how some things are shared by lots of us while others are unique.
We talk about how people in our family have different abilities and ages, and how everyone still belongs and matters.

VC2CP4O02.w: We are learning how the things we have in common and the ways we are different can affect our friendships, and when we might need to change our behaviour to be respectful.
y: Knowing how differences affect relationships helps us get along better with people who think or live differently from us.
When we learn to adjust our behaviour, we become better friends and classmates to everyone around us.
Understanding this helps us build a classroom where everyone feels safe and included.
eg: We read a story about two friends who disagree because they come from different backgrounds, and we talk about how they worked it out.
We discuss the idea that 'differences can actually make friendships stronger' and share examples from our own lives.
We role-play situations where someone feels left out because of a difference, and practise ways to include them.
We talk about times when we had to change our behaviour to be kind to someone — like being quieter when a friend was upset.
We create a class agreement about how to treat each other respectfully when we disagree or see things differently.
We discuss how being on a sports team with people who have different skills can make the whole team stronger.

VC2CP4O03.w: We are learning why it matters to include everyone in our activities, groups and games, and how we can make sure nobody gets left out.
y: Everyone deserves to feel like they belong — learning to include others helps us create a kind and caring community.
When we include everyone, our games and activities become more fun because we hear new ideas and learn from each other.
Practising inclusion now builds habits that help us be fair and welcoming people as we grow up.
eg: We think about how it feels to be left out of a game and share those feelings with our class.
We practise inviting someone who is sitting alone to come and join our group at lunchtime.
We design a poster showing ways to include everyone in playground games, even when people have different abilities.
We brainstorm rules for group work that make sure every person gets a turn to share their ideas.
We read a story about a child who felt excluded and discuss what the other characters could have done differently.
We create a class challenge to notice when someone is being left out and find a way to include them that day.

VC2CP4O04.w: We are learning why working together is better than working alone for some tasks, and how to check whether our teamwork is going well.
y: Good teamwork skills help us get more done and come up with better ideas than we could on our own.
Learning to collaborate teaches us to listen, share and respect different points of view.
These skills are useful everywhere — in our families, at school, in sports, and one day in our jobs.
eg: We try to solve a puzzle by ourselves first, then work on the same puzzle with a partner, and compare how it went.
We make a list of skills that good team members use — like taking turns, listening carefully and encouraging each other.
We use our teamwork checklist to rate how well our group worked together on a project.
We build something together using blocks or craft materials and talk about what each person contributed.
We reflect on a group task by writing one thing that went well and one thing our team could do better next time.
We compare what happened when a sports team worked together versus when everyone tried to do their own thing.

VC2CP4O05.w: We are learning about the disagreements we sometimes have with our friends and classmates, and how the way we act and think can change what happens next.
y: Conflicts happen to everyone — understanding them helps us handle disagreements without hurting ourselves or others.
When we understand how our behaviour affects outcomes, we gain the power to make things better instead of worse.
Learning to manage conflict now gives us skills we will use in friendships, family life and beyond.
eg: We brainstorm types of conflicts that happen between friends — like arguing over a game or feeling hurt by something someone said.
We name the emotions that come up during a disagreement, like frustration, sadness or anger.
We look at two different responses to the same conflict and talk about which one leads to a better outcome.
We role-play a playground argument and practise using calm words instead of shouting.
We draw a comic strip showing a conflict and two possible endings — one where someone stays angry and one where they talk it out.
We share stories about times we resolved a disagreement and what we learned from the experience.

VC2CP6S01.w: We are learning how and why our emotions and behaviour change depending on whether we are at home, at school, with friends or in other social situations.
y: Understanding why our feelings change in different settings helps us manage our reactions more effectively.
When we recognise how context affects our emotions, we can better understand why others behave the way they do too.
This self-awareness gives us more control over our actions and helps us make better choices in social situations.
eg: We create a short video or skit showing how the same person might feel and act differently at home, at school, and with friends.
We analyse characters in stories or movies and explain how their emotions and behaviour change depending on the situation they are in.
We keep a feelings journal for a week, noting how our emotions shifted in different contexts and what we think caused those shifts.
We discuss why we might feel confident talking to friends but nervous presenting in front of the class, and what that tells us about social context.
We map out the emotions we feel in different parts of our day — morning at home, arriving at school, lunchtime, after-school activities — and compare patterns.
We role-play the same scenario in two different contexts — like receiving feedback from a friend versus a teacher — and discuss how our emotional response changes.

VC2CP6S02.w: We are learning when and how to support our friends, manage our own emotions, use healthy coping strategies, and communicate with genuine understanding of how others feel.
y: Strong coping strategies and emotional regulation help us stay calm and clear-headed when life gets stressful.
Being able to genuinely understand and respond to others' feelings makes us the kind of friend and person others can rely on.
These skills protect our mental health and help us build deeper, more trusting relationships.
eg: We discuss the idea that 'listening is as important as speaking' and practise active listening strategies in peer support role-plays.
We create a personal coping profile by choosing from a range of strategies — like talking to someone, being active, journaling, or creative expression — and reflecting on why those work for us.
We practise identifying what a friend needs in a difficult moment — sometimes they need advice, but sometimes they just need someone to listen.
We learn about self-regulation techniques like deep breathing, counting to ten, or stepping away for a moment, and practise using them.
We write encouraging messages for a 'kindness jar' that classmates can read when they are having a tough day.
We discuss real scenarios where empathetic communication made a difference and identify what specific words or actions helped.

VC2CP6S03.w: We are learning how to recognise, use and further develop our personal strengths to face challenges, and how to explore and grow our personal interests.
y: Knowing our strengths gives us confidence to tackle challenges and support others who are struggling.
Developing our interests helps us discover what we are passionate about and brings more meaning and enjoyment to our lives.
When we know what we are good at, we can contribute more effectively in teams, projects and our wider community.
eg: We identify strengths we see in our classmates — like courage, determination, a sense of humour or creativity — and explain how these help them face challenges.
We list strengths that help us at home, at school and in the community, and discuss which ones appear in all three settings.
We set a goal to develop a personal interest further — like learning a musical instrument, a new sport, or a craft — and make a plan to pursue it.
We write about a time we used a personal strength to get through a difficult situation and what we learned from it.
We interview family members about their strengths and interests and create a family strengths profile.
We discuss how our interests might connect to future opportunities and create a vision board showing where our passions could take us.

VC2CP6S04.w: We are learning what it means to be confident, adaptable and persistent, when these qualities matter most, and what we can and cannot control through our own actions.
y: Understanding confidence, adaptability and persistence helps us keep going when things do not go the way we planned.
Knowing what we can and cannot control helps us focus our energy where it will actually make a difference.
These qualities help us cope with changes and challenges throughout our lives — from changing schools to facing setbacks.
eg: We share ideas about how people respond to challenging situations — like changing schools — and talk about what it means to be resilient.
We identify moments in a book or film where characters show confidence, adaptability or persistence, and explain how each quality helped them.
We create a Venn diagram showing things we can control, things we cannot control, and things we can partially influence.
We interview someone we admire and ask them about a time they had to be persistent or adaptable.
We try something new that is outside our comfort zone — like speaking in front of the class — and reflect on what confidence felt like.
We discuss how athletes respond to losing a game and what adaptability and persistence look like in sport.

VC2CP6S05.w: We are learning how to check how well we are going when working on our own, how to set good goals, and how to make thoughtful, responsible decisions.
y: Being able to evaluate our own work helps us improve without always needing someone else to tell us what to fix.
Setting clear goals gives us direction and motivation, making it easier to stay focused and measure our progress.
Making informed decisions is a skill we use every day, and getting better at it now helps us take on bigger responsibilities.
eg: We prepare a work plan for an independent project, listing the steps and deadlines we need to meet, then reflect on our progress along the way.
We create a checklist of success criteria for a task and use it to monitor our own work, ticking off items as we go.
We set a SMART goal — one that is Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Time-bound — and track our progress over a few weeks.
We reflect on a decision we made during an independent task — what information we used, what alternatives we considered, and whether we would decide the same way again.
We discuss how having a plan helps us make better decisions when unexpected challenges come up during a project.
We share our completed independent work with a partner and use peer feedback to evaluate what we did well and what we could improve.

VC2CP6O01.w: We are learning what stereotyping, discrimination and prejudice mean, how to spot them, and the real impact they have on people's lives.
y: Recognising stereotypes, discrimination and prejudice helps us stand up against unfairness when we see it in our school and community.
Understanding these concepts makes us more thoughtful about our own assumptions and how they might affect others.
This knowledge empowers us to help create a fairer, more just world where everyone is treated with respect.
eg: We define the words 'stereotype', 'discrimination' and 'prejudice' in our own words and come up with real examples of each.
We examine media articles and advertisements to identify hidden stereotypes and talk about the impact they might have on the people being stereotyped.
We research a real historical example of discrimination and present how it affected the community involved.
We discuss how assumptions about people based on their appearance, gender or background can lead to unfair treatment.
We create posters challenging a common stereotype and display them in our school to spark conversations.
We role-play scenarios where prejudice occurs and practise ways to speak up or intervene respectfully.

VC2CP6O02.w: We are learning how to act with sensitivity and respect towards people who are different from us, and why this matters for everyone.
y: Showing sensitivity to differences helps us build trust and genuine connections with a wide range of people.
When we are respectful of differences, our communities become safer and more welcoming for everyone.
Developing these behaviours now prepares us to work and live effectively in a diverse world.
eg: We explore different scenarios involving relationships and discuss how we would respond respectfully in each one.
We talk about why people give up their seats for elderly passengers or people with disabilities on public transport, and brainstorm other ways to show sensitivity in our community.
We research cultural practices that are different from our own and share what we learned with our class, focusing on what we admire or find interesting.
We discuss how using someone's preferred name or pronoun is a simple but powerful way to show respect.
We write a reflection about a time we noticed someone being treated insensitively and what could have been done differently.
We create a class guide to respectful communication that includes tips for talking with people from different backgrounds.

VC2CP6O03.w: We are learning what respectful relationships look like, how to build and maintain them, and what to do when a relationship needs repairing.
y: Strong, respectful relationships are the foundation of a happy life — they help us feel safe, valued and connected.
Knowing how to repair a relationship after a disagreement is just as important as building one in the first place.
These skills help us navigate friendships, family dynamics and eventually professional relationships too.
eg: We brainstorm the characteristics of a respectful relationship — like trust, honesty, good listening and kindness — and create a class poster.
We work in groups to develop a tip sheet for rebuilding a friendship after a falling out, including being sensitive to cultural differences.
We role-play scenarios where a relationship has been damaged and practise steps for repairing it, like apologising sincerely and listening to the other person.
We compare respectful and disrespectful relationships in stories or movies and identify what makes the difference.
We write a letter to a fictional friend explaining how to make things right after a conflict.
We discuss how showing respect looks different in different cultures and settings, and why flexibility matters.

VC2CP6O04.w: We are learning what makes a team work well together, the different roles people play in teams, and how to reflect on our own contribution.
y: Understanding team roles and leadership helps us contribute more effectively whenever we work with others.
Learning to reflect on our own performance helps us grow and become better collaborators over time.
These skills are essential for school projects, sports, community groups and future workplaces.
eg: We describe different team roles — like the leader, the note-taker and the presenter — and discuss what each one looks like in action.
We create role descriptions that spell out the skills and behaviours of good team members, then use them to evaluate our group's performance after a project.
We take turns being the team leader and reflect on what was easy and what was challenging about that role.
We watch footage of a team working together — like a sports team or a musical ensemble — and identify the different roles people played.
We use a reflection template to rate our own collaboration skills after a group task and set a goal for improvement.
We discuss what happens when one team member does not do their part and brainstorm solutions that are fair and respectful.

VC2CP6O05.w: We are learning strategies for preventing, calming down and resolving conflicts, and thinking about what causes conflicts and what happens because of them.
y: Conflict is a natural part of life — having strategies to deal with it means we do not let disagreements ruin our relationships.
Understanding causes and effects of conflict helps us spot problems early and stop them from getting worse.
These problem-solving skills help us in every area of life, from friendships and family to school and our wider community.
eg: We identify the causes of conflict in different scenarios and trace how one small misunderstanding can escalate if no one addresses it.
We explore how conflict between friends can also affect families and bystanders, not just the people directly involved.
We practise 'I-statements' — like 'I feel frustrated when…' — as a strategy for expressing ourselves without blaming others.
We role-play a conflict scenario and try three different approaches: avoiding the issue, arguing back, and having a calm conversation, then compare the outcomes.
We create a conflict resolution flowchart that shows steps from identifying the problem to finding a solution both sides agree on.
We discuss real examples from our school where conflicts were resolved peacefully and identify what strategies were used.

VC2CP8S01.w: We are learning to recognise when our emotions are complex — when we feel multiple or conflicting things at once — and to understand what causes that complexity and how it affects us.
y: Emotional complexity is a normal part of growing up — recognising it helps us make sense of confusing feelings instead of being overwhelmed by them.
Understanding the causes and consequences of complex emotions gives us greater control over how we respond to challenging situations.
This self-awareness is the foundation of emotional intelligence, which benefits our relationships, mental health and decision-making.
eg: We identify triggers that can create complex emotions — like feeling proud of a personal achievement but guilty about beating a friend.
We use 'what if' questions to explore a scenario and track how our emotional responses might shift and layer over time.
We discuss times when we felt two conflicting emotions at once — like being excited and scared about starting high school — and how we managed them.
We map out the emotional journey of a character in a book or film, identifying moments where their feelings became complex.
We journal about a recent experience where our emotions surprised us and explore what might have caused that reaction.
We discuss how understanding emotional complexity can help us be more empathetic when someone else's reaction does not make immediate sense to us.

VC2CP8S02.w: We are learning the difference between helpful and unhelpful coping strategies when facing challenges, and how to support our friends with empathy when they are going through difficult times.
y: Knowing which coping strategies actually help — and which ones just distract or delay — means we can recover from setbacks more effectively.
Being able to provide genuine peer support strengthens our friendships and helps create a community where people look out for each other.
These skills are protective factors for mental health, helping us and our friends build resilience during adolescence and beyond.
eg: We identify the range of emotions that might arise in a scenario involving adversity and explore productive ways to manage each one.
We brainstorm ways to acknowledge what a friend is going through without trying to immediately fix their problem, then reflect on what we learned from each other.
We categorise a list of coping strategies as productive or unproductive and discuss why some strategies that feel good in the short term can be harmful in the long run.
We create a resource guide for our year level with information about where to seek help — including trusted adults, school counsellors and helplines.
We practise empathetic responses to different scenarios, focusing on validating feelings rather than giving unsolicited advice.
We discuss the importance of knowing our own limits when supporting a friend and when it is appropriate to involve a trusted adult.

VC2CP8S03.w: We are learning strategies for reflecting on and developing our personal strengths so we can support ourselves and others, and how to connect our interests to broader life paths including potential careers.
y: Deepening our self-knowledge about our strengths helps us make better choices about how we spend our time and energy.
Connecting our personal interests to career paths and life goals gives us a sense of purpose and direction for the future.
When we understand our own strengths well, we can use them intentionally to help others and contribute to our communities.
eg: We discuss how feedback from others can help us see strengths we might not recognise in ourselves, then create a simple survey to gather feedback from family and friends.
We share our personal strengths with peers in a structured discussion and identify new insights from hearing about each other's perspectives.
We research careers or life paths that connect to our personal interests and strengths, and present our findings to the class.
We set a goal for developing a specific strength — like public speaking, organisation or creativity — and create a plan with concrete steps.
We interview someone whose career aligns with their personal interests and present what we learned about how they got there.
We write a reflective piece about how a personal strength helped us in a challenging situation and how we plan to develop it further.

VC2CP8S04.w: We are learning strategies for building our confidence, adaptability and perseverance when facing challenges, including how to draw on our personal strengths and healthy coping methods.
y: Confidence, adaptability and perseverance are not fixed traits — they are skills we can deliberately strengthen with practice.
These qualities help us navigate the pressures of adolescence and prepare us for the uncertainties and demands of adult life.
Understanding the enablers and barriers to these qualities helps us create conditions for our own success.
eg: We investigate perseverance and adaptability as entrepreneurial characteristics, studying case studies of people who succeeded in business because of these qualities.
We explore the lives of artists, athletes or scientists who persevered through significant challenges and reflect on what we can learn from their experiences.
We identify factors that build confidence — like preparation, practice, positive self-talk and supportive relationships — and create a personal confidence plan.
We discuss specific barriers to perseverance, such as fear of failure or perfectionism, and brainstorm strategies for overcoming them.
We share examples of times we adapted our approach when something was not working and reflect on what helped us make that shift.
We design a challenge for ourselves that requires stepping outside our comfort zone, then journal about the experience afterwards.

VC2CP8S05.w: We are learning what helps and what hinders us when it comes to working independently, making responsible decisions, and setting and achieving goals.
y: Understanding our enablers and barriers gives us practical strategies for overcoming obstacles and reaching our goals.
Strong independent learning skills set us up for success in senior school, further education and the workplace.
Making effective, responsible decisions is a life skill that becomes more important as we take on greater independence.
eg: We identify positive and negative factors that influence our ability to learn independently — like motivation, distractions, time management and support systems.
We respond to scenarios involving difficult decisions and analyse which responses would be most effective and responsible.
We set a personal goal using a structured framework and track our progress, reflecting on what strategies helped us stay on track.
We discuss how knowledge of our own enablers and barriers can improve our decision-making in different contexts.
We create a 'decision-making toolkit' with questions we can ask ourselves before making important choices.
We reflect on a time we worked independently and identify what factors contributed to success and what we would change next time.

VC2CP8O01.w: We are learning how people's values and beliefs shape who they are, how they can differ between people, and how those differences show up in relationships.
y: Understanding that people hold different values and beliefs helps us approach relationships with curiosity rather than judgement.
Recognising diverse perspectives prepares us for a world where we will live, work and connect with people whose views differ from our own.
This awareness helps us navigate disagreements more maturely by understanding the deeper reasons behind people's positions.
eg: We create a ranked list of values that matter most to us in a particular context — like friendship or school — and compare our lists with classmates to see similarities and differences.
We interview a parent, grandparent or carer to learn what they value most and how they show those values through their relationships.
We discuss how cultural, religious and family backgrounds shape the values people hold and how this influences their behaviour.
We explore a scenario where two people disagree because of different values and discuss how they could still maintain a respectful relationship.
We reflect on how our own values have changed as we have grown up and what experiences influenced those changes.
We research how communities with diverse values find ways to coexist and support one another, using real examples.

VC2CP8O02.w: We are learning what human rights and responsibilities are, and how respecting them helps hold communities together and creates social cohesion.
y: Understanding human rights gives us a framework for standing up against injustice and advocating for fair treatment of all people.
Knowing our responsibilities alongside our rights helps us contribute to communities where everyone can thrive.
These concepts are fundamental to active citizenship and help us understand how laws and institutions work to protect people.
eg: We investigate the UN Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the Convention on the Rights of the Child and the Victorian Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities, comparing their similarities and differences.
We explore real-world examples where human rights were challenged — like large-scale wage theft — and discuss the impact on social unity and trust.
We debate whether rights come with corresponding responsibilities and provide examples of how the two connect.
We research a historical human rights movement and present how it changed laws, attitudes and social cohesion.
We create a visual display showing how human rights protections apply in different contexts — home, school, workplace and community.
We discuss what happens to social cohesion when people's rights are consistently ignored or violated, using case studies.

VC2CP8O03.w: We are learning about the factors that shape how we experience personal and group relationships — including boundaries, levels of closeness, power dynamics and social expectations.
y: Understanding these factors helps us build healthier relationships where both people feel respected and valued.
Recognising power dynamics and boundaries protects us from harmful situations and helps us support others who may be vulnerable.
This knowledge helps us navigate the increasingly complex social world of adolescence with greater awareness and confidence.
eg: We define what personal boundaries look like in different contexts — with family, friends, online — and discuss why they matter for respectful relationships.
We explore how social expectations about relationships have changed over time and what has stayed the same.
We discuss how power imbalances in relationships — like between older and younger students, or employers and employees — can affect how people interact.
We analyse scenarios where someone's boundaries are crossed and discuss what respectful responses look like.
We examine how cultural and personal factors influence what people consider appropriate levels of closeness in different relationships.
We create guidelines for recognising when a relationship feels unbalanced and identifying steps to address it.

VC2CP8O04.w: We are learning to recognise when collaboration would be beneficial, how to set shared goals for our team, and practical ways team members can help each other succeed.
y: Effective collaboration skills are essential for nearly every career and community role we will take on in the future.
Learning to set team goals and support each other builds trust and produces better outcomes than working alone.
These skills help us navigate group projects, sporting teams, community organisations and eventually professional workplaces.
eg: We explore the differences between being a team leader and being a team member, and discuss when each role requires different skills.
We develop strategies with our team for how we will mutually support each other to achieve our shared goals, then reflect on the results.
We identify situations where collaboration would produce a better result than individual effort and explain our reasoning.
We set SMART goals for a group project and assign roles that play to each member's strengths.
We practise giving constructive feedback to teammates that is specific, helpful and encouraging.
We reflect on a past team experience, identifying what communication strategies helped the group succeed and what could have been improved.

VC2CP8O05.w: We are learning when and how to use different skills and strategies to prevent, calm down and resolve conflicts — whether within our own group or between different groups.
y: Conflict is inevitable in any group — having a toolkit of strategies means we can address issues before they escalate.
Understanding different approaches to conflict helps us choose the right strategy for each situation rather than reacting impulsively.
These skills protect our relationships and help us create environments where people feel safe to disagree respectfully.
eg: We explore different types of conflict — like misunderstandings, value clashes and competition for resources — and identify possible points of intervention.
We discuss the advantages and disadvantages of taking pre-emptive action versus waiting until conflict arises, considering likely outcomes in different contexts.
We practise mediation skills by role-playing a conflict between two groups and working through a structured resolution process.
We analyse a real-world conflict — in our school, community or the news — and identify what strategies were or could have been used.
We create a conflict prevention plan for a group project, identifying potential sources of disagreement and agreeing on how to handle them in advance.
We discuss how cultural background and personal experience influence how people approach conflict, and how to be sensitive to these differences.

VC2CP10S01.w: We are learning how to analyse and evaluate emotional complexity — situations where we feel multiple or conflicting emotions — across different contexts and from different people's perspectives.
y: The ability to analyse complex emotions helps us make thoughtful decisions rather than being driven by our immediate emotional reactions.
Understanding emotional complexity from multiple perspectives makes us more empathetic and effective in our relationships and communication.
These analytical skills support our mental health by helping us process difficult feelings rather than suppressing or being overwhelmed by them.
eg: We compare perspectives on the emotions felt and expressed in a range of scenarios and make recommendations about our preferred response, explaining our reasoning.
We analyse how the same situation can involve different levels of emotional complexity depending on the person's background, experiences and perspective.
We discuss how emotions in professional contexts — like feeling pride in our work but frustration with a colleague — require careful navigation.
We examine how cultural context influences the expression and interpretation of complex emotions.
We write a reflective analysis of a personal experience where our emotions were complicated, identifying the different layers and what influenced them.
We evaluate different emotional responses to a shared scenario and discuss why reasonable people can have genuinely different emotional reactions.

VC2CP10S02.w: We are learning when and how to choose effective help-seeking and coping strategies for different situations, and how to provide meaningful peer support and empathetic communication when others need us.
y: Knowing which coping strategies suit which situations means we can respond flexibly to a range of challenges rather than relying on one approach.
Providing effective peer support is a powerful way to strengthen our communities and can genuinely change someone's experience during a difficult time.
These skills are protective factors for mental health — both our own and that of the people we care about.
eg: We develop a guide for managing complex emotions in different situations, including how a preferred coping strategy could be adapted for different circumstances.
We rewrite a scenario to demonstrate alternative ways to express empathetic communication, comparing the impact of different approaches.
We create a decision tree for help-seeking that helps us identify the most appropriate source of support depending on the situation — a friend, family member, counsellor or helpline.
We practise providing peer support in role-plays, focusing on empathetic listening, validation and knowing when to suggest professional help.
We discuss the difference between sympathy and empathy in communication and why empathy is generally more helpful and connecting.
We reflect on our own coping patterns, evaluating which strategies have been most and least effective, and make a plan for strengthening our approach.

VC2CP10S03.w: We are learning strategies for reflecting on, using and further developing our personal strengths to support ourselves and others in challenging situations, and how to connect our strengths and interests to planning for our future.
y: A clear understanding of our personal strengths gives us confidence and direction when making important decisions about our future.
Being able to support others by drawing on our strengths makes us valuable team members, friends and community contributors.
Connecting strengths to future planning helps us approach career choices and life transitions with purpose rather than uncertainty.
eg: We imagine supporting a peer through a challenge — like seeking school funding to start a new club — and discuss how we could draw on our personal strengths to help.
We examine our personal strengths honestly, identifying areas for improvement and designing a plan for an upcoming challenge like starting senior secondary studies.
We create a strengths-based personal profile that connects our abilities and interests to potential career paths or life goals.
We conduct informational interviews with people in fields we are interested in and analyse how their personal strengths contribute to their work.
We write a reflective piece about how a challenging experience helped us discover or develop a personal strength we did not know we had.
We develop a transition plan for the next stage of our education or life, explicitly linking our strengths and interests to our chosen path.

VC2CP10S04.w: We are learning how to identify and adapt strategies for building confidence, adaptability and perseverance in response to challenges across different contexts, taking into account what helps and what gets in the way.
y: Understanding the enablers and barriers to confidence and resilience helps us take control of our own growth rather than leaving it to chance.
Being able to adapt these strategies across different contexts — school, relationships, work — makes us more versatile and resilient people.
These self-management skills are crucial during the transition to greater independence in senior school, further study and the workforce.
eg: We define protective behaviours, provide examples, and discuss how they act as enablers that help us maintain confidence and wellbeing.
We identify factors that lead to improved confidence, adaptability and self-reflection, and use this knowledge to adjust how we support ourselves and others.
We analyse how the same challenge might require different strategies in different contexts — for instance, perseverance at school versus adaptability in a part-time job.
We create a personal resilience plan that identifies our key enablers and strategies for overcoming the barriers we most commonly face.
We discuss how setbacks and failure, when processed constructively, can actually strengthen confidence and adaptability over time.
We evaluate a range of strategies for building perseverance and select those most suited to our own personality, circumstances and goals.

VC2CP10S05.w: We are learning about the importance of taking individual responsibility and being adaptable when making decisions, completing challenging tasks and planning for what comes next in our lives.
y: Taking personal responsibility for our decisions and actions is the foundation of integrity and earns the trust and respect of others.
Being adaptable in our decision-making helps us respond effectively when plans change or new information comes to light.
These qualities are essential as we take on more independence — making choices about education, career, relationships and community involvement.
eg: We create a mind map showing the links between independence, individual responsibility and the successful completion of a challenging task.
We explore the connection between the degree of responsibility we are given and our motivation to complete tasks, drawing on our own experiences.
We analyse a significant decision we need to make about our future — like subject selection or a career pathway — and work through a structured decision-making process.
We discuss how adaptability in decision-making has been important for leaders, entrepreneurs or community figures we admire.
We reflect on a time when taking individual responsibility for a mistake or setback led to a better outcome than avoiding it would have.
We develop a personal plan for an upcoming transition — like entering senior school or the workforce — that demonstrates individual responsibility and adaptability.

VC2CP10O01.w: We are learning how people's different values and beliefs lead to different perspectives on social issues, and exploring both the benefits and challenges that come from having diverse viewpoints.
y: Understanding multiple perspectives helps us engage more thoughtfully with complex social issues rather than seeing them in black and white.
Recognising how values shape perspectives makes us more effective communicators and critical thinkers in an increasingly polarised world.
Appreciating the benefits and challenges of diverse viewpoints prepares us for civic participation and leadership roles.
eg: We analyse letters to the editor on a current issue, identifying the different values expressed and how they lead to contrasting perspectives.

We participate in a hypothetical stakeholder panel where each of us represents a different perspective on a social issue, then reflect on the benefits and challenges of having to consider all viewpoints.
We explore how our own personal values influence our stance on a particular issue and consider how our perspective might look from someone else's position.
We research a social issue where divergent values have led to ongoing public debate and present the strongest arguments from multiple sides.
We discuss how exposure to diverse perspectives can strengthen our own thinking, even when — or especially when — we disagree.
We examine how media framing reflects particular values and can shape public opinion on social issues.

VC2CP10O02.w: We are learning what barriers and enablers exist for accepting diversity, how to evaluate strategies for being respectful, and how to navigate situations where different human rights and responsibilities may compete with each other.
y: Understanding the tensions between competing rights helps us engage with real-world ethical dilemmas with nuance rather than simplistic thinking.
Evaluating strategies for respecting diversity gives us practical tools for creating more inclusive communities and workplaces.
These skills are essential for informed citizenship in a multicultural democracy where balancing rights is an ongoing challenge.
eg: We discuss how human rights and responsibilities can come into tension — such as freedom of expression versus the right to feel safe — and explore strategies for managing these competing claims.
We analyse the Victorian Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities, examining the responsibilities it places on civic institutions and the challenges involved in upholding them.
We identify barriers to accepting diversity — such as fear, ignorance and historical prejudice — and evaluate strategies that communities have used to overcome them.
We debate a real-world scenario where two legitimate rights conflict and work through a structured process for finding a balanced resolution.
We research an organisation that promotes diversity and inclusion, evaluating the effectiveness of their strategies.
We reflect on our own potential biases and discuss how self-awareness is both an enabler and an ongoing practice.

VC2CP10O03.w: We are learning about the personal, social and cultural factors that shape our ability to have respectful relationships, and the rights and responsibilities that come with being in a relationship.
y: Understanding the factors that influence relationships helps us build healthier connections and recognise when a relationship may be harmful.
Knowing our rights and responsibilities in relationships empowers us to advocate for ourselves and treat others with genuine respect.
These insights prepare us for the increasingly complex interpersonal dynamics of adult life — in friendships, romantic relationships and professional settings.
eg: We discuss scenarios involving saying 'no' to a request and examine how personal, social and cultural factors influence our confidence in doing so.
We develop a list of rights and responsibilities that support respectful relationships and discuss how they apply in different contexts.
We explore how cultural expectations about relationships can create both support and pressure, and how to navigate these thoughtfully.
We analyse media representations of relationships, identifying which ones model respect and which normalise harmful dynamics.
We discuss how power imbalances — in age, social status or other factors — can affect relationship dynamics and what can be done to address them.
We create a resource for our peers about recognising the signs of respectful versus disrespectful relationships.

VC2CP10O04.w: We are learning strategies for building and managing effective teams, and how to evaluate how well a team is collaborating and recommend improvements.
y: The ability to construct, manage and evaluate teams is a leadership skill that is valued in every field and profession.
Learning to give and receive constructive feedback about collaboration helps teams grow stronger and produce better work.
These skills prepare us for collaborative environments in senior school, university, the workplace and community organisations.
eg: We reflect on each group member's contribution to a completed task and provide constructive feedback, demonstrating sensitivity to different perspectives.
We explain how clarity about roles contributes to group success and develop strategies for aligning role descriptions with both task demands and team members' skills.
