We evaluate a recent team experience using specific criteria — such as communication, task distribution, conflict resolution and outcomes — and make recommendations for improvement.
We research effective team management strategies used in professional settings and discuss which ones we could apply to our own group work.
We design a team charter before starting a major project, outlining goals, roles, expectations and processes for resolving disagreements.
We discuss what makes some teams highly effective while others struggle, drawing on examples from sport, business, science and the arts.

VC2CP10O05.w: We are learning when and how to adapt our skills and strategies to prevent, calm down or resolve conflicts that happen within or between groups, across different contexts.
y: Being able to adapt conflict resolution strategies to different contexts — school, workplace, community — makes us more versatile and effective problem-solvers.
Understanding how emotional complexity, language and power dynamics interact during conflict helps us navigate even the most challenging disagreements.
These advanced conflict management skills are essential for leadership, advocacy and maintaining healthy relationships throughout our lives.
eg: We research conflict management strategies appropriate for different contexts — home, school, community and workplace — considering how emotional complexity adds challenge.
We examine how language choices, listening skills and power dynamics can either help resolve or further entrench a conflict.
We analyse a complex real-world conflict and identify which strategies were used, which were effective, and what alternatives might have worked better.
We practise adapting our approach to conflict resolution when the context or power dynamics change — for example, mediating between peers versus raising an issue with an authority figure.
We role-play a workplace conflict scenario and develop a resolution plan that accounts for professional expectations and power differences.
We reflect on our own conflict resolution style, identifying strengths and areas for growth, and set goals for improvement.

VC2HPFAP01.w: We are learning to notice who we are.
y: Knowing who we are helps us feel safe and happy.
When we notice ourselves, we can start to know what we like.
Learning about ourselves is the beginning of understanding our world.
eg: Looking in a mirror and seeing our face.
Hearing our name and turning towards the sound.
Feeling our hands and feet during a sensory activity.
Noticing our own image in a photo.
Showing a reaction when someone says our name.
Touching different textures and noticing how they feel.

VC2HPFAP02.w: We are learning to feel our body parts moving and hear their names.
y: Knowing our body helps us understand how we move.
Hearing body part names helps us learn words for ourselves.
Feeling our body move builds our awareness of what we can do.
eg: Waving our hands when someone says 'hands'.
Kicking our legs during an activity.
Turning our head when someone touches our shoulder.
Feeling our arms stretch during a movement song.
Noticing when our toes are tickled.
Moving along to music and feeling our body respond.

VC2HPFAP03.w: We are learning to notice what the people near us are doing and saying.
y: Noticing others helps us connect with the people around us.
Paying attention to friends is how we start to play together.
Being aware of others is the first step in building friendships.
eg: Looking towards a friend who is making a sound.
Watching a peer do an action during a game.
Turning towards someone who is clapping.
Noticing when a friend picks up a toy.
Responding when a peer calls our name.
Following a friend's movements during music time.

VC2HPFAP04.w: We are learning to show what we like and what we do not like.
y: Expressing preferences helps others understand us.
Knowing what we like helps us feel in control.
Communicating our choices is important for our wellbeing.
eg: Reaching for a favourite toy.
Turning away from a food we do not like.
Smiling when we hear a favourite song.
Pushing away an object we do not want.
Making happy sounds during a preferred activity.
Showing we are unhappy when something is taken away.

VC2HPFAP05.w: We are learning to feel comfortable with safe, familiar people.
y: Feeling safe with trusted people helps us learn and grow.
Knowing who is safe builds our sense of security.
Trusting familiar people helps us try new things.
eg: Reaching for a familiar adult.
Calming down when held by a trusted person.
Smiling at a familiar teacher or carer.
Looking for a trusted person when feeling unsure.
Responding happily to a familiar voice.
Showing comfort when a favourite person is nearby.

VC2HPFAP06.w: We are learning to take part in activities that help keep us healthy and safe.
y: Healthy habits keep our bodies feeling good.
Joining in health activities helps us learn to look after ourselves.
Being part of safety routines helps protect us.
eg: Having our hands washed before eating.
Wearing a hat when going outside.
Sitting safely during snack time.
Participating in rest time.
Drinking water during the day.
Being helped to apply sunscreen.

VC2HPFAM01.w: We are learning to feel our body moving in different positions and places — inside, outside, and in water.
y: Moving our body in different ways helps us grow stronger.
Trying different environments helps us learn what our body can do.
Feeling movement builds our confidence and body awareness.
eg: Being supported to sit, lie, or stand in different positions.
Moving through water with support.
Rocking gently side to side.
Being pushed on a swing outdoors.
Rolling on a soft mat.
Feeling the sensation of sand or grass under our body.

VC2HPFAM02.w: We are learning how our body reacts to different feelings, textures, objects, and places.
y: Exploring sensations helps us understand the world around us.
Interacting with different textures builds our curiosity.
Learning how our body responds helps us feel more comfortable in new situations.
eg: Touching a soft fabric and a rough surface.
Squeezing a squishy ball.
Feeling cold water and warm water.
Holding different-sized objects.
Moving through a sensory tunnel.
Reaching for a floating object in water.

VC2HPFAM03.w: We are learning to be part of activities in outdoor and water settings.
y: Being outdoors helps us experience nature and fresh air.
Water activities help us feel comfortable in aquatic environments.
Outdoor play supports our health and happiness.
eg: Being in a garden or outdoor play area.
Sitting in shallow water with support.
Feeling the breeze outdoors.
Watching leaves or clouds while outside.
Touching plants or flowers.
Splashing gently in water.

VC2HPFAM04.w: We are learning about the space and things right around us.
y: Understanding our immediate space helps us feel safe.
Noticing what is around us builds our awareness.
Exploring our environment helps us learn about the world.
eg: Looking around our room or outdoor area.
Reaching for an object placed nearby.
Noticing sounds in our environment.
Exploring what is on our tray or desk.
Responding to changes in our space.
Following a moving object with our eyes.

VC2HPFAM05.w: We are learning to take part in planned movement activities on our own.
y: Individual activities let us practise at our own pace.
Structured movement helps us build our physical skills.
Taking part in activities helps us feel included and capable.
eg: Reaching for objects placed at different heights.
Practising sitting or standing with support.
Moving along a guided pathway.

eg: Trying a new outdoor game and talking about what made it easy or hard.

Exploring a bush trail and discussing how the terrain affected our movement.
Talking about how weather affects outdoor play.
Identifying factors like equipment, space, and rules that affect our participation.
Discussing what helps us feel safe and confident in new physical environments.

VC2HP2M04.w: We are learning to help make rules together that keep our games fair and inclusive.
y: Making rules together gives everyone a voice.
Fair rules make games more enjoyable for all players.
Learning to create and follow shared rules is an important life skill.
eg: Deciding as a class what rules we need for a new game.
Suggesting a rule change to make a game fairer.
Discussing what to do when someone breaks a rule.
Including a rule that makes sure everyone gets a turn.
Testing our rules during a game and adjusting them if needed.
Talking about why fair play matters more than winning.

VC2HP2M05.w: We are learning to use strategies that help us work together during physical activities.
y: Cooperative strategies make team activities more fun and successful.
Learning to work together builds trust and communication skills.
These teamwork skills help us in sport, school, and life.
eg: Passing the ball to a teammate who is in a better position.
Encouraging a friend who is finding an activity difficult.
Working together to come up with a team strategy.
Practising communication during a group game — calling for the ball, giving directions.
Taking on different roles in a team game.
Reflecting on what helped our team work well together.

VC2HP4P01.w: We are learning about how success, challenges, setbacks, and failure can actually make us stronger and help shape who we are.
y: Resilience means bouncing back from difficulty — and it grows every time we face a challenge.
Understanding that setbacks are a normal part of life reduces fear and builds confidence.
When we learn from failure, we develop a stronger sense of who we are.
eg: Sharing a time we tried something hard and what we learned from it.
Discussing how athletes use setbacks as motivation to improve.
Talking about how making mistakes helps us learn more than getting everything right.
Reading a story about someone who overcame a big challenge.
Reflecting on a time we failed at something and tried again.
Identifying qualities like persistence and courage that help us bounce back.

VC2HP4P02.w: We are learning to plan and practise ways to handle the different changes and transitions we go through as we grow.
y: Changes are a natural part of life — being prepared helps us handle them with confidence.
Having strategies ready makes transitions less scary and more manageable.
Reflecting on how we cope helps us improve our strategies for next time.
eg: Planning how to manage the move from Year 3 to Year 4 or to a new school.
Practising deep breathing or mindfulness to manage nervousness.
Discussing strategies for coping with changes in friendships.
Role-playing a new situation and practising what to say.
Reflecting on a transition we handled well and what helped.
Creating a personal toolkit of coping strategies.

VC2HP4P03.w: We are learning about how stereotypes can influence our choices and actions.
y: Recognising stereotypes helps us make choices based on who we really are, not what others expect.
Stereotypes can limit what people think they can do — understanding this helps us break free.
Challenging stereotypes creates a fairer world where everyone can be themselves.
eg: Discussing stereotypes about what boys and girls 'should' like or be good at.
Identifying stereotypes in advertising or TV shows.
Talking about how stereotypes can affect the activities people choose to do.
Challenging a stereotype by sharing a counter-example.
Discussing how cultural stereotypes can hurt people.
Exploring how stereotypes affect people's expectations of themselves.

VC2HP4P04.w: We are learning to choose, use, and improve personal and social skills that help us build and maintain strong relationships.
y: Strong relationships are built on skills like communication, empathy, and compromise.
Improving our social skills helps us navigate friendships more successfully.
These skills are useful in every area of life — school, home, and community.
eg: Practising active listening during a partner conversation.
Identifying a social skill we want to improve and setting a goal.
Using conflict resolution steps when we disagree with a friend.
Reflecting on how we handled a social situation and what we could do better.
Role-playing scenarios that require empathy and understanding.
Giving and receiving constructive feedback about our social interactions.

VC2HP4P05.w: We are learning about how valuing diversity helps everyone feel better and what we can do to make sure everyone is included.
y: Diversity makes our communities richer and more interesting.
When everyone feels included, wellbeing improves for all.
Taking action for inclusion is something every person can do.
eg: Discussing what diversity means — different backgrounds, abilities, cultures, and beliefs.
Identifying actions that help everyone feel welcome in our classroom.
Talking about how exclusion affects people's wellbeing.
Planning a class activity that celebrates diversity.
Discussing how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander perspectives enrich our community.
Creating a charter of inclusion for our class.

VC2HP4P06.w: We are learning that people's emotional responses can vary in different situations, and practising strategies to manage our own emotions.
y: Understanding that emotions are varied and normal helps us accept our feelings.
Emotion management strategies help us respond thoughtfully rather than impulsively.
These skills improve our relationships and our own wellbeing.
eg: Discussing why the same situation can make different people feel different emotions.
Practising calming strategies like counting to ten, deep breathing, or visualisation.
Identifying physical signs of strong emotions — like a racing heart or tight muscles.
Role-playing how to manage frustration during a game.
Keeping a feelings journal to track our emotional responses.
Discussing when it is helpful to express emotions and when to pause and think first.

VC2HP4P07.w: We are learning to get better at seeking, giving, and denying permission respectfully — and understanding why consent matters in all relationships.
y: Consent is a fundamental part of respectful relationships.
Being able to say no — and hearing no — builds trust and safety.
These skills protect us and the people around us.
eg: Practising ways to ask for permission clearly and politely.
Role-playing how to say no firmly but kindly.
Discussing why it is important to accept someone else's 'no' without pressure.
Identifying situations where seeking consent is important — borrowing, touching, sharing information.
Talking about what to do when someone does not respect our decision.
Creating a class agreement about respecting each other's boundaries.

VC2HP4P08.w: We are learning to describe and use protective behaviours and help-seeking strategies for both online and offline situations.
y: Protective behaviours keep us safe in all environments — including online.
Knowing help-seeking strategies means we are never stuck dealing with a problem alone.
These skills are increasingly important as we spend more time in digital spaces.
eg: Discussing what to do if someone online asks for personal information.
Identifying trusted adults we can go to with online or offline worries.
Practising how to report inappropriate content or behaviour online.
Role-playing protective behaviours in a scenario involving peer pressure.
Creating a safety plan for both online and offline situations.
Discussing the difference between helpful and harmful online interactions.

VC2HP4P09.w: We are learning to look at health messages in our community and think about what they are really trying to tell us.
y: Health messages are everywhere — learning to interpret them helps us make informed choices.
Not all health messages are equally reliable — thinking critically protects us.
Understanding the purpose behind messages helps us decide which ones to follow.
eg: Analysing a food advertisement and identifying what it is really trying to get us to do.
Comparing health information from different sources — school, internet, TV.
Discussing the difference between health facts and marketing claims.
Investigating who creates the health messages we see and why.
Looking at health messages aimed at different age groups and discussing the differences.
Evaluating a community health campaign and discussing whether its message is trustworthy.

VC2HP4P10.w: We are learning to investigate and try out strategies that support our own and others' health, safety, relationships, and wellbeing.
y: Strategies that we understand and practise become habits that protect us throughout life.
Helping others stay healthy and safe strengthens our whole community.
Investigating different strategies helps us find what works best for us.
eg: Researching strategies for getting a good night's sleep.
Trying different ways to manage stress and rating which works best.
Investigating how physical activity benefits mental health.
Practising strategies for online safety and teaching them to a younger student.
Developing a personal wellbeing plan that includes physical, social, and emotional strategies.
Investigating how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples' holistic health approaches include connection to Country, community, and culture.

VC2HP4M01.w: We are learning to practise and refine our fundamental movement skills in different settings — indoors, outdoors, and in water.
y: Refining our skills helps us move with more control and confidence.
Practising in different settings makes us adaptable movers.
Strong fundamental skills open the door to many sports and activities.
eg: Practising overarm throwing with increasing accuracy.
Refining catching technique using balls of different sizes.
Improving running form through drills and games.
Practising kicking for accuracy in a modified soccer game.
Working on swimming strokes in a pool setting.
Combining skills — like running and dodging — in a modified game.

VC2HP4M02.w: We are learning to practise and use basic movement strategies to achieve better results in games and activities.
y: Strategies help us play smarter, not just harder.
Applying strategies makes games more interesting and challenging.
Understanding strategies develops our tactical thinking.
eg: Using a defensive strategy in a modified invasion game.
Applying a strategy to create space during a team game.
Choosing when to pass and when to keep the ball based on the game situation.
Using positioning to gain an advantage in a net game.
Applying a batting strategy in a modified cricket game.
Discussing which strategies worked and which did not after a game.

VC2HP4M03.w: We are learning to show and explain how ideas about effort, space, time, objects, and people apply when we move.
y: Movement concepts help us understand why some movements work better than others.
Applying these concepts makes us more thoughtful and effective movers.
Understanding these ideas helps us improve our own and others' performance.
eg: Demonstrating how changing effort — from gentle to forceful — affects a throw.
Exploring how using more space changes the dynamics of a game.
Showing how timing affects a catch or a jump.
Adjusting movement based on where other people are positioned.
Experimenting with different pathways through space.
Explaining why a particular movement concept was important for success in an activity.

VC2HP4M04.w: We are learning to perform movement sequences that link fundamental movement skills together smoothly.
y: Linking movements together is how we develop fluency and coordination.
Sequences help us prepare for dance, gymnastics, and team sports.
Performing sequences builds our confidence and movement memory.
eg: Performing a gymnastics sequence that includes a roll, a balance, and a jump.
Creating a dance routine that combines different movement elements.
Linking a run-up with a jump and a safe landing.
Performing a sequence of ball skills — dribble, pass, shoot.
Designing a sequence of movements for a fitness circuit.
Performing and evaluating a partner movement sequence.

VC2HP4M05.w: We are learning to pay attention to how our body feels during exercise and describe why regular physical activity is good for us.
y: Being aware of how our body responds to exercise helps us exercise safely.
Understanding the benefits of physical activity motivates us to stay active.
Body awareness during exercise builds a lifelong relationship with movement.
eg: Noticing our heart rate increase during a running game and discussing why.
Describing how our muscles feel after a strength activity.
Discussing the mental health benefits of physical activity — like feeling happier and less stressed.
Comparing how our body feels before and after a workout.
Identifying the benefits of regular exercise — fitness, energy, sleep, mood.
Tracking how we feel after different types of physical activity.

VC2HP4M06.w: We are learning to take part in outdoor and water activities and think about what helps or hinders our participation.
y: Outdoor and aquatic activities develop unique skills and connect us with nature.
Understanding what affects participation helps us overcome barriers.
These experiences build confidence in diverse physical environments.
eg: Participating in a bushwalk and discussing what made it enjoyable or challenging.
Practising water safety skills at the pool.
Discussing how weather, equipment, and group size affect outdoor activities.
Reflecting on what helped us feel confident in a new outdoor environment.
Identifying barriers to participation and suggesting how to overcome them.
Exploring how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples' connection to Country relates to outdoor physical activity.

VC2HP4M07.w: We are learning about recommendations for how much physical activity we should do and how much sitting time is too much, and thinking about ways to be more active.
y: Knowing the guidelines helps us set realistic and healthy activity goals.
Reducing sedentary time is just as important as increasing active time.
These strategies help us build habits that support lifelong health.
eg: Learning that children need at least 60 minutes of physical activity every day.
Tracking our activity levels for a week and comparing with the guidelines.
Brainstorming ways to reduce screen time and sitting time at home.
Creating a plan to increase activity during the school day — like active breaks.
Discussing how small changes — like walking to school — add up.
Setting a personal goal to be more active and reviewing it after a week.

VC2HP4M08.w: We are learning to think creatively to solve movement problems and challenges.
y: Creative thinking in movement helps us find new solutions when the obvious approach does not work.
Problem-solving through movement builds skills we use in all areas of life.
Creativity makes physical activity more enjoyable and engaging.
eg: Working out how to get a ball past a defender using an unexpected move.
Inventing a new way to travel through an obstacle course.
Creating a game that requires a creative solution to a movement challenge.
Trying multiple approaches to a movement problem and evaluating which works best.
Designing a new movement sequence using unconventional ideas.
Collaborating with a partner to solve a movement puzzle.

VC2HP4M09.w: We are learning to use and create rules and scoring systems that make games fair and inclusive for everyone.
y: Fair rules ensure everyone gets an equal chance to participate and enjoy the game.
Inclusive design means people of all abilities can join in.
Creating rules builds our understanding of fairness and equity.
eg: Modifying a game's rules so that everyone gets equal playing time.
Designing a scoring system that rewards effort and teamwork, not just skill.
Discussing what makes a game feel unfair and how rules can fix it.
Creating a new game with built-in rules for inclusion.
Evaluating existing game rules and suggesting improvements for fairness.
Implementing a modification that allows a classmate with different abilities to participate fully.

VC2HP4M10.w: We are learning to take on different roles in group and team activities in respectful ways that help the group succeed.
y: Taking on different roles builds versatility and teamwork skills.
Respectful behaviour in teams makes the experience positive for everyone.
Understanding different roles helps us appreciate what each team member contributes.
eg: Taking a turn as team captain and encouraging all players.
Playing in different positions during a team game.
Acting as scorer, referee, or coach during a class activity.
Giving helpful feedback to teammates in a respectful way.
Supporting a teammate who is struggling rather than criticising.
Reflecting on which roles we enjoyed and why.

VC2HP6P01.w: We are learning about how the people and places around us influence our identity, and how we can build a positive view of ourselves.
y: Identity is shaped by many factors — understanding them helps us take control of our own story.
Building a positive self-identity supports our mental health and resilience.
Knowing what influences us helps us make more deliberate choices about who we want to be.
eg: Discussing how family, friends, media, and culture shape our sense of self.
Identifying strategies for building positive self-identity — like setting goals and celebrating strengths.
Exploring how connection to place — including Country for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander students — influences identity.
Analysing how media messages can both positively and negatively affect self-image.
Discussing how moving to a new place can change how we see ourselves.
Creating a personal identity map showing the influences that shape who we are.

VC2HP6P02.w: We are learning about resources and strategies that help us manage changes, including the physical and emotional changes of puberty.
y: Puberty and other major changes are easier to handle when we understand what to expect.
Having strategies and resources available reduces anxiety and builds confidence.
Managing change well is a skill that serves us throughout life.
eg: Learning about the physical changes that happen during puberty.
Discussing the emotional changes — mood swings, self-consciousness — and strategies for managing them.
Identifying trusted adults and resources we can turn to with questions.
Creating a personal toolkit of coping strategies for managing change.
Discussing how different cultures support young people through transitions.
Practising conversations about puberty using correct terminology.

VC2HP6P03.w: We are learning about how gender stereotypes can influence the roles and responsibilities people are expected to take on in society.
y: Gender stereotypes limit what people believe they can achieve — challenging them opens up possibilities.
Understanding stereotypes helps us make choices based on our interests and abilities, not expectations.
Creating a society free from limiting stereotypes benefits everyone.
eg: Discussing examples of gender stereotypes in the media — ads, movies, sport.
Analysing how stereotypes might affect career aspirations for different genders.
Exploring examples of people who have challenged traditional gender roles.
Investigating how gender stereotypes can contribute to inequality.
Discussing how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples have diverse roles within their communities.
Creating a campaign that challenges a common gender stereotype.

VC2HP6P04.w: We are learning to show respect and empathy in ways that positively influence our relationships.
y: Respect and empathy are the foundations of every healthy relationship.
Demonstrating these qualities creates a positive environment for everyone.
Practising empathy strengthens our ability to understand and connect with others.
eg: Demonstrating active listening when a friend is sharing something important.
Showing respect for someone whose views are different from ours.
Practising empathy by imagining how someone else might feel in a situation.
Discussing how respectful behaviour looks different in different cultural contexts.
Identifying the impact of disrespectful behaviour on relationships.
Role-playing respectful responses to challenging social situations.

VC2HP6P05.w: We are learning to describe and implement strategies that help communities value and celebrate diversity.
y: Actively valuing diversity creates communities where everyone can thrive.
Implementing strategies — not just talking about them — is what makes real change happen.
These skills prepare us to be leaders in creating inclusive communities.
eg: Planning an event that celebrates the cultural diversity in our school.
Implementing a strategy to include students who are often left out.
Discussing how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultural practices enrich our community.
Investigating how other schools or communities promote diversity and inclusion.
Creating resources that help classmates learn about different cultures and backgrounds.
Evaluating how well our school values diversity and suggesting improvements.

VC2HP6P06.w: We are learning to apply strategies that help us manage our emotions and thinking about how our emotional responses affect the way we interact with others.
y: Emotional regulation helps us respond thoughtfully rather than reactively.
Understanding how our emotions affect others builds stronger relationships.
These skills are essential for navigating increasingly complex social situations.
eg: Applying a calming strategy — like stepping away, breathing deeply, or journaling — when feeling overwhelmed.
Analysing how an angry response can escalate a conflict.
Discussing how different emotions can lead to different outcomes in the same situation.
Keeping an emotions log and reflecting on patterns.
Practising reframing negative thoughts into more helpful ones.
Role-playing de-escalation strategies for emotionally charged situations.

VC2HP6P07.w: We are learning strategies for seeking, giving, or denying consent, and practising how to communicate our intentions clearly.
y: Consent is about communication, respect, and understanding — skills that are essential for healthy relationships.
Being able to communicate clearly about consent protects us and the people around us.
These skills become increasingly important as our social world expands.
eg: Practising clear verbal communication — 'I am okay with that' or 'I do not want to do that'.
Discussing non-verbal cues that indicate someone is uncomfortable.
Role-playing how to check in with someone before doing something that affects them.
Discussing what freely given consent looks like versus consent given under pressure.
Identifying strategies for assertively denying consent without feeling guilty.
Discussing how consent applies in digital contexts — like sharing photos or information.

VC2HP6P08.w: We are learning to explain and practise protective behaviours and help-seeking strategies for online and offline situations.
y: Protective behaviours are our first line of defence in unsafe situations.
Help-seeking is not a sign of weakness — it is one of the most important skills we can develop.
As our online lives grow, so does the need for digital safety strategies.
eg: Identifying warning signs of unsafe situations — both online and offline.
Practising how to report cyberbullying or inappropriate content.
Creating a safety plan that includes trusted adults, helplines, and online reporting tools.
Discussing how to respond if someone asks us to keep an unsafe secret.
Investigating community resources available to young people — like Kids Helpline.
Role-playing help-seeking in a range of scenarios.

VC2HP6P09.w: We are learning to investigate different sources and types of health information and evaluate how reliable and relevant they are to our lives.
y: Health information comes from many sources — and not all of them are trustworthy.
Being able to evaluate health information protects us from misinformation.
Critical health literacy is an essential skill in a world full of competing messages.
eg: Comparing health information from a government website with information from a social media influencer.
Identifying criteria for evaluating health information — source, evidence, purpose.
Investigating who funds health campaigns and whether this affects their message.
Analysing a misleading health claim and identifying why it is unreliable.
Discussing how to find credible health information when we have a question.
Evaluating health apps and websites for accuracy and reliability.

VC2HP6P10.w: We are learning to analyse how different strategies affect the health, safety, relationships, and wellbeing of individuals and communities.
y: Understanding what works — and what does not — helps us choose strategies that genuinely make a difference.
Strategies that help individuals also strengthen communities.
Analytical skills help us contribute to health promotion in our own lives and beyond.
eg: Analysing the effectiveness of a school-based health promotion program.
Investigating how community sport programs contribute to wellbeing.
Evaluating the impact of a nutrition strategy on a community's health outcomes.
Discussing how strategies for mental health support — like school counsellors — benefit students.
Analysing a harm reduction strategy and debating its effectiveness.
Investigating how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities use holistic health approaches.

VC2HP6M01.w: We are learning to adapt our movement skills to work well in different situations — indoors, outdoors, and in water.
y: Adapting skills shows we can transfer what we know to new challenges.
Versatile movers can participate confidently in many different activities.
Adaptation is a higher-order skill that builds on our fundamental movement abilities.
eg: Adjusting our throwing technique for a different type of ball.
Adapting running technique for different surfaces — grass, court, sand.
Modifying swimming strokes for different aquatic environments.
Changing the way we catch based on the speed and trajectory of an object.
Adapting footwork for different sports — netball versus basketball.
Applying movement skills learned in one sport to a completely different activity.

VC2HP6M02.w: We are learning to transfer movement strategies we already know to new and different activities.
y: Strategy transfer shows deep understanding of how movement works.
Using familiar strategies in new situations makes us more effective participants.
This skill makes learning new sports and activities faster.
eg: Applying a defensive strategy from basketball to a different invasion game.
Using spacing principles from one game to improve performance in another.
Transferring a striking strategy from cricket to baseball.
Applying teamwork strategies from one context to a new group activity.
Recognising when a familiar strategy needs modification for a new situation.
Discussing how understanding general principles helps us across many sports.

VC2HP6M03.w: We are learning to investigate how changing effort, space, time, objects, and people affects the way we move and perform.
y: Understanding these concepts helps us make deliberate choices to improve our movement.
Investigating rather than just practising builds deeper understanding.
These concepts apply to all physical activities — from dance to sport to everyday movement.
eg: Investigating how changing the size of the playing area affects game dynamics.
Experimenting with different levels of effort in a swimming activity.
Analysing how the number of players affects the flow of a game.
Testing how different equipment (heavier bat, larger ball) changes our performance.
Exploring how timing affects success in jumping, catching, or dancing.
Drawing conclusions about which movement concepts had the biggest impact on our performance.

VC2HP6M04.w: We are learning to create and perform movement sequences that change elements like time, effort, and space.
y: Creating sequences develops both our physical and creative abilities.
Performing for others builds confidence and presentation skills.
Modifying movement elements shows sophisticated understanding of how movement works.
eg: Creating a dance routine that uses changes in speed and energy.
Designing a gymnastics sequence that moves through different levels and directions.
Performing a group routine that synchronises movements in time.
Modifying a sequence to make it more dynamic by changing effort levels.
Creating a fitness circuit that uses different movement elements.
Evaluating our performance and making improvements based on feedback.

VC2HP6M05.w: We are learning to take part in physical activities to find out how our body responds to different levels of exercise intensity.
y: Understanding our body's response to exercise helps us train more effectively.
Knowing how intensity affects fitness empowers us to make healthy choices.
Body awareness during exercise builds a lifelong foundation for physical health.
eg: Comparing how our heart rate responds to light, moderate, and vigorous exercise.
Noticing how our breathing changes at different intensity levels.
Tracking how we feel during and after high-intensity versus low-intensity activities.
Investigating the concept of the 'talk test' to gauge exercise intensity.
Discussing why different types of exercise — aerobic, strength, flexibility — affect our body differently.
Setting a goal based on our understanding of how our body responds to exercise.

VC2HP6M06.w: We are learning to take part in outdoor and water activities that are good for our health and wellbeing.
y: Outdoor and aquatic activities offer unique physical, mental, and social benefits.
These experiences build skills and confidence in diverse environments.
Connecting with nature through physical activity supports holistic wellbeing.
eg: Participating in orienteering or bushwalking.
Practising water safety and rescue skills.
Engaging in outdoor team-building activities.
Reflecting on how outdoor activities make us feel compared to indoor ones.
Discussing how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples' connection to Country is expressed through physical activity on the land.
Evaluating the health and wellbeing benefits of regular outdoor physical activity.

VC2HP6M07.w: We are learning to explore strategies that help us move more and sit less in our everyday lives.
y: Small changes in daily habits can significantly improve our health.
Understanding how to reduce sedentary time is particularly relevant in a screen-based world.
These strategies build healthy habits that last a lifetime.
eg: Designing a 'move more' challenge for our class.
Tracking screen time for a week and identifying opportunities to be more active.
Investigating the health risks of too much sitting.
Creating a daily plan that includes active breaks.
Designing a walking or cycling route to school.
Evaluating which strategies are most realistic and sustainable for our lifestyle.

VC2HP6M08.w: We are learning to predict and test whether different skills and strategies will work in different movement situations.
y: Predicting and testing builds scientific thinking in a physical context.
Testing our predictions helps us learn what works and what needs adjustment.
This trial-and-error approach develops resilience and analytical thinking.
eg: Predicting which strategy would be most effective in a game and testing it.
Trying different approaches to a movement challenge and comparing results.
Testing whether a specific skill transfers effectively to a new activity.
Evaluating our predictions after testing and adjusting our approach.
Discussing with a partner what we predicted, what happened, and why.
Using evidence from our testing to recommend a strategy to others.

VC2HP6M09.w: We are learning to create and test changes to rules and equipment that make games fairer and more inclusive.
y: Inclusive design ensures everyone can participate and enjoy physical activity.
Testing modifications teaches us about the impact of rules on the game experience.
These skills develop our understanding of equity and access in sport.
eg: Modifying a game so that players of different abilities can all participate meaningfully.
Testing a rule change and evaluating whether it improved fairness.
Redesigning equipment requirements to be more inclusive.
Collecting feedback from players about whether modifications improved their experience.
Investigating how para-sports modify rules and equipment for inclusion.
Creating a completely new game designed with inclusion as a primary principle.

VC2HP6M10.w: We are learning to be a positive group member by contributing, encouraging others, and negotiating to make activities work well.
y: Positive group participation makes physical activity more enjoyable for everyone.
Encouraging others builds a supportive team culture.
Negotiation skills help groups manage disagreements constructively.
eg: Encouraging a teammate who is struggling with an activity.
Contributing ideas during a team planning discussion.
Negotiating a compromise when team members disagree about a strategy.
Taking on different roles within a group — leader, supporter, mediator.
Giving constructive feedback to help the group improve.
Reflecting on our contribution to the group and setting goals for improvement.

VC2HP8P01.w: We are learning to analyse and reflect on how our values and beliefs influence the development of our identities.
y: Values and beliefs are core to who we are — understanding them helps us navigate identity with greater self-awareness.
Reflecting on these influences helps us make more conscious choices about the person we want to be.
This analysis builds the emotional intelligence needed for healthy relationships and personal growth.
eg: Identifying the values and beliefs that are most important to us and where they come from.
Analysing how cultural, family, or religious values have shaped our identity.
Reflecting on how our values influence our behaviour, choices, and relationships.
Discussing how values can change over time and what triggers those changes.
Examining how conflicting values can create internal tension and how to manage it.
Exploring how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander values of connection to Country, community, and culture shape identity.

VC2HP8P02.w: We are learning to analyse the impact of life changes and transitions, and to develop strategies to support ourselves and others through them.
y: Major transitions — like starting secondary school, changing friendships, or dealing with loss — are some of life's most challenging moments.
Having strategies and support systems makes us more resilient during difficult times.
Supporting others through transitions builds empathy and strengthens community.
eg: Analysing the impact of transitioning to a new school — academically, socially, and emotionally.
Developing a support strategy for someone going through a family change.
Discussing how the transition through puberty affects identity and relationships.
Creating a resource that lists supports available for young people during difficult transitions.
Examining how different cultures support young people through major life changes.
Reflecting on a transition we have experienced and evaluating what helped us through it.

VC2HP8P03.w: We are learning to examine how roles, decision-making, and power dynamics in relationships can affect the health and wellbeing of the people involved.
y: Power imbalances in relationships can be harmful — recognising them is the first step to addressing them.
Understanding coercion helps us identify unhealthy relationship patterns.
These insights are essential for building and maintaining safe, respectful relationships.
eg: Examining how power operates in different types of relationships — peer, romantic, family, online.
Analysing scenarios that illustrate healthy versus unhealthy decision-making dynamics.
Discussing how coercion and control can be subtle and hard to recognise.
Identifying strategies for responding to power imbalances in relationships.
Examining how gender, age, or social status can create power dynamics.
Discussing what respectful, equitable decision-making looks like in relationships.

VC2HP8P04.w: We are learning to examine how respect, empathy, power, and coercion interact in building or damaging respectful relationships.
y: Respect and empathy strengthen relationships, while power and coercion undermine them.
Understanding these dynamics helps us recognise red flags in our own and others' relationships.
These insights are fundamental for personal safety and healthy relationship development.
eg: Analysing how respect and empathy are demonstrated in healthy relationships.
Examining case studies where power and coercion damaged a relationship.
Discussing how to respond when we recognise coercive behaviour in our lives.
Investigating how community organisations support people in unhealthy relationships.
Role-playing assertive responses to controlling behaviour.
Creating a visual guide to the characteristics of healthy versus unhealthy relationships.

VC2HP8P05.w: We are learning to investigate strategies that help communities value diversity, and to propose actions we can take to promote inclusion.
y: Active inclusion requires deliberate strategies — good intentions alone are not enough.
Proposing actions develops our ability to create positive change in our communities.
Valuing diversity strengthens communities by drawing on the full range of human experience.
eg: Investigating a community program that promotes cultural understanding and evaluating its effectiveness.
Proposing an action our school could take to better support students from diverse backgrounds.
Examining how anti-discrimination laws aim to protect diversity.
Discussing strategies for challenging prejudice and discrimination when we encounter them.
Investigating how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities advocate for cultural recognition.
Designing a diversity and inclusion initiative for our school.

VC2HP8P06.w: We are learning to analyse the factors that influence our emotional responses and develop strategies to manage our emotions effectively.
y: Understanding what triggers our emotions gives us greater control over our reactions.
Effective emotion management improves our relationships, academic performance, and overall wellbeing.
Self-management of emotions is a key component of emotional intelligence.
eg: Identifying the triggers, thoughts, and physical sensations that accompany strong emotions.
Analysing how context — stress, tiredness, social pressure — influences emotional reactions.
Developing personalised strategies for managing anger, anxiety, or disappointment.
Practising mindfulness techniques and evaluating their effectiveness.
Discussing how unmanaged emotions can escalate conflicts.
Creating an emotional management plan for high-pressure situations like exams or competitions.

VC2HP8P07.w: We are learning to explain and apply strategies for communicating assertively and respectfully when seeking, giving, or denying consent.
y: Assertive communication about consent is essential for personal safety and respectful relationships.
These skills protect both ourselves and others in increasingly complex social situations.
Understanding consent deeply — beyond just 'yes' and 'no' — builds mature, ethical relationships.
eg: Practising assertive communication techniques — clear language, confident body language, calm tone.
Discussing the difference between assertive, aggressive, and passive communication.
Analysing scenarios where consent was or was not given freely.
Role-playing how to check in with someone about consent in various contexts.
Discussing how technology changes the context of consent — digital images, online interactions.
Creating educational materials about consent for younger students.

VC2HP8P08.w: We are learning to refine our protective behaviours and evaluate community resources available for help-seeking.
y: Refining protective behaviours as we mature helps us respond to increasingly complex risks.
Knowing what community resources exist — and evaluating their quality — ensures we can access real help.
These skills are literally life-saving in some situations.
eg: Evaluating the effectiveness of different help-seeking resources — helplines, counsellors, online services.
Refining our understanding of warning signs in relationships and situations.
Investigating local community resources for mental health, family violence, or substance use.
Practising how to support a friend who discloses they need help.
Discussing barriers to help-seeking — stigma, embarrassment, lack of awareness — and how to overcome them.
Creating a community resource guide for young people in our area.

VC2HP8P09.w: We are learning to investigate how media and influential people in our community shape attitudes, beliefs, decisions, and behaviours about health.
y: Media and influencers shape our health attitudes more than we often realise — understanding this gives us more control.
Critical media literacy is essential for making genuinely informed health decisions.
Recognising influence helps us evaluate whether the health messages we receive are trustworthy.
eg: Analysing how social media influencers promote health products or behaviours.
Investigating how advertising shapes attitudes towards body image, alcohol, or food.
Examining the role of public health campaigns in changing community behaviour.
Discussing how peer influence and community role models affect health decisions.
Evaluating the reliability of health information from different media sources.
Creating a critical analysis of a health-related media campaign.

VC2HP8P10.w: We are learning to plan and implement strategies that improve our own and others' health, safety, relationships, and wellbeing.
y: Moving from knowing about health to taking action is the critical step.
Implementing strategies builds our confidence and practical skills.
Helping others strengthens our community and deepens our own understanding.
eg: Designing and implementing a health promotion activity for our school.
Creating a personal wellbeing plan with specific, measurable goals.
Planning a peer education session on a health topic relevant to our age group.
Implementing a strategy to improve physical activity levels in our school community.
Evaluating the success of our implemented strategy and adjusting it based on feedback.
Investigating how community health programs are designed and implemented.

VC2HP8M01.w: We are learning to refine and transfer our movement skills across a variety of challenging situations — indoors, outdoors, and in water.
y: Refining skills builds technical proficiency that translates across multiple sports and activities.
Transferring skills to new contexts demonstrates adaptability and deep movement understanding.
Challenging situations push us beyond our comfort zone and accelerate growth.
eg: Refining a specific skill — like a basketball lay-up or a swimming tumble turn — through targeted practice.
Transferring striking skills from one sport to another.
Adapting movement skills for a challenging outdoor environment.
Applying familiar skills in a competitive game setting.
Refining technique based on feedback from a teacher or peer.
Demonstrating how skills learned in one context apply in a completely different one.

VC2HP8M02.w: We are learning to design and demonstrate movement strategies that can be adjusted to improve performance outcomes.
y: Strategic thinking elevates our physical performance beyond raw skill.
Designing strategies develops our analytical and creative thinking.
Understanding how to manipulate strategies makes us more adaptable and effective participants.
eg: Designing an offensive strategy for an invasion game and testing it.
Demonstrating how changing a defensive formation improves team performance.
Adjusting a strategy mid-game based on what the opposition is doing.
Analysing video of our performance to identify strategic improvements.
Comparing the effectiveness of different strategies for the same game situation.
Presenting our strategy design and its rationale to the class.

VC2HP8M03.w: We are learning to demonstrate and explain how movement concepts about effort, space, time, objects, and people can enhance our physical performance.
y: Explicit understanding of movement concepts makes us more intentional and effective movers.
Being able to explain these concepts shows deep understanding, not just physical ability.
This knowledge helps us analyse and improve both our own and others' performances.
eg: Explaining how adjusting effort level changes the effectiveness of a sporting action.
Demonstrating how using space strategically creates opportunities in team games.
Analysing how timing affects the success of a sequence of movements.
Discussing how awareness of other players improves decision-making during a game.
Applying movement concepts to improve a gymnastics or dance routine.
Coaching a peer using movement concept terminology to help them improve.

VC2HP8M04.w: We are learning to adapt and perform movement sequences in different contexts, showing how we can vary time, effort, and space.
y: Adapting sequences for different contexts shows creative and technical versatility.
Performance skills build confidence and communication through movement.
Varying movement elements demonstrates sophisticated physical literacy.
eg: Performing the same dance sequence at different tempos.
Adapting a gymnastics routine for a smaller or larger performance space.
Varying the effort dynamics in a movement composition for artistic effect.
Performing sequences in different contexts — indoor stage versus outdoor field.
Receiving and applying feedback to improve the quality of a performance.
Creating a movement composition that deliberately manipulates time, effort, and space.

VC2HP8M05.w: We are learning to participate in fitness activities and investigate how regular physical activity affects our health and fitness over time.
y: Understanding the link between regular activity and fitness empowers us to take charge of our health.
Investigation — not just participation — builds evidence-based understanding of fitness.
These insights help us make informed decisions about our physical activity habits.
eg: Participating in a fitness program and tracking our progress over several weeks.
Investigating how cardiovascular, strength, and flexibility training each affect the body differently.
Comparing fitness data before and after a period of regular training.
Discussing the mental health benefits — reduced anxiety, improved mood — of regular exercise.
Analysing how different types of activity contribute to different components of fitness.
Designing a fitness investigation with pre- and post-testing.

VC2HP8M06.w: We are learning to participate in physical activities in community spaces, outdoor environments, and aquatic settings that promote health, safety, and social outcomes.
y: Engaging with diverse physical environments builds well-rounded physical competence.
Community-based physical activity strengthens social connections and belonging.
These experiences develop risk management skills and environmental awareness.
eg: Participating in community sport or recreation programs.
Engaging in outdoor adventure activities like rock climbing, kayaking, or orienteering.
Practising and demonstrating water safety skills in aquatic settings.
Reflecting on the social benefits of participating in community physical activities.
Investigating how access to community sport facilities varies across different areas.
Discussing how connecting with nature through outdoor physical activity supports wellbeing.

VC2HP8M07.w: We are learning to design and justify a physical activity plan that includes strategies for being more active and spending less time sitting.
y: Personalised planning puts us in control of our own health and fitness.
Justifying our plan develops evidence-based reasoning skills.
These planning skills transfer to many areas of life — study, career, personal goals.
eg: Designing a weekly physical activity plan tailored to our interests, abilities, and schedule.
Justifying our choice of activities using knowledge of fitness components and health guidelines.
Including strategies for reducing sedentary time — active transport, standing breaks, screen time limits.
Setting SMART goals for our physical activity plan.
Implementing the plan for two weeks and evaluating its effectiveness.
Adjusting our plan based on what we learned during implementation.

VC2HP8M08.w: We are learning to propose, test, and evaluate movement strategies and skills to determine what is most effective in different situations.
y: Systematic testing and evaluation builds a scientific approach to movement improvement.
This process develops our capacity for evidence-based decision-making.
Understanding what works — and why — makes us more strategic and effective movers.
eg: Proposing a strategy for a game, testing it in practice, and evaluating the results.
Comparing the effectiveness of different techniques for the same skill.
Testing a skill in varying conditions — different opponents, environments, or pressure levels.
Using video analysis to evaluate our movement strategies.
Evaluating whether a strategy was effective and suggesting modifications.
Presenting our findings — what we proposed, tested, and concluded — to the class.

VC2HP8M09.w: We are learning to analyse modifications to equipment, rules, and scoring that support fair play and inclusive participation.
y: Fair play and inclusion are fundamental values in sport and physical activity.
Analysing modifications develops our understanding of equity versus equality.
These analytical skills help us advocate for inclusive practices in all contexts.
eg: Analysing how a rule modification changes the fairness of a game.
Evaluating equipment modifications used in para-sports and adaptive activities.
Discussing how scoring systems can be designed to reward effort and improvement, not just winning.
Investigating how different sports organisations promote inclusive participation.
Proposing and evaluating a modification to make a class activity more inclusive.
Comparing the inclusiveness of two different versions of the same game.

VC2HP8M10.w: We are learning to practise and apply leadership, collaboration, and group decision-making during physical activities.
y: Leadership and collaboration are essential for success in team settings — and in life.
Practising these skills in physical activity builds our confidence to lead and collaborate in all contexts.
Effective group decision-making leads to better outcomes and more positive experiences for everyone.
eg: Taking a leadership role in organising a team strategy.
Facilitating group decision-making during a game or activity.
Collaborating with teammates to solve a tactical problem.
Providing constructive feedback to peers in a leadership capacity.
Reflecting on our leadership style and its impact on the group.
Evaluating the effectiveness of group decision-making processes during physical activities.

VC2HP10P01.w: We are learning to evaluate the factors that shape our identities and to assess how we influence the identities of others.
y: Identity formation is complex and ongoing — evaluation helps us understand the forces at work.
Recognising our influence on others' identities carries responsibility and power.
This self-awareness is essential for ethical, empathetic relationships.
eg: Evaluating how social media, peer groups, family, and culture shape our sense of self.
Analysing how our words and actions can positively or negatively affect others' self-concept.
Discussing how systemic factors — like racism, sexism, or ableism — shape identity.
Examining how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander identities are strengthened through connection to Country, culture, and community.
Evaluating the impact of bullying or social exclusion on identity development.
Reflecting on how we can use our influence to support others' positive identity development.

VC2HP10P02.w: We are learning to refine, evaluate, and adapt our strategies for managing life's changes and transitions.
y: As life becomes more complex, our coping strategies need to evolve too.
Evaluating what works — and what does not — builds adaptive resilience.
These skills prepare us for the significant transitions ahead — further education, employment, independence.
eg: Evaluating the effectiveness of our current coping strategies and identifying areas for improvement.
Adapting strategies when moving from secondary school to further study or work.
Refining approaches to managing stress, grief, or relationship changes.
Developing a personalised transition plan for a significant upcoming change.
Discussing how different people use different strategies based on their personality and circumstances.
Sharing refined strategies with peers to build a collective toolkit.

VC2HP10P03.w: We are learning to investigate how gender equality and challenging gender assumptions can prevent violence and abuse in relationships.
y: Gender inequality is a root cause of relationship violence — understanding this connection is essential for prevention.
Challenging harmful gender norms creates safer relationships and communities for everyone.
This knowledge empowers us to be part of the solution to a significant social issue.
eg: Investigating the link between rigid gender norms and rates of family and relationship violence.
Examining how attitudes about masculinity and femininity can contribute to or prevent violence.
Discussing the role of bystanders in challenging violent or abusive behaviour.
Analysing how community campaigns promote gender equality to prevent violence.
Investigating the impact of respectful relationships education programs.
Discussing how intersecting factors — gender, culture, sexuality, disability — affect experiences of violence.

VC2HP10P04.w: We are learning to evaluate how respect, empathy, power, and coercion affect whether relationships remain healthy and respectful.
y: Evaluating relationship dynamics helps us recognise both healthy and harmful patterns.
Understanding how power and coercion operate in relationships is essential for personal safety.
These evaluative skills protect us and equip us to support others.
eg: Evaluating case studies that illustrate the spectrum from respectful to coercive relationships.
Analysing how power imbalances develop and escalate in relationships.
Discussing how technology — monitoring, controlling, or sharing without consent — can be used coercively.
Evaluating strategies for maintaining equality and respect in romantic relationships.
Investigating how community services support people experiencing coercive control.
Developing criteria for evaluating the health of our own relationships.

VC2HP10P05.w: We are learning to propose strategies and actions that individuals and groups can take to challenge biases, stereotypes, and discrimination.
y: Moving from awareness to action is essential for creating a more just society.
Proposing strategies develops our advocacy and leadership skills.
Challenging discrimination requires both courage and practical knowledge.
eg: Proposing a strategy for addressing bias in our school community.
Investigating effective anti-discrimination campaigns and identifying what made them work.
Discussing the role of allyship in challenging stereotypes and discrimination.
Examining how institutional policies can address systemic bias.
Creating a proposal for a diversity and inclusion initiative.
Evaluating the potential impact and feasibility of our proposed strategies.

VC2HP10P06.w: We are learning to evaluate our emotional responses in different situations and refine our strategies for managing emotions to promote positive outcomes.
y: Advanced emotional management is a cornerstone of mental health and relationship success.
Evaluating our responses — not just reacting — demonstrates emotional maturity.
Refined strategies help us navigate the increasingly complex emotional landscape of adolescence and young adulthood.
eg: Evaluating how we respond emotionally to stress, conflict, or disappointment and identifying patterns.
Refining our emotion management strategies based on what has worked and what has not.
Discussing how emotional regulation affects academic performance, relationships, and wellbeing.
Analysing how context — fatigue, pressure, social dynamics — influences our emotional responses.
Developing a personal toolkit of refined emotional management strategies.
Evaluating the effectiveness of different approaches — mindfulness, cognitive reframing, physical activity.

VC2HP10P07.w: We are learning to examine how strategies like communicating choices, seeking and giving consent, and expressing needs contribute to healthy, respectful relationships.
y: These strategies are the practical building blocks of consent and respect in relationships.
Examining how they work helps us apply them more effectively in our own lives.
Understanding consent deeply prepares us for the complex relationship dynamics of adulthood.
eg: Examining how clear communication about choices prevents misunderstandings in relationships.
Analysing scenarios where consent was negotiated effectively versus where it was not.
Discussing how expressing needs assertively strengthens rather than threatens relationships.
Investigating how consent education programs aim to prevent relationship harm.
Role-playing complex consent scenarios and evaluating different approaches.
Evaluating our own communication skills and identifying areas for development.

VC2HP10P08.w: We are learning to plan, practise, and evaluate strategies for managing situations where our safety or others' safety is at risk — including first aid and CPR.
y: Being prepared for emergencies can literally save lives.
First aid and CPR are practical skills that every young person should have.
Evaluating our preparedness helps us identify gaps and build confidence.
eg: Learning and practising CPR techniques on a training manikin.
Practising the DRSABCD action plan for emergency response.
Rehearsing what to do in scenarios like anaphylaxis, asthma attack, or choking.
Evaluating our confidence and competence in first aid situations.
Planning how to respond to different types of emergencies — from sports injuries to mental health crises.
Creating an emergency action plan for our school or community group.

VC2HP10P09.w: We are learning to critically evaluate health information, services, and media messages about relationships, lifestyle choices, health, and wellbeing.
y: Critical evaluation protects us from misinformation that could harm our health and relationships.
Media literacy about health is essential in a world saturated with competing messages.
These skills empower us to make genuinely informed decisions about our own health and wellbeing.
eg: Critiquing a social media health trend and evaluating the evidence behind it.
Analysing how media messages about body image, alcohol, or relationships can be misleading.
Evaluating the quality of health services available to young people in our community.
Investigating how commercial interests influence health messaging.
Comparing health information from peer-reviewed sources with that from social media influencers.
Developing criteria for evaluating the credibility of health information and services.

VC2HP10P10.w: We are learning to plan, justify, and critically evaluate strategies that enhance our own, others', and our community's health, safety, relationships, and wellbeing.
y: Planning and justifying health strategies develops our capacity for evidence-based advocacy.
Critical evaluation ensures our strategies are genuinely effective, not just well-intentioned.
Community-level thinking demonstrates our understanding that health is a shared responsibility.
eg: Designing a community health promotion project and justifying our approach with evidence.
Critically evaluating an existing health program and recommending improvements.
Planning a peer education initiative on a health topic relevant to our school community.
Justifying our chosen strategies using health research and data.
Evaluating the impact of our implemented strategies and identifying lessons learned.
Investigating how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health models — like social and emotional wellbeing frameworks — inform community-level strategies.

VC2HP10M01.w: We are learning to analyse, apply, and adapt our movement skills in challenging and complex physical activity situations.
y: Advanced movement competence requires the ability to analyse and adapt in real time.
Challenging situations push us to apply skills under pressure — building resilience and technical ability.
These higher-order movement skills prepare us for lifelong participation in sport and physical activity.
eg: Analysing our technique in a specific sport and identifying areas for refinement.
Adapting skills mid-game in response to opponents' tactics.
Applying skills in a new and challenging context — like a competition or unfamiliar environment.
Using video analysis to break down and improve our movement performance.
Receiving and applying coaching feedback to enhance our skill execution.
Demonstrating how we adapt our skills when conditions change — weather, surface, equipment.

VC2HP10M02.w: We are learning to create and refine movement strategies to achieve successful outcomes across a range of challenging activities.
y: Strategic thinking is what separates competent participants from truly effective ones.
Refining strategies based on experience and feedback builds adaptive expertise.
These skills develop our capacity for critical and creative thinking in physical contexts.
eg: Creating a game plan for a competitive team sport and refining it through practice.
Developing an individual performance strategy — like a race plan or competition approach.
Refining strategies based on analysis of what worked and what did not.
Analysing opponents' strategies to develop effective counter-strategies.
Creating innovative strategies that exploit opportunities in the game situation.
Evaluating the effectiveness of our strategies and presenting our analysis.

VC2HP10M03.w: We are learning to apply movement concepts in new or challenging situations and analyse their impact on our performance.
y: Deep understanding of movement concepts enables us to perform at our best in any situation.
Analysing the impact of our choices builds metacognitive awareness of our own physicality.
This analytical approach to movement is what drives continuous improvement.
eg: Applying effort concepts to improve power or control in a specific skill.
Analysing how spatial awareness influences our effectiveness in a team game.
Applying time concepts to improve the rhythm and flow of a performance.
Evaluating how our understanding of movement concepts has improved our performance over time.
Discussing how elite athletes apply movement concepts at the highest level.
Creating a presentation that explains how movement concepts improved our performance in a specific activity.

VC2HP10M04.w: We are learning to evaluate our own and others' movement compositions and provide constructive feedback to enhance performance quality.
y: Evaluation skills help us see movement with a critical, discerning eye.
Giving and receiving constructive feedback is essential for improvement.
These skills prepare us for roles as coaches, teachers, or informed supporters of physical activity.
eg: Evaluating a peer's dance, gymnastics, or movement sequence using specific criteria.
Providing constructive feedback that identifies strengths and specific areas for improvement.
Applying feedback we receive to enhance our own performance.
Developing evaluation criteria for a specific type of movement composition.
Using video to evaluate performances and identify opportunities for improvement.
Reflecting on how giving and receiving feedback has improved our performance.

VC2HP10M05.w: We are learning to participate in and evaluate physical activities designed to improve our health, fitness, and wellbeing.
y: Active participation combined with evaluation builds evidence-based understanding of fitness.
Evaluating activities helps us identify what is most effective for our individual needs.
These skills support lifelong engagement with physical activity for health.
eg: Participating in a range of fitness activities and evaluating their effectiveness for different fitness components.
Comparing high-intensity interval training with steady-state cardio and evaluating the benefits of each.
Evaluating how a specific activity program affected our fitness, mood, and wellbeing over time.
Investigating the evidence behind popular fitness trends.
Designing and evaluating a personal fitness program.
Discussing how physical activity supports mental health and presenting evidence.

VC2HP10M06.w: We are learning to participate in outdoor and aquatic physical activities that promote health, safety, and positive social outcomes.
y: Outdoor and aquatic activities develop unique physical skills while building connection to environment and community.
These experiences prepare us for lifelong recreation and adventure in natural settings.
Social outcomes — teamwork, trust, communication — are often strongest in challenging outdoor environments.
eg: Participating in outdoor adventure activities — bushwalking, climbing, paddling — with attention to safety.
Demonstrating and applying water safety and rescue skills.
Reflecting on how outdoor activities build teamwork and trust.
Evaluating the health and social benefits of participation in outdoor recreation.
Investigating how outdoor education connects with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander relationships with Country.
Planning and participating in a community-based outdoor physical activity event.

VC2HP10M07.w: We are learning to design, implement, and evaluate personalised plans for improving or maintaining our own or others' physical activity levels.
y: Personalised planning ensures our activity program suits our individual goals, interests, and circumstances.
Implementation and evaluation complete the cycle from planning to action to reflection.
These self-management skills are directly applicable to adult health and fitness.
eg: Designing a comprehensive physical activity plan that addresses fitness, health, and wellbeing goals.
Implementing the plan over several weeks and tracking progress systematically.
Evaluating the plan's effectiveness and making evidence-based adjustments.
Designing a plan for someone else — a family member or peer — based on their specific needs.
Presenting our plan, implementation, and evaluation findings.
Reflecting on the factors that supported or hindered our plan's success.

VC2HP10M08.w: We are learning to transfer and adapt skills and strategies from past experiences to create successful outcomes in new situations.
y: Transfer and adaptation are hallmarks of truly versatile, skilled movers.
Drawing on past experience accelerates learning in new contexts.
This skill prepares us for the variety of physical challenges we will encounter throughout life.
eg: Applying strategies learned in one sport to a different sport with similar tactical demands.
Adapting skills from training to competitive game situations.
Transferring leadership and teamwork skills from physical activity to other contexts.
Identifying which past experiences are most relevant to a new challenge.
Discussing how adaptability and transfer are valued in elite sport.
Reflecting on how our past experiences have prepared us for a recent physical challenge.

VC2HP10M09.w: We are learning to demonstrate fair play and reflect on how ethical behaviour influences outcomes and experiences in physical activity.
y: Fair play is a value that extends far beyond sport — it shapes the kind of people and communities we become.
Ethical behaviour creates positive experiences that encourage lifelong participation.
Reflecting on ethics develops our moral reasoning and sense of responsibility.
eg: Demonstrating fair play in a competitive situation — even when it is difficult.
Reflecting on a situation where fair play was or was not upheld and its impact on participants.
Discussing how doping, cheating, and poor sportsmanship undermine sport.
Analysing how ethical behaviour creates a more positive environment for all participants.
Investigating how sporting codes of conduct promote fair play.
Presenting a case study on ethical behaviour in sport and its impact on outcomes.

VC2HP10M10.w: We are learning to develop, implement, and refine strategies for decision-making in group and team settings that show leadership, collaboration, and fair play.
y: Effective group decision-making is one of the most valuable skills we can develop.
Leadership and collaboration look different in every context — practising them in sport builds versatile interpersonal skills.
Refining our approach based on experience demonstrates growth and maturity.
eg: Developing a team decision-making process for a sport or activity and implementing it.
Demonstrating leadership by facilitating fair and inclusive group decisions.
Refining our approach to collaboration based on feedback and reflection.
Evaluating how our decision-making strategy affected the team's performance and experience.
Discussing the balance between leadership and collaboration in effective teams.
Reflecting on our growth as a leader and collaborator over the course of the year.

VC2TDE2S01.w: We learn how everyday products and places are designed by people to meet needs.
y: Everything around us was designed by someone to solve a problem or meet a need.
Understanding design helps us see the world with curious, creative eyes.
Knowing how things are designed helps us become designers too.
eg: We look at things in our classroom — chairs, bags, cups — and talk about why they are shaped that way.
We notice how a raincoat is designed to keep us dry.
We explore how a playground is designed for fun and safety.
We talk about who designed the things we use and why.
We look at how packaging protects the food inside it.
We think about how designs can be different in different places around the world.

VC2TDE2C01.w: We explore how things move in products and machines.
y: Understanding movement helps us see how everyday things work.
We learn to notice clever designs that make things move.
Knowing about movement helps us make our own inventions.
eg: We look at how wheels help a toy car roll across the floor.
We explore how a see-saw goes up and down when we push.
We try making a simple machine that moves, like a windmill.
We notice how a door handle turns to open and close.
We explore how a ramp makes it easier to move things up.
We build something with moving parts using blocks or craft materials.

VC2TDE2C02.w: We explore how plants and animals are grown for food, clothing and shelter.
y: Knowing where food and materials come from helps us understand the world.
We learn to appreciate the work that goes into growing things.
Understanding where things come from helps us make good choices.
eg: We plant seeds and watch them grow into vegetables we can eat.
We learn that wool comes from sheep and cotton comes from plants.
We visit a school garden and help look after the plants.
We talk about how milk comes from cows before it reaches the shop.
We explore how wood from trees is used to build houses.
We learn about how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples have grown and harvested food for thousands of years.

VC2TDE2C03.w: We explore how to choose and prepare food for healthy eating.
