y: Knowing about healthy food helps us grow strong and feel good.
Learning to prepare food is a useful skill we will use every day.
Good food choices keep our bodies and brains working well.
eg: We sort foods into groups — fruits, vegetables, grains, dairy, protein.
We wash and prepare a simple fruit salad together.
We talk about which foods give us energy and which help us grow.
We taste different fruits and vegetables and describe how they taste.
We learn about safe food handling like washing our hands before cooking.
We make a healthy sandwich and practise spreading and cutting safely.

VC2TDE2C04.w: We explore what materials are like and how they are used to make things.
y: Understanding materials helps us choose the right ones for building things.
We learn that different materials have different strengths and uses.
Exploring materials is the first step in becoming a designer or inventor.
eg: We feel different materials — wood, fabric, plastic, metal — and describe them.
We test which materials bend easily and which are stiff.
We explore which materials soak up water and which keep water out.
We choose materials for a project based on what they need to do.
We compare paper, cardboard and plastic to see which is strongest.
We sort materials by their properties — hard, soft, smooth, rough.

VC2TDE2D01.w: We explore what people need, and the tools and materials for designing solutions.
y: Understanding what people need is the starting point for all good design.
Learning about tools and materials helps us make our ideas real.
Exploring the design process teaches us to solve problems step by step.
eg: We think about a problem — like keeping a drink cold — and brainstorm solutions.
We explore different tools like scissors, rulers and tape and learn how to use them safely.
We look at materials we could use and think about which ones would work best.
We ask our classmates what they need and design something to help.
We look at how things are already made and think about how to improve them.
We explore simple processes like cutting, joining, folding and shaping.

VC2TDE2D02.w: We explore and share our design ideas through drawing, describing or modelling.
y: Sharing our ideas helps others understand what we want to make.
Drawing and modelling lets us test our ideas before building them.
Communication is a key part of working as a designer.
eg: We draw a picture of something we want to make and label the parts.
We use building blocks or playdough to model our design idea.
We describe our idea to a friend and explain how it would work.
We use a tablet to take photos of our model from different angles.
We make a simple plan showing what we need and the steps to follow.
We share our design with the class and ask for suggestions.

VC2TDE2D03.w: We use materials, tools and techniques to safely make our designs.
y: Making things with our hands builds skills we will use forever.
Working safely means we can create without getting hurt.
Turning our designs into real objects is exciting and rewarding.
eg: We use scissors safely to cut out shapes for our project.
We join materials together using glue, tape or string.
We follow our plan step by step to build our design.
We learn to hold tools correctly and use them carefully.
We work with a partner to build something together.
We make a simple product like a puppet, a container or a shelter for a toy.

VC2TDE2D04.w: We choose our best design ideas based on what we like and what is good for the environment.
y: Thinking about what works well helps us improve our designs.
Considering the environment teaches us to design responsibly.
Making choices is an important part of the design process.
eg: We look at our designs and choose the one we think works best.
We think about whether our design uses materials wisely or wastes them.
We ask 'Could we reuse or recycle these materials?'.
We compare two design ideas and talk about the pros and cons.
We choose materials that are kind to the environment when we can.
We explain to a friend why we picked our favourite design.

VC2TDE2D05.w: We put the steps for making our designs in the right order and work together.
y: Planning the order of steps helps our project go smoothly.
Working together teaches us teamwork and cooperation.
Sequencing is a skill we use in making things and in everyday life.
eg: We number the steps we need to follow to make our design.
We work with a partner and take turns doing different steps.
We talk about what needs to happen first, next and last.
We use picture cards to put the steps in the right order.
We check off each step as we finish it.
We help a friend with their step while they help us with ours.

VC2TDI2S01.w: We explore digital devices and learn about the parts that make them work.
y: Understanding how devices work helps us use them better.
Knowing about hardware and software is the beginning of understanding technology.
Exploring devices makes us curious about how technology is built.
eg: We look at a computer and identify the screen, keyboard, mouse and speakers.
We explore what happens inside a tablet — it has a processor, memory and a battery.
We compare different devices — a laptop, a phone, a tablet — and what they can do.
We learn that software is the programs and apps that tell the hardware what to do.
We explore how a printer connects to a computer to print our work.
We talk about devices we use at home and school and what they are for.

VC2TDI2S02.w: We learn what personal data is and how to use a username and password for our school account.
y: Keeping our accounts safe with a password protects our work and information.
Learning about personal data helps us understand digital privacy from the start.
Using a username and password is a basic digital skill everyone needs.
eg: We learn to log in to our school account using our username and password.
We talk about why we should keep our password to ourselves.
We identify personal data like our name, birthday and address.
We practise typing our password without showing it to others.
We learn that our school account saves our work so we can find it again.
We discuss why we should not share our password with friends.

VC2TDI2D01.w: We explore patterns in information and show data using pictures, symbols, numbers and words.
y: Spotting patterns in data helps us understand the world around us.
Representing data in different ways helps others understand it too.
Working with data is a skill we use in many areas of life.
eg: We sort our favourite colours and make a picture graph to show the results.
We use symbols like smiley faces to show how many people chose each option.
We count and record how many of each type of shoe we are wearing.
We notice patterns — like most people in our class have brown hair.
We draw pictures to represent information about our pets.
We use numbers to show how many books each person read this week.

VC2TDI2D02.w: We use basic digital tools to create, find and share content.
y: Digital tools help us create and share our work with others.
Learning to use technology now prepares us for using it throughout life.
Being able to find and create content makes us more independent learners.
eg: We type words and add pictures using a simple app to make a poster.
We use a search engine to find pictures or information about a topic.
We record our voice telling a story using a tablet.
We take photos to document a project and share them with our class.
We create a simple slideshow about something we have learned.
We use a drawing app to design a picture for a class display.

VC2TDI2D03.w: We use digital tools to share our work and work together, following agreed rules for being safe and respectful.
y: Sharing and collaborating online is an important skill for learning and working together.
Following agreed rules keeps us safe and kind when using technology.
Good digital behaviour helps everyone have a positive experience online.
eg: We share a document with our partner so we can work on it together.
We follow class rules about being kind in online comments.
We take turns on shared devices and treat them carefully.
We ask permission before sharing someone else's work or photo.
We use a class platform to share our work with our teacher.
We practise being respectful when giving online feedback to classmates.

VC2TDI2D04.w: We learn that some websites and apps save our personal information online.
y: Knowing that our information is stored helps us be careful about what we share.
Understanding data privacy is an important part of staying safe online.
We learn early that our personal information is valuable and should be protected.
eg: We talk about what 'personal data' means — like our name, age and photos.
We discuss how some apps remember our name and what we like.
We learn that websites can store information about us even when we are not using them.
We talk about what is OK to share online and what should stay private.
We role-play deciding whether to share personal information on a website.
We learn that a trusted adult should help us decide what to share online.

VC2TDI2C01.w: We look at simple problems and think about how computers can help solve them.
y: Computers are powerful tools that can help us solve many kinds of problems.
Learning to think about problems clearly is the first step in using technology well.
Problem-solving skills help us in technology and in everyday life.
eg: We think about a problem like 'How can we sort our class library?' and how a computer might help.
We talk about what a user needs and how a digital tool could help them.
We identify a simple problem at school that technology could solve.
We explore how apps and programs are designed to help people do things.
We discuss what information we would need to solve a problem digitally.
We brainstorm ideas for a simple app or game that would be helpful.

VC2TDI2C02.w: We follow and describe step-by-step instructions that include choices and repeats.
y: Following instructions carefully is how we get computers to do what we want.
Understanding steps, choices and repeats is the basis of all computer programs.
These thinking skills help us solve problems in an organised way.
eg: We follow a set of instructions to guide a robot or character across a grid.
We describe the steps needed to make a sandwich or get ready for school.
We practise 'if-then' thinking: 'If it is raining, then take an umbrella.'.
We explore repeating patterns: 'Jump three times, clap twice — repeat!'.
We draw a simple map showing the steps to get from one place to another.
We use a coding app to create a sequence of commands for a character.

VC2TDI2C03.w: We talk about how digital tools and systems help people with what they need.
y: Understanding how technology helps people teaches us to use it wisely.
Thinking about whether technology works well helps us improve it.
We learn that good technology is designed with people's needs in mind.
eg: We talk about how a timer app helps us know when our turn is finished.
We discuss how a reading app helps someone who is learning new words.
We explore how a weather app helps people decide what to wear.
We talk about whether a classroom tool works well or could be improved.
We compare two apps that do the same thing and say which one is easier to use.
We discuss how technology helps people who have different needs or abilities.

VC2TDE4S01.w: We learn about people who work in design and technology jobs and how sustainability affects their work.
y: Learning about design careers helps us imagine future possibilities for ourselves.
Understanding sustainability shows us how designers can help solve big problems.
Knowing about real-world design work connects our classroom learning to the wider world.
eg: We learn about what architects, engineers and product designers do.
We explore how a local food producer considers sustainability in their business.
We investigate how a furniture maker chooses materials that are good for the environment.
We interview someone who works in a design or technology job.
We discuss how designers balance making things people want with being environmentally responsible.
We explore how technology workers solve problems in our community.

VC2TDE4C01.w: We describe how forces make products and systems work the way they do.
y: Understanding forces helps us see why things are designed the way they are.
Forces like pushing, pulling and gravity affect how everything around us works.
Knowing about forces helps us design better products and systems.
eg: We explore how a lever makes it easier to lift heavy things.
We investigate how gears work in a bicycle to change speed.
We test how different amounts of force affect how far a toy car travels.
We describe how a catapult uses force to launch an object.
We explore how springs store and release energy in everyday products.
We build a simple machine and explain the forces that make it work.

VC2TDE4C02.w: We describe the different ways food and fibre are produced.
y: Knowing how food and fibre are produced helps us make informed choices.
Understanding production methods connects us to the people who grow what we use.
Different production methods have different impacts on the environment.
eg: We compare how vegetables are grown in a home garden versus a large farm.
We learn about how wool is produced — from shearing sheep to spinning yarn.
We explore how rice is grown in paddies and how wheat is grown in fields.
We investigate how fish are caught from the ocean versus farmed in ponds.
We learn about how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples manage food production using fire and seasonal knowledge.
We describe how cotton plants become the fabric in our clothes.

VC2TDE4C03.w: We describe how to choose and prepare food for healthy eating.
y: Making good food choices supports our health, energy and concentration.
Learning preparation techniques builds our independence in the kitchen.
Understanding nutrition helps us look after ourselves and others.
eg: We sort foods using the Australian Guide to Healthy Eating.
We practise safe food preparation techniques like washing, peeling and chopping.
We plan a healthy lunch and explain why we chose each food.
We investigate how cooking changes food — making it softer, tastier or safer to eat.
We learn about food labels and what the information tells us.
We prepare a simple healthy snack for our class.

VC2TDE4C04.w: We describe how the properties of materials affect how well products work.
y: Choosing the right material is one of the most important decisions in design.
Understanding material properties helps us explain why things are made a certain way.
This knowledge helps us select the best materials for our own projects.
eg: We compare how plastic, wood and metal behave when bent, stretched or compressed.
We investigate why raincoats are made from waterproof material rather than cotton.
We test different materials to find which is strongest for building a bridge model.
We explore why glass is used for windows and not for playground equipment.
We describe how the flexibility of rubber makes it useful for tyres and bands.
We choose materials for a project and explain why each one is suitable.

VC2TDE4D01.w: We explore what people need and investigate the tools, materials and processes for creating design solutions.
y: Good designers always start by understanding the problem they are trying to solve.
Investigating tools and materials helps us choose the best approach for our project.
This investigation phase is what makes the difference between good and great designs.
eg: We identify a need in our school — like a better way to organise classroom supplies.
We research different tools and materials that could work for our project.
We test materials to see if they have the properties we need.
We compare different approaches to solving the same problem.
We visit a local business to see how they use tools and materials in their work.
We make a list of what we need before starting our design project.

VC2TDE4D02.w: We create and share our design ideas using technical words and drawings, both by hand and with digital tools.
y: Using technical language helps us communicate our ideas precisely.
Clear drawings and diagrams help others understand exactly what we plan to make.
Learning to use both hand-drawn and digital tools gives us flexibility as designers.
eg: We draw a labelled diagram of our design showing dimensions and materials.
We use technical terms like 'joint', 'axle', 'fastener' when describing our designs.
We create a digital design using a drawing or design app.
We present our design to the class using a poster with labels and annotations.
We use a table to list materials, tools and steps for our project.
We compare our design drawings with our finished product and discuss any changes.

VC2TDE4D03.w: We choose and use the right materials, tools and techniques to safely build our designs.
y: Selecting the right materials and tools is essential for creating quality products.
Safe use of tools protects us and everyone around us.
Hands-on making develops practical skills that connect our ideas to reality.
eg: We select appropriate materials and explain our choices to the class.
We use tools like saws, drills or hot glue guns safely under supervision.
We practise techniques like measuring, cutting, joining and finishing.
We follow safety rules when using sharp or heated tools.
We make adjustments to our design as we build and discover what works.
We clean up our workspace and store tools properly when we finish.

VC2TDE4D04.w: We use design rules, including thinking about the environment, to judge how well our designs work.
y: Evaluating our work helps us learn what works well and what to improve.
Thinking about sustainability ensures our designs are good for people and the planet.
Using clear criteria makes our evaluations fair and useful.
eg: We check our design against a list of criteria — Does it work? Is it strong? Is it safe?
We think about whether our materials could be reused or recycled.
We ask classmates to test our product and give us feedback.
We compare our finished product with our original design plan.
We discuss whether our design is environmentally friendly and how it could be better.
We suggest improvements based on what we learned during making and testing.

VC2TDE4D05.w: We plan the steps to make our designs, working both individually and as a team.
y: Good planning helps projects run smoothly and finish on time.
Working as a team teaches us to share responsibilities and support each other.
Sequencing steps is a planning skill we will use in all areas of our lives.
eg: We create a step-by-step plan before starting to build our design.
We assign roles within our team — one person measures, another cuts, another joins.
We use a checklist to track our progress through the project.
We adjust our plan when something does not go as expected.
We discuss time management — how long each step will take.
We reflect on how well our team worked together and what we could improve.

VC2TDI4S01.w: We explore a range of digital systems and their connected devices for different purposes.
y: Understanding digital systems helps us choose the right tool for each task.
Knowing about peripherals helps us use technology more effectively.
Exploring systems builds our confidence with new and unfamiliar technology.
eg: We identify input devices (keyboard, microphone, camera) and output devices (screen, printer, speakers).
We explore how a scanner converts a physical document into a digital file.
We compare devices used at school, home and in the community.
We investigate what peripherals connect to our school computers and what they do.
We explore how a robot uses sensors as input and motors as output.
We discuss why different tasks need different types of digital systems.

VC2TDI4S02.w: We explore how different types of data are sent between digital systems.
y: Understanding data transmission helps us see how devices talk to each other.
This knowledge explains how the internet, Bluetooth and Wi-Fi actually work.
Knowing how data moves helps us understand both the power and limitations of networks.
eg: We explore how files are sent from one device to another via Bluetooth or Wi-Fi.
We discuss how a message travels from our tablet to someone else's device.
We investigate what happens when we send a photo from a phone to a printer.
We explore the difference between wired and wireless data transmission.
We learn how a USB cable transfers files between a computer and a storage device.
We discuss why some transfers are faster than others.

VC2TDI4S03.w: We learn to use a password we have memorised and understand why it should be hard for others to guess.
y: A strong password is our first line of defence for keeping accounts safe.
Understanding password security builds habits that protect us throughout life.
Good password practice is a foundational digital citizenship skill.
eg: We create a password that is easy for us to remember but hard for others to guess.
We discuss why '12345' or our pet's name are weak passwords.
We learn strategies for creating strong passwords — mixing letters, numbers and symbols.
We practise logging in with our memorised password without writing it down.
We discuss why we should not share our password, even with friends.
We explore what could happen if someone guessed our password and got into our account.

VC2TDI4D01.w: We learn about different types of data and explore how the same data can look different depending on its purpose.
y: Understanding data types is fundamental to working with technology.
The same information can be shown in many ways depending on what we need.
Data literacy helps us make sense of information in our daily lives.
eg: We sort data into types — numbers, text, images, dates — and discuss their differences.
We show the same weather data as a table, a graph and a map.
We explore how a bar chart and a pie chart tell different stories about the same data.
We discuss why a number might be stored as text sometimes (like a phone number).
We represent our class survey results in three different formats and compare them.
We explore how changing the way data is displayed changes what people notice.

VC2TDI4D02.w: We collect, organise and present data using software to create information and solve problems.
y: Turning raw data into useful information is one of the most important digital skills.
Organising data helps us see patterns and make better decisions.
These data skills are used in every job and area of life.
eg: We collect survey data from our class and enter it into a spreadsheet.
We organise data into categories and sort it to find patterns.
We create a graph using software to present our findings clearly.
We use data to answer a question — like 'What is the most popular sport in our class?'.
We clean up our data by checking for mistakes before analysing it.
We present our data findings to the class using charts and explanations.

VC2TDI4D03.w: We use common digital tools to create, find and share content, following agreed rules for formatting and style.
y: Following conventions makes our digital work clear and professional.
Knowing how to create and find content efficiently saves time and improves quality.
Good formatting helps our audience understand our message.
eg: We create a document with headings, paragraphs and images in the right places.
We follow our school's conventions for file naming and organisation.
We use a search engine effectively, choosing good keywords to find what we need.
We format a presentation with consistent fonts, sizes and colours.
We cite where we found information when creating a report.
We use accessibility features like alt text on images.

VC2TDI4D04.w: We use digital tools to share our work, plan tasks and collaborate, showing good online behaviour.
y: Collaboration skills are essential in a world where people work together online every day.
Good online behaviour creates a safe and productive environment for everyone.
Learning to plan and manage tasks digitally prepares us for future study and work.
eg: We use a shared document to work on a group project together.
We plan a project using a simple digital task list.
We share files with classmates through our school platform.
We demonstrate respectful behaviour when commenting on shared work.
We use a digital calendar to keep track of due dates and tasks.
We practise giving helpful, kind feedback on others' digital work.

VC2TDI4D05.w: We learn what personal data is stored in our online accounts and talk about the risks.
y: Understanding what data is collected helps us make safer choices online.
Knowing the risks of data sharing protects us from potential harm.
Digital privacy awareness is an essential life skill in our connected world.
eg: We check what personal information our school accounts know about us.
We discuss what might happen if someone else got access to our account.
We learn that apps often collect more data than we realise.
We talk about the difference between data we choose to share and data collected automatically.
We identify which online services store our photos, messages or location.
We discuss strategies for keeping our personal information safe.

VC2TDI4C01.w: We define simple problems with clear requirements that our teacher helps us set.
y: Clearly defining a problem is the most important step in solving it with technology.
Working with requirements teaches us to think about what a solution really needs to do.
This skill transfers to problem-solving in all areas of life.
eg: We work with our teacher to write down exactly what our digital solution needs to do.
We define who will use our solution and what they need it for.
We list the requirements for a simple app — what it must do and how it should look.
We break a big problem into smaller, manageable parts.
We discuss what counts as 'success' for our digital solution.
We compare different problem definitions and choose the clearest one.

VC2TDI4C02.w: We follow, describe and represent step-by-step instructions that include decisions and repeats.
y: Algorithms are the recipes that tell computers exactly what to do.
Understanding sequences, decisions and loops builds the foundation for all programming.
These logical thinking skills help us solve problems in an organised, efficient way.
eg: We write step-by-step instructions for a simple task and include 'if-then' decisions.
We create a flowchart showing a process with branches for different choices.
We use comparison operators — 'Is the number greater than 5?' — to make decisions in our algorithm.
We identify where repetition (loops) can make our instructions shorter and more efficient.
We trace through an algorithm step by step to check if it produces the right result.
We represent our algorithm using symbols, diagrams or written instructions.

VC2TDI4C03.w: We design a simple screen layout for a digital tool, share our ideas and compare designs.
y: User interface design determines how easy and enjoyable a digital tool is to use.
Comparing designs helps us learn from each other and improve our ideas.
Good design makes technology accessible to everyone.
eg: We sketch a screen layout for a simple app showing where buttons and text would go.
We compare our designs with classmates and discuss what makes each one easy to use.
We think about what colours, sizes and positions make buttons easy to find.
We consider how someone who has never used our app would know what to do.
We create two versions of a screen and test which one people prefer.
We use feedback to improve our design before building it.

VC2TDI4C04.w: We build simple visual programs that use sequences, decisions and user input.
y: Programming brings our algorithms to life as working digital solutions.
Building programs teaches us that computers follow instructions exactly as given.
Coding develops our logical thinking and creativity at the same time.
eg: We use a block-based coding tool like Scratch to create a simple program.
We make a character respond to user input — like moving when we press a key.
We add an 'if-then' block so our program makes decisions based on conditions.
We use a loop to make something repeat — like a character dancing on a stage.
We debug our program when it does not work as expected.
We share our program with classmates and explain how it works.

VC2TDI4C05.w: We discuss how well digital solutions — both existing ones and ones we made — meet the requirements.
y: Evaluating solutions helps us understand what works and what needs improvement.
Comparing our work to requirements teaches us to be objective about quality.
This critical thinking skill is essential for creating better technology.
eg: We test our program against the requirements we wrote and check if it does everything.
We compare an app we use at school with the needs it was designed to meet.
We discuss what works well about our solution and what could be better.
We ask classmates to test our program and tell us if it meets their needs.
We evaluate a popular app and suggest features that could be added.
We reflect on what we would change if we could start our project again.

VC2TDE6S01.w: We learn how people in design and technology jobs consider competing ethical factors, including sustainability.
y: Real-world designers constantly balance different priorities — cost, quality, ethics and sustainability.
Understanding these trade-offs helps us make more thoughtful design decisions.
This knowledge prepares us to be responsible designers and informed consumers.
eg: We investigate how an architect balances cost, aesthetics and environmental impact.
We explore how a food company decides between cheap packaging and recyclable packaging.
We discuss how fashion designers can prioritise sustainability without increasing costs too much.
We examine case studies of products where ethical and commercial priorities conflict.
We interview a local professional about how they handle sustainability in their work.
We debate different perspectives on a real design dilemma.

VC2TDE6C01.w: We explain how electrical energy can be turned into movement, sound or light in a product or system.
y: Understanding energy transformation helps us design products that do useful work.
Electrical circuits are at the heart of most modern technology we use every day.
This knowledge connects science concepts to real-world design and engineering.
eg: We build a simple circuit that makes a motor spin, a buzzer sound or an LED light up.
We explore how a torch converts electrical energy from batteries into light.
We investigate how an electric fan transforms electrical energy into movement and airflow.
We design a product that uses a simple circuit to perform a useful function.
We explain how speakers convert electrical signals into sound waves we can hear.
We compare how different products use the same energy source for different outputs.

VC2TDE6C02.w: We explain how and why food and fibre are produced in managed environments.
y: Understanding food and fibre production connects us to the systems that sustain our lives.
Managed environments help produce enough food for growing populations while managing resources.
This knowledge helps us make informed choices about what we eat and wear.
eg: We compare food production in traditional farming, hydroponics and aquaculture.
We investigate how greenhouses create managed environments for growing crops.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples manage environments through practices like fire-stick farming.
We research how a local farm manages water, soil and pests to produce food.
We investigate how fibre crops like cotton or hemp are grown and processed.
We discuss why managed food production is important for feeding communities sustainably.

VC2TDE6C03.w: We explain how the properties of foods influence how we choose and prepare them for healthy eating.
y: Understanding food properties helps us make smarter choices about what and how we eat.
Different preparation methods can make food more nutritious, safer or more appealing.
Food knowledge empowers us to look after our own health and wellbeing.
eg: We investigate how cooking changes the texture, colour and flavour of food.
We explore why some foods need to be refrigerated and others can stay at room temperature.
We examine food labels to understand nutritional information and make healthy choices.
We prepare a recipe that uses different techniques — chopping, mixing, heating — and explain why each is needed.
We investigate how the properties of foods from different cultures contribute to balanced diets.
We discuss how food processing affects nutritional value.

VC2TDE6C04.w: We explain how the characteristics of materials, systems and tools affect their use in design solutions.
y: Choosing appropriate materials and tools is critical for creating effective, durable products.
Understanding how materials behave under different conditions helps us predict and prevent problems.
This knowledge is essential for any kind of engineering, manufacturing or creative design work.
eg: We test materials for strength, flexibility, water resistance and heat tolerance.
We explain why certain materials are chosen for specific products — steel for bridges, rubber for tyres.
We investigate how combining materials (composites) can create better properties than single materials.
We explore how tools affect the quality of what we produce — a sharp blade versus a dull one.
We select materials for a design project and justify our choices based on their properties.
We compare natural and synthetic materials and discuss when each is more appropriate.

VC2TDE6D01.w: We investigate needs or opportunities for designing, and the materials, tools and processes needed to create solutions.
y: Thorough investigation before designing leads to better, more thoughtful solutions.
Understanding available materials and processes helps us plan realistic projects.
Research and investigation are skills that professional designers use every day.
eg: We identify a real need in our community and research existing solutions.
We investigate which materials, tools and techniques would work best for our project.
We create a design brief that outlines the problem, requirements and constraints.
We research how similar products have been designed and what we can learn from them.
We test materials and processes before committing to our final design approach.
We consider cost, availability and environmental impact when selecting materials.

VC2TDE6D02.w: We generate, improve and communicate our design ideas using technical terms and graphics, both by hand and digitally.
y: Iterating on our designs is how we move from good ideas to great solutions.
Technical communication ensures our designs can be understood and built by others.
Using both hand-drawn and digital methods gives us flexibility across different design contexts.
eg: We sketch multiple design concepts and annotate them with technical labels.
We create detailed technical drawings with measurements and material specifications.
We use CAD software or design apps to create digital versions of our designs.
We iterate on our designs based on testing results and feedback.
We present our design process — from initial ideas through iterations to final solution.
We use exploded diagrams or cross-sections to show how parts fit together.

VC2TDE6D03.w: We select, explain and use the right materials, tools and techniques to safely build our designed solutions.
y: Justifying our material and tool choices demonstrates understanding of the design process.
Skilled and safe use of tools produces higher quality results.
Making things with precision and care develops practical skills valued in many careers.
eg: We select materials and explain how their properties suit our design requirements.
We use power tools under supervision, following all safety procedures.
We apply finishing techniques to improve the appearance and durability of our product.
We adapt our techniques when materials behave differently than expected.
We follow workplace safety practices — eye protection, correct tool handling, clean workspace.
We document our making process with photos and notes.

VC2TDE6D04.w: We develop evaluation rules that include ethical and environmental considerations to judge our design ideas and solutions.
y: Ethical evaluation ensures our designs consider impacts on people and the planet.
Developing our own criteria teaches us to think critically about what 'good design' means.
Sustainability thinking is essential for the future of design and technology.
eg: We develop criteria that include functionality, aesthetics, safety and environmental impact.
We evaluate whether our design uses resources efficiently and minimises waste.
We consider the lifecycle of our product — what happens when it is no longer needed?
We negotiate evaluation criteria as a class, discussing what matters most.
We assess whether our design could be made from recycled or renewable materials.
We evaluate our design from the perspective of different users and stakeholders.

VC2TDE6D05.w: We develop project plans that consider resources, time and teamwork to make our designed solutions.
y: Project planning is a key professional skill used in design, engineering and many other fields.
Managing resources and time helps us complete projects efficiently and to a high standard.
Collaborative planning teaches us to work effectively in teams.
eg: We create a project timeline showing when each step needs to be completed.
We list all the resources — materials, tools, time — needed for our project.
We assign roles and responsibilities within our design team.
We build in time for testing and iteration in our project plan.
We track our progress against the plan and adjust when needed.
We reflect on our project management and identify what we would do differently next time.

VC2TDI6S01.w: We investigate the main parts inside common digital devices and what they do.
y: Understanding hardware components helps us make informed choices about technology.
Knowing what is inside a computer demystifies how technology actually works.
This knowledge is the foundation for understanding computer performance and troubleshooting.
eg: We identify the CPU, RAM, storage, motherboard and power supply inside a computer.
We discuss what each component does — the CPU processes, RAM stores temporarily, storage keeps permanently.
We compare the specifications of different devices and discuss how they affect performance.
We investigate how upgrading a component (like adding RAM) improves performance.
We explore the components inside a smartphone and compare them to a desktop computer.
We create a labelled diagram of a computer's internal components.

VC2TDI6S02.w: We examine how digital systems form networks to send data to each other.
y: Networks are the infrastructure that connects the digital world — understanding them is essential.
Knowing how networks work helps us understand the internet, Wi-Fi and cloud services.
Network knowledge helps us troubleshoot problems and make safe choices online.
eg: We explore how devices in our school connect to a local network and the internet.
We trace the path data takes from our computer to a website and back.
We investigate the roles of routers, switches and access points in a network.
We compare wired (Ethernet) and wireless (Wi-Fi) network connections.
We explore how the internet uses protocols to send data between networks worldwide.
We create a diagram showing how devices in a network communicate.

VC2TDI6S03.w: We use unique, strong passwords for different accounts and understand the risks of using the same password everywhere.
y: Using unique passwords for each account is one of the most important security practices.
If one password is compromised, reusing it means all accounts are at risk.
Good password habits are a lifelong security practice.
eg: We create unique passphrases for each of our accounts using memorable but hard-to-guess combinations.
We discuss what could happen if someone uses the same password for email, gaming and banking.
We explore how a passphrase (several words together) can be stronger than a complex short password.
We practise creating passphrases that are long, unique and memorable.
We discuss strategies for managing multiple passwords without writing them down insecurely.
We investigate real examples of data breaches where password reuse caused problems.

VC2TDI6D01.w: We explain how digital systems represent all data using numbers and explore binary representation.
y: Understanding that everything digital is built on numbers is a fundamental insight about technology.
Binary is the language computers actually speak — everything we see on screen comes from zeros and ones.
This knowledge demystifies how computers work and prepares us for deeper study.
eg: We explore how text is represented using number codes like ASCII.
We convert small numbers to binary (base-2) and back again.
We investigate how pixels use numbers to represent colour on a screen.
We explore how sound is converted to numbers through sampling.
We play binary counting games using cards or physical objects.
We discuss why computers use binary — because electronic switches have two states: on and off.

VC2TDI6D02.w: We collect and work with different types of data from various sources using software tools like spreadsheets.
y: Being able to collect, organise and manipulate data is one of the most valuable digital skills.
Spreadsheets are powerful tools used in virtually every profession.
Working with real data helps us answer genuine questions and solve real problems.
eg: We collect data from surveys, sensors or online sources and import it into a spreadsheet.
We use formulas to calculate totals, averages and percentages from our data.
We sort and filter data to find specific information quickly.
We clean data by identifying and correcting errors or inconsistencies.
We combine data from multiple sources to build a more complete picture.
We use spreadsheet tools to organise data for analysis and presentation.

VC2TDI6D03.w: We analyse and visualise data using software, including spreadsheets, to create useful information and solve problems.
y: Data analysis turns raw numbers into insights that help us understand the world.
Effective data visualisation communicates complex information clearly and powerfully.
These analytical skills are increasingly important in every area of study and work.
eg: We create charts and graphs in spreadsheet software to visualise our data findings.
We analyse data to identify trends, patterns and outliers.
We choose the most appropriate chart type — bar, line, pie, scatter — for our data.
We draw conclusions from our data analysis and present them to the class.
We use data visualisation to support an argument or answer a research question.
We compare different visualisations of the same data and discuss which communicates best.

VC2TDI6D04.w: We select and use the right digital tools to create, find and share content, following common conventions.
y: Choosing the right tool for each task makes us more efficient and effective.
Following digital conventions ensures our work is professional and accessible.
These skills prepare us for the digital demands of further study and future careers.
eg: We choose the best tool for our task — word processor, presentation, spreadsheet or design app.
We apply formatting conventions — headings, captions, citations — to our digital work.
We use advanced search techniques to find reliable information online.
We create content that follows accessibility standards like alt text and clear contrast.
We select appropriate file formats for different purposes — PDF for sharing, editable for collaboration.
We manage our digital files with a logical folder structure and naming convention.

VC2TDI6D05.w: We select and use digital tools to share content, plan tasks and collaborate on projects responsibly.
y: Effective online collaboration is essential for learning and working in the modern world.
Responsible digital behaviour protects ourselves and others in shared online spaces.
Managing projects digitally develops organisational skills we will use throughout life.
eg: We use shared documents and cloud storage to collaborate on group projects.
We plan and track project tasks using digital tools like shared calendars or task boards.
We demonstrate responsible sharing by checking what we post before publishing.
We use version control to keep track of changes in shared documents.
We communicate respectfully and constructively in online collaboration spaces.
We manage our time and contributions to group projects using digital planning tools.

VC2TDI6D06.w: We explain what a digital footprint is, how it lasts forever, and think about privacy when collecting personal data.
y: Our digital footprint follows us throughout our lives — understanding this helps us manage it wisely.
Privacy awareness protects us and the people whose data we might collect.
This knowledge is essential for being a responsible digital citizen.
eg: We map out our own digital footprint — all the places online where our data exists.
We discuss how information posted online can persist even after we try to delete it.
We explore privacy settings on apps and platforms we use.
We discuss what we should consider before collecting personal data from others.
We investigate real examples of how digital footprints have affected people.
We create guidelines for our class about responsible data collection and sharing.

VC2TDI6C01.w: We define problems with functional requirements that we develop with our teacher or on our own.
y: Clear problem definition with specific requirements is the foundation of effective software development.
Co-developing requirements builds our ability to think critically about what solutions need to achieve.
This skill is essential for anyone who will work with or create technology.
eg: We define a problem and write functional requirements — what the solution must do.
We co-develop requirements with our teacher, discussing what is essential versus optional.
We create a clear problem statement that identifies the user, their need and the constraints.
We prioritise requirements — must-have versus nice-to-have features.
We compare our problem definition with classmates and refine it based on feedback.
We test our requirements by checking whether a proposed solution would satisfy them.

VC2TDI6C02.w: We design algorithms with multiple decision paths and repeating sections, and represent them using flowcharts and pseudocode.
y: Complex algorithms with branching and iteration can solve more sophisticated problems.
Flowcharts and pseudocode are standard tools used by programmers worldwide.
Algorithm design develops our logical thinking and problem-solving abilities.
eg: We design an algorithm that checks multiple conditions before deciding what to do.
We create a flowchart with decision diamonds showing different paths through our algorithm.
We write pseudocode that uses IF-ELSE and WHILE loops clearly.
We trace through our algorithm with different inputs to verify it works correctly.
We optimise our algorithm by finding unnecessary steps we can remove.
We compare different algorithmic approaches to the same problem and discuss trade-offs.

VC2TDI6C03.w: We design and improve the user interface of a digital system, then share, discuss and evaluate our designs.
y: Great user interface design makes technology accessible, intuitive and enjoyable to use.
Iterating on designs based on feedback is how professional designers work.
Understanding user experience helps us create technology that truly serves people's needs.
eg: We design a user interface for an app, considering layout, navigation and visual hierarchy.
We modify our design based on user testing — watching how people interact with our prototype.
We evaluate our design against usability principles — is it easy to learn and use?
We compare our interface designs with classmates and discuss what works best.
We consider accessibility — can people with different abilities use our design?
We create wireframes or mockups to communicate our design ideas clearly.

VC2TDI6C04.w: We write visual programs that use control structures, variables and user input.
y: Variables and control structures allow us to create programs that are dynamic and responsive.
Adding user input makes our programs interactive and useful for real people.
These programming concepts are the building blocks of all software applications.
eg: We create a program that uses variables to keep score in a game.
We use IF-ELSE statements to make our program respond differently to different inputs.
We implement loops that repeat actions until a condition is met.
We add user input so people can interact with our program through clicks or typing.
We debug our program by testing different scenarios and fixing errors.
We combine multiple control structures to create a more complex, useful program.

VC2TDI6C05.w: We evaluate solutions against the requirements and their broader impact on the community.
y: Good evaluation considers not just whether something works, but how it affects people and communities.
Thinking about broader impact prepares us to be responsible technology creators.
This critical perspective helps us see beyond the immediate function of a solution.
eg: We test our digital solution against every requirement and document the results.
We consider whether our solution could have unintended negative effects on some users.
We evaluate an existing app and discuss its impact on the community — both positive and negative.
We gather feedback from different users to understand diverse perspectives on our solution.
We discuss how a technology designed for one purpose might be used in unexpected ways.
We reflect on the environmental impact of the digital solutions we create and use.

VC2TDE8S01.w: We learn how people in design and technology jobs consider ethical factors when designing products, services and environments.
y: Professional designers make ethical decisions that affect millions of people.
Understanding real-world design ethics prepares us to be responsible practitioners.
Ethical design balances innovation with responsibility to people and the planet.
eg: We analyse case studies of ethical dilemmas in product design and manufacturing.
We investigate how companies address sustainability in their design processes.
We explore how ergonomic design considers the health and safety of users.
We discuss how inclusive design ensures products are accessible to people with diverse abilities.
We examine how cultural sensitivity influences design decisions in different markets.
We research how emerging technologies raise new ethical questions for designers.

VC2TDE8S02.w: We explore the impacts of innovation and new technologies on design solutions, with attention to ethical considerations including sustainability.
y: Innovation drives progress but also creates new challenges and responsibilities.
Understanding the impacts of new technologies helps us harness their benefits while managing risks.
Ethical awareness ensures innovation serves people and the planet, not just profit.
eg: We investigate how 3D printing has changed manufacturing and its environmental implications.
We explore how smart materials and nanotechnology are creating new design possibilities.
We analyse the lifecycle impact of innovative products — from raw materials to disposal.
We discuss the ethical implications of automation and AI in manufacturing.
We explore how biomimicry uses nature as inspiration for sustainable design.
We evaluate how innovation in renewable energy is changing product design.

VC2TDE8C01.w: We analyse how force, motion and energy are used to control engineered systems in ethical ways.
y: Understanding force, motion and energy in engineered systems connects physics to real-world design.
Ethical engineering means considering safety, sustainability and impact on communities.
These concepts underpin everything from robotics to renewable energy systems.
eg: We analyse how a mechanical system uses gears, levers and linkages to convert force into useful motion.
We investigate how energy efficiency affects the design of products like electric vehicles.
We design and build a simple mechanical device that converts energy from one form to another.
We evaluate the ethical implications of engineering choices in real-world products.
We explore how pneumatic and hydraulic systems use pressure to generate force.
We discuss how engineers balance performance, cost and environmental impact.

VC2TDE8C02.w: We analyse how food and fibre are produced in managed environments and how these can become more ethical.
y: Understanding food and fibre production systems helps us make informed choices as consumers and citizens.
Ethical production considers animal welfare, environmental impact and fair labour practices.
These are some of the biggest challenges facing our generation.
eg: We analyse the environmental impact of different farming methods — conventional, organic, regenerative.
We investigate ethical issues in food production like animal welfare standards and fair trade.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander land management practices contribute to sustainable agriculture.
We compare the efficiency and ethics of different food production systems worldwide.
We research how fibre industries (cotton, wool, synthetic) address sustainability challenges.
We evaluate food labels and certifications to understand what they actually guarantee.

VC2TDE8C03.w: We analyse how the properties of foods determine how they are prepared and presented for healthy eating.
y: Understanding food science helps us prepare food that is both nutritious and appealing.
The properties of food — chemical, physical, sensory — directly affect preparation techniques.
This knowledge empowers us to make informed decisions about nutrition and food safety.
eg: We investigate how heat causes chemical changes in food — like protein denaturation when cooking an egg.
We analyse how the properties of ingredients determine which cooking techniques work best.
We design healthy meals that balance macronutrients and consider sensory appeal.
We investigate how food preservation techniques work — dehydration, refrigeration, fermentation.
We explore how food from different cultures uses specific preparation techniques for their ingredients.
We evaluate recipes to determine whether they produce nutritionally balanced outcomes.

VC2TDE8C04.w: We analyse how characteristics and properties of tools, materials, systems and components can be selected and manipulated for design solutions.
y: Sophisticated material selection requires understanding how properties interact under real-world conditions.
Manipulating materials to achieve desired properties is at the heart of engineering and design.
This analytical approach produces more effective, efficient and sustainable solutions.
eg: We analyse how the properties of metals, polymers, ceramics and composites suit different applications.
We investigate how materials can be treated or combined to enhance their properties.
We test materials under different conditions — load, temperature, moisture — and document results.
We select materials for a design project based on a systematic analysis of requirements.
We explore how material innovation has led to breakthroughs in product design.
We evaluate the lifecycle environmental impact of different material choices.

VC2TDE8D01.w: We identify needs or opportunities for designing and investigate materials, tools, processes and components to create solutions.
y: Professional designers begin every project with thorough investigation and needs analysis.
Understanding constraints and opportunities early leads to more innovative and feasible solutions.
This investigative approach develops research skills that transfer to many fields.
eg: We identify a genuine need in our school or community and develop a design brief.
We research existing solutions and analyse their strengths and weaknesses.
We investigate materials, processes and components, testing their suitability for our project.
We consider constraints including budget, time, available tools and user requirements.
We consult potential users to understand their needs and preferences.
We document our investigation findings to inform our design decisions.

VC2TDE8D02.w: We generate, test, iterate and communicate our design ideas using technical terms and graphics, both by hand and digitally.
y: The iterative design process — generate, test, refine — is how professional designers create effective solutions.
Technical communication ensures our ideas can be understood, evaluated and manufactured.
Testing and iterating transforms promising ideas into proven solutions.
eg: We generate multiple design concepts and evaluate them against our design criteria.
We create prototypes and test them, using the results to refine our designs.
We produce technical drawings with proper conventions — dimensions, materials lists, assembly views.
We use CAD software to develop detailed digital models of our designs.
We present our design process showing how ideas evolved through iteration.
We communicate design decisions using appropriate technical terminology.

VC2TDE8D03.w: We select, justify and use suitable tools, materials, processes and components to safely make our designed solutions.
y: Justifying our choices demonstrates deep understanding of materials, processes and design requirements.
Safe, skilled use of tools and materials is essential for producing quality outcomes.
This hands-on experience builds practical capabilities valued in many careers.
eg: We select materials and provide written justification based on their properties and suitability.
We use workshop tools and equipment safely, following all safety procedures.
We apply manufacturing techniques — cutting, shaping, joining, finishing — with increasing precision.
We adapt our approach when materials or processes don't behave as expected.
We apply quality control measures throughout the making process.
We document our making process including any modifications from the original design.

VC2TDE8D04.w: We collaboratively develop ethical evaluation criteria, including sustainability, to assess our design ideas and solutions.
y: Collaborative criteria development teaches us to consider multiple perspectives and values.
Ethical evaluation ensures our designs consider broader impacts on people and the environment.
Sustainability criteria help us design products that minimise harm and maximise benefit.
eg: We collaboratively develop criteria that address function, ethics, aesthetics and sustainability.
We evaluate our designs against criteria using a structured framework.
We consider the social and environmental impacts of our design choices.
We debate different ethical positions when criteria conflict with each other.
We evaluate products on the market against our ethical criteria.
We refine our criteria based on research and consultation with stakeholders.

VC2TDE8D05.w: We develop project plans to manage time, cost and production individually, in teams and collaboratively.
y: Project management skills are essential for completing complex design projects successfully.
Managing time, cost and resources teaches us to work within real-world constraints.
Team-based project management mirrors how design work happens in professional contexts.
eg: We create detailed project plans with timelines, milestones and resource requirements.
We estimate costs for materials and factor budget constraints into our decisions.
We assign team roles and responsibilities based on members' strengths.
We use project management tools to track progress and identify potential delays.
We hold regular team meetings to review progress and resolve issues.
We reflect on our project management and identify improvements for future projects.

VC2TDE10S01.w: We learn how people in design and technology jobs consider ethical factors to innovate and improve products, services and environments.
y: Understanding how professionals navigate ethical complexity prepares us for responsible practice.
Innovation that considers ethics creates solutions that are not only effective but also just and sustainable.
These insights help us envision our own futures as ethical designers, engineers and citizens.
eg: We analyse how leading companies integrate ethical considerations into their innovation processes.
We investigate ethical frameworks that professionals use to guide design decisions.
We explore how regulatory requirements and industry standards reflect ethical priorities.
We research how different cultural perspectives influence what is considered ethical in design.
We examine case studies where ethical failures in design had significant consequences.
We discuss how we would navigate ethical dilemmas we might face in future careers.

VC2TDE10S02.w: We explore the impacts of innovation, enterprise and emerging technologies on design solutions, considering ethical implications including sustainability.
y: Emerging technologies are reshaping every aspect of design and production — understanding their implications is critical.
Evaluating the ethical dimensions of innovation helps us advocate for technology that serves humanity well.
Enterprise thinking combines creative innovation with practical, responsible implementation.
eg: We investigate how artificial intelligence, automation and robotics are transforming manufacturing and design.
We analyse the environmental impacts of emerging technologies — both positive and negative.
We explore how circular economy principles are changing product design and production.
We evaluate how biotechnology, nanotechnology and smart materials create new design possibilities.
We discuss the social implications of technological disruption — job displacement, inequality, access.
We assess how emerging technologies can contribute to solving global sustainability challenges.

VC2TDE10C01.w: We analyse and make judgements on how material properties are combined with force, motion and energy to produce engineered solutions.
y: Sophisticated engineering analysis requires integrating knowledge of materials, forces and energy systems.
Making informed judgements about engineered solutions prepares us for careers in design and engineering.
This analytical capability helps us evaluate the quality and sustainability of the technology around us.
eg: We analyse how material selection, force application and energy efficiency interact in engineered products.
We make judgements about the suitability of different engineering approaches for specific applications.
We design and build a product that demonstrates effective integration of materials, force and energy.
We evaluate existing engineered systems and propose evidence-based improvements.
We investigate how emerging materials and energy technologies are changing engineering design.
We assess the sustainability and ethical implications of different engineering solutions.

VC2TDE10C02.w: We analyse and make judgements on the ethical and secure production and marketing of food and fibre enterprises.
y: Food and fibre systems face critical challenges around sustainability, ethics and security.
Making informed judgements about these industries is essential for responsible citizenship.
Understanding the complexity of food systems helps us advocate for fair and sustainable practices.
eg: We analyse the supply chain of a food product and evaluate its ethical and environmental credentials.
We investigate food security issues and how they affect different communities around the world.
We evaluate marketing claims about food and fibre products against the reality of production methods.
We research how technology and innovation are addressing sustainability challenges in agriculture.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander land management contributes to sustainable food systems.
We debate the trade-offs between intensive production for food security and sustainable, ethical practices.

VC2TDE10C03.w: We analyse and make judgements on how the sensory and functional properties of food influence the design and preparation of food for healthy eating.
y: Understanding the science of food properties enables us to design meals that are both nutritious and appealing.
Analysing how properties interact during preparation leads to better cooking outcomes.
Food science knowledge empowers informed decisions about nutrition, safety and quality.
eg: We analyse how the Maillard reaction affects flavour, colour and nutritional value during cooking.
We investigate how emulsification, gelatinisation and fermentation change food properties.
We design meals that optimise both nutritional content and sensory appeal.
We evaluate how different preparation techniques affect the functional properties of ingredients.
We investigate how food science innovations are creating healthier processed foods.
We make evidence-based judgements about food preparation methods for specific dietary needs.

VC2TDE10C04.w: We analyse and make judgements on how material, system and component properties can be combined and manipulated for design solutions.
y: Advanced material analysis enables us to create more innovative, effective and sustainable solutions.
Judgement about combining materials and systems is a core competency in professional design and engineering.
This knowledge enables us to push beyond conventional approaches and create better products.
eg: We analyse how advanced materials — smart materials, composites, biomaterials — can be used in design.
We make judgements about material combinations based on how properties complement each other.
We investigate how manufacturing processes affect the final properties of products.
We evaluate the performance, cost and sustainability trade-offs of different material systems.
We design solutions that demonstrate sophisticated understanding of material properties and interactions.
We research cutting-edge material innovations and assess their potential applications.

VC2TDE10D01.w: We analyse needs or opportunities for designing, develop design briefs, and investigate materials, systems and processes to create solutions.
y: Professional-level design practice begins with rigorous needs analysis and comprehensive investigation.
Developing design briefs teaches us to define problems clearly and set measurable success criteria.
Thorough investigation ensures our solutions are informed, feasible and well-suited to real needs.
eg: We conduct stakeholder research to identify genuine needs and opportunities for design.
We develop comprehensive design briefs with clear objectives, constraints and success criteria.
We investigate materials, systems and processes through testing, research and expert consultation.
We analyse competing requirements and make informed decisions about priorities.
We document our investigation findings and use them to guide design development.
We present our design brief and investigation to peers for critical feedback.

VC2TDE10D02.w: We apply innovation and enterprise skills to generate, test, iterate and communicate design ideas, processes and solutions using technical conventions.
y: Innovation and enterprise skills transform creative ideas into viable, impactful solutions.
Professional-level technical communication ensures our designs can be manufactured and evaluated.
The iterative process of testing and refining is how the best design solutions emerge.
eg: We apply creative thinking techniques to generate innovative design concepts.
We test prototypes rigorously and use the data to iterate and improve our designs.
We communicate our designs using industry-standard technical conventions and documentation.
We use digital tools including CAD and simulation software to develop and present solutions.
We consider how our designs could be manufactured at scale, marketed and distributed.
We present our complete design process from concept through development to final solution.

VC2TDE10D03.w: We select, justify, test and use suitable technologies and skills to safely make our designed solutions, applying safety procedures.
y: At this level, our making skills should demonstrate both precision and a clear rationale for every choice.
Testing our material and process choices provides evidence that our approach is sound.
Safe, skilled making is the mark of a competent and responsible practitioner.
eg: We select technologies and materials with written justification based on testing and research.
We apply advanced manufacturing techniques with precision and attention to quality.
We follow comprehensive safety procedures appropriate to the tools and processes we use.
We test our making processes and adjust techniques to achieve the best results.
We apply quality control throughout production, not just at the end.
We document our making process comprehensively, including problem-solving and adaptations.

VC2TDE10D04.w: We develop design evaluation criteria that include sustainability to assess our ideas, processes and solutions.
y: Sustainability-integrated evaluation ensures our designs address long-term environmental and social impacts.
Developing rigorous criteria demonstrates our ability to think critically about what makes design successful.
This evaluative capability is essential for responsible design practice.
eg: We develop comprehensive evaluation criteria spanning function, aesthetics, ethics, sustainability and user experience.
We use lifecycle analysis to evaluate the total environmental impact of our design solutions.
We evaluate our design processes as well as our final products — is our method itself sustainable?
We apply our criteria consistently and document our evaluation findings.
We compare our evaluation criteria with industry standards and adjust accordingly.
We use our evaluation to make evidence-based recommendations for improvement.

VC2TDE10D05.w: We develop project management plans for intended purposes and audiences to manage the creation of designed solutions individually, in teams and collaboratively.
y: Sophisticated project management is essential for bringing complex design projects to successful completion.
Managing projects for specific purposes and audiences develops professional communication skills.
These organisational capabilities transfer directly to further study and professional work.
eg: We develop comprehensive project plans with detailed timelines, resource allocation and risk assessment.
We use project management software to coordinate team activities and track progress.
We manage budgets, deadlines and quality requirements across all project phases.
We communicate project progress to different stakeholders — team, teacher, client, audience.
We adapt our project plans in response to unexpected challenges and changing requirements.
We conduct post-project reviews to evaluate our management effectiveness.

VC2TDI8S01.w: We explain how hardware specifications affect performance and select appropriate hardware for particular tasks.
y: Understanding hardware specifications helps us make informed purchasing and configuration decisions.
Matching hardware to workload requirements is essential for efficient and cost-effective computing.
This knowledge enables us to troubleshoot performance problems and plan upgrades.
eg: We compare how CPU speed, RAM amount and storage type affect system performance.
We recommend hardware specifications for different use cases — gaming, video editing, office work.
We investigate how GPU capabilities affect graphics performance and machine learning tasks.
We benchmark different hardware configurations and compare their performance.
We explore how hardware requirements have changed as software has become more demanding.
We discuss trade-offs between performance, cost and energy consumption in hardware selection.

VC2TDI8S02.w: We investigate how data is transmitted and secured in wired and wireless networks, including the internet.
y: Understanding data transmission and network security is essential in our connected world.
Knowing how networks work helps us understand both the capabilities and vulnerabilities of digital systems.
Network knowledge is foundational for cybersecurity and many technology careers.
eg: We investigate how data packets travel across the internet using TCP/IP protocols.
We compare the characteristics of wired and wireless network technologies.
We explore how encryption protects data during transmission.
We investigate how firewalls, VPNs and HTTPS keep network communications secure.
We trace the path of a web request from browser to server and back.
We discuss the security implications of public Wi-Fi networks versus private networks.

VC2TDI8S03.w: We explain how multi-factor authentication protects accounts and learn to identify phishing and other social engineering attacks.
y: Multi-factor authentication is one of the most effective security measures available to us.
Recognising social engineering attacks protects us from one of the most common cybersecurity threats.
These security skills are essential for protecting our personal and professional digital lives.
eg: We explain how multi-factor authentication uses something you know, have and are to verify identity.
We identify the characteristics of phishing emails, messages and websites.
We discuss real examples of social engineering attacks and how they could have been prevented.
We set up multi-factor authentication on our accounts and explain why each factor matters.
We practise evaluating suspicious communications — checking sender addresses, links and urgency claims.
We discuss why human behaviour is often the weakest link in cybersecurity.

VC2TDI8D01.w: We investigate how digital systems represent text, images and audio using integers and binary.
y: Understanding data representation reveals the fundamental principles behind all digital technology.
Knowing how data is encoded helps us understand file sizes, quality and compression.
This knowledge bridges the gap between the digital world we see and how computers actually work.
eg: We investigate how text characters are encoded using standards like ASCII and Unicode.
We explore how images are represented as grids of pixels, each with colour values stored as numbers.
We examine how audio is digitised through sampling — converting sound waves to numerical values.
We calculate file sizes based on data representation — bits per pixel, sample rate, resolution.
We experiment with changing image resolution and colour depth to see the effects on quality and file size.
We explore how different data types require different amounts of storage.

VC2TDI8D02.w: We acquire, store, manipulate and validate data from various sources using spreadsheets and simple databases.
y: Data management skills are essential in virtually every field — science, business, health, government.
Validation ensures our data is accurate and reliable before we draw conclusions from it.
Working with real-world data from multiple sources reflects authentic professional practice.
eg: We import data from external sources like CSV files, APIs or web scraping into spreadsheets.
We design simple database tables with appropriate fields and data types.
We validate data by checking for errors, inconsistencies and missing values.
We use formulas and functions to manipulate and transform data efficiently.
We combine data from multiple sources to create a more complete dataset.
We document our data management process including how we handled quality issues.

VC2TDI8D03.w: We analyse and visualise data using spreadsheets and simple database queries, draw conclusions and make recommendations.
y: Data analysis and visualisation are powerful tools for understanding complex information.
Drawing conclusions from data and making recommendations are key professional skills.
The ability to query databases is increasingly important across all industries.
eg: We use database queries to filter, sort and aggregate data to answer specific questions.
We create meaningful visualisations that communicate data insights clearly.
We analyse data to identify trends, correlations and anomalies.
We draw evidence-based conclusions from our data analysis.
We make recommendations supported by data and present them to an audience.
We evaluate the limitations of our data and analysis methods.

VC2TDI8D04.w: We select and use a range of digital tools, including unfamiliar features, to create, find and communicate content.
y: Being able to learn unfamiliar tools independently is one of the most important digital skills.
Selecting the right tool for each task maximises our productivity and the quality of our output.
Adaptability with technology prepares us for a world where tools constantly evolve.
eg: We explore unfamiliar features of digital tools by experimenting and consulting help resources.
We select the most appropriate tool for each task based on the outcome we need.
We use advanced features of word processors, spreadsheets and presentation tools.
We create content in multiple formats — text, image, video, interactive — using appropriate tools.
We evaluate new digital tools and decide whether they would improve our workflow.
We adapt quickly when required to use a tool we haven't used before.

VC2TDI8D05.w: We select and use digital tools responsibly to share content, plan and manage projects, and collaborate with others.
y: Responsible digital collaboration is essential for productive teamwork in the modern world.
Managing projects with digital tools develops organisational skills valued in every profession.
Being responsible online protects ourselves, our collaborators and our community.
eg: We use project management tools to plan, assign and track tasks in group projects.
We collaborate on shared documents, using commenting and suggestion features constructively.
We make responsible choices about what content to share and with whom.
We manage version control so team members don't accidentally overwrite each other's work.
We communicate professionally and respectfully in digital collaboration spaces.
We reflect on the effectiveness of our digital collaboration and suggest improvements.

VC2TDI8D06.w: We investigate the digital footprint that systems collect and assess whether the data collected is necessary.
y: Understanding what data is collected about us empowers us to make informed privacy choices.
Assessing whether data collection is necessary develops critical thinking about surveillance and consent.
This knowledge is essential for designing ethical digital systems that respect users.
eg: We investigate what data popular apps and websites collect about their users.
We assess whether the data collected is genuinely necessary for the service provided.
We examine the privacy policies of digital systems we use and evaluate their fairness.
We explore how our own digital solutions collect and store data, and whether all of it is needed.
We discuss the balance between data collection for useful features and privacy protection.
We investigate examples of excessive data collection and its consequences.

VC2TDI8C01.w: We define and break down real-world problems by considering what the solution must do and the constraints it faces.
y: Decomposing complex problems into manageable parts is a fundamental computational thinking skill.
Considering both requirements and constraints leads to realistic, achievable solutions.
