This analytical approach to problem-solving transfers to virtually every field and profession.
eg: We analyse a real-world problem and identify its functional requirements — what the solution must do.
We identify constraints — time, budget, technology, user skill level — that limit our solution.
We decompose a large problem into smaller sub-problems that can be solved independently.
We create requirements specifications that clearly define what success looks like.
We consider edge cases and unusual scenarios that our solution must handle.
We compare our problem definition with peers and refine it based on feedback.

VC2TDI8C02.w: We design algorithms with nested control structures, represent them as flowcharts and pseudocode, and use tracing to check they work.
y: Nested control structures allow us to solve increasingly complex and nuanced problems.
Formal representation using flowcharts and pseudocode is standard practice in software development.
Tracing algorithms manually develops our ability to think precisely and debug effectively.
eg: We design algorithms with nested loops and conditional statements for complex tasks.
We represent our algorithms using standard flowchart symbols and clear pseudocode.
We trace through our algorithms with sample inputs to verify they produce correct outputs.
We identify and fix logical errors by stepping through our algorithm carefully.
We compare the efficiency of different algorithmic approaches to the same problem.
We document our algorithms with clear comments explaining each section's purpose.

VC2TDI8C03.w: We design and modify user interfaces and user experiences for digital systems, then evaluate our design alternatives.
y: User interface and user experience design directly determine whether people can and will use a system.
Evaluating alternatives systematically leads to better design decisions.
UX/UI skills are among the most in-demand capabilities in the technology industry.
eg: We design user interfaces following established design principles — consistency, feedback, simplicity.
We create wireframes and interactive prototypes to test our design ideas with users.
We conduct user testing and observe how people interact with our interface.
We generate multiple design alternatives and evaluate them against user needs.
We consider accessibility requirements when designing our interfaces.
We iterate on our designs based on user feedback and usability testing results.

VC2TDI8C04.w: We write, modify and debug programs using control structures and functions in a general-purpose programming language.
y: Programming in a general-purpose language gives us the power to create real-world applications.
Functions help us organise code into reusable, manageable pieces — a key professional practice.
Debugging teaches us persistence, analytical thinking and attention to detail.
eg: We write programs in Python, JavaScript or another general-purpose language.
We use functions to organise our code into logical, reusable modules.
We implement control structures — conditionals, loops, nested structures — to handle complex logic.
We debug programs systematically using print statements, breakpoints and error messages.
We modify existing programs to add features or fix issues.
We follow coding conventions — meaningful variable names, comments, consistent indentation.

VC2TDI8C05.w: We evaluate digital solutions against requirements, constraints and their possible future impacts.
y: Evaluating solutions against multiple criteria ensures they are truly fit for purpose.
Considering future impacts develops forward-thinking, responsible design habits.
Critical evaluation skills help us improve our own work and assess technology in general.
eg: We test our solution against every requirement and document whether each is met.
We evaluate whether our solution works within the identified constraints.
We consider how our solution might need to change as user needs evolve.
We assess potential negative impacts — privacy concerns, exclusion, environmental cost.
We gather user feedback and use it to evaluate our solution's effectiveness.
We compare our solution with existing alternatives and identify relative strengths and weaknesses.

VC2TDI10S01.w: We investigate how hardware and software manage, control and secure access to data in networked digital systems.
y: Understanding how data is managed and secured in networks is fundamental to cybersecurity.
This knowledge enables us to design systems that protect sensitive information effectively.
Network security skills are among the most critically needed capabilities in the technology sector.
eg: We investigate how operating systems manage hardware resources and control data access.
We explore how authentication and authorisation systems control who can access what data.
We analyse how encryption, firewalls and intrusion detection systems protect networked data.
We investigate how cloud computing infrastructure manages and secures data across distributed systems.
We explore how access control lists and permissions are implemented in real systems.
We discuss the security trade-offs between convenience and protection in networked environments.

VC2TDI10S02.w: We develop cyber security threat models and explore software, user and supply chain vulnerabilities.
y: Threat modelling is a professional cybersecurity practice that helps us think systematically about risks.
Understanding vulnerabilities across software, people and supply chains gives us a comprehensive view of security.
These skills are increasingly critical as cyber threats grow in sophistication and impact.
eg: We develop threat models for digital systems by identifying assets, threats and vulnerabilities.
We explore common software vulnerabilities — injection attacks, buffer overflows, insecure APIs.
We investigate how social engineering exploits human behaviour to compromise security.
We analyse supply chain vulnerabilities — how compromised third-party components can affect security.
We discuss real-world cybersecurity incidents and analyse how they could have been prevented.
We propose security measures that address the threats and vulnerabilities we identified.

VC2TDI10D01.w: We investigate simple data compression techniques.
y: Data compression makes it possible to store and transmit the vast amounts of data our digital world depends on.
Understanding compression helps us make informed decisions about quality, file size and bandwidth.
This knowledge connects directly to how streaming, storage and communication technologies work.
eg: We investigate how run-length encoding compresses data by replacing repeated sequences with counts.
We explore the difference between lossless and lossy compression and when each is appropriate.
We experiment with compressing images and audio at different quality levels and compare results.
We investigate how text compression algorithms like Huffman coding work.
We calculate compression ratios and discuss the trade-offs between file size and quality.
We explore how compression enables streaming services, messaging and cloud storage.

VC2TDI10D02.w: We represent online documents as content (text), structure (markup) and presentation (styling) and explain why this separation matters.
y: The separation of content, structure and presentation is a fundamental principle of web technology.
Understanding HTML, CSS and content management is essential in our web-driven world.
This separation makes websites more accessible, maintainable and adaptable.
eg: We create web pages using HTML for structure and CSS for visual presentation.
We explain why separating content from presentation makes websites easier to maintain.
We demonstrate how the same HTML content can look completely different with different CSS styles.
We explore how accessibility is improved when content and presentation are properly separated.
We investigate how responsive design uses CSS to adapt presentation for different screen sizes.
We discuss how content management systems use this separation to enable non-technical content editing.

VC2TDI10D03.w: We develop techniques to acquire, store, manipulate and validate data from various sources using software tools including databases.
y: Professional data management skills are among the most in-demand capabilities across all industries.
Developing robust data techniques ensures the quality and reliability of our analysis and applications.
These skills enable us to work with the real-world data that powers modern technology and decision-making.
eg: We develop techniques for acquiring data from diverse sources — APIs, web scraping, sensors, surveys.
We design and implement database schemas that efficiently store and organise our data.
We implement data validation procedures that catch errors, inconsistencies and anomalies.
We manipulate data using SQL queries and programming libraries for data processing.
We develop data cleaning and transformation pipelines for messy real-world datasets.
We document our data management techniques and the rationale behind our design choices.

VC2TDI10D04.w: We analyse and visualise data interactively using spreadsheets, relational databases and other software to draw conclusions and make recommendations.
y: Interactive data analysis and visualisation enables deeper exploration and more nuanced insights.
The ability to draw evidence-based conclusions from data is one of the most valued professional skills.
Relational database skills open doors to careers in data science, business analysis and technology.
eg: We create interactive dashboards that allow users to explore data from multiple perspectives.
We use relational database queries (SQL) to extract, filter and aggregate data for analysis.
We apply statistical techniques to identify significant patterns and relationships in data.
We create compelling data visualisations that communicate complex findings clearly.
We draw evidence-based conclusions and present actionable recommendations.
We evaluate the limitations and potential biases in our data and analysis methods.

VC2TDI10D05.w: We select and use emerging digital tools and advanced features to create and communicate interactive content for diverse audiences.
y: Staying current with emerging tools ensures we can leverage the latest technology for our work.
Creating interactive content for diverse audiences requires both technical skill and inclusive design thinking.
Advanced digital content creation skills prepare us for creative careers and effective communication.
eg: We explore emerging tools — AI assistants, interactive media platforms, no-code app builders — and evaluate their capabilities.
We create interactive content that responds to user input and provides personalised experiences.
We design content that is accessible to audiences with diverse needs and preferences.
We use advanced features of digital tools to produce professional-quality output.
We evaluate new tools critically — considering privacy, sustainability and effectiveness.
We share our knowledge of emerging tools with peers through tutorials or demonstrations.

VC2TDI10D06.w: We use simple project management tools to plan and manage individual and collaborative iterative projects, accounting for risks and responsibilities.
y: Iterative project management is the standard approach in modern software and technology development.
Managing risks and responsibilities ensures projects are completed successfully and ethically.
These project management skills are essential for success in further study and professional work.
eg: We use project management tools to plan iterative development sprints with clear deliverables.
We identify and assess project risks and develop mitigation strategies.
We define clear roles and responsibilities for each team member.
We track progress using burndown charts, task boards or similar tools.
We conduct regular retrospectives to evaluate what worked and what needs improvement.
We manage project scope, time and resources to deliver quality outcomes.

VC2TDI10D07.w: We apply the Australian Privacy Principles to critique and manage the digital footprint of our systems and solutions.
y: Understanding privacy law is essential for creating digital solutions that are legally compliant and ethically sound.
The Australian Privacy Principles provide a framework for responsible data handling that applies across all industries.
Applying these principles demonstrates professional-level awareness of our obligations as technology creators.
eg: We apply relevant Australian Privacy Principles to evaluate the data practices of digital systems.
We critique the digital footprint of apps and services against privacy legislation.
We design our own digital solutions with privacy-by-design principles built in from the start.
We evaluate what data our solutions collect and whether each piece is genuinely necessary.
We develop privacy notices and consent mechanisms for our digital solutions.
We discuss how privacy regulations vary across jurisdictions and their implications for global technology.

VC2TDI10C01.w: We define and decompose real-world problems considering functional and non-functional requirements, and by interviewing stakeholders.
y: Professional problem definition considers both what a system must do and how well it must do it.
Stakeholder interviews reveal needs and perspectives that assumptions alone cannot capture.
This rigorous approach to problem definition is the foundation of effective software engineering.
eg: We define problems with both functional requirements (what it does) and non-functional requirements (how well — speed, security, usability).
We conduct stakeholder interviews to understand the real-world context of our problem.
We decompose complex problems into well-defined sub-problems with clear interfaces.
We prioritise requirements using techniques like MoSCoW (Must, Should, Could, Won't).
We document our problem definition comprehensively for use throughout the development process.
We validate our problem definition with stakeholders to ensure accuracy before development begins.

VC2TDI10C02.w: We design algorithms with logical operators, represent them as flowcharts and pseudocode, and validate them using test cases and trace tables.
y: Logical operators enable algorithms to handle complex decision-making scenarios.
Formal validation with test cases and trace tables ensures our algorithms are correct before implementation.
These skills are directly applicable to professional software development.
eg: We design algorithms using AND, OR and NOT operators for complex conditional logic.
We represent our algorithms clearly using standard flowchart notation and structured pseudocode.
We create comprehensive test cases with expected outputs to validate our algorithms.
We use trace tables to step through our algorithms manually and verify correctness.
We identify and resolve edge cases that could cause our algorithms to fail.
We optimise our algorithms for efficiency while maintaining correctness.

VC2TDI10C03.w: We design, modify and prototype user interfaces and user experiences for digital systems, then critically evaluate our alternatives.
y: Prototyping and critical evaluation are how professional designers create user-centred solutions.
Understanding the full UX design process — research, design, prototype, test, iterate — is a highly valued skill.
Critical evaluation ensures our designs genuinely serve users rather than just looking good.
eg: We create interactive prototypes that allow realistic user testing of our interface designs.
We apply UX design principles including accessibility, consistency and user feedback.
We conduct structured usability testing and analyse the results to inform design improvements.
We critically evaluate our design alternatives against user needs and design heuristics.
We iterate our designs through multiple rounds of testing and refinement.
We document our complete UX design process from research through to final design rationale.

VC2TDI10C04.w: We write, modify and debug modular programs using selected algorithms and data structures, including in an object-oriented language.
y: Modular, object-oriented programming is the industry standard for building maintainable, scalable software.
Working with algorithms and data structures develops our ability to solve complex computational problems efficiently.
These skills directly prepare us for further study in computer science or careers in software development.
eg: We write modular programs with well-defined functions or methods that each handle a specific task.
We implement programs using object-oriented concepts — classes, objects, inheritance, encapsulation.
We select appropriate data structures — arrays, lists, dictionaries — for different programming needs.
We implement algorithms like searching and sorting and understand their efficiency trade-offs.
We debug complex programs using systematic approaches — breakpoints, logging, unit tests.
We follow software development best practices — version control, code documentation, testing.

VC2TDI10C05.w: We evaluate solutions against requirements, design criteria, user needs, possible future impacts and sustainability.
y: Comprehensive evaluation against multiple criteria ensures our solutions are truly fit for purpose.
Considering future impacts and sustainability demonstrates mature, responsible technology practice.
This evaluative capability is essential for creating technology that benefits rather than harms.
eg: We evaluate our solutions against all functional and non-functional requirements systematically.
We assess our designs against established criteria including usability, accessibility and sustainability.
We conduct user acceptance testing to validate that our solution meets real user needs.
We evaluate the potential long-term impacts of our solution — scalability, maintenance, obsolescence.
We assess the environmental impact of our digital solution — energy use, hardware requirements.
We present a comprehensive evaluation report with evidence-based conclusions and recommendations.

VC2AFAE01.w: We watch and listen to art made by different people.
y: Watching others helps us notice new things about the world.
We learn that art comes from many different people and places.
Enjoying art helps us feel connected to others.
eg: We watch a dance and smile or clap when we enjoy it.
We move our arms or legs when we hear music playing.
We look at a painting and point at things we notice.
We listen to a song and bounce or sway to the sounds.
We watch a puppet show and notice the colourful puppets.
We look at photos of art from different communities.

VC2AFAD01.w: We try out art tools and materials to see what we can do.
y: Exploring different tools helps us find what we enjoy.
We discover what our body and hands can do with art materials.
Trying new things helps us learn about the world around us.
eg: We try moving in a space where people perform.
We wear a hat or scarf as a costume while we dance.
We use props like a wand or ribbon to help us move.
We watch someone else perform and notice how it feels.
We listen to sounds and try swaying or bouncing.
We feel the beat of a drum and move our body to it.

VC2AFAC01.w: We help make art together with others.
y: Making art with others helps us feel part of a group.
We learn we can share ideas even without words.
Creating together shows us everyone can be an artist.
eg: We move our hands or body while music plays and a friend copies us.
We help choose a colour or sound for a group artwork.
We touch soft fabric or bumpy paper to help make a collage.
We tap a drum or shake a maraca when it is our turn.
We pick a picture to add to our class story.
We wave ribbons or scarves together during a group dance.

VC2AFAP01.w: We share the art we have helped make with others.
y: Sharing our art helps others see what we have done.
We feel proud when someone watches our art.
Sharing lets us enjoy our work again.
eg: We watch a video of ourselves dancing with our class.
We listen to a recording of sounds we helped make.
We look at a photo of our group painting together.
Our teacher shows our artwork on a screen for everyone to see.
We watch a video of our class performance at assembly.
We look at a picture book our class helped create.

VC2AFBE01.w: We look at and listen to art from different people and places.
y: Art from different places teaches us about how people live.
Seeing others' art helps us think about our own ideas.
We learn that art connects people to their culture and Country.
eg: We watch Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander dance and notice how special it is.
We smile or clap when we enjoy a performance.
We look at pictures made by artists from our community.
We listen to music from another country and move to the beat.
We point at things we like in a painting or sculpture.
We watch a puppet show and talk about what we saw.

VC2AFBD01.w: We try out art ideas by using different materials and our body.
y: Trying things out helps us find new ways to express ourselves.
Practising movements and actions builds our confidence.
We learn what materials can do when we explore them.
eg: We try copying movements from a favourite song.
We practise balancing and stepping in different ways.
We use crayons and paint to make marks on paper.
We tap sticks together to keep a steady beat.
We try building with blocks to make a small stage.
We use playdough to shape a character from a story.

VC2AFBC01.w: We help build an artwork by sharing our own ideas.
y: Our ideas matter and can be part of something bigger.
Choosing what to add helps us practise making decisions.
Working together shows us that many ideas make art richer.
eg: We pick an animal movement to add to a group dance.
We choose a colour for our part of a big painting.
We suggest a sound to add to a class song.
We pick a puppet to use in a group story.
We choose a material like feathers or paper for a collage.
We point to a picture we want to include in a class book.

VC2AFBP01.w: We share something we helped make with people we know.
y: Sharing our work helps others see what we can do.
Performing for others builds our confidence.
We feel happy when people enjoy what we have made.
eg: We dance to a favourite song while our teacher records us.
We see a photo of our artwork and recognise it is ours.
We do a familiar action in a group performance for our class.
We show a painting to our family and point to our part.
We sing along with our class and parents watch us.
We play an instrument with the group for our teacher.

VC2AFCE01.w: We look at art from different people to get new ideas.
y: Other people's art can inspire us to try new things.
We learn there are many ways to make and enjoy art.
Exploring art helps us understand different ways of seeing the world.
eg: We pretend to roll like a log or float like a cloud in dance.
We try moving fast and then very slowly to music.
We look at artwork from our community and talk about it.
We listen to music from different countries and clap along.
We act out a character from a story we read together.
We use leaves and sticks to make art outside, like land artists do.

VC2AFCD01.w: We try out art tools and techniques to show our ideas.
y: Learning different techniques gives us more ways to express ourselves.
Experimenting helps us discover what we are good at.
Using tools and materials builds our skills for future art.
eg: We clap when we hear a drum beat in a dance.
We try to show happy or sad feelings through our movements.
We use paint and a big brush to make bold marks.
We try tapping fast and slow on a drum.
We use a camera to take a photo of something interesting.
We try mixing two paint colours to see what happens.

VC2AFCC01.w: We make art about things we have seen or done.
y: Making art helps us show what matters to us.
We learn to turn our ideas and experiences into something real.
Creating art lets us explore and try new things.
eg: We use blocks to build a setting for a dance.
We draw a picture about something fun we did at school.
We use a torch to make shadow puppets on the wall.
We wave streamers while dancing to music.
We sit in the audience and watch our friends perform.
We use recycled materials to build a sculpture.

VC2AFCP01.w: We show our art to a small group and see how they respond.
y: Sharing with others helps us feel proud of our work.
We learn from watching how people react to our art.
Presenting builds our confidence to share with bigger groups later.
eg: We perform a dance for our class and watch their faces.
We show our painting to our friends and tell them about it.
We act out a little scene for our teacher.
We play a rhythm pattern for our small group.
We present a collage we made and explain what is in it.
We watch our classmate perform and tell them what we liked.

VC2AFDE01.w: We explore how people from different places use art to share ideas.
y: Learning about different art helps us see the world in new ways.
We understand that art carries stories and meanings from many cultures.
Exploring others' art helps us think about what our own art could say.
eg: We watch a dance and then move our bodies in response to the story.
We listen to music from another culture and talk about how it makes us feel.
We try different dance movements after watching a video of performers.
We look at paintings from artists around the world and spot patterns.
We act out a scene from a story shared by our community.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples tell stories through art.

VC2AFDD01.w: We try different art tools and ways of working to share our ideas.
y: Experimenting helps us find the best way to show what we mean.
Trying many techniques gives us more choices as artists.
We discover new possibilities when we play with materials.
eg: We put dance moves in order to tell a short story.
We try using our body, space and speed in different ways when dancing.
We experiment with soft and loud sounds on instruments.
We try using chalk, crayons and paint to see which works best.
We act out a scene and try changing our voice to suit the character.
We take photos and arrange them to tell a story.

VC2AFDC01.w: We make art that shows meaning using movements or ideas we know.
y: Using what we already know helps us create something meaningful.
Making art with purpose helps us communicate with others.
Practising familiar ideas builds confidence to try new ones.
eg: We use movements we have practised to make up our own short dance.
We match our dance movements to whether the music is fast or slow.
We draw a picture that tells a story about our family.
We build a sculpture using shapes we have learnt about.
We make a pattern with sounds using instruments we know.
We create a scene with puppets using a story we remember.

VC2AFDP01.w: We show our art to people we know and talk about it.
y: Talking about our art helps us understand what we have made.
Sharing with familiar people builds our confidence as artists.
Describing our art helps us notice what we like about it.
eg: We perform a familiar dance and tell our teacher about it.
We show our drawing to a family member and explain what is happening.
We describe the best part of our painting to a friend.
We sing a song we learnt and tell someone why we like it.
We act out a story for our class and answer questions about it.
We play a rhythm for our friend and say how we made it.

VC2ADAFD01.w: We use play and imagination to try out dance ideas.
y: Playing with movement helps us discover what our bodies can do.
Using imagination makes dance more exciting and personal.
Trying things out is how we find new dance ideas.
eg: We make letters of the alphabet with our bodies.
We pretend the floor is slippery and change how we walk.
We imagine we are on hot sand and dance around quickly.
We create shapes with our body — tall, wide, curled up small.
We move like our favourite animal across the room.
We pretend we are melting ice cream and slowly sink down.

VC2ADRFD01.w: We use play and imagination to come up with characters and situations.
y: Imagining characters helps us think about how other people feel.
Play is a safe way to try out ideas and explore stories.
Using our voice and body in drama builds our communication skills.
eg: We imagine how a character from a story might walk or talk.
We make frozen pictures of important moments in a story.
We try using different voices — loud, quiet, squeaky, deep.
We pretend to be characters in a whole-class role-play.
We use puppets to show how a character might feel.
We act out what might happen next in a story we are reading.

VC2AMAFD01.w: We play and use our imagination to discover ideas for media art.
y: Imagining stories is the first step in making great media art.
Playing with images and sounds helps us learn what media can do.
Exploring media through play helps us find our creative voice.
eg: We imagine what a story character looks like and draw them.
We work together to act out a story and wonder 'What happens next?'.
We notice shapes and colours around us and think about how to use them in art.
We try making still and moving images of the same thing.
We make up a character for a story that teaches something important.
We explore how different shapes can represent people and objects.

VC2AMUFD01.w: We play and explore to discover new ideas for our music.
y: Playing with sounds helps us learn what we can do as musicians.
Practising instruments and singing builds skills we can use forever.
Exploring different sounds helps us find music we love.
eg: We talk about what a piece of music made us think about.
We practise singing games to get better at matching notes.
We try making our voice go high and low, loud and soft.
We play classroom instruments and discover different sounds.
We learn the difference between our speaking voice and singing voice.
We play a music game where we copy a rhythm pattern.

VC2AVAFD01.w: We play and explore materials to find ideas for art.
y: Exploring materials helps us discover what we can create.
Play is how we find our best ideas as artists.
Trying different materials gives us more ways to make art.
eg: We squish, roll and poke playdough to explore how it feels.
We imagine how a story character dresses and draw them.
We find shapes around us — circles, squares, triangles — and use them in art.
We make frozen body shapes that look like different objects.
We explore smooth stones, bumpy bark and soft fabric to get ideas.
We try using collage to make a face out of different shapes.

VC2ADAFE01.w: We explore why dance is special to people and communities.
y: Dance is part of celebrations and stories in every culture.
Understanding why people dance helps us respect different traditions.
Knowing about dance in our community helps us feel connected.
eg: We talk about dances we see at family celebrations or events.
We watch a dance from a cartoon or film and say what we notice.
We learn about dances that are part of special community events.
We share with our class what kinds of dancing we do at home.
We notice dance in music videos, games and movies.
We talk about why people dance at weddings, festivals and ceremonies.

VC2ADAFE02.w: We play with movement to find ideas for our dances.
y: Play is a great way to discover new movements and ideas.
Exploring movement through stories sparks our creativity.
When we play, we learn without worrying about being perfect.
eg: We read a story and then move like the characters in it.
We look at a picture and use our bodies to show what is happening.
We try moving different body parts — just our arms, then just our feet.
We explore big movements and tiny movements to see the difference.
We play a movement game where we freeze when the music stops.
We move like waves in the ocean after hearing a story about the sea.

VC2ADAFC01.w: We make up a dance that shows an idea.
y: Dance lets us share ideas using our whole body instead of words.
Creating our own dance helps us be creative and have fun.
When we make a dance, we learn to put ideas together in order.
eg: We make up moves to tell a story about rain falling.
We start in a shape and then move to a new spot in the room.
We jump and stretch to show how a plant grows.
We join our moves with a partner to make a longer dance.
We copy a pattern — jump, spin, freeze — and add our own move.
We change a known dance by making it faster or adding a new step.

VC2ADAFP01.w: We show our dance to our class.
y: Sharing our dance helps others enjoy what we have created.
Performing builds our confidence and helps us feel proud.
Watching each other teaches us new dance ideas.
eg: We do our dance for the class and they watch from their seats.
We walk around the room looking at friends in frozen dance shapes.
We tell our classmates what our dance is about.
We say what we liked about a friend's dance.
We notice things that are the same or different between dances.
We perform a group dance for another class.

VC2ADRFE01.w: We explore why drama is important to people and communities.
y: Drama has been part of human storytelling for thousands of years.
Understanding drama helps us learn about different cultures and times.
Knowing why people create drama helps us connect to our community.
eg: We notice how faces and bodies show feelings in a performance.
We use dramatic play to explore a story we are learning about.
We share what we think about a drama we watched at school.
We learn about storytelling in different cultures through drama.
We explore songs and chants from different times and places.
We talk about dramas we see at school, on TV or at community events.

VC2ADRFE02.w: We play with characters and stories to find ideas for drama.
y: Exploring characters through play helps us understand different people.
Playing with stories sparks new ideas we can use in our drama.
Trying different ways to tell stories builds our creativity.
eg: We use a favourite fairytale to explore different characters.
We try puppets to act out a story — maybe shadow puppets or sock puppets.
We look at how characters move and speak in a drama we watched.
We explore a poem by acting out the words and feelings.
We create recycled puppets to tell a story about caring for the Earth.
We explore characters from stories connected to different cultures.

VC2ADRFC01.w: We make up drama stories to share ideas.
y: Making stories helps us use our imagination in exciting ways.
Drama lets us explore ideas about the world through characters.
Creating stories together teaches us to work with others.
eg: We look at a painting and make up a story about what is happening.
We work in a small group to create a story with a beginning and end.
We decide on the most important part of a story to act out.
We use a picture book to help us create our own scene.
We make still images like a frozen photo to show a moment.
We act out a short story about helping someone in our community.

VC2ADRFP01.w: We share our drama stories with an audience.
y: Performing helps us share our ideas with others.
Presenting drama teaches us to speak clearly and confidently.
Watching others respond to our drama helps us improve.
eg: We think about what we want our audience to understand from our drama.
We act out a scene we created for our class.
We talk about what we enjoyed most about performing.
We present a short scene based on a poem or image.
We answer questions about our drama story after performing.
We watch a friend's drama and say what we liked about it.

VC2AMAFE01.w: We explore why media arts are important to people and communities.
y: Media arts are everywhere — in stories, films, games and on screens.
Understanding media helps us make sense of the world around us.
Learning about media arts from different cultures helps us respect each other.
eg: We talk about animations, games and videos we see at home and school.
We listen to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people share how media connects them to culture and Country.
We learn about media arts during NAIDOC Week or Reconciliation Week.
We look at how different communities use film and images to tell stories.
We share our favourite movie or show and explain why we like it.
We notice media art in our everyday life — posters, signs, cartoons.

VC2AMAFE02.w: We play with media ideas to learn how images and stories work.
y: Playing with media helps us notice how pictures tell stories.
Exploring images teaches us to look more carefully at the world.
We learn how faces, movements and voices show feelings in art.
eg: We look at a picture and talk about what is happening in it.
We explore how faces show feelings — happy, sad, surprised, scared.
We guess what will happen next in a story based on the pictures.
We look at how a book uses pictures to help tell the story.
We act out how characters from a cartoon move and speak.
We compare pictures from different stories and notice what is similar.

VC2AMAFC01.w: We use media tools to make art that shows our ideas.
y: Learning to use media tools helps us share stories in new ways.
Media lets us combine pictures, sounds and words to show ideas.
Creating with media builds skills we will use throughout our lives.
eg: We take photos of our friends playing a game and talk about what is happening.
We draw characters for an animated story our class creates.
We record our voice telling a story about a favourite activity.
We make a picture book using drawings and photos.
We create a short scene showing our favourite things.
We use a tablet to take pictures and add words about our day.

VC2AMAFP01.w: We share our media art with others.
y: Sharing our work helps us learn to present ideas clearly.
Others can enjoy and learn from what we create.
Talking about our art helps us think about what we made.
eg: We show our media art to our teacher and say what it is about.
We take photos at a class event and put them in order to tell the story.
We add sounds to our photos using instruments and our voices.
We draw a series of pictures to illustrate a story and record ourselves telling it.
We share our class picture book and answer questions about it.
We display our photos on a screen and talk about what is happening in each one.

VC2AMUFE01.w: We explore why music is special to people and communities.
y: Music brings people together for celebrations and special events.
Understanding music from different cultures helps us respect each other.
Knowing about music in our community helps us feel connected.
eg: We share songs we hear at home or at community events.
We notice how faces show feelings when people sing or play music.
We talk about music in our lives — at school, home, on TV, in games.
We listen to songs from different cultures and say what we notice.
We explore how music connects people during celebrations.
We learn about songs that are important in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities.

VC2AMUFE02.w: We explore music ideas through play.
y: Playing with rhythm and sound is the most fun way to learn music.
Games help us feel the beat without worrying about being perfect.
Exploring through play helps us discover patterns in music.
eg: We play rhyming games and clap along to the beat.
We make up movements that match the rhythm of a song.
We tap out a pattern and our friend tries to copy it.
We walk fast when the music is fast and slow when it slows down.
We play a freeze game — dance to music and stop when it pauses.
We explore loud and soft sounds by whispering and shouting a rhyme.

VC2AMUFC01.w: We make up music that shares an idea or tells a story.
y: Making music lets us express feelings and ideas in a special way.
Creating our own music helps us learn how sounds work together.
Music is a way to share stories without needing many words.
eg: We make up a song or chant about our favourite food.
We change the words of a known song to tell our own story.
We add body percussion — claps, stamps, pats — to a familiar rhyme.
We create music that sounds like rain using shakers and tapping.
We use instruments to make sounds that match a picture or feeling.
We work together to create a soundscape of our school playground.

VC2AMUFP01.w: We sing and play music for others to enjoy.
y: Performing music helps us share our hard work and creativity.
Singing and playing for others builds our confidence.
Sharing music brings joy to both performers and listeners.
eg: We show our music to our teacher and say what we liked about making it.
We sing a song we learned for our class and try to match the starting note.
We play instruments together, staying in time with the group.
We take photos at our music performance to remember the event.
We add sounds to photos using instruments and voices to tell a musical story.
We perform a soundscape using a plan we followed as a group.

VC2AVAFE01.w: We explore why art is important to people and communities.
y: Art helps people share stories and connect with each other.
Understanding why art matters helps us care about it more.
Art is all around us and connects us to our culture.
eg: We notice art in our community — murals, statues, signs.
We look at pictures in a book and talk about how they tell the story.
We listen to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples share why art connects them to culture, Country and Place.
We share our favourite artwork and explain why we like it.
We look at art in our school and around our neighbourhood.
We notice that art can be on screens, on walls, in parks and in books.

VC2AVAFE02.w: We play with ideas for art by looking and exploring.
y: Looking carefully at the world gives us great ideas for art.
Exploring through play helps us feel free to try anything.
We learn how images show feelings and tell stories.
eg: We look at a picture and explore what is happening in it.
We notice how faces in portraits show different feelings.
We use play to explore ideas from a story or image.
We talk about how characters in cartoons show emotions.
We look at pictures from different cultures and times.
We explore how colours and lines can show feelings like happiness or sadness.

VC2AVAFC01.w: We make art that shows our ideas and experiences.
y: Art lets us share what matters to us, even without words.
Making art helps us notice and remember our experiences.
Creating art is a way to explore our imagination.
eg: We draw a picture of our home with the people and animals we love.
We make a character for an animated story using drawing or collage.
We use a tablet to record our friends playing and add a voice over.
We make art about our favourite game or activity.
We retell a story using puppets or cutout pictures.
We create art inspired by stories from our community.

VC2AVAFP01.w: We share our art with others and talk about it.
y: Sharing art helps us feel proud of what we have created.
Talking about our art helps us notice important details.
Looking at each other's work teaches us new ideas.
eg: We show our painting to our teacher and tell them what it is about.
We do a gallery walk around the room looking at everyone's art.
We say what we like about a friend's artwork.
We take photos of our art and put them in a class display.
We notice things that are the same or different between artworks.
We add sounds to photos of our art event to remember the day.

VC2ADA2E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how communities create dance, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander dance.
y: Every culture has its own dance traditions and stories told through movement.
Learning about dance from different communities helps us respect all people.
Understanding why people dance helps us connect our own dancing to bigger ideas.
eg: We talk about why artists make dance and what dance means to them.
We think about the kinds of dancing we do — at home, at parties, at school.
We learn dances from different cultures through guided activities.
We watch Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander dance performances and discuss what we notice.
We explore dance that people do at celebrations around the world.
We ask 'Do I dance?' and share our dancing experiences with the class.

VC2ADA2D01.w: We learn to use the building blocks of dance and move safely.
y: Understanding dance elements gives us more ways to express ourselves.
Safe dance practice means we can enjoy dancing without getting hurt.
Asking questions about our dance helps us improve and grow.
eg: We ask ourselves 'What levels am I using — high, medium or low?'.
We try moving to music and explore fast, slow, strong and gentle movements.
We improvise dance ideas using a story or piece of music.
We experiment with different energies — bursting, melting, whirling, slinking.
We explore how much space our body takes up when we move big versus small.
We practise warming up before dancing and cooling down afterwards.

VC2ADA2C01.w: We make dances that use our body to share ideas with others.
y: Dance lets us communicate ideas through movement instead of just words.
Creating dance builds our skills in putting ideas together in a sequence.
Working with music and movement helps us connect to stories and feelings.
eg: We create a dance about a big storm using fast, swirling movements.
We listen to music and use it as inspiration for our dance moves.
We make a starting shape, travel across the space, and end in a new shape.
We work with a partner to combine our dance ideas into one sequence.
We try using high, medium and low levels in our dance.
We create a dance that shows how water flows from a river to the sea.

VC2ADA2P01.w: We share our dances with others and talk about what we watched.
y: Sharing dance with an audience makes our creative work come alive.
Watching and discussing dance teaches us to appreciate different styles.
Performing helps us build confidence and teamwork.
eg: We watch a friend's dance and say what it made us think about.
We talk about what the dancer did to show their idea — did they spin, jump or freeze?
We perform our dance for our teacher and classmates.
We share a movement idea like 'heavy and light' through stamping and tiptoeing.
We present a group dance we learned and practised together.
We answer questions about our dance: 'What was your dance about?'.

VC2ADR2E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how communities create drama, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander drama.
y: Drama tells stories that are important to every culture and community.
Learning about drama from diverse cultures builds our understanding of the world.
Exploring the elements of drama helps us make richer stories.
eg: We ask 'Where is this drama happening?', 'Who are the characters?' and 'What is the story about?'.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples share culture through storytelling and drama.
We learn about drama elements like character, setting, mood and tension.
We watch a drama and discuss the characters and what happened.
We explore how communities use drama for celebrations and special events.
We compare drama from different cultures and notice similarities and differences.

VC2ADR2D01.w: We build our skills in creating and performing drama.
y: Practising drama skills helps us become better storytellers.
Learning to use our voice, face and body well makes our drama powerful.
Understanding how drama works helps us create more interesting stories.
eg: We ask 'What is this drama about?' and 'What does it mean to me?'.
We practise showing characters using our face, voice and body.
We learn about the space between performers and the audience.
We try using different expressions to show how a character feels.
We watch drama and think about how the actors used their voices.
We develop our own drama scenes and talk about how to improve them.

VC2ADR2C01.w: We use different drama forms to create real and imagined stories.
y: Drama helps us explore stories and imagine new possibilities.
Creating drama teaches us to think about characters and what happens to them.
Using different drama forms gives us many ways to tell a story.
eg: We explore 'What might happen next?' and 'What happened before the story began?'.
We use puppets to create a new version of a story we know.
We use costume pieces and props to help bring characters to life.
We play a drama game where we act out different situations.
We create a short play based on a personal experience.
We explore process drama to imagine what it would be like to be someone else.

VC2ADR2P01.w: We share our drama stories with an audience.
y: Performing for others helps us bring our creative ideas to life.
Sharing drama teaches us to speak clearly and follow cues.
Hearing stories through drama connects us to each other.
eg: We present a scene with a clear beginning, middle and end.
We retell a community story with the help of someone from that community.
We follow stage directions and respond to cues during a performance.
We speak clearly so our audience can understand us.
We present our drama to our class and ask for their feedback.
We perform a short scene based on a story we have been exploring.

VC2AMA2E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how people experience media arts, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander media arts.
y: Media arts connect us to stories from many different communities.
Understanding who makes media helps us think about the messages we see.
Learning about media from diverse cultures teaches us respect and understanding.
eg: We ask 'What story is this media arts work telling us?' and 'Who made it?'.
We discuss what permission means when using other people's images or work.
We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use media to share culture.
We learn about media arts during NAIDOC Week or Reconciliation Week.
We create class rules for respectfully using images and sounds in our work.
We compare media arts from different places and talk about what we notice.

VC2AMA2D01.w: We explore how to use cameras, computers and other tools to capture images and sounds.
y: Learning to use media tools opens up new ways to create and share.
Experimenting with technology helps us understand how media works.
These skills help us express our ideas using images, sounds and words.
eg: We take a photo up close and then far away to see how it changes.
We use a device to capture short videos and add sound effects.
We retell a school day using photos and captions.
We explore how changing the words on an image changes its meaning.
We record our voice telling a story to go with our pictures.
We experiment with video to capture our class at play.

VC2AMA2C01.w: We use media tools and techniques to build stories and images.
y: Learning media skills helps us tell stories in exciting new ways.
Understanding how images and sounds work together makes us better creators.
Media skills help us communicate ideas to lots of people.
eg: We choose which photos to keep or delete when making a media story.
We select and edit images, sounds and text to recreate characters from a story.
We capture images and put them in order to tell a story, like a comic book.
We ask 'Which image interests me and why?' to make design choices.
We make a simple slideshow that retells a familiar story.
We add text to images to help explain what is happening.

VC2AMA2P01.w: We share our media art with others in our class or school.
y: Presenting our work helps us practise explaining our ideas clearly.
Sharing media art teaches us about respecting others' stories and images.
Seeing how people respond to our media helps us grow as creators.
eg: We collect class stories and present them as a news bulletin, with permission from everyone involved.
We show a media artwork to our class and explain how we made it.
We create, rehearse and record a radio play and share it with another class.
We follow protocols about asking permission before using cultural material.
We present a photo story and talk about what we enjoyed making.
We watch a classmate's media artwork and give positive feedback.

VC2AMU2E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how people make music, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples.
y: Music is part of every community and culture on Earth.
Learning about music from different places helps us appreciate diversity.
Understanding how music is used in communities connects us to the wider world.
eg: We explore music in our local community and ask 'What instruments can I hear?'.
We listen to music from different cultures and notice instruments and voices.
We describe shapes and patterns we hear in music — like a repeating rhythm.
We clap along to repeating rhythmic patterns in songs.
We compare music from different places and talk about similarities and differences.
We learn about how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use music to connect with culture and Country.

VC2AMU2D01.w: We develop our skills in listening, singing and playing instruments.
y: Good listening skills help us in music and in everything else we do.
Practising singing and playing gives us more ways to express ourselves.
Building musical skills lets us join in and enjoy music with others.
eg: We experiment with our voice — making it slide, bend and snap between notes.
We play music games that help us listen carefully to each other.
We try to match pitch when singing a song together.
We practise holding instruments properly to make a clear, clean sound.
We explore making different moods with our voice — whisper, speak, sing, shout.
We play listening games where we identify which instrument is playing.

VC2AMU2C01.w: We combine different musical ideas when we compose and perform music.
y: Learning to put musical ideas together builds our creative skills.
Composing music lets us express ourselves in a unique way.
Making decisions about music teaches us to think carefully and creatively.
eg: We decide whether our music should be fast or slow, loud or soft.
We improvise melodies and add a steady drone accompaniment underneath.
We use our voices and body percussion to create a piece with loud/soft and fast/slow parts.
We combine pitch and rhythm patterns to make a short composition.
We choose instruments and decide how they will be played in our piece.
We create contrasting sections — one part fast and exciting, another slow and calm.

VC2AMU2P01.w: We sing and play music for an audience.
y: Performing for others lets us share the music we have worked hard on.
Working together to perform teaches us teamwork and cooperation.
Sharing music with an audience brings everyone joy.
eg: We decide whether to stand in curved or straight rows so we can see the conductor.
We sing a song we have learnt for our peers and teachers.
We use our singing voice (not speaking voice) when performing.
We hold our instruments so they make a clear, clean sound.
We perform a chant or rap we composed for another class.
We stay in time with the group by watching for cues from the leader.

VC2AVA2E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how people make visual arts, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples.
y: Art has been made by people in every culture for thousands of years.
Learning about art from different communities builds our respect and understanding.
Exploring artists and their work helps us get inspired for our own art.
eg: We look at an artwork and ask 'What do I think is the story in this?'.
We investigate who the artist is, where they are from and what materials they used.
We use 'See, Think, Wonder' to explore artworks from a gallery or collection.
We explore artworks by living Australian artists from diverse backgrounds.
We learn about art by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists and what it means.
We look at art from our local community and talk about what we recognise.

VC2AVA2D01.w: We explore different art techniques and materials to see what they can do.
y: Exploring materials helps us discover what works best for our ideas.
Making mistakes and experiments in art often lead to exciting discoveries.
The more techniques we try, the more choices we have as artists.
eg: We play with natural materials like sticks, leaves and mud to make art.
We ask 'What was challenging?' and 'What did I discover about this material?'.
We try layering pastels, then watercolour, then ink to see what happens.
We experiment with playdough to explore texture and shape.
We look at artworks by others and try layering materials to build up a surface.
We reflect on which materials worked well and which we want to try again.

VC2AVA2C01.w: We use art techniques and materials to create artworks that show our ideas.
y: Making art teaches us to turn our ideas into something real and visible.
Experimenting with materials helps us find new ways to express ourselves.
Art is a powerful way to share experiences and tell stories.
eg: We brainstorm ideas and ask 'What happens if I make the subject really big?'.
We use both messy, accidental marks and careful, detailed marks in one painting.
We make a relief print using textured paper, glue and string.
We experiment with dark and light tones to change the mood of our work.
We combine paint blobs with added outlines to see what pictures emerge.
We cut out printed shapes and arrange them into a collage of an animal or face.

VC2AVA2P01.w: We share our art and explain what we like about it.
y: Talking about our art helps us understand our own creative choices.
Sharing art with others helps them see the world through our eyes.
Presenting art builds our confidence and communication skills.
eg: We describe our artwork to the class and explain what we like about it.
We create an experimental artwork using unusual objects as brushes.
We use a cardboard window frame to choose the best part of a painting.
We draw an everyday object, then play a matching game with our classmates.
We display our art in a class gallery and do a gallery walk.
We explain how we made our artwork and what choices we made along the way.

VC2ADA4E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how dance is created across cultures, times and places.
y: Every culture has dance traditions that carry history and meaning.
Learning about dance from different times and places broadens our view of the world.
Understanding the purpose behind dances helps us appreciate them more deeply.
eg: We explore how shapes are combined and varied in dances from the past and present.
We investigate the elements used in dances we watch and create.
We research a cultural dance and present what we learn to the class.
We compare how dance is used in different celebrations around the world.
We explore the role of dance in community events and gatherings.
We investigate how technology has changed the way dances are shared and experienced.

VC2ADA4E02.w: We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use dance to express connection to Country and Place.
y: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander dance carries deep cultural knowledge and responsibility.
Understanding First Nations dance helps us respect and appreciate the world's oldest living cultures.
Dance is one of the ways cultural knowledge has been shared for tens of thousands of years.
eg: We explore specific cultural dances guided by a cultural leader or cultural dancer.
We learn how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples express their responsibility for Country through dance.
We explore how dance connects to stories about the land, seasons and animals.
We learn about the protocols for watching and learning cultural dances respectfully.
We discuss how dance helps keep cultural knowledge alive across generations.
We explore how different Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities have their own unique dance traditions.

VC2ADA4D01.w: We develop our dance skills by using the elements of dance and practising safe movement.
y: Building strong dance skills gives us more tools for expressing our ideas.
Safe dance practice protects our bodies so we can keep dancing.
Understanding the elements of dance helps us communicate meaning through movement.
eg: We create short dances that show feelings based on a stimulus, like a found object or a song.
We practise locomotor movements like skipping, galloping and sliding with good technique.
We learn to warm up our bodies properly before dancing and cool down after.
We explore how changing speed, level and direction changes the feeling of a movement.
We practise transitions — how we smoothly move from one section of a dance to the next.
We reflect on our practice and set goals to improve a specific dance skill.

VC2ADA4C01.w: We use different movements and structures to create dances that communicate ideas and meaning.
y: Choreography lets us arrange movements to tell a story or share a perspective.
Learning to structure a dance teaches us to organise ideas clearly.
Creating dances builds our ability to express things that are hard to put into words.
eg: We create a dance that tells a story and ask 'Is there a clear beginning, middle and end?'.
We arrange dancers in different groups or formations to show relationships between characters.
We use improvisation to discover new movement ideas before shaping them into a dance.
We create a dance inspired by a natural event like a bushfire or flood.
We experiment with unison (moving together) and contrast (moving differently) in our dance.
We use repetition to emphasise an important moment in our dance story.

VC2ADA4P01.w: We perform dances we have learnt or created for an audience.
y: Performing helps us share our creative work and connect with viewers.
Presenting dance teaches us to communicate clearly through our bodies.
Watching and discussing performances deepens our understanding of dance.
eg: We ask 'What clues in the dance tell you where it was made and why?' when watching others perform.
We present a dance we created to an audience at a school assembly.
We perform a dance we learnt from a different cultural tradition.
We rehearse so our movements are clear and confident for our audience.
We reflect on our performance and identify what went well and what to improve.
We present our dance in both formal (stage) and informal (classroom) settings.

VC2ADR4E01.w: We explore how stories are created and performed in drama across cultures, times and places.
y: Understanding drama traditions from around the world enriches our own storytelling.
Exploring drama across cultures helps us see different ways people make sense of the world.
Investigating how drama works helps us become more thoughtful audience members.
eg: We watch a drama performance and ask 'What is the purpose of this drama?'.
We discuss which features and ideas in a drama come from specific cultural traditions.
We compare storytelling techniques from different cultures and time periods.
We explore how drama has been used throughout history to share important messages.
We investigate how different communities use drama for entertainment, education or ceremony.
We research a drama form from another culture and share what we discover.

VC2ADR4E02.w: We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use drama and storytelling to show their connection to Country and Place.
y: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander storytelling is one of the oldest traditions in the world.
Understanding First Nations drama helps us respect the deep cultural knowledge it carries.
Drama and storytelling help keep languages, laws and histories alive across generations.
eg: We explore how drama communicates knowledge about Country, seasons and natural events.
We learn how storytelling connects to cultural responsibilities and protocols.
We engage with resources created by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples about their drama traditions.
We learn about the role of Elders in passing on stories through drama.
We explore how drama carries knowledge about caring for the land and waterways.
We discuss how these storytelling traditions continue and evolve today.

VC2ADR4D01.w: We build our drama skills by using voice, movement and the elements of drama.
y: Developing expressive skills makes our performances more powerful and believable.
Practising drama techniques gives us more ways to create engaging stories.
Refining our skills helps us communicate ideas more clearly to an audience.
eg: We experiment with mood by creating different scenes that explore conflict.
We practise changing the volume, speed and pitch of our voice to create characters.
We use body language and facial expressions to show how a character feels.
We explore dramatic tension by building suspense in a scene.
We try different approaches to the same scene and compare the results.
We give and receive constructive feedback on our drama work.

VC2ADR4C01.w: We create drama and scripts using expressive skills and drama elements to share ideas.
y: Creating drama helps us explore different perspectives and understand other people's experiences.
Devising original drama builds our creativity and problem-solving skills.
Drama lets us experiment with characters, relationships and storylines in a safe space.
eg: We create drama by exploring different characters and their relationships.
We improvise scenes from a starting point or stimulus, like a photograph or news story.
We take on character roles and accept dramatic situations to see where the story goes.
We develop a short script that includes a conflict and a resolution.
We use 'hot seat' — where we ask questions to a classmate in character.
We explore a community issue through drama, showing different viewpoints.

VC2ADR4P01.w: We rehearse and perform drama for audiences in both casual and formal settings.
y: Presenting drama to an audience brings our creative work to life.
Performing teaches us to use our voice, body and space effectively.
Sharing drama with others builds confidence and teamwork skills.
eg: We use facial expressions and movements to create believable characters for our audience.
We vary our voice to show changes in mood or character during a performance.
We perform an improvised drama for our class and respond to feedback.
We present a scripted scene at a school assembly.
We use simple costumes or props to help communicate our characters.
We reflect on audience reactions and what they tell us about our performance.

VC2AMA4E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how media arts are created across cultures, times and places.
y: Understanding how media is made helps us think critically about what we see and hear.
Exploring media from different contexts teaches us about diverse perspectives and stories.
Knowing the history and purpose of media helps us use it more thoughtfully.
eg: We sequence images, sounds and text to create a clear story structure.
We identify and describe what is being represented in media arts from different societies.
We explore how media arts have changed over time with new technologies.
We compare how different cultures use media to share their stories.
We discuss the role of media in our daily lives — news, games, film, social media.
We investigate how media arts connect communities across distances.

VC2AMA4E02.w: We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use media arts to share their connection to Country and Place.
y: Media arts give Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples powerful tools to share their stories with the world.
Understanding how First Nations media artists work helps us appreciate the depth of their cultural expression.
Media arts help keep traditional stories alive while reaching new audiences.
eg: We explore how traditional stories are shared through film, animation and digital media.
We investigate media arts works created or co-created by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples.
We learn about the protocols for sharing cultural stories through media.
We explore how First Nations filmmakers use media to share knowledge about Country.
We discuss how media arts help revitalise languages and cultural practices.
We look at how media technology allows communities to record and share their stories widely.

VC2AMA4D01.w: We develop media production skills by exploring how images, sounds and text can be shaped to share ideas.
y: Experimenting with media tools helps us discover new ways to tell stories.
Understanding how changing one element changes the whole meaning gives us creative power.
Building technical skills opens up more possibilities for our creative work.
eg: We ask 'How does the meaning change when I change the sound effects on this image?'.
We explore how camera framing — close-up, medium, wide — changes how we see a subject.
We experiment with editing images and sounds to create different moods.
We try different transitions between scenes to see which works best for our story.
We explore how text placement and font choice changes the feeling of a media work.
We investigate how lighting and colour affect the mood of photographs.

VC2AMA4C01.w: We use media tools and production processes to create works that communicate ideas and meaning.
y: Learning media production skills prepares us to communicate in a media-rich world.
Understanding how media is constructed helps us be both better creators and critical viewers.
Media arts let us tell stories using images, sounds, text and technology together.
eg: We take photographs that show characters through setting, costume and body language.
We create a soundscape to accompany a moving image and set the mood.
We plan and create a short animation that tells a story with a clear beginning, middle and end.
We design a comic strip that communicates a message about our community.
We combine images and text to create a digital poster about an issue we care about.
We create a short film trailer that uses camera angles and music to build excitement.

VC2AMA4P01.w: We share our media arts works with audiences while practising responsible media use.
y: Sharing our work teaches us to present ideas clearly and professionally.
Responsible media practice means we respect other people's stories, images and rights.
Learning to credit and acknowledge others prepares us for ethical media use.
eg: We present our media work and use appropriate language to discuss its meaning.
We create credits in a title sequence to acknowledge everyone who helped.
We consider ethical questions about whose stories we are telling and how.
We present a short film to different audiences — our class, parents, another year level.
We write artist statements about our media arts works to accompany a display.
We follow protocols about permission when using images or stories from others.

VC2AMU4E01.w: We explore where, when, why and how music is composed and performed across cultures, times and places.
y: Music exists in every culture and serves many different purposes.
Learning about music from around the world broadens our understanding and appreciation.
Exploring different music traditions helps us see how music connects people.
eg: We explore the role of music in celebrations like Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander ceremonies, Lunar New Year and Christmas.
We discuss how music is used to express identity, joy, grief and community belonging.
We compare instruments and musical styles from different parts of the world.
We investigate how music has changed over time — from ancient to contemporary.
We explore how technology has changed the way music is created and shared.
We listen to music from diverse traditions and describe what we notice about the elements.

VC2AMU4E02.w: We explore how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples use music to express their connection to Country and Place.
y: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander music carries deep cultural knowledge about Country.
Understanding First Nations music helps us respect the world's oldest continuous musical traditions.

VC2ADR8D01.w: We develop and refine our skills in drama elements, performance, expression and design across specific styles and forms.

Understanding style-specific conventions helps us create authentic and effective performances.
Refining our skills through practice and feedback leads to consistent improvement.
eg: We develop vocal techniques appropriate to different drama styles — projection, accent, rhythm.
We refine our physical performance skills including movement, gesture and spatial awareness.
We practise using design elements to enhance our drama pieces.
We study specific drama forms and practise the conventions that define them.
We give and receive detailed feedback using appropriate drama terminology.
We set skill-development goals and work towards them throughout the term.

VC2ADR8D02.w: We reflect on, analyse and document our own and others' drama to inform our creative decisions.
y: Reflective practice is what separates thoughtful art-making from simply going through the motions.
Analysing drama — our own and others' — deepens our understanding of how theatre communicates.
Documenting our process creates a valuable record of our artistic development.
eg: We maintain a drama journal documenting our creative process, decisions and reflections.
We analyse professional theatre productions and discuss how they achieve their effects.
We use video recordings to evaluate our rehearsals and identify areas for improvement.
We write critical responses to drama we have watched, using appropriate terminology.
We reflect on how feedback from others has influenced our creative decisions.
We compare different interpretations of the same script or stimulus and discuss the choices made.

VC2ADR8C01.w: We devise drama and interpret scripts, manipulating drama elements and applying conventions of specific styles and forms.
y: Working with both devised and scripted drama develops our versatility as theatre-makers.
Understanding different styles and forms gives us a wider range of tools for storytelling.
Manipulating drama elements with intention creates more powerful and meaningful performances.
eg: We devise an original drama piece that explores a contemporary social issue from multiple angles.
We interpret a scripted scene, making deliberate choices about character, subtext and staging.
We apply conventions of a specific drama style — such as physical theatre, verbatim or absurdist.
We experiment with non-linear storytelling techniques in our devised work.
We use elements of drama including tension, mood, contrast and symbol to deepen meaning.
We collaborate to create ensemble-based drama that challenges and engages our audience.

VC2ADR8C02.w: We apply design elements like set, costume, lighting and sound to enhance meaning in our drama.
y: Design elements transform drama from simple staging into a rich theatrical experience.
Understanding how design communicates meaning makes us more thoughtful creators and audience members.
Integrating design with performance teaches us to think about theatre as a complete art form.
eg: We design a simple set that communicates the time, place and atmosphere of our drama.
We select or create costumes that reveal character and support the style of our production.
We experiment with lighting to create mood shifts, focus attention and suggest time changes.
We use sound design — music, effects, silence — to enhance tension and emotion.
We create a design concept that unifies set, costume, lighting and sound around a central idea.
We evaluate how our design choices affect the audience's understanding of the drama.

VC2ADR8P01.w: We rehearse, refine and perform drama for different audiences using appropriate performance skills and conventions.
y: Performing for varied audiences develops our adaptability and communication skills.
The rehearsal process teaches us discipline, collaboration and attention to detail.
Presenting polished work demonstrates our respect for both the art form and our audience.
eg: We rehearse systematically, refining performance quality through multiple run-throughs.
We perform in a range of settings — classroom, theatre space, outdoor venue — adapting our approach.
We use appropriate conventions for the style we are performing in.
We adapt our performance for different audiences while maintaining artistic integrity.
We manage pre-performance preparation including warm-ups, technical checks and cue management.
We evaluate our performances and identify specific areas for growth.

VC2AMA8E01.w: We investigate how media arts concepts communicate ideas across cultures, times and places.
y: Understanding how media operates across different contexts develops our critical media literacy.
Investigating diverse media practices reveals how culture, technology and storytelling intersect.

We investigate how media representations differ across cultures and societies.
We analyse how media constructs meaning through codes, conventions and technical choices.
We explore the relationship between media institutions, audiences and texts.
We compare media practices from different countries and discuss cultural influences.
We investigate how social media and digital platforms have changed media creation and consumption.

VC2AMA8E02.w: We explore the diversity of media arts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples and culturally responsive approaches to creating media.
