Culturally responsive media practice ensures we engage with First Nations media work respectfully and ethically.
Understanding First Nations media arts challenges us to think about representation, ownership and voice.
eg: We explore films, animations, digital art and other media created by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists.
We learn about cultural protocols for creating and sharing media that involves First Nations content.
We investigate how First Nations filmmakers and media artists tell their own stories on their own terms.
We discuss issues of representation, ownership and voice in Australian media.
We explore how media arts support the revitalisation and sharing of First Nations languages and cultures.
We engage with media works that challenge stereotypes and present diverse First Nations perspectives.

VC2AMA8D01.w: We develop media production skills throughout the production process using media languages and technologies.
y: Building strong production skills gives us greater creative control over our media work.
Understanding media languages means we can deliberately construct meaning through our technical choices.
Developing skills across the production process prepares us for complex creative projects.
eg: We develop our skills in camera operation, including framing, focus and movement.
We learn editing techniques that create pace, rhythm and meaning in our media work.
We experiment with sound design — recording, mixing and manipulating audio.
We develop skills in digital image manipulation and graphic design.
We explore how different media technologies offer different creative possibilities.
We build our technical skills through guided practice and independent experimentation.

VC2AMA8D02.w: We reflect on, analyse and document our own and others' media arts works to inform our production decisions.
y: Critical analysis of media helps us understand how production choices create meaning.
Reflecting on our own process helps us make more deliberate and effective decisions.
Documenting our practice creates a record of our learning and artistic development.
eg: We maintain a production journal documenting our creative decisions and their rationale.
We analyse professional media works and discuss how they achieve their effects.
We use audience feedback to evaluate and refine our media productions.
We compare different approaches to the same brief and discuss the choices made.
We write critical analyses of media works using appropriate media terminology.
We reflect on how our skills have developed throughout a production project.

VC2AMA8C01.w: We design and structure media arts works to communicate ideas, perspectives and meaning to an intended audience.

Structuring media works effectively means our message reaches and resonates with viewers.

We design a short film that uses narrative structure to engage a specific audience.

We consider how genre conventions and audience expectations shape our design decisions.
We evaluate our design choices against the needs and preferences of our intended audience.

VC2AMA8C02.w: We apply production processes and media concepts to construct media works that communicate ideas and meaning.
y: Production skills enable us to realise our creative vision through media technologies.
Understanding media concepts helps us construct representations that are intentional and effective.
Developing production competence prepares us for creative work in an increasingly digital world.
eg: We manage the full production process — pre-production, production and post-production.
We apply media concepts like representation, audience and institution to our creative work.
We use camera techniques, editing and sound design to construct meaning in our media work.
We create media work that demonstrates understanding of how codes and conventions create meaning.
We collaborate on a production, taking on different roles — director, editor, sound designer.
We reflect on how our production choices shape the audience's interpretation of our work.

VC2AMA8P01.w: We refine and present our media works, considering genre conventions, audience engagement and context.
y: Presenting media professionally teaches us to consider how context shapes meaning and reception.
Understanding genre conventions helps us meet and creatively subvert audience expectations.

eg: We refine our media work based on test screenings and audience feedback.
We present our media arts works in appropriate formats and settings for our audience.
We consider how genre conventions shape audience expectations and use this knowledge deliberately.
We write artist statements or production notes to accompany our media presentations.
We select appropriate distribution methods — screening, exhibition, online platform — for our work.
We evaluate the effectiveness of our presentation choices in reaching our intended audience.

VC2AMU8E01.w: We investigate how composers and performers use music elements and compositional devices across cultures, times and places.
y: Studying music across diverse contexts reveals both universal principles and culturally specific practices.
Understanding how music communicates across cultures develops our critical and musical thinking.
Exploring diverse music traditions inspires innovation in our own composing and performing.

We compare compositional approaches across different musical cultures and historical periods.
We analyse how technology has influenced the development of music over time.
We investigate the social, cultural and historical contexts that shape musical practices.
We explore how different cultures use music for ceremony, storytelling, celebration and protest.
We study how globalisation has led to fusion styles and cross-cultural musical collaboration.

VC2AMU8E02.w: We explore the diversity of music by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander musicians and consider culturally appropriate approaches.

Culturally appropriate engagement ensures we approach First Nations music with the respect it deserves.
Understanding these musical traditions challenges stereotypes and reveals the depth of the world's oldest continuous cultures.

We learn about cultural protocols for engaging with First Nations music and performance.
We investigate how contemporary First Nations musicians blend traditional and modern elements.
We discuss the importance of cultural ownership and appropriate use of First Nations music.
We explore how music connects to Country, language, ceremony and community.
We engage with resources created by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander musicians about their practice.

VC2AMU8D01.w: We develop and practise listening, vocal and instrumental skills to achieve expressive effects when making music.
y: Technical proficiency is the foundation that enables musical expression and creativity.

Developing our skills allows us to bring our musical ideas to life with greater precision.

We practise aural skills like interval recognition, rhythmic dictation and chord identification.
We work on achieving specific expressive effects through dynamic control and articulation.
We develop our ability to sight-read music in different styles.
We practise ensemble skills including listening, blending and following a conductor.
We set technical goals and work towards them through structured practice routines.

VC2AMU8D02.w: We reflect on, analyse and document our own and others' music to inform our choices as composers and performers.
y: Critical listening and analysis deepens our understanding of how music achieves its effects.
Reflecting on our own work helps us make more intentional creative decisions.
Documenting our musical thinking creates a record of our artistic growth.
eg: We maintain a music journal documenting our compositional choices and performance reflections.

We write programme notes that explain our compositional intentions and process.

VC2AMU8C01.w: We interpret music in various forms and styles, manipulating music elements and using relevant vocal and instrumental techniques.
y: Interpreting diverse musical styles develops our versatility and deepens our musical understanding.
Manipulating music elements with skill allows us to shape the emotional and expressive impact of our performance.

VC2AMU8P01.w: We rehearse and perform music for audiences using relevant vocal and instrumental techniques and performance skills.

We evaluate our performances and set goals for ongoing improvement.

VC2AVA8E01.w: We investigate how visual conventions, processes and materials communicate ideas across cultures, times and places.

VC2AVA8D01.w: We develop and refine our visual arts skills using conventions, processes and materials to create artworks.

VC2AVA8D02.w: We reflect on, analyse and document our own and others' art practices to inform our creative decisions.

VC2AVA8C01.w: We select and manipulate visual conventions, processes and materials to create artworks.

VC2AVC8E01.w: We investigate how visual communication practices are used to communicate ideas across cultures, times and places.

VC2AVC8E02.w: We explore the diversity of design solutions across different fields and consider culturally, legally and ethically responsible approaches.

VC2AVC8D01.w: We develop our visual communication skills by experimenting with visual language, methods, media and materials.

VC2AVC8D02.w: We reflect on, analyse and document how we and others develop design ideas, concepts and solutions.

VC2ADA10E01.w: We investigate how performers and choreographers across cultures and times use dance elements and techniques to communicate ideas and achieve specific purposes.

Rigorous reflection and analysis are fundamental to mature artistic practice.
Documenting our evaluative thinking demonstrates the depth of our engagement with the art form.
eg: We write detailed critical responses to professional theatre productions.
We evaluate our own and others' drama work against artistic intentions and audience reception.
We analyse how directors and playwrights use theatrical conventions to construct meaning.
We maintain comprehensive documentation of our creative process and critical reflections.
We use theatrical terminology with precision when analysing and discussing drama.
We engage in structured peer critique that is both rigorous and constructive.

VC2ADR10C01.w: We create drama and produce theatre using narrative and non-narrative forms, drama elements and style-specific conventions to communicate ideas and challenge perspectives.
y: Creating theatre at this level involves making sophisticated artistic choices that challenge and engage audiences.
Working with both narrative and non-narrative forms develops our versatility as theatre-makers.
The ability to challenge perspectives through drama is a powerful tool for social and artistic expression.
eg: We create devised theatre that challenges audience assumptions about a social or political issue.
We interpret a contemporary script, making sophisticated choices about style, form and meaning.
We experiment with non-narrative forms — physical theatre, verbatim, postdramatic — to communicate ideas.
We develop multi-layered drama that operates on both literal and symbolic levels.
We integrate diverse theatrical influences into our work to create original and compelling theatre.
We produce drama that demonstrates a clear directorial vision and artistic coherence.

VC2ADR10C02.w: We apply design areas to both devised and scripted works to enhance meaning for an intended audience.
y: Design is integral to how audiences experience and interpret theatre.
Applying design with sophistication demonstrates understanding of theatre as a unified art form.
The ability to integrate design with performance creates richer, more powerful theatrical experiences.
eg: We develop a unified design concept that supports and enhances the meaning of our drama.
We use lighting design to create atmosphere, symbolism and emotional shifts.
We design soundscapes that deepen the audience's immersion in the dramatic world.
We create costume and set designs that communicate character, period and style.
We experiment with multimedia and digital technologies as design elements.
We evaluate how our design choices affect audience interpretation and engagement.

VC2ADR10P01.w: We plan, rehearse, refine and perform drama in different contexts for various audiences and in a range of spaces.
y: Performing in diverse contexts develops our adaptability, professionalism and artistic maturity.
Managing all aspects of performance preparation builds essential project management skills.
The ability to connect with different audiences in various settings demonstrates true performance mastery.
eg: We plan and manage complete productions from concept through rehearsal to performance.
We perform in diverse spaces — theatre, classroom, site-specific, community venues.
We adapt our performance approach for audiences of different ages, backgrounds and expectations.
We demonstrate mastery of style-specific conventions and performance techniques.
We collaborate effectively across artistic, technical and production roles.
We critically evaluate our production work and articulate areas for professional development.

VC2AMA10E01.w: We investigate how media artists across cultures and times use media concepts to construct representations in media arts works.
y: Deep investigation of media practices reveals how media shapes our understanding of the world.
Understanding how representations are constructed across contexts develops sophisticated media literacy.
This knowledge empowers us to be both critical consumers and responsible creators of media.
eg: We research how media artists from different cultures and periods construct representations.
We investigate the relationship between media institutions, technologies and cultural production.
We analyse how media representations reflect and shape societal attitudes and values.
We compare media practices across different cultural and historical contexts.
We study how digital technologies and social media have transformed media creation and distribution.
We explore the ethical and social responsibilities of media practitioners.

VC2AMA10E02.w: We investigate how media artists, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples, celebrate and challenge multiple perspectives of Australian identity.
y: Australian media arts is enriched by diverse perspectives that celebrate and question what it means to be Australian.
First Nations media artists are leading voices in redefining Australian identity and challenging dominant narratives.
Investigating these works develops our understanding of representation, power and voice in media.
eg: We study media works by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists that celebrate cultural identity.
We analyse how First Nations media artists challenge stereotypes and dominant narratives.
We investigate how diverse Australian media artists represent and question Australian identity.
We discuss issues of representation, ownership and self-determination in Australian media.
We explore how media arts support cultural revitalisation and community empowerment.
We engage critically with questions about whose stories are told, by whom and for whom.

VC2AMA10D01.w: We experiment with media processes, skills, language and technologies throughout the production process to construct representations that communicate ideas.
y: Experimentation with media tools and techniques is how we develop innovative creative approaches.
Understanding how media language constructs representations gives us power as both creators and critical consumers.
Pushing the boundaries of our technical skills leads to more original and compelling media work.
eg: We experiment with emerging media technologies to discover new creative possibilities.
We develop advanced skills in editing, compositing and post-production techniques.
We explore how different media languages construct different representations of the same subject.
We push creative boundaries by combining traditional and digital media techniques.
We develop specialised skills in areas like documentary, animation, interactive media or sound design.
We maintain a practice journal documenting our technical experimentation and discoveries.

VC2AMA10D02.w: We reflect on, analyse, evaluate and document our own and others' media works to refine and inform our choices throughout the production process.
y: Critical evaluation of media at this level requires sophisticated understanding of how media constructs meaning.
Continuous reflection throughout production leads to more intentional and effective creative decisions.
Analytical skills developed in media arts transfer to critical engagement with all forms of media.
eg: We write detailed critical analyses of professional media works using media theory and terminology.
We evaluate our production work against artistic intentions, technical standards and audience impact.
We analyse how media practitioners use codes and conventions to position audiences.
We maintain comprehensive production journals documenting decisions, reflections and evaluations.
We compare contrasting media approaches to similar content and evaluate their effectiveness.
We use audience research and feedback to refine our production decisions.

VC2AMA10C01.w: We design and structure media arts works that communicate ideas, perspectives and meaning.
y: Sophisticated design and structuring of media demonstrates our ability to control how audiences receive our message.
Understanding media structure at a deep level is essential for effective communication in our media-rich world.
Design thinking in media arts combines artistic vision with strategic audience engagement.
eg: We design media works with clear conceptual frameworks that guide all creative decisions.
We structure our media work using both conventional and experimental approaches.
We consider how platform, format and distribution channel affect the design of our work.
We create media that uses genre conventions strategically — following, adapting or subverting them.
We design media experiences that encourage audience participation or multiple interpretations.
We develop design proposals that articulate our vision, audience and communication purpose.

VC2AMA10C02.w: We select and apply production processes and media concepts to produce media works that communicate ideas and meaning for intended audiences.
y: Advanced production skills enable us to produce professional-quality media that achieves our artistic intentions.
Understanding media concepts at a theoretical level strengthens the purposefulness of our production choices.
Connecting production skills with conceptual understanding is what distinguishes thoughtful media practice.
eg: We manage complex production projects from pre-production planning through to final delivery.
We apply media concepts like representation, ideology and audience positioning in our work.
We use advanced camera, editing and sound techniques to construct sophisticated media texts.
We produce work that demonstrates both technical proficiency and conceptual depth.
We collaborate across production roles, demonstrating professional communication and teamwork.
We produce media arts works in specific genres, applying conventions with understanding and purpose.

VC2AMA10P01.w: We critique and evaluate media arts presentations in different contexts to inform how we present our own media works.
y: Critical evaluation of media presentation helps us make informed choices about how our own work reaches audiences.
Understanding how context shapes reception is essential for effective media communication.
Professional presentation skills prepare us for careers and further study in media and creative industries.
eg: We critique media presentations across different genres, platforms and contexts.
We evaluate how context — festival, gallery, online, broadcast — affects audience experience.
We select appropriate presentation formats and platforms for our media work.
We develop professional presentation materials including artist statements and production notes.
We present our work in contexts that maximise its impact and reach its intended audience.
We critically reflect on the reception of our media work and use this to inform future practice.

VC2AMU10E01.w: We investigate how composers and performers use music elements, compositional devices and techniques to develop personal voice and achieve specific purposes across cultures and times.
y: Investigating how musicians develop personal voice informs our own journey toward artistic individuality.
Understanding how music achieves specific purposes across contexts reveals its extraordinary communicative power.
Cross-cultural and historical study provides the knowledge base for informed and sophisticated music-making.
eg: We research how specific composers developed their personal voice within and beyond established traditions.
We investigate how music serves different social, cultural, spiritual and political purposes across societies.
We analyse how technological developments have shaped compositional and performance practices.
We compare musical traditions from different cultures and examine their distinctive features.
We study the relationship between music, identity and community across different contexts.
We explore how musicians navigate tradition and innovation in developing their artistic practice.

VC2AMU10E02.w: We investigate how musicians, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander performers and composers, celebrate and challenge multiple perspectives of Australian identity.
y: Australian music is enriched by diverse voices that celebrate, question and redefine what it means to be Australian.
First Nations musicians are at the forefront of challenging dominant narratives and asserting cultural sovereignty through music.
Investigating these works develops critical understanding of representation, identity and cultural expression.
eg: We study how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander musicians celebrate cultural identity through their work.
We analyse how First Nations musicians challenge stereotypes and assert self-representation.
We investigate how diverse Australian musicians explore questions of identity, place and belonging.
We discuss how music contributes to reconciliation, cultural understanding and social change.
We explore how First Nations musicians blend traditional cultural practices with contemporary forms.
We engage critically with questions about cultural representation, ownership and voice in Australian music.

VC2AMU10D01.w: We develop, practise and refine listening skills and style-specific techniques to interpret music and communicate meaning.
y: Advanced skill development is the foundation for all sophisticated music-making.
Style-specific technique enables authentic and expressive performance across diverse musical traditions.
Refined listening skills underpin our ability to respond, adapt and collaborate musically.
eg: We maintain disciplined practice routines to develop and refine our vocal or instrumental technique.
We develop advanced aural skills including complex interval recognition and harmonic analysis.
We work on achieving nuanced expressive effects through control of dynamics, articulation and tone.
We develop our sight-reading and improvisation skills in our chosen instrument or voice.
We practise advanced ensemble skills including leading, following and blending.
We set ambitious technical goals and work towards them through structured, reflective practice.

VC2AMU10D02.w: We reflect on, analyse, evaluate and document our own and others' music to inform our choices as composers and performers.
y: Rigorous critical analysis is essential for continued growth as both performers and composers.
Evaluating music from multiple perspectives — performer, composer, listener — deepens our understanding.
Documenting our analytical thinking demonstrates the intellectual depth of our musical engagement.
eg: We write detailed analytical essays on musical works using appropriate theoretical frameworks.
We evaluate our own compositions and performances against artistic intentions and musical standards.
We analyse how composers from different traditions achieve specific effects through their musical choices.
We compare and evaluate contrasting interpretations of the same musical work.
We maintain comprehensive documentation of our musical thinking, practice and development.
We engage in structured peer critique of compositions and performances.

VC2AMU10C01.w: We interpret music in various forms and styles, manipulating music elements and using style-specific vocal and instrumental techniques.
y: Sophisticated interpretation requires deep understanding of how musical elements create meaning within different styles.
Mastering style-specific techniques enables us to perform diverse music with authenticity and expression.
Interpretive skill is what transforms technical proficiency into genuinely musical performance.
eg: We interpret music from contrasting styles, applying appropriate techniques and conventions.
We make informed interpretive choices about tempo, dynamics, phrasing and articulation.
We perform demanding repertoire that demonstrates both technical mastery and musical understanding.
We study how professional musicians interpret the same piece differently and discuss the choices.
We perform in ensembles that require sophisticated listening and responsiveness.
We develop our personal interpretive voice while respecting the conventions of each style.

VC2AMU10C02.w: We compose music, exploiting and combining elements and compositional devices relevant to our chosen styles to communicate ideas and meaning, and we notate or record our work.
y: Composing at this level involves making sophisticated artistic decisions about structure, texture and expression.
Understanding how to exploit compositional devices gives us powerful tools for musical communication.
Notating and recording our compositions ensures they can be performed, shared and preserved.
eg: We compose extended works that demonstrate mastery of form, structure and compositional technique.
We exploit compositional devices like augmentation, diminution, retrograde and modulation.
We compose for specific ensembles or contexts, considering the capabilities and character of each instrument.
We use notation software, DAWs or other tools to document our compositions professionally.
We compose music that responds to a complex brief or explores a substantial theme.
We present and discuss our compositions, articulating our artistic intentions and creative choices.

VC2AMU10P01.w: We rehearse and present planned performances of music we have learnt or composed, using relevant techniques and performance skills.
y: Polished performance is the culmination of sustained musical development and artistic commitment.
Professional-level rehearsal and presentation skills prepare us for future musical endeavours.
Live performance creates a powerful connection between musicians and audiences that recording cannot replicate.
eg: We plan and execute performance programs that demonstrate range, skill and musical understanding.
We rehearse methodically, addressing technical, expressive and collaborative dimensions of our music.
We perform in both solo and ensemble contexts for diverse audiences and settings.
We demonstrate appropriate performance practice and conventions for each style we present.
We manage all aspects of performance preparation with increasing independence.
We critically reflect on our performances and develop strategies for ongoing improvement.

VC2AVA10E01.w: We investigate how artists across cultures and times develop personal expression to communicate ideas, challenge perspectives and achieve specific purposes.
y: Studying how artists develop personal expression across contexts reveals the diverse possibilities of visual art.
Understanding the relationship between personal expression and cultural context enriches our own practice.
This knowledge provides the foundation for informed, sophisticated engagement with contemporary art.
eg: We research how specific artists developed their personal practice within cultural and historical contexts.
We investigate the relationship between art, society and the ideas of specific periods or movements.
We analyse how artists use their work to challenge perspectives and provoke dialogue.
We study how art serves different functions — aesthetic, political, spiritual, commemorative — across cultures.
We explore how globalisation and technology have influenced contemporary art practice.
We compare artistic approaches from different traditions and evaluate their significance.

VC2AVA10E02.w: We investigate how Australian artists, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples, celebrate and challenge multiple perspectives of Australian identity.
y: Australian art is enriched by diverse perspectives that celebrate, question and redefine what it means to be Australian.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists are central to the ongoing story of Australian art and identity.
Engaging with these works challenges us to think critically about representation, identity and belonging.
eg: We study how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander artists celebrate cultural identity through their work.
We analyse how First Nations artists challenge stereotypes and assert cultural sovereignty through visual art.
We investigate how diverse Australian artists explore questions of identity, place and belonging.
We discuss how art contributes to reconciliation, cultural understanding and social dialogue.
We explore how contemporary First Nations artists connect traditional practices with contemporary art forms.
We engage critically with questions about representation, appropriation and voice in Australian art.

VC2AVA10D01.w: We experiment with visual conventions, processes and materials to develop skills and personal expression for creating artworks that communicate ideas.
y: Experimentation at this level is purposeful and directed toward developing our personal artistic practice.
Pushing technical boundaries leads to innovation and the discovery of new forms of expression.
Developing personal expression through experimentation is how we find our unique artistic voice.
eg: We experiment with unconventional materials, techniques or combinations to develop new approaches.
We push the boundaries of our chosen medium through sustained experimentation.
We develop specialised skills in specific art forms or techniques through focused practice.
We use experimentation to develop and refine our personal artistic style.
We document our experiments and analyse how they inform our resolved artworks.
We take creative risks that lead to new discoveries and artistic growth.

VC2AVA10D02.w: We reflect on, analyse, evaluate and document how we and others respond to artworks and influences to inform and refine our own practice.
y: Rigorous critical engagement with art — our own and others' — is fundamental to mature artistic practice.
Understanding how we are influenced by other artists helps us develop our practice with greater self-awareness.
Documentation of our analytical thinking demonstrates the intellectual depth of our artistic engagement.
eg: We write substantial critical analyses of artworks using appropriate art historical and theoretical frameworks.
We document our artistic influences and analyse how they shape our own practice.
We evaluate our artworks against our artistic intentions and identify areas for development.
We engage in peer critique that is both rigorous and supportive.
We maintain comprehensive visual diaries that record our research, experiments and reflections.
We articulate our artistic position and how it has developed over time.

VC2AVA10C01.w: We plan, develop and resolve artworks that communicate ideas, perspectives and meaning.
y: Developing a sustained body of art that communicates complex ideas demonstrates artistic maturity.
The plan-develop-resolve process teaches us disciplined creative thinking applicable across many fields.
Effective visual communication of ideas and meaning requires both technical skill and conceptual depth.
eg: We develop a sustained body of work that investigates a complex theme or concept.
We plan our artworks through research, experimentation and concept development.
We resolve artworks that demonstrate a clear relationship between concept and execution.
We create work that communicates personal perspectives on social, cultural or environmental issues.
We demonstrate ability to sustain a creative investigation from initial concept to resolved artwork.
We present our artistic intentions alongside our resolved works.

VC2AVA10C02.w: We select and apply visual conventions, processes and materials to create artworks that reflect personal expression and communicate or challenge ideas.
y: At this level, our artistic choices become deliberate tools for personal expression and social commentary.
The ability to both communicate and challenge ideas through art is a powerful form of participation in society.
Selecting and applying visual language with sophistication demonstrates artistic maturity and independence.
eg: We create artworks that challenge conventional representations or audience expectations.
We select visual conventions, processes and materials that best serve our artistic intentions.
We develop a personal visual language that reflects our identity and artistic perspective.
We create work that engages with contemporary issues and debates.
We demonstrate mastery of our chosen media and processes in service of our artistic vision.
We produce artworks that function effectively on both aesthetic and conceptual levels.

VC2AVA10P01.w: We critique and evaluate art exhibits in various contexts to inform how we curate and exhibit our own and others' artworks.
y: Understanding exhibition practice is essential for presenting art effectively in professional contexts.
Critiquing exhibitions develops our understanding of how curation shapes meaning and audience experience.
Exhibition skills prepare us for careers in the visual arts and creative industries.
eg: We visit and critique exhibitions in galleries, museums and alternative art spaces.
We analyse how curatorial decisions — selection, placement, lighting, interpretation — shape viewer experience.
We curate and install exhibitions of our own and our peers' work.
We develop professional exhibition documentation including artist statements and curatorial rationales.
We present our work in both physical and digital exhibition formats.
We evaluate our exhibition practice and identify areas for professional development.

VC2AVC10E01.w: We investigate how designers from different cultures, times and places use visual communication practices to communicate ideas and information.
y: Understanding the global history and cultural diversity of design enriches our own practice and perspective.
Investigating how design operates across contexts reveals its profound influence on human communication.
Design literacy helps us navigate and critically evaluate the designed world we inhabit.
eg: We research how visual communication practices have evolved across cultures and historical periods.
We investigate how designers from different traditions approach communication challenges.
We analyse the cultural, social and technological factors that shape design practices.
We explore how design movements and philosophies have influenced contemporary practice.
We study how design communicates across language barriers and cultural contexts.
We investigate the role of design in shaping public discourse, culture and identity.

VC2AVC10E02.w: We investigate how Australian designers, including Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Peoples, use visual communication design to celebrate and challenge ideas about Australian identity.
y: Australian design is shaped by diverse perspectives that celebrate, question and redefine what it means to be Australian.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander design knowledge represents the oldest continuous design tradition on Earth.
Investigating these practices develops critical understanding of design's role in culture, identity and representation.
eg: We study how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander designers celebrate cultural identity through visual communication.
We investigate how First Nations design challenges stereotypes and asserts cultural self-determination.
We analyse how diverse Australian designers explore questions of identity, place and belonging.
We discuss how design can contribute to reconciliation and cultural understanding.
We explore how contemporary First Nations designers integrate cultural knowledge with modern practice.
We engage critically with questions about representation, appropriation and cultural sensitivity in design.

VC2AVC10D01.w: We experiment with visual language, methods, media and materials to generate and develop design ideas and refine our visual communication designs.
y: Purposeful experimentation is the engine of design innovation and personal creative development.
Developing a broad range of technical skills gives us maximum creative flexibility.
Pushing the boundaries of our capabilities leads to more original and effective design solutions.
eg: We experiment with emerging technologies and techniques for visual communication.
We develop advanced skills in digital design tools and traditional rendering techniques.
We push creative boundaries by exploring unconventional approaches to design problems.
We use experimentation to develop our personal design style and approach.
We document our experiments and analyse how they inform our design development.
We take creative risks in our experimentation while maintaining design principles.

VC2AVC10D02.w: We reflect on, analyse, evaluate and document our own and others' design ideas, concepts and solutions to inform our decisions throughout the design process.
y: Critical evaluation of design is essential for continuous improvement and professional development.
Analysing how other designers solve problems provides valuable insights for our own practice.
Comprehensive documentation demonstrates the depth and rigour of our design thinking.
eg: We maintain detailed design folios documenting our research, process and reflections.
We analyse professional design work and discuss how designers solve complex communication problems.
We evaluate our designs against briefs, audience needs and professional standards.
We compare different design solutions to similar problems and evaluate their effectiveness.
We use feedback from peers, teachers and target audiences to refine our designs.
We articulate our design rationale with precision and clarity.

VC2AVC10C01.w: We generate, develop, resolve and refine design ideas, concepts and solutions for specific audiences using the visual communication design process.
y: Mastering the design process prepares us for professional practice in the creative industries.
Designing for specific audiences requires sophisticated understanding of communication, culture and context.
The iterative design process develops problem-solving skills applicable across many disciplines.
eg: We apply the full design process — research, ideation, development, resolution, refinement — to complex briefs.
We generate diverse design concepts and evaluate them against criteria and audience needs.
We develop solutions across different fields of design — graphic, product, environmental, digital.
We refine our designs through prototyping, testing and evaluation.
We present comprehensive design proposals showing the evolution of our ideas.
We demonstrate understanding of how design decisions affect communication effectiveness.

VC2AVC10C02.w: We select and apply visual language, methods, media and materials to generate ideas, refine concepts and present visual communication designs for intended audiences.
y: Applying visual language with precision and creativity is the core skill of visual communication design.
Understanding how visual language operates in different contexts enables effective cross-cultural communication.
Professional-quality design presentation demonstrates our readiness for further study or practice.
eg: We apply sophisticated visual language — typography, colour, composition, imagery — to professional-standard designs.
We select media and materials that best serve our design purpose and audience.
We create designs that demonstrate a personal design approach within professional conventions.
We use digital and traditional methods effectively to generate and present design solutions.
We present final designs with supporting documentation that communicates our design rationale.
We produce design work that is technically proficient, conceptually strong and audience-appropriate.

VC2AVC10P01.w: We critique and evaluate design presentations across different fields to present our own visual communication design work effectively.
y: Critical evaluation of design presentations develops our ability to present our own work professionally.
Understanding how presentation context affects design reception is essential for effective communication.
Professional presentation skills prepare us for careers and further study in design.
eg: We critique design presentations across different fields and evaluate their effectiveness.
We select appropriate presentation methods and formats for our design solutions.
We present comprehensive design proposals that communicate our process and rationale.
We develop professional-quality presentation materials including portfolios and pitch decks.
We evaluate how our presentation choices affect audience understanding of our designs.
We reflect on our presentation practice and identify areas for professional development.

VC2EFALA01.w: We are learning to show people what we want or do not want, using the way we communicate best.
y: Being able to say yes or no helps us make choices about our own lives.
When we can tell people what we want, they understand us better.
This is the beginning of having our voice heard by others.
eg: We reach for a toy to show we want it, or push it away to show we do not.
We make a sound or tap something to ask for more of an activity we enjoy.
We look away or turn our body when we do not want something.
We use a button or switch to say 'yes' or 'no' when someone asks us a question.
We smile or laugh to show we want to keep doing something fun.
We point to a picture to tell someone what we would like.

VC2EFALA02.w: We are learning to look at pictures in books and on screens.
y: Looking at pictures helps us understand stories and ideas.
Pictures are a way of sharing meaning with everyone.
When we look at images, we start to connect what we see with what we know.
eg: We look at a picture in a book when someone points to it.
We follow where an adult is looking to find the picture on the page.
We watch images on a screen during a story.
We look at a photo and connect it to something we know, like a pet or food.
We point to a picture that interests us in a book.
We turn our head to look at an image when we hear a sound connected to it.

VC2EFALA03.w: We are learning to show that we know what something is when someone says its name.
y: Knowing the names of things helps us understand the world around us.
When we recognise words, we can follow what people are saying.
This is how we start building a vocabulary of words we understand.
eg: We look at a cup when someone says 'cup' and offers us a drink.
We reach for our hat when someone says 'hat'.
We look towards the door when someone says 'outside'.
We smile or get excited when we hear the name of something we like, such as 'music'.
We pick up the right toy when someone says its name.
We turn towards someone when they say our name.

VC2EFALE01.w: We are learning to pay attention and share the experience when we listen to or look at stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander creators.
y: Sharing stories together helps us feel connected to each other and to Country.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories teach us about the world in special ways.
Paying attention to stories is how we learn to enjoy and understand them.
eg: We sit with others and look at a book by an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander author.
We watch a digital story together and show we are interested by looking at the screen.
We listen to a Dreaming story and show we are paying attention.
We point to pictures in a book by a First Nations illustrator.
We smile or react when something interesting happens in a shared story.
We sit with a friend and look at the same book together.

VC2EFALE02.w: We are learning to pay attention to different kinds of stories and books and explore them.
y: Exploring many different texts helps us find ones we enjoy.
Handling books and stories helps us learn how they work.
The more we explore, the more confident we become with stories.
eg: We pick up different books from the classroom library and feel their pages.
We choose a book to look at from a selection of different shapes and sizes.
We explore a thick book and a thin book and notice how they are different.
We touch a textured book and feel the bumpy pictures.
We turn the pages of a picture book on our own.
We look at a digital story on a tablet and tap the screen.

VC2EFALY01.w: We are learning to get someone's attention and show them how we feel — whether we are comfortable or uncomfortable.
y: Telling people how we feel helps them take care of us.
When we can get attention, we are not alone with our feelings.
This is the start of communicating with the people around us.
eg: We make a sound to let someone know we need help.
We reach towards a person when we want their attention.
We cry or make a face when we are uncomfortable.
We smile and make happy sounds when we are feeling good.
We use a gesture to show we want someone to come closer.
We push something away when it makes us uncomfortable.

VC2EFALY02.w: We are learning to notice and respond to sounds around us and to words we know.
y: Listening to sounds is how we start to understand language.
When we respond to words, it shows we are making connections between sounds and meaning.
Noticing sounds in our environment keeps us safe and helps us learn.
eg: We turn our head when we hear someone call our name.
We look towards the door when we hear a knock.
We get excited when we hear the word for something we like, such as 'play'.
We respond to the sound of rain or music during a sensory activity.
We look at a sign or symbol when someone reads the word on it.
We stop what we are doing when we hear a familiar instruction.

VC2EFALY03.w: We are learning to help make a text with someone else — like choosing what goes in it.
y: Creating something together is exciting and makes us feel part of a team.
When we choose what goes in a text, our ideas matter.
This is the start of being a writer and storyteller.
eg: We look at two objects and choose which one to include in a class story.
We reach for a picture to add to a shared poster.
We point to a toy we want the teacher to write about.
We use our eyes to show which photo should go in our class book.
We smile or nod when the teacher suggests including something we like.
We help choose a picture for a card we are making together.

VC2EFALY04.w: We are learning to use tools to make marks — the very beginning of writing.
y: Making marks is how we start to express ourselves on paper or screen.
Using tools to create marks helps us get ready for writing letters and words.
Every mark we make is a step towards telling our own stories.
eg: We hold a crayon and make marks on paper.
We use eye-gaze technology to make marks on a screen.
We drag a finger through paint to leave a trail.
We press buttons on a device to make something appear on the screen.
We use a thick marker to make dots and lines.
We stamp shapes onto paper using an ink pad.

VC2EFBLA01.w: We are learning to show what we prefer — choosing the thing we like best.
y: Showing our preferences is a way of making our own choices.
When people know what we prefer, they can help us enjoy things more.
Making choices helps us feel in control of our world.
eg: We press a button again and again to hear a sound we enjoy.
We reach for one toy instead of another to show which one we prefer.
We choose a picture of our favourite food from two options.
We turn towards the activity we like more when given a choice.
We use a device to select which song we want to hear.
We push away the thing we do not want and hold onto the one we do.

VC2EFBLA02.w: We are learning to turn pages in books and hold them the right way up.
y: Knowing how to handle a book helps us enjoy stories more easily.
Turning pages one at a time helps us follow what is happening.
These are the first steps to becoming a confident reader.
eg: We turn the pages of a picture book during shared reading time.
We hold a book the right way up and open it from the front.
We look at a digital text and swipe to the next page.
We notice which end of the book is the front and which is the back.
We sit with a partner and take turns turning pages.
We explore how a book opens and which way the pages go.

VC2EFBLA03.w: We are learning to describe something using a single word, gesture or picture.
y: Describing things helps us share what we notice with others.
Using words, gestures or symbols is how we build our communication skills.
When we can describe objects, we can talk about the world around us.
eg: We choose a picture that says 'big' to describe a large ball.
We use a gesture to show that something is round.
We say 'red' while pointing to a red block.
We use a symbol on our device to describe something as 'soft'.
We sign 'hot' when we feel something warm.
We choose from a set of pictures to describe what a toy looks like.

VC2EFBLA04.w: We are learning to find a picture we really like in a book or on a screen.
y: Choosing a favourite picture shows we are thinking about what we see.
When we pick what we like, we are making a personal connection with a text.
This is how we start to develop our own taste in stories and books.
eg: We point to our favourite picture in a storybook.
We match a picture in a book to a toy or object that looks the same.
We tap an image on a screen that we want to see again.
We smile or react when we see a picture we enjoy.
We choose a picture from the book and show it to a friend.
We go back to the same page to look at an image we liked.

VC2EFBLA05.w: We are learning to recognise and say the names of things, actions and activities we know well.
y: Naming things helps us communicate clearly with others.
When we recognise names, we understand what is happening around us.
Building our vocabulary of names and words opens up new conversations.
eg: We hear the sound that means it is time for group learning and get ready.
We look at a picture of 'lunch' and know it is time to eat.
We say or sign the name of a favourite toy.
We point to a picture of 'outside' and say what it means.
We recognise routine symbols like 'reading time' or 'music'.
We name familiar objects when asked 'What is this?'

VC2EFBLE01.w: We are learning to notice and point out something that happens in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander creators.
y: Following events in stories helps us understand how stories work.
Stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors show us different ways of seeing the world.
Noticing events is the start of understanding what a story is about.
eg: We listen to a story by an Aboriginal author and point to a picture of something that happened.
We watch a digital story by a First Nations creator and show what we noticed.
We listen to a story told by a Torres Strait Islander storyteller and identify one thing that happened.
We choose an image that shows an event from the story we just heard.
We use our device to select a symbol that represents something from the story.
We match a picture from the story to something real we have seen or done.

VC2EFBLE02.w: We are learning to react to something that happens in a story.
y: Reacting to stories shows we are understanding and feeling what happens in them.
When we respond to stories, we are sharing the experience with others.
Our reactions are the beginning of sharing our thoughts about what we read or hear.
eg: We look surprised or excited when something happens in a story.
We listen to animal sounds from a story and respond by looking for the source.
We laugh when something funny happens during a read-aloud.
We make a face when a character in a story is in trouble.
We clap or bounce when an exciting part of the story comes.
We use a sound or gesture to show we liked a part of the story.

VC2EFBLE03.w: We are learning to pick out something that happens in a story we know well.
y: Remembering events from familiar stories shows our understanding is growing.
When we can identify events, we are learning to follow how stories unfold.
This helps us talk about stories with others and share what we remember.
eg: We choose a picture or symbol that shows something that happened in a story we know.
We point to the part of the book where something exciting happened.
We select the right sound or image to match an event from a familiar story.
We act out something that happened in a story we have heard many times.
We put two pictures in order to show what happened first and what happened next.
We use our device to pick an event when asked 'What happened in the story?'

VC2EFBLE04.w: We are learning to respond to and enjoy chants, rhymes and songs.
y: Rhymes and songs help us hear the patterns and rhythms in language.
Joining in with chants and songs connects us with others through shared enjoyment.
The more we listen to rhymes, the more we notice how sounds work.
eg: We show we are interested when a familiar song starts playing.
We rock or sway to the rhythm of a chant.
We smile or make sounds when we hear a rhyme we know.
We clap along to a song during circle time.
We look towards the teacher when they start a familiar rhyme.
We use a switch or button to play a favourite song again.

VC2EFBLY01.w: We are learning to pay attention and respond when someone is talking or interacting with us.
y: Responding to others is how conversations begin.
When we show we are listening, people know we are part of the conversation.
These interactions help us build relationships with the people around us.
eg: We look at a person who is talking to us.
We reach towards a favourite person when they come near.
We choose a symbol to respond when someone asks us a question.
We make a sound or gesture to keep a conversation going.
We take a turn in a back-and-forth exchange with a partner.
We show we are paying attention by staying focused on the person speaking.

VC2EFBLY02.w: We are learning to say or copy some sounds that letters make.
y: Letter sounds are the building blocks of reading and writing.
Practising sounds helps us get ready to decode words.
When we know letter sounds, we start to connect written letters with spoken language.
eg: We join in with chants that repeat letter sounds.
We try to copy the sound a teacher makes for a letter.
We hear the 'sss' sound in a song and try to make it ourselves.
We practise saying letter sounds during a rhyming game.
We use our voice or device to repeat a sound after the teacher.
We listen to letter sounds and try to match them to the right letter.

VC2EFBLY03.w: We are learning to spot some familiar pictures, symbols, letters or words in the world around us.
y: Recognising symbols and words in our environment is the start of reading.
When we notice familiar things in writing, we realise the world is full of messages.
This helps us navigate our classroom and daily routines.
eg: We see the symbol for 'chair' and move towards our seat.
We stop what we are doing when we see the 'stop' sign.
We find our name card on the classroom wall.
We recognise the symbol for our favourite activity on the daily schedule.
We point to a letter in the environment that matches one in our name.
We notice a familiar word on a sign and show we recognise it.

VC2EFBLY04.w: We are learning to recognise ourselves when we look in a mirror.
y: Knowing who we are is the beginning of understanding ourselves.
Recognising our own reflection builds our sense of self and identity.
This helps us start to think about who we are and how we are different from others.
eg: We look at our reflection in a mirror and smile.
We wave or move our hands and watch ourselves in the mirror.
We point to ourselves when we see our reflection.
We make different faces in the mirror and watch them change.
We touch our own nose or cheek when we see it in the mirror.
We look at a photo of ourselves and recognise who it is.

VC2EFBLY05.w: We are learning to show we are interested and engaged when someone reads or shows us a text.
y: Being engaged with stories and texts helps us learn from them.
When we show interest, people share more with us and we learn more.
Paying attention to texts is the foundation of all reading and learning.
eg: We reach towards a book that someone is reading to us.
We look at the pictures as someone turns the pages.
We touch a prop that goes with the story being told.
We lean forward or get closer when something interesting happens in the text.
We show excitement when someone picks up our favourite book.
We use our gaze to follow along as someone reads.

VC2EFBLY06.w: We are learning to help create a short text that captures one idea.
y: Recording our ideas is the beginning of writing.
When we put our ideas into a text, other people can see what we think.
Every idea we record shows that our thoughts matter.
eg: We make marks or choose letters to put our name on a piece of work.
We select a picture to go with a sentence about our day.
We help decide what a class text should say about our experience.
We choose a word or symbol to add to a shared text.
We use a device to type one word that captures our idea.
We draw a picture that shows our idea and watch the teacher write words beside it.

VC2EFBLY07.w: We are learning to use different tools to make different kinds of marks on a surface.
y: Making different marks helps us build the hand skills we need for writing.
Experimenting with tools lets us discover new ways to express ourselves.
Each new kind of mark brings us closer to writing letters and words.
eg: We copy simple marks and shapes that a teacher demonstrates.
We draw spirals and squiggles when helping make a class text.
We use different pens, crayons and markers to make different marks.
We paint lines and shapes on a big piece of paper.
We use a finger to trace shapes in sand or on a touchscreen.
We experiment with thick and thin marks using different tools.

VC2EFCLA01.w: We are learning to use short phrases to ask for things, say yes to things, or say no to things.
y: Using phrases helps people understand exactly what we want or need.
When we can ask and respond with phrases, we join in more conversations.
This builds our confidence in communicating with the people around us.
eg: We point to a picture of a tree in a book and say 'big tree' to describe what we see.
We say 'more please' when we want another turn at an activity.
We use our device to say 'no thanks' when offered something we do not want.
We combine two words like 'want ball' to request something specific.
We say 'yes' and nod when asked if we would like to join an activity.
We use a short phrase like 'my turn' during a game.

VC2EFCLA02.w: We are learning to turn one page at a time and tell the difference between words and pictures in books.
y: Turning pages carefully helps us follow the story properly.
Knowing the difference between words and pictures helps us understand how books work.
These skills prepare us to start reading on our own.
eg: We pick up a book, open it, and turn it the right way if it is upside down.
We point to the pictures on a page and then point to the words.
We turn one page at a time instead of flipping through lots of pages at once.
We notice that words look different from pictures — they have letters in them.
We show someone where the words are and where the pictures are in a book.
We practise turning pages on a tablet by swiping carefully.

VC2EFCLA03.w: We are learning to put words together into short phrases to share our ideas.
y: Using phrases helps us say more than just one word at a time.
When we combine words, we can explain our ideas more clearly.
This is an important step towards having longer conversations.
eg: We say 'giraffe tall' after listening to a story about a giraffe.
We combine words like 'dog running' to describe what we see in a picture.
We use our device to put two words together to make a comment.
We say 'bird flying' when we see a picture of a bird in the sky.
We tell someone 'big house' to describe a building in a story.
We comment 'funny cat' after hearing something silly in a book.

VC2EFCLA04.w: We are learning to describe things using short phrases that say what they look like, feel like or are like.
y: Describing things helps others see what we see and understand our world.
Using describing words makes our communication richer and more interesting.
This skill helps us talk about objects, animals and people in more detail.
eg: We describe a teddy bear as 'soft and brown' using our preferred way of communicating.
We say 'round ball' to describe a ball we are playing with.
We use two words together like 'cold water' to describe what we are drinking.
We choose describing pictures from a word bank — like 'bumpy' for a pinecone.
We tell a friend about a toy by saying what colour it is and what it feels like.
We describe our lunch using short phrases like 'red apple' and 'warm soup'.

VC2EFCLA05.w: We are learning to pick out a picture or object that shows an idea from a story we shared.
y: Finding the right picture shows we understood the story.
Matching ideas to images helps us think about what stories mean.
This is how we show we are connecting with what we hear and see.
eg: We choose the picture of a mouse when asked 'Who is in the house?' after a story.
We pick up a toy that matches a character from the story we just heard.
We point to an image that shows where the story took place.
We select the right picture from a group when asked about something in the story.
We match a sound to an image from the story — like choosing the picture of rain when we hear a rain sound.
We hold up an object that represents something that happened in the story.

VC2EFCLA06.w: We are learning to use new words about things we are interested in and topics we study at school.
y: Learning new words helps us talk about more things in more detail.
The more words we know, the better we can express our ideas.
Using topic words shows we are understanding what we are learning about.
eg: We learn the words 'ocean' and 'iceberg' when studying cold places and use them in sentences.
We use new words from our science topic when talking about animals.
We add topic words to our personal word bank and practise using them.
We select the right word from a picture bank to label something we learned about.
We use a new word from a story in our own conversation later that day.
We practise using words about our personal interests, like the names of different dinosaurs.

VC2EFCLE01.w: We are learning to find the setting, characters and events in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander creators.
y: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories teach us about culture, Country and community.
Finding characters, settings and events helps us understand any story better.
These stories give us different ways of seeing and understanding the world.
eg: We watch a story by an Aboriginal storyteller and name a character we saw.
We point to the setting in a picture book by a First Nations illustrator.
We describe something that happened in a Torres Strait Islander story.
We use pictures to retell an event from a story by an Aboriginal author.
We identify where a story takes place — like 'by the river' or 'in the bush'.
We draw a character from a story we heard and tell someone about them.

VC2EFCLE02.w: We are learning to share what we think about the characters and events in a story.
y: Sharing our opinions helps us think more deeply about stories.
When we talk about characters and events, we understand them better.
Our thoughts and feelings about stories matter and are worth sharing.
eg: We say 'yes' or 'no' when asked if we liked a character.
We choose a happy or sad face to show how we felt about something in the story.
We tell the teacher which character was our favourite and why.
We comment on an event by saying something like 'That was funny!'
We select an emotion symbol to show how a story made us feel.
We share with a partner whether we thought the ending was good or not.

VC2EFCLE03.w: We are learning to pick out a character or an event in a story we know.
y: Identifying characters and events shows we are following the story.
This helps us remember and talk about stories after we hear them.
Recognising story parts is the foundation for understanding more complex stories later.
eg: We match a toy or picture of a character to the right page in the book.
We point to the part of the story where an important event happened.
We select the character's image from a group when asked 'Who was in the story?'
We pick up a prop that represents a character from a familiar story.
We use a symbol to show an event from the story, like 'the bear went to sleep'.
We sequence two pictures to show what happened first and what happened next.

VC2EFCLE04.w: We are learning to join in with chants, rhymes and songs that we know.
y: Joining in with rhymes and songs helps us feel the rhythm and patterns of language.
Participating in shared songs builds our confidence and connects us with others.
Chants and rhymes help us remember words and patterns more easily.
eg: We use a device, our voice, signs or actions to join in with a song.
We clap along to the beat of a familiar chant.
We say the last word of each line in a rhyme we know well.
We do the actions for a favourite song along with our classmates.
We press a button to play the next part of a familiar rhyme.
We hum or move our body to the rhythm of a song during music time.

VC2EFCLE05.w: We are learning to retell something that happened in a short story we know or enjoy.
y: Retelling shows we understood and remembered the story.
When we retell, we practise organising our ideas in order.
Sharing stories with others helps us become better communicators.
eg: We answer questions about what happened in a story by pointing to pictures.
We use our preferred way of communicating to tell someone 'who', 'what' or 'where' from the story.
We put pictures from the story in the right order to retell it.
We act out a part of the story using props.
We select images on a device to tell someone what happened in the story.
We draw a picture of the most important event and tell a partner about it.

VC2EFCLY01.w: We are learning to start a short conversation with someone using the way we communicate best.
y: Starting a conversation means we do not have to wait for others to come to us.
Initiating interactions helps us make friends and build relationships.
This gives us more control over our social world.
eg: We use gestures or facial expressions to start a chat with an adult or friend.
We say someone's name or tap them to get their attention before talking.
We use our device to start a conversation by selecting a greeting.
We make eye contact and wave to begin an interaction.
We hold up something interesting to start a conversation about it.
We ask a simple question like 'What's that?' to begin talking with someone.

VC2EFCLY02.w: We are learning to use short phrases when presenting an idea or showing something to our classmates.
y: Presenting to others helps us build confidence in sharing our ideas.
Using phrases makes our sharing clearer and easier for others to understand.
Show-and-tell helps us practise being speakers in front of a group.
eg: We hold up something from home during show-and-tell and say a phrase about it.
We share a picture we drew and use short phrases to explain what it shows.
We tell classmates about a weekend activity using two or three words together.
We present a favourite toy and describe it using phrases like 'it is soft' and 'I like it'.
We use our device to say a short sentence about something we want to share.
We show an object to the class and tell them where we got it.

VC2EFCLY03.w: We are learning to recognise and name some common letters and the sounds they make.
y: Knowing letter names and sounds is the key that unlocks reading.
When we know what sounds letters make, we can start to figure out words.
This is one of the most important steps in learning to read and write.
eg: We learn letter sounds by tracing letters in sand while saying the sound.
We play matching games where we pair letters with pictures that start with that sound.
We sing letter songs that help us remember the sounds each letter makes.
We point to letters in the classroom and say their names and sounds.
We feel textured letters and say the sound as we touch them.
We practise letter sounds using a multi-sensory approach — seeing, hearing and touching.

VC2EFCLY04.w: We are learning to use what we know about letters and sounds to work out the first letter and sound in a word.
y: Hearing the first sound in a word is a big step towards reading.
Connecting sounds to letters helps us decode words we see in writing.
This skill gives us a strategy for figuring out new words.
eg: We hear the word 'ball' and say that it starts with the sound 'b'.
We match a word to the right letter tile by listening to its first sound.
We sort pictures into groups based on their first letter sound.
We play a game where we find objects that start with a certain sound.
We point to the letter that matches the beginning sound of our name.
We practise in activities where the teacher says a word and we identify the first sound.

VC2EFCLY05.w: We are learning to find and recognise our own name when we see it written down with a picture.
y: Recognising our name is one of the first steps in reading.
Our name is special — it is often the first word we learn to read.
Seeing our name in writing helps us feel we belong in the classroom.
eg: We pick out our name card and photo from the attendance board each morning.
We find our name label on our tray or bag hook.
We point to our name when we see it on a class list.
We match our photo to our written name on a display.
We circle our name when it appears in a group of names.
We recognise the letters in our name and point them out in other words.

VC2EFCLY06.w: We are learning to answer simple questions about a story or text we listened to or watched together.
y: Answering questions shows we were paying attention and understanding the story.
Practising responses helps us get better at sharing our ideas with others.
Thinking about questions deepens our understanding of what we hear and see.
eg: We answer 'Where does the bird live?' by pointing to a picture of a tree.
We use our device to answer 'Who was in the story?' by selecting the right character.
We nod or shake our head to answer yes/no questions about a shared text.
We choose from two pictures to answer a 'what' question about the story.
We use signs or gestures to respond when asked about an event in the text.
We select the right image to show what happened in the story.

VC2EFCLY07.w: We are learning to help create short texts together and put pictures in the right order.
y: Creating texts with others teaches us how stories and information are organised.
Putting pictures in order helps us understand that events happen in a sequence.
This is the foundation for writing our own stories and information texts.
eg: We use sentence starters like 'I like' and add our own idea to make a sentence like 'I like swimming'.
We put three or more words together to share an idea in a class text.
We put pictures in order to show what happened first, next and last.
We help the teacher decide what to write in our shared class story.
We arrange photos from an excursion in the order they happened.
We contribute an idea to a class book by choosing images and words.

VC2EFCLY08.w: We are learning to use tools to make shapes and symbols that look like letters.
y: Making letter-like shapes is an important step on the way to writing real letters.
Using writing tools builds the hand control we need for writing.
Every letter-like mark we make brings us closer to being a writer.
eg: We use a pencil or marker to make shapes that look like letters.
We trace over letter-like shapes on a worksheet or whiteboard.
We use a stylus on a tablet to create letter-like marks.
We form circular and straight marks that are starting to look like letters.
We practise making the shapes we see in our name.
We use chalk, crayons or paint to create rows of letter-like symbols.

VC2EFDLA01.w: We are learning to use phrases when we talk to different people at school, thinking about who they are — like an adult or a friend.
y: We talk differently to different people — knowing this helps us communicate well in every situation.
Understanding relationships helps us choose the right words and tone.
This skill helps us get along with all the people in our school community.
eg: We practise greeting a teacher differently from greeting a friend.
We talk about how we speak to adults compared to how we speak to other children.
We role-play asking a teacher for help versus asking a classmate.
We notice that we might say 'please' and 'thank you' more formally with adults.
We practise introducing ourselves to a visitor at school.
We discuss why we might speak louder on the playground but softer in the library.

VC2EFDLA02.w: We are learning to use phrases to tell people about the things we like and do not like.
y: Sharing our preferences helps others understand who we are.
When we express what we like and dislike, we practise forming opinions.
Using phrases instead of single words helps us communicate more clearly.
eg: We practise saying 'I like painting' or 'I don't like loud noises' during sharing time.
We use visual symbols or our device to express preferences like 'I want the blue one'.
We tell a partner about our favourite food using a full phrase.
We create a likes and dislikes chart using pictures and words.
We respond to questions about preferences with phrases like 'I prefer playing outside'.
We practise politely saying 'No thanks, I don't like that' when offered something.

VC2EFDLA03.w: We are learning that texts come in many different forms — like signs, books and things on screens.
y: Recognising different text forms helps us find information in many different places.
Texts are all around us — in books, on screens, on signs and on labels.
When we notice different text types, we understand the world is full of messages meant for us.
eg: We compare information shown on a screen with information in a book.
We notice signs around the school and talk about what they tell us.
We look at a recipe, a storybook and a poster and talk about how they are different.
We find texts in different places — on food packaging, on the whiteboard, on a website.
We sort different texts into groups based on where we find them.
We discuss how a shopping list looks different from a storybook.

VC2EFDLA04.w: We are learning about how books work — which is the front and back, that we read from left to right, and that words are separated by spaces.
y: Knowing how books are organised helps us follow along when we read.
Understanding these patterns is essential for becoming an independent reader.
Every book follows the same rules, so once we learn them, we can read any book.
eg: We find the front cover of a book and open it the right way.
We follow the teacher's finger as it moves from left to right across a page.
We practise return sweep — going back to the start of the next line when we reach the end.
We point to the spaces between words and explain what they are for.
We hold a book that has been handed to us upside down and turn it the right way.
We track words from left to right while someone reads a big book to us.

VC2EFDLA05.w: We are learning to put words together into phrases to share our ideas.
y: Combining words lets us express more complete ideas.
Phrases help others understand exactly what we are trying to say.
Building phrases is an important step towards speaking in full sentences.
eg: We use sentence prompts to say things like 'The dog is big' by filling in the blanks.
We combine a 'who' and a 'what' to make a phrase like 'The cat is sleeping'.
We practise putting describing words with naming words, like 'red flower'.
We tell a partner about a picture using a phrase rather than a single word.
We use word cards to build short phrases on our desk.
We extend our ideas from one word to a phrase — from 'ball' to 'the bouncy ball'.

VC2EFDLA06.w: We are learning to use phrases to describe objects and actions — what things look like and what is happening.
y: Describing helps us paint a picture with words so others can understand what we mean.
When we describe actions, we can tell people about events and experiences.
These skills help us participate more fully in conversations and classroom discussions.
eg: We tell someone about our day by saying 'I read a book' or 'We did writing'.
We describe a toy by saying 'It is soft and white'.
We use our device to describe an action, like 'running fast'.
We look at a picture and describe what is happening, like 'the bird is flying'.
We tell a partner about something we did at home using a describing phrase.
We play a guessing game where we describe an object and others try to guess what it is.

VC2EFDLA07.w: We are learning how pictures and words work together to help us understand stories and information texts.
y: Pictures and words each tell us something different — together they tell us more.
Understanding how images and words combine helps us get the full meaning of a text.
This skill helps us with all kinds of texts, from picture books to websites.
eg: We point to the words on a page and then to the picture, noticing how they connect.
We look at a page with both words and a picture and talk about what each one tells us.
We cover the words and guess the story from just the pictures, then check with the words.
We cover the pictures and try to understand the story from just the words.
We notice when a picture adds extra information that the words do not say.
We talk about how illustrations help us understand what characters look like or feel.

VC2EFDLA08.w: We are learning to use words we have picked up from everyday life, from things we are interested in, and from what we study at school.
y: A bigger vocabulary means we can talk about more things more clearly.
Using words from our interests makes conversations more fun and personal.
Learning topic words helps us understand and remember what we study.
eg: We recognise and respond to frequently used words in the classroom, like 'pack up' or 'circle time'.
We use words from our science topic, like 'habitat' or 'camouflage', in conversation.
We share words connected to our hobbies — like 'goal', 'score' or 'dribble' for football.
We add new words we learn to a personal word wall or dictionary.
We play vocabulary games where we match new words to their meanings.
We try to use at least one new word we learned this week in our speaking or writing.
