VC2EFDLA09.w: We are learning that spaces between words show where one word ends and the next one begins.
y: Spaces help us read — without them, all the words would run together.
Understanding word spacing is important for both reading and writing.
This helps us recognise individual words in a sentence.
eg: We use finger spacing when writing to put gaps between our words.
We press the space bar on a keyboard after each word we type.
We look at a sentence and count the words by pointing to each space.
We compare writing with spaces and writing without spaces and see which is easier to read.
We practise using spacer sticks between words when writing on paper.
We circle individual words in a sentence by finding the spaces.

VC2EFDLE01.w: We are learning to find experiences in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors that are like our own experiences.
y: Connecting stories to our own lives helps us understand them more deeply.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories often share experiences that are familiar to all of us — like family, friendship and belonging.
Finding connections between stories and our lives helps us feel that stories are for us.
eg: We listen to a story by an Aboriginal author and talk about something similar that happened to us.
We watch a story by a Torres Strait Islander storyteller and find a character who reminds us of someone we know.
We compare something from a First Nations story with something from our own family life.
We draw a picture of a moment in the story that is like something we have experienced.
We discuss how themes like family, friendship and caring for Country connect to our own lives.
We share with a partner one way the story is like our life and one way it is different.

VC2EFDLE02.w: We are learning to share how we feel about the characters and things that happen in stories.
y: Sharing our feelings about stories helps us think about them more deeply.
Our personal responses to characters and events show that we are connecting with the text.
Talking about our feelings builds our emotional vocabulary and empathy.
eg: We answer questions about how a story made us feel — happy, sad, excited or scared.
We draw a picture showing our favourite part and explain why we liked it.
We choose a feeling word or emoji to describe our response to a character's actions.
We tell a partner 'I felt worried when the character got lost'.
We use our device to select an emotion that matches how the story made us feel.
We give a thumbs up or thumbs down to show whether we liked what a character did.

VC2EFDLE03.w: We are learning to identify a setting, a character and an event in a story.
y: Settings, characters and events are the building blocks of every story.
When we can find these parts, we understand how stories are put together.
This helps us retell stories and eventually create our own.
eg: We use words like 'first', 'next' and 'then' to describe events in a story.
We point to the picture that shows where the story takes place — the setting.
We name the main character and describe what they did.
We use an anchor chart showing 'beginning, middle, end' to organise our retelling.
We match characters to their names using picture cards.
We draw the setting of a story and label it with words.

VC2EFDLE04.w: We are learning to copy and repeat the rhythms of chants, rhymes and songs we know.
y: Feeling the rhythm of language helps us hear how words and sounds are organised.
Joining in with rhythm activities builds our confidence in spoken language.
Rhythm awareness is connected to reading — it helps us break words into syllables.
eg: We clap the rhythm of a familiar chant along with the class.
We tap our knees and clap our hands to match the beat of a song.
We copy a drumming pattern that goes with a rhyme.
We add body percussion — like stomping feet — to a favourite classroom song.
We use instruments to keep the beat while saying a chant together.
We listen to a rhyme and clap once for each syllable.

VC2EFDLE05.w: We are learning to retell events from stories through acting, drawing, or writing — bringing stories to life in our own way.
y: Retelling stories helps us remember and understand them better.
When we act out or draw stories, we show what we have learned in creative ways.
This helps us become storytellers ourselves.
eg: We role-play characters or events from a favourite story using props.
We draw a picture of what happened in the story and write or dictate words to go with it.
We act out a scene from a story with classmates.
We use puppets to retell a story event to a partner.
We create a simple comic strip showing what happened in the story.
We perform our retelling for the class, using our voice and actions to show what happened.

VC2EFDLY01.w: We are learning to start a conversation and keep it going back and forth with someone.
y: Sustaining a conversation helps us build stronger connections with others.
Back-and-forth interactions are the heart of communication and friendship.
The more we practise, the easier and more natural conversations become.
eg: We start a conversation by choosing a friend's name from a board or saying it aloud.
We take turns in a small-group discussion, listening and then responding.
We keep a conversation going by asking a follow-up question like 'And then what?'
We use our device to initiate a discussion and respond when someone replies.
We practise staying on topic for two or three turns of conversation.
We choose a photo or object to spark a conversation and keep talking about it.

VC2EFDLY02.w: We are learning to use phrases to retell a story or event to our classmates.
y: Retelling events practises our memory and our ability to organise ideas.
Sharing stories and events helps us contribute to our classroom community.
When we retell, we become the storyteller — and that builds our confidence.
eg: We share ideas during morning message or show-and-tell using phrases.
We retell something from the weekend using words like 'first' and 'then'.
We describe a story event to a partner using two or three phrases.
We contribute to a class retelling of a shared experience, like an excursion.
We use picture prompts to help us retell a story in order.
We practise retelling by saying what happened at the beginning, middle and end.

VC2EFDLY03.w: We are learning to break words apart into their sounds and put sounds together to make words.
y: Blending and segmenting sounds is how we learn to read and spell words.
When we can hear the separate sounds in a word, we can write them down.
When we can blend sounds together, we can read new words.
eg: We clap out the syllables in words like 'but-ter-fly' — three claps for three parts.
We practise blending sounds together — hearing 'c-a-t' and saying 'cat'.
We segment a word by saying each sound separately — 'dog' becomes 'd-o-g'.
We play a game where we break words into their sound parts and count how many there are.
We use counters to represent each sound in a word and push them together as we blend.
We sing songs that break words into syllables and then put them back together.

VC2EFDLY04.w: We are learning to use what we know about letters and sounds to read simple three-letter words.
y: Reading simple words is a huge milestone — it is the start of independent reading.
Connecting letters to their sounds is the code that unlocks written language.
Once we can read short words, we can build up to reading longer ones.
eg: We look at the word 'cat' and sound out each letter — 'c-a-t' — then say the word.
We use a voice-output device to reproduce individual letter sounds.
We read simple words like 'sat', 'pin' and 'dog' by blending the sounds together.
We build three-letter words with magnetic letters, sounding out each one.
We match a written word like 'cup' to the right picture.
We practise reading CVC words on flash cards, getting faster each time.

VC2EFDLY05.w: We are learning to recognise familiar words when we see them around our school and in our environment.
y: Recognising words in the world around us shows we are becoming readers.
Familiar words are everywhere — on signs, labels and displays — and we can read them.
This gives us confidence that reading is something we can do in real life, not just in books.
eg: We spot our name on our tray, locker or art display.
We read familiar words on classroom signs like 'exit', 'library' or 'toilet'.
We find words we know on food packaging or signs around the school.
We remove our name card from the attendance board, reading it as we go.
We point to words we recognise in the school newsletter or on posters.
We find a word we know in a new place and tell someone about it.

VC2EFDLY06.w: We are learning to use our growing knowledge of letter sounds to work out words in short sentences and simple texts.
y: Decoding words is the key skill that turns marks on a page into meaningful language.
The more we practise, the faster and more automatic our reading becomes.
This lets us start reading short books and texts on our own.
eg: We listen for individual sounds in words during rhyming games.
We sound out each letter in an unfamiliar word and blend them together to read it.
We use our phonics knowledge to read simple decodable sentences.
We play sound matching games — pairing pictures to their beginning, middle or ending sounds.
We read a short decodable text and use letter sounds to figure out new words.
We point to each word as we read a sentence, sounding out the ones we do not know yet.

VC2EFDLY07.w: We are learning to use strategies like predicting, connecting and asking questions to help us understand stories and texts we hear, see or read.
y: Comprehension strategies help us make sense of what we read and hear.
When we predict and question, we are actively thinking — not just passively listening.
These strategies help us understand even when we come across unfamiliar words or ideas.
eg: We look at the cover of a book and predict what the story might be about.
We answer questions about a familiar story by putting events in order.
We connect something in a story to something that happened in our own life.
We stop during a story to ask 'What will happen next?'
We retell a well-known story in order using picture prompts.
We make a prediction before turning the page and check if we were right.

VC2EFDLY08.w: We are learning to create short texts to record an idea or write about things that happened.
y: Writing helps us capture our ideas so we can share them with others.
When we record events, we practise putting our thoughts into words.
Creating our own texts makes us authors — people who have something to say.
eg: We draw a picture and write a sentence underneath it about our idea.
We select an image and dictate or write words to describe what happened.
We create a simple recount of our day using pictures and words.
We write our name and one sentence about something important to us.
We use a device to type a short sentence about an event.
We contribute a page to a class book by drawing and writing about our experience.

VC2EFDLY09.w: We are learning to help edit a class text together, looking for capital letters and checking that there are spaces between words.
y: Editing helps us make our writing clearer and easier to read.
Learning to spot mistakes is an important part of the writing process.
When we edit together, we learn from each other and improve our writing skills.
eg: We look at a class text on the whiteboard and find where capital letters are needed.
We check that there are spaces between words in a shared sentence.
We circle a word that needs a capital letter because it starts a sentence.
We point to where a full stop should go at the end of a sentence.
We help the teacher fix the spacing in a class text by pointing to words that are too close together.
We compare a sentence before and after editing and talk about what changed.

VC2EFDLY10.w: We are learning to write most letters — both big (upper-case) and small (lower-case) — using the correct way to form them.
y: Forming letters correctly makes our writing easier for others to read.
Good letter formation helps us write more quickly and neatly as we get more practice.
These are the foundations of handwriting that we will use for the rest of our lives.
eg: We follow the teacher's demonstration of how to start and form each letter.
We practise writing letters on lined paper, starting from the correct spot.
We trace over letters to learn the correct direction and formation.
We practise forming letters in sand, on whiteboards and on paper.
We compare our letters to a model and try to make them look the same.
We write all the letters in our name using correct formation.

VC2EFLA01.w: We are learning that we use different words and ways of talking depending on where we are and who we are with.
y: Knowing how language changes in different situations helps us communicate well everywhere we go.
This helps us understand why we talk one way at home and another way at school.
When we match our language to the situation, people understand us better.
eg: We notice that we might ask a teacher for help differently from how we ask a friend.
We practise asking polite questions in class and compare with how we talk at the playground.
We role-play being at a shop and notice the language we use there.
We talk about how our voice might be louder at the park and quieter in the library.
We compare how we say 'hello' to a friend versus 'good morning' to a teacher.
We notice that we use different words with our family than with people we have just met.

VC2EFLA02.w: We are learning different ways to tell people about the things we like and do not like.
y: Expressing our preferences is part of having a voice and making choices.
Learning different ways to say what we think helps us be understood.
This helps us share our feelings and opinions confidently.
eg: We practise saying 'I like…' and 'I don't like…' about foods, games and activities.
We recognise that we can show feelings with our face, our voice and our words.
We draw a picture of our favourite thing and tell a partner why we like it.
We sort pictures into 'like' and 'don't like' piles and explain our choices.
We use words, gestures and facial expressions to share how we feel about something.
We listen to a friend talk about their favourite thing and notice the words they use.

VC2EFLA03.w: We are learning that texts come in many forms — like signs, books, and things on screens.
y: Texts are everywhere around us, not just in books, and we can learn to read them all.
Understanding different text forms helps us navigate the world.
This opens our eyes to all the places we can find information and stories.
eg: We compare pictures in a storybook with pictures in an information book.
We look at a road sign and a picture book and talk about how they are different.
We explore a website on a tablet and compare it to a printed book.
We notice texts in the classroom — timetables, labels, books and posters.
We sort different types of texts — like a menu, a letter and a comic.
We walk around the school and spot as many different types of texts as we can.

VC2EFLA04.w: We are learning how books and simple digital texts are put together — like which way to read and how pages are organised.
y: Understanding how texts are organised helps us become confident readers.
These are the basic rules that all books follow, so we only need to learn them once.
Knowing print concepts means we can focus on the words and stories inside.
eg: We learn that we read English text from left to right and from the top to the bottom.
We find the front cover, the back cover and the title of a book.
We practise turning pages in the right direction.
We notice that a digital text might scroll down instead of having pages to turn.
We point to where the reading starts on a page.
We follow the teacher's pointer as it tracks across a big book during shared reading.

VC2EFLA05.w: We are learning that sentences are the way we put ideas together in speaking and writing.
y: Sentences help us express complete thoughts that make sense.
Understanding sentences is essential for both reading and writing.
When we know what a sentence is, we can start creating our own.
eg: We learn that 'The boy sat on the dog' means something different from 'The dog sat on the boy' — word order matters!
We listen to a sentence and count how many words are in it.
We practise saying complete sentences instead of just single words.
We look at a sentence and identify where it starts and finishes.
We make up silly sentences by changing the words around and see how the meaning changes.
We clap once for each word in a sentence to feel its rhythm and length.

VC2EFLA06.w: We are learning that words in a sentence work together in groups to create meaning.
y: Understanding how words connect helps us read and write sentences that make sense.
This is the beginning of learning grammar — the rules that hold language together.
When we see how words relate to each other, we can build clearer sentences.
eg: We learn how 'a cat' (the thing) connects to 'ate' (the action) in a sentence.
We match describing words like 'black' to the thing they describe, like 'cat'.
We build sentences using word cards, putting the naming word with the doing word.
We play a game where we change one word in a sentence and see how it changes the meaning.
We sort words into groups — naming words, doing words and describing words.
We practise adding more words to a simple sentence to give it more detail.

VC2EFLA07.w: We are learning how pictures, words and sounds all work together to tell us what a story or text means.
y: Texts use images, words and sounds together — understanding this helps us get the full picture.
Sometimes the pictures tell us things the words don't, and the words tell us things the pictures don't.
This helps us understand texts more completely, whether we are reading a book or watching a video.
eg: We look at a picture book and notice that the words and images tell different parts of the story.
We read a page with only words and then look at the picture to see what extra information it gives us.
We listen to a story being read aloud and notice how the reader's voice adds meaning.
We compare two versions of the same story — one with pictures and one without — and talk about the difference.
We look at an information text and notice how diagrams and labels help us understand the words.
We watch an animated story and talk about how music and sound effects add to the meaning.

VC2EFLA08.w: We are learning new words connected to our daily life, our interests and the topics we study at school.
y: The more words we know, the better we can express ourselves and understand others.
Building our vocabulary helps us in every subject, not just English.
Learning new words makes reading easier because we recognise more of what we see.
eg: We role-play being a shopkeeper and customer, using words like 'price', 'change' and 'receipt'.
We learn new words from our class topic and use them in sentences during the week.
We create a class word wall with new vocabulary words and their pictures.
We play matching games that connect new words to pictures or definitions.
We use new words in our conversations and writing throughout the day.
We keep a personal word collection where we add interesting new words we discover.

VC2EFLA09.w: We are learning that punctuation marks are different from letters, and that capital letters are used for names and at the start of sentences.
y: Punctuation helps readers understand where sentences start and stop.
Knowing about capitals and punctuation is important for both reading and writing.
These are the signposts that help us navigate written language.
eg: We spot capital letters in texts and say 'That starts my name!' when we find familiar ones.
We notice that names of people and places always start with a capital letter.
We find full stops in a text and learn that they mark the end of a sentence.
We look at different punctuation marks — full stops, question marks, exclamation marks — and talk about what they do.
We practise writing our name with a capital letter at the start.
We circle all the capital letters on a page and discuss why each one is there.

VC2EFLE01.w: We are learning to find things in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors that are similar to or different from our own experiences.
y: Connecting stories to our own lives helps us understand and enjoy them more.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories share experiences about family, belonging and Country that connect with all of us.
When we compare our experiences with characters in stories, we develop empathy and understanding.
eg: We listen to a story by an Aboriginal author and talk about something in it that reminds us of our own family.
We read a book by a Torres Strait Islander illustrator and discuss how the characters' feelings are like our own.
We notice experiences in First Nations stories that are different from ours and talk about what we learned.
We draw a picture showing something from the story that is similar to our life.
We share with a partner one thing the character did that we have also done.
We discuss how the story's setting — like the bush, the beach or a community — is like or unlike where we live.

VC2EFLE02.w: We are learning to share how we feel about the characters and events in stories.
y: Sharing our feelings about stories helps us become thoughtful readers who care about what we read.
When we express our thoughts about characters and events, we deepen our understanding.
Talking about stories builds our emotional vocabulary and helps us understand ourselves and others.
eg: We draw a picture and write a few words about our favourite part of a story.
We tell a partner how a character made us feel — happy, sad, scared or excited.
We use smiley faces or emojis to rate how a story made us feel.
We act out a scene that we thought was funny or exciting.
We talk about whether we agreed with what a character did and explain why.
We write a simple sentence like 'I liked when the bear found his friend' to express our response.

VC2EFLE03.w: We are learning to recognise different types of stories and spot their features — like events, characters, and how they begin and end.
y: Knowing the features of stories helps us follow them and predict what might happen.
Understanding story structure helps us enjoy stories more and creates a foundation for writing our own.
Recognising patterns in stories means we can spot them in every new book we read.
eg: We notice that many stories start with 'Once upon a time' or 'A long time ago'.
We identify the main character in a story and describe them.
We point out the beginning, middle and ending of a story.
We compare how two different stories begin and notice similarities.
We list the events in a story in the order they happened.
We look for patterns — like a problem in the middle and a solution at the end.

VC2EFLE04.w: We are learning to listen to, explore and copy the rhythms and sound patterns in poems, chants, rhymes and songs.
y: Feeling the rhythm of language helps us hear how sounds work and fit together.
Playing with rhymes and sounds builds our awareness of the building blocks of words.
Enjoying poems, chants and songs makes language learning fun and memorable.
eg: We clap along to the beat of a familiar rhyme or song.
We add actions and movements to enhance our enjoyment of poems and chants.
We listen for the rhyming words in a song and call them out.
We join in with the repeated parts of a favourite chant.
We make up our own silly rhyming words after hearing a poem.
We move our bodies to the rhythm of different songs and notice when the rhythm changes.

VC2EFLE05.w: We are learning to retell stories we know — through play, acting, drawing and writing.
y: Retelling stories shows we understood them and helps us remember them.
When we retell in creative ways, we practise being storytellers ourselves.
Adapting stories through play and performance builds our imagination and confidence.
eg: We draw pictures of characters and events from a favourite story and explain our drawings.
We role-play a well-known story using costumes and props.
We retell a familiar story to a partner using our own words.
We use puppets or small figures to act out the main events of a story.
We draw a comic strip that retells a story in three or four panels.
We change part of a story — like giving it a different ending — and share our version with the class.

VC2EFLY01.w: We are learning to take turns speaking and listening in group conversations, using the right voice level for the situation.
y: Good conversations need both speakers and listeners — learning to do both makes us effective communicators.
Using the right voice level shows respect for others and helps our message be heard.
These skills help us participate fully in class discussions and group work.
eg: We practise putting our hand up to speak and waiting for our turn in class discussions.
We share ideas in pairs, practising listening carefully while our partner speaks.
We use a quiet indoor voice during class and a louder voice outside.
We play a game where we pass a talking stick — only the person holding it can speak.
We practise looking at the person who is talking to show we are listening.
We contribute an idea during a class meeting and listen to what others say afterwards.

VC2EFLY02.w: We are learning to stand up and speak to a group, using our voice in different ways to make our talk interesting.
y: Speaking to an audience builds our confidence and helps us share our ideas with more people.
Using our voice well — changing volume, speed and expression — makes people want to listen.
Being able to present to others is a skill we will use all through our lives.
eg: We share a personal experience with the class during show-and-tell.
We practise speaking clearly and loudly enough for everyone to hear.
We tell our classmates about something we discovered, using an excited voice for the interesting parts.
We present a favourite book to the class and explain why we like it.
We practise speaking slowly and clearly instead of rushing through.
We watch a classmate present and give them feedback on what they did well.

VC2EFLY03.w: We are learning to hear and play with the sounds in spoken words — syllables, rhymes and individual sounds.
y: Hearing the sounds in words is the foundation of learning to read and write.
Playing with sounds helps our brain get ready to connect spoken words with written letters.
The better we hear sounds, the easier it will be to spell and decode words.
eg: We clap out the syllables in our names — 'Ma-ri-a' gets three claps.
We listen to two words and decide if they rhyme — like 'cat' and 'hat'.
We think of words that start with the same sound — like 'sun', 'sand' and 'sing'.
We play a game where we change one sound in a word to make a new word — 'cat' becomes 'bat'.
We listen for the odd one out — which word does not rhyme with the others?
We generate our own rhyming words after hearing a pattern — 'dog, log, fog…'

VC2EFLY04.w: We are learning to blend sounds together to say a word, and break a word apart into its separate sounds.
y: Blending sounds is how we read — we put the sounds together to hear the word.
Segmenting sounds is how we spell — we hear the word and write each sound.
Mastering this skill is one of the biggest steps towards becoming a reader and writer.
eg: We hear 'c-a-n' and blend the sounds together to say 'can'.
We hear the word 'ship' and break it apart into 'sh-i-p'.
We use sound boxes to push a counter for each sound we hear in a word.
We practise blending and segmenting with a partner — one person says the sounds, the other says the word.
We use magnetic letters to build a word sound by sound.
We play 'robot talk' where we say words in a segmented, robotic way and then blend them.

VC2EFLY05.w: We are learning to connect letters with the sounds they make, so we can read and spell simple three-letter words.
y: Knowing the sound each letter makes is the key to cracking the code of reading.
When we can match sounds to letters, we can start writing words by ourselves.
This is the core skill that turns us from beginners into real readers and writers.
eg: We write CVC words like 'mat' by listening to each sound and writing the matching letter.
We read a word like 'pin' by sounding out each letter and blending them together.
We build words with magnetic letters by putting one letter down for each sound.
We play a game where we match picture cards to three-letter words.
We practise reading and writing word families like 'cat, bat, hat, sat'.
We use whiteboards to write a CVC word, then change one letter to make a new word.

VC2EFLY06.w: We are learning to recognise and name every letter of the alphabet — both upper-case and lower-case — and know the sounds they make.
y: Knowing all the letters and their sounds gives us the tools we need to read any word.
Letter knowledge is the alphabet toolbox that we use every time we read or write.
The faster we can recognise letters and sounds, the more fluent our reading will become.
eg: We recognise the letters in our own name and find them in words around the classroom.
We practise naming letters quickly using flashcards or letter mats.
We sing the alphabet song and point to each letter as we go.
We sort letters into upper-case and lower-case pairs.
We play a letter hunt — finding specific letters hidden around the room.
We match each letter to a picture of something that starts with its sound — like 'b' with 'ball'.

VC2EFLY07.w: We are learning to read and write some common words that we see a lot — words like 'the', 'and', 'is' and 'my'.
y: High-frequency words appear everywhere in reading — knowing them makes reading much smoother.
When we can recognise these words automatically, we can focus on sounding out the trickier ones.
Writing common words quickly helps us get our ideas down faster.
eg: We practise reading high-frequency words like 'the', 'and', 'is' and 'my' on sight.
We find these words in books and point them out during shared reading.
We write common words from memory using a whiteboard.
We play bingo with high-frequency words — covering them as the teacher reads them out.
We build sentences using high-frequency word cards and read them to a partner.
We keep a personal list of words we can read and write, adding new ones each week.

VC2EFLY08.w: We are learning that words are made up of meaningful parts — like how 'dogs' is made of 'dog' plus the 's' that means more than one.
y: Understanding word parts helps us figure out the meaning of new words.
This is the beginning of learning about how words are built — a skill that helps us with spelling and vocabulary.
When we see how words are made of parts, we can read and understand bigger words more easily.
eg: We learn that 'cats' has two parts — 'cat' and 's' — and the 's' means there is more than one.
We notice that 'playing' is made of 'play' and 'ing' and talk about what each part means.
We find words with 'un' at the start and discover it means 'not' — like 'unhappy'.
We build words by adding parts — starting with 'jump' and adding 'ing' or 'ed'.
We sort words into groups based on their endings — words with 's', words with 'ed', words with 'ing'.
We take apart compound words like 'rainbow' to find 'rain' and 'bow' inside.

VC2EFLY09.w: We are learning to read simple books by using our phonics skills and checking that what we read makes sense.
y: This is where all our phonics learning comes together — reading real texts on our own.
Checking that our reading makes sense teaches us to be active, thinking readers.
The more we practise reading, the more fluent and confident we become.
eg: We read a decodable text, sounding out words we do not know.
We start at the right place on the page and track from left to right with our finger.
We stop and re-read when something does not make sense.
We use the pictures to help us check whether our reading matches the story.
We notice when a word we read does not sound right and try again.
We read a short text to a partner and they help us fix any mistakes.

VC2EFLY10.w: We are learning to recognise different types of texts — like stories and information texts — and where we might find them.
y: Knowing different text types helps us choose the right one when we need information or entertainment.
Understanding text types helps us know what to expect when we start reading.
This helps us become smart readers who pick up the right text for the right purpose.
eg: We recognise symbols in everyday places — like exit signs and school crossing signs — and discuss why they are used.
We compare a storybook with an information book and talk about how they are different.
We sort a pile of texts into stories and non-fiction.
We find examples of different text types around our classroom — books, labels, instructions, charts.
We discuss where we might find a recipe versus where we might find a fairy tale.
We look at a text and predict whether it will be a story or an information text based on the cover.

VC2EFLY11.w: We are learning to spot some differences between stories (narratives) and information texts.
y: Knowing the difference helps us read each type in the right way.
Stories and information texts are organised differently — recognising this helps us find what we need.
This is an important reading skill that we will keep developing throughout school.
eg: We talk about what is 'made up' in a story versus what is 'real' in an information text.
We compare a fairy tale with a book about real animals and list the differences.
We look at the features of each — stories have characters and plot; information texts might have headings and facts.
We sort books into two groups: stories and information texts.
We notice that a story takes us on a journey, while an information text teaches us facts.
We discuss why an author might write a story versus an information text about the same topic.

VC2EFLY12.w: We are learning to use reading strategies — like picturing what is happening, guessing what might come next, making connections and asking questions — to understand texts better.
y: Active readers use strategies to make sense of what they read — and that is what we are learning to do.
These strategies help us understand texts even when we come across tricky parts.
The more strategies we have, the stronger our reading becomes.
eg: We visualise — make a picture in our mind — while listening to a story.
We predict what might happen next and then check if we were right.
We connect something in the story to something from our own life.
We summarise what happened by retelling the main events in order.
We ask questions while we read — 'Why did the character do that?' or 'What will happen now?'
We retell a story using picture cues, putting the events in the correct sequence.

VC2EFLY13.w: We are learning to write short texts to share our ideas and tell about things that happened.
y: Writing is how we capture our thinking and share it with others.
When we write about our ideas and experiences, we become authors with our own voice.
Practising writing now builds the foundation for all the writing we will do in the future.
eg: We write a sentence about something interesting that happened, using words we know.
We use our knowledge of sounds and letters to spell new words in our writing.
We draw a picture and write a sentence that matches it.
We create a short recount of a class activity using 'first', 'then' and 'last'.
We write labels and captions for our drawings to explain what they show.
We contribute a page to a class book about a topic we have been learning about.

VC2EFLY14.w: We are learning to look back at our writing together with others and check for meaning, spelling, capital letters and full stops.
y: Editing is an important part of writing — even professional writers edit their work.
When we check our writing, we make it clearer and easier for others to read.
Learning to edit now builds a habit that will improve all our future writing.
eg: We read our own writing back to the teacher or a partner to check it makes sense.
We look for capital letters at the start of sentences and fix any that are missing.
We check that every sentence ends with a full stop.
We circle words we are not sure how to spell and try again using sounds.
We help a partner find mistakes in their writing and suggest fixes.
We compare our first draft with our edited version and see how it improved.

VC2EFLY15.w: We are learning to write most letters — both upper-case and lower-case — forming them the correct way.
y: Correct letter formation makes our writing clear and easy to read.
When we form letters correctly, writing becomes faster and more natural.
Good handwriting skills help us express our ideas without struggling with the mechanics.
eg: We follow demonstrations of how to start and form each letter correctly.
We practise letters on lined paper, making sure they sit on the line.
We trace letters in sand, on whiteboards and in the air to learn the right movements.
We check our letters against a model to see if they look right.
We practise writing all the letters in our name neatly.
We build up to writing short words and sentences with correctly formed letters.

VC2E1LA01.w: We are learning how we use language, facial expressions and gestures to communicate with others — like when we ask for something, share information, or give instructions.
y: Understanding how we communicate helps us get our message across clearly.
We communicate with more than just words — our face and body help too.
This helps us interact well with different people in different situations.
eg: We practise asking a friend to play using polite words and a friendly face.
We role-play giving simple instructions and use gestures to help explain.
We notice how a smile or frown changes the meaning of what we say.
We practise asking the teacher for help using clear words and eye contact.
We compare asking for something nicely versus demanding it, and discuss the difference.
We look at pictures of people's faces and guess what they might be saying.

VC2E1LA02.w: We are learning to use language to explain why we like or dislike something and share our preferences.
y: Giving reasons for our opinions helps others understand our thinking.
This builds our ability to express ourselves clearly and confidently.
Explaining our preferences is the beginning of making thoughtful arguments.
eg: We say 'I like this book because the pictures are funny' instead of just 'I like it'.
We tell a partner which activity we prefer at school and explain why.
We use the word 'because' to connect our opinion with a reason.
We vote on a class choice and each share our reason for the way we voted.
We write a sentence explaining why a book is our favourite.
We discuss our favourite foods and give at least one reason for our choice.

VC2E1LA03.w: We are learning that different types of texts are set up differently depending on their purpose — like a recount tells what happened, while an information text teaches us facts.
y: Knowing how texts are organised helps us find information quickly.
Understanding text types helps us know what to expect when we start reading.
This also helps us write our own texts because we know how to structure them.
eg: We compare a story with an information report and talk about how they look different.
We notice that a recount starts with when and where something happened.
We look at how a procedure text uses numbered steps to tell us what to do.
We identify that an opinion text has a view and reasons to support it.
We sort different texts into groups based on their purpose.
We use a text map to plan our own writing, choosing the right structure.

VC2E1LA04.w: We are learning how repetition, rhyme and rhythm make simple poems, chants and songs flow smoothly and hang together.
y: Repetition and rhyme make language fun and memorable.
Understanding these patterns helps us enjoy and create our own poems and songs.
These language features help hold a text together so it feels complete.
eg: We listen to a poem and clap every time we hear a repeated word or line.
We find the rhyming words in a song and list them on the board.
We notice how a chant repeats a line to create a pattern.
We create our own short poem using repeated lines.
We tap out the rhythm of a favourite song and feel how the beat holds it together.
We compare two poems — one with rhyme and one without — and talk about how they feel different.

VC2E1LA05.w: We are learning how printed and digital texts are organised using features like page numbers, tables of contents, headings and titles.
y: Text features help us find our way around a book or website quickly.
When we know how texts are set up, we can find information without reading every word.
Understanding these features makes us smarter, more efficient readers.
eg: We use a table of contents to find a chapter about a topic we want to read.
We look at page numbers to find a specific page in a book.
We use headings to scan a text and decide which part to read first.
We explore a simple digital text and find the links that take us to different sections.
We compare a printed book with a digital text and find similar features in both.
We create a table of contents for our own class book.

VC2E1LA06.w: We are learning that a simple sentence is one idea, made of one group of words that goes together.
y: Understanding sentence structure helps us write clear, complete ideas.
Simple sentences are the building blocks of all good writing.
When we know what makes a sentence work, we can check our own writing.
eg: We identify simple sentences in a text, like 'The cat sat on the mat'.
We practise writing one complete idea in each sentence.
We check our writing to make sure each sentence expresses a whole thought.
We sort a group of words into 'sentences' and 'not sentences'.
We build simple sentences using subject-verb cards.
We read a sentence aloud and decide if it sounds complete.

VC2E1LA07.w: We are learning that different types of words do different jobs in a sentence — naming words, doing words, and describing words.
y: Knowing word types helps us build stronger, more interesting sentences.
Understanding how words work together is the foundation of grammar.
This helps us choose the right words when we write.
eg: We find the naming words (nouns) in a sentence — the people, places and things.
We identify the doing words (verbs) — the actions and happenings.
We spot the describing words (adjectives) that tell us more about the nouns.
We sort word cards into groups — naming words, doing words and describing words.
We build sentences by choosing one word from each group.
We play a game where we replace one word type in a sentence and see how it changes.

VC2E1LA08.w: We are learning how pictures and sounds in different types of texts add to or change the meaning.
y: Images and sounds can tell us things that words alone cannot.
Understanding how visuals and audio work helps us get the full message from any text.
This skill helps us with picture books, videos, websites and all sorts of texts.
eg: We compare the same story told with pictures versus told without pictures.
We listen to a story with sound effects and discuss how the sounds add meaning.
We look at photos in an information text and discuss what they teach us.
We watch a video with the sound off and then with sound, comparing what we understand each time.
We draw images to go with a simple text and talk about what they add.
We discuss how a scary picture in a book makes us feel different from a happy picture.

VC2E1LA09.w: We are learning to recognise and understand vocabulary from different subjects — like special words we use in maths, science or other topics.
y: Every subject has its own special words — learning them helps us understand the topic better.
A strong vocabulary means we can talk and write about many different things.
Knowing topic words helps us learn across all our school subjects.
eg: We learn maths words like 'addition' and 'subtraction' and use them when doing maths.
We collect new science words on a word wall and refer to them during experiments.
We use topic words in our writing, not just in class discussions.
We play vocabulary games that match topic words to their meanings.
We create a personal dictionary of new words we learn in different subjects.
We highlight special words in a text and discuss what they mean.

VC2E1LA10.w: We are learning how to use full stops, question marks and exclamation marks in our writing, and how to use capital letters correctly.
y: Punctuation guides readers through our writing, telling them when to pause or change tone.
Using the right punctuation makes our writing clearer and easier to understand.
Capital letters and punctuation are part of writing correctly in English.
eg: We put a full stop at the end of every telling sentence.
We use a question mark when we write a sentence that asks something.
We add an exclamation mark when a sentence shows strong feeling — like 'Wow!'
We start every sentence with a capital letter.
We use capital letters for people's names and the word 'I'.
We proofread a text and fix any missing or wrong punctuation marks.

VC2E1LE01.w: We are learning to talk about how words and pictures are used to create characters, settings and events in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors.
y: Understanding how language and images build a story helps us appreciate the craft of storytelling.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories use language and images in rich, meaningful ways.
This helps us think more deeply about the stories we read and hear.
eg: We look at the illustrations in a book by a First Nations illustrator and discuss how they show the setting.
We find the words an Aboriginal author uses to describe a character and talk about what picture they paint.
We compare how two stories use language differently to describe their settings.
We draw a character based only on the words the author uses and compare our drawings.
We discuss how the pictures and words together tell us more than either one alone.
We retell a scene from a First Nations story, using the same describing words the author used.

VC2E1LE02.w: We are learning to talk about different stories and connect them to our own experiences and feelings.
y: Connecting stories to our lives makes reading more meaningful and personal.
Sharing our responses helps us learn from each other's perspectives.
This builds our love of reading and our understanding of the world.
eg: We read a story and share something from our own life that it reminds us of.
We tell a partner how a character's experience is similar to or different from ours.
We discuss our favourite part of a story and explain why it stood out to us.
We write or draw a personal connection to a story we heard.
We compare two stories and talk about which one we connected with more.
We discuss how a story made us feel and whether anyone else felt the same way.

VC2E1LE03.w: We are learning to talk about the plot, characters and settings across different stories.
y: Discussing story elements helps us understand how stories are built.
Comparing stories helps us notice what makes each one unique.
This deepens our enjoyment and understanding of everything we read.
eg: We describe the main character in a story — what they look like, feel and do.
We identify the setting and explain where and when the story takes place.
We retell the main events of a plot in the right order.
We compare characters from two different stories.
We draw a story map showing the setting, characters and events.
We discuss how the setting affects what happens in the story.

VC2E1LE04.w: We are learning to listen to, recite and discuss poems, chants, rhymes and songs, and play with sound patterns like alliteration and rhyme.
y: Playing with sounds in poetry makes language fun and creative.
Reciting poems builds our confidence with spoken language.
Experimenting with sound patterns helps us become creative with language.
eg: We recite a favourite poem from memory, using expression and rhythm.
We listen for alliteration — words that start with the same sound, like 'silly snakes'.
We invent our own rhyming couplets for a class poem.
We clap the rhythm of a poem and feel how it flows.
We discuss what we like about a particular poem or song.
We create a tongue-twister using alliteration and perform it for the class.

VC2E1LE05.w: We are learning to retell or change stories we know, using the plot, characters, structure and language from the original.
y: Retelling builds our memory and storytelling skills.
Adapting stories lets us be creative while learning from how stories are made.
This helps us move from being readers to being authors.
eg: We retell a fairy tale using the same plot but adding our own details.
We rewrite a story's ending to create a different outcome.
We use story vocabulary we learned — like 'once upon a time' — in our own retelling.
We create a new character for a familiar story and explain how they fit in.
We perform a retelling with puppets or props.
We write a new version of a known story, keeping the structure but changing the setting.

VC2E1LY01.w: We are learning to take turns, speak clearly, listen actively, and respond to others during conversations and discussions.
y: Good communication means being both a great speaker and a great listener.
These skills help us learn from each other during class discussions.
Being able to interact well makes group work and conversations more enjoyable.
eg: We wait for our turn to speak and do not interrupt others.
We look at the person speaking and show we are listening.
We respond to what someone says by adding to their idea or asking a question.
We practise speaking clearly so everyone in the group can hear.
We use phrases like 'I agree because…' or 'I think differently because…' to respond.
We play a listening game where we repeat back what a partner said.

VC2E1LY02.w: We are learning to speak in front of a small audience, using our voice well to make our message clear and interesting.
y: Presenting to others helps us share our ideas with confidence.
Learning to use our voice effectively is a skill we will use throughout our lives.
Practising now makes speaking in front of groups less scary.
eg: We share a short presentation about a favourite hobby or object with our class.
We practise changing our volume — loud enough for everyone, but not shouting.
We use expression to make interesting parts sound exciting.
We slow down our speech to make sure every word is clear.
We pause between ideas to give listeners time to follow along.
We present a retelling of a story, using different voices for different characters.

VC2E1LY03.w: We are learning to blend, break apart and change the sounds in words — including words with consonant blends like 'cl' or 'nd'.
y: Being able to hear and manipulate sounds in words is the key to reading and spelling.
Working with consonant blends makes us ready for bigger, more complex words.
These skills make us faster and more accurate readers.
eg: We blend the sounds in 'clap' — 'c-l-a-p' — to read the word.
We break the word 'band' into its sounds — 'b-a-n-d'.
We change the first sound in 'cat' to 'b' to make 'bat'.
We practise hearing and saying consonant blends like 'st', 'bl' and 'tr'.
We play a game where we add or remove a sound from a word to make a new one.
We use letter tiles to build words with blends and then swap sounds.

VC2E1LY04.w: We are learning to use what we know about vowels, consonant blends and digraphs to read and write one- and two-syllable words.
y: Understanding sound patterns helps us decode new words we encounter in reading.
These phonics skills are the tools we use to unlock harder words.
The more patterns we learn, the more words we can read and spell independently.
eg: We read words with common long vowel sounds, like 'cake' and 'bike'.
We write words with consonant digraphs like 'sh', 'ch' and 'th'.
We break two-syllable words into parts and read each syllable.
We sort words by their vowel patterns — short 'a' versus long 'a'.
We use our phonics knowledge to attempt spelling new words in our writing.
We build words with magnetic letters, practising different consonant blends.

VC2E1LY05.w: We are learning that one letter can make more than one sound, and that every syllable has a vowel sound.
y: Understanding this helps us try different sounds when we get stuck on a word.
Knowing about vowels in syllables helps us break bigger words into manageable parts.
This makes us more flexible and resourceful readers.
eg: We learn that 'c' can sound like 'k' (in 'cat') or 's' (in 'city').
We discover that 'a' makes different sounds in 'cat', 'cake' and 'car'.
We clap the syllables in a word and notice each one has a vowel sound.
We highlight the vowel sound in each syllable of multi-syllable words.
We sort words by whether they use the hard or soft sound of 'g'.
We try both sounds of a letter when a word does not sound right the first time.

VC2E1LY06.w: We are learning to spell one- and two-syllable words using common letter patterns.
y: Knowing spelling patterns helps us spell many words, not just the ones we memorise.
Good spelling makes our writing easier for others to read.
Patterns give us strategies for spelling new words we have never written before.
eg: We use the '-at' pattern to spell 'cat', 'bat', 'hat' and 'flat'.
We learn common patterns like '-ight' to spell 'light', 'night' and 'sight'.
We practise spelling words with common endings like '-ed', '-ing' and '-er'.
We sort spelling words into pattern groups and find what they have in common.
We use our knowledge of patterns to have a go at spelling words in our writing.
We play a game where we build new words from a given pattern.

VC2E1LY07.w: We are learning to read and write more and more high-frequency words — the common words that appear everywhere in reading.
y: The more common words we know by sight, the faster and smoother our reading becomes.
These words are the glue that holds sentences together.
Knowing them helps us focus our energy on the trickier words.
eg: We add new high-frequency words to our personal word bank each week.
We read flash cards quickly to build speed with common words.
We find high-frequency words in our reading books and highlight them.
We practise writing common words from memory without looking.
We use common words we know to write sentences more quickly.
We play word games that help us recognise high-frequency words faster.

VC2E1LY08.w: We are learning how to use word parts — like prefixes and suffixes — to build families of related words.
y: Understanding word parts helps us figure out the meaning of new words.
Word families show us how one root word can become many different words.
This is a powerful strategy for both reading and spelling.
eg: We add '-ed' to 'jump' to make 'jumped' and talk about how the meaning changes.
We build a word family from 'play' — 'playing', 'played', 'player', 'playful'.
We add 'un-' to words to make their opposites — 'happy' becomes 'unhappy'.
We sort words into families based on their root word.
We discover that 'teach' and 'teacher' are related and the '-er' means 'a person who'.
We create a word family tree on a poster and add new members as we find them.

VC2E1LY09.w: We are learning to read real books and decodable texts, using phonics to work out words and checking that our reading makes sense.
y: Reading real texts is where all our phonics and vocabulary learning comes together.
Monitoring our reading — checking it makes sense — is what good readers do.
The more we read, the better we get at it.
eg: We read a decodable text and use phonics to sound out unfamiliar words.
We stop and self-correct when something we read does not make sense.
We use the pictures and the meaning of the sentence to help us when we are stuck.
We read with phrasing — grouping words together instead of reading one word at a time.
We re-read a sentence when it does not sound right to work out where we went wrong.
We read a short text to a partner and help each other with tricky words.

VC2E1LY10.w: We are learning to talk about different types of texts from a range of cultures and experiences.
y: Reading texts from different cultures opens our minds to new perspectives.
Discussing diverse texts helps us appreciate the richness of the world around us.
This builds our understanding and empathy for people whose lives are different from ours.
eg: We read a story from another culture and discuss what we learned about their traditions.
We compare a story from our own background with one from a different culture.
We talk about what is similar and different in stories from around the world.
We discuss how the characters in a multicultural text think, feel and act.
We share our own cultural stories with the class and explain what they mean to us.
We create a class display of stories from the many cultures in our community.

VC2E1LY11.w: We are learning to describe some differences between stories, information texts and opinion texts.
y: Knowing the difference between text types helps us choose the right one for what we need.
Understanding these differences helps us read each type more effectively.
This also helps us write the right kind of text for our purpose.
eg: We compare a story about a dog with an information text about dogs and note the differences.
We identify whether a text is trying to entertain, inform or persuade us.
We look at the features of each type — stories have characters, information texts have headings, opinion texts have arguments.
We sort a collection of texts into narrative, informative and persuasive groups.
We discuss which type of text we would use to convince our teacher to have a class party.
We create examples of all three types about the same topic — like our school.

VC2E1LY12.w: We are learning to use reading strategies — like visualising, predicting, connecting, summarising and questioning — to understand texts better.
y: Active readers do not just read words — they think while they read.
These strategies help us understand and remember more of what we read.
The more strategies we use, the deeper our understanding becomes.
eg: We close our eyes and visualise a scene from a story while someone reads it aloud.
We predict what will happen next based on clues in the text.
We connect something in the text to our own experience or another book.
We summarise a chapter in one or two sentences.
We ask questions about the text — 'Why did the character do that?'
We use our learnt vocabulary to help us understand difficult parts of a text.

VC2E1LY13.w: We are learning to write short stories and information texts — both written and spoken — about things we know and things we have learned.
y: Writing helps us organise and share our ideas clearly.
Creating our own texts makes us authors with our own voice and message.
Practising both narrative and informative writing gives us flexibility as writers.
eg: We write a short story with a beginning, middle and end.
We create an information text about a topic we studied, using topic-specific words.
We write a recount of a class excursion or special event.
We tell a spoken story to the class, using structure and interesting words.
We include describing words to make our writing more vivid and interesting.
We use a planning template to organise our ideas before we start writing.

VC2E1LY14.w: We are learning to re-read our own writing and think about ways to improve the meaning, spelling and punctuation.
y: Good writers always go back and check their work.
Re-reading our writing helps us catch mistakes and find ways to make it better.
Editing is a skill that improves all our writing, not just the piece we are working on.
eg: We read our writing out loud to hear if it sounds right.
We check that every sentence starts with a capital letter and ends with a full stop.
We look for spelling mistakes and try to fix them using our phonics knowledge.
We ask ourselves 'Does this make sense?' after reading each sentence.
We share our writing with a partner and discuss what could be changed.
We compare our first draft with our final version and notice how editing improved it.

VC2E1LY15.w: We are learning to write words using unjoined upper-case and lower-case letters.
y: Neat, clear handwriting means other people can read what we write.
Using correct letter sizes helps our writing look tidy and organised.
Practising handwriting now builds the muscle memory we need for fluent writing later.
eg: We practise writing on lined paper, making sure lower-case letters sit on the line.
We form tall letters like 'b', 'd' and 'h' reaching up to the top line.
We write sentences with all letters formed correctly and consistently sized.
We check our handwriting against a model and fix any letters that are not right.
We practise writing quickly while still keeping our letters clear.
We copy short sentences neatly, paying attention to spacing and letter formation.

VC2E2LA01.w: We are learning that the language we choose changes depending on the situation — including the role we are playing in a conversation.
y: Adjusting our language to the context helps us communicate effectively with different people.
Understanding roles in conversation helps us know when to be formal or informal.
This skill helps us in every social situation, from the classroom to the playground.
eg: We compare how we speak when playing with friends versus how we speak to a school visitor.
We role-play different scenarios and notice how our language changes each time.
We discuss why a doctor might use different words than a friend when talking about being sick.
We practise speaking as a reporter, a teacher and a student to feel the difference in language.
We listen to two conversations and identify which one is more formal.
We write a friendly letter and a polite request and compare the language we used.

VC2E2LA02.w: We are learning to use language to talk about what we think of texts and give reasons for our preferences.
y: Expressing informed opinions about texts builds our critical thinking.
Giving reasons for our preferences helps others understand our point of view.
This skill helps us become thoughtful, engaged readers.
eg: We tell a partner which book we preferred from two choices and explain why.
We write a simple book review sharing our opinion with reasons.
We use words like 'I enjoyed this because…' and 'My favourite part was…'.
We compare two poems and explain which one we liked more and why.
We discuss what made a text interesting, boring, funny or surprising.
We recommend a book to a friend, giving at least two reasons why they should read it.

VC2E2LA03.w: We are learning that different types of texts across all our subjects are set up differently and use different language features depending on their purpose.
y: Recognising text structures helps us read and write more efficiently in every subject.
Different purposes need different text types — and knowing this makes us flexible communicators.
This understanding helps us create the right kind of text for any situation.
eg: We compare how a science report is structured versus a narrative writing piece.
We identify the features of a procedure text — title, materials, steps — in a recipe.
We notice that an information text uses headings while a story uses paragraphs.
We look at texts from different subjects and identify their purpose and features.
We match text types to their purposes — to inform, to persuade, to entertain, to instruct.
We plan our own writing by first deciding on our purpose and then choosing the right structure.

VC2E2LA04.w: We are learning how texts are held together using pronouns and by leaving out words that the reader can work out.
y: Understanding cohesion helps us read smoothly without getting confused.
Using pronouns well makes our writing flow naturally instead of repeating the same words.
This is an important step towards writing that sounds polished and clear.
eg: We learn that instead of saying 'The dog ran. The dog was fast.' we can say 'The dog ran. It was fast.'
We identify pronouns like 'he', 'she', 'it' and 'they' in a text and work out who or what they refer to.
We notice when a writer leaves out a word we can already guess from context.
We rewrite a paragraph that repeats the same noun by replacing some with pronouns.
We read a text and draw arrows connecting pronouns to the nouns they replace.
We edit our own writing to include pronouns where we have repeated a name too many times.

VC2E2LA05.w: We are learning to use features like chapters, tables of contents, indexes, menus and links to find our way around books and digital texts.
y: Navigation features save us time by helping us find exactly what we need.
These skills work for both printed books and digital texts like websites.
Being able to navigate texts independently makes us stronger, more efficient learners.
eg: We use an index at the back of a book to find the page about a specific topic.
We navigate a website using sidebar menus and drop-down menus to find information.
We use chapter headings to decide which section to read for our research.
We use links in a digital text to jump to related information.
We compare the table of contents in a book with the menu on a website.
We practise finding specific facts quickly by using text features rather than reading everything.

VC2E2LA06.w: We are learning that we can join two ideas together into one longer sentence using connecting words like 'and', 'but' and 'so'.
y: Compound sentences help us express more complex ideas in our writing.
Joining ideas makes our writing more interesting and less choppy.
Learning to connect clauses is a big step towards being a more skilled writer.
eg: We join 'I like dogs' and 'I like cats' into 'I like dogs and I like cats'.
We use 'but' to show a contrast — 'I wanted to play outside, but it was raining'.
We use 'so' to show a result — 'It was cold, so we put on our jackets'.
We find compound sentences in our reading and identify the connecting word.
We practise turning two short sentences into one compound sentence.
We edit our writing to combine some short sentences using connectives.

VC2E2LA07.w: We are learning to make our sentences more interesting by adding extra detail to nouns with adjectives and articles, and to verbs with adverbs.
y: Adding detail to our sentences paints a clearer picture for the reader.
Understanding noun groups and verb groups makes our writing more descriptive.
This helps us write sentences that are both interesting and precise.
eg: We expand 'the cat' to 'the fluffy grey cat' by adding adjectives.
We change 'she ran' to 'she ran quickly' by adding an adverb.
We compare a plain sentence with an expanded one and discuss which is more interesting.
We identify noun groups in our reading — finding the noun and all the words that describe it.
We play a game where we keep adding words to a noun group until the sentence is very detailed.
We edit our own writing to expand some sentences with more description.

VC2E2LA08.w: We are learning that images and sounds can add to or multiply the meaning of a text — they do not just repeat what the words say.
y: Visuals and sounds can communicate things that words cannot easily express.
Understanding multimodal texts helps us get the full meaning from videos, websites and picture books.
This skill is especially important in our digital world, where texts combine words, images and sound.
eg: We look at an illustration in a picture book and find details that are not mentioned in the words.
We watch a short video and discuss how the music changes how we feel about the scene.
We compare an information text with photos versus one with diagrams and talk about which works better.
We notice how colour choices in illustrations create mood — dark colours for scary scenes, bright for happy ones.
We discuss how a voice-over in a video adds information that the images alone do not give us.
We create a multimodal text where our images add extra meaning beyond what our words say.

VC2E2LA09.w: We are learning to use the vocabulary we have learned and to start making deliberate word choices that suit the topic we are writing or talking about.
y: Choosing the right word for the right moment makes our communication more powerful.
Consciously selecting vocabulary helps us write with more precision and style.
A strong vocabulary is one of the most valuable tools a learner can have.
eg: We choose a more precise word — like 'sprinted' instead of 'ran' — in our writing.
We use topic-specific words from our science or maths learning in our writing.
We keep a word bank for the topic we are studying and use it when we write.
We discuss why an author chose a particular word and what effect it has.
We play synonym games where we find different words that mean nearly the same thing.
We read our writing aloud and swap weak or repeated words for stronger ones.

VC2E2LA10.w: We are learning that capital letters are used in titles and commas are used to separate items in a list.
y: Using capitals and commas correctly makes our writing professional and easy to read.
Punctuation rules are shared conventions that everyone follows, so learning them helps us write like real authors.
These are practical skills we use every time we write.
eg: We look at book titles and notice which words have capital letters.
We write a list of items using commas to separate them — like 'apples, bananas, oranges and grapes'.
We check our own titles to see if we used capital letters correctly.
We add commas to a list in our writing and read it aloud to hear the natural pauses.
We find commas in a text and discuss what they are separating.
We edit a piece of writing that is missing commas in lists and capitals in titles.

VC2E2LE01.w: We are learning to talk about how characters and settings are connected in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors and illustrators.
y: Characters and settings are deeply linked — understanding this makes us better readers.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories often show strong connections between people and place.
This helps us understand how storytelling works and appreciate different cultural perspectives.
eg: We read a story by an Aboriginal author and discuss how the setting affects what the characters do.
We notice how a First Nations illustrator shows the relationship between a character and Country.
We compare two stories and talk about how different settings create different moods and challenges for characters.
We draw a map of a story's setting and place the characters in it, showing their connection to place.
We discuss how a character would behave differently if the story was set somewhere else.
We talk about how place and Country are central to many First Nations stories.

VC2E2LE02.w: We are learning to compare features of different stories — like their characters and settings — and share which ones we prefer and why.
y: Comparing texts helps us notice what makes each one unique or effective.
Sharing preferences with reasons develops our ability to think critically about what we read.
This builds our identity as readers with our own tastes and opinions.
eg: We compare two stories and describe how their characters are similar and different.
We share our preference for one story over another and give two or three reasons.
We create a Venn diagram comparing features of two literary texts.
We vote on a class favourite and each explain our choice.
We discuss what kind of characters, settings or plots we enjoy most in stories.
We write a short review comparing two books we read this term.

VC2E2LE03.w: We are learning to discuss the plot, characters and settings of stories and identify how language is used to bring these features to life.
y: Noticing how authors use language helps us learn from their craft.
Understanding how language creates characters and settings deepens our enjoyment of stories.
This helps us use similar language techniques in our own writing.
eg: We find the words an author uses to describe a character's personality and discuss their effect.
We identify how the author uses language to make the setting feel real — like 'the cold wind howled'.
We trace the plot of a story, noting the key events and how language builds tension.
We discuss how an author's word choices make us feel about a character.
We highlight descriptive language in a text and explain what it helps us picture.
We try using similar language techniques in our own story writing.

VC2E2LE04.w: We are learning to identify, copy and experiment with the rhythmic sound and word patterns in poems, chants, rhymes and songs.
y: Experimenting with rhythm and sound builds our creativity with language.
Understanding these patterns helps us appreciate poetry and create our own.
Playing with sound and rhythm makes language learning enjoyable and memorable.
eg: We identify the rhythm pattern in a poem by clapping the beats.
We reproduce the rhyme scheme of a poem, writing new lines that follow the same pattern.
We experiment with alliteration in our own poem — like 'slippery snakes slide silently'.
We create our own chant using a repeated rhythm pattern.
We listen to different songs and compare their rhythms — fast, slow, bouncy, smooth.
We write a short poem that plays with sound patterns, then perform it for the class.

VC2E2LE05.w: We are learning to create our own texts by borrowing and adapting the structures and language features of stories we have read — through writing, drawing and performance.
y: Adapting stories teaches us how authors structure and craft their work.
Creating our own versions builds our confidence as writers and performers.
Learning from other authors is how all great writers develop their skills.
eg: We write a new story using the same structure as a familiar fairy tale but with different characters.
We draw a comic strip that adapts a story we read, adding our own twist.
We perform a reader's theatre version of a story, adapting the dialogue.
We borrow language features from a favourite author — like their use of repetition — in our own writing.
We rewrite a story from a different character's point of view.
We create a picture book inspired by the style of an author we studied.

VC2E2LY01.w: We are learning to actively listen, follow instructions, and extend our ideas and the ideas of others when we are discussing topics and texts.
y: Active listening and building on others' ideas makes group discussions richer and more productive.
Following instructions well helps us learn more efficiently.
These skills are essential for working with others in every part of school and life.
eg: We listen carefully to a classmate's idea and then add our own thought that builds on it.
We follow multi-step instructions during a class activity.
We ask a question to extend a discussion — 'Can you tell me more about that?'
We paraphrase what someone said to show we understood — 'So you're saying that…'
We stay on topic during a group discussion instead of changing the subject.
We reflect after a discussion on something new we learned from listening to others.

VC2E2LY02.w: We are learning to speak in front of an audience, using our voice in different ways to make our talk clear and engaging.
y: Speaking skills help us share our ideas with confidence and clarity.
Using vocal features like pace, volume and expression keeps our audience interested.
Practising spoken presentations builds life-long communication skills.
eg: We deliver a short talk about a topic we researched, using notes to stay on track.
We experiment with changing our pace — slowing down for important points.
We use expression to match our content — excited for happy news, serious for important facts.
We project our voice so everyone in the room can hear clearly.
We make eye contact with our audience during our presentation.
We practise our talk with a partner first and get feedback before presenting to the class.

VC2E2LY03.w: We are learning to use our skills in blending, segmenting and changing sounds to read and write increasingly complex words.
y: Strong phonemic awareness helps us tackle bigger and more challenging words.
These skills are the engine behind our growing reading and spelling abilities.
The better we get at hearing sounds in words, the more independently we can read and write.
eg: We blend sounds in longer words with consonant clusters, like 'string' or 'splash'.
We segment complex words into individual sounds for spelling.
We manipulate sounds — changing 'strip' to 'strap' by swapping the vowel.
We practise with words that have multiple consonant blends.
We use our phonemic skills to attempt spelling unfamiliar words.
We play word games that challenge us to blend and segment more difficult words.

VC2E2LY04.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds and letters to read and write words with vowel digraphs, less common letter patterns, and silent letters.
y: English has many spelling patterns — learning them gives us tools to decode harder words.
Understanding these patterns helps us spell words that do not follow simple rules.
This knowledge makes us more confident when we encounter unfamiliar words.
eg: We read words with vowel digraphs like 'oi' in 'coin' and 'ea' in 'bread'.
We learn about silent letters — like the 'k' in 'knee' and the 'w' in 'write'.
We spell words with less common patterns, like the 'igh' in 'light'.
We sort words by their vowel patterns and look for rules.
We use our knowledge to decode multi-syllable words we have not seen before.
We create a reference chart of tricky spelling patterns we have learned.

VC2E2LY05.w: We are learning to use spelling patterns and word parts to read and write words whose spelling is not easy to guess from how they sound.
y: Not all words are spelled the way they sound — patterns and word parts help us manage these tricky words.
This knowledge gives us strategies for spelling beyond just sounding out.
Understanding morphemes means we can spell related words correctly.
eg: We learn that 'sign' has a silent 'g' but the 'g' shows up in related words like 'signal'.
We use morphemes to spell words — knowing 'kind' helps us spell 'kindness' and 'unkind'.
We sort words with unusual spellings and look for common patterns.
We use mnemonics and word origins to remember tricky spellings.
We practise spelling words that have irregular vowel sounds.
We connect spelling to meaning — words that look similar often share a meaning link.

VC2E2LY06.w: We are learning to build families of words by adding prefixes and suffixes to root words.
y: Understanding prefixes and suffixes helps us figure out the meaning of new words.
Word-building skills expand our vocabulary rapidly.
This is a powerful strategy for both reading comprehension and spelling.
eg: We add the prefix 're-' to 'build' to make 'rebuild' and discuss how the meaning changes.
We explore suffixes like '-ful', '-less' and '-ment' and how they change word meaning.
We build a word web starting with a root word and branching out with prefixes and suffixes.
We sort words into families — 'help', 'helpful', 'helpless', 'unhelpful'.
We use morphemic knowledge to decode a word we have never seen before — like 'unbreakable'.
We create new words using prefixes and suffixes and check if they are real words.

VC2E2LY07.w: We are learning to read different types of texts smoothly and with expression, using our phonics and word knowledge, and checking that what we read makes sense.
