y: Fluent reading with expression shows strong comprehension and makes reading enjoyable.
Monitoring our reading — stopping when something does not make sense — is a powerful self-checking strategy.
The more fluently we read, the more we can focus on understanding the meaning.
eg: We read a text with phrasing and expression, not word by word.
We re-read a sentence when it does not make sense and try a different approach.
We use our knowledge of word patterns and phonics to decode unfamiliar words quickly.
We read different text types with the right tone — a story with expression, an information text with clarity.
We practise reading aloud to a partner, focusing on fluency and accuracy.
We self-correct when a word we read does not fit the meaning of the sentence.

VC2E2LY08.w: We are learning to discuss how the same topic or information can be presented differently in different types of texts.
y: Understanding that the same topic can be presented many ways helps us think critically about what we read.
Comparing text types builds our awareness of how authors make choices to suit their purpose.
This helps us choose the best text type when we need to write about a topic ourselves.
eg: We compare how frogs are presented in a story versus an information text.
We discuss how a poster about recycling presents information differently from a report.
We look at three texts about the same topic and identify the purpose of each one.
We talk about which text type was most effective at teaching us something new and why.
We compare a persuasive text and an informative text on the same topic.
We choose a topic and create two different text types about it, then compare them.

VC2E2LY09.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies to understand texts we listen to, watch and read — and to discuss our understanding with others.
y: Comprehension strategies help us get the most out of every text we encounter.
Discussing our understanding with others deepens our thinking and reveals new perspectives.
These strategies make us independent, thoughtful readers.
eg: We visualise scenes as we read and describe what we see in our minds.
We make predictions before and during reading and check them afterwards.
We ask questions about confusing parts and work to answer them.
We summarise the main idea of a text in our own words.
We make connections between the text and our own experiences, other texts or the world.
We discuss our understanding with a partner and compare our interpretations.

VC2E2LY10.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — stories, information texts and opinion texts — using our growing knowledge of text structure, grammar and vocabulary.
y: Being able to write different text types gives us the power to inform, entertain and persuade.
Using good structure, grammar and vocabulary makes our writing clear and effective.
Every text we create builds our skills as writers.
eg: We write a narrative with a clear orientation, complication and resolution.
We create an information text with an introduction, body paragraphs and a conclusion.
We write a persuasive text giving our opinion with at least two supporting reasons.
We use topic-specific vocabulary in our information writing.
We include descriptive language in our stories to create vivid settings and characters.
We plan our writing before we start, choosing the right structure for our purpose.

VC2E2LY11.w: We are learning to re-read and edit our own texts and the texts of others, improving the meaning, structure, spelling and punctuation.
y: Editing is what turns good writing into great writing.
Learning to edit others' work as well as our own builds our critical reading skills.
These editing skills improve everything we write, now and in the future.
eg: We re-read our writing and check that each sentence makes sense.
We edit a partner's writing and suggest improvements for clarity.
We check for spelling, punctuation and grammar mistakes in our own work.
We read our text aloud to hear where it sounds awkward and fix those parts.
We use an editing checklist to systematically review our writing.
We compare our first draft with our edited version and celebrate the improvements.

VC2E2LY12.w: We are learning to write legibly and fluently using unjoined upper-case and lower-case letters.
y: Legible handwriting ensures our ideas can be read and appreciated by others.
Fluent handwriting means we can focus on our ideas rather than on forming letters.
Neat, consistent writing is a skill that serves us in every subject.
eg: We practise writing at a comfortable speed while keeping letters neat and even.
We use correct letter formation for both upper-case and lower-case letters.
We check our spacing between words and letters to make sure our writing is clear.
We write longer passages, maintaining consistent letter size and formation throughout.
We compare our handwriting from the start of the year to now and notice our improvement.
We copy a short text neatly, paying attention to presentation.

VC2E2LY13.w: We are learning to write letters neatly and smoothly, using lower-case and upper-case letters that aren't joined together.
y: Writing neatly helps other people read and understand what we've written.
Practising our handwriting makes it faster and easier so we can focus on our ideas.
Good handwriting skills give us confidence when we write stories, letters, and schoolwork.
eg: We might practise writing each letter of the alphabet on lined paper, making sure they sit on the line.
We could trace over dotted letters to help our hands learn the right movements.
We might write our name at the top of every page, trying to make it neater each time.
We could copy a short sentence from the board, focusing on keeping our letters the same size.
We might practise writing upper-case letters for the start of names and sentences.
We could have a handwriting warm-up each morning where we write a few words as neatly as we can.

VC2E3LA01.w: We are learning that working well with others depends on shared social conventions — like taking turns in conversation — and that these vary depending on the situation.
y: Understanding conversational conventions helps us collaborate more smoothly in groups.
These social skills make us better communicators in every setting — school, home and community.
Different situations call for different communication styles, and recognising this gives us flexibility.
eg: We practise turn-taking in a group discussion and notice how it keeps the conversation fair.
We compare how we speak in a classroom debate versus a casual chat with friends.
We role-play different scenarios and adjust our language to suit each one.
We discuss what happens when social conventions break down — like when people talk over each other.
We create a set of discussion norms for our class and explain why each one matters.
We notice how conventions change in digital communication, like online chats versus face-to-face talks.

VC2E3LA02.w: We are learning how to make our language about feelings and opinions stronger or softer depending on the effect we want.
y: Controlling the force of our language helps us express ourselves appropriately in different situations.
Knowing how to soften or strengthen our words gives us more power as communicators.
This skill helps us be persuasive without being aggressive, and gentle without being weak.
eg: We compare 'I think this is wrong' with 'This is absolutely wrong!' and discuss the difference in force.
We practise turning a strong statement into a softer one using words like 'perhaps' or 'I feel'.
We find examples in texts where authors use strong emotional language and discuss its impact.
We rewrite a sentence to make it more forceful, then less forceful, and compare the effects.
We discuss when it is appropriate to use strong language versus gentle language.
We identify emotional language in advertisements and discuss how it persuades us.

VC2E3LA03.w: We are learning that different types of texts across all our school subjects have different structures and language features depending on what they are trying to do.
y: Recognising text structures helps us read more efficiently across all subjects.
Understanding how texts are built helps us create well-organised writing of our own.
This knowledge helps us switch confidently between different types of writing.
eg: We compare a science report with a history recount and identify their different features.
We describe how a procedure text is structured differently from an explanation text.
We identify the language features of persuasive texts — like emotive words and rhetorical questions.
We look at how information texts use headings, subheadings and diagrams to organise ideas.
We match text types to their purposes and discuss why the structure suits the purpose.
We plan our own writing by first identifying the text type and its expected structure.

VC2E3LA04.w: We are learning that paragraphs group related information together and are an important way to organise our writing.
y: Paragraphs help readers follow our thinking by breaking ideas into manageable chunks.
Well-organised paragraphs make our writing clearer and more professional.
Understanding paragraphs is essential for writing longer, more complex texts.
eg: We identify paragraphs in a text and explain what each one is about.
We practise starting a new paragraph when we move to a new idea or topic.
We reorganise a jumbled text by grouping sentences into logical paragraphs.
We write a three-paragraph text with an introduction, body and conclusion.
We compare a well-paragraphed text with one big block of text and discuss which is easier to read.
We use topic sentences to signal what each paragraph is about.

VC2E3LA05.w: We are learning to recognise the layout features of printed and digital texts — like headings, menus and navigation words — and understand their purpose.
y: Layout features help us find information quickly without reading every word.
Understanding these features makes us more efficient readers across all text types.
Digital navigation skills are especially important in a world full of online information.
eg: We identify headings, subheadings and bold words in an information text and explain their purpose.
We navigate a website using menus, links and search bars to find specific information.
We compare the layout of a webpage with a printed book and discuss similarities and differences.
We create our own information text with effective headings and layout features.
We discuss why a table of contents is useful and practise using one to find information.
We evaluate whether a text's layout features actually help the reader or just look decorative.

VC2E3LA06.w: We are learning that a clause usually has a subject and a verb that need to match — this is the basic building block of sentences.
y: Understanding clauses helps us write grammatically correct sentences.
Subject-verb agreement is one of the most important grammar rules in English.
This knowledge helps us check and fix our own writing.
eg: We identify the subject and verb in simple sentences — 'The dog (subject) runs (verb)'.
We check that our subjects and verbs agree — 'she runs' not 'she run'.
We fix sentences where the subject and verb do not match.
We combine clauses to build more complex sentences.
We underline the clause in each sentence and circle the subject and verb.
We play a matching game where we pair subjects with the correct verb form.

VC2E3LA07.w: We are learning that verbs do not just describe actions — they also describe feelings, thoughts, speech and relationships between things.
y: Understanding different types of verbs helps us write more precisely and expressively.
This expands the range of things we can describe in our writing.
Knowing verb types helps us analyse how authors create meaning.
eg: We sort verbs into groups: doing (run, build), feeling (love, fear), thinking (believe, wonder), saying (whispered, shouted) and relating (is, has).
We find examples of each type of verb in our reading.
We use different verb types in our own writing to add variety.
We discuss why an author chose a 'saying' verb like 'whispered' instead of 'said'.
We rewrite sentences using different verb types and compare the effect.
We create a verb wall in our classroom organised by process type.

VC2E3LA08.w: We are learning that verbs are connected to time — they can be in the past, present or future tense.
y: Using the right tense tells the reader when something happened, is happening, or will happen.
Consistent tense use keeps our writing clear and easy to follow.
Understanding tense is essential for recounts, narratives and reports.
eg: We identify whether a sentence is in the past, present or future tense.
We change a sentence from past to present tense and notice how the verb changes.
We check our writing to make sure we have used a consistent tense throughout.
We notice that recounts use past tense while instructions use present tense.
We sort verb cards into past, present and future groups.
We write three versions of the same event — one in each tense — and compare them.

VC2E3LA09.w: We are learning how words like 'must', 'should', 'could' and 'might' show how certain or necessary something is.
y: Modal verbs help us express degrees of certainty, possibility and obligation.
Choosing the right modal verb changes the strength of our message.
Understanding modals helps us read critically — noticing whether a text is stating a fact or a possibility.
eg: We compare 'You must wear a hat' with 'You could wear a hat' and discuss the difference.
We rank modal verbs from strongest to weakest — 'must, should, could, might'.
We find modal verbs in texts and discuss what they tell us about certainty.
We use different modal verbs in persuasive writing to vary the strength of our arguments.
We rewrite rules using different modal verbs and discuss how the meaning changes.
We identify modal verbs in science texts and discuss whether statements are facts or possibilities.

VC2E3LA10.w: We are learning how images and sounds go beyond the words in a text to add extra or deeper meaning.
y: Images and sounds can communicate information and feelings that words alone cannot.
Understanding multimodal elements makes us better readers of picture books, websites, videos and presentations.
This helps us create our own texts that combine words, images and sound effectively.
eg: We compare a text with illustrations to the same text without and discuss what the images add.
We listen to a podcast and discuss how background music and sound effects create mood.
We analyse how an image in an information text extends the meaning beyond what the words say.
We create a multimodal presentation where our images add information the words do not cover.
We discuss how colour, size and placement of images affect how we read a text.
We compare a printed version and a video version of the same story and discuss how sound adds meaning.

VC2E3LA11.w: We are learning to build our vocabulary of topic-specific and technical words, and understand that the same word can mean different things in different contexts.
y: A strong technical vocabulary helps us understand and communicate about specialised topics.
Knowing that words have multiple meanings prevents confusion when we read across subjects.
The more words we know, the more precisely we can express our ideas.
eg: We learn that 'table' means furniture in everyday language but a data display in maths.
We collect technical words from our science unit and use them correctly in our writing.
We create a glossary for a topic we are studying.
We discuss how the word 'current' means different things in science, geography and everyday life.
We use context clues to work out which meaning of a word is being used.
We play a game where we give two definitions for a word and classmates guess the context.

VC2E3LA12.w: We are learning how apostrophes work — showing where letters are missing in shortened words, and showing who owns something.
y: Apostrophes are one of the most commonly used (and misused) punctuation marks in English.
Understanding apostrophes helps us read and write more accurately.
Knowing the two jobs of apostrophes prevents common mistakes in our writing.
eg: We learn that 'don't' means 'do not' — the apostrophe shows where the 'o' is missing.
We practise writing contractions: 'can't', 'isn't', 'we're', 'they'll'.
We use apostrophes to show possession: 'the dog's bone' means the bone belonging to the dog.
We compare singular possession (the girl's book) with plural possession (the girls' books).
We identify apostrophes in texts and explain whether they show contraction or possession.
We edit a text that has missing or incorrectly placed apostrophes.

VC2E3LE01.w: We are learning to talk about characters, settings and events in stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors and how they connect to different contexts.
y: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander stories offer perspectives that deepen our understanding of culture, Country and community.
Discussing literary elements like characters and settings builds our analytical thinking about stories.
Understanding different contexts in First Nations literature broadens our worldview.
eg: We discuss how a character in an Aboriginal story relates to their Country and community.
We compare settings in First Nations stories with settings in other stories we have read.
We identify events in a Torres Strait Islander story and discuss what they mean in their cultural context.
We explore how different First Nations stories present similar themes in different ways.
We discuss the relationship between characters and the land in stories by Aboriginal authors.
We share what we learn about different cultures and places through the stories we read.

VC2E3LE02.w: We are learning to talk about how characters' experiences in stories connect to our own experiences, and to explain why we prefer certain texts.
y: Connecting stories to our own lives deepens our understanding and enjoyment of reading.
Giving reasons for our preferences helps us develop as thoughtful, critical readers.
Making personal connections with characters builds empathy and self-awareness.
eg: We share how a character's experience reminds us of something that happened in our own life.
We explain why we prefer one story over another, using specific reasons.
We discuss how a character dealt with a challenge and compare it with how we might respond.
We write a response connecting a character's feelings to our own feelings in a similar situation.
We recommend a book to a friend and explain why they might connect with it.
We create a 'me and the character' comparison chart showing similarities and differences.

VC2E3LE03.w: We are learning how authors use language and illustrations to build plots, create characters and develop settings, and how these features shape the meaning of a text.
y: Understanding an author's craft helps us appreciate how stories are made.
Analysing language and illustrations deepens our enjoyment and understanding of what we read.
Learning from how published authors work helps us improve our own writing.
eg: We find examples of descriptive language an author uses to make a setting feel real.
We discuss how an illustrator's choices — like colour and composition — add meaning to the story.
We identify how an author builds tension in a plot through word choices and pacing.
We compare how two authors create a similar type of character using different language techniques.
We analyse how dialogue reveals character personality and motivations.
We try using a technique we noticed in our reading — like foreshadowing — in our own story.

VC2E3LE04.w: We are learning about literary devices — like rhythm and onomatopoeia — and how they create effects and shape meaning in poems and stories.
y: Literary devices are tools that writers use to make their words more powerful and memorable.
Recognising these devices helps us understand why some texts are more impactful than others.
Learning about these techniques helps us use them in our own creative writing.
eg: We find examples of onomatopoeia — words like 'crash', 'buzz' and 'sizzle' — and discuss their effect.
We identify the rhythm in a poem and discuss how it creates a particular mood or feel.
We explore how repetition is used in poetry and prose for emphasis or effect.
We write our own poem using onomatopoeia and rhythm deliberately.
We discuss how an author uses a simile to help us picture something clearly.
We compare two poems — one with strong rhythm and one without — and discuss the difference.

VC2E3LE05.w: We are learning to create our own stories and texts by using or adapting ideas, characters, settings and language features from stories we have read.
y: All writers learn from other writers — adapting ideas is how we develop our own style.
Using techniques from published texts gives us a strong foundation for our creative work.
This process helps us experiment and take creative risks in our writing.
eg: We write a story using a plot structure we noticed in a book we enjoyed.
We create a character inspired by one we read about, but in a new setting.
We borrow a language technique — like using short sentences for tension — from an author we admire.
We adapt a fairy tale by changing the setting to a modern Australian city.
We write a poem using the same structure as one we studied in class.
We create a new adventure for a character from a book, imagining what happens next.

VC2E3LY01.w: We are learning to use conversation skills to share ideas and information about texts and topics with others.
y: Good discussion skills help us learn from each other and build shared understanding.
Contributing to conversations is an important part of being part of a learning community.
These skills help us in every subject, not just English.
eg: We contribute relevant ideas during a class discussion about a text we read.
We share information about a topic using evidence from our reading.
We ask questions to clarify something a classmate said.
We build on another person's idea by saying 'I agree, and I'd also add…'.
We take turns and ensure everyone gets a chance to contribute.
We stay on topic and help redirect the conversation if it goes off track.

VC2E3LY02.w: We are learning to deliver spoken presentations to an audience, using our voice well to communicate our message clearly.
y: Speaking confidently to an audience is a skill we will use throughout our lives.
Using vocal features like pace, volume and expression makes our presentations engaging.
Practising oral presentations builds our self-confidence.
eg: We deliver a short talk about a topic we researched, using notes as a guide.
We practise using pace — slowing down for important points and speeding up for exciting bits.
We adjust our volume so everyone can hear without shouting.
We use expression and emphasis to keep our audience interested.
We make eye contact with different people in the audience.
We listen to feedback after our talk and use it to improve next time.

VC2E3LY03.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds, syllables, blending and segmenting to read and write longer, multi-syllable words fluently.
y: Being able to read and write multi-syllable words opens up more complex texts to us.
Fluent decoding means we can focus more on understanding what we read.
These skills make us more confident and independent readers and writers.
eg: We break a multi-syllable word like 'butterfly' into parts — 'but-ter-fly' — and read each part.
We use blending to read unfamiliar multi-syllable words in our reading.
We apply segmenting to spell long words by writing each syllable.
We practise reading multi-syllable words quickly and smoothly.
We identify common letter patterns within multi-syllable words that help us decode them.
We use our phonics knowledge to attempt spelling words we have not written before.

VC2E3LY04.w: We are learning how to use our knowledge of base words, prefixes and suffixes — and the rules for adding endings to words — to read and spell more words.
y: Morphological knowledge is one of the most powerful tools for expanding our vocabulary.
Understanding how words are built helps us decode unfamiliar words and spell them correctly.
These patterns work across thousands of words, so learning them pays off enormously.
eg: We identify the base word, prefix and suffix in words like 'unhappiness' (un + happy + ness).
We learn the rules for adding suffixes — like doubling the consonant in 'running' or dropping the 'e' in 'making'.
We use prefixes to work out the meaning of unfamiliar words — 'dis' means 'not' or 'opposite'.
We build word families from a base word, adding different prefixes and suffixes.
We apply suffix rules when spelling — knowing when to change 'y' to 'i' before adding '-ed'.
We use morphemic knowledge to read a new word by recognising its parts.

VC2E3LY05.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of how sounds connect to letters, including less common patterns, to spell words.
y: English spelling has many patterns — learning the less common ones helps us spell a wider range of words.
Good spelling makes our writing easier to read and more professional.
Understanding phoneme-grapheme relationships gives us strategies for spelling any word.
eg: We learn less common letter patterns like 'ph' for the 'f' sound in 'phone'.
We practise spelling words with unusual vowel combinations like 'ough' in 'thought'.
We sort words by their letter patterns and look for spelling rules.
We use mnemonics to remember tricky spellings.
We apply our phonics knowledge to have a go at spelling new words, then check.
We keep a personal list of words with unusual letter patterns we have learned.

VC2E3LY06.w: We are learning to recognise, read and write most high-frequency words — including homophones, which are words that sound the same but are spelled differently.
y: Knowing high-frequency words by sight makes our reading faster and our writing more accurate.
Understanding homophones prevents common spelling mistakes.
These words appear so often that getting them right improves all our reading and writing.
eg: We learn the difference between homophones like 'their', 'there' and 'they're'.
We practise distinguishing 'to', 'too' and 'two' in our writing.
We use context to decide which spelling of a homophone to use.
We create homophone flash cards with the word, its meaning and a sentence.
We proofread our writing specifically looking for homophone errors.
We play a game matching homophones to their correct definitions.

VC2E3LY07.w: We are learning to read different types of texts using our knowledge of sounds, word meanings and grammar to read accurately and with understanding.
y: Combining multiple reading strategies makes us powerful, flexible readers.
Reading accurately and fluently means we can focus on understanding and enjoying texts.
Self-correcting when something does not make sense is what skilled readers do.
eg: We read a new text, using phonics to decode unfamiliar words.
We use the meaning of the sentence to help us work out a word we do not recognise.
We re-read a sentence when it does not make sense and try a different strategy.
We self-correct when we realise a word we read does not fit grammatically.
We read aloud with fluency and expression, showing we understand the text.
We adjust our reading approach for different text types — slower and more careful for complex information.

VC2E3LY08.w: We are learning to discuss how texts with the same purpose can be created differently for different audiences.
y: Understanding audience helps us create texts that speak directly to the people we are writing for.
Recognising how audience shapes a text helps us be more critical readers.
This skill is essential for all communication — from writing to speaking to creating.
eg: We compare an information text about animals written for young children with one written for older students.
We discuss how a persuasive text about recycling might change depending on whether it is for kids or adults.
We identify the audience of different texts and explain the clues that told us.
We rewrite a paragraph for a different audience and discuss what we changed.
We look at two versions of a recipe — one for beginners and one for experienced cooks — and compare them.
We create two versions of the same text — one for our peers and one for younger students.

VC2E3LY09.w: We are learning to figure out the purpose and audience of a text by looking at its language features and images.
y: Knowing the purpose and audience of a text helps us understand why it was written the way it was.
This critical reading skill helps us evaluate texts and detect bias.
Understanding purpose and audience makes us more intentional when we create our own texts.
eg: We read a text and identify whether its purpose is to inform, persuade, entertain or instruct.
We look at the language features and images in an advertisement and discuss who it targets.
We compare two texts about the same topic with different purposes and identify the clues.
We discuss how images in a text help us identify its intended audience.
We evaluate whether a text achieves its purpose effectively.
We plan our own text by first deciding on our purpose and audience, then choosing appropriate features.

VC2E3LY10.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies — like visualising, predicting, connecting, summarising, monitoring and questioning — to analyse texts deeply.
y: Active reading strategies help us understand texts at a deeper level than just surface meaning.
Using multiple strategies makes us flexible readers who can handle any text.
These habits of mind transfer to all our learning, not just reading.
eg: We visualise a scene from a text and describe what we see in our minds.
We make predictions and then check them against what actually happens.
We draw on what we already know about a topic to help us understand a new text.
We summarise the main idea of each paragraph in our own words.
We monitor our understanding and stop when something does not make sense.
We ask deep questions about a text — 'Why did the author include this detail?'

VC2E3LY11.w: We are learning to create stories, information texts and persuasive texts that have ideas grouped into clear paragraphs, using topic-specific vocabulary.
y: Writing in paragraphs with the right vocabulary shows we are becoming skilled, organised writers.
Using topic-specific words makes our writing more precise and credible.
Creating different text types builds our versatility as writers.
eg: We write a narrative with paragraphs for the orientation, complication and resolution.
We create an information text with a clear introduction, body paragraphs and conclusion.
We write a persuasive text with an opinion statement and supporting paragraphs.
We use technical vocabulary from our topic in our writing.
We plan our paragraphs before writing, using graphic organisers.
We peer-review a classmate's text and check that their paragraphs are well-organised.

VC2E3LY12.w: We are learning to re-read and edit our texts to check the meaning, structure, grammar and punctuation.
y: Editing is where good writing becomes great writing.
Checking our own work for errors and improvements is a life-long writing skill.
Editing helps us communicate our ideas as clearly as possible.
eg: We re-read our writing and check that each paragraph focuses on one main idea.
We look for grammar errors like incorrect verb tense or subject-verb agreement.
We check that we have used punctuation correctly throughout our text.
We read our work aloud to hear where sentences are confusing or incomplete.
We use an editing checklist to systematically review our writing.
We make revisions to improve the clarity and flow of our text.

VC2E3LY13.w: We are learning to write using joined (cursive) handwriting with clearly formed letters that are the same size.
y: Joined writing is faster than printing, which means we can get our ideas down more quickly.
Consistent, clear handwriting shows care and makes our work easy for anyone to read.
This is the handwriting style we will use throughout school and into adult life.
eg: We practise joining letters together smoothly — like 'th', 'ing' and 'tion'.
We focus on keeping our letters the same size throughout our writing.
We write on lined paper, making sure our letters sit neatly on the lines.
We compare our joined writing from the start of the year to now and see our progress.
We copy a passage using our best joined handwriting.
We use joined writing in all our daily work to build fluency and speed.

VC2E4LA01.w: We are learning how language is used to build and maintain relationships in both formal and informal situations.
y: Understanding how language builds relationships helps us communicate more effectively with all kinds of people.
Knowing when to be formal or informal is an important social skill.
This awareness helps us navigate different contexts — from casual conversations to more structured settings.
eg: We compare formal greetings (like 'Good morning, Ms Smith') with informal ones (like 'Hey!').
We discuss why we change our language when talking to friends versus teachers or unfamiliar adults.
We role-play formal and informal situations and reflect on the language choices we made.
We identify formal and informal language in different texts and discuss their purposes.
We write a formal letter and an informal message about the same topic and compare them.
We discuss how language builds trust and respect in relationships.

VC2E4LA02.w: We are learning to tell the difference between language that expresses an opinion, states a fact, or expresses a feeling.
y: Being able to distinguish fact from opinion is one of the most important thinking skills we can develop.
Understanding the language of feelings helps us write and speak with emotional intelligence.
This skill helps us read critically and evaluate the reliability of information.
eg: We sort statements into categories: fact ('Water boils at 100°C'), opinion ('Swimming is the best sport') and feeling ('I feel nervous about the test').
We find examples of each type in newspaper articles and advertisements.
We discuss how opinion language can be disguised as fact and why that matters.
We write three sentences about the same topic — one fact, one opinion and one feeling.
We identify opinion language in book reviews and movie reviews.
We practise using phrases like 'I believe', 'evidence shows' and 'I feel' to signal the type of language we are using.

VC2E4LA03.w: We are learning that different text types across all our subjects have characteristic structures and language features that suit their purpose.
y: Understanding text structures helps us comprehend and create texts more efficiently.
Different subjects require different text types, and knowing their features helps us succeed across the curriculum.
This knowledge makes us more strategic readers and writers.
eg: We identify the characteristic stages of a narrative — orientation, complication, resolution.
We compare how a historical recount is structured versus a scientific explanation.
We find the language features that make a persuasive text convincing — like emotive words and rhetorical questions.
We create a reference chart showing the key features of text types we use regularly.
We analyse a text and identify its type based on its structure and language.
We choose the right text structure when planning a piece of writing for a specific subject.

VC2E4LA04.w: We are learning how connecting words — including time words like 'then' and conditional words like 'if' — help us link and sequence ideas in texts.
y: Connectives are the links that hold our writing together, making it flow logically.
Using a range of connectives makes our writing more sophisticated and easier to follow.
Understanding connectives helps us follow the logic of texts we read.
eg: We identify temporal connectives in a recount — 'first', 'then', 'after', 'finally'.
We use conditional words like 'if', 'unless' and 'when' to show conditions in our writing.
We highlight connectives in a text and discuss what relationship each one creates.
We rewrite a paragraph with better connectives to improve its flow.
We sort connectives into groups — time, cause, contrast, condition.
We practise using connectives to join ideas within and between paragraphs.

VC2E4LA05.w: We are learning to recognise navigation features in digital texts — like headlines, menus, links and graphics — that make them easier to read and use.
y: Navigating digital texts effectively is essential in our technology-rich world.
Understanding these features helps us find information quickly and evaluate digital sources.
This skill helps us create our own digital texts that are user-friendly.
eg: We explore a website and identify all the navigation features — links, menus, search bars, headings.
We discuss how graphics and layout help us understand digital content.
We compare the navigation features of two websites and evaluate which is easier to use.
We use drop-down menus and links to find specific information on a website.
We discuss how digital navigation differs from navigating a printed text.
We design a simple web page layout that includes effective navigation features.

VC2E4LA06.w: We are learning that a complex sentence has one main part (independent clause) and at least one extra part (dependent clause) joined by words like 'because', 'when', 'although' or 'if'.
y: Complex sentences let us express more detailed ideas and show relationships between them.
Using complex sentences makes our writing more mature and nuanced.
Understanding how clauses connect helps us read and write more effectively.
eg: We identify the independent clause and dependent clause in a complex sentence.
We use 'because' to add a reason: 'We stayed inside because it was raining.'
We use 'although' to show contrast: 'Although it was cold, we still went outside.'
We combine simple sentences into complex sentences using subordinating conjunctions.
We find complex sentences in our reading and identify the joining word.
We practise writing complex sentences that begin with the dependent clause.

VC2E4LA07.w: We are learning how adverb groups and prepositional phrases give extra details about activities — like where, when, how and why something happens.
y: These word groups add important detail that brings our writing to life.
Understanding how they work helps us write more descriptive and informative sentences.
This grammar knowledge gives us tools for crafting precise and engaging writing.
eg: We identify prepositional phrases in sentences — 'in the garden', 'after lunch', 'with great care'.
We add adverb groups to sentences to show how something is done — 'She ran very quickly.'
We discuss how changing the prepositional phrase changes the meaning — 'The cat sat on the mat' vs 'under the table'.
We expand basic sentences by adding where, when and how details.
We find adverb groups and prepositional phrases in our reading and discuss what information they add.
We edit our writing to include more descriptive phrases that paint a clearer picture.

VC2E4LA08.w: We are learning about past, present and future tenses and how choosing the right tense affects the meaning of our sentences.
y: Using tense correctly tells the reader exactly when things happen.
Different text types require different tenses — recounts use past, reports often use present.
Tense control is essential for clear, professional writing.
eg: We identify whether a text uses past, present or future tense and explain why.
We rewrite a paragraph in a different tense and notice how the meaning changes.
We check our own writing for tense consistency — not accidentally switching between past and present.
We discuss why instructions use present tense ('Mix the flour') while recounts use past ('We mixed the flour').
We practise using future tense in planning texts — 'We will visit the museum next week.'
We correct a text that switches tense inconsistently.

VC2E4LA09.w: We are learning how sound effects, camera angles, placement and prominence of elements in images all affect meaning in multimodal texts.
y: Understanding how images and sound are composed helps us read visual and multimodal texts critically.
These skills are essential for analysing advertisements, films, websites and other media.
This knowledge helps us make thoughtful choices when we create our own multimodal texts.
eg: We analyse how a close-up shot of a face creates a different effect from a wide shot of a landscape.
We discuss how background music in a video changes the mood of a scene.
We look at advertisements and identify what the most prominent element is and why the designer made it stand out.
We discuss how the placement of objects in an image guides the viewer's eye.
We compare two versions of the same scene filmed from different angles.
We create a multimodal text and make deliberate choices about image composition and sound.

VC2E4LA10.w: We are learning to expand our vocabulary by exploring synonyms (words with similar meanings) and antonyms (words with opposite meanings), and by using words from our reading.
y: A rich vocabulary gives us more choices for expressing exactly what we mean.
Knowing synonyms helps us avoid repeating the same words in our writing.
Understanding antonyms helps us grasp the full range of meanings in language.
eg: We brainstorm synonyms for common words like 'happy' — 'joyful', 'cheerful', 'delighted', 'thrilled'.
We find antonym pairs in our reading — 'ancient/modern', 'brave/cowardly', 'expand/shrink'.
We replace overused words in our writing with more precise synonyms.
We play a synonym and antonym matching game to build our word knowledge.
We use a thesaurus to find alternatives for common words in our writing.
We keep a vocabulary journal where we collect interesting new words from our reading.

VC2E4LA11.w: We are learning that quotation marks show when someone is speaking in a text, and how to use capital letters, commas and other punctuation correctly in dialogue.
y: Writing dialogue correctly makes our stories feel more real and professional.
Understanding dialogue conventions helps us read conversations in texts more easily.
Dialogue is one of the most powerful tools a storyteller has.
eg: We use quotation marks to show the exact words a character says — 'Let's go!' said Tom.
We practise using a comma before the speech tag — 'I agree,' said Sarah.
We start a new line for each new speaker in dialogue.
We add quotation marks to a passage of dialogue that is missing them.
We write a short conversation between two characters using correct dialogue punctuation.
We find dialogue in a published story and check how the author punctuates it.

VC2E4LA12.w: We are learning the difference between direct speech (the exact words someone said) and reported speech (someone telling us what was said).
y: Understanding direct and reported speech helps us write about events and conversations accurately.
This skill helps us in both creative writing and factual reporting.
Knowing the difference helps us be precise when retelling what people said.
eg: We compare 'She said, "I love this book"' (direct) with 'She said she loved that book' (reported).
We convert direct speech into reported speech and notice how the tense changes.
We find examples of both direct and reported speech in newspaper articles.
We discuss when a writer might choose direct speech over reported speech and why.
We practise writing the same conversation using both forms.
We identify the differences in punctuation between direct and reported speech.

VC2E4LE01.w: We are learning to recognise similar storylines, ideas and relationships across stories by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors set in different contexts.
y: Recognising shared themes across stories deepens our understanding of universal and culturally specific ideas.
Comparing First Nations stories from different contexts broadens our appreciation of the diversity within Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander cultures.
This helps us understand how stories connect to place, culture and community.
eg: We compare storylines in two Aboriginal stories from different regions and find shared themes.
We discuss how relationship to Country appears across multiple First Nations texts.
We identify similar ideas — like caring for family or respecting Elders — in stories from different cultural contexts.
We create a comparison chart showing common themes across several texts by First Nations authors.
We discuss why some storylines appear again and again in different cultures.
We explore how the same theme is expressed differently depending on the cultural context of the story.

VC2E4LE02.w: We are learning to describe how the structure and language features of stories create particular effects, and to share our opinions about what we read.
y: Analysing how texts work helps us move from simply enjoying stories to understanding what makes them effective.
Sharing informed opinions builds our critical literacy.
This analytical skill helps us improve our own writing by learning from others.
eg: We describe how short, punchy sentences create tension in a suspenseful story.
We explain how an author's use of descriptive language makes a setting feel vivid and real.
We share our opinion on a book and support it with references to specific text features.
We discuss how a text's structure — like a flashback or a surprise ending — affects the reader.
We compare two stories and explain which one uses language more effectively.
We write a review that evaluates both the strengths and weaknesses of a text.

VC2E4LE03.w: We are learning how authors and illustrators make stories exciting, moving and absorbing — through tension, character development and setting.
y: Understanding how published authors create engaging stories helps us apply the same techniques.
Analysing craft builds our appreciation of literature and our skills as critical readers.
These insights directly improve our own creative writing.
eg: We identify how an author builds tension through pacing — short sentences during action, longer ones during reflection.
We trace how a character develops throughout a story and what causes them to change.
We discuss how an illustrator's use of colour and perspective creates mood and emotion.
We find the turning point in a story and analyse how the author builds towards it.
We compare how two authors create suspense using different techniques.
We experiment with using plot tension, detailed characters and vivid settings in our own stories.

VC2E4LE04.w: We are learning about literary devices and deliberate wordplay in poems and stories — including how grammar is used creatively — and how these shape meaning.
y: Literary devices are the secret tools that make writing powerful and memorable.
Recognising these devices helps us understand why some texts move us more than others.
Experimenting with these techniques in our own writing builds our creative skills.
eg: We identify similes and metaphors in a poem and discuss what images they create.
We find examples of personification — where non-human things are given human qualities.
We explore how authors break grammar rules deliberately for effect — like sentence fragments for impact.
We analyse wordplay in a poem, such as puns or double meanings.
We discuss how a poet uses line breaks and spacing to create rhythm and emphasis.
We write our own poem using at least two literary devices we studied.

VC2E4LE05.w: We are learning to create our own texts by developing storylines, characters and settings, using ideas and language features we have encountered in our reading.
y: Creating original texts is where all our reading and analysis skills come together.
Developing characters and settings with depth makes our stories more engaging for readers.
Drawing on our reading experiences gives us a rich toolkit of techniques to use.
eg: We develop a character with a personality, backstory and motivation for their actions.
We create a setting using descriptive language that appeals to the senses.
We build a plot with a clear problem, rising tension and a satisfying resolution.
We borrow a narrative structure from a book we enjoyed and use it in our own story.
We write a story that uses dialogue to reveal character personality.
We revise our creative writing to strengthen the language features and story elements.

VC2E4LY01.w: We are learning to use conversation skills for gathering information, contributing to discussions, acknowledging other opinions, and linking our responses to what others have said.
y: Effective discussion skills help us learn collaboratively and build on each other's thinking.
Acknowledging different opinions is a sign of mature, respectful communication.
Linking our responses to others shows we are actively listening and thinking.
eg: We ask focused questions to gather information for a class project.
We acknowledge a classmate's opinion before sharing our own — 'That's an interesting point. I also think…'.
We link our response to what the previous speaker said — 'Building on what Maya said…'.
We practise summarising someone else's viewpoint before agreeing or disagreeing.
We contribute evidence-based ideas during a group discussion about a text.
We reflect after a discussion on how listening to others changed our thinking.

VC2E4LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken presentations to an audience, using our voice effectively to communicate clearly.
y: Being able to present information clearly and confidently is a valuable life skill.
A well-structured spoken text keeps the audience engaged and informed.
Practising formal presentations now prepares us for many future situations.
eg: We plan a structured talk with an introduction, key points and a conclusion.
We use visual aids like slides or posters to support our spoken presentation.
We vary our pace, volume and emphasis to keep the audience interested.
We make eye contact and use natural gestures while presenting.
We practise our presentation with a partner and improve based on their feedback.
We deliver a presentation about a topic we researched, using topic-specific vocabulary.

VC2E4LY03.w: We are learning how to use our knowledge of sounds and word parts to read and write multi-syllable words with complex letter combinations.
y: Tackling multi-syllable words confidently opens up more challenging and interesting texts.
Combining phonological and morphological knowledge gives us multiple strategies for decoding.
These skills are essential for the increasingly complex reading we do across all subjects.
eg: We break complex words into syllables and morphemes to decode them — like 'un-for-get-table'.
We use our knowledge of common letter combinations to read words with unusual spelling patterns.
We apply phonological knowledge to spell long words by working through them syllable by syllable.
We practise reading multi-syllable words fluently so they do not slow us down.
We identify the base word in complex words and use it to figure out meaning.
We keep a personal list of challenging multi-syllable words we have learned to decode.

VC2E4LY04.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of letter patterns, double letters, morphological word families, word origins and spelling generalisations to spell accurately.
y: Multiple spelling strategies give us the tools to spell a wide range of words correctly.
Understanding word origins and patterns means we rely less on rote memorisation.
Good spelling helps us communicate professionally and clearly.
eg: We learn rules for when to double letters — like 'running' (double 'n') versus 'eating' (no double).
We use word origins to understand and remember tricky spellings — like 'telephone' from Greek 'tele' (far) + 'phone' (sound).
We apply spelling generalisations like 'i before e except after c' and learn the common exceptions.
We sort words into morphological families and notice common spelling patterns.
We use knowledge of prefixes and suffixes to spell complex words correctly.
We develop personal spelling strategies for the words we find most challenging.

VC2E4LY05.w: We are learning to read and write high-frequency words — including homophones — and use context to figure out which spelling is correct.
y: Homophones are one of the trickiest parts of English spelling, and mastering them improves our writing accuracy.
Using context to determine correct spelling is a sophisticated reading and writing strategy.
Getting common words right gives our writing polish and credibility.
eg: We distinguish between 'weather' and 'whether' by thinking about the context.
We use context clues to decide between 'bare' and 'bear' in a sentence.
We create sentences that show the correct use of commonly confused homophones.
We proofread our writing specifically looking for homophone errors.
We play a context game where we fill in the correct homophone in sentences.
We make homophone reference cards for the pairs we find most challenging.

VC2E4LY06.w: We are learning to read different types of texts fluently and accurately, combining our phonics, vocabulary and grammar knowledge, and monitoring our understanding as we go.
y: Fluent reading with monitoring is what independent, skilled readers do.
Integrating multiple strategies makes us efficient, flexible readers across all subjects.
Self-monitoring means we catch our own mistakes and fix them in real time.
eg: We read a challenging text, using phonics, context and grammar to decode unfamiliar words.
We stop and re-read when a passage does not make sense, trying a different approach.
We adjust our reading speed — faster for easy parts, slower for complex or important sections.
We read with expression and phrasing that shows we understand the text.
We monitor our understanding by periodically asking ourselves 'What has this been about so far?'.
We try multiple strategies when stuck on a word — sounding out, using context, looking for word parts.

VC2E4LY07.w: We are learning to compare how texts from different time periods — that have similar purposes and audiences — present ideas or events.
y: Comparing texts across time reveals how language, values and perspectives change over the years.
This builds our understanding of how texts reflect the culture and beliefs of their time.
Critical comparison is a sophisticated reading skill that deepens our literacy.
eg: We compare a children's story from 50 years ago with a modern story and discuss what has changed.
We look at how an information text about health from the past differs from a current one.
We discuss how language used in older texts might be considered inappropriate today.
We explore how advertisements for the same type of product have changed over time.
We compare a historical account of an event with a modern retelling.
We discuss what the differences between old and new texts tell us about changing values and knowledge.

VC2E4LY08.w: We are learning to identify the typical features that different text types use to meet their purpose and reach their audience.
y: Recognising text features helps us understand how texts work and how to create effective ones.
This analytical skill helps us evaluate the quality and effectiveness of any text.
Understanding text features makes us both better readers and better writers.
eg: We identify the features of a persuasive text — opinion statement, arguments, evidence, call to action.
We analyse how an information text uses headings, diagrams and topic sentences to organise information.
We discuss which features make a narrative engaging — like dialogue, description and plot tension.
We compare a text that uses its features effectively with one that does not.
We use our knowledge of text features to plan our own writing strategically.
We create a class reference chart of text types and their characteristic features.

VC2E4LY09.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies to expand our knowledge, build understanding and discuss our ideas about texts with others.
y: Comprehension strategies are the tools that help us go beyond surface-level reading.
Discussing our understanding with others exposes us to different perspectives and deepens our thinking.
These strategies help us learn independently from any text we encounter.
eg: We visualise complex scenes and describe our mental images to a partner.
We predict how a text will develop and use evidence from the text to support our prediction.
We connect ideas from a text to what we already know about the topic.
We summarise key information in our own words and discuss it with our group.
We ask inferential questions — ones that require reading between the lines.
We monitor our comprehension and use fix-up strategies when we lose understanding.

VC2E4LY10.w: We are learning to create stories, information texts and persuasive texts using connected ideas for different audiences, including multimodal elements where appropriate.
y: Writing for different audiences and purposes shows we are versatile, skilled communicators.
Using connected ideas and multimodal elements makes our texts more engaging and effective.
Creating a range of text types prepares us for the diverse writing demands of school and life.
eg: We write a narrative with well-linked ideas, developed characters and a satisfying plot.
We create an information text for a specific audience, using headings, images and topic-specific vocabulary.
We write a persuasive text with a clear position, supporting arguments and a call to action.
We include multimodal elements — like images, diagrams or audio — to enhance our text.
We adapt our language and content to suit our intended audience.
We peer-review each other's texts and give feedback on how well ideas are connected.

VC2E4LY11.w: We are learning to re-read and edit our texts by adding, deleting or moving words and word groups to improve the content and structure.
y: Revision is where we transform a rough draft into a polished piece of writing.
Learning to add, delete and rearrange text gives us precise control over our writing.
Effective editing skills improve not just individual texts but our overall writing ability.
eg: We re-read our writing and add descriptive words to make our ideas clearer.
We delete unnecessary words or sentences that distract from our main point.
We move a paragraph to a more logical position in our text.
We read our writing aloud to identify sections that need improvement.
We use editing symbols to mark changes before rewriting.
We compare our first and final drafts and reflect on the improvements we made.

VC2E4LY12.w: We are learning to write with clearly formed joined letters that are becoming faster and more automatic.
y: Fluent, automatic handwriting means we can focus our attention on our ideas rather than on forming letters.
Speed with legibility is the goal — writing that is both quick and easy to read.
This automaticity is built through consistent, regular practice.
eg: We practise joined writing daily to build speed and fluency.
We focus on writing smoothly and consistently, keeping our letters evenly sized.
We write longer passages without stopping to think about how to form each letter.
We compare our handwriting from the beginning and end of term to see our improvement.
We use joined writing consistently across all our school work.
We develop a comfortable, personal style of joined writing that is both legible and efficient.

VC2E5LA01.w: We are learning how language choices signal our social role and the relationship we have with the people we are talking to.
y: Understanding how language signals roles and relationships makes us more effective communicators in any social situation.
This awareness helps us adapt our language to be appropriate and respectful in different contexts.
Recognising these patterns in others' language helps us understand social dynamics more clearly.
eg: We compare the language a news presenter uses with the language a friend uses when telling us the same story.
We identify how a teacher's language differs when giving instructions versus chatting at lunchtime.
We role-play job interviews and casual conversations and discuss how our language choices change.
We analyse how language in emails varies depending on who the recipient is.
We discuss how cultural background can influence the level of formality people use.
We examine how language choices in a text reveal the relationship between the writer and their audience.

VC2E5LA02.w: We are learning how to go beyond simply stating what we think by considering different viewpoints and using evidence to support our ideas.
y: Backing up our ideas with evidence and acknowledging other viewpoints makes our arguments much stronger.
This is the foundation of critical thinking and academic writing.
Learning to consider different perspectives helps us engage more thoughtfully with complex issues.
eg: We rewrite a bare assertion like 'Dogs are the best pets' by adding evidence and acknowledging another viewpoint.
We find examples in texts where authors use authoritative sources to support their claims.
We practise using phrases like 'According to…', 'Research shows…' and 'Some people argue…'.
We identify when a text only presents one side and discuss what is missing.
We write a balanced paragraph that presents two different views on a topic.
We evaluate the strength of different arguments by examining their evidence.

VC2E5LA03.w: We are learning to describe how different text types use specific language features and are organised into characteristic stages and phases to achieve their purpose.
y: Understanding the internal structure of text types helps us read and write them more skillfully.
Recognising stages and phases gives us a framework for creating well-organised texts.
This knowledge helps us across all our subjects where different text types are required.
eg: We map the stages of a persuasive text — thesis, arguments, reinforcement of position.
We identify the phases within a narrative — orientation, complication, series of events, resolution.
We compare the stages of an explanation text with those of a procedure text.
We discuss how the language features in each stage serve the text's overall purpose.
We use our knowledge of text stages to plan our own writing before we begin.
We analyse a text that does not follow its expected structure and discuss why it is less effective.

VC2E5LA04.w: We are learning how the starting point of a sentence or paragraph — called the theme — helps guide the reader and create a logical flow in our writing.
y: How we begin a sentence or paragraph sets up what the reader expects to hear about next.
Controlling theme positions is a sophisticated writing technique that creates clear, cohesive texts.
This knowledge helps us structure our writing so ideas build on each other logically.
eg: We examine how the first word or phrase in each sentence of a paragraph connects to the overall topic.
We rearrange the opening words of sentences to improve the flow of a paragraph.
We notice how authors use the beginning of paragraphs to signal new ideas or topics.
We rewrite a paragraph to create better cohesion by changing the starting points of sentences.
We identify how transitional words at the start of sentences create connections between ideas.
We practise beginning sentences in different ways to vary our writing style.

VC2E5LA05.w: We are learning more about complex sentences — how the independent clause carries the main idea and the dependent clause adds extra information.
y: Mastering complex sentences allows us to express sophisticated, layered ideas in our writing.
Understanding clause structure helps us read and write more complex texts with confidence.
This grammatical knowledge is essential for academic writing across all subjects.
eg: We identify the independent and dependent clauses in complex sentences we find in our reading.
We practise writing complex sentences where the dependent clause comes at the beginning, middle or end.
We use subordinating conjunctions like 'although', 'because', 'while', 'unless' and 'after' to join clauses.
We experiment with moving the dependent clause to different positions and discuss the effect.
We combine simple sentences into complex sentences to add detail and nuance.
We edit our writing to include more complex sentences where they improve clarity.

VC2E5LA06.w: We are learning how noun groups can be expanded with extra words to give a fuller, more detailed description of people, places, things or ideas.
y: Expanded noun groups add richness and precision to our writing.
This technique is used by skilled writers to create vivid descriptions without needing extra sentences.
Understanding noun group expansion helps us read and appreciate descriptive writing.
eg: We expand 'the house' to 'the crumbling old stone house on the edge of the cliff'.
We identify expanded noun groups in published texts and discuss what information each part adds.
We practise adding adjectives, classifiers and qualifiers to basic noun groups.
We compare writing with simple noun groups to writing with expanded ones and discuss the difference.
We edit our own writing to expand key noun groups where more detail is needed.
We analyse how an author uses expanded noun groups to create a vivid setting or character description.

VC2E5LA07.w: We are learning how the order and arrangement of images — both still and moving — and the use of sound affect the meaning of a text.
y: Understanding how images and sound are sequenced helps us interpret multimodal texts critically.
These skills are essential for analysing films, documentaries, advertisements and digital media.
This knowledge helps us make informed choices when we create our own multimodal texts.
eg: We analyse how the sequence of images in a picture book creates tension, humour or surprise.
We discuss how editing techniques in a video — like cutting between scenes — affect meaning.
We examine how sound effects and music in a film scene create atmosphere and emotion.
We create a storyboard that uses the sequence of images deliberately to communicate our message.
We compare how the same event is presented in still images versus moving images.
We analyse an advertisement and explain how the arrangement of images and sound persuades the viewer.

VC2E5LA08.w: We are learning how to use vocabulary more precisely, including specialist and technical words that give our communication greater accuracy and depth.
y: Precise vocabulary allows us to express exactly what we mean, without ambiguity.
Specialist vocabulary shows we understand a topic deeply enough to use its language correctly.
A more precise vocabulary makes both our speaking and writing more powerful and credible.
eg: We replace vague words with more precise ones — 'walked' becomes 'strode', 'shuffled' or 'tiptoed'.
We use specialist vocabulary in our subject-area writing — like 'habitat', 'equation' or 'perspective'.
We discuss how choosing the right word can change the meaning of a sentence entirely.
We keep a vocabulary notebook organised by subject and topic.
We analyse how an author uses precise language to create strong images or convey expertise.
We practise using technical terms correctly in both speaking and writing.

VC2E5LA09.w: We are learning to use commas to mark prepositional phrases in sentences, and how apostrophes work when more than one person owns something.
y: Correct punctuation makes our writing clearer and more professional.
Understanding commas with phrases and apostrophes with plural possession addresses two common writing challenges.
These skills demonstrate growing mastery of English writing conventions.
eg: We use commas to set off prepositional phrases at the start of sentences — 'After the storm, we went outside.'
We learn the difference between 'the student's book' (one student) and 'the students' books' (many students).
We practise placing apostrophes correctly for both singular and plural possession.
We identify prepositional phrases in our reading and notice how commas are used with them.
We edit a text that has missing commas and incorrect apostrophes.
We create examples that demonstrate the difference between singular and plural possession.

VC2E5LE01.w: We are learning to identify details in stories that reflect historical, cultural and social contexts — including in texts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors.
y: Literary texts are windows into other times, places and cultures — learning to read them this way deepens our understanding.
Recognising how context shapes a text helps us appreciate the perspectives and experiences it represents.
This skill helps us understand how literature reflects and sometimes challenges the world around us.
eg: We identify details in a story by an Aboriginal author that reflect cultural practices and connections to Country.
We discuss how a historical novel reflects the social attitudes of the time it is set in.
We compare how different cultures are represented in a range of literary texts.
We research the historical context of a story and discuss how it influences the plot and characters.
We identify details that tell us about the social world of the characters — their customs, values and daily life.
We discuss how understanding context changes our interpretation of a text.

VC2E5LE02.w: We are learning to form and share opinions about literary texts using the correct technical language to describe literary devices, text structures and language features.
y: Using metalanguage — the language we use to talk about language — shows sophisticated literary understanding.
Forming opinions supported by specific textual evidence builds our critical analysis skills.
This prepares us for more advanced literary study where precise discussion of texts is expected.
eg: We share our opinion on a text using terms like 'metaphor', 'foreshadowing', 'narrative arc' and 'point of view'.
We write a response to a text that references specific literary devices and their effects.
We discuss how an author's use of imagery shapes our response to the text.
We compare two texts using metalanguage to describe the differences in their structures.
We identify the language features that make a particular text effective and explain our reasoning.
We present a critical response to a poem, using appropriate literary terms.

VC2E5LE03.w: We are learning that the point of view from which a story is told influences how we, as readers, interpret and respond to the plot, characters and settings.
y: Point of view shapes everything about how a story is told and understood.
Recognising narrative perspective helps us think critically about whose voice is heard and whose is not.
This understanding helps us make more thoughtful interpretations and write more intentionally.
eg: We compare a story told in first person with one told in third person and discuss how our experience as readers differs.
We discuss how a villain's actions might seem justified if the story were told from their perspective.
We rewrite a scene from a different character's point of view and see how the story changes.
We identify whose perspective is represented in a text and whose is missing.
We analyse how an author uses point of view to create sympathy, suspense or surprise.
We discuss how an unreliable narrator changes our interpretation of events.

VC2E5LE04.w: We are learning to compare the effects of different literary devices — like simile, metaphor and personification — and sound devices in poems and stories.
y: Comparing literary devices deepens our appreciation of how language creates meaning and emotion.
Understanding these devices helps us interpret complex texts and recognise the author's craft.
Learning to use these devices in our own writing makes our creative work more powerful.
eg: We compare a simile ('The moon was like a silver coin') with a metaphor ('The moon was a silver coin') and discuss the different effects.
We find examples of personification in poetry and explain how giving human qualities to non-human things creates meaning.
We identify sound devices like alliteration, assonance and onomatopoeia in poems and discuss their effect.
We analyse how an author uses imagery to create a particular mood or atmosphere.
We write two versions of the same description — one with literal language and one with figurative — and compare them.
We create a poem that deliberately uses at least three different literary devices.

VC2E5LE05.w: We are learning to create our own texts by experimenting with vocabulary, figurative language, storylines, characters and settings inspired by what we have read.
y: Experimentation is how we develop our unique voice and style as writers.
Drawing on our reading gives us a rich bank of techniques to try in our own creative work.
Taking creative risks — trying new techniques and approaches — is how all writers grow.
eg: We experiment with figurative language in our writing — trying similes, metaphors and personification.
We create a character inspired by one we read about but placed in a completely different setting.
We try writing in a genre or style we have not attempted before.
We use vocabulary we encountered in our reading to enrich our own writing.
We adapt a storyline from a text we studied, changing key elements to make it our own.
We write multiple drafts, experimenting with different approaches before choosing our preferred version.

VC2E5LY01.w: We are learning to use skills like paraphrasing and questioning to clarify meaning, make connections and extend discussions about texts and topics.
y: Paraphrasing shows we truly understand what someone has said and helps prevent misunderstanding.
Asking good questions drives deeper thinking and richer discussions.
These skills make us effective participants in any collaborative learning situation.
eg: We paraphrase a classmate's idea to check our understanding — 'So you're saying that…'.
We ask clarifying questions when we do not fully understand someone's point.
We make connections between what is being discussed and our own experiences or other texts.
We extend a discussion by asking 'Can you give an example of that?' or 'What evidence supports that?'.
We summarise the main points of a discussion before adding our own contribution.
We practise active listening by making notes during a discussion and referring to them in our response.

VC2E5LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken and multimodal presentations for a specific purpose, using our voice effectively.
y: Being able to present information clearly and purposefully is a skill valued in every field.
Multimodal presentations combine spoken words with visuals and sound for greater impact.
Practising formal presentations builds confidence that serves us throughout our lives.
eg: We plan and deliver a structured presentation with a clear introduction, body and conclusion.
We create slides or visual aids that support and enhance our spoken message.
We vary our pace, volume and expression to match the purpose of our presentation.
We tailor our language and content to our specific audience.
We practise our presentation multiple times, refining based on feedback.
We include multimodal elements like images, sound clips or demonstrations to engage our audience.

VC2E5LY03.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds, word parts and vocabulary to read and spell words that share common letter patterns but have different pronunciations or meanings.
y: English has many words that look similar but sound different — understanding this prevents reading errors.
Combining phonological, morphological and vocabulary knowledge gives us multiple strategies for tricky words.
This flexibility is what distinguishes a skilled reader from a developing one.
eg: We compare words like 'cough', 'through' and 'though' — same letter pattern, different sounds.
We use context to determine the correct pronunciation of words that can be said two ways — like 'read' (past or present).
We identify word families where the base word helps us read and spell related words.
We practise reading homophones and homographs accurately using context.
We use morphological knowledge to decode unfamiliar words by identifying their parts.
We discuss how word origins explain why some English words have unexpected spellings.

VC2E5LY04.w: We are learning to build and spell new words using our knowledge of word families, prefixes, suffixes, word origins and spelling rules.
y: Understanding how words are constructed gives us powerful tools for both vocabulary and spelling.
Knowing word origins helps us make connections between related words and remember their spellings.
These strategies help us tackle unfamiliar words independently.
eg: We use knowledge of Latin and Greek roots to understand and spell words — 'port' means 'carry' in 'transport', 'export', 'portable'.
We apply suffix rules — like changing 'y' to 'i' before adding '-ly' (happy → happily).
We build word families from base words, exploring how prefixes and suffixes change meaning.
We use etymology to explain unusual spellings and make them easier to remember.
We create a word web showing the relationships between words that share a common root.
We use our morphological knowledge to attempt spelling unknown words and then verify.

VC2E5LY05.w: We are learning about less common plural forms and how adding a suffix can change both the meaning and the grammatical role of a word.
y: English has many irregular plurals — knowing them prevents common errors.
Understanding how suffixes change words grammatically helps us use language more precisely.
This morphological knowledge deepens our understanding of how English works.
eg: We learn irregular plurals like 'child → children', 'mouse → mice', 'cactus → cacti'.
We explore how adding '-tion' to a verb creates a noun — 'educate → education'.
We discuss how '-ful' changes a noun to an adjective — 'hope → hopeful'.
We sort words by the type of suffix and the grammatical change it creates.
We use our knowledge of suffix effects to work out the meaning of unfamiliar words.
We create a reference chart of common suffixes and how they change word meaning and grammatical form.

VC2E5LY06.w: We are learning to read increasingly complex texts fluently and accurately, using all our knowledge of sounds, meaning and grammar together.
y: Reading fluency at this level means we can access more challenging and rewarding texts.
Integrating multiple reading strategies makes us independent, efficient readers.
Self-monitoring and self-correcting are hallmarks of a skilled reader.
eg: We read complex texts at an appropriate pace, adjusting speed for difficulty.
We use context, word parts and phonics together to decode unfamiliar words.
We self-correct quickly when we notice a mistake, re-reading for accuracy.
We read with expression that shows we understand the meaning and mood of the text.
We tackle texts from different subjects confidently, adapting our approach as needed.
We identify and resolve comprehension breakdowns by re-reading, questioning or seeking clarification.

VC2E5LY07.w: We are learning to describe how a text reflects the time and place in which it was created.
y: Understanding context helps us interpret texts more accurately and appreciate why they were written the way they were.
Texts are products of their time — recognising this prevents us from judging them only by modern standards.
This analytical skill deepens our understanding of history, culture and social change.
