eg: We identify language in an older text that reflects the social norms of its time.
We discuss how a text's setting and themes reflect the author's historical context.
We compare two texts from different eras and discuss what changed and what remained the same.
We research the historical context of a text and explain how it influenced the content.
We discuss how a modern reader might interpret a text differently from its original audience.
We analyse how cultural context shapes the values and perspectives presented in a text.

VC2E5LY08.w: We are learning to explain the characteristic features that different text types use to meet their purpose and engage their audience.
y: Being able to explain text features shows a deep understanding of how texts work.
This knowledge helps us evaluate the effectiveness of texts and create better ones ourselves.
Understanding purpose and audience is central to all effective communication.
eg: We explain how a persuasive text uses emotive language, rhetorical questions and evidence to convince the reader.
We describe how an information report uses topic sentences, technical vocabulary and diagrams to inform.
We analyse how a narrative uses dialogue, descriptive language and plot structure to engage readers.
We compare two texts with different purposes and explain how their features reflect those purposes.
We evaluate whether a text's features effectively serve its purpose and audience.
We use our understanding of text features to plan our own writing strategically.

VC2E5LY09.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies to expand our knowledge of topics, build our understanding of texts, and discuss our ideas with others.
y: Strategic reading helps us learn new information efficiently from any text.
Combining multiple strategies gives us the best possible understanding of complex texts.
Discussing our comprehension with others refines our thinking and reveals new perspectives.
eg: We use visualising to create mental images of complex descriptions or processes.
We make predictions based on text features and prior knowledge, then verify them.
We connect new information in a text to what we already know about the topic.
We summarise key ideas in our own words, distinguishing main ideas from supporting details.
We monitor our understanding and use fix-up strategies when comprehension breaks down.
We discuss our interpretations with others and consider alternative viewpoints.

VC2E5LY10.w: We are learning to create different types of texts with well-developed, elaborated and logically sequenced ideas, using appropriate text structures and language features.
y: Writing with elaborated and sequenced ideas shows we are becoming skilled, independent writers.
Using appropriate text structures and features demonstrates our understanding of how texts work.
Creating a variety of text types prepares us for the diverse writing demands we will encounter.
eg: We write a narrative with well-developed characters, a detailed setting and a compelling plot.
We create an information report with logically sequenced paragraphs and supporting evidence.
We write a persuasive text with elaborated arguments and a strong conclusion.
We include appropriate language features — like technical vocabulary in an explanation or figurative language in a story.
We sequence our ideas logically, using connectives and topic sentences to guide the reader.
We create both written and spoken texts, adapting our approach for each mode.

VC2E5LY11.w: We are learning to re-read and edit our own texts and others' texts using agreed criteria for structure, language features and conventions.
y: Editing against criteria makes revision focused and productive rather than random.
Being able to edit both our own and others' work builds our critical literacy skills.
Consistent editing practice transforms us from good writers into excellent writers.
eg: We use an editing checklist that covers structure, language features, grammar and punctuation.
We re-read our text specifically looking for one thing at a time — first meaning, then structure, then conventions.
We give specific, constructive feedback to a partner's writing based on agreed criteria.
We reflect on the editing choices we make and explain why each change improves the text.
We compare edited and unedited versions of a text and discuss the improvements.
We develop our own criteria for editing based on the text type we are working with.

VC2E5LY12.w: We are learning to write legibly, fluently and automatically — so that handwriting does not slow down our thinking.
y: Automatic handwriting frees our brain to focus on the content and quality of our writing.
Fluent, legible handwriting is a practical skill that serves us in tests, note-taking and daily work.
At this level, handwriting should support rather than hinder our communication.
eg: We write for sustained periods without needing to stop and think about letter formation.
We maintain consistent, legible handwriting even when writing quickly.
We adapt our handwriting for different purposes — neat for final copies, quick for notes.
We practise writing at speed while maintaining readability.
We reflect on our handwriting habits and identify areas for improvement.
We use joined handwriting fluently across all our school work.

VC2E6LA01.w: We are learning how language changes as situations become more formal and as the social distance between people increases.
y: Understanding formality helps us communicate appropriately in every context, from casual to highly formal.
Being able to control our register is a sophisticated social and communication skill.
This awareness is essential for professional, academic and civic communication.
eg: We analyse how the language in a legal document differs from a casual text message.
We compare the levels of formality in different types of letters — a letter to a friend versus a letter to a Member of Parliament.
We rewrite a casual message in formal language and discuss what changes we had to make.
We discuss how social distance affects language — we speak differently to close friends than to strangers.
We examine how formality varies across cultures and contexts.
We practise adjusting our register for different audiences in both spoken and written tasks.

VC2E6LA02.w: We are learning to recognise objective and subjective language, and to identify when a text contains bias.
y: Distinguishing fact from opinion and detecting bias is one of the most important critical literacy skills.
This helps us evaluate the reliability and fairness of the information we encounter.
Understanding bias makes us more thoughtful consumers and creators of information.
eg: We compare an objective news report with an opinion editorial on the same topic.
We identify subjective language in a text — words that reveal the author's personal feelings or judgments.
We discuss how bias can be subtle — present in word choice, image selection and which information is included or excluded.
We analyse an advertisement and identify the techniques used to create a biased impression.
We rewrite a biased paragraph to be more objective, and discuss the changes we made.
We evaluate texts from multiple sources on the same issue and identify where bias appears.

VC2E6LA03.w: We are learning to explain how different text types are typically organised into stages and phases depending on their purpose, audience and context.
y: Deep understanding of text organisation helps us both comprehend and create texts more effectively.
Knowing how context and audience influence text structure makes us flexible writers.
This analytical skill transfers across all subjects where we work with different text types.
eg: We explain how a discussion text is organised differently from a persuasive text, even though both deal with opinions.
We analyse how the purpose of a text determines which stages it includes.
We discuss how the same text type might be adapted for different audiences.
We identify the stages and phases of a complex text and explain each one's role.
We create a text for a specific purpose and audience, deliberately choosing the right structure.
We compare real-world examples of the same text type and evaluate which is most effectively structured.

VC2E6LA04.w: We are learning that writers create sequence and cohesion by deliberately repeating text structures, language features and vocabulary throughout a text.
y: Intentional repetition creates rhythm, emphasis and coherence in writing.
Understanding how cohesion works helps us create texts that flow smoothly and hold together.
This technique is used by skilled writers in every genre — from poetry to academic essays.
eg: We identify how an author repeats a phrase or structure throughout a text for emphasis or unity.
We analyse how vocabulary chains — groups of related words — create cohesion in a paragraph.
We discuss how repeated text structures (like recurring verse patterns in poems) create rhythm and predictability.
We experiment with deliberate repetition in our own writing to create effect.
We compare a cohesive text with a disjointed one and identify what makes the difference.
We use referencing, synonyms and word chains to connect ideas across paragraphs in our own writing.

VC2E6LA05.w: We are learning how embedded clauses — clauses placed inside other clauses — allow us to write more detailed and varied complex sentences.
y: Embedded clauses let us pack more information into a single sentence without it becoming confusing.
This grammatical technique is common in academic and professional writing.
Understanding embedded clauses helps us read complex texts more easily and write more sophisticated ones.
eg: We identify embedded clauses in sentences — 'The book, which I borrowed from the library, was fascinating.'
We practise embedding clauses using 'who', 'which', 'that' and 'where'.
We compare sentences with and without embedded clauses and discuss how they differ in detail.
We add embedded clauses to simple sentences to elaborate, extend or explain ideas.
We find embedded clauses in our reading and discuss what information they add.
We experiment with different positions for embedded clauses in our writing.

VC2E6LA06.w: We are learning how choosing the right verb, using elaborated tenses and including different types of adverb groups can sharpen and expand our ideas.
y: Precise verb choices and expanded verb groups make our writing more vivid and accurate.
Understanding elaborated tenses — like 'had been running' — gives us tools for expressing complex time relationships.
These grammatical tools help us write with the precision and sophistication expected at this level.
eg: We compare 'walked' with 'strolled', 'marched' and 'wandered' and discuss the different images each creates.
We explore elaborated tenses — 'She was running' versus 'She had been running' — and discuss the difference in meaning.
We add adverb groups to sentences to sharpen the meaning — 'He spoke quietly and carefully.'
We analyse how an author's verb choices create a particular tone or mood.
We edit our writing to replace vague verbs with more precise ones.
We practise using a range of adverb groups to add detail about how, when, where and why.

VC2E6LA07.w: We are learning to identify the ways still images, moving images and sound combine in texts to create meaning and influence viewers.
y: In a media-saturated world, being able to critically analyse visual and audio elements is essential.
Understanding how these elements are used helps us evaluate the messages we receive.
This knowledge makes us more thoughtful creators of our own multimodal texts.
eg: We analyse how figures, tables and diagrams present information differently from written text.
We discuss how camera angles and editing in a documentary influence the viewer's interpretation.
We examine how maps and graphs can be used to support or mislead — depending on how they are designed.
We compare how the same information is presented in a graph versus in words and discuss which is more effective.
We analyse how music and sound design in a film create emotion and guide the audience's response.
We create a multimodal text that uses visuals and sound deliberately to support our message.

VC2E6LA08.w: We are learning how authors use vivid vocabulary and figurative language — like metaphors, similes, personification and idioms — to create meaning and effect.
y: Figurative language is what transforms ordinary writing into writing that resonates with readers.
Understanding how these techniques work helps us appreciate literature and use them in our own writing.
Knowing about idioms and expressions helps us navigate the language we encounter in everyday life.
eg: We analyse how an author uses a metaphor to make an abstract concept feel concrete and real.
We identify idioms in everyday language — like 'it's raining cats and dogs' — and discuss their meaning.
We examine how personification brings non-human things to life in a poem.
We compare the effect of a literal description with a figurative one.
We experiment with using vivid vocabulary and figurative language in our own writing.
We create a class collection of effective figurative language examples from our reading.

VC2E6LA09.w: We are learning how to use commas to separate a dependent clause from an independent clause in a complex sentence.
y: Correct comma placement in complex sentences makes our writing clearer and more professional.
This is one of the most practical punctuation skills for the increasingly complex writing we do.
Understanding this convention helps us read complex sentences more fluently.
eg: We use a comma when the dependent clause comes first — 'Although it was late, we kept working.'
We identify that no comma is usually needed when the independent clause comes first — 'We kept working although it was late.'
We find examples in published texts and check the comma placement.
We practise writing complex sentences and placing commas correctly.
We edit a text with missing or misplaced commas in complex sentences.
We create a rule sheet for comma use in complex sentences and use it as a reference.

VC2E6LE01.w: We are learning to explore our responses to characters and events in stories from historical, cultural or social contexts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors.
y: Engaging deeply with First Nations literature builds cultural understanding and empathy.
Exploring our personal responses to diverse texts helps us understand ourselves and the world better.
Literature from different contexts challenges our assumptions and broadens our perspectives.
eg: We read a story by an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander author and reflect on how the characters and events made us feel.
We discuss how the historical or cultural context of a story influences our response to it.
We compare our responses with classmates' and explore why people respond differently to the same text.
We identify moments in a text that surprised, moved or challenged us and explain why.
We research the cultural context of a First Nations text to deepen our understanding.
We write a personal response that connects the text to our own experiences and values.

VC2E6LE02.w: We are learning to compare how different authors use language choices, repetition, emphasis, metaphor and other techniques, and to analyse the themes and plots across literary texts.
y: Comparative analysis reveals how different authors achieve similar effects through different means.
Understanding these techniques helps us recognise what makes particular texts powerful.
This analytical skill deepens our appreciation of literature and strengthens our own writing.
eg: We compare how two authors use metaphor differently to explore the same theme.
We analyse how repetition in one text creates a different effect from repetition in another.
We discuss how authors use modality to convey certainty or doubt.
We compare the themes of two texts from different genres and discuss similarities and differences.
We identify how emphasis is created through word choice, sentence structure and positioning.
We present a comparative analysis of two texts to our class, using examples to support our points.

VC2E6LE03.w: We are learning to describe the characteristics that define an individual author's style — the distinctive way they use language.
y: Recognising authorial style helps us appreciate what makes each writer unique.
Understanding style deepens our literary analysis and enriches our reading experience.
Studying different styles gives us ideas for developing our own distinctive writing voice.
eg: We identify recurring features in an author's work — like their use of humour, sentence length or particular themes.
We compare two books by the same author and identify the consistent stylistic features.
We discuss how an author's style reflects their personality, values or cultural background.
We read extracts by different authors and try to guess who wrote each one based on style alone.
We describe an author's style using specific examples — 'She uses short, punchy sentences and dark humour.'
We experiment with imitating the style of an author we admire in a short piece of our own writing.

VC2E6LE04.w: We are learning to explain how authors use sound devices and imagery to create meaning and effect in both poetry and prose.
y: Sound and imagery are the tools that make language come alive on the page.
Understanding how these techniques work helps us interpret literature more deeply.
Applying these techniques in our own writing makes it more engaging and powerful.
eg: We analyse how alliteration and assonance create a particular sound quality in a poem.
We explain how an author uses visual imagery to help readers see a scene vividly.
We discuss how sound devices like rhythm, rhyme and repetition contribute to the meaning of a text.
We compare the use of imagery in prose and poetry and discuss similarities and differences.
We identify sensory imagery in a text — sight, sound, touch, taste and smell — and discuss its effect.
We write a piece of prose or poetry that deliberately uses sound and imagery for effect.

VC2E6LE05.w: We are learning to create our own texts by adapting plot structures, characters, settings and ideas from stories we have read, experimenting with our own style.
y: Adapting from published texts is how writers learn their craft and develop their own voice.
Experimentation with style helps us discover what kind of writers we want to be.
This creative work draws together all our reading and analytical skills.
eg: We adapt a familiar plot structure and set it in a completely new context.
We create characters inspired by literature but with unique traits and motivations.
We experiment with our writing style — trying different sentence lengths, tones and voices.
We write a story that references or responds to a text we studied.
We develop our own distinctive writing voice through deliberate experimentation.
We seek feedback on our creative work and revise it to strengthen our style choices.

VC2E6LY01.w: We are learning to use formal and informal interaction skills including paraphrasing, questioning and interrogating ideas, and to develop and sustain discussion with others.
y: Sophisticated discussion skills help us explore ideas deeply and learn from diverse perspectives.
Being able to adjust our formality for different contexts shows communicative maturity.
These skills are essential for academic debate, group work and collaborative problem-solving.
eg: We paraphrase complex ideas to check our understanding and advance the discussion.
We ask probing questions that push thinking deeper — 'What evidence supports that?' or 'Have you considered…?'.
We adjust our formality depending on the context — more formal in a debate, more casual in a brainstorm.
We sustain a discussion over time, building on previous points and maintaining focus.
We challenge ideas respectfully while acknowledging the value of different perspectives.
We reflect on our discussion skills and set goals for improvement.

VC2E6LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken and multimodal presentations for a specific audience and purpose, using appropriate features of voice and visual elements.
y: Tailoring presentations to specific audiences and purposes makes them more effective and engaging.
Combining spoken and visual elements creates presentations that are more memorable and impactful.
Strong presentation skills are valued in education, the workplace and community life.
eg: We plan a presentation with a clear structure adapted to our specific audience.
We use visual elements — slides, images, videos — that complement and extend our spoken content.
We adjust our language, tone and complexity for the intended audience.
We use voice features — pace, pause, volume and emphasis — to enhance our message.
We rehearse our presentation and refine it based on peer feedback.
We evaluate our own and others' presentations against criteria for purpose and audience effectiveness.

VC2E6LY03.w: We are learning to combine our knowledge of sounds, word parts and vocabulary to read and write increasingly complex words.
y: At this level, we need all our phonological and morphological knowledge working together to tackle complex vocabulary.
Strong word-level skills free up our attention for comprehension and higher-order thinking.
These skills support our reading and writing across all subjects.
eg: We decode multi-syllable words with complex letter combinations by breaking them into manageable parts.
We use morphological knowledge to read and spell words with multiple prefixes or suffixes.
We apply our understanding of word origins to make sense of unfamiliar vocabulary.
We read technical and academic vocabulary fluently in different subject areas.
We spell complex words by drawing on multiple strategies — phonological, morphological and visual.
We self-correct quickly when we mispronounce or misspell a complex word.

VC2E6LY04.w: We are learning to use our growing knowledge of letter patterns, spelling rules, word families and word origins to spell with accuracy and confidence.
y: Strong spelling skills give our writing polish and credibility.
Using multiple strategies — patterns, rules, morphology and etymology — gives us the best chance of spelling correctly.
Confident spelling means we can focus on expressing our ideas rather than worrying about getting words right.
eg: We use knowledge of word origins to spell words with unusual patterns — like Greek-origin words with 'ph'.
We apply spelling generalisations and know the common exceptions.
We use morphological word families to check and correct our spelling.
We identify the base word in a complex word and use it as a spelling anchor.
We use multiple strategies when unsure — trying the word visually, checking with morphology, consulting a reference.
We maintain a personal spelling journal with words we find challenging and the strategies that help.

VC2E6LY05.w: We are learning to select and read different types of increasingly complex texts, integrating all our reading knowledge to read accurately and fluently with understanding.
y: Being able to select appropriate texts and read them fluently is the mark of an independent reader.
Integrating all our reading strategies allows us to comprehend complex texts across all subjects.
Fluent, strategic reading is the foundation for lifelong learning.
eg: We choose texts at an appropriate challenge level and read them fluently.
We integrate phonic, semantic and grammatical knowledge to decode and comprehend simultaneously.
We adjust our reading strategies for different text types — skimming for key ideas, close-reading for analysis.
We monitor our understanding and re-read or seek clarification when needed.
We read aloud with expression and phrasing that shows deep comprehension.
We reflect on our reading habits and push ourselves to tackle more challenging texts.

VC2E6LY06.w: We are learning to examine texts — including media texts — that represent ideas and events, and to identify how they reflect the context in which they were created.
y: All texts are shaped by when, where and why they were created — understanding this makes us critical readers.
Examining media texts critically is essential in an age of constant information and persuasion.
This skill helps us evaluate the reliability, perspective and purpose of any text we encounter.
eg: We analyse a news article and identify how it reflects the values or biases of its source.
We examine how an older text reflects the social attitudes of its time.
We compare how the same event is represented in different media and discuss the differences.
We identify the cultural context that shaped a particular text and explain how it influences the content.
We discuss how a text might be received differently by different audiences depending on their context.
We evaluate media texts for reliability, considering the creator's purpose and context.

VC2E6LY07.w: We are learning to analyse how text structures and language features work together to meet a text's purpose and engage and influence its audience.
y: Understanding the interplay between structure and language is key to evaluating text effectiveness.
This analytical skill helps us create texts that are purposeful and powerful.
Being able to deconstruct how texts work gives us control over our own communication.
eg: We analyse how a persuasive text uses both its structure (thesis, arguments, conclusion) and language features (emotive words, rhetorical questions) to persuade.
We evaluate how an information text's organisation and vocabulary work together to inform clearly.
We discuss how a narrative's structure and literary language combine to engage and move readers.
We compare two texts with the same purpose and evaluate which one uses structure and language more effectively.
We identify where a text's structure or language fails to serve its purpose and suggest improvements.
We create a text where we deliberately align structure and language features to achieve our purpose.

VC2E6LY08.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies to compare and analyse ideas across texts, and to deepen our understanding through critical thinking.
y: Comparing ideas across texts develops our ability to think critically and synthesise information.
These strategies help us build deep understanding of complex topics.
Analytical comprehension is the reading skill that drives all advanced learning.
eg: We compare how different texts present the same topic and analyse the differences in perspective.
We use questioning strategies to interrogate a text — 'Why did the author choose to include this? What is missing?'.
We synthesise information from multiple sources to build a comprehensive understanding of a topic.
We connect ideas across texts, identifying patterns, contradictions and complementary information.
We evaluate the strength of arguments and evidence presented in different texts.
We discuss our analytical findings with others, refining our thinking through dialogue.

VC2E6LY09.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — written and spoken — with well-developed, organised ideas for a specific purpose and audience, including multimodal elements.
y: Creating purposeful, audience-aware texts with developed ideas shows mature writing ability.
Including multimodal elements enhances our communication and reflects how texts work in the real world.
This versatility as writers prepares us for the demands of secondary school and beyond.
eg: We write a well-structured argument with developed reasoning and evidence for a specific audience.
We create an information text with organised ideas, technical vocabulary and supporting visuals.
We write a narrative with a complex plot, developed characters and a distinctive voice.
We deliver a spoken presentation that combines content, structure and visual elements effectively.
We adapt our writing style and content for different purposes and audiences.
We peer-review each other's texts against criteria for purpose, audience and quality.

VC2E6LY10.w: We are learning to re-read and edit our own texts and others' texts using agreed criteria, exploring the reasons behind our editing choices.
y: Reflective editing — understanding why we make changes — deepens our writing skills.
Using agreed criteria makes editing systematic and focused.
Being able to explain our editing choices shows a sophisticated understanding of effective writing.
eg: We edit a text using specific criteria and annotate our changes with brief explanations.
We discuss editing choices with a partner — why we moved a paragraph, changed a word, or restructured a sentence.
We compare different editing approaches and discuss which improvements had the greatest impact.
We develop criteria for editing different text types, recognising that each type has different expectations.
We reflect on our own editing habits and identify areas where we consistently need improvement.
We use rubrics and exemplars to guide our editing decisions.

VC2E6LY11.w: We are learning to write legibly, fluently and automatically for sustained periods of time.
y: Sustained, automatic handwriting allows us to write at length without fatigue or loss of quality.
This physical fluency supports all our writing tasks — from timed assessments to creative projects.
Automatic handwriting means our mental energy goes to thinking and creating, not to forming letters.
eg: We write for extended periods maintaining consistent legibility and speed.
We take handwritten notes efficiently during lessons and discussions.
We complete timed writing tasks with neat, readable handwriting.
We maintain our handwriting quality from the first paragraph to the last.
We adapt our handwriting for different contexts — cursive for extended writing, print for labels.
We reflect on our handwriting fluency and set personal improvement goals.

VC2E7LA01.w: We are learning how the language we use both expresses and creates our personal and social identities.
y: Language is not just a tool for communication — it actively shapes who we are and how others see us.
Understanding this connection helps us make more intentional choices about how we present ourselves.
This awareness helps us recognise how identity is constructed in texts and in everyday interactions.
eg: We analyse how slang, dialect and code-switching express belonging to particular social groups.
We discuss how our language choices change depending on who we are with and what identity we want to project.
We examine how characters in texts use language to establish their identity — accent, vocabulary, register.
We explore how language can reinforce or challenge stereotypes about different groups.
We reflect on our own language use and what it says about our identities.
We discuss how digital communication — emojis, abbreviations, memes — creates new forms of identity expression.

VC2E7LA02.w: We are learning about the language of evaluation — how people express judgments — and substantiation — how they back up their claims with evidence.
y: Understanding evaluative language helps us read critically and recognise when someone is trying to persuade us.
Knowing how to substantiate claims makes our own arguments more convincing and credible.
These skills are fundamental to academic writing, media literacy and informed citizenship.
eg: We identify evaluative language in a review or editorial — words that express judgment like 'brilliant', 'inadequate' or 'controversial'.
We analyse how a text uses evidence, examples and expert opinions to substantiate its claims.
We compare a well-substantiated argument with an unsubstantiated assertion and evaluate the difference.
We practise using evaluative language with appropriate levels of force in our own writing.
We examine how different types of evidence — statistics, anecdotes, expert testimony — support arguments.
We write a persuasive text that uses both evaluative language and solid substantiation.

VC2E7LA03.w: We are learning to identify and describe how texts are structured differently depending on their purpose, and how language features vary across different types of texts.
y: Deep understanding of text structures helps us comprehend complex texts and create our own effectively.
Recognising how language features serve purpose builds our analytical and writing skills.
This knowledge transfers to every subject where we encounter or create different text types.
eg: We analyse the structure of an analytical essay and compare it with a narrative structure.
We describe how language features in a scientific report differ from those in a persuasive speech.
We identify the stages of a discussion text and explain how each stage serves the text's purpose.
We examine how texts that combine purposes — like an informative text with a persuasive angle — are structured.
We compare the structures of texts from different media — print, digital, broadcast.
We use our understanding of text structures to plan and produce more effective texts.

VC2E7LA04.w: We are learning how texts use devices like overviews, examples, illustrations and other structural signals to create cohesion and guide the reader through the content.
y: Understanding cohesive devices helps us follow complex texts and create well-organised writing of our own.
These structural signals are what make the difference between confusing and clear communication.
Mastering these devices is essential for academic and professional writing.
eg: We identify how overviews at the beginning of sections signal what is coming and help us navigate a text.
We analyse how examples and illustrations are used to support and clarify main ideas.
We examine how transition words and structural signals connect ideas across paragraphs.
We evaluate whether a text's cohesive devices effectively guide the reader.
We use structural devices like topic sentences, previews and summaries in our own writing.
We compare texts with strong cohesion to those that lack it and discuss the impact on readability.

VC2E7LA05.w: We are learning how complex and compound-complex sentences can be used to elaborate, extend and explain ideas in sophisticated ways.
y: Mastering complex sentence structures gives us the tools to express nuanced, layered thinking.
Varying sentence structure makes our writing more engaging and professional.
This grammatical skill is expected in academic writing across all subjects.
eg: We analyse compound-complex sentences in published texts and identify their constituent clauses.
We practise combining ideas from several simple sentences into one well-crafted complex sentence.
We use compound-complex sentences to add detail and nuance to our arguments.
We experiment with sentence length and complexity to create rhythm and emphasis in our writing.
We identify when a complex sentence structure improves clarity versus when simpler is better.
We edit our writing to vary sentence structures for greater effect.

VC2E7LA06.w: We are learning how using consistent tense through verbs and verb groups keeps our writing clear and easy to follow.
y: Tense consistency is essential for clear communication — inconsistency confuses readers.
Understanding verb groups helps us express precise time relationships in our writing.
This is a fundamental skill for all types of extended writing.
eg: We identify tense inconsistencies in a text and discuss how they create confusion.
We edit a passage to make the tense consistent throughout.
We discuss how some texts deliberately shift tense for effect — like flashbacks in narratives.
We analyse how verb groups like 'had been working' express complex time relationships.
We check our own extended writing for tense consistency before submitting it.
We compare the same events described in different tenses and discuss the effects.

VC2E7LA07.w: We are learning how still images, moving images and sound can be used to create a particular perspective or point of view.
y: Visual and audio media are powerful tools for shaping how audiences think and feel.
Understanding how perspective is constructed in media helps us be critical, informed viewers.
This skill is essential in a world where we are constantly exposed to visual media.
eg: We analyse how camera angles in a documentary create sympathy for or distance from the subject.
We discuss how the selection and arrangement of images in a news story shapes the viewer's perspective.
We examine how background music in a film scene influences the audience's emotional response.
We compare two media representations of the same event and discuss how they construct different perspectives.
We create a multimodal text that uses images and sound deliberately to construct a particular viewpoint.
We discuss how social media posts use images, filters and captions to construct and project perspectives.

VC2E7LA08.w: We are learning about the role vocabulary plays in building specialist knowledge, including understanding words that mean different things in everyday versus specialist contexts.
y: Specialist vocabulary is the gateway to deeper understanding in every field of knowledge.
Recognising that words shift meaning across contexts prevents misunderstanding.
Building our academic and technical vocabulary is essential for success across all subjects.
eg: We explore how 'culture' means something different in everyday conversation versus in science (growing cultures in a lab).
We build glossaries of specialist terms for different subjects and note where meanings overlap or differ.
We discuss how learning the vocabulary of a field is inseparable from learning the field itself.
We analyse how authors use specialist vocabulary to establish authority and precision.
We practise using technical language accurately in our own writing across subjects.
We identify when everyday words are being used in specialist ways and explain the difference.

VC2E7LA09.w: We are learning how to use colons and brackets to support and extend meaning in our writing.
y: Colons and brackets are sophisticated punctuation tools that help us organise and elaborate ideas.
Using these correctly adds clarity and polish to our academic and creative writing.
Understanding these conventions helps us read complex texts more fluently.
eg: We use colons to introduce lists, explanations or examples — 'There are three reasons: cost, time and quality.'
We use brackets to add supplementary information — 'The novel (first published in 1988) won several awards.'
We find colons and brackets in published texts and explain their function.
We practise incorporating colons and brackets naturally into our own writing.
We discuss when to use brackets versus commas for parenthetical information.
We edit a text to add colons and brackets where they would improve clarity.

VC2E7LE01.w: We are learning to identify and explore ideas, points of view, characters, events and issues in stories from different historical, cultural and social contexts — including texts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors.
y: Engaging with literature from diverse contexts builds our cultural understanding and empathy.
Exploring different perspectives through literature helps us see the world from multiple viewpoints.
First Nations literary texts offer unique perspectives that deepen our understanding of Australia's history and cultures.
eg: We explore how an Aboriginal author's text reflects connections to Country, culture and community.
We identify the social and political issues raised in a text from a specific historical period.
We compare how different cultural contexts shape the characters, events and themes of literary texts.
We discuss how our own cultural position influences our reading of texts from other contexts.
We analyse points of view in a text and consider whose perspectives are represented and whose are absent.
We research the historical or cultural context of a text to deepen our understanding.

VC2E7LE02.w: We are learning to develop opinions about characters, settings and events in stories, identifying where we agree or disagree with other readers' interpretations.
y: Developing informed opinions about literature builds our critical thinking and analytical skills.
Recognising that texts can be interpreted differently teaches us to value diverse perspectives.
Being able to articulate and defend our literary opinions is a foundational academic skill.
eg: We form an opinion about a character's motivations and support it with evidence from the text.
We discuss a text in a group and identify areas where our interpretations agree and differ.
We write a response that presents our opinion while acknowledging alternative readings.
We analyse how different readers' backgrounds and experiences lead to different interpretations.
We challenge a peer's interpretation respectfully, using textual evidence to support our own view.
We revise our initial interpretation after hearing others' perspectives.

VC2E7LE03.w: We are learning how literary devices, language features, dialogue and visual elements are used to create meaning and emotion in literary texts.
y: Understanding these techniques helps us appreciate the craft of storytelling and interpret texts more deeply.
Recognising how emotion is constructed in texts makes us more discerning and engaged readers.
These analytical skills prepare us for more advanced literary study.
eg: We analyse how dialogue reveals character personality, relationships and conflict.
We examine how an author uses literary devices like foreshadowing to build suspense.
We discuss how still and moving images in a graphic novel or film adaptation create meaning alongside text.
We identify the language features that create emotional responses — like short sentences for shock or flowing descriptions for calm.
We compare how different authors achieve similar emotional effects through different techniques.
We apply our understanding of these techniques in our own creative writing.

VC2E7LE04.w: We are learning to identify and explain how characters, settings and events work together to create meaning in literary texts.
y: Understanding the interplay between narrative elements deepens our comprehension and appreciation of literature.
This analytical skill helps us see how authors construct meaning through deliberate choices.
Recognising these connections enriches both our reading experience and our writing.
eg: We explain how a character's development is influenced by the setting they inhabit.
We analyse how events in a plot are shaped by the cultural or historical context of the setting.
We discuss how the interaction between characters drives the plot forward.
We map the relationships between characters, settings and events in a complex text.
We compare how two texts use the relationship between these elements differently.
We create a text where characters, settings and events are deliberately interconnected.

VC2E7LE05.w: We are learning to identify and explain how literary devices create layers of meaning and aesthetic qualities in texts — including in poetry.
y: Literary devices create the depth and beauty that distinguish literature from ordinary communication.
Understanding how layers of meaning work helps us interpret texts at multiple levels.
Appreciating aesthetic qualities builds our love of language and literature.
eg: We analyse how metaphor creates meaning on both a literal and symbolic level.
We explain how sound devices in poetry — like assonance, consonance and meter — create aesthetic effects.
We discuss how imagery appeals to the senses and creates vivid experiences for readers.
We identify layers of meaning in a poem — the surface narrative and the deeper themes.
We compare how two poets use the same device to create different effects.
We experiment with literary devices in our own poetry to create layers of meaning.

VC2E7LE06.w: We are learning to create our own texts that experiment with the literary language features and devices we have encountered in our reading.
y: Experimentation is how we develop our creative skills and find our own voice as writers.
Applying literary devices we have studied deepens our understanding of how they work.
Taking creative risks builds confidence and helps us grow as writers.
eg: We write a short story that uses a literary device we studied — like an extended metaphor or unreliable narrator.
We create a poem that experiments with sound, imagery and structure.
We write a narrative that uses dialogue, description and pacing techniques from texts we have analysed.
We experiment with different narrative perspectives and discuss their effects.
We craft a piece that deliberately creates layers of meaning through symbolism.
We share our creative work and discuss how our use of literary devices contributes to its effectiveness.

VC2E7LY01.w: We are learning to use interaction skills when discussing ideas and information, including when evaluating features of texts.
y: Effective discussion skills are essential for collaborative learning and intellectual growth.
Being able to evaluate texts through discussion helps us develop and refine our analytical thinking.
These interaction skills are transferable to academic, professional and civic contexts.
eg: We evaluate the text features of a media article during a class discussion, supporting our views with evidence.
We practise building on others' ideas, challenging respectfully and synthesising different viewpoints.
We use discussion skills to explore complex questions that do not have simple answers.
We reflect on how discussion has changed or deepened our understanding of a text.
We practise formal discussion techniques like debating and Socratic seminars.
We evaluate our own contribution to discussions and set goals for improvement.

VC2E7LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken texts for specific purposes and audiences, using formal language and effective vocal features.
y: Being able to speak formally and persuasively is a powerful skill in academic, professional and civic life.
Structuring our spoken texts helps our audience follow our argument or information clearly.
Practising formal presentations builds the confidence we need for high-stakes speaking situations.
eg: We plan and deliver a formal presentation with a clear thesis, supporting points and conclusion.
We use appropriate formal language, avoiding colloquialisms in academic presentations.
We employ vocal features — pace, pause, emphasis, volume — strategically to enhance our message.
We adapt our delivery for different audiences and contexts.
We use cue cards or notes effectively without reading directly from them.
We evaluate our own and peers' presentations against specific criteria.

VC2E7LY03.w: We are learning to use spelling rules, word parts, patterns and word origins to spell accurately and independently — including when using a dictionary or digital tools.
y: Independent, accurate spelling demonstrates control over written language.
Using multiple spelling strategies gives us the best tools for any word we encounter.
Being able to use reference tools efficiently supports lifelong learning.
eg: We apply spelling rules and generalisations to spell unfamiliar words.
We use knowledge of word origins — Latin, Greek, French — to inform our spelling of complex words.
We use dictionaries and digital tools to verify and correct our spelling.
We identify patterns in words from the same origin and use them to spell related words.
We spell subject-specific vocabulary accurately across all our school work.
We develop personal strategies for the words we consistently find challenging.

VC2E7LY04.w: We are learning to explain how current technology affects the way we read, create and respond to texts, including media texts.
y: Technology has fundamentally changed how texts are created, distributed and consumed.
Understanding these changes helps us navigate the digital textual landscape critically.
This awareness makes us more thoughtful creators and consumers of digital content.
eg: We discuss how social media has changed the way news is created, shared and consumed.
We examine how digital tools — like hyperlinks, comments and sharing — change the reading experience.
We analyse how technology enables new text forms — podcasts, vlogs, interactive stories.
We discuss the challenges technology creates — misinformation, shortened attention spans, echo chambers.
We compare reading a physical book with reading on a device and discuss the differences.
We evaluate how digital tools have changed our own reading and writing habits.

VC2E7LY05.w: We are learning to analyse how language features shape meaning and vary depending on the audience and purpose of a text.
y: Understanding how language creates meaning is the core skill of critical literacy.
Analysing language variation helps us read between the lines and evaluate texts effectively.
This skill helps us make more deliberate language choices in our own communication.
eg: We analyse how an author's word choices shape the reader's perception of a character or event.
We compare the language features of a text written for teenagers with one written for adults on the same topic.
We identify how language features like modality, evaluative language and sentence structure serve the text's purpose.
We discuss how the same information can be presented differently through language choices.
We evaluate whether an author's language choices effectively serve their purpose and audience.
We experiment with different language features in our own writing to achieve different effects.

VC2E7LY06.w: We are learning to explain how ideas in texts are organised using structures like taxonomies, cause and effect, extended metaphors and chronological order.
y: Understanding the underlying organisation of ideas helps us comprehend complex texts more efficiently.
Recognising these structures helps us organise our own ideas more effectively.
This analytical skill is essential for academic reading and writing across all disciplines.
eg: We identify cause-and-effect structures in explanation texts and discuss how they organise ideas logically.
We analyse how a text uses taxonomy — classifying ideas into categories and subcategories.
We explain how an extended metaphor structures and unifies a text's ideas.
We compare chronological and non-chronological organisation and discuss the effects of each.
We map the organisational structure of a complex text using a graphic organiser.
We apply our understanding of these structures when planning our own extended texts.

VC2E7LY07.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies — including inferring — to build literal and inferred meaning from increasingly complex texts.
y: At this level, much of a text's meaning lies beneath the surface — inferring is how we access it.
Combining multiple comprehension strategies helps us understand texts at the deepest level.
These skills are essential for engaging with the complex texts we encounter across all subjects.
eg: We make inferences about a character's feelings or motivations based on clues in the text.
We use visualising to engage with complex descriptions and abstract ideas.
We monitor our understanding of a complex text and apply fix-up strategies when comprehension breaks down.
We connect ideas across multiple texts to build a richer understanding of a topic.
We ask deep interpretive questions that require us to go beyond the literal meaning.
We summarise complex arguments in our own words, demonstrating accurate comprehension.

VC2E7LY08.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — written and spoken — selecting subject matter, language and print features suited to our purpose.
y: Being able to select and control the elements of our writing demonstrates maturity and skill.
Creating purposeful texts requires deliberate decisions about content, language and presentation.
This versatility prepares us for the diverse writing demands of secondary school and beyond.
eg: We write an analytical essay, selecting formal language and an appropriate structure for academic purposes.
We create a persuasive speech, choosing emotive language and rhetorical techniques to influence our audience.
We produce a narrative that uses specific literary techniques and structural choices for creative effect.
We select the most effective text type for our purpose and justify our choice.
We make deliberate decisions about vocabulary, sentence structure and paragraph organisation.
We create both written and spoken texts, adapting our approach for each mode.

VC2E7LY09.w: We are learning to review and edit our own texts and others' by removing repetition, refining ideas, reordering sentences and adding or substituting words for impact.
y: Skilled editing transforms competent writing into excellent writing.
Being able to identify and fix specific issues — repetition, unclear ideas, weak word choices — shows advanced writing control.
Editing others' work develops our critical eye and builds our own writing awareness.
eg: We identify and remove unnecessary repetition in our writing.
We refine vague ideas by adding specific details or evidence.
We reorder sentences within paragraphs to improve logical flow.
We substitute weak or imprecise words with stronger, more effective alternatives.
We peer-edit a classmate's text using specific criteria and provide constructive feedback.
We reflect on our editing process and discuss the impact of our changes.

VC2E8LA01.w: We are learning how language actively shapes relationships and the roles people take on within those relationships.
y: Language does not just reflect power dynamics — it creates and reinforces them.
Understanding this helps us navigate and challenge unequal power relationships in our own lives.
This awareness is essential for ethical communication and leadership.
eg: We analyse how language in workplace settings establishes hierarchies — formal directives versus collaborative suggestions.
We discuss how language can build rapport or create distance in personal relationships.
We examine how language in media shapes public perceptions of social roles and identities.
We identify language patterns that reinforce or challenge power dynamics in different contexts.
We explore how language is used to include or exclude people from groups.
We reflect on how our own language choices affect the relationships and roles we establish.

VC2E8LA02.w: We are learning how language features like simile and metaphor can add layers of meaning to evaluative and substantiating language.
y: Figurative language in evaluation adds emotional depth and persuasive power beyond literal statements.
Understanding these layered techniques helps us read persuasive and analytical texts more critically.
Incorporating figurative language into our own evaluations makes our writing more engaging and impactful.
eg: We analyse how a reviewer uses metaphor to evaluate a film — 'This movie is a rollercoaster of emotions.'
We identify similes in persuasive texts and discuss how they strengthen the evaluation.
We compare evaluations that use figurative language with those that use only literal language.
We discuss how metaphorical language in academic writing can make abstract concepts more accessible.
We experiment with using simile and metaphor to add depth to our own evaluative writing.
We evaluate the effectiveness of figurative language in published reviews, editorials and essays.

VC2E8LA03.w: We are learning how texts are structured according to their purpose, how language features vary, and that some texts combine features from multiple text types.
y: Real-world texts often blend purposes and structures — recognising this makes us more sophisticated readers.
Understanding hybrid texts reflects how communication works in practice.
This knowledge helps us create texts that serve complex purposes effectively.
eg: We analyse a text that combines informative and persuasive purposes and identify features of each.
We examine how a documentary uses narrative, informative and persuasive structures together.
We discuss how hybrid texts — like opinion pieces that use narrative — achieve their purposes.
We create a text that deliberately combines structures and language features from different text types.
We evaluate whether hybrid structures serve the text's purpose more effectively than a single structure would.
We map the structure of a complex real-world text, identifying where different text type features appear.

VC2E8LA04.w: We are learning how cohesion in texts is improved by strengthening internal structures — using evidence, quotations and references to connect and support ideas.
y: Strong cohesion with evidence and references elevates writing from opinion to informed analysis.
These skills are essential for academic writing, research and professional communication.
Understanding how evidence creates cohesion helps us evaluate the strength of others' arguments.
eg: We analyse how a well-written essay uses evidence and quotations to support and link its arguments.
We practise incorporating evidence smoothly into our own writing using signal phrases.
We evaluate the cohesion of a text by examining how well its internal structures connect ideas.
We discuss how different types of evidence — quotes, statistics, examples — serve different cohesive functions.
We improve the cohesion of a draft text by strengthening its evidence and internal links.
We compare texts with strong and weak cohesion and identify what makes the difference.

VC2E8LA05.w: We are learning to examine different clause structures — including embedded clauses — that add information and expand ideas in complex sentences.
y: Mastering varied clause structures gives us precise control over how we express complex ideas.
Understanding these structures helps us read and comprehend demanding academic texts.
This grammatical flexibility is a hallmark of mature, skilled writing.
eg: We analyse complex sentences with multiple embedded clauses and identify each clause's function.
We experiment with different clause positions — beginning, middle and end — to see how meaning shifts.
We use embedded clauses to add essential and non-essential information to our sentences.
We compare the effect of a complex sentence with multiple clauses versus several shorter sentences.
We identify how published authors use clause structures to control pace, emphasis and information flow.
We edit our writing to improve clarity and sophistication through varied clause structures.

VC2E8LA06.w: We are learning how nominalisation — turning verbs or adjectives into nouns — changes the way information is presented in texts.
y: Nominalisation is a key feature of academic and formal writing that we need to both recognise and use.
Understanding this technique helps us read dense, formal texts more easily.
Using nominalisation in our own writing makes it more concise and authoritative.
eg: We identify nominalisations in academic texts — 'investigate → investigation', 'develop → development'.
We compare a nominalised sentence ('The investigation revealed...') with a verbal version ('When we investigated, we found...') and discuss the different effects.
We practise converting verbal expressions into nominalisations in our own writing.
We discuss how nominalisation can make texts more abstract and sometimes harder to understand.
We analyse how authors use nominalisation to condense information and create a formal tone.
We evaluate when nominalisation improves writing and when simpler expression is more effective.

VC2E8LA07.w: We are learning how still images, moving images and sound use intertextual references — connections to other texts — to enhance and layer meaning.
y: Recognising intertextual references enriches our understanding of the texts we encounter.
This skill is especially important for interpreting contemporary media, which is rich with references.
Understanding intertextuality helps us create more sophisticated and resonant texts of our own.
eg: We identify how a film scene references a famous painting or historical photograph.
We discuss how a music video uses visual references to other cultural texts to create meaning.
We analyse how advertisements use intertextual references to connect their product with particular values.
We explore how parody and satire rely on the audience recognising the source text.
We examine how authors of picture books use visual intertextuality to layer meaning.
We create a multimodal text that deliberately uses intertextual references to enhance its message.

VC2E8LA08.w: We are learning about the role of academic vocabulary — the formal, precise language used across disciplines — and how to use it effectively.
y: Academic vocabulary is the language of education, research and professional discourse.
Mastering academic language gives us access to more complex ideas and higher-level thinking.
Using academic vocabulary accurately demonstrates expertise and builds credibility.
eg: We identify academic vocabulary that appears across multiple subjects — words like 'analyse', 'evaluate', 'significant' and 'contribute'.
We discuss the difference between everyday and academic uses of words like 'argument', 'theory' and 'critical'.
We practise using academic vocabulary naturally in our essays and discussions.
We build a personal academic word list organised by subject and usage.
We analyse how published academics use vocabulary to signal precision and authority.
We evaluate our own writing for appropriate use of academic register and vocabulary.

VC2E8LA09.w: We are learning how to use semicolons and dashes to extend our ideas and support meaning in our writing.
y: Semicolons and dashes are versatile punctuation tools that add sophistication and clarity to our writing.
Using these correctly demonstrates strong command of written English conventions.
These marks give us more options for connecting and emphasising ideas.
eg: We use semicolons to connect two closely related independent clauses — 'The rain stopped; the sun came out.'
We use dashes for emphasis or to insert a dramatic aside — 'The answer — surprisingly — was no.'
We find semicolons and dashes in published texts and explain their function in each case.
We practise using these marks in our own writing for variety and precision.
We compare sentences with and without semicolons/dashes and discuss the difference in effect.
We edit a piece of writing to include semicolons and dashes where they would improve clarity or impact.

VC2E8LE01.w: We are learning to explain how ideas, issues and points of view in literary texts from diverse historical, cultural and social contexts — including by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors — reflect their context.
y: Literature is both a product of and a response to its context — understanding this deepens our interpretation.
Engaging with diverse literary contexts builds our empathy and cultural understanding.
This analytical skill is fundamental to literary study at secondary level and beyond.
eg: We explain how a novel by an Aboriginal author reflects experiences of colonisation, resistance or cultural continuity.
We analyse how a literary text's historical context shapes its themes, characters and conflicts.
We discuss how cultural values and social norms in a text reflect the time and place of its creation.
We compare texts from different contexts that address similar themes and explain how context shapes each one.
We research the social and political context of a text and present how this knowledge enriches our reading.
We identify how our own context influences our interpretation of texts from other times and places.

VC2E8LE02.w: We are learning to explore and discuss opinions about the language features, literary devices and text structures that contribute to the styles of literary texts.
y: Engaging with opinions about literary style develops our critical thinking and aesthetic appreciation.
Exploring different perspectives on style helps us understand that there is no single 'correct' interpretation.
This prepares us for the kind of literary discussion expected in senior secondary and tertiary studies.
eg: We discuss how an author's consistent use of particular literary devices creates a recognisable style.
We compare critical opinions on a text's style and explain which we find most convincing.
We analyse how text structures contribute to the overall stylistic effect of a literary work.
We identify how language features like sentence rhythm, vocabulary choices and figurative language create style.
We form and justify our own opinion about an author's style using specific textual evidence.
We write a critical response evaluating the effectiveness of a text's style.

VC2E8LE03.w: We are learning to explain how language and visual elements in a range of literary texts influence how audiences respond to and interpret characters, events, settings and themes.
y: Understanding how texts construct meaning and influence audiences is a core analytical skill.
This knowledge helps us be more thoughtful and critical readers of all texts.
Recognising these techniques in literature transfers to understanding persuasion in media and everyday life.
eg: We analyse how an author's language choices lead readers to sympathise with or judge a character.
We explain how visual elements in a graphic novel or illustrated text shape our interpretation of events.
We discuss how the same story told with different language or images could elicit different audience responses.
We examine how setting descriptions create atmosphere that influences our reading of events.
We compare adaptations of a text — film versus novel — and discuss how different modes create different responses.
We create a text that deliberately uses language and visual elements to construct a particular audience response.

VC2E8LE04.w: We are learning to identify intertextual references in literary texts and explain how they create new layers of understanding.
y: Intertextuality is how texts speak to each other across time and culture.
Recognising these connections enriches our reading and reveals deeper layers of meaning.
This skill is essential for understanding how literature participates in broader cultural conversations.
eg: We identify a reference to a myth, fairy tale or earlier literary work within a text we are studying.
We explain how an intertextual reference adds meaning that would be missed without recognising the source.
We discuss how a modern retelling of a classic story creates new meanings through its references.
We analyse how allusion — a brief reference to another text — works to enrich a literary passage.
We compare a text with its source material and discuss how the intertextual relationship shapes meaning.
We create a text that deliberately uses intertextual references to enhance its meaning.

VC2E8LE05.w: We are learning how language features like sentence patterns create tone and voice, and how literary devices like imagery shape the reader's interpretation.
y: Tone and voice are what give a text its personality — understanding how they are created makes us more perceptive readers.
These features are what distinguish memorable writing from ordinary communication.
Developing our own tone and voice as writers is central to finding our creative identity.
eg: We analyse how short, clipped sentences create tension while longer, flowing sentences create a contemplative tone.
We identify how an author establishes and maintains a distinctive voice throughout a text.
We discuss how imagery shapes our interpretation — creating associations, moods and symbolic meanings.
We compare the tone and voice of two texts and identify the specific language features that create the difference.
We experiment with creating different tones in our own writing through sentence pattern choices.
We evaluate how effectively a text's tone and voice serve its purpose and audience.

VC2E8LE06.w: We are learning to create texts that experiment with literary language features and devices for particular purposes and effects.
y: Experimentation with literary techniques is how we develop our craft and find our creative voice.
Applying what we have learned about literary devices deepens our understanding of how they work.
Creating texts for particular purposes and effects demonstrates purposeful, controlled creativity.
eg: We write a piece that experiments with tone and voice for a specific emotional effect.
We create a text that uses imagery and symbolism to communicate ideas below the surface.
We experiment with narrative structure — nonlinear chronology, multiple perspectives, frame narratives.
We use literary devices we have studied to create particular effects and explain our choices.
We write for a specific audience and purpose, selecting literary techniques that serve both.
We reflect on our creative choices and discuss how well our techniques achieved the desired effects.

VC2E8LY01.w: We are learning to use interaction skills for specific purposes and contexts, including when supporting or challenging ideas and positions in discussions.
y: Being able to support and challenge ideas skillfully is essential for academic debate and collaborative inquiry.
Context-appropriate interaction skills help us contribute effectively in any discussion setting.
These skills develop our ability to think critically and communicate persuasively.
eg: We support a position in a discussion using evidence, reasoning and appropriate language.
We challenge an idea respectfully, focusing on the argument rather than the person.
We adapt our interaction style for different contexts — formal debate versus collaborative brainstorming.
We use language that acknowledges the validity of others' views while presenting our own.
We identify implicit positions in a discussion and bring them to the surface for examination.
We evaluate our own effectiveness in discussions and identify strategies for improvement.

VC2E8LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken texts for particular purposes and audiences, adjusting for formal and informal contexts and using effective vocal and visual features.
y: Versatile presentation skills — able to shift between formal and informal registers — are highly valued in all areas of life.
Adapting to context and audience demonstrates communicative maturity and audience awareness.
Strong spoken presentation skills build confidence for future academic, professional and civic speaking.
eg: We deliver a formal presentation using appropriate register, structure and vocal features.
We adapt a presentation for different audiences — more formal for a panel, more casual for peers.
We use visual elements like slides, diagrams or props to enhance our spoken message.
We employ features of voice — tone, pace, emphasis, pause — to maintain audience engagement.
We respond to audience questions and feedback effectively.
We evaluate presentations against criteria for purpose, audience, structure and delivery.

VC2E8LY03.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of spelling patterns to spell technical and academic words consistently and accurately.
y: Accurate spelling of technical and academic vocabulary demonstrates competence and credibility.
Consistent spelling is expected at this level and supports clear communication across subjects.
These spelling skills build on all the phonological and morphological knowledge we have developed.
eg: We spell subject-specific vocabulary correctly in all our written work — science, humanities, mathematics.
We use morphological knowledge to spell complex academic words — understanding how 'psychology' connects to 'psyche'.
We apply consistent spelling practices across different writing tasks and contexts.
We use reference tools efficiently to verify uncertain spellings.
We identify and correct our own recurring spelling errors.
We develop strategies for remembering the spelling of particularly challenging words.

VC2E8LY04.w: We are learning to describe how representations of people, places and events in texts reflect the context in which those texts were created.
y: All representations are shaped by their context — understanding this makes us critical, informed readers.
This skill helps us evaluate texts for bias, perspective and cultural influence.
Recognising contextual shaping is essential for media literacy and informed citizenship.
eg: We describe how a news article's representation of an event reflects the values and perspective of its publisher.
We analyse how historical texts represent people and events through the lens of their era.
We discuss how the same event is represented differently in texts from different cultural contexts.
We examine how advertising represents people in ways that reflect current social trends and attitudes.
We compare representations of the same place in a tourism brochure and a news report.
We evaluate our own texts for the representations we construct and the context that influences them.

VC2E8LY05.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how language features represent perspectives on issues, events, situations, individuals or groups.
y: Language is never neutral — every text constructs a particular perspective through its language choices.
Being able to analyse how perspectives are constructed through language makes us critical, empowered readers.
This skill helps us evaluate the reliability and fairness of the texts we encounter.
eg: We analyse how word choices in a news article frame a particular perspective on an issue.
We evaluate how language features like passive voice or nominalisation can obscure responsibility.
We identify how a text uses emotive language to position the reader to adopt a particular viewpoint.
We compare how two texts use different language to represent the same individual or group.
We discuss how language can marginalise or empower different groups.
We write a response that analyses the language features a text uses to construct its perspective.

VC2E8LY06.w: We are learning to analyse how authors organise ideas to develop and shape the meaning of their texts.
y: Understanding the organisation of ideas reveals an author's strategy for building their argument or narrative.
This analytical skill helps us read complex texts efficiently and evaluate their effectiveness.
Applying this understanding to our own writing makes us more strategic and purposeful authors.
eg: We map the organisational structure of an essay and explain how each section builds the argument.
We analyse how a narrative's structure — chronological, flashback, parallel — shapes meaning.
We identify how an author sequences ideas to build towards a conclusion or climax.
We evaluate whether a text's organisation effectively supports its purpose.
We compare different organisational approaches to the same topic and assess their effectiveness.
We plan our own extended texts by deliberately choosing an organisational structure that serves our purpose.

VC2E8LY07.w: We are learning to use comprehension strategies — including inferring — to build both literal and inferred meaning from complex texts across subjects.
y: Complex texts require us to read at multiple levels — what is said explicitly and what is implied.
Inferential reading is essential for engaging with sophisticated literature, media and academic texts.
These strategies help us become truly independent learners who can extract meaning from any text.
eg: We infer an author's attitude or purpose from the language they use, even when it is not explicitly stated.
We connect ideas across complex texts to build a comprehensive understanding of a topic.
We use questioning strategies to interrogate a text — identifying what is present, absent and implied.
We monitor our comprehension of challenging texts and apply targeted fix-up strategies.
We synthesise information from multiple sources, identifying complementary and contradictory ideas.
We discuss our inferences with others and refine them through dialogue.

VC2E8LY08.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — written and spoken — that raise issues, report events and advance opinions, using deliberate language choices.
y: Creating texts that engage with real issues demonstrates our ability to think critically and communicate effectively.
Deliberate language choices show control and intentionality in our writing.
This prepares us for the kind of purposeful, impactful writing expected in senior secondary and beyond.
eg: We write an essay that raises a significant issue and develops a well-supported position.
We create a report that presents events accurately while making deliberate choices about language and structure.
We write an opinion piece that advances a clear thesis using persuasive techniques.
We deliver a spoken text that engages an audience with a current issue.
We make and justify deliberate vocabulary, structure and stylistic choices in our texts.
We seek and respond to feedback to refine the effectiveness of our communication.

VC2E8LY09.w: We are learning to review and edit to refine and clarify ideas, improving the effectiveness and coherence of our own texts and others'.
y: Refining and clarifying through editing is what transforms competent writing into excellent communication.
Being able to improve both our own and others' texts develops our critical judgment and writing skills simultaneously.
These editing practices are essential for producing high-quality work in any context.
eg: We review a text for coherence, checking that ideas flow logically and are clearly connected.
We refine vague or ambiguous ideas by adding precision, examples or evidence.
We improve the effectiveness of our writing by strengthening word choices and sentence structures.
We peer-edit with specific feedback on content, organisation, language and conventions.
We compare drafts to evaluate the impact of our revisions.
We reflect on our editing process and identify patterns in the types of improvements we consistently need to make.

VC2E9LA01.w: We are learning how language strengthens relationships and the dynamics of roles within those relationships.
y: Understanding how language consolidates or shifts power in relationships helps us communicate with greater intentionality and ethics.
This awareness is critical for navigating complex social, professional and civic interactions.
Recognising these dynamics in texts helps us analyse how language is used to influence and persuade.
eg: We analyse how political speeches use language to strengthen bonds with their audience and establish authority.
We discuss how language choices in professional settings reinforce hierarchies or build collaborative relationships.
We examine how language in digital communication platforms shapes and sometimes distorts relationships.
We compare how different cultural traditions use language to strengthen community relationships.
We evaluate how characters in literary texts use language strategically to manage relationships.
We reflect on how our own language choices strengthen or weaken our relationships in different contexts.

VC2E9LA02.w: We are learning how evaluative and substantiating language expresses individual views and values through specific language features including modality.
y: Recognising how language expresses values beneath the surface of a text is a sophisticated critical literacy skill.
Understanding modality helps us gauge the degree of certainty, obligation or commitment in any text.
This analytical ability is essential for evaluating arguments, media messages and academic writing.
