eg: We analyse how modality choices — 'must', 'should', 'could', 'might' — reveal an author's level of commitment to their claims.
We identify how evaluative language in a text reveals the author's underlying values and beliefs.
We compare texts that express different values on the same issue and identify the language features that signal those differences.
We examine how subtle language choices — like using 'claimed' versus 'stated' — position the reader.
We write our own evaluative text, making deliberate modality and language choices to express our position.
We evaluate a political speech or editorial for the values it expresses through its language features.

VC2E9LA03.w: We are learning how text structures and language features can be adapted and altered according to the purpose of a text.
y: Understanding text adaptation gives us creative control over how we structure and present our ideas.
Recognising how skilled communicators adapt conventions helps us evaluate texts more critically.
This flexibility is what distinguishes competent writers from truly skilled ones.
eg: We analyse how an author adapts a traditional text structure to serve a unique purpose — like a persuasive text that uses narrative structure.
We examine how language features shift when a text's purpose changes — from informing to persuading.
We compare a standard and an adapted version of the same text type and evaluate the effectiveness of the adaptation.
We create texts that deliberately adapt or subvert expected structures for particular purposes.
We discuss how genre conventions can be broken effectively versus when breaking them undermines the text.
We evaluate real-world texts that blend or adapt structures and assess their effectiveness.

VC2E9LA04.w: We are learning how cohesive devices — including nominalisation — condense information and link, expand and elaborate on ideas across a text.
y: Mastering advanced cohesive devices allows us to write complex, authoritative texts.
Understanding how information is condensed helps us read and comprehend dense academic texts.
These techniques are essential for the kind of sustained analytical writing expected at senior levels.
eg: We analyse how nominalisation condenses information — turning 'scientists investigated the problem' into 'the investigation of the problem'.
We identify how a range of cohesive devices work together to create a seamless, well-connected text.
We use nominalisation strategically in our own academic writing to condense and link ideas.
We evaluate the cohesion of complex texts and identify where cohesive devices strengthen or weaken the argument.
We compare texts with strong and weak cohesion and identify the specific devices that make the difference.
We practise using a variety of cohesive devices — reference, substitution, ellipsis, conjunction and lexical cohesion — in our own writing.

VC2E9LA05.w: We are learning how authors vary sentence structures for effect — such as using sentence fragments, or deliberately placing dependent clauses in unusual positions.
y: Intentional variation of sentence structure is a sophisticated technique that creates emphasis, rhythm and style.
Understanding these effects helps us analyse how skilled writers craft their prose.
Experimenting with sentence structure is one of the most powerful tools for developing our own writing style.
eg: We analyse how a sentence fragment creates emphasis — 'Gone. Everything was gone.'
We examine how front-loading a dependent clause creates a different effect from placing it at the end.
We compare texts that use varied sentence structures with those that do not and discuss the impact.
We experiment with sentence fragments, single-word sentences and unusual clause placement in our own writing.
We identify how an author uses sentence length variation to control pace — short for tension, long for reflection.
We evaluate when unconventional sentence structures enhance writing versus when they distract.

VC2E9LA06.w: We are learning how abstract nouns can be used to summarise and encapsulate complex ideas in a text.
y: Abstract nouns allow us to discuss concepts, qualities and ideas with precision and efficiency.
Understanding this technique helps us comprehend and produce sophisticated academic writing.
Using abstract nouns strategically makes our writing more concise and intellectually rigorous.
eg: We identify how abstract nouns like 'resilience', 'inequality' or 'sustainability' summarise complex ideas into single terms.
We analyse how an academic text uses abstract nouns to build and connect arguments efficiently.
We practise using abstract nouns to summarise a paragraph's ideas in a single concluding sentence.
We discuss how abstract nouns can sometimes obscure meaning and when concrete expression is preferable.
We compare informal explanations with their abstract noun equivalents and discuss the trade-offs.
We use abstract nouns strategically in our own analytical and persuasive writing.

VC2E9LA07.w: We are learning to analyse how symbols in still and moving images and the use of sound augment and extend the meaning of a text.
y: Symbols and sound are powerful meaning-making tools that communicate beyond what words alone can express.
Analysing these elements develops our visual and media literacy for an increasingly multimodal world.
Understanding symbolic communication helps us create more resonant and effective multimodal texts.
eg: We analyse how colour symbolism in a film creates associations that augment the narrative's meaning.
We examine how recurring visual symbols in a text create motifs that deepen thematic meaning.
We discuss how sound design — silence, ambient sounds, musical motifs — extends meaning in multimedia texts.
We compare how the same story uses different symbols across different media adaptations.
We create a multimodal text that uses visual symbols and sound deliberately to layer meaning.
We evaluate the effectiveness of symbolic and sound elements in professional media productions.

VC2E9LA08.w: We are learning to analyse how vocabulary choices contribute to the style, mood and tone of a text.
y: Vocabulary is one of the most powerful tools an author has for establishing style, mood and tone.
Understanding this connection helps us analyse literary and non-literary texts with greater depth.
Applying this knowledge to our own writing helps us create the precise effects we intend.
eg: We analyse how an author's vocabulary choices create a particular mood — dark and foreboding, light and hopeful, clinical and detached.
We compare the vocabulary of two texts and explain how the choices contribute to their different tones.
We identify how formal vocabulary creates authority while colloquial vocabulary creates intimacy.
We experiment with changing the vocabulary in a passage and observing how the mood and tone shift.
We evaluate how effectively an author's vocabulary serves the overall style and purpose of their text.
We make deliberate vocabulary choices in our own writing to establish and maintain a consistent tone.

VC2E9LA09.w: We are learning to use punctuation conventions for condensing information and for formal referencing and citation.
y: Correct referencing and citation is essential for academic integrity and credibility.
Understanding how punctuation condenses information helps us write more efficiently.
These conventions are required for formal writing at senior secondary and tertiary levels.
eg: We use in-text citations correctly, following a standard referencing style.
We use colons, semicolons and brackets to condense information efficiently in academic writing.
We format direct quotations correctly, using quotation marks, ellipses and square brackets as needed.
We create a reference list or bibliography following standard conventions.
We discuss why proper citation matters — giving credit, avoiding plagiarism, enabling readers to check sources.
We practise integrating citations smoothly into our own analytical and research writing.

VC2E9LE01.w: We are learning to analyse how people and places are represented in literary texts from diverse historical, cultural and social contexts — including texts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors.
y: Analysing representation reveals how literature constructs meaning about identity, place and power.
Engaging with diverse literary contexts develops our capacity for empathy and critical understanding.
This analytical skill is central to advanced literary study and cultural literacy.
eg: We analyse how an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander author represents the relationship between people and Country.
We examine how representations of place in a text reflect or challenge particular cultural values.
We discuss how representations of people in literature are shaped by the author's social and historical position.
We compare how different authors represent similar people or places from different cultural vantage points.
We evaluate how representations in a text might reinforce or disrupt stereotypes.
We write an analytical response examining representation in a literary text with specific textual evidence.

VC2E9LE02.w: We are learning to explore and develop our personal responses to literary texts, comparing how our initial impressions change as we engage more deeply.
y: Tracking how our responses evolve through deeper engagement teaches us about our own reading process.
Reflecting on changing interpretations builds intellectual honesty and analytical depth.
This metacognitive skill helps us become more self-aware and sophisticated readers.
eg: We record our initial response to a text and compare it with our response after discussion and analysis.
We explore what specific elements of a text triggered our personal response and why.
We discuss how our backgrounds, experiences and values shape our initial impressions of a text.
We analyse how subsequent readings reveal layers of meaning we missed initially.
We compare our evolving response with classmates' and discuss what influenced the differences.
We write a reflective response that traces the development of our interpretation over time.

VC2E9LE03.w: We are learning to analyse how literary devices, language features and visual elements shape an audience's preferences about the representation of characters, events, settings and themes.
y: Understanding how audience preferences are shaped by textual techniques reveals the persuasive power of literature.
This analytical skill helps us recognise how we are being positioned as readers.
Applying this understanding makes us more intentional in our own creative and analytical writing.
eg: We analyse how an author uses literary devices to make a morally ambiguous character sympathetic.
We examine how language features position readers to prefer one interpretation of events over another.
We discuss how visual elements in graphic novels or films guide audience responses to settings and themes.
We compare how different literary techniques create different audience preferences for the same type of character.
We evaluate how effectively a text shapes audience preferences and whether this manipulation is ethically justified.
We create a text that deliberately uses literary techniques to shape the reader's preferences.

VC2E9LE04.w: We are learning to analyse the effect of text structures, language features and literary devices such as extended metaphor, metonymy, allegory and symbolism on the interpretation of literary texts.
y: These advanced literary devices create some of the richest and most enduring meanings in literature.
Understanding how these devices shape interpretation helps us engage with complex, canonical texts.
This analytical expertise is essential for senior literary study and enhances our appreciation of great writing.
eg: We analyse how an extended metaphor structures an entire text and shapes its interpretation.
We identify metonymy — where something closely associated represents the thing itself, like 'the Crown' for the monarchy.
We discuss how allegory creates a layer of meaning where characters and events represent abstract concepts.
We examine how symbolism creates resonance and depth in a literary text.
We compare how different literary devices create different types of interpretive challenges for readers.
We write a literary analysis that examines how specific devices shape the interpretation of a text.

VC2E9LE05.w: We are learning to analyse how text structures, language features and literary devices in literary texts create aesthetic qualities that appeal to readers.
y: Understanding how aesthetic quality is achieved helps us appreciate literature as an art form.
Analysing the aesthetic dimension of texts develops our sensitivity to language and our creative skills.
This appreciation deepens our engagement with literature beyond content to craft and beauty.
eg: We analyse how the rhythm and sound patterns in a poem create aesthetic pleasure.
We discuss how an author's prose style creates beauty through sentence construction, word choice and imagery.
We examine how text structure contributes to aesthetic qualities — like a perfectly balanced narrative arc.
We compare texts that prioritise aesthetic quality with those that prioritise information and discuss the differences.
We evaluate how particular literary devices — like imagery, allusion and wordplay — contribute to a text's aesthetic appeal.
We create a text that deliberately aims for aesthetic quality through careful attention to craft.

VC2E9LE06.w: We are learning to create texts — which may blend genres — that experiment with literary structures, language features, devices and voice.
y: Creating hybrid and experimental texts demonstrates creative sophistication and confidence.
Genre-blending reflects how contemporary literature often works, pushing against traditional boundaries.
This experimentation helps us discover and refine our unique creative identity.
eg: We create a text that blends two genres — like a detective story told as poetry.
We experiment with unconventional structures — fragmented narrative, multiple voices, non-linear chronology.
We develop a distinctive voice through deliberate choices about vocabulary, syntax and tone.
We use literary devices — extended metaphor, symbolism, intertextuality — with conscious intent.
We write a text that challenges genre expectations and explain our creative choices.
We share our experimental work and discuss how our structural and linguistic choices contribute to meaning.

VC2E9LY01.w: We are learning to use interaction skills to discuss opinions about texts with different purposes and audiences, analysing how language is used to influence.
y: Discussing how language influences is a critical skill for participating meaningfully in democratic society.
These analytical discussion skills help us evaluate media, advertising, political discourse and academic arguments.
Being able to identify and articulate how language influences makes us harder to manipulate.
eg: We discuss how a particular text uses language to influence its audience and evaluate the ethics of those techniques.
We compare texts with different purposes and analyse how their language strategies differ.
We participate in structured discussions where we analyse persuasive techniques in real-world texts.
We challenge unsupported or manipulative language use in texts, providing evidence for our critique.
We identify how our own opinions are influenced by language in the texts we encounter.
We lead discussions that explore the relationship between language, purpose and audience influence.

VC2E9LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken texts for particular purposes, demonstrating different levels of formality and using features of voice, visual elements and content selection effectively.
y: Commanding a range of formality registers in spoken presentations demonstrates communicative versatility.
Effective content selection and delivery skills are essential for academic, professional and civic life.
These skills help us advocate for ourselves and others in any speaking situation.
eg: We deliver a formal spoken text — like a policy proposal or research presentation — with appropriate register.
We demonstrate the ability to shift formality levels within a presentation when appropriate.
We select content strategically, including only what serves our purpose and audience.
We use vocal features and visual elements that enhance rather than distract from our message.
We adapt our presentation in real time based on audience response.
We evaluate our own delivery critically and implement specific improvements.

VC2E9LY03.w: We are learning to spell accurately and consistently, and to understand that non-standard spelling can be used in texts deliberately for particular effects.
y: Accurate, consistent spelling demonstrates professional writing competence.
Understanding deliberate non-standard spelling helps us interpret creative texts that use it.
This dual understanding — standard accuracy plus creative flexibility — shows mature language control.
eg: We spell accurately and consistently across all formal writing tasks.
We identify how authors use non-standard spelling to represent dialect, voice or identity.
We analyse how non-standard spelling in advertising or social media creates particular effects.
We discuss the difference between spelling errors and deliberate non-standard choices.
We use standard spelling in formal contexts while understanding when deviation is purposeful.
We evaluate the effectiveness of non-standard spelling in published creative and commercial texts.

VC2E9LY04.w: We are learning to analyse how representations of people, places, events and concepts in texts reflect the contexts in which those texts were created.
y: All representations are constructed and contextual — understanding this is fundamental to critical literacy.
This analytical skill helps us evaluate the texts we encounter with sophistication and discernment.
Recognising how context shapes representation is essential for informed, ethical reading.
eg: We analyse how a text's representation of a community reflects the author's cultural position and the text's historical context.
We compare representations of the same concept in texts from different time periods and contexts.
We evaluate how media representations of events are shaped by the outlet's editorial context.
We discuss how our own context influences the representations we create in our writing.
We identify how texts can naturalise particular representations, making constructed views seem like common sense.
We write an analysis of how representation in a specific text reflects its context.

VC2E9LY05.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how language features are used to explicitly represent values, beliefs and attitudes in texts.
y: Every text embodies values — being able to identify and evaluate them is essential critical literacy.
This skill helps us recognise how texts attempt to shape our thinking and beliefs.
Understanding how values are encoded in language empowers us to make informed judgments about the texts we encounter.
eg: We analyse how particular vocabulary choices in a text explicitly signal the author's values and attitudes.
We evaluate how language features like modality, evaluative language and intensifiers represent beliefs.
We compare texts that represent different value positions on the same issue and identify the language that marks those positions.
We discuss how texts can simultaneously express and promote particular values.
We identify the language features in our own writing that reveal our values and consider whether this is intentional.
We write a critical analysis of how a text uses language to represent specific values and attitudes.

VC2E9LY06.w: We are learning to analyse the organisation of ideas in paragraphs and extended texts, and to evaluate the impact of that organisation on meaning.
y: How ideas are organised is inseparable from what they mean — structure shapes meaning.
Evaluating organisational impact develops our ability to create well-structured texts of our own.
This skill is essential for producing and assessing the quality of extended academic writing.
eg: We analyse how a text's paragraph structure builds an argument step by step.
We evaluate whether the order of ideas in a text creates the most effective impact.
We discuss how different organisational approaches — deductive versus inductive, for instance — affect how meaning is received.
We compare two texts on the same topic with different organisations and evaluate which is more effective.
We reorganise a text and evaluate how the changed structure affects its meaning.
We plan extended texts with deliberate organisational choices, explaining how our structure serves our purpose.

VC2E9LY07.w: We are learning to integrate comprehension strategies to build literal, inferred and evaluative meanings from complex and challenging texts.
y: At this level, comprehension involves not just understanding a text but evaluating its arguments, techniques and values.
Integrating strategies seamlessly is what distinguishes an expert reader.
These skills support independent, lifelong learning across all domains.
eg: We build inferred meaning from subtle cues in a complex text — an author's tone, deliberate omissions, structural choices.
We evaluate the quality and credibility of arguments in academic and media texts.
We integrate visualising, connecting and questioning strategies automatically while reading challenging material.
We identify how authors use language to imply meaning without stating it directly.
We synthesise meaning across multiple complex texts to develop our own informed position.
We reflect on our comprehension processes and refine our strategies for tackling difficult texts.

VC2E9LY08.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — written and spoken — that present a point of view, advance ideas, and use evidence and argument effectively.
y: Creating texts that present and advance ideas with evidence is the foundation of academic and civic discourse.
These skills prepare us for the writing demands of senior secondary and tertiary education.
Being able to communicate our ideas persuasively and convincingly is a powerful life skill.
eg: We write an analytical essay that presents a clear thesis, develops supporting arguments and uses evidence effectively.
We create a persuasive text that advances a particular position using rhetorical and structural techniques.
We deliver a spoken text that presents a nuanced point of view on a complex issue.
We select and deploy evidence — quotations, statistics, examples — strategically to support our arguments.
We adapt our approach for different types of texts while maintaining consistent quality.
We revise our texts to strengthen the clarity and persuasiveness of our ideas.

VC2E9LY09.w: We are learning to review and edit our own texts and others' to improve clarity, coherence and control over content, organisation, sentence structure, vocabulary and punctuation.
y: Comprehensive review and editing at this level involves attention to every dimension of writing quality.
This systematic approach to revision is essential for producing polished, professional texts.
The ability to identify and improve specific aspects of writing demonstrates advanced literacy.
eg: We review a text systematically — checking content, then organisation, then sentence-level features.
We improve coherence by strengthening connections between paragraphs and ideas.
We refine sentence structures for clarity, variety and impact.
We select more precise vocabulary to replace vague or repeated words.
We proofread meticulously for punctuation and grammatical accuracy.
We provide detailed, constructive feedback on peers' writing that addresses multiple dimensions of quality.

VC2E10LA01.w: We are learning how language can have inclusive and exclusive social effects — how it can empower or disempower people and groups.
y: Language is a powerful social tool that can open doors or shut them — understanding this makes us more responsible communicators.
This critical understanding is essential for recognising and challenging linguistic discrimination and exclusion.
Awareness of language's social effects helps us use language ethically and advocate for inclusive communication.
eg: We analyse how inclusive language practices — like using people-first language — affect how groups are perceived and treated.
We examine how exclusionary language — jargon, gatekeeping, derogatory terms — disempowers people.
We discuss how language policies in institutions can include or exclude particular communities.
We evaluate how language in media can empower some voices while silencing others.
We compare texts that use inclusive language with those that do not and assess the social effects.
We reflect on our own language practices and their potential to include or exclude.

VC2E10LA02.w: We are learning that evaluative and substantiating language — whether stated directly or implied — reveals an author's views and values.
y: Detecting implicit values in language is one of the most sophisticated critical literacy skills.
This helps us read between the lines and evaluate the trustworthiness and bias of any text.
Understanding how language reveals values empowers us as both critical readers and ethical writers.
eg: We identify how an author's choice of evaluative language — even subtle word choices — reveals their position.
We analyse how implied evaluation works — where a text never directly states an opinion but clearly conveys one through language choices.
We compare how two texts substantiate their claims and evaluate whose evidence is stronger.
We examine how modality, hedging and intensification reveal different levels of commitment to claims.
We discuss how academic writing uses apparently neutral language that nevertheless contains implicit values.
We write a text that deliberately controls the explicitness of its evaluative language for a specific audience.

VC2E10LA03.w: We are learning to analyse text structures and language features and evaluate their effectiveness in achieving a text's purpose.
y: Being able to evaluate text effectiveness demonstrates advanced analytical and communication skills.
This skill helps us assess the quality of any text — from academic articles to media campaigns.
Applying evaluative criteria to texts improves our own ability to create effective communication.
eg: We analyse a complex text and evaluate whether its structure effectively supports its purpose.
We assess how well specific language features — vocabulary, grammar, literary devices — achieve the text's intended effect.
We compare texts with similar purposes and evaluate which is more effective, explaining our reasoning with evidence.
We identify where a text's structures or language features undermine its stated purpose.
We evaluate the effectiveness of our own texts against specific criteria.
We write a detailed evaluation of a text's structural and linguistic effectiveness.

VC2E10LA04.w: We are learning how authors select and vary text structures to create sequence and cohesion throughout a text.
y: Understanding structural selection and variation is key to creating sophisticated, well-crafted texts.
This knowledge helps us read and analyse how meaning is built across extended texts.
Mastering structural control is essential for the extended writing demands of senior secondary studies.
eg: We analyse how an author selects a particular text structure and then varies it strategically for effect.
We examine how structural choices create cohesion across a long text — essays, novels, reports.
We evaluate how varying structure within a text — like including a narrative within an argument — creates engagement.
We discuss how authors sequence their structural choices to build towards a particular conclusion or effect.
We create extended texts that demonstrate deliberate structural selection and variation.
We compare the structural strategies of texts in different genres and evaluate their effectiveness.

VC2E10LA05.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how specific sentence structures express and craft ideas with precision and effect.
y: At this level, sentence construction becomes a deliberate craft — every structural choice shapes meaning.
Evaluating sentence-level effectiveness helps us fine-tune our own writing for maximum impact.
This skill distinguishes competent writing from writing that is truly compelling and effective.
eg: We analyse how authors use different sentence structures — simple, compound, complex — strategically within a text.
We evaluate the effectiveness of particular sentence constructions in achieving specific effects.
We examine how sentence structure can create emphasis, rhythm, pace and emotional impact.
We compare the sentence-level strategies of different skilled writers.
We experiment with crafting sentences that use structure deliberately for precision and effect.
We edit our writing at the sentence level, evaluating and improving each sentence's contribution to the whole.

VC2E10LA06.w: We are learning to analyse how syntax — the arrangement of words and phrases — contributes to both meaning and style.
y: Syntactic analysis reveals how meaning is constructed at the most fundamental level of language.
Understanding syntax gives us precise control over how our ideas are expressed and received.
This knowledge bridges grammar and style, helping us write with both correctness and artistry.
eg: We analyse how an author's syntax creates a distinctive style — short, staccato sentences versus long, flowing ones.
We examine how word order within sentences affects emphasis and meaning.
We discuss how syntactic complexity or simplicity serves different purposes and audiences.
We compare the syntax of texts from different genres and discuss how syntax contributes to genre conventions.
We experiment with syntactic choices in our own writing, evaluating the effects.
We evaluate how syntactic features contribute to the overall style and effectiveness of published texts.

VC2E10LA07.w: We are learning to evaluate how the features of still and moving images and the use of sound create effects and shape representations.
y: In our media-saturated world, evaluating visual and audio representations is a critical life skill.
Understanding how representations are constructed helps us resist manipulation and think independently.
This evaluative skill applies to everything from news media to advertising to art and entertainment.
eg: We evaluate how camera angles, lighting and composition in a photograph or film construct a particular representation.
We analyse how editing choices in a video — cuts, transitions, pacing — shape the viewer's interpretation.
We discuss how sound design — music, ambient sound, silence — creates emotional effects and shapes meaning.
We evaluate how visual representations in news media can be constructed to serve particular perspectives.
We compare visual and audio treatments of the same subject and evaluate the different effects created.
We create a multimodal text and critically evaluate the representational effects of our own visual and audio choices.

VC2E10LA08.w: We are learning to understand and use an expanded vocabulary with precision — choosing exactly the right word for every context.
y: Precise vocabulary is the hallmark of effective communication in any field.
At this level, word choice should be deliberate, accurate and appropriate to context and purpose.
A broad, precise vocabulary supports sophisticated thinking and expression across all areas of life.
eg: We use precisely the right word for our meaning — choosing between near-synonyms based on their connotations and register.
We deploy academic and technical vocabulary accurately across different subjects and contexts.
We evaluate our vocabulary choices and replace imprecise or inappropriate words with better alternatives.
We discuss how vocabulary precision distinguishes expert writing from adequate writing.
We analyse how vocabulary choices in published texts create particular tones, moods and effects.
We expand our vocabulary deliberately through wide reading, word study and conscious practice.

VC2E10LA09.w: We are learning to use and experiment with punctuation for meaning and effect — going beyond correctness to use punctuation as a creative and rhetorical tool.
y: At this level, punctuation is not just about following rules — it is a tool for creating emphasis, pace and nuance.
Experimenting with punctuation develops our authorial control and creative confidence.
Understanding punctuation as a meaning-making tool helps us interpret how published authors use it.
eg: We use a dash for dramatic emphasis or an unexpected addition to a sentence.
We experiment with semicolons to create balanced, sophisticated sentence structures.
We use a colon to build anticipation or introduce a key idea.
We analyse how published authors use punctuation creatively — like deliberate sentence fragments or unconventional comma use.
We experiment with minimal or unconventional punctuation in creative writing and evaluate the effects.
We use punctuation strategically in our academic writing to control pace, emphasis and meaning.

VC2E10LE01.w: We are learning to analyse representations of individuals, groups and places in literary texts — including texts by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors — and evaluate how those representations reflect their context.
y: Understanding how representation works in literature is essential for critical, ethical reading.
Evaluating contextual influence on representation helps us read texts with historical and cultural awareness.
This skill develops our capacity to engage with diverse perspectives and challenge limiting representations.
eg: We analyse how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander authors represent Country, community and cultural identity.
We evaluate how representations of individuals in a literary text reflect the author's social and historical context.
We compare how different texts represent the same group and evaluate the influences at work.
We discuss how representations can reinforce, challenge or subvert dominant cultural narratives.
We examine how place is represented in literature and what those representations reveal about cultural values.
We write a critical analysis evaluating representation in a literary text with reference to its context.

VC2E10LE02.w: We are learning to synthesise our own interpretation of a literary text and explore how others interpret the same text differently.
y: Synthesising an interpretation requires us to bring together all our analytical skills into a coherent personal reading.
Exploring others' interpretations teaches us that texts are rich enough to sustain multiple valid readings.
This skill is essential for senior literary study and demonstrates intellectual maturity.
eg: We develop a sustained, evidence-based interpretation of a literary text that integrates multiple analytical perspectives.
We compare our interpretation with those of published critics and discuss areas of agreement and difference.
We discuss how different theoretical lenses — feminist, postcolonial, historical — produce different readings of the same text.
We present our interpretation to the class and defend it while remaining open to alternative views.
We write an extended response that synthesises our interpretation with reference to specific textual evidence.
We reflect on how our interpretation evolved through discussion and further analysis.

VC2E10LE03.w: We are learning to analyse how social, moral or ethical positions are represented across a range of literary texts.
y: Literature is a powerful vehicle for exploring social, moral and ethical questions — analysing this deepens our engagement.
Understanding how positions are represented — not just what they are — develops our critical and ethical thinking.
This analytical skill prepares us to engage with complex ethical issues in literature and in life.
eg: We analyse how a literary text represents a moral dilemma and positions readers to view it in a particular way.
We compare how different texts represent the same ethical issue — like justice, freedom or responsibility.
We discuss how an author's social position influences the ethical perspectives represented in their work.
We evaluate whether a text explores ethical complexity or presents simplistic moral positions.
We identify how language and literary techniques shape our moral response to characters and events.
We write a critical response examining the ethical positions represented in a literary text.

VC2E10LE04.w: We are learning to analyse how text structure, language features, literary devices and intertextual connections all work together to shape how we interpret literary texts.
y: At this level, we analyse all elements of a text as an integrated system of meaning-making.
Understanding these interconnections is essential for sophisticated literary interpretation.
This holistic analytical approach reflects how complex texts actually create meaning.
eg: We analyse how a text's structure interacts with its language features and literary devices to create a unified effect.
We identify intertextual connections and explain how they shape our interpretation of the text.
We discuss how multiple textual elements work together to create ambiguity, irony or thematic depth.
We evaluate how well a text's structural and linguistic elements serve its overall purpose and meaning.
We compare how two texts use different combinations of these elements to explore similar themes.
We write a comprehensive literary analysis that addresses structure, language, devices and intertextuality as an integrated system.

VC2E10LE05.w: We are learning to compare and evaluate how 'voice' as a literary device is used across different texts to evoke emotional responses and create aesthetic effects.
y: Voice is what makes a text come alive — it is the personality and presence that distinguishes one writer from another.
Understanding voice as a literary device helps us appreciate the artistry of literature at the highest level.
Developing our own critical understanding of voice enriches both our reading and our writing.
eg: We compare the voices in two literary texts and evaluate how each creates distinctive emotional and aesthetic effects.
We analyse how an author sustains a particular voice throughout a text and what techniques maintain it.
We discuss how voice can be character-based (a narrator's voice) or author-based (the writer's distinctive style).
We evaluate how effectively voice evokes emotional responses — empathy, discomfort, admiration, unease.
We compare how voice operates differently in prose, poetry and drama.
We experiment with voice in our own writing, attempting to create distinctive aesthetic and emotional effects.

VC2E10LE06.w: We are learning to create texts with a sustained voice, selecting and adapting literary structures, devices, language and audiovisual features for specific purposes and effects.
y: Creating texts with a sustained voice demonstrates mature creative control and authorial confidence.
This is the culmination of our creative writing development — integrating everything we have learned about literary craft.
Purposeful, sustained creative writing is both personally fulfilling and a valuable communication skill.
eg: We create a text that maintains a consistent, distinctive voice throughout.
We select and adapt literary structures to serve our creative purpose — conventional or experimental.
We use literary devices deliberately and effectively to create specific effects.
We incorporate audiovisual elements where they enhance the overall impact of our creative work.
We write for a specific purpose and audience while maintaining our creative vision and voice.
We reflect critically on our creative choices and their effectiveness in achieving our intended effects.

VC2E10LY01.w: We are learning to use interaction skills to discuss opinions about texts, analysing the purposes and effects of text structures and language features in group discussions.
y: At this level, discussion becomes a tool for collaborative intellectual inquiry and critical analysis.
Being able to analyse texts through discussion helps us develop and refine our interpretations.
These interaction skills prepare us for the academic, professional and civic discussions we will participate in.
eg: We lead and participate in discussions that analyse how text structures and language features achieve particular effects.
We present well-supported opinions about texts and engage constructively with opposing views.
We use discussion as a thinking tool — developing our analysis through the exchange of ideas.
We evaluate the quality of arguments made in discussion, including our own.
We facilitate discussions that draw out diverse perspectives and deepen collective understanding.
We reflect on how discussion has refined or changed our analysis of a text.

VC2E10LY02.w: We are learning to deliver structured spoken texts for particular purposes, experimenting with formality and making deliberate decisions about how to engage our audience.
y: Confident, purposeful public speaking is one of the most valuable communication skills we can develop.
Experimenting with formality demonstrates sophisticated audience awareness and communicative range.
These presentation skills prepare us for senior secondary, university, career and civic life.
eg: We deliver a formal academic presentation and an informal persuasive pitch, demonstrating our range of formality.
We experiment with rhetorical techniques — humour, anecdote, provocation — to engage our audience.
We make deliberate decisions about structure, content and delivery based on our analysis of the audience.
We use multimedia elements strategically to enhance our spoken message.
We adapt our delivery in real time, responding to audience engagement.
We evaluate our own presentations against professional standards and implement improvements.

VC2E10LY03.w: We are learning to use our full knowledge of the spelling system to spell accurately and to manipulate standard and non-standard spelling for particular effects.
y: Complete mastery of the spelling system gives us both accuracy and creative freedom.
Understanding how to break rules deliberately — and knowing what effect it creates — is a sign of true mastery.
This dual command of standard and non-standard spelling serves us in both academic and creative contexts.
eg: We spell accurately and consistently in all formal writing contexts.
We analyse how published authors use non-standard spelling to represent accent, dialect or character voice.
We manipulate spelling deliberately in our own creative writing for specific effects.
We discuss the relationship between standard spelling conventions and the reality of language variation.
We evaluate when non-standard spelling enhances a text and when it undermines it.
We use our comprehensive spelling knowledge to support error-free academic and professional writing.

VC2E10LY04.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how representations of people, places, events and concepts in texts reflect the contexts in which they were created.
y: Critical evaluation of representation is essential for navigating the complex information landscape of the modern world.
Understanding how context shapes representation helps us challenge biased, misleading or harmful texts.
This evaluative skill is fundamental to responsible citizenship in a democratic society.
eg: We evaluate how media representations of events are shaped by political, commercial and cultural contexts.
We analyse how historical texts represent events in ways that reflect the values of their time.
We compare representations of the same issue across different contexts and evaluate the influences at work.
We discuss how digital platforms shape representation through algorithms, user behaviour and commercial incentives.
We evaluate how our own texts construct representations and consider the ethical implications.
We write a critical evaluation of how context shapes representation in a specific text or set of texts.

VC2E10LY05.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how language features are used to both implicitly and explicitly represent values, beliefs and attitudes.
y: Detecting implicit values in language is a sophisticated skill that protects us from manipulation.
Being able to evaluate how language constructs ideology makes us more informed and independent thinkers.
This critical analytical ability is essential for academic, professional and civic engagement.
eg: We analyse how implicit language features — assumptions, presuppositions, connotations — represent values without stating them directly.
We evaluate how explicit evaluative language positions readers to adopt particular attitudes.
We compare texts that represent different ideological positions and identify the language features that signal each.
We discuss how apparently neutral or objective language can carry implicit values and beliefs.
We identify how our own writing contains implicit values and consider whether these are intentional.
We write a critical analysis examining both implicit and explicit value representation in a text.

VC2E10LY06.w: We are learning to analyse and evaluate how authors organise ideas in texts to achieve their purpose — examining the relationship between structure and meaning.
y: At this level, we evaluate not just what an author says but how the organisation of ideas creates meaning.
Understanding the purpose-structure relationship helps us create maximally effective texts of our own.
This evaluative skill is essential for advanced academic reading and writing.
eg: We analyse how the organisational structure of a complex argument supports or undermines its effectiveness.
We evaluate how an author's sequencing of ideas creates cumulative impact.
We compare different organisational strategies for the same content and evaluate which is most effective for the purpose.
We discuss how innovative or unexpected organisation can create particular effects — surprise, irony, revelation.
We plan our own extended texts with careful attention to how organisation will serve our purpose.
We evaluate published texts' organisation against criteria of effectiveness, coherence and impact.

VC2E10LY07.w: We are learning to integrate comprehension strategies seamlessly to build literal, inferred and evaluative meaning from the most complex and challenging texts.
y: Integrated, automatic strategy use is the hallmark of an expert reader.
Building evaluative meaning — judging the quality, credibility and significance of texts — represents the highest level of comprehension.
These skills equip us for the reading demands of senior secondary, tertiary education and professional life.
eg: We read complex academic or literary texts, integrating strategies automatically to build deep comprehension.
We evaluate the quality of arguments, evidence and reasoning in challenging texts.
We synthesise meaning from multiple complex sources to develop nuanced, well-informed positions.
We identify and interrogate the assumptions underlying complex texts.
We use our comprehension strategies flexibly, selecting and combining them as the text demands.
We reflect on our reading processes and continue to refine our strategies for maximum effectiveness.

VC2E10LY08.w: We are learning to create different types of texts — written and spoken — that reflect on challenging and complex issues, combining different modes and using sophisticated language choices.
y: Creating texts that engage with complex issues demonstrates intellectual maturity and communicative skill.
Combining modes — written, spoken, visual, digital — reflects how sophisticated communication works in the real world.
These are the culminating writing skills that prepare us for post-secondary academic and professional demands.
eg: We write an extended analytical essay on a complex issue, demonstrating control over content, structure and language.
We create a multimodal text that combines written, visual and audio elements to address a challenging topic.
We deliver a spoken text that engages with a complex issue and demonstrates sophisticated reasoning.
We select language features deliberately to achieve specific effects suited to our purpose and audience.
We create texts that reflect multiple perspectives on an issue rather than simplifying to a single view.
We evaluate our own texts for the sophistication and effectiveness of our language and structural choices.

VC2E10LY09.w: We are learning to review, edit and refine our own texts and others' for control and coherence of content, organisation, sentence structure, vocabulary and punctuation.
y: Comprehensive editing at this level involves fine-tuning every dimension of a text for maximum effectiveness.
Refining texts to a professional standard is essential for academic, professional and public writing.
These editing skills represent the culmination of our development as skilled, independent writers.
eg: We review texts holistically for coherence, evaluating how well all elements work together.
We refine content by strengthening arguments, improving evidence and eliminating weaknesses.
We improve organisation by ensuring logical flow and effective use of structural devices.
We fine-tune sentence structures for precision, variety and impact.
We select the most effective vocabulary and verify punctuation conventions.
We provide sophisticated editorial feedback on peers' work and implement feedback we receive on our own.

VC2EALALL01.w: We are learning to listen carefully for a short time.
y: Listening helps us understand what people say to us.
When we listen well, we can join in with our class.
Good listening helps us learn new words in English.
eg: We sit quietly and listen when our teacher reads a story.
We listen to a friend say their name and try to remember it.
We stop and listen when someone gives us a simple instruction like 'sit down'.
We listen to a short song and try to clap along.
We watch and listen when the teacher shows us something new.
We listen to the sounds around us and point to where they come from.

VC2EALALL02.w: We are learning to answer simple questions when someone asks us.
y: Answering questions shows we understand what people say.
It helps us practise using English words.
When we respond, people know what we need.
eg: We nod or shake our head when the teacher asks 'Do you want water?'
We point to the right picture when asked 'Where is the cat?'
We say 'yes' or 'no' when a friend asks us a question.
We hold up the right colour when the teacher says 'Show me red'.
We answer 'here' when the teacher calls our name for the roll.
We try to say a word back when the teacher repeats a question.

VC2EALALL03.w: We are learning to join in activities at school with help.
y: Joining in helps us make friends and feel part of the class.
We learn English faster when we do things with other people.
Being part of activities makes school feel safe and fun.
eg: We join a group game at lunchtime by watching and copying others.
We sit in a circle with the class and follow what others do.
We take turns with a toy or a book during class time.
We line up with the class when the bell rings.
We share pencils and crayons with the person next to us.
We follow the class routine, like packing up when the music plays.

VC2EALALL04.w: We are learning to follow simple instructions with lots of help.
y: Following instructions helps us do the right thing at school.
It shows us we can understand more English each day.
When we follow steps, we can finish our work.
eg: We listen when the teacher says 'Get your book' and then 'Open it'.
We follow two steps like 'Stand up' and 'Push in your chair'.
We watch the teacher do something first, then we copy the steps.
We listen to 'Put your hat on, then go outside' and do both things.
We follow along when the teacher says 'First draw, then colour'.
We use pictures on the board to help us remember the steps.

VC2EALALL05.w: We are learning to notice parts of what someone is saying.
y: Noticing parts of a talk helps us understand the whole thing.
It helps us pick out the important words we know.
This is how we start to understand longer messages in English.
eg: We hear the word 'dog' in a sentence and know the story is about a dog.
We notice when the teacher's voice goes up because they are asking a question.
We hear a familiar word like 'lunch' and know it is time to eat.
We pick out a name we know when the teacher is talking to the class.
We recognise the word 'stop' in a longer sentence.
We notice when the teacher says numbers we have been learning.

VC2EALALL06.w: We are learning to use short phrases we have practised.
y: Using phrases we know helps us talk to people right away.
It builds our confidence to say more things in English.
Short phrases help us get what we need at school.
eg: We say 'Good morning' when we arrive at school.
We say 'Can I have...' when we want something.
We say 'Thank you' when someone helps us.
We say 'I don't know' when we are not sure of an answer.
We say 'Excuse me' when we need to talk to the teacher.
We say 'My name is...' when we meet someone new.

VC2EALALL07.w: We are learning to make simple sentences with a person and an action.
y: Simple sentences help people understand what we mean.
Learning to put words in order is how we start building English.
This helps us tell people about things happening right now.
eg: We say 'I like apples' using a person (I) and an action (like).
We say 'She is running' to tell about what someone is doing.
We write 'The cat sits' with a thing and what it does.
We say 'We play' to tell the teacher what we are doing.
We make a sentence like 'He eats lunch' about a friend.
We practise saying 'I see the bird' to describe what we notice.

VC2EALALL08.w: We are learning to use describing words to say more about things.
y: Describing words help us say exactly what we mean.
They make our talking and writing more interesting.
Using these words helps people picture what we are telling them.
eg: We say 'big dog' instead of just 'dog' to describe what we see.
We say 'I run fast' to tell how we do something.
We write 'red ball' to say which ball we mean.
We say 'very happy' to show a strong feeling.
We describe our lunch as 'yummy sandwich'.
We say 'little bird' to tell about the size of something.

VC2EALALL09.w: We are learning to use everyday words we need at school.
y: Knowing everyday words helps us say what we need.
These are the words we hear and use the most.
Learning these words first helps us understand more English quickly.
eg: We learn words for things in the classroom like 'pencil', 'book' and 'chair'.
We learn words for feelings like 'happy', 'sad' and 'tired'.
We learn words for food we eat at school like 'apple' and 'water'.
We learn words for people like 'teacher', 'friend' and 'mum'.
We learn words for places like 'home', 'school' and 'playground'.
We learn action words like 'sit', 'stand', 'come' and 'go'.

VC2EALALL10.w: We are learning to say English words so people can understand us.
y: Clear speaking helps people know what we are saying.
Trying to say sounds clearly builds our English skills.
When people understand us, we feel good about talking.
eg: We try to copy the way the teacher says a new word.
We practise saying sounds that are different from our home language.
We say a word slowly and then try it faster.
We look at the teacher's mouth to see how they make a sound.
We repeat a word a few times until it sounds right.
We try to say the beginning and end sounds in a word clearly.

VC2EALALL11.w: We are learning how English sounds go up and down when people talk.
y: The way a voice goes up or down tells us a lot about meaning.
Hearing these patterns helps us understand if someone is asking or telling.
Copying these patterns helps us sound natural in English.
eg: We notice the teacher's voice goes up at the end of a question.
We copy the rhythm when we say a nursery rhyme together.
We hear which word is said loudest in a sentence.
We clap along to the beat of words in a song.
We notice that 'Hello!' sounds different from 'Hello?'
We try to copy the teacher when they say a sentence with feeling.

VC2EALALL12.w: We are learning to tell the difference between English and our home language.
y: Knowing the difference helps us switch between languages.
It helps us know when to use English at school.
Our home language is important too — knowing both makes us strong.
eg: We hear the teacher speaking and know it is English, not our home language.
We notice that some sounds in English are different from sounds at home.
We know that the word for 'water' is different in English and our language.
We recognise when a classmate is speaking their home language.
We can tell if a song is in English or another language.
We notice that English letters might look different from writing at home.

VC2EALALL13.w: We are learning tricks to help us understand when people speak English.
y: Good tricks help us understand even when we don't know every word.
Using these tricks makes us feel more confident.
They help us keep learning even when English is tricky.
eg: We look at the person's face and hands for clues about meaning.
We listen for words we already know in a sentence.
We look at pictures to help us understand what someone is saying.
We ask 'What?' or 'Again please' when we don't understand.
We watch what other children do to understand instructions.
We point or use gestures when we can't find the English word.

VC2EALALL14.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us learn English.
y: Our home language is full of knowledge we can use.
Thinking in our language first can help us understand English.
Using both languages together makes our learning stronger.
eg: We think of a word in our language first, then try to say it in English.
We use a picture dictionary that has words in both languages.
We explain something to a friend in our home language to help them understand.
We remember a story from home and connect it to a story at school.
We notice when a word sounds similar in both languages.
We talk with someone who speaks our language to check we understood.

VC2EALALL15.w: We are learning to use polite words and actions when we talk to people.
y: Polite words help us get along with everyone at school.
Knowing what to say and do makes social situations easier.
Using good manners helps us feel welcome and included.
eg: We say 'Hello' and wave when we see our teacher.
We say 'Please' when we ask for something.
We wave goodbye to a friend at the end of the day.
We smile and nod when someone talks to us.
We say 'Sorry' if we bump into someone.
We shake hands or wave when we meet someone new.

VC2EALALR01.w: We are learning to look for meaning when we see words and pictures.
y: Looking for meaning helps us start to read and understand texts.
It teaches us that writing and pictures carry messages.
This is how we begin to make sense of English books and signs.
eg: We look at the pictures in a book to understand the story.
We point to a sign at school and try to work out what it means.
We notice a label on a box and guess what is inside.
We look at a poster and talk about what the pictures show.
We notice words on the screen and try to read them.
We look at the cover of a book and guess what it might be about.

VC2EALALR02.w: We are learning to spot words we know when we read with our teacher.
y: Spotting words we know helps us read more each day.
It shows us how much English we are learning.
Recognising words is the first big step in reading.
eg: We see the word 'the' in a sentence and read it by ourselves.
We point to our name on the class roll.
We find the word 'cat' in a story the teacher is reading.
We recognise colour words like 'red' and 'blue' on labels.
We spot the word 'and' in a sentence with help.
We read familiar words on the daily schedule with the teacher.

VC2EALALR03.w: We are learning to understand signs and pictures around us with help.
y: Signs and pictures give us information without needing many words.
Understanding them helps us know what to do at school.
This shows us that meaning can come from images, not just words.
eg: We look at the toilet sign and know where to go.
We see a picture of scissors and know it is time to cut.
We read a 'STOP' sign and know what it means.
We look at the lunch menu pictures to choose our food.
We follow picture instructions to build something.
We see a smiley face sticker and know we did good work.

VC2EALALR04.w: We are learning to guess what might happen in a text with help.
y: Guessing helps us think about what we are reading.
It makes us curious and want to read more.
Predicting is a skill that helps us understand stories better.
eg: We look at the cover picture and guess what the story is about.
We see a picture of rain and guess the character will get wet.
We think about what might happen next when the teacher pauses.
We guess what a character will do based on the pictures.
We predict what food might be in a lunch box based on the cover picture.
We look at the title and guess if the book is happy or scary.

VC2EALALR05.w: We are learning that there are different kinds of texts.
y: Knowing different types helps us read them the right way.
Stories and signs work differently — it helps to know which is which.
This helps us find the right kind of text for what we need.
eg: We know a storybook is different from a shopping list.
We see that a letter has a greeting at the top.
We know a recipe tells us how to make food, step by step.
We notice a poster has big writing and bright pictures.
We see that a menu has food names and sometimes prices.
We know a class timetable shows us what happens each day.

VC2EALALR06.w: We are learning to read words and phrases we have seen before.
y: Reading familiar words helps us feel confident with English.
The more words we know, the more we can read by ourselves.
This builds a strong base for reading longer texts.
eg: We read our own name on our locker or tray.
We read the days of the week on the classroom calendar.
We see the word 'open' on a door and know what it means.
We read colour words when we are doing an art activity.
We spot words like 'mum', 'dad' and 'home' in a story.
We read number words like 'one', 'two', 'three' on a chart.

VC2EALALR07.w: We are learning about letters and the sounds they make.
y: Knowing letters and sounds is how we learn to read words.
Sounds help us work out new words we haven't seen before.
This is one of the most important steps in learning to read English.
eg: We learn that the letter 'b' makes a 'buh' sound.
We clap out the sounds in a word like 'c-a-t'.
We match a letter card to a picture that starts with that sound.
We sing the alphabet song and point to each letter.
We hear the 'sss' sound at the start of 'sun' and 'sit'.
We trace a letter with our finger and say its sound.

VC2EALALR08.w: We are learning to see capital letters, spaces and full stops in writing.
y: These marks help us know where sentences start and finish.
Noticing them helps us read more smoothly.
This is how English organises words on a page.
eg: We point to the capital letter at the start of a sentence.
We notice the full stop and know the sentence has ended.
We see spaces between words and know each word is separate.
We find capital letters at the start of names like 'Sam'.
We practise putting spaces between words in our writing.
We circle the full stops in a short sentence.

VC2EALALR09.w: We are learning to tell English writing apart from our home language writing.
y: Knowing the difference helps us switch between languages when reading.
It helps us know which books and texts are in English.
Both kinds of writing are valuable — we are learning to use them both.
eg: We look at two books and say which one is in English.
We notice English letters look different from characters in our language.
We see that English reads from left to right.
We pick out the English label from two labels in different languages.
We point to English words on a bilingual poster.
We notice our home language might have different marks or symbols.

VC2EALALR10.w: We are learning to choose and enjoy different texts with help from our teacher and friends.
y: Choosing texts helps us find things we enjoy reading.
Reading with others makes it easier and more fun.
Trying different texts helps us learn new words and ideas.
eg: We pick a book from the class library to look at during quiet reading.
We sit with a friend and look at a picture book together.
We choose a story we like and ask the teacher to read it.
We look at books about animals because we like animals.
We try a new kind of book a friend recommends.
We use the class tablet to look at a story with the teacher's help.

VC2EALALR11.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us read English.
y: Our home language gives us ideas and knowledge we can use.
Thinking in our own language first can help us understand English texts.
Using both languages makes us stronger readers.
eg: We know what a dog is in our language, so we understand the English word too.
We remember a story from home that is like the one we are reading at school.
We use a bilingual dictionary to check a word.
We explain a picture to a friend in our home language.
We connect a topic we know about from home to what we are reading in English.
We think about what a word might mean using what we know in our language.

VC2EALALR12.w: We are learning to watch and join in when the teacher reads with the class.
y: Shared reading helps us hear how English sounds when read aloud.
Joining in helps us learn new words in a fun, safe way.
Watching the teacher read shows us how books and reading work.
eg: We sit with the class and watch as the teacher reads a big book.
We join in saying a repeated phrase like 'Run, run, as fast as you can!'
We point to pictures when the teacher asks 'Where is the bear?'
We turn the page when the teacher signals it is time.
We listen and follow along as the teacher tracks the words with a finger.
We try to say a word the teacher pauses on during reading.

VC2EALALW01.w: We are learning to copy letters, words and short sentences with help.
y: Copying helps us practise making English letters and words.
It helps our hands learn how to write.
Writing familiar words helps us remember them.
eg: We copy our name from a name card at our desk.
We trace over dotted letters on a worksheet.
We copy the date from the whiteboard into our book.
We write a word under a picture by looking at the teacher's example.
We copy a short sentence like 'I like cats' from the board.
We trace symbols and letters in sand or on a tablet.

VC2EALALW02.w: We are learning to draw a picture and write a word next to it.
y: Drawing and labelling helps us show what we know.
It connects pictures to English words in our mind.
This is an early step in learning to write about our ideas.
eg: We draw a picture of our family and write 'mum' and 'dad' next to them.
We draw an apple and copy the word 'apple' beside it.
We draw our pet and the teacher helps us write its name.
We draw the weather and label it 'sunny' or 'rainy'.
We draw our lunch and write what food it is.
We draw a picture of our house and label the door and window.

VC2EALALW03.w: We are learning to copy a simple way of organising writing.
y: Copying how texts are set out helps us learn to write our own.
It shows us that different kinds of writing look different.
Following a pattern makes writing easier when we are starting out.
eg: We copy a short list, like a shopping list, with items underneath each other.
We copy a sentence pattern like 'I can see a ___'.
We look at a greeting card and copy its layout.
We copy a simple recount: 'First... Then...'
We follow a pattern to write our own 'My favourite ___ is ___' sentence.
We write a label for a diagram by copying the teacher's example.

VC2EALALW04.w: We are learning to write simple sentences by copying examples.
y: Copying sentences helps us learn how English words go together.
It builds our confidence to write our own sentences later.
Practising with models helps us remember sentence patterns.
eg: We copy 'The cat is big' from the board into our book.
We look at the teacher's sentence and write the same one.
We copy a sentence from a chart and change one word to make it our own.
We write 'I like ___' by filling in our favourite thing.
We copy a sentence from a word wall onto our page.
We write a sentence the teacher has modelled on the whiteboard.

VC2EALALW05.w: We are learning to put a person or thing together with an action word.
y: Putting a noun and a verb together is how we make sentences.
This is the most basic building block of English writing.
It helps people understand who is doing what.
eg: We write 'I run' to say what we do.
We write 'Dog sits' to tell about an animal.
We write 'Mum cooks' to say what our mum does.
We write 'We play' to tell about our group.
We write 'Bird flies' to describe what we see.
We write 'He jumps' to tell about a friend.

VC2EALALW06.w: We are learning to put a describing word with a thing word.
y: Adding describing words makes our writing clearer.
It helps people see exactly what we are talking about.
Using these word pairs makes our writing more interesting.
eg: We write 'big house' instead of just 'house'.
We write 'red apple' to say which apple we mean.
We write 'happy girl' to tell about someone's feeling.
We write 'little dog' to say the dog is small.
We write 'cold water' to describe what we are drinking.
We write 'new shoes' to tell about what we are wearing.

VC2EALALW07.w: We are learning to write words we see a lot about our life and school.
y: These words come up again and again in our reading and writing.
Knowing how to write them helps us write faster.
These are the words we need most to tell about our day.
eg: We write words like 'school', 'home', 'play' and 'eat'.
We write our own name and our teacher's name.
We write the days of the week in our diary.
We write words like 'mum', 'dad', 'friend' and 'teacher'.
We write colour words when doing art activities.
We write words about our feelings like 'happy' and 'sad'.

VC2EALALW08.w: We are learning to use sounds to help us spell short words.
y: Sounding out words helps us write them on our own.
This teaches us to connect sounds with letters.
It means we can try to write new words, not just ones we have memorised.
eg: We hear 'at' and write the letters a-t.
We sound out 'cat' and write c-a-t.
We hear the word 'dog' and try to write d-o-g.
We stretch out the word 'sun' and write s-u-n.
We listen for the first sound in 'big' and write 'b'.
We sound out 'map' slowly and write m-a-p.

VC2EALALW09.w: We are learning to use capital letters and full stops in our writing.
y: Capitals and full stops show where sentences start and end.
They make our writing easier for other people to read.
Learning these rules helps us write like a reader expects.
eg: We start our sentence with a big letter like 'The cat sat.'
We put a full stop at the end of our sentence.
We write our name with a capital letter at the start.
We check our writing to make sure each sentence starts with a capital.
We read our sentence and add a full stop if we forgot one.
We practise writing 'I' as a capital letter every time.

VC2EALALW10.w: We are learning that writing and drawing are different things.
y: Knowing the difference helps us use both at the right time.
Writing uses letters and words; drawing uses pictures and shapes.
Understanding this helps us learn how English text works.
eg: We draw a picture of a tree, then write the word 'tree' beside it.
We know that letters make words, but drawings show pictures.
We use a pencil to draw, then write our name underneath.
We see that the teacher writes words on the board, not pictures.
We point to the writing in a book, then point to the pictures.
We practise writing letters on one part of the page and drawing on another.

VC2EALALW11.w: We are learning that letters and words need to be written carefully.
y: Neat writing helps other people read what we write.
Getting letters right helps us spell words correctly.
Careful writing helps us remember how words look.
eg: We try to write our letters sitting on the line.
We look at a letter and try to make ours look the same.
We erase and try again when a letter doesn't look right.
We use the right pencil grip so our letters come out neatly.
We practise writing letters over and over to get them right.
We compare our writing to the teacher's example on the board.

VC2EALALW12.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us with English writing.
y: Our home language gives us ideas we can write about in English.
Thinking in our language first can help us plan our writing.
Using both languages makes us stronger writers.
eg: We think of what we want to say in our language, then try it in English.
We write a word in our home language and then find the English word.
We tell a friend in our language what we want to write, then write it in English.
We use ideas from stories in our home language in our English writing.
We draw something from home and try to label it in English.
We talk about our drawing in our language before writing about it in English.

VC2EALALW13.w: We are learning to write together as a class with our teacher.
y: Writing together shows us how to put ideas into English sentences.
We learn from watching and helping the teacher write.
Shared writing makes us feel confident to try writing on our own.
eg: We help the teacher choose words for a class story on the whiteboard.
We suggest the next word when the teacher is writing a sentence.
We watch the teacher write and notice how they spell words.
We help write a class letter to someone, like 'Dear Mr Smith'.
We add ideas to a class brainstorm that the teacher writes down.
We help the teacher write about our class excursion.

VC2EALA1L01.w: We are learning to listen carefully and pay attention when people talk.
y: Active listening helps us catch more of what people say in English.
It shows our teacher and friends that we care about their words.
The better we listen, the faster we learn new English words and ideas.
eg: We look at the person talking and think about what they are saying.
We put down our pencils and face the teacher during instructions.
We listen to a story and remember one thing that happened.
We sit still and listen when a classmate tells us about their weekend.
We listen to a song and try to catch some of the words.
We pay attention when someone explains the rules of a game.

VC2EALA1L02.w: We are learning to answer simple questions when people ask us things.
y: Answering questions shows that we understand what we hear.
It helps us practise putting English words together.
When we respond, we become part of the conversation.
eg: We answer 'I am seven' when the teacher asks 'How old are you?'
We say our favourite colour when a friend asks us.
We point to the right answer when the teacher asks 'Which one is bigger?'
We say 'yes' or 'no' and add a small reason when we can.
We answer 'I had cereal' when asked 'What did you eat for breakfast?'
We tell the teacher what we need by saying 'I need a pencil'.

VC2EALA1L03.w: We are learning to join in activities by listening for key words and watching what others do.
y: We don't need to understand every word to join in — key words help a lot.
Watching body language and actions gives us clues about what is happening.
Joining in helps us practise English and make friends at the same time.
eg: We hear the word 'outside' and know it is time to go to the playground.
We watch a classmate do the activity first and then copy them.
We see the teacher's hand signals and know to stop or start.
We hear 'line up' and follow what the other children do.
We notice a friend waving us over and join their group.
We listen for our name and look up to see what to do next.

VC2EALA1L04.w: We are learning to follow a few instructions in a row with help.
y: Following steps helps us complete tasks and activities at school.
It shows us we can understand longer messages in English.
Teachers often give instructions in order, so this skill is very useful.
eg: We listen to 'Get your book, open to page 5, and start reading' and do each step.
We follow 'Wash your hands, then sit at the table' with the teacher pointing.
We do three steps in an art activity the teacher explains.
We listen to the steps for a science experiment and do them in order.
We follow cooking instructions like 'Pour, stir, then taste'.
We use pictures on the board to help us remember the order of steps.

VC2EALA1L05.w: We are learning to recognise texts we have heard before, like songs and stories.
y: Recognising familiar texts helps us feel confident with English.
Hearing the same texts again helps us remember words and patterns.
It shows us how much English we already know.
eg: We hear the beginning of a familiar song and know what comes next.
We recognise a story the teacher has read before and say 'We know this one!'
We remember a rhyme and join in when the class says it together.
We hear the pattern 'once upon a time' and know a story is starting.
We recognise instructions we hear every day, like the morning routine.
We notice a poem the teacher reads again and try to say some words with them.

VC2EALA1L06.w: We are learning to recognise and use simple sentence patterns in English.
y: Sentence patterns are like recipes for making sentences.
Knowing a few patterns helps us say many different things.
Patterns make English feel less tricky because we can follow the same shape.
eg: We use the pattern 'I can ___' to say things like 'I can jump' and 'I can draw'.
We recognise 'This is a ___' and use it to name things.
We say 'I like ___' to talk about our favourite things.
We use 'I have a ___' to tell about things we own.
We hear 'Do you want ___?' and know someone is offering us something.
We follow the pattern 'It is ___' to describe the weather or how things look.

VC2EALA1L07.w: We are learning to build sentences with a person, an action and a thing.
y: These three-part sentences help us say more about what is happening.
Adding a thing after the action word makes our sentences clearer.
This pattern is one of the most common in English.
eg: We say 'I eat lunch' with a person (I), action (eat) and thing (lunch).
We write 'She reads a book' using all three parts.
We say 'He kicks the ball' to tell what a friend is doing.
We write 'We play a game' to describe what we are doing together.
We say 'Mum drives the car' to tell about something at home.
We describe a picture by saying 'The dog chases the cat'.

VC2EALA1L08.w: We are learning where to put describing words in English sentences.
y: In English, describing words go in a special order — learning this helps us sound natural.
Putting these words in the right spot makes our meaning clear.
This is different from some other languages, so it is worth practising.
eg: We say 'big red ball' with the describing words before the thing.
We say 'She runs quickly' with the describing word after the action.
We try saying 'a happy dog' instead of 'a dog happy'.
We say 'very slowly' to add emphasis to how something happens.
We notice that 'blue sky' sounds right but 'sky blue' sounds different.
We practise saying 'the tall tree' and 'he walks carefully'.

VC2EALA1L09.w: We are learning words for things we need, like and do every day.
