y: These words help us talk about our life and what matters to us.
The more words we know, the more we can say in English.
Learning words connected to our life makes them easier to remember.
eg: We learn words for things we do at school like 'write', 'draw' and 'read'.
We learn words for things we like, such as 'soccer', 'music' or 'painting'.
We learn food words like 'rice', 'bread', 'chicken' and 'fruit'.
We learn body words like 'hand', 'eye', 'head' and 'foot'.
We learn feeling words like 'excited', 'worried' and 'proud'.
We learn family words like 'brother', 'sister', 'grandma' and 'uncle'.

VC2EALA1L10.w: We are learning to say English words clearly by breaking them into sounds.
y: Clear pronunciation helps people understand what we say.
Breaking words into sounds helps us read and spell them too.
Practising sounds builds our confidence in speaking English.
eg: We break 'cat' into three sounds: c-a-t, then blend them together.
We practise saying the 'th' sound, which might not exist in our home language.
We clap out the parts in 'butterfly' — but-ter-fly.
We say a word slowly, then speed it up until it sounds natural.
We listen to the teacher say a word and try to match the sounds exactly.
We swap the first sound in 'mat' to make 'bat', 'sat' and 'hat'.

VC2EALA1L11.w: We are learning how the way people say things changes what they mean.
y: The same words can mean different things depending on how they are said.
Recognising these changes helps us understand English conversations better.
This helps us hear the difference between a question and a statement.
eg: We hear that 'You're going?' with a rising voice is a question.
We notice the teacher stresses the word 'now' to mean 'right away'.
We hear that 'Great job!' sounds different from 'Great job?'
We notice someone's voice gets louder on the most important word.
We practise saying 'I like apples' as a statement and then as a surprised question.
We listen to how the teacher reads a story with different feelings in their voice.

VC2EALA1L12.w: We are learning when to use English and when our home language is better.
y: Choosing the right language helps us communicate well in different situations.
There are times at school when English is needed, and times our home language is fine.
Both languages are important — we are learning to use them both wisely.
eg: We speak English to the teacher and use our home language with a friend who speaks it.
We switch to English during class discussions.
We use our home language at lunchtime to chat with friends who speak it.
We answer in English when the teacher asks us a question.
We explain something tricky to a classmate in our home language, then try in English.
We know it is okay to think in our language and speak in English.

VC2EALA1L13.w: We are learning what to do when we don't understand or when someone doesn't understand us.
y: Everyone has moments when communication goes wrong — knowing what to do helps.
These strategies stop us feeling stuck or frustrated.
Fixing misunderstandings is a normal part of learning a new language.
eg: We say 'Can you say that again?' when we don't understand.
We try different words if someone doesn't understand us the first time.
We use gestures or point to help explain what we mean.
We ask 'What does ___ mean?' when we hear a new word.
We show someone a picture to help them understand our idea.
We ask a friend who speaks our language to help explain.

VC2EALA1L14.w: We are learning to use what we know from our home language to help with English.
y: Everything we know in our home language is a strength we can build on.
Many ideas and skills transfer between languages.
Using our home language knowledge makes learning English faster and deeper.
eg: We know about the topic in our language, so we understand the English lesson better.
We notice that some English words sound like words in our language.
We use our home language to plan what we want to say in English.
We talk through a hard idea in our language first, then try it in English.
We compare how our language and English organise sentences differently.
We use bilingual books or resources to support our learning.

VC2EALA1L15.w: We are learning to use polite phrases and actions to talk to different people.
y: Knowing the right thing to say in social situations helps us feel confident.
Polite language helps us make friends and get along with adults.
These are the phrases English speakers use every day.
eg: We say 'Excuse me' before asking the teacher a question.
We say 'Can I play?' when we want to join a game.
We greet people by saying 'Good morning' or 'Hi, how are you?'
We say 'See you tomorrow' when we leave school.
We use 'Thank you' and 'You're welcome' during conversations.
We smile and make eye contact when greeting someone.

VC2EALA1R01.w: We are learning to understand what short texts are mostly about with our teacher's help.
y: Finding the main idea helps us understand what we read.
It helps us talk about texts with our teacher and friends.
This skill works for books, screens and everything we read.
eg: We read a short story with the teacher and say what it was about.
We look at a page on a tablet and tell the teacher the main idea.
We listen to a short text and point to the picture that shows what it is about.
We read a simple page about animals and say 'It is about pets'.
We look at a poster and explain what message it is sharing.
We watch a short video and tell a partner what happened.

VC2EALA1R02.w: We are learning to find important information in simple texts we know.
y: Finding key information helps us answer questions about what we read.
It helps us know which parts of a text matter most.
This skill helps us learn new things from reading.
eg: We find the name of the main character in a story.
We look at a simple timetable and find what we do after lunch.
We read a short text and find the answer to the teacher's question.
We look at a labelled diagram and find the part the teacher asks about.
We find the day and date on the class calendar.
We read a recipe and find what ingredients we need.

VC2EALA1R03.w: We are learning to match pictures with words we know.
y: Matching pictures to words helps us remember new vocabulary.
Pictures give us clues about what words mean.
This is a great way to build our English reading skills.
eg: We match a picture of an apple to the word 'apple'.
We play a memory game matching picture cards to word cards.
We point to the right picture when the teacher shows us a word.
We draw a line from a picture to its label on a worksheet.
We sort pictures and words into groups that go together.
We use a picture dictionary to find words we want to learn.

VC2EALA1R04.w: We are learning to make guesses about what a text will say or mean.
y: Making predictions helps us think more deeply about what we read.
Guessing and checking keeps our brain active during reading.
It helps us understand texts even when we don't know every word.
eg: We look at the pictures and guess what will happen next in the story.
We read the title of a book and predict what it might be about.
We guess how a story will end and check if we are right.
We look at a picture of a sad character and predict something bad happened.
We use what we already know about a topic to guess what the text will say.
We predict what a character might say next based on the pictures.

VC2EALA1R05.w: We are learning what makes different kinds of texts look and work differently.
y: Knowing text types helps us read them in the right way.
A recipe works differently from a story — it helps to know the difference.
This helps us choose the right kind of text for what we need.
eg: We notice a story has a beginning, middle and end.
We see that a list uses dot points, not paragraphs.
We know a recipe has ingredients and steps to follow.
We notice letters start with 'Dear' and end with a name.
We see that an information text has a heading that tells us the topic.
We notice a timetable is organised in rows and columns.

VC2EALA1R06.w: We are learning to read more and more words and phrases we have seen before.
y: The more words we recognise, the more fluently we can read.
Knowing common words helps us focus on learning new ones.
Reading familiar words quickly helps us understand whole sentences.
eg: We read common words like 'is', 'the', 'and', 'go' and 'my' in a text.
We spot phrases we know, like 'once upon a time' or 'the end'.
We read words on signs around the school without needing help.
We recognise words from our word wall in a new story.
We read the names of our friends in the class.
We read days of the week and months on the calendar by ourselves.

VC2EALA1R07.w: We are learning to spot sounds in words and recognise common letter patterns.
y: Knowing sounds and letter patterns helps us read new words.
It is like having a code-breaker for English words.
This skill helps us work out words we have never seen before.
eg: We hear the 'sh' sound in 'shop', 'fish' and 'ship'.
We see the pattern '-at' in 'cat', 'bat' and 'hat'.
We notice 'oo' makes the same sound in 'book' and 'look'.
We clap out the sounds in 'stamp': s-t-a-m-p.
We find words that rhyme because they have the same ending pattern.
We spot the 'ing' at the end of 'running', 'jumping' and 'playing'.

VC2EALA1R08.w: We are learning why capital letters, spaces and full stops are important in writing.
y: These marks help us know where sentences begin and end.
Understanding them makes our reading smoother and easier.
They help us make sense of written English.
eg: We know a capital letter means a new sentence is starting.
We use spaces to tell where one word ends and the next begins.
We know a full stop means the sentence is finished and we can take a breath.
We find capital letters at the start of people's names.
We see a capital 'I' always stands alone in English.
We point out the full stops in a short paragraph.

VC2EALA1R09.w: We are learning to tell English texts apart from texts in our home language.
y: Recognising English text helps us know what to read at school.
It helps us switch between our languages with confidence.
Knowing both writing systems is a special skill we have.
eg: We look at two websites and say which one is in English.
We see a bilingual book and know which side is the English side.
We recognise English on a screen compared to our home language.
We sort books into English and home language piles.
We notice that English uses the same alphabet as many signs around school.
We see a message on the computer and can tell if it is in English.

VC2EALA1R10.w: We are learning to pick books and texts that are right for us to read.
y: Choosing the right text helps us enjoy reading more.
Picking books we can manage builds our confidence.
Learning to choose helps us become independent readers.
eg: We look at several books and choose one that is not too hard or too easy.
We pick a book about a topic we are interested in, like animals or space.
We ask a friend to suggest a good book for us.
We try reading the first page of a book to see if we can understand most of it.
We choose a book with pictures that help us understand the words.
We pick a familiar book we want to read again to practise fluency.

VC2EALA1R11.w: We are learning to use what we know from our home language to help us read English.
y: Our home language gives us knowledge and ideas we can use when reading.
Skills from reading in our language help us read in English too.
Using both languages together makes us stronger readers.
eg: We already know what a dog looks like, so reading about dogs in English makes sense.
We use a word we know in our language to help us guess an English word.
We connect a topic from home with what we are reading at school.
We remember a story from our culture that is similar to the English one.
We use our knowledge of how stories work to understand an English story.
We talk about a text in our home language to make sure we understand it.

VC2EALA1R12.w: We are learning to read along with the teacher and try reading parts aloud.
y: Reading with the teacher helps us hear how English sounds when read aloud.
Trying to read aloud builds our confidence and fluency.
It helps us practise sounds, words and sentence patterns.
eg: We follow along with our finger as the teacher reads a big book.
We read the repeated part of a story out loud with the class.
We try reading a sentence the teacher just read, copying their speed.
We take turns reading one word each with a partner.
We join in during a group reading of a familiar text.
We read a short book with the teacher helping us with tricky words.

VC2EALA1W01.w: We are learning to write words and short texts about our life and school with help.
y: Writing helps us share our ideas and experiences in English.
Practising writing makes us better at spelling and using sentences.
Functional writing like labels and lists is useful every day.
eg: We write a sentence about what we did on the weekend.
We write a short label for a picture we drew.
We write our name, the date and a short sentence in our book.
We write a list of things we need for an activity.
We write about our family using simple sentences.
We fill in a sentence frame like 'My favourite animal is ___'.

VC2EALA1W02.w: We are learning to draw pictures that help explain our writing.
y: Pictures and words together tell a fuller story.
Drawing helps us plan what to write about.
Illustrations help other people understand our writing.
eg: We draw a picture of our pet and write a sentence about it underneath.
We illustrate a story we wrote with colourful pictures.
We draw what happened at a school event and add labels.
We draw a map of our classroom and label the important parts.
We create a picture to go with our recount of an excursion.
We draw the characters from our story so people know what they look like.

VC2EALA1W03.w: We are learning to use the same text pattern again and again to build confidence.
y: Repeating a pattern helps it stick in our memory.
Using the same structure lets us focus on choosing new words.
This is how we build up to writing our own original texts.
eg: We write 'I can see a ___' several times, changing the last word each time.
We write a recount using 'First... Then... Next... Finally...' each time.
We use the list format to write about different topics.
We follow the same letter-writing pattern to write to different people.
We use the sentence starter 'On Monday I...' to write about each day.
We write several 'My favourite ___ is ___' sentences about different things.

VC2EALA1W04.w: We are learning to write simple sentences by following examples.
y: Following models helps us learn the right way to build sentences.
It takes away the worry about getting it wrong.
With enough practice, we start writing our own sentences.
eg: We look at 'The dog is brown' and write 'The cat is grey'.
We copy a sentence pattern and change one or two words to make it our own.
We use the teacher's sentence on the board as a guide for our own.
We write 'I like swimming' after seeing 'I like reading' as a model.
We follow a sentence frame the teacher wrote and fill in the blanks.
We repeat a simple sentence pattern to write about each member of our family.

VC2EALA1W05.w: We are learning to use action words in the right form when we write.
y: Using the right verb form helps our sentences make sense.
It helps people understand when things are happening.
This is an important step in making our writing clearer.
eg: We write 'I am playing' instead of 'I am play'.
We use 'She likes' instead of 'She like' when writing about one person.
We write 'We went' when talking about the past, not 'We go'.
We use 'He is running' to describe what is happening right now.
We write 'They played' to tell about something that already happened.
We practise changing 'jump' to 'jumped' and 'jumping'.

VC2EALA1W06.w: We are learning to put describing words and action words together with thing words.
y: Combining these words makes our writing more interesting and detailed.
It helps us paint a clearer picture with our words.
This is how we start to write sentences that say more.
eg: We write 'the fluffy cat' instead of just 'the cat'.
We say 'the boy runs quickly' to add detail about how he moves.
We write 'a beautiful flower' to describe what we see in the garden.
We use 'I eat a yummy apple' to say more about our snack.
We write 'the tall tree' and 'the tiny ant' to show different sizes.
We describe our classroom by writing 'the colourful wall display'.

VC2EALA1W07.w: We are learning to write more and more everyday words from memory.
y: Writing common words quickly helps us focus on our ideas.
These words appear in almost everything we read and write.
The more words we can write easily, the longer our writing can be.
eg: We write 'because', 'when', 'they' and 'were' without looking them up.
We add new words to our personal word bank each week.
We write words about school subjects like 'maths', 'art' and 'reading'.
We spell words about our hobbies correctly in our writing.
We write feeling words like 'happy', 'sad' and 'scared' from memory.
We can write 'said', 'went', 'came' and 'made' by ourselves.

VC2EALA1W08.w: We are learning to spell short words and common words correctly.
y: Correct spelling helps other people read our writing.
Practising spelling builds patterns in our memory.
Starting with short words gives us confidence to try longer ones.
eg: We spell CVC words like 'dog', 'pen' and 'bus' correctly.
We spell common words like 'the', 'and' and 'was' in our writing.
We use letter sounds to work out how to spell a new short word.
We check our spelling against the word wall.
We practise tricky words like 'said' and 'come' until we remember them.
We write words we learned in class this week in a sentence.

VC2EALA1W09.w: We are learning to use full stops, capital letters and other punctuation in our writing.
y: Punctuation makes our writing easier to read and understand.
It shows where our ideas start and stop.
Using punctuation correctly is an important part of writing in English.
eg: We put a full stop at the end of each sentence we write.
We start every sentence with a capital letter.
We use a question mark when we write a question.
We put an exclamation mark when something is exciting or surprising.
We check our writing and add any missing full stops.
We use a capital letter for people's names and 'I'.

VC2EALA1W10.w: We are learning to understand the difference between writing and drawing.
y: Knowing the difference helps us use each one at the right time.
Writing and drawing work together to share our ideas.
This helps us respond correctly when the teacher asks us to write or draw.
eg: When the teacher says 'Write about your pet', we use words, not just pictures.
We draw a picture first, then add writing underneath to explain it.
We know that writing uses letters and words arranged in sentences.
We understand that drawing shows ideas through images and shapes.
We can tell which part of our work is the writing and which is the drawing.
We use both writing and drawing together to make our work complete.

VC2EALA1W11.w: We are learning to make our writing better with the teacher's help.
y: Improving our writing is how we get better at it over time.
Checking and fixing our work shows we care about doing well.
Getting feedback from the teacher helps us learn new things.
eg: We read our writing aloud and fix parts that don't sound right.
We add a missing full stop when the teacher points it out.
We try to spell a word again after the teacher helps us.
We add more detail to a sentence when the teacher suggests it.
We check that our writing makes sense by reading it back.
We cross out a word and write a better one in its place.

VC2EALA1W12.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us write in English.
y: Our home language is full of ideas we can express in English writing.
Planning in our language first can make English writing easier.
Using both languages is a strength, not a problem.
eg: We plan our story in our home language before writing it in English.
We write a word in our language and then look up the English word.
We talk through our ideas with a friend in our home language first.
We use a bilingual dictionary to find the right English word.
We think about a story from our culture and retell it in simple English.
We explain what we want to write to a helper in our home language.

VC2EALA1W13.w: We are learning to add our ideas to a class story or group writing.
y: Sharing ideas in a group helps everyone's writing get better.
It gives us a chance to practise English in a safe way.
Group writing shows us that everyone's ideas are valued.
eg: We suggest a character's name for the class story.
We think of what happens next and tell the teacher to write it down.
We offer a word to describe something in the group story.
We vote on the best ending for our class story.
We say a sentence out loud for the teacher to write on the board.
We help choose a topic for a group letter or story.

VC2EALA2L01.w: We are learning to listen carefully and show we are paying attention.
y: Active listening helps us catch details and understand longer messages.
It shows respect for the person speaking.
Good listeners learn more and build stronger friendships.
eg: We face the speaker and nod to show we understand.
We listen to a classmate's story and ask them a question about it.
We follow a set of instructions without needing them repeated.
We listen to a short explanation and retell the main point to a partner.
We keep our eyes on the teacher during a group discussion.
We wait for someone to finish speaking before we start talking.

VC2EALA2L02.w: We are learning to pick out the important information from something we hear or watch.
y: Finding key information helps us understand and remember what matters.
It helps us answer questions about what we have heard.
This skill works for videos, talks and everyday conversations.
eg: We listen to a short talk and say what the most important fact was.
We watch a video and tell a partner the main thing that happened.
We hear an announcement and remember the time and place mentioned.
We listen to a story and name the main characters.
We hear the weather report and remember if it will rain.
We listen to instructions for a game and remember the key rules.

VC2EALA2L03.w: We are learning to use the right kind of language for different situations at school.
y: Different situations need different kinds of language.
Using the right language helps us communicate well with everyone.
This makes us more confident in social and learning situations.
eg: We speak politely to the principal but more casually with our friends.
We use classroom language like 'May I borrow...' during lessons.
We negotiate with a partner about who goes first in a game.
We ask for help using the right words for the situation.
We join a group activity by asking 'Can I help with that?'
We use 'please' and 'thank you' when talking to adults at school.

VC2EALA2L04.w: We are learning to follow and give short sets of instructions with help.
y: Being able to follow and give instructions helps us work with others.
It builds our ability to use English in longer stretches.
This is important for completing tasks and helping classmates.
eg: We follow three or four steps to complete a craft activity.
We tell a partner how to draw something, step by step.
We listen to the teacher explain how to do an experiment and follow along.
We give a friend directions to find something in the classroom.
We follow steps in a recipe and tell someone else what to do next.
We retell a simple routine like 'First we line up, then we walk, then we sit'.

VC2EALA2L05.w: We are learning to recognise different kinds of spoken texts we hear.
y: Knowing what kind of text we are hearing helps us listen in the right way.
Different text types give us different kinds of information.
This makes us better at understanding all sorts of spoken English.
eg: We know the difference between a story being read aloud and an announcement.
We recognise when someone is giving instructions versus telling a joke.
We hear a news report and know it is different from a conversation.
We notice when the teacher switches from explaining to asking questions.
We recognise a song, a poem and a recount as different types.
We know a phone message is different from a face-to-face chat.

VC2EALA2L06.w: We are learning to use different sentence types and words like 'first', 'then' and 'after' to link our talking.
y: Linking words help our talking flow smoothly from one idea to the next.
Using different sentence types makes our speaking clearer.
This helps people follow what we are saying.
eg: We say 'First we read the story, then we answered the questions'.
We use 'after that' to connect two things that happened.
We say 'Next, we need to...' when explaining steps.
We use 'before lunch' and 'after school' to say when things happen.
We tell a recount using 'In the morning... Later... Finally...'
We ask questions, make statements and give commands in our talking.

VC2EALA2L07.w: We are learning to use action words in their correct forms when we speak.
y: Using verbs correctly helps people understand when things happen.
It makes our speaking sound more natural in English.
Getting verb forms right is a big step in mastering English.
eg: We say 'I went to the park' not 'I goed to the park'.
We use 'She is reading' for right now and 'She read' for yesterday.
We say 'They have finished' to show something is done.
We use 'We will go' to talk about the future.
We say 'He doesn't like' instead of 'He don't like'.
We practise irregular verbs like 'ate', 'swam' and 'ran'.

VC2EALA2L08.w: We are learning to use groups of words together that sound natural in English.
y: Phrases are groups of words that go together naturally.
Using them correctly helps us sound more fluent.
This helps us express more complex ideas.
eg: We say 'a lot of' instead of just 'many' to sound more natural.
We use 'in the morning' as a whole phrase to say when things happen.
We say 'on the left side' to give clearer directions.
We use 'next to the door' to describe where something is.
We say 'a cup of water' using the right phrase structure.
We practise phrases like 'kind of', 'sort of' and 'a bit'.

VC2EALA2L09.w: We are learning to pick out key words and ideas from short talks we hear.
y: Key words carry the most important meaning in a sentence.
Focusing on key words helps us understand even when we miss some words.
This is a skill that makes us better listeners in all situations.
eg: We hear a sentence about animals and pick out 'koala' and 'trees' as key words.
We listen to instructions and focus on the action words to know what to do.
We hear a story and identify the main character and what they did.
We listen to a description and pick out the colours and sizes mentioned.
We catch the topic word in a sentence to know what the talk is about.
We note down key words while listening to a short explanation.

VC2EALA2L10.w: We are learning to say words clearly, including ones with blended sounds and more than one part.
y: Clear pronunciation helps everyone understand what we say.
Blended sounds like 'str' and 'pl' are common in English.
Saying longer words correctly gives us confidence to use them.
eg: We say 'string' clearly, blending the 's', 't' and 'r' at the start.
We say 'help' with a clear 'lp' sound at the end.
We break 'elephant' into parts — el-e-phant — and say each one.
We practise words with 'bl' like 'blue', 'black' and 'blend'.
We say 'interesting' with all four parts: in-ter-est-ing.
We work on words ending in '-tion' like 'station' and 'action'.

VC2EALA2L11.w: We are learning to use our voice to add meaning by stressing words and changing our tone.
y: How we say something matters just as much as what we say.
Stress and tone help listeners understand our feelings and intentions.
This makes our speaking more expressive and easier to follow.
eg: We stress 'I' in 'I did it!' to show pride.
We use a rising tone to ask 'Are you coming?' as a question.
We say 'That was AMAZING!' with strong stress to show excitement.
We slow down and lower our voice to show something is serious.
We use a cheerful tone when sharing good news.
We stress the key word in a sentence to highlight the most important part.

VC2EALA2L12.w: We are learning to notice what is different between spoken English and our home language.
y: Noticing differences helps us understand how English works.
It helps us avoid common mistakes based on our home language habits.
Understanding both languages deeply makes us better at each one.
eg: We notice English puts the verb in the middle of a sentence, but our language might put it at the end.
We see that English uses 'the' and 'a', but our language might not have these words.
We notice English has sounds that our home language doesn't have.
We compare how questions are formed in English and in our language.
We notice that English word order might be different from what feels natural.
We see that some ideas need more words in English than in our language.

VC2EALA2L13.w: We are learning to check if we understand and to fix communication when it goes wrong.
y: Self-checking helps us catch mistakes before they cause confusion.
Knowing how to fix a misunderstanding keeps conversations going.
These strategies make us more independent communicators.
eg: We ask 'Do you mean...?' to check we understood correctly.
We rephrase what we said when someone looks confused.
We repeat the important part of a message to make sure we got it right.
We say 'I think you said...' to confirm what we heard.
We slow down and try different words when someone doesn't understand.
We ask 'Can you explain that another way?' when we are stuck.

VC2EALA2L14.w: We are learning to use everything we know from our home language to boost our English.
y: Our home language gives us a rich source of knowledge to draw on.
Many skills transfer between languages — we don't start from zero.
Using both languages actively makes our learning faster and deeper.
eg: We think about what we know about a topic in our language before the English lesson.
We use our home language literacy skills to help with English reading.
We compare word meanings across our languages to deepen understanding.
We talk through a tricky idea in our home language before explaining in English.
We notice grammar patterns in our language that also exist in English.
We translate key vocabulary to help us remember English words.

VC2EALA2L15.w: We are learning to start and keep simple conversations going in English.
y: Starting conversations helps us practise English and make connections.
Keeping a conversation going shows we can use English in real life.
This builds our confidence to talk to more people in more situations.
eg: We start a conversation by asking 'What did you do on the weekend?'
We keep talking by asking follow-up questions like 'What happened next?'
We respond to what someone says and add our own idea.
We invite someone to play by saying 'Do you want to play with us?'
We chat with a classmate about a book we both read.
We ask 'What do you think about...?' to start a discussion.

VC2EALA2R01.w: We are learning to understand what simple, familiar texts are mainly about.
y: Finding the main idea helps us understand and remember texts.
It helps us talk about what we have read with confidence.
This is a key reading skill that we use with all kinds of texts.
eg: We read a short story and tell a partner what it was about in one sentence.
We look at a simple information text and say what topic it covers.
We read a page about weather and say 'This is about how clouds form'.
We figure out the main idea of a text even if we don't know every word.
We read a familiar fairy tale and explain what lesson it teaches.
We summarise a short text by saying what the most important point is.

VC2EALA2R02.w: We are learning to understand the information in texts we read and watch in class.
y: Understanding information from texts helps us learn about new topics.
It builds our reading skills and our knowledge at the same time.
We use these skills every day in all our subjects.
eg: We read a text about Australian animals and remember three facts.
We watch a video and answer questions about what we saw.
We read a chart and explain what the numbers mean.
We find the answer to a question by re-reading a paragraph.
We look at a diagram and explain how something works.
We read instructions for an activity and know what to do.

VC2EALA2R03.w: We are learning how pictures, photos and diagrams in texts help us understand meaning.
y: Images carry important information that words alone might not tell us.
Understanding images helps us make sense of the whole text.
Many texts combine words and images, so we need to read both.
eg: We look at a diagram in a science text and understand the process better.
We use photos in a book to understand what a place looks like.
We read a map and use the images to find where things are.
We notice that a graph shows information in a visual way.
We look at the illustrations in a story to understand the characters' feelings.
We compare a photo with the words in a text to get the full meaning.

VC2EALA2R04.w: We are learning to use what we know about topics, text types and language to understand what a text says directly.
y: Using all our knowledge together helps us understand texts better.
Knowing how texts are organised helps us find information quickly.
Understanding the literal meaning is the first step to deeper understanding.
eg: We use what we know about stories to predict what will happen.
We look at the heading to know what the paragraph will be about.
We use our knowledge of the topic to understand tricky sentences.
We recognise that an information text tells us facts, not a made-up story.
We use sentence structure to figure out who did what.
We reread a sentence carefully to make sure we understand what it actually says.

VC2EALA2R05.w: We are learning to spot the differences between types of texts like stories, reports and instructions.
y: Different text types are built differently and serve different purposes.
Recognising the type helps us know how to read it.
This helps us choose the right type of text when we need information.
eg: We know a narrative tells a story with characters and events.
We see that a procedure gives step-by-step instructions.
We recognise an information report because it has headings and facts.
We notice a recount tells events in the order they happened.
We tell the difference between a poem and a letter by how they look.
We know a persuasive text tries to convince us of something.

VC2EALA2R06.w: We are learning to recognise common words quickly when we see them in different places.
y: Recognising words quickly helps us read more smoothly.
Seeing the same words in new places shows us they are useful everywhere.
The more words we know on sight, the more we can focus on meaning.
eg: We spot the word 'because' in a story and in a science text.
We read words like 'their', 'about' and 'could' without sounding them out.
We notice the word 'different' in a maths problem and a reading passage.
We recognise 'important' in a poster and in our textbook.
We read high-frequency words on a new page without pausing.
We see 'together' in a song lyric and in an instruction and read it straight away.

VC2EALA2R07.w: We are learning to use letter sounds to work out words we haven't seen before.
y: Sounding out words helps us read new words independently.
This skill means we can tackle any English text, even with unfamiliar words.
It also helps us find key words when scanning a text.
eg: We see an unfamiliar word and sound it out letter by letter.
We recognise the 'tion' pattern and know it says 'shun'.
We use the 'ea' pattern to read 'beach', 'teach' and 'peach'.
We look for a smaller word inside a bigger word to help us read it.
We blend the sounds in an unfamiliar word until it sounds like something we know.
We scan a page for a key word by looking at the first letters.

VC2EALA2R08.w: We are learning that full stops and question marks break text into sentences.
y: Punctuation marks help us know where one idea ends and another begins.
They tell us how to read with the right pauses and expression.
Understanding punctuation makes reading smoother and more accurate.
eg: We pause at a full stop before reading the next sentence.
We change our voice to sound like a question when we see a question mark.
We use full stops to break a text into sentence-sized chunks.
We notice that a question mark means someone is asking something.
We read a paragraph and count the sentences by counting the full stops.
We practise reading aloud, pausing at each full stop.

VC2EALA2R09.w: We are learning to compare English texts with texts in our home language.
y: Comparing texts helps us see how English and our language are different and similar.
It deepens our understanding of how each language works in writing.
This makes us more aware and thoughtful readers in both languages.
eg: We compare a book in English with one in our language and talk about what is different.
We notice that English books read left to right, but our language might go differently.
We look at a website in each language and compare the layout.
We notice different punctuation marks used in each language.
We compare how the same story is told in English and our home language.
We talk about what is easier to read in each language and why.

VC2EALA2R10.w: We are learning to choose good books for ourselves and read them with some help.
y: Choosing our own books makes reading more enjoyable.
It helps us learn what kinds of books we like.
Reading texts at the right level helps us grow as readers.
eg: We browse the library and pick a book that interests us and isn't too hard.
We choose a book about a topic we are studying in class.
We pick a book a friend recommended and read it with help from the teacher.
We try the 'five finger test' — if we don't know five words on a page, it might be too hard.
We choose a digital text on a tablet that matches our reading level.
We select a book with both pictures and words to support our reading.

VC2EALA2R11.w: We are learning to use everything we know from our home language to help with reading English.
y: Our home language reading skills are a powerful tool for English reading.
Knowledge and understanding from our language transfers to English.
Drawing on both languages makes us stronger, more flexible readers.
eg: We use our understanding of how stories work from our home language.
We recognise a concept from our language and connect it to the English word.
We think about what we already know about a topic from reading in our language.
We compare how books are organised in English and our home language.
We use our vocabulary from home to help us guess the meaning of English words.
We notice when an English text discusses something our culture talks about too.

VC2EALA2R12.w: We are learning to join in shared reading and read aloud with the class.
y: Reading together helps us practise pronunciation and fluency.
It builds our confidence to read by ourselves.
Shared reading lets us enjoy texts as a community of readers.
eg: We read a repeated part of a text out loud with the whole class.
We take turns reading sentences from a big book.
We read along when the class reads a poem together.
We read our part in a readers' theatre performance.
We join in during echo reading — the teacher reads a line, then we repeat it.
We read a familiar text aloud to a partner with expression.

VC2EALA2W01.w: We are learning to write short stories and information texts on our own, and harder texts with help.
y: Writing our own texts helps us share our ideas and knowledge.
Practising different types of writing prepares us for school work.
With practice and support, we can write about more complex topics.
eg: We write a short story about something that happened to us.
We write an information text about an animal we learned about.
We write a recount of a class excursion.
We write a description of our family with help from the teacher.
We write a simple report about a science experiment.
We write a letter to a friend telling them about our school.

VC2EALA2W02.w: We are learning to add pictures and drawings to different kinds of writing.
y: Illustrations help explain our writing and make it clearer.
Pictures and text together communicate more than either one alone.
Adding visuals makes our work more interesting for readers.
eg: We draw a diagram to go with our science report.
We illustrate the main event in our story.
We add a map to our recount of a trip.
We draw and label the steps in a procedure.
We create a picture timeline to go with our history writing.
We add a picture of our family to our personal description.

VC2EALA2W03.w: We are learning to use different ways of organising our writing for different purposes.
y: Different text structures help us present information clearly.
Using the right structure makes our writing easier to follow.
Knowing different structures means we can write about more things.
eg: We write a story with a beginning, middle and end.
We use a list with dot points to organise information.
We write a recount in the order things happened.
We use headings and subheadings to organise an information text.
We follow a procedure format with numbered steps.
We write a description starting from general information to specific details.

VC2EALA2W04.w: We are learning to write longer texts using sentence patterns we have practised.
y: Using patterns helps us write more without getting stuck.
Longer texts let us share more detail and information.
Repeating patterns builds our writing stamina and confidence.
eg: We write several sentences following the same pattern to make a longer text.
We use 'Every day I...' as a pattern to write about our routine.
We write a whole page about our favourite things using a repeating sentence pattern.
We extend our writing by adding 'because...' to each sentence.
We write a recount with each paragraph starting with a time word.
We use 'First... Then... After that... Finally...' to write a longer procedure.

VC2EALA2W05.w: We are learning to use a few different verb forms correctly in our writing.
y: Using verb forms correctly helps our writing make sense.
It tells the reader when things happen — past, present or future.
Getting verbs right is an important step in clear writing.
eg: We write 'I walked to school' using past tense correctly.
We use 'is playing' and 'are playing' correctly in our sentences.
We write 'She has a dog' instead of 'She have a dog'.
We use 'We will go' for something happening in the future.
We write 'They were happy' using the right past tense form.
We check our writing to make sure our verb forms are correct.

VC2EALA2W06.w: We are learning to write describing words and thing words in the right order.
y: English has a specific order for adjectives and nouns.
Getting the order right makes our writing sound natural.
This helps readers understand exactly what we are describing.
eg: We write 'a big red ball' with the adjectives before the noun.
We put 'old' before 'house' to say 'an old house'.
We write 'three little kittens' with the number and size before the noun.
We write 'a beautiful sunny day' in the right English order.
We use 'the long blue river' instead of 'the blue long river'.
We check that our describing words come before the thing they describe.

VC2EALA2W07.w: We are learning to use common words from our classroom activities in our writing.
y: Using words from class helps us write about what we are learning.
It builds connections between our speaking, reading and writing vocabulary.
The more words we can use confidently, the richer our writing becomes.
eg: We use words from our maths lessons like 'add', 'equal' and 'group' in writing.
We include science words like 'grow', 'change' and 'observe' in our reports.
We write using new vocabulary from our class reading book.
We use words from our spelling lists in our stories.
We include words from art lessons like 'colour', 'shape' and 'design'.
We use topic words from our current unit of work in our writing.

VC2EALA2W08.w: We are learning to spell words we know well and words that follow common patterns.
y: Correct spelling makes our writing easier for others to read.
Knowing common patterns helps us spell many words correctly.
Building strong spelling skills helps us write more confidently.
eg: We spell words like 'they', 'have', 'from' and 'were' correctly.
We use the '-ing' pattern to spell 'running', 'jumping' and 'reading'.
We apply the 'magic e' rule to spell 'make', 'like' and 'home'.
We use the '-ed' pattern for 'played', 'walked' and 'started'.
We spell words with 'ee' like 'tree', 'see' and 'green'.
We check our spelling by looking for patterns we know.

VC2EALA2W09.w: We are learning to use some punctuation marks correctly every time we write.
y: Consistent punctuation makes our writing clear and professional.
It shows our readers where ideas start, stop and change.
Good punctuation habits make all our writing easier to understand.
eg: We always start sentences with a capital letter.
We put a full stop at the end of every statement.
We use question marks when we write questions.
We use capital letters for names of people and places.
We begin to use commas in lists.
We check our writing to make sure we haven't missed any punctuation.

VC2EALA2W10.w: We are learning to plan our writing by saying our ideas aloud first.
y: Saying ideas aloud helps us organise our thoughts before writing.
It helps us work out what we want to say in a safe way.
Planning makes the actual writing easier and faster.
eg: We tell a partner what we want to write before we start.
We say our sentence out loud to check it sounds right, then write it.
We talk about our story ideas with the teacher before putting them on paper.
We rehearse our writing by saying it in our own words first.
We discuss our plan with a friend and add their suggestions.
We use simple words we can say easily and write them down.

VC2EALA2W11.w: We are learning to check and improve our writing with our teacher's help.
y: Editing helps us make our writing clearer and more correct.
Getting feedback shows us what we can improve next time.
Good writers always look over their work before they finish.
eg: We read our writing aloud and fix parts that don't make sense.
We check for capital letters and full stops with the teacher.
We add more detail when the teacher suggests a sentence needs it.
We fix a spelling mistake the teacher underlines.
We swap a word for a better one when we read our writing again.
We cross out a sentence that doesn't fit and write a better one.

VC2EALA2W12.w: We are learning to use our home language knowledge to make our English writing stronger.
y: Everything we know about writing in our home language helps us.
Planning or drafting in our language first can make English writing easier.
Drawing on both languages enriches our ideas and expression.
eg: We brainstorm ideas in our home language, then write them in English.
We use a bilingual dictionary to find the English word we need.
We think about how we would write something in our language to get started.
We compare text structures in our home language and English.
We draw on cultural stories and knowledge from our language for writing topics.
We ask a bilingual friend to help us find the right English phrase.

VC2EALA2W13.w: We are learning to contribute our ideas when the class writes together.
y: Shared writing lets us be part of creating a text as a team.
It is a safe way to practise English writing skills.
Our ideas matter and make the class writing richer.
eg: We suggest a sentence for the class story and the teacher writes it.
We offer a better word choice when the teacher asks for ideas.
We help decide what should happen next in a group story.
We suggest a topic sentence for a shared information text.
We contribute ideas during brainstorming for a class letter.
We help choose the right ending for a group narrative.

VC2EALBLL01.w: We are learning to listen with focus, paying attention to how someone speaks and what their body language tells us.
y: The way someone speaks — their tone, speed and emphasis — tells us a lot about their meaning.
Body language and gestures give us extra clues, especially when we are still learning English.
Listening with attention to all these signals helps us understand more in every conversation.
eg: We notice the teacher's voice gets serious when they give an important instruction.
We watch a speaker's hand gestures to help understand what they are describing.
We hear the rising tone at the end of a sentence and know it is a question.
We notice when someone leans forward because they want to tell us something important.
We pay attention to facial expressions to understand if someone is joking or being serious.
We watch a classmate's body language during a presentation to help follow their talk.

VC2EALBLL02.w: We are learning to respond when someone speaks to us, using words or actions.
y: Responding shows the speaker that we are listening and trying to understand.
It keeps conversations going and helps us practise English.
Even a gesture or nod is a valuable response when we are building our English.
eg: We nod to show we understand what the teacher just explained.
We answer a question using a few English words we know.
We raise a hand to show we want to have a turn to speak.
We point to the correct answer when the teacher asks a question.
We use a thumbs-up to show agreement in a group discussion.
We repeat a key word back to show we heard it.

VC2EALBLL03.w: We are learning to join in everyday school activities and social situations with help from our teacher or classmates.
y: Joining in helps us feel part of the class and build friendships.
Support from teachers and peers makes it easier to participate while we learn English.
Familiar routines give us a safe way to practise using English every day.
eg: We join in the morning routine by following what classmates do.
We participate in a group art activity by watching others and copying.
We take part in a class game with a buddy helping us understand the rules.
We sit in a small group and share materials during a class activity.
We follow lunchtime routines like lining up and sitting in the right spot.
We join a partner activity with a classmate who helps explain what to do.

VC2EALBLL04.w: We are learning to understand short conversations between people.
y: Understanding conversations helps us follow what is happening around us.
It helps us learn how English speakers interact and take turns.
This skill is essential for participating in group work and social situations.
eg: We listen to two classmates talking and understand what they are discussing.
We follow a short conversation between the teacher and a student.
We understand when someone asks a question and another person answers it.
We listen to a dialogue in a video and grasp the main point.
We follow a back-and-forth exchange about what game to play at recess.
We understand when the teacher asks a classmate something and they respond.

VC2EALBLL05.w: We are learning to recognise types of spoken texts we have heard before, like stories, songs and instructions.
y: Recognising what kind of text we are hearing helps us listen more effectively.
Familiarity with different spoken formats builds our confidence in English.
Knowing what to expect from a text type helps us understand it faster.
eg: We recognise when the teacher is reading a story because of the 'once upon a time' opening.
We know the class song when we hear the first few notes and words.
We recognise instructions because the teacher uses action words like 'first', 'next' and 'then'.
We identify a poem by its rhythm and rhyming pattern.
We know an announcement because of the formal tone and clear information.
We recognise a recount because someone is telling us about something that happened.

VC2EALBLL06.w: We are learning to say short phrases of two or three words, using actions, gestures or pictures to help.
y: Short phrases are the building blocks for longer sentences in English.
Using gestures and visuals alongside words helps us communicate clearly.
Every phrase we learn gives us more ways to express our ideas.
eg: We say 'my book' while pointing to our book on the desk.
We say 'go outside' and gesture towards the door.
We say 'I'm hungry' and rub our stomach to show what we mean.
We point to a picture and say 'big tree' to describe it.
We hold up our work and say 'look here' to get the teacher's attention.
We say 'need help' while raising our hand.

VC2EALBLL07.w: We are learning to use simple action words correctly when we speak.
y: Verbs are the engine of every sentence — they tell us what is happening.
Using them in context helps us get the form right naturally.
This is a crucial step in making ourselves understood in English.
eg: We say 'I like reading' using the right form of 'like'.
We use 'She is drawing' to describe what a classmate is doing right now.
We say 'We went to the library' using past tense correctly.
We tell the teacher 'I need a pencil' with the right verb.
We say 'He plays soccer' about a friend who plays regularly.
We use 'Can I have...' to make a polite request.

VC2EALBLL08.w: We are learning to use simple describing words when we speak.
y: Describing words help us give more detail about what we mean.
They make our speaking clearer and more interesting.
Learning common describing words is a fast way to expand what we can say.
eg: We describe our drawing as 'a big, colourful house'.
We say our lunch is 'really yummy' or 'very cold'.
We describe a classmate's pet as 'small and fluffy'.
We tell the teacher we are 'tired' or 'excited' to describe how we feel.
We say 'the loud music' or 'the quiet room' to describe what we hear.
We use words like 'fast', 'slow', 'long' and 'short' to compare things.

VC2EALBLL09.w: We are learning to listen for and understand key words in everyday situations.
y: Key words carry the most important meaning in a sentence.
Catching key words helps us understand even when we miss other parts.
This builds our confidence to follow along in class and social situations.
eg: We hear 'library' in a sentence and know we are going to the library.
We catch 'homework' and 'tomorrow' and know what we need to do.
We hear 'maths' and 'page 12' and know which page to open.
We pick out 'lunch' and 'outside' and understand the instruction.
We hear 'partner' and 'share' and know to work with someone.
We catch 'assembly' and 'hall' to know where to go.

VC2EALBLL10.w: We are learning to say familiar English words clearly enough for people to understand.
y: Clear pronunciation helps us be understood by our teachers and classmates.
Starting with familiar words builds our confidence to try new ones.
Good pronunciation makes conversations smoother and more enjoyable.
eg: We say common words like 'school', 'teacher' and 'water' clearly.
We practise words we use every day until they sound natural.
We say our classmates' names clearly and correctly.
We pronounce words from our spelling list so others can recognise them.
We try saying a tricky word slowly first, then at normal speed.
We copy the teacher's pronunciation of words we use in class.

VC2EALBLL11.w: We are learning to use stress, tone and body language to make our meaning clear.
y: How we say something can completely change what it means.
Body language and facial expressions add layers of meaning to our words.
These non-verbal skills help us communicate even when our vocabulary is limited.
eg: We stress the important word in a sentence, like 'I want THAT one'.
We raise our eyebrows and use a rising tone when asking a question.
We use a firm voice and say 'Stop!' to show we mean it.
We nod our head while saying 'yes' to reinforce our meaning.
We use a soft tone and a smile when comforting a friend.
We shrug our shoulders and say 'I don't know' to show uncertainty.

VC2EALBLL12.w: We are learning to tell when English is being spoken and try to respond in English.
y: Recognising English helps us switch to the right language at school.
Trying to respond in English builds our skills, even if it is hard at first.
Knowing when to use each language is a sign of being a skilled communicator.
eg: We hear the teacher speaking English and reply in English too.
We notice a classmate is speaking English and switch from our home language.
We hear an announcement and know it is in English, not another language.
We try to answer questions in English during class, even with short answers.
We recognise English in a video and try to follow along.
We notice the difference between English and other languages spoken at school.

VC2EALBLL13.w: We are learning to try again when people don't understand us and to ask for help when we need it.
y: Trying again shows determination and helps us improve our communication.
Asking for help is a smart strategy, not a weakness.
These skills keep conversations going even when communication gets tricky.
eg: We say a word again more slowly when someone doesn't understand.
We try pronouncing a word a different way if the first attempt doesn't work.
We ask 'How do you say...?' when we need help with a word.
We ask the teacher 'Can you repeat that?' when we miss something.
We point to what we mean while trying to say the word again.
We ask a friend who speaks our language to help us explain.

VC2EALBLL14.w: We are learning to use what we know from our home language to help us listen and speak in English.
y: Our home language is a valuable resource that helps us learn English faster.
Many concepts and ideas transfer between languages.
Using our full language knowledge makes us stronger learners.
eg: We use what we know about a topic in our language to understand an English explanation.
We compare how sounds work in our language and English.
We think of the word in our language first, then try the English version.
We use our understanding of grammar from our language to help with English patterns.
We explain an English idea to ourselves in our home language to check understanding.
We use our knowledge of story structure from our language to follow English stories.

VC2EALBLL15.w: We are learning to take turns listening and speaking during class conversations.
y: Turn-taking is how conversations work — everyone gets a chance to contribute.
Listening to others and waiting for our turn shows respect.
Practising this helps us participate more effectively in discussions.
eg: We wait for a classmate to finish speaking before we start.
We raise our hand to show we have something to say.
We listen to someone's idea and then add our own.
We share our thoughts in a 'think-pair-share' activity.
We respond to a classmate's comment rather than starting a new topic.
We give others a chance to speak in a group discussion.

VC2EALBLR01.w: We are learning to understand the main ideas of simple, familiar texts with our teacher's help.
y: Finding the main idea helps us know what a text is really about.
This is the first step to understanding and discussing any text.
With practice, we can do this more and more on our own.
eg: We read a short story with the teacher and tell the class what it was about.
We look at an information text and identify the topic in one sentence.
We read a picture book and explain the main message to a partner.
We watch the teacher read a text and then tell them the biggest idea.
We find the main idea by looking at the title, headings and pictures.
We discuss with a partner what we think the most important part of the text is.

VC2EALBLR02.w: We are learning to find specific information in short, simple texts with our teacher's support.
y: Being able to find information in a text is essential for learning.
It helps us answer questions and use texts as tools for discovery.
This skill builds towards reading independently for information.
eg: We read a short text about a place and find where it is located.
We look at a simple timetable and find out when art class happens.
We find a character's name by scanning the first page of a story.
We read a label on a diagram and find the part the teacher asks about.
We search a short text for the answer to a specific question.
We find a date or number mentioned in a simple information text.

VC2EALBLR03.w: We are learning to get information from pictures, diagrams and other images with our teacher's help.
y: Images carry important information that helps us understand texts better.
Learning to read images is a valuable skill for all subjects.
This gives us extra ways to understand meaning beyond just words.
eg: We look at a diagram of the water cycle and the teacher explains what it shows.
We study a map and find where our school is located.
We look at photos in a book and describe what we see.
We use pictures in a text to understand unfamiliar words.
We read a simple bar graph and compare the heights of the bars.
We study an image in a history text to learn about the past.

VC2EALBLR04.w: We are learning to guess what might happen in a text or work out things the author doesn't say directly.
y: Predictions and inferences help us think more deeply about what we read.
This makes reading more engaging and helps us understand hidden meanings.
These thinking skills are useful for all kinds of reading.
eg: We look at the cover of a book and predict what the story might be about.
We see a character crying in a picture and infer they are sad.
We predict what might happen next based on what we have read so far.
We guess the meaning of a new word by looking at the picture nearby.
We read about dark clouds and predict that rain is coming in the story.
We think about why a character did something, even though the text doesn't explain.

VC2EALBLR05.w: We are learning to recognise and explore different kinds of texts in print and on screens.
y: Texts come in many forms — books, websites, videos, posters and more.
Exploring different formats helps us become flexible, confident readers.
Each format has different features that we need to learn to navigate.
eg: We compare reading a story in a book versus watching it as an animation.
We explore a website and notice it has menus, links and images.
We look at a poster and discuss how it uses size, colour and words.
We notice the difference between reading a printed book and an e-book.
We look at a comic and see how pictures and speech bubbles work together.
We explore an app that tells a story using text, audio and images.

VC2EALBLR06.w: We are learning how simple texts are set out and organised on the page.
y: Knowing how texts are laid out helps us find information quickly.
Text conventions like titles, paragraphs and labels guide our reading.
Understanding layout is a key part of becoming an independent reader.
eg: We notice that a title at the top tells us what the text is about.
We see that paragraphs break the text into chunks of related ideas.
We find the page numbers and use them to navigate a book.
We notice that bold words often mean important vocabulary.
We see that a contents page shows us where to find different sections.
We understand that captions under photos explain what the picture shows.

VC2EALBLR07.w: We are learning to put words in the right order to make sentences that make sense.
y: Word order is essential in English — changing the order changes the meaning.
This helps us read sentences correctly and write our own.
Understanding word order builds a strong foundation for English grammar.
eg: We arrange word cards to make the sentence 'The cat sat on the mat'.
We notice that 'The dog bit the man' means something different from 'The man bit the dog'.
We put jumbled words in order to make a question.
We rearrange words to fix a mixed-up sentence.
We build sentences starting with 'who' then 'did what' then 'where'.
We check our sentences by reading them aloud to see if the order sounds right.

VC2EALBLR08.w: We are learning to use present and past tense verbs to talk about what is happening and what happened.
y: Tenses help us tell the difference between now and before.
Using the right tense makes our speech and reading comprehension clearer.
This is a fundamental building block of English communication.
eg: We use 'I am reading' to talk about what we are doing right now.
We say 'We played outside yesterday' using past tense.
We read a story and notice it uses past tense to tell events that already happened.
We describe our morning routine using present tense: 'I eat breakfast, then I walk to school'.
We notice that 'walked' and 'played' have '-ed' at the end for past tense.
We compare 'I go to school' (every day) with 'I went to school' (yesterday).

VC2EALBLR09.w: We are learning to use common word pairs like 'big house' and 'runs quickly' when we read and speak.
y: Common word combinations help us read more fluently and sound more natural.
Learning words in pairs helps us remember and use them more easily.
These combinations add detail and precision to our communication.
eg: We recognise 'tall building' as a common adjective-noun pair in a text.
We say 'runs fast' to describe how someone moves.
We read 'bright sunlight' and understand what kind of light is meant.
We use 'speaks softly' to describe someone who talks quietly.
We notice 'deep water' and 'heavy rain' as common combinations.
We practise using pairs like 'sharp teeth', 'strong wind' and 'hot day'.

VC2EALBLR10.w: We are learning to recognise common English words and phrases when we see them.
y: Recognising common words helps us read with more speed and confidence.
These words appear in almost every text we will read.
Building a bank of sight words is a fast path to better reading.
eg: We spot words like 'the', 'and', 'is', 'was' and 'they' straight away.
We recognise phrases like 'a lot of' and 'next to' in a sentence.
We read classroom labels and signs without needing to sound them out.
We recognise our classmates' names on the class list.
We read familiar words on the daily schedule board.
We spot common words in a new text and use them to understand the rest.

VC2EALBLR11.w: We are learning the letters of the English alphabet, their sounds and how they work together.
y: Knowing letters and sounds is the key to reading English independently.
Understanding how letters relate to sounds helps us decode new words.
This foundational knowledge supports all our future reading and writing.
eg: We match each letter of the alphabet to its most common sound.
We learn that 'c' can say /k/ as in 'cat' or /s/ as in 'city'.
We identify the sounds in a word and match them to letters.
We practise writing each letter while saying its sound.
We recognise that some sounds need two letters, like 'sh' and 'ch'.
We play games where we sort words by their first letter sound.

VC2EALBLR12.w: We are learning to notice spaces between words, capital letters and full stops when we read.
y: These features help us know where words and sentences start and end.
Recognising them makes our reading smoother and more accurate.
Understanding these conventions is essential for reading English text.
eg: We use spaces to tell where one word ends and the next begins.
We notice a capital letter and know a new sentence is starting.
We see a full stop and pause before continuing to the next sentence.
We find capital letters at the beginning of names.
We point to the spaces between words in a sentence.
We use the full stop to know when we have finished reading one idea.

VC2EALBLR13.w: We are learning to tell the difference between English texts and texts in our home language.
y: Recognising English text helps us know which language we are reading.
It helps us switch between our languages with awareness.
Being able to read in multiple scripts is a valuable skill.
eg: We sort a pile of books into English and home language groups.
We look at a bilingual poster and point to the English section.
We recognise the English alphabet in a text and know it is English.
We notice our home language has different characters or letter shapes.
We see a website and know which part is in English.
We identify a text as English based on the letters and words we recognise.

VC2EALBLR14.w: We are learning to choose texts that are right for our reading level with help from our teacher and classmates.
y: Choosing the right text helps us learn without getting frustrated.
Reading texts we can mostly understand builds our skills and confidence.
Learning to choose well is part of becoming an independent reader.
eg: We pick a book from a levelled reading box with the teacher's guidance.
We try reading the first page and decide if we understand enough to continue.
We ask a friend to suggest a book they enjoyed.
We choose a book about a topic we already know something about.
We select a text with pictures that support the words.
We try a new book and ask the teacher if it is at the right level.

VC2EALBLR15.w: We are learning to use what we know from our home language to help us read and understand English texts.
y: Our home language gives us concepts and reading strategies we can transfer to English.
Using both languages together deepens our understanding.
Every language skill we have is a tool for learning more.
eg: We think about what we already know about a topic from books in our home language.
We use our understanding of how stories work to follow an English story.
We compare a word in our language with the English word to remember its meaning.
We talk about a text in our home language to clarify our understanding.
We recognise a familiar concept in English because we have learned about it in our language.
We use reading skills like predicting and summarising that we developed in our first language.

VC2EALBLR16.w: We are learning to join in when the teacher reads with the class.
y: Shared reading gives us a model of how fluent reading sounds.
It is a safe way to practise reading aloud with support.
Participating helps us build our vocabulary and reading confidence.
eg: We follow along as the teacher reads a big book to the class.
We join in reading a repeated phrase or chorus in a story.
We try reading a sentence after the teacher reads it first.
We point to the words as the teacher reads them aloud.
We read along with the class during a shared reading of a poem.
We take turns reading a line with a partner during shared reading time.

VC2EALBLW01.w: We are learning to copy words, phrases and sentences neatly and accurately.
y: Copying carefully helps us learn how English words look and are spelled.
It trains our hands to form letters correctly.
Accurate copying is a stepping stone to writing our own texts.
eg: We copy the day's schedule from the whiteboard into our notebook.
We carefully write out vocabulary words from the board.
We copy a model sentence, making sure every letter is correct.
We write out the class rules from a poster.
We copy a short paragraph from a book to practise handwriting.
We trace and copy new words into our personal dictionary.

VC2EALBLW02.w: We are learning to write familiar words and complete simple sentences by following a pattern.
y: Writing familiar words builds our confidence with English spelling.
Following sentence patterns helps us write correctly from the start.
This is how we begin to create our own written texts.
eg: We write our name, our school and our teacher's name from memory.
We complete the sentence 'I like ___ because ___' using our own ideas.
We fill in the blanks in 'My favourite food is ___'.
We write common words like 'friend', 'school' and 'home' without looking.
We finish a sentence pattern the teacher starts on the board.
We write simple sentences using word cards to help with spelling.

VC2EALBLW03.w: We are learning to write imaginative ideas in simple ways.
y: Expressing our imagination in writing is exciting and creative.
It shows us that writing can be fun, not just for school work.
Creative writing helps us practise English in a personal, meaningful way.
eg: We write a short story about an imaginary adventure.
We create a character and write two sentences about them.
We write about a dream we had using simple English.
We make up a funny ending to a story and write it down.
We write a short poem about our favourite season.
We write about what we would do if we could fly.

VC2EALBLW04.w: We are learning to draw pictures about events and tell the teacher what to write for us.
y: Drawing helps us communicate ideas we can't write in English yet.
Dictating sentences to the teacher lets us create real texts while building skills.
This connects our thinking to English writing, even at an early stage.
eg: We draw a picture of our weekend and tell the teacher what happened so they can write it.
We draw a science experiment and dictate what we observed.
We illustrate a class excursion and tell the teacher the sentences to go with it.
We draw our family and tell the teacher a sentence about each person.
We create a picture of a favourite place and dictate why we like it.
We draw a sequence of events and the teacher writes our words underneath.

VC2EALBLW05.w: We are learning to write very short, simple texts on our own.
y: Writing our own texts, even short ones, is a big achievement.
It shows we can use English to put our ideas on paper.
Each short text we write is a step towards longer, more complex writing.
eg: We write two or three sentences about what we did today.
We write a short description of our pet or favourite animal.
We write a simple shopping list for a pretend trip to the shops.
We write a short message to a classmate.
We write a caption for a photo we like.
We write a few sentences about a book we read in class.

VC2EALBLW06.w: We are learning to put a few ideas in order when we write.
y: Sequencing ideas helps our writing make sense to the reader.
It teaches us to plan our writing logically.
Ordered writing is easier to understand and more enjoyable to read.
eg: We write about our morning using 'first', 'then' and 'last'.
We put three steps of an activity in the correct order.
We write a recount with events in the order they happened.
We number our ideas before writing them as sentences.
We use a simple plan with a beginning, middle and end.
We sequence our ideas about how to make a sandwich.

VC2EALBLW07.w: We are learning to use simple action words in our writing.
y: Verbs are the most important words in our sentences — they tell what happens.
Using verbs correctly makes our writing clear and understandable.
Building a bank of verbs helps us write about more topics.
eg: We use 'run', 'eat', 'play' and 'sleep' in our sentences.
We write 'I walk to school' using the verb 'walk'.
We use 'made' to write about something we created in art.
We write 'The bird sings' using 'sings' as our verb.
We use 'help' to write about working with a partner.
We choose the right verb to describe what we see in a picture.

VC2EALBLW08.w: We are learning to use simple describing words in our writing.
y: Describing words add colour and detail to our writing.
They help our reader imagine what we are writing about.
Using these words makes our writing more interesting and specific.
eg: We write 'the blue sky' instead of just 'the sky'.
We describe our friend as 'kind and funny'.
We use 'loud', 'soft', 'bright' and 'dark' to describe things.
We write 'a hot, sunny day' to describe the weather.
We describe our pet using words like 'small', 'brown' and 'playful'.
We add the word 'delicious' to describe what we ate.

VC2EALBLW09.w: We are learning to use everyday words correctly in our writing, even if we use some of them a lot.
y: Using high-frequency words accurately is the foundation of good writing.
These are the words that appear in almost everything we write.
Getting them right helps our writing be understood by everyone.
eg: We spell 'the', 'and', 'is', 'was' and 'they' correctly every time.
We use 'because' to connect two ideas in our writing.
We might use 'and' many times, but we spell it right each time.
We write 'said' correctly when telling what someone spoke.
We use 'then' accurately when ordering events.
We write 'very' and 'really' in the right places to add emphasis.

VC2EALBLW10.w: We are learning to spell common words correctly from memory.
y: Correct spelling helps everyone read our writing easily.
Memorising common words means we can write faster and focus on our ideas.
This builds a strong foundation for spelling harder words later.
eg: We spell 'come', 'have', 'like' and 'some' without looking them up.
We write our spelling words in a sentence to show we know them.
We use our personal word list to check words we are learning.
We spell the days of the week correctly from memory.
We practise tricky words like 'friend', 'because' and 'people'.
We spell colour words and number words correctly in our writing.

VC2EALBLW11.w: We are learning to use capital letters and full stops in our writing by copying how they are used in texts.
y: Punctuation makes our writing clear and organised.
Copying punctuation from texts helps us learn the patterns.
Using punctuation correctly shows we understand how English writing works.
eg: We put a capital letter at the start of each sentence we write.
We copy the full stop at the end of a sentence from a model.
We use a capital letter for names we see in a text and copy that pattern.
We put a question mark at the end when we copy a question.
We notice where commas appear in a text and try to use them too.
We check our writing against a model to make sure our punctuation matches.

VC2EALBLW12.w: We are learning that writing is different from drawing, and that we write differently depending on what we are writing and why.
y: Knowing when to draw and when to write helps us communicate more effectively.
Different writing purposes need different styles and formats.
Understanding this helps us choose the best way to share our ideas.
eg: We know a story needs sentences, while a label just needs a word or two.
We understand that a list looks different from a letter.
We write instructions in steps but write a story in paragraphs.
We see that a birthday card has different writing from a school report.
We know drawing a picture is not the same as writing about it.
We adjust our writing style for a message to a friend versus a note to the teacher.

VC2EALBLW13.w: We are learning to fix mistakes in our writing when the teacher shows us what to change.
y: Fixing mistakes is how we improve as writers.
Learning from corrections helps us avoid the same mistakes next time.
Rewriting carefully shows we care about doing our best work.
eg: We fix a misspelled word after the teacher circles it.
We rewrite a sentence with the correct word order after the teacher explains.
We add a missing full stop when the teacher points out where it should go.
We change 'runned' to 'ran' after the teacher corrects it.
We rewrite a sentence more neatly when the teacher asks.
We add a capital letter at the start of a name after the teacher shows us.

VC2EALBLW14.w: We are learning to use our home language to help with our English writing.
y: Our home language gives us ideas, knowledge and writing strategies to draw on.
Thinking in our language first can help us plan what to write in English.
Being multilingual is a strength that enriches our writing.
eg: We think about what we want to say in our language, then write it in English.
We use a bilingual dictionary or app to find the English word we need.
We plan our story in our home language before starting the English version.
We ask a bilingual friend to help us translate a key idea.
