We use our home language writing skills, like planning and paragraphing, in English.
We write about a topic we know well from our culture and translate key ideas.

VC2EALBLW15.w: We are learning to join in when the class writes together as a group.
y: Shared writing shows us how experienced writers make decisions.
Contributing our ideas helps us feel part of the writing community.
This is a supported way to practise writing before doing it on our own.
eg: We suggest a word or idea for the class story the teacher is writing.
We help choose what the next sentence in a shared text should be.
We offer a describing word when the teacher asks for one.
We contribute to a group brainstorm before the class starts writing.
We help decide on the title for a class information text.
We suggest how to end a shared story.

VC2EALB1L01.w: We are learning to listen carefully, noticing how someone's tone, rhythm and body language add meaning to their words.
y: Tone and body language carry a huge amount of information beyond the words themselves.
Picking up on these cues helps us understand messages more completely.
This makes us better communicators in English and in all our interactions.
eg: We notice the teacher's voice drops when they are being serious about a rule.
We see a classmate crossing their arms and understand they might be upset.
We hear excitement in someone's voice even before we fully understand all the words.
We watch the speaker's gestures during a presentation to follow their explanation.
We pick up on a sarcastic tone and realise the person means the opposite of what they said.
We notice a pause in someone's speech and understand they are thinking or uncertain.

VC2EALB1L02.w: We are learning to pick out some key pieces of information from short spoken texts with help.
y: Identifying specific information helps us answer questions and follow conversations.
We don't need to understand every word — just the key points.
This builds our listening skills for more complex texts later.
eg: We listen to a short talk and identify who or what it was about.
We catch the time and place mentioned in an announcement.
We listen to instructions and pick out the three main steps.
We hear a story and identify what happened at the beginning and end.
We listen to a weather report and find out tomorrow's forecast.
We identify the topic of a classroom discussion from the key words we hear.

VC2EALB1L03.w: We are learning to take part in simple conversations and daily class activities, relying on others to help us when we get stuck.
y: Participating in conversations is how we practise real English communication.
Having support from others means we can join in even while we are still learning.
Every conversation helps us build our confidence and skills.
eg: We join a class discussion and a classmate helps explain a word we don't know.
We take part in morning circle time, with the teacher rephrasing things for us.
We ask a partner to explain the instructions when we miss something.
We participate in a group activity and a friend clarifies what to do next.
We follow classroom routines by watching what others do when we are unsure.
We contribute to a conversation and the teacher restates our idea more clearly for the group.

VC2EALB1L04.w: We are learning to act out or retell short conversations and situations.
y: Role-playing helps us practise English in a fun, low-pressure way.
It builds our memory of useful phrases and sentence patterns.
Acting out conversations helps us prepare for real-life situations.
eg: We act out a conversation between a shopkeeper and a customer.
We role-play greeting a new student at school.
We practise a phone conversation with a partner.
We recreate a scene from a story we read in class.
We act out ordering food at a restaurant using polite phrases.
We role-play asking for directions and giving simple answers.

VC2EALB1L05.w: We are learning to say short phrases and sentences with the help of pictures, actions or the situation around us.
y: Context clues help us say more than our English vocabulary alone allows.
Short utterances are the building blocks for longer, more complex speech.
Using support makes it easier to communicate while our English grows.
eg: We look at a picture and say 'The boy is swimming in the pool'.
We point to our lunch and say 'I have a sandwich today'.
We describe what is happening outside the window using a few words.
We tell a partner about our drawing using short sentences.
We describe a character in a book using two or three phrases.
We use the classroom environment to help us say what we need.

VC2EALB1L06.w: We are learning to use joining words like 'and', 'but' and 'because' to connect our ideas.
y: Conjunctions link ideas together and make our speech flow better.
They help us explain reasons, add information and show contrasts.
Using these words helps us say more complex things in English.
eg: We say 'I like soccer and basketball' to list things we enjoy.
We explain 'I was late because the bus was slow'.
We say 'I wanted to play but it was raining' to show a contrast.
We use 'so' to explain a result: 'I was hungry so I ate my lunch'.
We connect ideas with 'then': 'We read a book then we did some writing'.
We say 'I like cats but my sister likes dogs' to compare.

VC2EALB1L07.w: We are learning to use a range of common action words correctly when we speak.
y: Using verbs accurately helps people understand what we are saying.
Getting familiar verb forms right shows our English is growing stronger.
Verbs carry the core meaning of every sentence.
eg: We say 'I went to the park' using the past tense correctly.
We use 'She is writing' for something happening right now.
We say 'They have finished their work' accurately.
We use 'We can play after lunch' with the right verb form.
We say 'He doesn't understand' instead of 'He don't understand'.
We use 'I was reading' to describe what we were doing earlier.

VC2EALB1L08.w: We are learning to use a small set of describing words and phrases when we speak.
y: Descriptive language helps us paint a clearer picture with our words.
Even a few well-chosen describing words make our speech much richer.
This helps listeners understand exactly what we mean.
eg: We describe a book as 'really interesting' or 'a bit boring'.
We say food is 'delicious', 'spicy' or 'too sweet'.
We describe the weather as 'freezing cold' or 'really hot'.
We tell someone a movie was 'exciting and funny'.
We describe a place as 'quiet and peaceful' or 'loud and busy'.
We say a task was 'quite easy' or 'really hard'.

VC2EALB1L09.w: We are learning to use important English words across different everyday situations.
y: Knowing key words helps us communicate in many different contexts.
The same words can be useful at school, at home and in the community.
A strong vocabulary is the foundation for everything else in English.
eg: We use words like 'explain', 'describe' and 'compare' in class discussions.
We use food and cooking words when talking about lunch or cooking activities.
We use time words like 'yesterday', 'tomorrow' and 'later' in conversations.
We use feeling words like 'nervous', 'proud' and 'disappointed' to express emotions.
We use direction words like 'left', 'right', 'next to' and 'between'.
We use action words for sport and play like 'throw', 'catch', 'kick' and 'score'.

VC2EALB1L10.w: We are learning to pronounce common words we have learned in class clearly.
y: Clear pronunciation helps our classmates and teachers understand us.
Practising words from class connects our speaking to our learning.
Confident pronunciation encourages us to speak up more often.
eg: We say vocabulary words from our current topic clearly and confidently.
We pronounce words from our spelling list accurately.
We say subject-specific words like 'experiment', 'fraction' or 'character' clearly.
We practise words that are tricky to pronounce, like 'through' and 'thought'.
We say words with silent letters correctly, like 'knife' and 'write'.
We read aloud from a text and try to pronounce every word clearly.

VC2EALB1L11.w: We are learning to use stress, intonation and body language to help get our message across.
y: These features add emotion, emphasis and clarity to what we say.
They help us express things that words alone might not fully communicate.
Using them well makes us more effective and engaging speakers.
eg: We stress the word 'never' when we say 'I have never been there' for emphasis.
We use a questioning intonation when asking 'Is this yours?'
We shake our head while saying 'No, that's not right' to reinforce meaning.
We use excited intonation when sharing good news.
We lean forward and speak more quietly to share something private.
We gesture with our hands to show the size or shape of something while describing it.

VC2EALB1L12.w: We are learning to notice what makes spoken English different from other languages.
y: Noticing differences helps us understand how English works.
It helps us avoid transferring patterns from our home language that don't work in English.
This awareness makes us more effective learners of English.
eg: We notice that English always puts the verb after the subject, unlike some languages.
We hear that English uses 'do' in questions: 'Do you like it?' which is unusual.
We compare the sound systems and notice English has sounds our language doesn't.
We see that English uses articles ('a', 'the') while our language might not.
We notice English word stress patterns are different from our language.
We compare how plurals work in English versus our home language.

VC2EALB1L13.w: We are learning to ask people to repeat or explain things when we don't understand.
y: Asking for clarification is a sign of a smart, active learner.
It prevents misunderstandings and helps us learn new things.
Everyone needs to ask for help sometimes — it is a normal part of communication.
eg: We say 'Sorry, can you say that again?' when we miss something.
We ask 'What does that word mean?' when we hear an unfamiliar word.
We say 'Do you mean...?' to check our understanding.
We ask the teacher 'Can you explain that in a different way?'
We request help by saying 'I don't understand — can you show me?'
We ask a partner to speak more slowly so we can follow.

VC2EALB1L14.w: We are learning to use everything we know from our home language to boost our listening and speaking in English.
y: Our home language is a powerful resource for learning English.
Skills and knowledge from our first language transfer to English in many ways.
Using our full language repertoire makes us stronger learners overall.
eg: We use what we know about a topic in our language to understand the English lesson.
We compare sentence structures between our language and English.
We recognise a cognate — a word that sounds similar in both languages.
We use our home language to plan what we want to say before speaking in English.
We draw on listening strategies we use in our home language.
We think about the meaning in our language first, then express it in English.

VC2EALB1L15.w: We are learning to listen and speak at the right times during small-group work.
y: Good group skills help us learn from others and share our ideas.
Knowing when to listen and when to speak makes group work effective.
These skills are important for learning at school and for life beyond school.
eg: We wait for our turn to speak during a group discussion.
We listen carefully to what a group member says before responding.
We add to someone else's idea by saying 'I agree, and I also think...'
We take turns being the speaker and the listener in a pair activity.
We make sure everyone in the group gets a chance to share their thoughts.
We stay quiet and focused when a group member is presenting their work.

VC2EALB1R01.w: We are learning to use key words to figure out the main idea in simple texts with predictable language.
y: Finding the main idea is the most important reading comprehension skill.
Key words act as signposts that point us towards the central meaning.
Predictable texts build our confidence to tackle more challenging reading later.
eg: We read a simple information text and use the key words to state the topic.
We look for repeated words in a text to identify what it is mainly about.
We read a story with familiar vocabulary and summarise it in one sentence.
We scan a text for key words before reading it fully to get the gist.
We use the heading and key words together to predict and confirm the main idea.
We tell a partner the main idea of a simple text using our own words.

VC2EALB1R02.w: We are learning to find information in short, simple texts with our teacher's help.
y: Finding information in texts is essential for all our school work.
With teacher support, we can access texts that would be too hard on our own.
This skill prepares us to research and learn independently.
eg: We find the answer to a question by scanning a short text.
We locate a character's name and what they did in a simple story.
We find a specific fact in a short information text with the teacher guiding us.
We use the teacher's questions to know what information to look for.
We read a table or chart and extract a piece of data.
We find the who, what and where in a short recount.

VC2EALB1R03.w: We are learning to get information from pictures, diagrams and other images with help.
y: Images often carry essential information that supports the written text.
Learning to read images helps us across all subject areas.
Many texts combine words and images — we need to read both to understand fully.
eg: We look at a labelled diagram and explain what it shows.
We study a photograph and describe the information it gives us.
We read a simple map and find places on it with the teacher's help.
We look at a graph and say what the tallest bar represents.
We use pictures in a story to understand what is happening.
We extract information from a poster by reading both the images and the words.

VC2EALB1R04.w: We are learning to predict what will happen next in a text or work out things the author hints at.
y: Making predictions keeps our brain active and engaged while reading.
Inferring hidden meaning helps us understand texts more deeply.
These skills make reading more interesting and help us comprehend more.
eg: We predict the ending of a story based on what has happened so far.
We infer that a character is scared because they are described as shaking.
We guess what a new chapter will be about based on the title.
We predict what information will come next in a report.
We work out why a character did something even though the text doesn't say directly.
We predict the meaning of a new word from the sentences around it.

VC2EALB1R05.w: We are learning to compare different types of texts and spot what makes them different.
y: Understanding text types helps us read each one in the most effective way.
Comparing texts builds our knowledge of how English writing works.
Knowing the differences helps us write our own texts in the right format.
eg: We compare a story with an information text and list the differences.
We notice a recipe has a list of ingredients but a story does not.
We see that a letter starts with 'Dear...' but a report starts with a heading.
We compare a poem and a recount and talk about how they are different.
We notice that instructions use command verbs but stories use past tense.
We compare how a newspaper article and a fairy tale are structured.

VC2EALB1R06.w: We are learning what different text types are for and how they are organised.
y: Knowing the purpose of a text helps us understand why it was written.
Understanding how texts are structured helps us find information efficiently.
This knowledge helps us read and write different text types confidently.
eg: We know a procedure is written to tell someone how to do something.
We see that an information report has a title, introduction and body paragraphs.
We understand that a narrative is written to entertain with a story.
We notice a letter has a greeting, body and closing.
We see that a recount tells events in time order.
We recognise that a persuasive text tries to convince us of something.

VC2EALB1R07.w: We are learning to follow the order of key words, phrases and ideas in texts we know.
y: Understanding sequence helps us follow the flow of a text.
Knowing the order of ideas helps us retell and summarise what we read.
This is essential for understanding stories, instructions and recounts.
eg: We retell a story in the right order using key words from the text.
We put sentence strips in the correct sequence to match the original text.
We identify the first, middle and last events in a story.
We follow the steps of a procedure in the order they are written.
We track how a character changes from the beginning to the end of a story.
We number key phrases in the order they appear in the text.

VC2EALB1R08.w: We are learning to read sentences with a subject, verb and object when the words are familiar.
y: These simple sentence patterns are the most common in English.
Reading them fluently builds the foundation for more complex sentences.
Understanding this pattern helps us comprehend what we read.
eg: We read 'The girl kicked the ball' and understand who did what.
We identify the subject, verb and object in 'The teacher reads a book'.
We read 'We ate our lunch' and picture the action.
We practise reading sentences like 'The dog chased the cat' quickly and smoothly.
We identify who is doing the action in 'My friend draws pictures'.
We read sentences from our class texts and point to each part.

VC2EALB1R09.w: We are learning to spot present tense and past tense verbs when we read.
y: Recognising tenses tells us when things are happening in a text.
It helps us follow timelines in stories and recounts.
This grammar awareness improves both our reading and writing.
eg: We spot '-ed' endings like 'walked' and 'played' and know they are past tense.
We recognise 'is running' as present tense — happening now.
We see 'goes' and know it describes a regular action in the present.
We find 'was' and 'were' and know the text is talking about the past.
We highlight present and past tense verbs in different colours in a text.
We notice when a story switches from past to present tense.

VC2EALB1R10.w: We are learning to recognise and read some English words and phrases we have seen before.
y: Reading familiar words quickly helps us focus on understanding the text.
Each word we can recognise on sight builds our reading speed and confidence.
A strong sight vocabulary is the foundation for fluent reading.
eg: We read words like 'said', 'were', 'their' and 'people' without sounding them out.
We recognise classroom vocabulary like 'homework', 'pencil' and 'library'.
We read familiar phrases like 'once upon a time' straight away.
We spot words we practised in spelling activities in a new text.
We read instructions on worksheets because we know the key words.
We recognise subject words like 'science', 'music' and 'art' around the school.

VC2EALB1R11.w: We are learning to spot common syllable patterns and word parts to help us read.
y: Syllable patterns help us break big words into manageable chunks.
Recognising patterns means we can read new words more easily.
This is a powerful strategy for tackling unfamiliar English words.
eg: We break 'hap-py' into two syllables to read it.
We spot the '-tion' pattern in 'action', 'station' and 'nation'.
We recognise 'un-' at the start of 'unhappy' and know it means 'not'.
We see the '-ing' ending and know someone is doing something right now.
We break 'to-geth-er' into three syllables to read it.
We notice the '-ful' ending in 'helpful', 'careful' and 'beautiful'.

VC2EALB1R12.w: We are learning why spaces, capital letters and full stops matter in reading.
y: These conventions help us break text into meaningful units.
Understanding them helps us read more fluently and with correct phrasing.
They are the basic building blocks of how English writing is organised.
eg: We use spaces to identify individual words in a line of text.
We see a capital letter and know a new sentence or a proper noun is starting.
We pause at a full stop and take a breath before the next sentence.
We notice capital letters in names like 'Melbourne', 'Australia' and 'Mr Smith'.
We know that 'I' is always a capital letter in English.
We use these features to read aloud with correct pauses and expression.

VC2EALB1R13.w: We are learning to notice important differences between English texts and texts in other languages.
y: Understanding these differences helps us read English more effectively.
It builds our awareness of how different languages organise writing.
This knowledge makes us more thoughtful and flexible readers.
eg: We notice that English texts read from left to right, but some languages go right to left.
We compare how English and our home language use punctuation differently.
We see that English uses spaces between words, which some languages do not.
We notice that English books have the cover on the left side.
We compare the alphabet in English with the writing system of our language.
We talk about how paragraphs and text layout might differ between languages.

VC2EALB1R14.w: We are learning to choose good books for ourselves to read with some help from our teacher.
y: Choosing appropriate books helps us enjoy reading and learn at the right pace.
It develops our independence and helps us know ourselves as readers.
The right book keeps us challenged without being too frustrating.
eg: We browse the class library and pick a book we think we can read.
We use the 'five finger test' to check if a book is at the right level.
We ask the teacher to suggest books on a topic we are interested in.
We choose a book from our reading level box.
We pick a book by an author we have enjoyed before.
We select a book with illustrations that help us understand the text.

VC2EALB1R15.w: We are learning to use our home language knowledge and skills to help us read English.
y: Reading skills from our home language support our English reading development.
Our multilingual knowledge is an asset that speeds up our learning.
Connecting languages strengthens our understanding of both.
eg: We use our knowledge of a topic from our home language to understand an English text.
We apply reading strategies like predicting that we learned in our first language.
We connect a concept we know from home to what we are reading in English.
We use our vocabulary knowledge from our language to guess the meaning of English words.
We think about how stories are structured in our language to follow English stories.
We discuss a text in our home language to deepen our understanding.

VC2EALB1R16.w: We are learning to join in teacher-led shared reading and connect the stories to our own life.
y: Shared reading gives us a model of fluent, expressive English reading.
Connecting stories to our own experiences makes them more meaningful.
This builds our reading skills in a supported, enjoyable way.
eg: We listen as the teacher reads a story aloud and join in with familiar parts.
We share how a character's experience is similar to something that happened to us.
We read along with the class during a shared big book reading.
We talk about how a story reminds us of something from our family or culture.
We predict what will happen based on our own experience with similar situations.
We retell a part of the story in our own words during the discussion.

VC2EALB1W01.w: We are learning to write personal texts about real things in our life with help.
y: Writing about our own life gives us meaningful things to say in English.
Personal writing connects our experiences to our English skills.
This kind of writing helps us practise expressing who we are in a new language.
eg: We write a short recount about what we did on the weekend.
We write a description of our family with help from the teacher.
We write about a favourite memory from before we came to this school.
We write a short 'All about me' text with our name, age and interests.
We write about something that makes us happy or proud.
We write a message to a family member in English.

VC2EALB1W02.w: We are learning to write simple information texts for different class activities with help.
y: Writing information texts helps us organise and share what we learn.
These texts are useful across all subjects at school.
Learning to write clearly about facts is a skill we will use our whole life.
eg: We write a short report about an animal we researched.
We write a procedure for a science experiment we did in class.
We write labels and captions for a classroom display.
We write a simple summary of a topic we studied this term.
We write facts about our local area for a class project.
We write a list of materials needed for an activity.

VC2EALB1W03.w: We are learning to write simple stories and creative texts by following familiar patterns with help.
y: Creative writing lets us use our imagination in English.
Following patterns gives us a structure so we can focus on our ideas.
Writing stories helps us understand how narratives work in English.
eg: We write a story using the pattern 'Once upon a time... One day... In the end...'
We create a character and write a short adventure about them.
We write a retelling of a fairy tale in our own words.
We follow a story template to write about a problem and how it was solved.
We write a simple poem following a pattern we learned in class.
We write an imaginative recount about a day we were a superhero.

VC2EALB1W04.w: We are learning to use pictures and drawings alongside our written sentences to tell a story or recount.
y: Images support our writing and help readers understand our meaning.
Drawing is a way to express ideas we might not yet have the English words for.
Combining pictures and words is how many real-world texts work.
eg: We draw a sequence of pictures for our recount and write a sentence under each one.
We illustrate the main event of our story and write sentences to go with it.
We create a picture timeline of our day and add written descriptions.
We draw a map for our narrative and label the important places.
We add illustrations to a class story to show what is happening.
We use pictures to help plan our writing before we start the sentences.

VC2EALB1W05.w: We are learning to organise our writing into paragraphs with topic sentences, using patterns the teacher has shown us.
y: Paragraphs help organise our ideas so readers can follow our writing.
Topic sentences tell the reader what each paragraph is about.
Using modelled patterns gives us a framework to build on.
eg: We start a new paragraph for each new main idea in our writing.
We write a topic sentence like 'There are many types of birds in Australia' to begin a paragraph.
We follow the teacher's model to write an introduction, body and conclusion.
We use a planning template to organise our ideas into paragraphs.
We write a paragraph about each aspect of an animal: appearance, habitat and diet.
We look at how the teacher's example text is organised and do the same.

VC2EALB1W06.w: We are learning to put our ideas in a logical order using short sentences.
y: Ordering ideas logically makes our writing easy to follow.
Short, clear sentences help readers understand one point at a time.
Good sequencing is the foundation of all effective writing.
eg: We write the steps of an activity in the order they happened.
We use 'First', 'Next', 'Then' and 'Finally' to sequence our ideas.
We write a recount with events in time order.
We number our ideas in a plan before writing them as sentences.
We write three sentences about a topic, each building on the previous one.
We arrange our ideas from most important to least important.

VC2EALB1W07.w: We are learning to use common action words in the right form in our writing.
y: Correct verb forms make our writing clear and easy to understand.
They tell the reader when things happened and who did them.
Mastering common verbs is a big milestone in English writing.
eg: We write 'She played' instead of 'She play' for past events.
We use 'I am learning' correctly for something happening now.
We write 'They went home' using the irregular past tense.
We use 'He has finished' to show something is complete.
We write 'We will visit the museum' for future plans.
We check our writing to make sure our verb forms match the time of the events.

VC2EALB1W08.w: We are learning to use a small range of simple describing phrases in our writing.
y: Describing phrases add detail and interest to our writing.
They help our reader visualise what we are writing about.
Using even a few good descriptions can make writing much stronger.
eg: We write 'the tall, green tree' instead of just 'the tree'.
We describe a character as 'a kind old man with grey hair'.
We write 'the noisy, crowded playground' to set the scene.
We describe our lunch as 'a warm, tasty bowl of soup'.
We use 'slowly and carefully' to describe how someone did something.
We describe the weather as 'a bright, sunny morning'.

VC2EALB1W09.w: We are learning to use new vocabulary we have been taught in our writing.
y: Using new words in writing helps us remember and really learn them.
It makes our writing more precise and interesting.
Applying taught vocabulary shows our English is growing.
eg: We use science vocabulary like 'habitat', 'observe' and 'predict' in a report.
We include maths words like 'estimate', 'measure' and 'compare' in our work.
We use words from our class novel in our own creative writing.
We apply topic words like 'community', 'culture' and 'tradition' in a project.
We use new adjectives the teacher taught us in a descriptive paragraph.
We include words from our vocabulary list in our weekly writing.

VC2EALB1W10.w: We are learning to spell common words we have learned in class correctly.
y: Accurate spelling makes our writing professional and easy to read.
Spelling common words correctly frees us to focus on our ideas.
This builds strong foundations for spelling more complex words later.
eg: We spell high-frequency words like 'because', 'different' and 'through' correctly.
We use our class word wall to check our spelling.
We spell topic-specific words we have studied and practised.
We write our spelling words in sentences to show we know them.
We check our writing for common words and fix any misspellings.
We use strategies like 'look, cover, write, check' to learn spellings.

VC2EALB1W11.w: We are learning to use spaces, full stops and capital letters correctly in our writing.
y: Basic punctuation makes our writing readable and well-organised.
Getting these right shows we understand how English sentences work.
This is essential for all writing we do at school and beyond.
eg: We leave clear spaces between every word in our writing.
We start every sentence with a capital letter.
We put a full stop at the end of every sentence.
We use capital letters for people's names and place names.
We always write 'I' as a capital letter.
We proofread our writing to check these three things are correct.

VC2EALB1W12.w: We are learning to write using language that sounds like the way we speak.
y: Writing the way we talk is a natural starting point for building writing skills.
It helps us get our ideas down without worrying too much about formal rules.
As our English grows, our writing will become more formal over time.
eg: We write 'I went to my friend's house and we played games' the way we would say it.
We use simple, natural sentences in our recounts.
We write dialogue the way people actually talk.
We describe events using everyday language we are comfortable with.
We write a journal entry using the same language we would use to tell a friend.
We express our opinions the way we would say them out loud.

VC2EALB1W13.w: We are learning to improve our writing after getting feedback from our teacher.
y: Revising after feedback is how all writers get better.
It teaches us to see our own mistakes and fix them.
Each correction helps us learn something new about English writing.
eg: We fix spelling errors the teacher has identified.
We rewrite a sentence with better word order after the teacher explains.
We add a missing full stop or capital letter after being prompted.
We expand a sentence with more detail after a teacher-student conference.
We correct a verb form after the teacher models the right one.
We read through our whole piece and fix errors we discussed with the teacher.

VC2EALB1W14.w: We are learning to use our home language to support and strengthen our English writing.
y: Our home language gives us ideas, structures and vocabulary to draw on.
Thinking in our language first can help us organise our English writing.
Multilingual writers have a rich pool of resources to work with.
eg: We brainstorm ideas for our writing in our home language first.
We use a bilingual dictionary to find the exact English word we want.
We plan the structure of our text in our language before writing in English.
We discuss our writing topic with a peer who speaks our language.
We draw on a story or experience from our culture as a writing topic.
We compare how writing is structured in our language and English to learn from both.

VC2EALB1W15.w: We are learning to share our ideas when the class writes together.
y: Contributing to shared writing gives us practice in a supportive environment.
Our ideas add to the richness of the class text.
Seeing our suggestions written down builds our confidence as writers.
eg: We suggest a descriptive word for the teacher to add to the class story.
We offer an idea for what should happen next in the group narrative.
We suggest a topic sentence for a class information text.
We contribute a fact we learned for a shared report.
We help choose the best word from several options during shared writing.
We suggest how to improve a sentence the class has drafted.

VC2EALB2L01.w: We are learning to understand the main idea or theme of something someone says, with help from our teacher.
y: Understanding the main idea helps us follow spoken texts and conversations.
It lets us participate in discussions even when we miss some details.
This skill is essential for learning across all subjects at school.
eg: We listen to a short talk and say the topic in our own words.
We identify the theme of a story the teacher reads aloud.
We summarise the main point of an announcement after hearing it.
We listen to a classmate's presentation and state what it was about.
We hear a discussion and identify what the group was trying to decide.
We tell a partner the key message of a spoken explanation.

VC2EALB2L02.w: We are learning to pick out key pieces of information from short spoken texts.
y: Identifying key points helps us remember and use the most important information.
This skill helps us take notes and recall what we heard.
It prepares us for listening in more complex situations.
eg: We listen to a set of instructions and note down the three main steps.
We hear a story and identify the main events.
We listen to a report about an animal and remember its habitat and diet.
We identify who, what, where and when from a short spoken recount.
We hear a news update and pick out the most important fact.
We listen to a science explanation and name the key concept.

VC2EALB2L03.w: We are learning to start and keep short conversations going at school, using others to help us understand when needed.
y: Starting conversations shows growing confidence in English.
Conversation partners help us learn natural English in real time.
This builds our social skills and our language at the same time.
eg: We start a conversation with a classmate about a shared activity.
We ask follow-up questions to keep a chat going.
We check with a partner by saying 'Do you mean...?' when we're unsure.
We talk to the teacher about our work using classroom language.
We chat with friends during recess using our growing English skills.
We ask a peer to explain a tricky word during a conversation.

VC2EALB2L04.w: We are learning to act out, retell or present stories and recounts to others.
y: Retelling stories helps us practise vocabulary, grammar and narrative structure.
Role-playing makes learning English fun and memorable.
Presenting to others builds our confidence in spoken English.
eg: We retell a fairy tale to a partner using sequence words.
We role-play a scene from a class story with a group.
We present a 'show and tell' about a special object from home.
We retell a weekend event to the class in the right order.
We act out a legend or fable with characters and dialogue.
We use puppets to retell a familiar story to younger students.

VC2EALB2L05.w: We are learning to recognise and use the main features of spoken texts like stories, explanations and recounts.
y: Knowing how spoken texts are structured helps us follow and create them.
Different spoken texts have different features — recognising them makes listening easier.
This knowledge helps us speak in an organised way for different purposes.
eg: We recognise that a recount tells events in order using time words.
We notice that an explanation uses cause-and-effect language.
We hear a story and identify the beginning, problem and resolution.
We use the right structure when we give an oral report to the class.
We notice that instructions use command verbs at the beginning of sentences.
We recognise the greeting and sign-off structure in a spoken conversation.

VC2EALB2L06.w: We are learning to use time words and sequence markers like 'first', 'after that' and 'finally' when we speak.
y: Time markers help our listeners follow the order of our ideas.
They make our spoken recounts and explanations clear and logical.
Using these words shows we can organise our thinking in English.
eg: We tell a story using 'first', 'then', 'next' and 'finally'.
We use 'before that' and 'after that' to explain when things happened.
We say 'At the beginning' and 'In the end' when retelling a story.
We use 'yesterday', 'last week' and 'this morning' to talk about time.
We explain steps in a process using 'first you need to... then you...'
We use 'meanwhile' when telling about things happening at the same time.

VC2EALB2L07.w: We are learning to use different verb forms more and more accurately when we speak.
y: Accurate verb use makes our speaking clearer and more natural.
It helps us express when things happen — past, present, future and ongoing.
This is one of the most important grammar skills in English.
eg: We use past tense correctly: 'We visited the zoo yesterday'.
We say 'I have been to the library' using present perfect.
We use continuous tense: 'She was playing when the bell rang'.
We say 'We are going to make a poster' for future plans.
We use 'could' and 'would' correctly in sentences.
We self-correct when we notice we used the wrong verb form.

VC2EALB2L08.w: We are learning to use simple describing phrases to add detail to what we say.
y: Descriptive phrases make our speaking more vivid and precise.
They help our listeners picture exactly what we are talking about.
Adding description shows growing control of English.
eg: We describe a place as 'a really busy market with lots of colourful stalls'.
We say 'the small brown dog with floppy ears' instead of just 'the dog'.
We describe our weekend as 'an exciting trip to the beach with my family'.
We use phrases like 'quite difficult' or 'really interesting' to express opinions.
We describe the weather as 'a cold, windy afternoon'.
We say 'she spoke very quietly and carefully' to describe someone's speech.

VC2EALB2L09.w: We are learning to use a small range of describing words to make our speaking more specific.
y: Having a bank of descriptive vocabulary makes our communication richer.
Specific words are more effective than general ones.
Building vocabulary is a continuous process that makes English easier over time.
eg: We use 'enormous' instead of 'big' and 'tiny' instead of 'small'.
We describe food with words like 'crunchy', 'creamy', 'sour' and 'spicy'.
We use emotion words like 'anxious', 'grateful', 'frustrated' and 'relieved'.
We describe textures like 'rough', 'smooth', 'soft' and 'sticky'.
We use words like 'ancient', 'modern', 'traditional' and 'unusual' for describing things.
We try using new adjectives we have learned in class during conversations.

VC2EALB2L10.w: We are learning to pronounce common words and key topic words clearly.
y: Clear pronunciation of topic words is important for school discussions.
Being understood gives us confidence to participate more in class.
Good pronunciation helps us connect our spoken and written vocabulary.
eg: We pronounce topic words like 'ecosystem', 'multiplication' and 'parliament' correctly.
We say common words like 'different', 'important' and 'actually' with clear pronunciation.
We practise words with tricky English sounds like 'th' in 'through'.
We say words with multiple syllables correctly by breaking them down first.
We use correct stress patterns: 'PHOtograph' versus 'phoTOGraphy'.
We check our pronunciation of new words with the teacher before using them.

VC2EALB2L11.w: We are learning to use stress, intonation and body language to make our communication clearer and more engaging.
y: These features help us express emotion, emphasis and meaning beyond words.
They make our speaking more natural and easier for others to follow.
Mastering these aspects helps us communicate like confident English speakers.
eg: We stress key words to highlight important information in a sentence.
We use intonation to show the difference between a question and a statement.
We gesture to show size, direction or location while speaking.
We vary our volume and speed when telling a story for dramatic effect.
We use facial expressions to reinforce how we feel about something.
We pause before an important point to build emphasis.

VC2EALB2L12.w: We are learning to identify what is similar and different between spoken English and our home language.
y: Comparing languages deepens our understanding of how both work.
Knowing the differences helps us avoid common mistakes in English.
Recognising similarities helps us learn English faster by building on what we know.
eg: We notice that both English and our language use intonation for questions, but in different ways.
We compare how past tense is formed in our language and in English.
We see that some sounds exist in both languages, making them easier to produce.
We notice that word order might differ between the two languages.
We compare polite language forms in English and our home language.
We discuss with classmates how different languages express the same idea.

VC2EALB2L13.w: We are learning to notice and fix errors in our spoken English that stop people understanding us.
y: Self-correction is a sign of growing language awareness.
Fixing communication breakdowns helps conversations flow smoothly.
Monitoring our own speech helps us improve faster.
eg: We catch ourselves saying the wrong verb form and correct it mid-sentence.
We notice a pronunciation error and try the word again more clearly.
We rephrase a sentence when we see the listener looks confused.
We slow down and speak more clearly when someone asks us to repeat.
We notice we used the wrong word and quickly choose a better one.
We practise correcting errors the teacher has pointed out in previous conversations.

VC2EALB2L14.w: We are learning to draw on everything we know from our home language to strengthen our English listening and speaking.
y: Our home language knowledge is a rich resource for learning English.
Drawing on both languages makes us more effective communicators.
Multilingual learners have unique advantages in understanding language.
eg: We use our home language understanding of a topic to follow an English discussion.
We translate a key idea in our head to help us understand a complex English sentence.
We use grammar patterns from our language that also work in English.
We draw on storytelling skills from our culture when speaking in English.
We use our vocabulary from both languages to express ideas more precisely.
We compare how we would say something in our language versus English.

VC2EALB2L15.w: We are learning to contribute effectively to small-group work and learning activities.
y: Group work is where a lot of important learning happens at school.
Contributing our ideas helps us and our group learn more.
Collaboration skills are important in school and in future careers.
eg: We share our ideas during a group discussion and listen to others.
We take on a role in a group project, like note-taker or presenter.
We help the group stay on task by reminding everyone of the goal.
We ask quieter group members for their opinion to include everyone.
We summarise what the group has decided to make sure everyone agrees.
We offer feedback on a group member's idea in a respectful way.

VC2EALB2R01.w: We are learning to identify the main idea in short, simple texts with some help from our teacher.
y: The main idea is the most important point the author wants us to understand.
Finding it helps us summarise and discuss texts confidently.
This is a core reading skill we will use in every subject.
eg: We read a short article and state the main idea in one sentence.
We identify the topic sentence that tells us what a paragraph is about.
We distinguish the main idea from supporting details in a text.
We compare the main ideas of two different texts on the same topic.
We use headings and topic sentences to find the main idea quickly.
We tell a partner the main idea of a text we just read.

VC2EALB2R02.w: We are learning to find and pull out key information from different types of texts with some help.
y: Extracting information is essential for learning and research.
Working across different text types builds our reading flexibility.
This skill helps us answer questions and complete tasks using texts.
eg: We find specific facts in an information text about a historical event.
We locate key details in a story, like the setting and the problem.
We extract data from a table or chart to answer questions.
We find the steps in a procedure text.
We scan a text for a specific piece of information the teacher asks about.
We read a biography and extract key facts about the person's life.

VC2EALB2R03.w: We are learning to get key information from different types of images in texts with some help.
y: Images like maps, diagrams, graphs and photos all carry important information.
Reading images alongside text gives us a fuller understanding.
This skill is increasingly important in our visual, digital world.
eg: We read a bar graph and identify which category has the most.
We study a diagram of a life cycle and explain the stages.
We look at a map and find specific locations mentioned in the text.
We use photographs in a text to gather additional information.
We read a flow chart and explain the process it shows.
We compare information from an image with what the written text says.

VC2EALB2R04.w: We are learning to make predictions and inferences about texts and back them up with evidence.
y: Supporting predictions with evidence shows deeper thinking about texts.
Inferring meaning goes beyond what is literally written on the page.
These skills make us more critical, thoughtful readers.
eg: We predict what will happen next and explain why using clues from the text.
We infer a character's feelings from their actions and say what made us think that.
We predict the content of a text from its title and images, then check if we were right.
We use evidence from the text to support an inference about a character's motivation.
We infer the author's purpose and point to details that support our thinking.
We make a prediction about the ending and compare it with what actually happens.

VC2EALB2R05.w: We are learning to recognise when a text is trying to inform, entertain or persuade us.
y: Every text has a purpose — knowing it helps us read with the right mindset.
This awareness helps us think critically about what we read and hear.
Recognising purpose is a key step towards becoming a critical reader.
eg: We read an article about animals and say 'This is to inform us'.
We listen to a story and recognise it is meant to entertain.
We see an advertisement and understand it is trying to persuade us to buy something.
We compare a news report (informative) with an editorial (persuasive).
We read a book review and identify its persuasive purpose.
We sort a collection of texts into informative, imaginative and persuasive groups.

VC2EALB2R06.w: We are learning what common text types are for and how they are put together.
y: Knowing a text's purpose and structure helps us read it more effectively.
It prepares us to write our own texts in the right format.
This knowledge applies across all subjects and all kinds of reading.
eg: We know a procedure lists steps in order to explain how to do something.
We recognise a narrative has an orientation, complication and resolution.
We understand an information report uses paragraphs with topic sentences.
We see that a persuasive text has an argument supported by reasons.
We identify a recount by its chronological structure.
We notice that different text types use different language features.

VC2EALB2R07.w: We are learning to understand how events and ideas in a text are connected to each other.
y: Understanding connections between ideas helps us comprehend texts more deeply.
It helps us see cause and effect, sequence and comparison in texts.
This skill is essential for understanding complex information.
eg: We see that one event caused another in a story.
We understand that a character's decision led to a specific consequence.
We notice how paragraphs connect through linking words like 'however' and 'therefore'.
We follow the logical sequence of an explanation text.
We see how an author compares two things in an information text.
We trace how a problem develops and is resolved in a narrative.

VC2EALB2R08.w: We are learning to read sentences that have more than one part joined by words like 'and', 'but', 'because' or 'when'.
y: Complex sentences carry more information and are common in school texts.
Reading them fluently is important for understanding academic language.
This builds our ability to handle more challenging reading.
eg: We read 'I wanted to go outside, but it was raining' and understand both parts.
We follow a sentence like 'When the bell rings, we pack up our work'.
We understand 'She was tired because she stayed up late reading'.
We read 'The animals that live in the desert need very little water'.
We follow 'Although it was cold, we decided to walk to school'.
We read texts with a mix of short and long sentences and understand them all.

VC2EALB2R09.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of tenses and negative words to understand what texts mean.
y: Tenses tell us when things happen, and negation changes meaning completely.
Understanding these grammar features prevents misunderstanding texts.
This is a key skill for reading accurately in English.
eg: We read 'She did not agree' and understand the negative meaning.
We notice 'They will visit' is about the future, not the past.
We understand that 'He never goes' means it does not happen at all.
We read 'We were not allowed to run' and know running was prohibited.
We interpret 'hasn't finished yet' as meaning the work is still in progress.
We distinguish 'She walked' (past) from 'She is walking' (now).

VC2EALB2R10.w: We are learning to read some common words and familiar phrases quickly and easily.
y: Instant word recognition makes reading faster and more enjoyable.
These words are the building blocks of almost every text.
Reading them automatically lets us focus on understanding the meaning.
eg: We read words like 'always', 'another', 'together' and 'different' instantly.
We recognise phrases like 'a long time ago' or 'at the end of' when reading.
We read classroom vocabulary like 'paragraph', 'sentence' and 'chapter' quickly.
We spot familiar phrases in a new text and use them to understand the context.
We read subject words like 'geography', 'experiment' and 'fraction' without pausing.
We recognise high-frequency words in texts across different subjects.

VC2EALB2R11.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of how sounds and letters work together to read new words, with some help.
y: Sound-letter knowledge is our main tool for decoding unfamiliar words.
This skill helps us become more independent readers.
The more patterns we know, the fewer words will be hard to read.
eg: We use our knowledge of 'ough' patterns to read 'thought' and 'through'.
We sound out an unfamiliar word by breaking it into syllables.
We recognise the 'tion' ending and read 'information' and 'education'.
We use known word parts to read new compound words like 'underground'.
We apply our knowledge of silent letters to read 'island' and 'listen'.
We read a multi-syllable word by finding smaller words or familiar parts inside it.

VC2EALB2R12.w: We are learning how punctuation marks help us understand what we read.
y: Punctuation guides how we read and understand sentences.
It tells us when to pause, when to be excited and when something is a question.
Understanding punctuation improves both our reading fluency and comprehension.
eg: We pause at commas and stop at full stops when reading aloud.
We use question marks to read questions with the right intonation.
We understand that speech marks show someone is talking.
We see an exclamation mark and know to read the sentence with feeling.
We notice a comma in a list and read each item separately.
We see an apostrophe and understand it shows possession or a contraction.

VC2EALB2R13.w: We are learning to compare English texts with texts from other languages and cultures to find what is similar and different.
y: Comparing texts across languages builds deep understanding of how texts work.
It helps us appreciate different cultural approaches to communication.
This awareness enriches our reading in both languages.
eg: We compare a traditional story from our culture with an English fairy tale.
We notice how letter formats differ between English and our home language.
We compare how information texts are organised in different languages.
We discuss how the same story might be told differently in our culture.
We compare the use of images in English texts versus texts from home.
We notice that some text types exist in both cultures but have different features.

VC2EALB2R14.w: We are learning to choose and read texts we haven't read before with some help from our teacher.
y: Reading new texts builds our skills and exposes us to new vocabulary and ideas.
Choosing our own reading helps us become independent, motivated readers.
With some support, we can tackle more challenging texts.
eg: We choose a new book from the school library on a topic that interests us.
We try reading a text from a different genre we haven't explored before.
We select an unfamiliar information text and read it with some teacher guidance.
We pick a book that is slightly harder than our usual level to challenge ourselves.
We read a new text and ask the teacher about words or ideas we don't understand.
We explore different sections of a newspaper or magazine.

VC2EALB2R15.w: We are learning to use all our home language knowledge and skills to help with reading English.
y: Our home language reading skills directly support our English reading development.
Drawing on both languages gives us more tools for understanding texts.
Multilingual readers have advantages that monolingual readers do not.
eg: We use comprehension strategies we learned in our home language when reading English.
We connect what we know about a topic from reading in our language to an English text.
We compare how our language and English handle similar types of texts.
We use our home language vocabulary to help guess the meaning of English words.
We translate a key paragraph in our head to make sure we understand.
We discuss a tricky English text with someone in our home language.

VC2EALB2R16.w: We are learning to participate in shared reading and share our personal thoughts about stories and texts.
y: Sharing our responses to texts helps us think more deeply about what we read.
Personal connections make reading more meaningful and enjoyable.
Discussing texts with others exposes us to different perspectives.
eg: We share what we liked about a story during a class discussion.
We say how a character reminded us of someone we know.
We contribute our opinion about the best part of a text.
We tell the class what surprised us in the story.
We connect a theme in the book to something we have experienced.
We explain which character we related to and why.

VC2EALB2W01.w: We are learning to write simple personal texts that express our opinion or point of view.
y: Writing our opinions helps us develop our voice as writers.
It teaches us to express what we think clearly in English.
Persuasive and personal writing are skills we use throughout life.
eg: We write a short piece about why our favourite sport is the best.
We write a review of a book we read, saying what we liked and didn't like.
We write a letter to the teacher explaining why we think something should change.
We write about our favourite school subject and give reasons.
We write an opinion piece about a topic we discussed in class.
We write about why a certain rule is fair or unfair.

VC2EALB2W02.w: We are learning to write simple information texts by following models and examples from class.
y: Model texts show us how good information writing is structured.
Following examples helps us learn the right format while building our skills.
Writing information texts teaches us to communicate facts clearly.
eg: We follow a model to write an information report about a country.
We use the structure of a class example to write about an animal.
We write a procedure for a science experiment, following the format we studied.
We write a description of our school based on a model text.
We use a template to write a short biography of a famous person.
We follow a class example to write a compare-and-contrast text.

VC2EALB2W03.w: We are learning to write stories and creative texts by following examples and models from class.
y: Following models helps us understand how stories are built in English.
Creative writing lets us use our imagination and explore new ideas.
Model texts give us a safe framework while we develop our own style.
eg: We write a story using the same structure as a model narrative: orientation, problem, resolution.
We create a character based on techniques we saw in a model story.
We write a poem following the pattern of one we studied in class.
We write a fairy tale retelling using the structure from the original.
We base our story on a familiar tale but change the setting or characters.
We use descriptive techniques from a model text in our own creative writing.

VC2EALB2W04.w: We are learning to add pictures, diagrams and other visuals to support our written texts.
y: Visuals make our writing clearer and more engaging for readers.
Many real-world texts combine words and images effectively.
Adding visual information shows we can communicate in multiple ways.
eg: We add a labelled diagram to our science report.
We include a hand-drawn map in our geography writing.
We create a timeline to go with our history recount.
We add photographs or pictures to our book review.
We draw a chart to present data in our maths investigation write-up.
We illustrate key scenes in our narrative to support the story.

VC2EALB2W05.w: We are learning to use headings and paragraphs to organise our writing clearly.
y: Headings and paragraphs break our writing into clear, readable sections.
They help readers find information quickly in our texts.
Good organisation is a hallmark of effective writing.
eg: We give our information report a title and use subheadings for each section.
We start a new paragraph each time we move to a new idea.
We write an introduction paragraph, body paragraphs and a conclusion.
We use headings like 'Habitat', 'Diet' and 'Appearance' in an animal report.
We indent or space our paragraphs so readers can see where each one starts.
We organise a persuasive text with a heading, argument paragraphs and a concluding statement.

VC2EALB2W06.w: We are learning to use linking words like 'and', 'but', 'then' and pronouns like 'he', 'she' and 'they' to connect our ideas.
y: Connectives join ideas together and make our writing flow smoothly.
Pronouns stop us from repeating the same nouns over and over.
Using both well makes our writing sound natural and mature.
eg: We use 'and' to add information: 'The dog ran and the cat chased it'.
We use 'but' to show contrast: 'He tried hard but didn't win'.
We replace 'The girl' with 'she' after introducing the character.
We use 'because' to explain reasons in our writing.
We use 'then' to show what happened next.
We refer back to 'the experiment' as 'it' to avoid repetition.

VC2EALB2W07.w: We are learning to use a range of verb forms correctly in our writing.
y: Correct verb forms tell the reader when things happened.
Using them accurately makes our writing clearer and more professional.
This is a key grammar skill that improves all our writing.
eg: We use past tense consistently in recounts: 'walked', 'ate', 'went'.
We write 'is growing' for something happening now.
We use 'will' for future: 'We will visit the museum next week'.
We write 'has been raining' to show something started in the past and continues.
We use irregular past tenses correctly: 'caught', 'thought', 'brought'.
We check our writing to make sure all verbs match the time of the events.

VC2EALB2W08.w: We are learning to write longer describing phrases to add more detail to our texts.
y: Extended descriptions make our writing more vivid and engaging.
They show we can use English to create detailed pictures with words.
Descriptive writing is important in both creative and information texts.
eg: We write 'the old, crumbling castle on top of the hill' instead of 'the castle'.
We describe a character as 'a tall, friendly girl with curly brown hair and bright eyes'.
We write 'the cool, clear water of the creek running through the valley'.
We describe a scene as 'a noisy classroom full of excited students working on art projects'.
We use phrases like 'as quiet as a mouse' and 'as fast as lightning' in our stories.
We describe emotions in detail: 'she felt a warm rush of pride and happiness'.

VC2EALB2W09.w: We are learning to choose the right words for different types of writing and topics.
y: Good word choice makes our writing more precise and effective.
Different topics and text types need different vocabulary.
Varied vocabulary shows our English is becoming stronger and more flexible.
eg: We use scientific words like 'habitat', 'adaptation' and 'species' in a science report.
We choose strong verbs like 'sprinted', 'whispered' and 'crashed' for our stories.
We use formal language in a letter and informal language in a diary entry.
We select precise adjectives instead of vague ones: 'scorching' instead of 'hot'.
We use topic-specific words we have learned in our current unit.
We choose words that match the mood we want to create in our writing.

VC2EALB2W10.w: We are learning to spell words with common patterns more and more accurately.
y: Recognising spelling patterns helps us spell many words correctly.
Accurate spelling builds over time as we practise and learn more patterns.
Good spelling helps readers focus on our ideas rather than our mistakes.
eg: We spell words with the '-tion' pattern correctly: 'station', 'education', 'information'.
We use the 'silent e' rule to spell 'make', 'ride' and 'hope'.
We spell words with double letters like 'running', 'bigger' and 'happy'.
We use the '-ight' pattern in 'night', 'light' and 'right'.
We spell common word families correctly, like 'play', 'played' and 'playing'.
We proofread our writing for spelling errors and fix the ones we can.

VC2EALB2W11.w: We are learning to try using more complex punctuation in our writing, like commas, apostrophes and speech marks.
y: Complex punctuation makes our writing clearer and more sophisticated.
Experimenting with punctuation is how we learn to use it correctly.
These marks help us write longer, more interesting sentences.
eg: We try using commas in a list: 'We need paint, paper, glue and scissors'.
We use apostrophes for contractions: 'don't', 'can't' and 'it's'.
We experiment with speech marks: '"Let's go!" said the teacher'.
We use commas before 'but' and 'because' in longer sentences.
We try using apostrophes for possession: 'the dog's tail' and 'Sam's book'.
We use exclamation marks to show strong feelings in our stories.

VC2EALB2W12.w: We are learning to write in a way that sounds more like written English than spoken English.
y: Written English has different features from spoken English.
Learning to write more formally helps us succeed in school writing tasks.
This is an important step in becoming a skilled English writer.
eg: We use complete sentences instead of fragments we might say in conversation.
We write 'The children were excited' instead of the casual 'The kids were, like, really excited'.
We use more formal vocabulary in information texts.
We avoid writing 'gonna', 'wanna' and other spoken shortcuts.
We write longer, more structured sentences than we would say aloud.
We use paragraphs and topic sentences to organise our ideas formally.

VC2EALB2W13.w: We are learning to take responsibility for checking and improving our own writing, with some teacher help.
y: Self-editing is an essential skill for all writers.
Taking responsibility for our writing helps us become independent.
Reviewing our own work helps us catch mistakes and improve our English.
eg: We reread our writing and circle words we think might be spelled wrong.
We check that every sentence starts with a capital letter and ends with the right punctuation.
We read our writing aloud to check it makes sense.
We ask ourselves 'Have I included enough detail?' and add more if needed.
We use a checklist the teacher provides to review our work.
We make changes based on feedback the teacher writes on our draft.

VC2EALB2W14.w: We are learning to use our home language to strengthen and enrich our English writing.
y: Our home language provides ideas, structures and vocabulary we can transfer.
Thinking bilingually helps us produce better English writing.
Using both languages is a strategic advantage for our learning.
eg: We plan a complex text in our home language before writing it in English.
We use a cultural story from our language as inspiration for English creative writing.
We look up precise vocabulary using bilingual resources.
We compare essay structures in our language and English to improve our organisation.
We discuss our writing topic with a peer in our home language to generate ideas.
We translate phrases from our language that express an idea we can't yet say in English.

VC2EALB2W15.w: We are learning to share our writing with classmates and give and receive helpful feedback.
y: Peer feedback helps us see our writing through someone else's eyes.
Giving feedback to others strengthens our own understanding of good writing.
Sharing writing builds a supportive classroom writing community.
eg: We read a partner's writing and tell them one thing they did well.
We suggest one thing a classmate could improve in their writing.
We listen to feedback about our own writing and decide which suggestions to use.
We share our draft with a partner and ask 'Does this make sense?'
We read our best sentence to the class during sharing time.
We give specific feedback like 'I like how you used the word enormous here'.

VC2EALB3L01.w: We are learning to understand what someone is saying in a spoken text, with some help from our teacher.
y: Understanding spoken texts fully is key to learning in all subjects.
With decreasing support, we build towards independent listening comprehension.
This skill helps us participate confidently in class discussions and activities.
eg: We listen to the teacher explain a new concept and understand the main points.
We follow a guest speaker's presentation with some help from prepared questions.
We watch an educational video and understand the information with teacher guidance.
We listen to a classmate's oral report and ask relevant questions.
We follow a set of verbal instructions to complete a task.
We understand a read-aloud of a chapter book with occasional vocabulary help.

VC2EALB3L02.w: We are learning to understand more complex spoken English, even when there are fewer visual clues or context to help us.
y: In real life, we often hear English without pictures or actions to help us — we need to understand the words alone.
This prepares us for more demanding listening tasks in upper primary and secondary school.
Understanding abstract language opens up more complex ideas and discussions.
eg: We understand a teacher's explanation of a concept without pictures or diagrams.
We follow a discussion about feelings or ideas that we cannot see or touch.
We understand a spoken text about a topic we have not studied before.
We listen to someone describe a historical event and picture it in our minds.
We follow a spoken argument and understand the reasoning behind it.
We understand instructions for an unfamiliar task by listening carefully to the words.

VC2EALB3L03.w: We are learning to start and join in a range of conversations and learning activities with confidence.
y: Initiating conversations shows growing independence in English.
Participating across different contexts builds flexible communication skills.
This helps us succeed socially and academically at school.
eg: We start a conversation about a class project with a new partner.
We join a group discussion and add our perspective to the topic.
We ask thoughtful questions during a class Q&A session.
We participate in a debate by sharing our opinion with reasons.
We negotiate roles and tasks with group members for a project.
We chat with friends about weekend plans using natural English.

VC2EALB3L04.w: We are learning to prepare and give a short talk about a topic we know.
y: Presenting to others is an important skill for school and life.
Preparing a talk helps us organise our ideas clearly in English.
Public speaking builds our confidence and spoken fluency.
eg: We prepare a short talk about our favourite hobby and present it to the class.
We give a presentation about a country we researched.
We do a 'show and tell' about a meaningful object from home.
We present our findings from a science experiment to the group.
We give a book talk recommending a book to classmates.
We prepare and deliver a short speech about something we care about.

VC2EALB3L05.w: We are learning to recognise and use features that make spoken English formal or informal.
y: We speak differently in different situations — knowing when to be formal matters.
Using the right level of formality shows awareness of audience and context.
This is a social skill as much as a language skill.
eg: We use formal language when presenting to the class and informal language with friends.
We notice that TV news uses more formal English than a chat show.
We adjust our language when speaking to the principal versus a classmate.
We recognise slang and colloquial language in informal speech.
We use polite, formal phrases in a role-play about a job interview.
We compare the same message delivered formally and informally.

VC2EALB3L06.w: We are learning to use the right words to show the order and timing of events when we speak.
y: Sequence markers organise our spoken ideas logically.
They help our listeners follow complex explanations and recounts.
Using varied markers shows growing sophistication in English.
eg: We use 'to begin with', 'following that' and 'in conclusion' in a presentation.
We tell a recount using 'initially', 'subsequently' and 'eventually'.
We explain a process with 'the first step is', 'after this' and 'the final stage'.
We use 'meanwhile' and 'at the same time' for things happening simultaneously.
We transition between ideas with 'in addition', 'on the other hand' and 'as a result'.
We use 'prior to' and 'following' instead of just 'before' and 'after'.

VC2EALB3L07.w: We are learning to use more complex verb forms with greater accuracy when we speak.
y: Complex verb forms let us express subtle differences in meaning.
Getting these right makes our English sound more natural and mature.
This grammar skill is crucial for academic speaking.
eg: We say 'I have been studying English for two years' using present perfect continuous.
We use passive voice: 'The experiment was conducted yesterday'.
We say 'If I had known, I would have come earlier' for hypothetical situations.
We use 'She might have forgotten' to express possibility about the past.
We say 'By next week, we will have finished the project'.
We self-correct verb forms during conversation when we notice an error.

VC2EALB3L08.w: We are learning to use longer and more detailed describing phrases when we speak.
y: Longer descriptions help us communicate more precisely and vividly.
They make our spoken English richer and more engaging for listeners.
This shows our growing ability to handle complex English expression.
eg: We describe a place as 'a busy market filled with the smell of fresh bread and the sound of people bargaining'.
We describe a person as 'my grandmother, who is a kind woman with a great sense of humour'.
We say 'the experiment, which took about 20 minutes, showed surprising results'.
We describe an experience as 'one of the most exciting days I've had since arriving in Australia'.
We use relative clauses: 'The book that we read last week was about migration'.
We describe feelings in detail: 'I was nervous at first, but then I started to feel more confident'.

VC2EALB3L09.w: We are learning to choose words carefully to express subtle differences in meaning.
y: Nuanced vocabulary helps us say exactly what we mean.
It shows listeners we have a deep understanding of English.
Precise word choice prevents misunderstandings in communication.
eg: We use 'enormous' instead of 'big' and 'minuscule' instead of 'tiny'.
We choose 'frustrated' over 'angry' when that is the more accurate feeling.
We say 'suggested' instead of 'said' to show the nature of the communication.
We use 'investigate' rather than 'look at' for a more precise meaning.
We choose between 'ancient', 'old' and 'historic' depending on context.
We distinguish between 'challenging', 'difficult' and 'impossible' for different degrees.

VC2EALB3L10.w: We are learning to pronounce all the words and phrases we know clearly and accurately.
y: Accurate pronunciation ensures we are always understood.
It builds our confidence to speak in front of others.
Good pronunciation connects our spoken English to our reading and writing.
eg: We pronounce multi-syllable academic words like 'environment' and 'significant' correctly.
We say words with tricky consonant clusters like 'strengths' and 'twelfths' clearly.
We pronounce homophones correctly in context: 'there', 'their' and 'they're'.
We use correct word stress: 'PHOtograph' vs 'phoTOGraphy' vs 'photoGRAPHic'.
We pronounce borrowed words like 'café', 'genre' and 'debris' accurately.
We read aloud fluently, pronouncing all words clearly.

VC2EALB3L11.w: We are learning to use our voice, pace, volume and body language skilfully to communicate meaning.
y: These features turn ordinary speech into powerful, engaging communication.
They help us emphasise what matters and keep listeners interested.
Mastering these aspects makes us confident, effective speakers.
eg: We slow down when making an important point in a presentation.
We lower our volume to create tension when telling a story.
We use hand gestures to reinforce key ideas while speaking.
We pause after a key statement to let it sink in for the audience.
We vary our pace and volume throughout a talk to keep it engaging.
We make eye contact with different audience members during a presentation.

VC2EALB3L12.w: We are learning to talk about how different cultures and languages use spoken language differently.
y: Understanding cultural differences in communication helps us interact respectfully with everyone.
It builds intercultural understanding and appreciation for diversity.
This awareness helps us navigate multicultural school and community environments.
eg: We discuss how eye contact means different things in different cultures.
We compare how greeting customs vary across cultures.
We talk about how some cultures value direct communication while others prefer indirectness.
We discuss how personal space and gestures differ between cultures.
We compare how politeness is expressed in English and in our home language.
We share examples of cultural misunderstandings caused by language differences.

VC2EALB3L13.w: We are learning to evaluate our own spoken English and work on improving areas that need attention.
y: Self-evaluation makes us active, responsible learners.
Identifying our own areas for improvement accelerates our progress.
This is a lifelong learning skill that applies beyond language learning.
eg: We listen to a recording of ourselves speaking and identify areas to improve.
We notice patterns in our speech that cause misunderstandings and work on them.
We set a personal goal like 'I will use past tense more accurately this week'.
We ask for feedback from a trusted peer about our speaking.
We compare our pronunciation with a model and practise the differences.
We reflect on a presentation we gave and plan what to do better next time.

VC2EALB3L14.w: We are learning to draw deeply on our home language to enhance all our English listening and speaking.
y: Our home language is an extensive resource that enriches our English skills.
Advanced bilingual strategies help us communicate complex ideas.
Using our full linguistic repertoire makes us stronger communicators overall.
eg: We use concepts from our home language to understand abstract English vocabulary.
We draw on our cultural knowledge to add depth to class discussions.
We use sophisticated thinking strategies from our first language when listening to complex English.
We translate nuanced ideas from our language to express them in English.
We compare rhetorical styles across our languages to improve our spoken English.
We use our understanding of formal registers in our home language to inform our English.

VC2EALB3L15.w: We are learning to start and manage interactions when working in groups.
y: Managing group interactions is a leadership skill that helps everyone learn.
It helps groups work more efficiently and include all voices.
These skills are important for school projects and for working with others in the future.
eg: We suggest a plan for how the group should approach a task.
We make sure every group member gets a turn to share their ideas.
We redirect the group when the discussion goes off-topic.
We summarise what the group has agreed on before moving to the next step.
We resolve a disagreement by suggesting a compromise.
We assign roles like timekeeper, note-taker and presenter to organise the group.

VC2EALB3R01.w: We are learning to find and compare the main ideas across different types of texts.
y: Comparing main ideas across texts deepens our understanding of topics.
It builds critical thinking skills and shows us different perspectives.
This is an important skill for research and for forming our own opinions.
eg: We read two articles on the same topic and compare what each one emphasises.
