We identify the main idea in a narrative and compare it with a related information text.
We compare the messages of two texts that take different positions on an issue.
We find the central argument in a persuasive text and compare it with a balanced report.
We read a poem and a short story on the same theme and compare their main ideas.
We create a Venn diagram showing shared and unique ideas from two texts.

VC2EALB3R02.w: We are learning to find detailed, specific information from a variety of texts.
y: Extracting detailed information is essential for research and study.
It helps us go beyond surface-level understanding of texts.
This skill supports deeper learning across all school subjects.
eg: We read a text and locate specific dates, figures and facts to answer detailed questions.
We find evidence in a story to support a claim about a character.
We extract data from a table or graph and explain what it shows.
We scan a long text to find the specific paragraph that answers our question.
We compare details across multiple sources on the same topic.
We use index and contents pages to locate specific information efficiently.

VC2EALB3R03.w: We are learning to interpret and explain the information in images, diagrams and visual features of texts.
y: Visual literacy is as important as reading words in today's world.
Interpreting images helps us understand complex information more quickly.
Many academic and real-world texts rely heavily on visual communication.
eg: We explain what a pie chart shows and what the biggest segment represents.
We interpret a scientific diagram and describe the process it illustrates.
We analyse a photograph in a text and explain what information it adds.
We read a timeline and explain the sequence of events it shows.
We interpret a cross-section diagram and describe its parts.
We explain how an infographic combines data and visuals to communicate a message.

VC2EALB3R04.w: We are learning to discuss texts by looking beyond what the words say directly, moving towards reading between the lines.
y: Inferential reading helps us understand deeper meanings authors don't state directly.
It develops our critical thinking and makes us more sophisticated readers.
This is an essential skill for literary analysis and for understanding complex texts.
eg: We discuss why a character acted a certain way, even though the text doesn't explain.
We infer the author's attitude towards a topic from the language they use.
We identify a moral or lesson in a story that is implied but not stated.
We discuss the mood of a poem and explain what creates that mood.
We read between the lines to understand what a character is really feeling.
We make connections between a text and real-world events to deepen our understanding.

VC2EALB3R05.w: We are learning to recognise and compare different text types and the features that make each one work.
y: Understanding text types helps us read strategically and write effectively.
Comparing features across types builds deep knowledge of how English texts work.
This knowledge helps us choose the right format for our own writing.
eg: We compare how a narrative and a report are structured differently.
We identify the features that make a persuasive text different from an information text.
We compare a poem with a short story and discuss their different organisational features.
We recognise the features of a discussion text that presents multiple viewpoints.
We identify how a biography is organised compared to an autobiography.
We compare the language features of formal and informal text types.

VC2EALB3R06.w: We are learning how ideas are linked together between and within paragraphs in different texts.
y: Understanding cohesion helps us follow the logical flow of a text.
It shows us how skilled authors connect ideas to build meaning.
This knowledge improves both our reading comprehension and our writing.
eg: We identify how pronouns refer back to nouns mentioned earlier to avoid repetition.
We notice how linking words like 'however', 'therefore' and 'in addition' connect paragraphs.
We see how a topic sentence in each paragraph links to the overall thesis.
We track how an author develops an argument across several paragraphs.
We notice how repeated key words create connections throughout a text.
We identify how transition sentences link one paragraph's idea to the next.

VC2EALB3R07.w: We are learning to understand the meaning of sentences with complex structures and multiple parts.
y: Complex sentences carry more information and are common in academic texts.
Understanding them is essential for reading at year level.
This skill prepares us for the increasingly complex texts we will encounter.
eg: We understand sentences with embedded clauses: 'The boy, who had just arrived from overseas, felt nervous'.
We interpret passive constructions: 'The bridge was built in 1850 by colonial workers'.
We follow sentences with multiple subordinate clauses.
We understand conditional sentences: 'If the temperature drops below zero, the water will freeze'.
We read sentences that begin with adverbial phrases: 'Despite the heavy rain, the match continued'.
We untangle long sentences by identifying the main clause and supporting clauses.

VC2EALB3R08.w: We are learning to use our understanding of different tenses and negative forms to interpret meaning accurately.
y: Tense and negation change the meaning of sentences significantly.
Accurate interpretation of these features prevents misunderstanding.
This grammar awareness is essential for reading academic and literary texts.
eg: We distinguish 'had already left' (past perfect) from 'was leaving' (past continuous).
We understand double negatives and their effect on meaning.
We interpret 'would have succeeded' as a hypothetical past situation.
We understand the difference between 'didn't need to go' and 'needn't have gone'.
We recognise that 'They are not unlike each other' means they are similar.
We interpret tense shifts within a text and understand why the author uses them.

VC2EALB3R09.w: We are learning to read and understand a range of descriptive language in texts.
y: Rich description creates atmosphere, develops characters and conveys complex ideas.
Understanding descriptive language helps us appreciate how authors craft their writing.
This skill enriches our reading experience and inspires our own writing.
eg: We visualise a setting from a detailed description in a novel.
We understand how similes and metaphors create vivid images.
We interpret the mood created by an author's choice of descriptive words.
We recognise how authors use sensory language — sight, sound, smell, taste and touch.
We understand how description of a character's appearance reveals personality.
We appreciate how technical description in a science text creates precise understanding.

VC2EALB3R10.w: We are learning to work out the meaning of unfamiliar words by using the sentence structure and context around them.
y: Context clues are one of the most powerful tools for learning new vocabulary.
This strategy means we can keep reading fluently instead of stopping to look up every word.
It helps us build vocabulary naturally through reading.
eg: We use the words around an unfamiliar word to guess its meaning.
We recognise that a word is an adjective from its position in the sentence and guess its meaning.
We look at the rest of the paragraph to understand what an unfamiliar word refers to.
We use a definition or explanation within the sentence to understand a technical term.
We work out the meaning of a word from its prefix or suffix.
We test our guess by substituting a familiar word and checking if the sentence still makes sense.

VC2EALB3R11.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sound-letter patterns to work out how to say unfamiliar words.
y: Decoding pronunciation helps us say new words confidently when reading aloud.
It connects our reading to our spoken vocabulary.
This skill becomes more important as we encounter longer, more complex words.
eg: We break a multi-syllable word into parts and sound each one out.
We use knowledge of common patterns like '-ough' to attempt pronunciation.
We recognise Greek and Latin roots and use them to pronounce scientific words.
We apply stress rules to work out how to say a new word naturally.
We use analogy with known words to pronounce new ones: 'if I know height, I can read weight'.
We check our pronunciation with a dictionary or the teacher when we are unsure.

VC2EALB3R12.w: We are learning to recognise and understand direct speech (with quotation marks) and indirect speech (reported) in texts.
y: Knowing the difference helps us understand who said what in a text.
It helps us interpret characters' voices and the narrator's voice in stories.
This is an important skill for reading fiction and for understanding reported news.
eg: We identify direct speech by the quotation marks: '"I'll be there," she promised'.
We recognise indirect speech: 'She said that she would be there'.
We compare the same event reported with direct and indirect speech.
We understand that indirect speech changes pronouns and tense.
We track who is speaking in a dialogue by following the speech marks and tags.
We notice the difference between what a character says and what the narrator reports.

VC2EALB3R13.w: We are learning to compare texts from different languages and cultures to see their similarities and differences.
y: Cross-cultural comparison develops our appreciation of diverse perspectives.
It deepens our understanding of how language and culture shape texts.
This builds intercultural capability alongside our English skills.
eg: We compare a creation story from our culture with one from another culture.
We look at how newspapers in different countries report the same event.
We compare poetry forms from different cultural traditions.
We discuss how persuasive texts use different techniques in different cultures.
We compare how personal writing (like letters or diaries) differs across cultures.
We examine how the same fairy tale is told differently in various cultural traditions.

VC2EALB3R14.w: We are learning to read by ourselves for fun or to find information, both in and out of class.
y: Independent reading is one of the best ways to improve all our English skills.
Reading for pleasure builds vocabulary, knowledge and fluency naturally.
This habit supports lifelong learning and enjoyment of books.
eg: We choose a novel from the library and read it for enjoyment during free time.
We research a topic of interest by reading articles and websites independently.
We read the news online to stay informed about current events.
We explore different genres like fantasy, mystery and non-fiction to find what we enjoy.
We keep a reading log of books and texts we have read independently.
We recommend books to classmates based on our independent reading.

VC2EALB3R15.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to support all our English reading.
y: Our home language is a deep resource for understanding complex English texts.
Advanced cross-language strategies make us more powerful readers.
Bilingual readers have unique strengths that enhance comprehension.
eg: We use our home language understanding of a complex concept to access an English text.
We compare text structures in our language and English to deepen comprehension.
We read about the same topic in both languages to build fuller understanding.
We use our knowledge of literary techniques from our home language when reading English literature.
We translate key sentences to check our understanding of difficult passages.
We draw on our cultural knowledge to interpret texts that reference diverse contexts.

VC2EALB3R16.w: We are learning to share our personal responses to poems, stories, films and songs during shared reading.
y: Personal responses show we are engaging deeply with creative texts.
Sharing our reactions helps us learn from others' perspectives.
This develops our appreciation of literature and creative expression.
eg: We share how a poem made us feel and explain which words created that feeling.
We discuss a character's choices in a story and say whether we agree with them.
We talk about which scene in a film was most powerful and why.
We connect a song's lyrics to our own experiences.
We compare our response to a text with a classmate's and discuss the differences.
We explain what we would change about a story's ending and why.

VC2EALB3W01.w: We are learning to write different types of texts that present our point of view on topics we discuss in class.
y: Expressing our point of view in writing is a powerful communication skill.
Writing persuasively prepares us for academic writing and civic participation.
Practising across different text types builds our flexibility as writers.
eg: We write a persuasive text arguing for or against school uniforms.
We write a letter to the editor about an environmental issue.
We write a discussion text presenting both sides of a class debate topic.
We write a review of a book or film that includes our opinion.
We write a response to a class reading that explains our personal view.
We write an essay presenting our position on a topic studied in humanities.

VC2EALB3W02.w: We are learning to write information texts about topics from all our subjects, using information from different sources.
y: Combining information from multiple sources shows strong research skills.
Writing across the curriculum deepens our understanding of every subject.
This is essential preparation for secondary school and beyond.
eg: We write a science report combining information from our experiment and a textbook.
We write a history essay using details from multiple sources.
We create an information text about a country using data from books, websites and videos.
We write a geography report integrating maps, statistics and written sources.
We write an explanation text about a natural process using diagrams and written research.
We compile a fact file on a topic using information gathered from three different sources.

VC2EALB3W03.w: We are learning to write imaginative texts that blend our own experiences with ideas from other texts we have read.
y: Drawing on our experiences makes our creative writing authentic and unique.
Borrowing techniques from other texts helps us develop our craft.
This combination produces richer, more engaging creative writing.
eg: We write a story set in a place we know, using narrative techniques from a class novel.
We write a poem inspired by a poet we studied, incorporating our own experiences.
We create a character based on someone we know and place them in a situation from another story.
We retell a traditional story from our culture, adding our own personal twist.
We write a memoir piece that uses descriptive techniques we admired in a mentor text.
We blend real events from our life with fictional elements inspired by our reading.

VC2EALB3W04.w: We are learning to use images, diagrams, charts and other visual elements to support and enhance our writing.
y: Visuals can communicate complex information more effectively than words alone.
Using them shows we understand how multimodal texts work.
This is increasingly important in digital communication and academic work.
eg: We include a graph in our report to present numerical data clearly.
We add a labelled diagram to our science explanation.
We create an illustration for a key scene in our narrative.
We use a photograph with a caption in our information text.
We include a map in our geography writing to show location.
We add a flow chart to our explanation of a process.

VC2EALB3W05.w: We are learning to use the right structure and organisation for the type of text we are writing.
y: Different text types need different structures to work effectively.
Using the right structure helps our writing achieve its purpose.
This shows we understand how English writing is organised for different audiences.
eg: We use orientation-complication-resolution structure for narratives.
We organise information reports with an introduction, body paragraphs by subtopic, and conclusion.
We write procedures with a goal, materials list and numbered steps.
We structure persuasive texts with a thesis, supporting arguments and a concluding statement.
We use chronological order for recounts and historical accounts.
We organise discussion texts with an introduction, arguments for and against, and a summary.

VC2EALB3W06.w: We are learning to use different techniques to make our writing flow smoothly from one idea to the next.
y: Cohesive writing is easier to read and shows mature language use.
These devices help readers follow our thinking without getting lost.
Good cohesion is what separates effective writing from disconnected sentences.
eg: We use pronouns to refer back to nouns without repeating them.
We use connectives like 'furthermore', 'consequently' and 'in contrast'.
We use synonym substitution to avoid word repetition.
We write transition sentences that link one paragraph to the next.
We use consistent tense throughout to maintain cohesion.
We use referencing words like 'this', 'that', 'these' and 'those' to connect ideas.

VC2EALB3W07.w: We are learning to keep the same tense throughout a piece of writing without accidentally switching.
y: Consistent tense use makes our writing clear and professional.
Unintentional tense shifts confuse readers.
This shows strong control of English grammar.
eg: We write a recount entirely in past tense without switching to present.
We maintain present tense throughout an information report.
We use past tense consistently in a narrative, only shifting for dialogue.
We check our writing specifically for tense consistency during editing.
We write an explanation in present tense: 'Water evaporates... It then condenses...'
We notice and fix any accidental tense shifts when proofreading.

VC2EALB3W08.w: We are learning to write with longer, more detailed describing phrases to paint vivid pictures with words.
y: Extended description brings our writing to life for readers.
It shows growing sophistication in our English expression.
Detailed description is important in both creative and academic writing.
eg: We write 'the ancient, weathered stone wall that surrounded the abandoned garden' instead of 'the old wall'.
We describe a character using a clause: 'the girl, whose eyes sparkled with curiosity, stepped forward'.
We write 'a heavy, suffocating silence that seemed to swallow every sound' for atmosphere.
We use prepositional phrases: 'the village, nestled between two towering mountains'.
We add adverbial phrases: 'she walked carefully through the dark, narrow corridor'.
We describe emotions in depth: 'a growing sense of unease that twisted in his stomach'.

VC2EALB3W09.w: We are learning to choose and use the right vocabulary for different topics and types of writing.
y: Appropriate vocabulary makes our writing precise and effective.
Different subjects and purposes require different word choices.
A rich vocabulary is one of the strongest tools in a writer's toolkit.
eg: We use technical vocabulary accurately in a science report.
We choose emotive language for persuasive writing.
We use formal academic vocabulary in essays.
We select vivid, sensory words for creative writing.
We use subject-specific terms correctly in maths, history and geography writing.
We vary our vocabulary to avoid repetition across a long text.

VC2EALB3W10.w: We are learning to spell most words correctly, using a range of strategies including letter patterns, word parts and our home language knowledge.
y: Accurate spelling lets readers focus on our ideas, not our errors.
Using multiple strategies means we have more tools to tackle any word.
Drawing on our home language makes our spelling strategies stronger.
eg: We use knowledge of common letter patterns to spell words like 'receiving' and 'necessary'.
We break words into meaningful parts (morphemes) to spell 'un-help-ful' and 'dis-agree-ment'.
We use context clues to choose between homophones like 'their' and 'there'.
We use our home language knowledge of word origins to help with English spelling.
We check unfamiliar spellings using a dictionary or spell-checker.
We proofread our work specifically for spelling and correct any errors.

VC2EALB3W11.w: We are learning to use a range of punctuation marks correctly and consistently throughout our writing.
y: Consistent, correct punctuation makes our writing professional and clear.
It shows strong control of written English conventions.
This skill is essential for all academic writing.
eg: We use commas correctly in lists and complex sentences.
We use apostrophes for contractions and possession without errors.
We use quotation marks for direct speech and titles.
We use colons to introduce lists or explanations.
We use semicolons to join related independent clauses.
We use exclamation marks, question marks and ellipses for effect in creative writing.

VC2EALB3W12.w: We are learning to present our writing in a way that suits who will read it and why we are writing.
y: Presentation affects how readers receive and respond to our writing.
Matching presentation to purpose and audience shows mature writing skills.
This includes layout, formatting and the overall look of our work.
eg: We format a formal letter correctly with the right layout and tone.
We present a report with a clear title page, headings and page numbers.
We use appropriate font and spacing for a published class anthology.
We format a poster with large headings, images and key points.
We present a digital text with hyperlinks, images and clear navigation.
We adjust our handwriting and layout when writing for a display versus a journal.

VC2EALB3W13.w: We are learning to edit and improve our own writing without needing the teacher to tell us what to fix.
y: Independent editing is a hallmark of a capable, self-directed writer.
Catching our own errors and improving our work shows strong self-awareness.
This skill ensures our writing is always as good as it can be.
eg: We reread our draft and revise unclear sentences on our own.
We check spelling, punctuation and grammar independently.
We remove unnecessary words or sentences that don't add to our meaning.
We add more detail or evidence where we notice our writing is thin.
We restructure paragraphs for better flow and logic.
We use an editing checklist we created to review every piece of writing.

VC2EALB3W14.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to support and enrich our English writing.
y: Our home language offers sophisticated ideas, structures and vocabulary.
Advanced bilingual strategies help us write more complex English texts.
This is a unique strength that monolingual writers do not have.
eg: We use rhetorical techniques from our home language tradition in English essays.
We plan complex writing in our language before drafting in English.
We translate culturally important concepts into English for our writing.
We use our understanding of formal writing in our language to improve our English style.
We draw on literary traditions from our culture to enrich our creative writing.
We compare and contrast writing conventions across our languages to improve both.

VC2EALB3W15.w: We are learning to work with partners and groups to plan, write and review our writing.
y: Collaborative writing helps us learn from others and produce better work.
Peer review gives us fresh perspectives on our writing.
Working together builds teamwork skills alongside our English skills.
eg: We brainstorm ideas with a partner before starting a writing task.
We co-write a text with a partner, combining our strengths.
We review a classmate's draft and give constructive feedback.
We discuss our writing plans in a group and refine them together.
We peer-edit each other's work, focusing on specific aspects like clarity or evidence.
We present our collaborative writing to the class and explain our process.

VC2EALCLL01.w: We are learning to understand the main themes and ideas of familiar spoken texts, with significant teacher support.
y: Understanding the main idea is the foundation for all listening comprehension.
Working with familiar texts builds our confidence as we develop our English.
Teacher support helps us access meaning we could not yet reach on our own.
eg: We listen to the teacher explain a routine classroom task and identify what the task is about.
We hear a short talk on a familiar topic like sport or food and state the main idea.
We watch a video clip about school life and tell the teacher the key message.
We listen to a story the class has been working on and identify the main theme.
We hear an announcement and, with help, explain what it is asking us to do.
We listen to a classmate's short talk and say what the main topic was.

VC2EALCLL02.w: We are learning to pick out single pieces of information from short spoken texts.
y: Being able to catch specific details helps us follow instructions and conversations.
Starting with single pieces of information builds towards comprehending more.
This skill is immediately useful in everyday school life.
eg: We hear the teacher say 'The test is on Friday' and remember the day.
We listen to an announcement and catch the time mentioned.
We hear a short description and identify the colour or size of something.
We listen to a classmate introduce themselves and remember their name.
We hear a brief instruction and identify the key action word.
We pick out a number or date from a short spoken text.

VC2EALCLL03.w: We are learning to take part in face-to-face conversations and school activities we are familiar with.
y: Participating in familiar situations builds our confidence to try new ones.
Face-to-face communication gives us visual cues that help us understand.
Each interaction is practice that strengthens our English skills.
eg: We greet classmates and respond when they ask how we are.
We participate in roll call by responding when our name is called.
We join in a familiar classroom activity like a warm-up game.
We follow routine instructions during a lesson transition.
We have a simple conversation with a peer during a paired activity.
We participate in lunchtime interactions with classmates.

VC2EALCLL04.w: We are learning to contribute to short conversations and class discussions about topics we know, with lots of teacher help.
y: Contributing to dialogue is how we start to find our voice in English.
Teacher support gives us the scaffolding we need to participate successfully.
Every contribution we make builds our spoken English skills.
eg: We answer a yes/no question the teacher asks during a class discussion.
We share a one-sentence opinion about a topic with teacher prompting.
We contribute a word or phrase to a class brainstorm.
We respond to a partner's question in a structured pair activity.
We add a comment to a discussion when the teacher invites us to speak.
We ask a simple question about the topic being discussed.

VC2EALCLL05.w: We are learning to understand short, modelled phrases and sentences that the teacher has shown us.
y: Modelled language gives us clear examples of how English works.
Understanding these phrases first helps us use them ourselves.
This builds a foundation of language patterns we can use in many situations.
eg: We understand the teacher's modelled sentence 'I would like some water, please'.
We recognise and understand classroom instructions the teacher has demonstrated.
We follow modelled phrases like 'Can I borrow...?' and 'May I go to...?'
We understand short exchanges the teacher has role-played with another student.
We recognise polite request patterns the teacher has modelled repeatedly.
We understand sentence starters the teacher uses regularly, like 'Today we will...'.

VC2EALCLL06.w: We are learning to understand words that show when things happen and in what order, like 'first', 'next' and 'yesterday'.
y: Time and sequence words help us understand the order of events and instructions.
They are essential for following stories, recounts and procedures.
Understanding these words helps us organise our own speaking and writing.
eg: We understand 'first' and 'then' in a set of classroom instructions.
We hear 'yesterday' and know the speaker is talking about the past.
We follow a story that uses 'in the beginning', 'next' and 'at the end'.
We understand 'before' and 'after' in descriptions of events.
We recognise 'today', 'tomorrow' and 'last week' in everyday conversation.
We follow a procedure using 'step one', 'step two' and 'finally'.

VC2EALCLL07.w: We are learning to say short, simple sentences where the subject and verb match correctly.
y: Subject-verb agreement is a fundamental rule of English grammar.
Getting this right makes our sentences clear and understandable.
This is a building block for all future English speaking and writing.
eg: We say 'She likes music' instead of 'She like music'.
We say 'They are playing' matching the plural subject with the right verb form.
We use 'I am' and 'He is' correctly in our sentences.
We say 'The students have finished' with the correct plural verb.
We say 'It was cold' using the right past tense form for a singular subject.
We practise saying 'We were' and 'He was' to get the agreement right.

VC2EALCLL08.w: We are learning to use simple describing words when we speak in English.
y: Descriptive words help us say more about what we see, feel and experience.
Even basic descriptions make our communication much clearer.
Building descriptive vocabulary early helps us express ourselves as our English grows.
eg: We describe food as 'hot', 'cold', 'sweet' or 'salty'.
We say 'a big classroom' or 'a small bag' to describe size.
We use feeling words like 'happy', 'tired', 'bored' and 'excited'.
We describe colours and shapes in objects around us.
We say 'good', 'bad', 'easy' and 'hard' to give simple opinions.
We describe the weather using words like 'rainy', 'sunny', 'windy' and 'cloudy'.

VC2EALCLL09.w: We are learning to recognise and use common English words for everyday school life and personal needs.
y: These are the words we need most urgently to function at school.
Knowing them helps us communicate our needs and follow routines.
Building this core vocabulary is the fastest way to start using English.
eg: We learn words for classroom equipment: 'laptop', 'whiteboard', 'locker', 'textbook'.
We know words for daily routines: 'timetable', 'recess', 'assembly', 'homework'.
We use words for personal needs: 'bathroom', 'water', 'hungry', 'sick'.
We learn subject names: 'English', 'maths', 'science', 'PE'.
We know words for school places: 'library', 'office', 'canteen', 'gym'.
We use words for common requests: 'help', 'please', 'sorry', 'excuse me'.

VC2EALCLL10.w: We are learning to recognise how English words sound and try to say them the same way.
y: Copying pronunciation is how we start to sound natural in English.
Recognising English sounds helps us hear and understand spoken English better.
Good pronunciation helps us be understood by teachers and classmates.
eg: We listen to the teacher say a word and repeat it, trying to match the sounds.
We notice that some English sounds don't exist in our home language and practise them.
We copy the pronunciation of new vocabulary words during class.
We listen to recorded English and try to repeat phrases with the same rhythm.
We focus on tricky sounds like 'th', 'r' and 'v' that may be different from our language.
We compare our pronunciation with a classmate's and adjust if needed.

VC2EALCLL11.w: We are learning to notice and use English stress patterns, intonation and body language in our conversations.
y: Stress and intonation carry meaning that words alone do not.
Understanding body language gives us extra information in conversations.
Using these features ourselves makes our English communication more natural.
eg: We notice the teacher's voice rises at the end of a question.
We copy the stress pattern when we learn a new multi-syllable word.
We use a louder voice on the word we want to emphasise.
We nod and make eye contact to show we are listening.
We use facial expressions to show we agree, disagree or are confused.
We try matching the rhythm and intonation of native speakers in class.

VC2EALCLL12.w: We are learning to recognise when English is being spoken and try to respond in English.
y: Knowing when to switch to English helps us function in an English-speaking environment.
Attempting to respond in English, even imperfectly, builds our skills rapidly.
This shows we are actively engaging with English at school.
eg: We hear the teacher speaking English and attempt a response in English.
We notice a classmate is speaking English and switch from our home language.
We respond to English greetings with an English greeting of our own.
We try to answer questions in English during class, using whatever words we know.
We recognise English in videos and audio and try to follow along.
We know when to use English in class versus when our home language is appropriate.

VC2EALCLL13.w: We are learning to try saying a word again more clearly when someone doesn't understand us.
y: Repeating and adjusting is a natural part of learning any new language.
It shows persistence and a desire to communicate successfully.
This strategy keeps conversations going instead of shutting them down.
eg: We say a word again more slowly when someone looks confused.
We try stressing different syllables to see if it helps.
We point to what we mean while repeating the word.
We try a different word if the first one isn't understood.
We ask 'Did you understand?' and try again if the answer is no.
We listen to how someone else says the word and copy their pronunciation.

VC2EALCLL14.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us listen and speak in English.
y: Our home language contains knowledge and skills that support English learning.
Thinking in our own language first can help us understand English better.
Being multilingual is a strength we should use, not hide.
eg: We think about what a word means in our language to help understand it in English.
We use our language to discuss a concept with a friend before explaining it in English.
We compare words that sound similar across our languages.
We translate key instructions in our head to make sure we understand.
We use our home language literacy skills to help learn English reading and writing.
We draw on our cultural knowledge when discussing topics in English.

VC2EALCLL15.w: We are learning to interact with classmates during spoken group activities in simple ways.
y: Group work gives us real opportunities to practise speaking English.
Interacting with peers is less stressful than speaking to the whole class.
Building these skills helps us participate more fully in school life.
eg: We share a simple idea with our group using short phrases.
We listen to group members and nod or give a thumbs up to show agreement.
We take a turn to speak during a small-group discussion.
We ask a group member a simple question about the task.
We say 'I agree' or 'I think...' to contribute to a group conversation.
We follow along with group instructions and participate in our assigned role.

VC2EALCLR01.w: We are learning to understand the main idea of short, simple texts with teacher modelling and support.
y: Identifying the main idea is the most fundamental reading comprehension skill.
Teacher modelling shows us how skilled readers find the main idea.
Starting with simple texts builds our confidence for more complex reading.
eg: We follow along as the teacher models how to find the main idea of a paragraph.
We identify the topic of a short text after the teacher demonstrates the process.
We read a simple text and state what it is mainly about in one sentence.
We use headings and pictures to help us identify the main idea with teacher guidance.
We listen as the teacher thinks aloud about the main idea, then try it ourselves.
We tell a partner the main idea of a short text we just read together.

VC2EALCLR02.w: We are learning to spot and understand familiar words and phrases in different types of texts, with teacher support.
y: Recognising familiar words in new contexts shows our vocabulary is growing.
Using words we know as anchors helps us understand unfamiliar texts.
This builds our confidence to tackle increasingly diverse reading material.
eg: We spot words we learned in class when reading a new text.
We recognise familiar phrases in a different type of text, like a sign or a website.
We find words we know in a menu, timetable or form.
We identify familiar vocabulary in a science text we have not read before.
We recognise key words from our vocabulary list in an assessment task.
We spot familiar phrases in instructions for a new activity.

VC2EALCLR03.w: We are learning to understand simple charts, graphs and images in texts, with teacher modelling and support.
y: Visual elements are a major part of how information is presented in texts.
Learning to read visuals is as important as reading words.
Teacher support helps us build these skills step by step.
eg: We read a simple bar graph and identify which item has the most.
We look at a picture in a textbook and describe what information it gives us.
We study a simple table and find specific information the teacher asks about.
We read a map with the teacher's help and locate places mentioned in a text.
We look at a pie chart and identify the largest section.
We study labelled diagrams and explain what each part shows.

VC2EALCLR04.w: We are learning to use features like titles, pictures and headings to predict what a text will be about.
y: Predicting activates our thinking before we start reading.
It helps us connect what we already know to new information.
Making predictions keeps us engaged and curious during reading.
eg: We look at the title of a text and predict the topic before reading.
We study the cover image of a book and guess what the story might be about.
We read headings and predict what each section will cover.
We look at pictures throughout a text and predict the sequence of events.
We use bold words or captions to predict the key ideas in a text.
We share our predictions with a partner and check them as we read.

VC2EALCLR05.w: We are learning to recognise different types of texts by looking at their features, purpose, topic and layout.
y: Recognising text types helps us read them with the right expectations.
Each text type has distinct features that signal how to read it.
This knowledge helps us find the right text for our purpose.
eg: We see numbered steps and know the text is a procedure.
We notice a text has a heading, introduction and conclusion and identify it as a report.
We recognise a narrative by its story structure with characters and events.
We see 'Dear...' and 'Yours sincerely' and know it is a letter.
We notice dot points and images on a large page and identify it as a poster.
We see columns of data and recognise a table or chart.

VC2EALCLR06.w: We are learning to notice basic connecting words and features that hold a text together.
y: Cohesive devices help us understand how sentences and ideas relate to each other.
Recognising them improves our reading comprehension.
This awareness helps us write more connected texts ourselves.
eg: We notice 'and', 'but', 'so' and 'because' linking ideas in a text.
We see that 'he' or 'she' refers back to a person mentioned earlier.
We recognise 'then' connecting two events in time order.
We notice 'this' referring back to an idea in the previous sentence.
We see 'however' and understand it signals a contrast with the previous idea.
We notice how 'also' adds more information to what was already said.

VC2EALCLR07.w: We are learning to arrange words in the right order to form simple, familiar English sentences.
y: English word order follows specific patterns that differ from many other languages.
Getting word order right is essential for both reading and writing in English.
This skill helps us decode sentences we read and construct our own.
eg: We arrange word cards to form 'The teacher reads a book' in the correct order.
We rearrange jumbled words to make a sentence that makes sense.
We identify that English usually puts the subject before the verb.
We notice that questions rearrange word order: 'Are you coming?' vs 'You are coming.'
We build sentences from a set of given words in the right sequence.
We check our own sentences to see if the word order sounds natural.

VC2EALCLR08.w: We are learning to spot present tense and past tense verbs when we read.
y: Verb tense tells us when events in a text are happening.
Recognising tenses is essential for understanding timelines in stories and recounts.
This grammar knowledge improves our reading comprehension significantly.
eg: We recognise '-ed' endings as past tense: 'walked', 'played', 'started'.
We identify 'is' and 'are' as present tense markers.
We see 'was' and 'were' and know the text is about the past.
We notice irregular past tenses like 'went', 'ate' and 'saw'.
We identify 'runs', 'eats' and 'plays' as present tense actions.
We highlight verbs in a text and sort them into present and past tense.

VC2EALCLR09.w: We are learning to spot common word pairs like 'old building' or 'runs quickly' in texts.
y: Common word combinations are building blocks of natural English.
Recognising them helps us read more fluently.
Learning words in pairs is more effective than learning them alone.
eg: We notice 'tall building', 'loud noise' and 'dark room' as adjective-noun pairs.
We see 'runs quickly', 'speaks softly' and 'works hard' as verb-adverb pairs.
We identify common combinations like 'heavy rain', 'strong wind' and 'bright light'.
We spot 'carefully planned' and 'neatly written' in texts.
We recognise 'a good idea', 'the right answer' and 'an important lesson'.
We collect common word pairs in a vocabulary notebook as we read.

VC2EALCLR10.w: We are learning to recognise common English words and phrases we have seen or heard before.
y: Sight word recognition is the foundation of reading fluency.
Recognising words instantly helps us focus on understanding meaning.
Each word we can read on sight makes every text a little easier.
eg: We read high-frequency words like 'because', 'always', 'every' and 'should' instantly.
We recognise classroom words like 'question', 'answer', 'page' and 'chapter'.
We spot phrases like 'for example' and 'in other words' in texts.
We read familiar words on signs, labels and screens around the school.
We identify words from our vocabulary lists when we see them in new texts.
We recognise everyday words like 'morning', 'lunch', 'home' and 'friend'.

VC2EALCLR11.w: We are learning the names and sounds of English letters and how they connect to each other.
y: Letter-sound knowledge is the fundamental tool for reading English.
Understanding these relationships helps us decode any English word.
This is especially important if our home language uses a different writing system.
eg: We name each letter of the alphabet and say its most common sound.
We recognise that some letters have more than one sound, like 'c' in 'cat' and 'city'.
We learn common letter combinations like 'sh', 'ch', 'th' and 'ck'.
We match letters to their sounds when trying to read a new word.
We practise writing letters while saying their sounds.
We identify the beginning, middle and end sounds in simple English words.

VC2EALCLR12.w: We are learning how to read basic punctuation marks and understand what spaces between words mean.
y: Punctuation and spacing are the traffic signs of written English.
Understanding them helps us read with correct pauses and expression.
This is essential for making sense of any written text.
eg: We use spaces to identify individual words in a line of text.
We pause at a full stop when reading aloud.
We recognise that a question mark means a question is being asked.
We see a capital letter and know a new sentence is beginning.
We notice an exclamation mark and read the sentence with emphasis.
We understand that commas signal a brief pause within a sentence.

VC2EALCLR13.w: We are learning to notice what is different about reading in English compared to reading in our home language.
y: Understanding these differences helps us adapt our reading strategies for English.
It prevents confusion caused by applying home language reading habits to English.
Awareness of both systems makes us more skilled, flexible readers.
eg: We notice that English reads from left to right, which may differ from our language.
We see that English uses the Latin alphabet, while our language may use a different script.
We compare how words are spaced in English versus our home language.
We notice different punctuation conventions between the two languages.
We compare how books are laid out in English versus in our home language.
We discuss the direction of reading and page-turning with classmates.

VC2EALCLR14.w: We are learning to choose and read texts for different reasons — to learn, to enjoy or to find specific information.
y: Reading for different purposes is a skill we use throughout life.
Choosing texts ourselves helps us become independent, motivated readers.
Knowing why we are reading helps us read more effectively.
eg: We choose a book for pleasure reading during silent reading time.
We select a text to find information for a class project.
We pick up a magazine or website that interests us.
We read instructions to learn how to do something new.
We choose a text about a hobby or sport we enjoy.
We select different texts depending on whether we want to learn facts or enjoy a story.

VC2EALCLR15.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us when we read English texts.
y: Our home language literacy gives us skills and strategies we can transfer to English.
Using what we already know speeds up our English reading development.
Drawing on our full linguistic knowledge makes us stronger readers.
eg: We use our knowledge of a topic from our home language to understand an English text.
We compare how stories are told in our language and in English.
We translate a key word to check our understanding of a sentence.
We use reading strategies from our first language, like predicting and questioning.
We connect ideas from our cultural background to what we read in English.
We discuss a tricky text with a peer in our home language to aid comprehension.

VC2EALCLR16.w: We are learning to respond to texts we read together as a class.
y: Responding to shared texts shows we are engaging with and thinking about what we read.
It helps us practise expressing ideas about texts in English.
Shared reading builds a community of readers where all voices matter.
eg: We share what we liked or found interesting about a text the class read together.
We answer questions about a shared text during class discussion.
We draw a picture in response to a story the teacher read aloud.
We tell a partner one thing we learned from a shared reading.
We connect something in the text to our own life experience.
We give a thumbs up or down to show our opinion about a shared text.

VC2EALCLW01.w: We are learning to write short, simple texts for a clear purpose, based on our own experiences.
y: Writing about our own life gives us real things to say in English.
Having a clear purpose gives our writing direction and meaning.
Personal writing is a safe, authentic way to practise English.
eg: We write a short email to a friend about something that happened.
We write a diary entry about our day at school.
We write a simple description of ourselves for a class display.
We write a note to the teacher explaining that we need to leave early.
We write about a memory from our home country.
We write a short text about our family for a class activity.

VC2EALCLW02.w: We are learning to write words and simple sentences clearly enough for someone to understand our meaning.
y: Getting our meaning across is the primary goal of writing.
Even imperfect writing that communicates meaning is a success.
Each sentence we write builds our skills and confidence.
eg: We write a sentence about our weekend that a classmate can understand.
We write labels for a poster that clearly identify what each image shows.
We write answers to comprehension questions in our own words.
We write a simple sentence describing a picture.
We fill in a form with our name, age, school and other personal details.
We write a short message that communicates what we want to say.

VC2EALCLW03.w: We are learning to combine writing and drawing to create short texts about familiar topics.
y: Combining words and images is a powerful way to communicate.
Drawing supports our writing when our English vocabulary is still growing.
Many real-world texts use both visual and written elements.
eg: We draw and label the parts of a plant for a science activity.
We create a poster about our culture with pictures and simple text.
We draw a comic strip about our week and add speech bubbles.
We illustrate a step-by-step procedure and write captions.
We create a 'My Life' page with photos or drawings and written descriptions.
We draw a map of our school and label the important places.

VC2EALCLW04.w: We are learning to draw and label simple visuals like diagrams, maps and pictures.
y: Labelling visuals helps us learn vocabulary and show our understanding.
Visual communication is an important skill across all subjects.
Drawing and labelling work together to make our ideas clear.
eg: We draw and label a simple diagram of the human body.
We label the rooms on a floor plan of our school.
We draw and label items in our pencil case or bag.
We create a simple weather chart with labelled symbols.
We draw a picture of our home and label the main features.
We label a diagram of a life cycle for science.

VC2EALCLW05.w: We are learning to use basic text structures to organise the information in our writing.
y: Organising information helps readers follow our writing.
Text structures provide a framework that makes writing easier.
Learning these conventions prepares us for more complex writing tasks.
eg: We use a title at the top of our writing to signal the topic.
We write events in time order for a recount.
We use a simple list format to organise information.
We write a beginning, middle and end for a simple story.
We use headings to separate different sections of an information text.
We follow a basic letter format with a greeting, body and closing.

VC2EALCLW06.w: We are learning to use basic joining words like 'and', 'but' and 'because' to link our ideas in writing.
y: Conjunctions connect our ideas and make our writing flow.
Using them helps us write longer, more detailed sentences.
These words are among the most useful in the English language.
eg: We write 'I like swimming and running' to join two interests.
We use 'but' to show a contrast: 'I was tired but I kept working'.
We explain a reason: 'I was late because the bus was delayed'.
We use 'so' to show a result: 'It was raining so we stayed inside'.
We join two events: 'We had lunch and then we went to class'.
We use 'or' to offer a choice: 'We can play basketball or volleyball'.

VC2EALCLW07.w: We are learning to write sentences that sound natural and have words in the right English order.
y: Natural word order is essential for clear English writing.
Writing as we speak is a good starting point for building English writing skills.
Getting word order right prevents confusion for the reader.
eg: We write 'I go to school every day' with the subject first, then the verb.
We put adjectives before nouns: 'a beautiful garden' not 'a garden beautiful'.
We write questions with the right word order: 'Where do you live?'
We place adverbs in natural positions: 'She always arrives early'.
We write sentences the way we would say them naturally.
We check our sentences by reading them aloud to see if they sound right.

VC2EALCLW08.w: We are learning to use simple action words correctly in our writing.
y: Verbs are the most important words in any sentence.
Using them correctly is the first step to clear English writing.
Building a bank of verbs we can use confidently helps all our writing.
eg: We use 'go', 'eat', 'read', 'write' and 'play' in our sentences.
We write 'I watch TV after school' using the verb 'watch'.
We use 'She studies hard' with the correct verb ending.
We write 'We cleaned the classroom' using past tense.
We use 'He is sleeping' to describe something happening now.
We choose the right verb for the action: 'swim' not 'run' when talking about the pool.

VC2EALCLW09.w: We are learning to use simple describing words in our writing to add detail.
y: Descriptive words make our writing more specific and interesting.
They help readers picture what we are writing about.
Even a few well-chosen descriptions can transform our writing.
eg: We write 'the tall boy' instead of just 'the boy'.
We describe our school as 'big and friendly'.
We use 'delicious' to describe food we liked.
We write 'a scary movie' to express how a film made us feel.
We describe the weather as 'cold and rainy'.
We use 'beautiful', 'interesting' and 'important' in our information writing.

VC2EALCLW10.w: We are learning to write common English words that we already know from hearing them spoken.
y: Writing words we can say builds a bridge between our spoken and written English.
These familiar words are the most useful ones for our everyday writing.
Seeing how spoken words look in writing strengthens our overall English.
eg: We write words we use every day like 'school', 'teacher', 'friend' and 'home'.
We write feeling words we can already say: 'happy', 'sad', 'tired'.
We write food words we hear at lunch: 'sandwich', 'water', 'fruit'.
We write the names of our subjects: 'English', 'maths', 'science'.
We write time words we hear daily: 'today', 'yesterday', 'tomorrow'.
We write action words from PE: 'run', 'jump', 'throw', 'catch'.

VC2EALCLW11.w: We are learning to spell simple, familiar words correctly by matching sounds to letters.
y: Sound-letter knowledge is our main tool for independent spelling.
Spelling familiar words correctly makes our writing easier to read.
This skill grows as we learn more English sound patterns.
eg: We spell three-letter words like 'cat', 'dog' and 'pen' using their sounds.
We use our knowledge of sounds to attempt longer familiar words.
We spell words with common patterns like '-ing', '-ed' and '-tion'.
We use the sounds we hear to have a go at spelling new words.
We check our spelling against a word list or dictionary.
We practise spelling commonly used words until they become automatic.

VC2EALCLW12.w: We are learning to use basic punctuation like capital letters, full stops and question marks in our writing.
y: Punctuation organises our writing and makes it readable.
Using it correctly shows we understand how written English works.
These basic marks are the foundation for all English writing.
eg: We start every sentence with a capital letter.
We put a full stop at the end of each statement.
We use a question mark when we write a question.
We write our name and other proper nouns with capital letters.
We always capitalise the pronoun 'I'.
We check our writing for missing punctuation before handing it in.

VC2EALCLW13.w: We are learning to think about who will read our writing and why we are writing it, and to present our work accordingly.
y: Considering audience and purpose makes our writing more effective.
Different readers and purposes require different approaches.
This is a key skill that professional writers use every day.
eg: We write neatly and clearly when our work will be displayed in the classroom.
We use a friendly tone in a message to a classmate.
We present our work with a title and our name for a school assignment.
We use polite, formal language when writing to the principal.
We make our poster eye-catching with big writing and colour for a class display.
We consider whether our reader needs more detail or less.

VC2EALCLW14.w: We are learning to check our writing for mistakes and try to fix them.
y: Checking our own work is an important step in becoming an independent writer.
Finding and fixing errors improves the quality of our writing.
This habit helps us learn from our mistakes and avoid them next time.
eg: We reread our writing and fix any spelling mistakes we notice.
We check that every sentence has a capital letter and a full stop.
We read our writing aloud to hear if anything sounds wrong.
We check that our sentences make sense by reading them back.
We look for missing words and add them in.
We ask a partner to read our writing and tell us if anything is unclear.

VC2EALCLW15.w: We are learning to use our home language to help us with our English writing.
y: Our home language is a rich source of ideas, structures and vocabulary.
Using our first language strategically helps us produce better English writing.
This is a strength that bilingual writers should embrace.
eg: We think about what we want to write in our home language first.
We use a bilingual dictionary to find English words we need.
We plan our writing structure using patterns we know from our language.
We discuss our writing topic with a friend in our home language.
We compare how writing is done in our language versus English.
We use cultural knowledge from our language as content for English writing.

VC2EALCLW16.w: We are learning to participate in shared writing activities where the class writes together.
y: Shared writing shows us how experienced writers make decisions about text.
It gives us a chance to contribute ideas in a supported environment.
Seeing writing composed in real time helps us understand the writing process.
eg: We suggest ideas or words for a class text the teacher is writing.
We help decide what should come next in a shared story.
We offer a sentence or phrase during a group writing activity.
We contribute to a class letter, report or recount.
We discuss word choices as a class and vote on the best option.
We watch the teacher model writing techniques and then suggest improvements.

VC2EALC1L01.w: We are learning to understand a simple spoken text with some help from our teacher.
y: Understanding spoken texts is essential for learning in all secondary school subjects.
With teacher support, we can access content that pushes us to grow our English.
Each spoken text we comprehend builds our listening skills and vocabulary.
eg: We listen to a teacher's explanation of a new topic and identify the key points.
We watch a short educational video and understand the main information with teacher guidance.
We follow a set of spoken instructions for a classroom task.
We listen to a classmate's presentation and understand their main argument.
We comprehend a read-aloud of a simple text with teacher pauses for checking understanding.
We follow along during a class discussion, understanding the flow of ideas with some help.

VC2EALC1L02.w: We are learning to pick out important information from short spoken texts about topics we know, with teacher help.
y: Extracting key information is critical for note-taking and study skills.
Personal connection to topics makes information easier to find and remember.
This skill builds towards independent listening in academic contexts.
eg: We listen to a talk about a familiar topic and note down three key facts.
We identify the who, what and where in a short spoken recount.
We hear a description of a process and extract the main steps.
We listen to an interview and identify the speaker's main point.
We extract specific details from a news clip about a familiar topic.
We listen to a classmate's experience and identify the key events they describe.

VC2EALC1L03.w: We are learning to take part in familiar social situations and school routines with teacher support.
y: Participating in school routines helps us feel part of the school community.
Social participation builds both our English skills and our confidence.
Teacher support ensures we can engage meaningfully while still developing our language.
eg: We participate in roll call and follow morning routines.
We navigate the canteen by ordering food using appropriate language.
We follow classroom procedures like transitioning between subjects.
We interact with school staff, such as asking the librarian for help.
We participate in group activities during homeroom or pastoral care.
We follow school-wide routines like assembly procedures and fire drills.

VC2EALC1L04.w: We are learning to prepare and deliver a simple spoken response about a topic we know, with teacher help.
y: Preparing spoken responses teaches us to organise our ideas in English.
Presenting to others builds our spoken fluency and public speaking confidence.
This prepares us for oral assessments and class presentations.
eg: We prepare a short talk about our home country or culture and present it to a small group.
We give a prepared response to a question in a class discussion.
We present a brief summary of something we read to the class.
We share a personal experience using a prepared structure.
We present a simple explanation of something we learned in a subject.
We prepare and deliver a short introduction of ourselves to a new group.

VC2EALC1L05.w: We are learning to understand and produce a small range of short spoken text types like recounts, descriptions and instructions.
y: Knowing different spoken text types helps us communicate for different purposes.
Producing as well as understanding them builds both our receptive and expressive English.
This prepares us for the varied spoken tasks required in secondary school.
eg: We understand and give a short recount of an event in time order.
We follow and give simple spoken instructions for a task.
We understand and produce a brief description of a person, place or thing.
We listen to and give a short explanation of how something works.
We understand a simple narrative told aloud and retell it.
We recognise the difference between a recount and a set of instructions when we hear them.

VC2EALC1L06.w: We are learning to put events in order when we speak, using time words like 'first', 'then', 'after that' and 'finally'.
y: Chronological ordering is essential for clear recounts and explanations.
Time markers guide our listeners through the sequence of events.
This is a skill we use in all subjects when describing processes or events.
eg: We retell what happened during a class excursion using time order.
We explain the steps of an experiment using 'first', 'next', 'then' and 'finally'.
We describe our daily routine in order from morning to evening.
We tell a short story with events in chronological sequence.
We use 'before' and 'after' to explain the relationship between events.
We sequence a historical event using 'initially', 'subsequently' and 'eventually'.

VC2EALC1L07.w: We are learning to use different verb forms with growing consistency when we speak.
y: Consistent verb use makes our speaking clearer and more natural.
Different verb forms let us talk about past, present and future events.
Improving accuracy with verbs is one of the most impactful grammar goals.
eg: We use past tense consistently when retelling something that happened.
We say 'I am studying' for current actions and 'I studied' for past ones.
We use 'will' and 'going to' for future events.
We try to maintain one tense throughout a spoken recount.
We self-correct when we accidentally switch tenses.
We use common irregular past tenses like 'went', 'saw', 'took' and 'made'.

VC2EALC1L08.w: We are learning to use a small range of common describing words and phrases in our spoken English.
y: Descriptive language makes our speaking more precise and engaging.
Even a small range of well-used descriptions significantly improves communication.
This builds towards more sophisticated and varied English expression.
eg: We describe objects using size, colour and shape words.
We use words like 'interesting', 'difficult', 'important' and 'popular' to give opinions.
We describe people using appearance and personality words.
We use comparative language: 'bigger than', 'more interesting than', 'the best'.
We describe places using words like 'crowded', 'peaceful', 'modern' and 'traditional'.
We use emotion words like 'nervous', 'confident', 'confused' and 'relieved'.

VC2EALC1L09.w: We are learning to recognise and use key English words and ideas from familiar spoken texts.
y: Key vocabulary is the gateway to understanding any spoken text.
Using these words ourselves helps us remember and internalise them.
Context helps us understand and use words more accurately.
eg: We learn and use key words from a topic we are studying, like 'ecosystem' or 'migration'.
We pick up new vocabulary from a class discussion and try using it.
We use words from a listened text in our own sentences.
We recognise key words in an announcement and act on them.
We build a personal vocabulary list from words we hear in class.
We try using new words from a video or podcast in conversation.

VC2EALC1L10.w: We are learning to recognise and copy English pronunciation patterns.
y: Recognising patterns helps us predict how new words might sound.
Copying pronunciation builds natural-sounding English speech.
Good pronunciation supports both our speaking and our spelling.
eg: We notice and copy the stress pattern in two-syllable words like 'TEACHer' and 'beFORE'.
We reproduce the pronunciation of words that rhyme: 'light', 'night', 'right'.
We copy the teacher's pronunciation of challenging sounds like 'th' and 'r'.
We practise word families with the same pattern: 'nation', 'station', 'education'.
We mimic the natural rhythm of English sentences when speaking.
We listen to and reproduce the pronunciation of words with silent letters.

VC2EALC1L11.w: We are learning to use stress and intonation patterns with more confidence and fluency in our spoken English.
y: Confident use of stress and intonation makes our English sound natural.
These patterns convey meaning that words alone cannot express.
Fluent use of these features shows growing mastery of English.
eg: We stress content words in sentences to convey meaning naturally.
We use rising intonation for questions and falling intonation for statements.
We vary our intonation to express different emotions.
We use contrastive stress: 'I said TUESDAY, not THURSDAY'.
We produce fluent sentences with natural English rhythm.
We use intonation to show we have more to say or that we are finished speaking.

VC2EALC1L12.w: We are learning to notice what makes spoken English different from other languages.
y: Noticing differences helps us understand why some English features are challenging.
It prevents us from transferring patterns from our home language that don't work in English.
This linguistic awareness makes us more effective language learners.
eg: We compare how English and our language form questions.
We notice that English uses auxiliary verbs like 'do' and 'have' differently.
We compare the sound systems of English and our home language.
We notice that English word stress can change meaning: 'REcord' vs 'reCORD'.
We discuss how English and our language handle formality differently.
We compare how negation works in English versus our home language.

VC2EALC1L13.w: We are learning to ask people to repeat or explain when we don't understand, and to ask for help when we need it.
y: Asking for clarification is an essential communication strategy in any language.
It shows self-awareness and a commitment to understanding.
These phrases are among the most useful we will learn for real-world English.
eg: We say 'Could you repeat that, please?' when we miss something.
We ask 'What does ___ mean?' to learn new vocabulary.
We say 'I didn't quite catch that — could you say it differently?'
We ask 'Can you give me an example?' to help us understand.
We request help by saying 'I'm not sure how to do this — can you help?'
We say 'Sorry, I don't understand' and wait for the speaker to rephrase.

VC2EALC1L14.w: We are learning to use our home language as a resource to help with English listening and speaking.
y: Our home language gives us concepts, skills and knowledge we can apply to English.
Strategic use of both languages accelerates our English development.
Multilingualism is a cognitive advantage, not a barrier.
eg: We translate complex ideas from our language to help express them in English.
We use our knowledge of a topic from our language to follow an English discussion.
We compare grammar rules across our languages to understand English better.
We use our home language to plan what we want to say before speaking English.
We recognise cognates between our language and English.
We discuss difficult concepts with a bilingual peer to clarify understanding.

VC2EALC1L15.w: We are learning to listen and speak at the right times during small-group activities.
y: Effective group communication is essential for collaborative learning.
Knowing when to listen and when to speak shows social and linguistic awareness.
These skills are crucial for success in secondary school and beyond.
eg: We wait for others to finish before sharing our own ideas.
We listen actively to group members and respond to their points.
We contribute our opinion during a group decision-making activity.
We ask questions to clarify what a group member meant.
We summarise the group's ideas to check we all agree.
We ensure quieter members are included by inviting them to speak.

VC2EALC1R01.w: We are learning to understand the main ideas of both familiar and unfamiliar texts, with teacher support.
y: Tackling unfamiliar texts pushes our reading skills to grow.
Understanding main ideas helps us engage with a wider range of content.
Teacher support helps us bridge the gap between what we know and what is new.
eg: We read a text on a new topic and identify the main idea with teacher guidance.
We find the main theme in a short story we haven't encountered before.
We read a news article and summarise the key point in our own words.
We identify the thesis or argument in a simple persuasive text.
We read an unfamiliar information text and state what it is mainly about.
We compare the main ideas of a familiar and an unfamiliar text on similar topics.

VC2EALC1R02.w: We are learning to find specific information in simple texts with predictable language, with teacher help.
y: Locating specific information is a key skill for research and study.
Predictable language structures help us know where to look.
This builds towards the independent information-finding skills we need for senior school.
eg: We scan a simple text to find the answer to a specific question.
We locate dates, names and numbers in an information text.
We find specific evidence to support an answer about a text.
We use text features like headings and bold words to find information quickly.
We extract key details from a biographical text.
We find specific facts in a comparison text about two topics.

VC2EALC1R03.w: We are learning to find specific information from simple charts, tables and maps, with teacher help.
y: Charts, tables and maps are common in academic and everyday texts.
Reading them accurately is essential for maths, science, geography and other subjects.
These visual formats present information efficiently once we know how to read them.
eg: We read a simple data table and find a specific value.
We locate a place on a map using the legend and grid references.
We interpret a bar chart and identify the highest and lowest values.
We read a timetable and find out when a specific event occurs.
We use a line graph to identify a trend over time.
We extract data from a pie chart and explain what proportion each section represents.

VC2EALC1R04.w: We are learning to use clues from the text to work out the meaning of unfamiliar words, with teacher help.
y: Context clues help us understand new words without always needing a dictionary.
This strategy allows us to keep reading fluently through challenging texts.
Building this skill helps us learn new vocabulary naturally from our reading.
eg: We read the sentence around an unfamiliar word and guess its meaning.
We use a picture near the word to help understand what it means.
We look for a definition or explanation within the text itself.
We think about what word would make sense in the context.
We use our knowledge of the topic to help figure out a new word.
We check our guess by re-reading the sentence with our predicted meaning.

VC2EALC1R05.w: We are learning to use headings, images and other visual features to predict what a text will be about.
y: Previewing text features activates our prior knowledge and prepares us for reading.
It makes reading more efficient because we know what to expect.
This is a strategy skilled readers use automatically.
eg: We look at a text's headings and predict the topics covered.
We study images and captions to anticipate the content.
We use the table of contents to get an overview of a book.
We look at bold or highlighted words to identify key concepts.
We use a text's layout to distinguish between different sections.
We skim the first sentence of each paragraph to get the gist before reading in detail.

VC2EALC1R06.w: We are learning to understand what words like 'it', 'they', 'this' and 'which' refer to in a text.
y: Cohesive devices connect ideas and prevent texts from being repetitive.
Understanding what they refer to is essential for following a text's meaning.
This skill prevents misunderstandings when reading complex sentences.
eg: We identify what 'it' refers to in a sentence like 'The dog ran away. It was scared.'
We figure out who 'they' means in a paragraph about several characters.
We understand what 'this' refers back to in an argument.
We trace the referent of 'which' in a relative clause.
We follow a text where a noun is replaced by a pronoun several times.
We identify what 'these results' points to in a science report.

VC2EALC1R07.w: We are learning to recognise basic sentence patterns and question patterns in English.
y: Recognising patterns helps us read sentences more quickly and accurately.
Understanding question patterns helps us respond appropriately.
This grammar awareness supports both our reading and our writing.
eg: We recognise the subject-verb-object pattern in 'The student completed the task.'
We identify questions that start with 'Who', 'What', 'Where', 'When', 'Why' and 'How'.
We recognise yes/no questions that start with 'Is', 'Are', 'Do' and 'Can'.
We see the pattern in 'There is/There are' sentences.
We recognise compound sentences joined by 'and', 'but' or 'or'.
We identify command sentences that begin with a verb.

VC2EALC1R08.w: We are learning to recognise common commands and instructions in texts.
y: Imperatives are used in procedures, recipes, rules and classroom instructions.
Recognising them helps us follow written instructions correctly.
Understanding commands is essential for tasks across all subjects.
eg: We recognise 'Cut the paper in half' as a command.
We identify imperatives in a recipe: 'Mix', 'Stir', 'Bake'.
We follow written instructions for a science experiment: 'Pour', 'Measure', 'Record'.
We read classroom rules like 'Listen carefully' and 'Raise your hand'.
We recognise commands in a game: 'Throw the dice', 'Move three spaces'.
We identify imperatives in safety instructions: 'Do not run', 'Wait here'.

VC2EALC1R09.w: We are learning to understand simple describing words and phrases within sentences we read.
y: Descriptive language gives us important details about people, places and things.
Understanding it helps us visualise what the author is describing.
This improves both our reading comprehension and our own descriptive writing.
eg: We understand 'the ancient ruins' and know it means very old.
We read 'She spoke in a quiet, gentle voice' and imagine how she sounds.
We interpret 'a vast, barren landscape' to picture the scene.
We understand comparative descriptions like 'taller than' and 'the most colourful'.
We read 'the exhausted traveller' and understand the person's condition.
We interpret 'a thriving community' and understand it means successful and growing.

VC2EALC1R10.w: We are learning to read and recognise words for everyday things, actions, and topic-specific words we have been taught.
y: Recognising these words helps us read more fluently across all our subjects.
Topic vocabulary connects our reading to our classroom learning.
Building word recognition is the fastest path to confident reading.
eg: We read everyday words like 'responsible', 'communicate' and 'collaborate'.
We recognise science words we have studied like 'photosynthesis' and 'molecule'.
We read maths vocabulary like 'equation', 'ratio' and 'percentage'.
We identify action words in instructions: 'analyse', 'compare', 'evaluate'.
We recognise humanities vocabulary like 'democracy', 'colonisation' and 'culture'.
We read technology words like 'algorithm', 'database' and 'interface'.

VC2EALC1R11.w: We are learning to pronounce words correctly when reading aloud, using our vocabulary knowledge and sound-letter rules.
y: Correct pronunciation when reading aloud builds fluency and confidence.
It connects our reading to our spoken vocabulary.
Good oral reading is important for presentations and class participation.
eg: We pronounce multi-syllable words accurately when reading a passage aloud.
We use sound-letter patterns to attempt unfamiliar words when reading.
We read a paragraph aloud with natural pronunciation and rhythm.
We self-correct pronunciation errors when they don't sound right.
We apply stress rules correctly when reading words like 'present' (noun vs verb).
We read dialogue in a story with appropriate expression and pronunciation.

VC2EALC1R12.w: We are learning how punctuation marks help us understand what we read.
y: Punctuation guides our reading and affects how we interpret meaning.
Understanding it helps us read with correct phrasing and expression.
This skill is essential for accurate comprehension of all written texts.
eg: We pause at full stops and commas when reading.
We use question marks to identify when a question is being asked.
We recognise speech marks and understand that dialogue is being quoted.
We see an apostrophe and understand it shows possession or a contraction.
We interpret a semicolon as a pause between related ideas.
We understand that dashes can add extra information or emphasis.

VC2EALC1R13.w: We are learning to notice what makes English texts different from texts in our home language.
y: Understanding these differences helps us read English more effectively.
It builds our metalinguistic awareness across both languages.
This knowledge helps us transfer reading skills between languages.
eg: We compare how English and our language structure sentences in written texts.
We notice different punctuation conventions between the two languages.
We compare how our languages handle formality in written texts.
We observe differences in text layout and organisation.
We compare how English and our language use images in texts.
We discuss differences in script, direction of reading and spacing.

VC2EALC1R14.w: We are learning to choose appropriate books and texts to read, with teacher guidance.
y: Selecting the right texts ensures we are reading at a level that promotes growth.
Making our own choices builds reading motivation and independence.
Teacher guidance helps us find texts that are both enjoyable and appropriately challenging.
eg: We browse the school library and select books at our reading level with teacher advice.
We choose texts related to topics we are studying in other subjects.
We select both fiction and non-fiction to broaden our reading experience.
