We try a new genre the teacher recommends.
We choose a text that is slightly more challenging to stretch our skills.
We select engaging texts that motivate us to read independently.

VC2EALC1R15.w: We are learning to use our home language as a resource to help with English reading.
y: Our home language literacy skills are directly transferable to English reading.
Using both languages strategically deepens our comprehension.
This is a proven strategy for effective bilingual learning.
eg: We use our knowledge of a topic from our language to help understand an English text.
We apply reading strategies from our first language, like scanning and skimming.
We translate key sentences to verify our understanding.
We compare text types we know from our language with English text types.
We draw on our cultural knowledge to interpret texts about diverse topics.
We discuss a text with a bilingual peer to check our comprehension.

VC2EALC1R16.w: We are learning to participate in teacher-led reading activities.
y: Teacher-led reading gives us access to texts we might not manage alone.
It models how skilled readers approach and interpret texts.
Active participation helps us build the skills for independent reading.
eg: We follow along as the teacher reads a text aloud to the class.
We take turns reading sections of a shared text.
We participate in guided reading groups with the teacher.
We answer comprehension questions during a teacher-led reading session.
We discuss vocabulary and ideas that arise during shared reading.
We contribute our thoughts about a text during whole-class reading activities.

VC2EALC1W01.w: We are learning to write short, simple texts for different social purposes, with teacher help.
y: Social writing is practical and immediately useful in our daily life.
Different purposes require different writing approaches.
This builds our ability to communicate in English for real-world situations.
eg: We write a simple email to a teacher about a school matter.
We write a thank you note to someone who helped us.
We write a message to a classmate inviting them to an event.
We write a brief apology or explanation for being late.
We write a birthday message for a friend.
We write a simple request for information.

VC2EALC1W02.w: We are learning to write short information texts about topics we know, using simple sentences.
y: Information writing is essential for school assignments across all subjects.
Organising facts into simple sentences is the foundation of academic writing.
This skill builds towards writing longer, more detailed texts independently.
eg: We write a short report about a topic we studied in science.
We write a description of a process using simple, clear sentences.
We create a fact file about a country, animal or historical figure.
We write a brief summary of what we learned from a class activity.
We write an information paragraph about our local community.
We compose simple sentences that present facts about a topic.

VC2EALC1W03.w: We are learning to write to share our own personal ideas and thoughts, with teacher help.
y: Personal writing helps us express who we are in our new language.
It builds a connection between our identity and our English skills.
Writing about our ideas in English gives us a voice in our school community.
eg: We write about our hopes for the future.
We write a personal reflection on something we learned.
We write about an experience that was important to us.
We express our opinion on a topic discussed in class.
We write about our cultural traditions and what they mean to us.
We write about how we felt during our first days at our new school.

VC2EALC1W04.w: We are learning to label images and diagrams using vocabulary we have learned in class.
y: Labelling connects visual information with specific English vocabulary.
It reinforces vocabulary learning and demonstrates our understanding.
This skill is important for science, geography and other visual subjects.
eg: We label the parts of a cell in a science diagram.
We add labels to a map showing geographical features.
We label parts of a machine or device in a technology class.
We annotate a picture with descriptive vocabulary.
We label a historical photograph with key terms we have learned.
We add vocabulary labels to a poster for a class display.

VC2EALC1W05.w: We are learning to organise our writing and try to use the right structure for the type of text we are creating.
y: Appropriate text structure makes our writing clear and effective.
Each text type has a structure that readers expect to see.
Learning structures helps us write more independently and confidently.
eg: We use introduction-body-conclusion structure for an information text.
We organise a recount chronologically with a beginning, middle and end.
We use the goal-materials-steps format for a procedure.
We attempt to structure a narrative with orientation, complication and resolution.
We organise a description from general to specific details.
We write a letter with greeting, body paragraphs and a closing.

VC2EALC1W06.w: We are learning to use simple connecting words and devices to hold our writing together.
y: Cohesive devices link ideas and make our writing easier to follow.
They prevent our writing from being a series of disconnected sentences.
This is a key step towards producing mature, connected writing.
eg: We use 'and', 'but', 'so' and 'because' to join ideas.
We use pronouns like 'he', 'she', 'it' and 'they' to avoid repeating nouns.
We use time words like 'first', 'next' and 'finally' to sequence ideas.
We use 'also' and 'for example' to add information.
We use 'however' to introduce a contrasting idea.
We use 'this' and 'these' to refer back to ideas mentioned earlier.

VC2EALC1W07.w: We are learning to write single-clause sentences or use 'and', 'but', 'because' and 'when' to join clauses together.
y: Learning to combine clauses helps us write longer, more informative sentences.
This is an important step from simple to more complex English writing.
Understanding clause combination improves both our writing and reading.
eg: We write a simple sentence like 'The experiment was successful.'
We join two ideas with 'and': 'We mixed the chemicals and observed the reaction.'
We show contrast with 'but': 'I wanted to go but it was too late.'
We give a reason with 'because': 'The plant died because it didn't get enough water.'
We show when with 'when': 'When the bell rang, we packed up.'
We use 'although' to write: 'Although it was difficult, I finished the task.'

VC2EALC1W08.w: We are learning to experiment with different tenses and use some common irregular past tense verbs correctly in our writing.
y: Experimenting with tenses helps us write about different times and situations.
Irregular past tenses are common in English and need specific practice.
Getting these right significantly improves the clarity of our writing.
eg: We write a recount using past tense: 'went', 'saw', 'ate', 'made'.
We try writing in present tense for an information text.
We use future tense with 'will' for predictions.
We correctly write irregular past tenses like 'thought', 'caught' and 'brought'.
We experiment with present perfect: 'I have visited three countries.'
We try to maintain consistent tense throughout a paragraph.

VC2EALC1W09.w: We are learning to use words like 'very', 'some', 'many', 'a few' and 'most' to express different amounts and degrees in our writing.
y: Qualifiers and quantifiers let us express precise amounts and degrees.
They add nuance and accuracy to our writing.
Using them correctly shows growing sophistication in English expression.
eg: We write 'Many students enjoyed the excursion' to show a large number.
We use 'very' to intensify: 'The experiment was very interesting.'
We write 'A few people disagreed' to show a small number.
We use 'most' to show majority: 'Most of the class understood.'
We write 'quite difficult' to moderate an adjective.
We use 'some' for an indefinite amount: 'Some animals migrate in winter.'

VC2EALC1W10.w: We are learning to use a range of simple subject-specific vocabulary in our writing.
y: Subject vocabulary shows we understand the content we are writing about.
Using the right terms makes our writing more precise and academic.
This is essential for success in all secondary school subjects.
eg: We use science terms like 'evaporation', 'condensation' and 'precipitation'.
We use maths vocabulary like 'equation', 'variable' and 'calculation'.
We include humanities words like 'government', 'territory' and 'society'.
We use English literature terms like 'character', 'setting' and 'plot'.
We include technology vocabulary like 'design', 'prototype' and 'evaluate'.
We use PE terms like 'technique', 'strategy' and 'fitness'.

VC2EALC1W11.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds and letters to attempt spelling unfamiliar words.
y: Attempting to spell new words independently shows confidence and growing skill.
Sound-letter knowledge gives us a strategy for any word we encounter.
Even imperfect attempts help us learn and improve over time.
eg: We sound out a new word and write the letters that match each sound.
We use known letter patterns to attempt the spelling of an unfamiliar word.
We break a multi-syllable word into parts and spell each one.
We use analogies with known words to spell new ones.
We attempt a word, check it in a dictionary and learn the correct spelling.
We use our knowledge of prefixes and suffixes to help spell new words.

VC2EALC1W12.w: We are learning to use common punctuation like full stops, capitals, question marks and commas with some consistency.
y: Consistent punctuation makes our writing clear and easy to read.
It shows we understand the basic conventions of written English.
Building good punctuation habits now supports all our future writing.
eg: We start every sentence with a capital letter and end with a full stop.
We use question marks at the end of questions consistently.
We begin to use commas in lists.
We capitalise proper nouns and the pronoun 'I'.
We use exclamation marks for emphasis where appropriate.
We check our punctuation during editing and fix inconsistencies.

VC2EALC1W13.w: We are learning to use the right features for the type of text we are writing.
y: Each text type has specific language features that make it effective.
Using the right features shows we understand how different texts work.
This knowledge helps us write appropriately for different tasks and subjects.
eg: We use past tense and time connectives in a recount.
We use technical vocabulary and present tense in an information report.
We use command verbs in a procedure.
We use descriptive language and dialogue in a narrative.
We use persuasive language and opinion words in an argument.
We use formal language and a clear structure in a letter.

VC2EALC1W14.w: We are learning to revise and edit our writing with support, using corrections and suggestions from others.
y: Revision is how good writing becomes great writing.
Incorporating feedback teaches us to see our writing from a reader's perspective.
This process builds our independent editing skills over time.
eg: We read through our draft and fix errors the teacher has highlighted.
We add more detail to a section the teacher identified as too brief.
We reorganise paragraphs based on teacher feedback about structure.
We correct verb tenses that the teacher has pointed out.
We replace vague words with more specific ones as suggested.
We use a peer review to identify areas for improvement in our writing.

VC2EALC1W15.w: We are learning to use our home language to help with our English writing.
y: Our home language provides concepts, structures and strategies we can transfer to English.
Bilingual writing processes produce stronger English texts.
Using our full linguistic knowledge is an asset, not a limitation.
eg: We plan our writing in our home language before drafting in English.
We use a bilingual dictionary to find precise English vocabulary.
We think about how we would structure an argument in our language and apply it to English.
We discuss our topic with a peer in our language to generate ideas.
We draw on our knowledge of formal writing in our language.
We use cultural examples from our background to enrich our English writing.

VC2EALC1W16.w: We are learning to participate in writing activities that the teacher leads.
y: Teacher-led writing gives us models and support for developing our skills.
Participating actively helps us learn techniques we can use independently.
It creates a supportive environment where we can take risks with our writing.
eg: We contribute ideas during a shared writing session.
We follow along as the teacher models how to write a specific text type.
We practise writing techniques the teacher demonstrates.
We participate in collaborative writing activities guided by the teacher.
We respond to writing prompts the teacher provides.
We discuss writing choices and strategies during teacher-led writing lessons.

VC2EALC2L01.w: We are learning to understand the main themes and ideas of spoken texts that rely more on language than on visual support.
y: Many academic spoken texts have fewer visual clues — we need to understand from the words alone.
This prepares us for lectures, discussions and complex explanations in secondary school.
Understanding context-reduced language is a significant step towards English fluency.
eg: We follow a teacher's verbal explanation of an abstract concept with some support.
We understand the main argument in a short spoken text without accompanying images.
We listen to a podcast clip and identify the key themes.
We follow a class discussion about ideas and concepts, not just objects or actions.
We understand a spoken summary of a topic without needing to see the text.
We listen to a peer explain a concept and grasp the main point from words alone.

VC2EALC2L02.w: We are learning to find specific information from spoken or visual texts, using the teacher's guide questions to help.
y: Guide questions direct our listening to the most important details.
This structured approach helps us develop independent note-taking skills.
Targeted listening is more effective than trying to catch everything at once.
eg: We listen to a video and answer pre-set questions about specific facts.
We use guiding questions to focus on key information during a lecture.
We watch a documentary clip and extract answers to questions the teacher provided.
We listen to a speaker and note down information that answers specific prompts.
We use teacher-provided questions to focus our listening during an interview.
We watch a presentation and record specific details the guide questions asked for.

VC2EALC2L03.w: We are learning to participate in a growing range of school and classroom routines with confidence.
y: Handling more routines independently shows our English is becoming functional.
School routines are opportunities to practise English in natural, meaningful ways.
Building routine competence helps us focus on learning content, not just language.
eg: We navigate the school library system independently.
We participate in subject-specific routines like science lab procedures.
We manage locker, canteen and timetable routines without needing extra help.
We follow instructions for excursions and special school events.
We participate in whole-school activities like assemblies and sports carnivals.
We handle administrative tasks like filling in forms and handing in work.

VC2EALC2L04.w: We are learning to prepare and deliver a short formal talk on a topic we have researched, using notes and visual aids.
y: Prepared presentations are a common assessment in secondary school.
Using notes and props shows we can plan and organise our ideas.
This builds our confidence in formal English speaking.
eg: We research a topic and present key findings to the class using dot-point notes.
We create a poster or slideshow to support our spoken presentation.
We use cue cards to stay on track while presenting.
We bring in objects or images as props to enhance our talk.
We present research on a current issue to a small group.
We rehearse our talk to improve fluency and timing before presenting.

VC2EALC2L05.w: We are learning to recognise and use different text types and structures across our school subjects.
y: Different subjects use different text types — knowing them helps us in every class.
Understanding structures helps us both comprehend and produce subject-specific texts.
This is essential for academic success across the curriculum.
eg: We recognise the structure of a science report versus a history essay.
We understand how a maths problem text is structured differently from an English narrative.
We produce a spoken recount for English and a spoken procedure for technology.
We identify the features of a geographical explanation versus a literary analysis.
We adapt our speaking style for a formal debate versus an informal discussion.
We recognise how persuasive texts work differently in English and humanities.

VC2EALC2L06.w: We are learning to use connecting words and phrases to link our ideas smoothly when we speak.
y: Cohesive devices make our spoken English flow naturally from one idea to the next.
They help listeners follow our reasoning and understand our arguments.
Using them shows growing control of academic English.
eg: We use 'firstly', 'secondly' and 'finally' to organise points in a talk.
We connect ideas with 'in addition', 'however' and 'as a result'.
We use 'for example' and 'such as' to provide specific evidence.
We use 'on the other hand' to introduce a contrasting viewpoint.
We say 'in conclusion' to signal we are summarising our main points.
We use 'therefore' to show a logical outcome of our argument.

VC2EALC2L07.w: We are learning to use a range of verb forms with increasing accuracy when we speak.
y: Accurate verb use is fundamental to being understood in English.
Different verb forms express different times, conditions and possibilities.
Improving accuracy shows real progress in our English development.
eg: We consistently use past tense in recounts without shifting to present.
We use conditional forms: 'If we had more time, we could do more research.'
We use passive voice in appropriate contexts: 'The experiment was completed.'
We employ present perfect to discuss experiences: 'I have studied three languages.'
We use future forms accurately: 'We will present our findings next week.'
We self-monitor and correct verb form errors during conversation.

VC2EALC2L08.w: We are learning to use simple describing phrases to add detail and precision to our spoken English.
y: Descriptive phrases enrich our communication and help listeners visualise our ideas.
They allow us to express opinions and observations more precisely.
Building this skill prepares us for more sophisticated academic language.
eg: We describe a character as 'a determined young athlete who never gives up'.
We describe an experiment as 'a carefully controlled investigation using precise measurements'.
We talk about 'the rapidly growing city' or 'the slowly declining population'.
We describe emotions using phrases like 'increasingly confident' or 'deeply concerned'.
We describe a process as 'a complex series of interconnected steps'.
We use phrases like 'the most significant factor' and 'a relatively minor issue'.

VC2EALC2L09.w: We are learning to use familiar vocabulary in careful ways to express subtle differences in meaning.
y: Nuanced vocabulary use shows sophisticated thinking in English.
Choosing the right word from our vocabulary makes our meaning precise.
This skill distinguishes competent English users from beginners.
eg: We choose 'challenging' over 'hard' and 'straightforward' over 'easy' for academic discussion.
We distinguish between 'suggest' and 'argue' when discussing an author's purpose.
We use 'contribute' instead of 'help' in a formal context.
We select 'significant' rather than 'big' when discussing research findings.
We choose between 'frequently', 'occasionally' and 'rarely' for different degrees.
We use 'demonstrate' instead of 'show' in an academic presentation.

VC2EALC2L10.w: We are learning to use clear, accurate pronunciation to support our communication in English.
y: Clear pronunciation ensures our ideas are communicated effectively.
It prevents misunderstandings and builds listener confidence in our speaking.
Good pronunciation supports our academic participation and social integration.
eg: We pronounce academic vocabulary correctly during class presentations.
We articulate key words clearly in group discussions.
We adjust our pronunciation when we notice a listener hasn't understood.
We use correct word stress in multi-syllable words.
We pronounce words with challenging English sounds accurately.
We speak clearly enough for unfamiliar listeners to understand us.

VC2EALC2L11.w: We are learning to understand and use stress, intonation and body language to enhance our communication.
y: These features add layers of meaning beyond the words themselves.
Understanding them helps us interpret speakers' intentions and emotions.
Using them effectively makes us more engaging and persuasive speakers.
eg: We use contrastive stress to highlight key information: 'We need MORE time, not more resources.'
We interpret a speaker's sarcasm through their intonation.
We use appropriate body language when presenting formally.
We vary our pace and volume for different parts of a presentation.
We read non-verbal cues from listeners to adjust our communication.
We use pausing effectively to let important points land with our audience.

VC2EALC2L12.w: We are learning to identify what is similar and different between spoken English and our home language.
y: Comparative linguistic awareness helps us understand both languages more deeply.
It helps us identify why certain English features are challenging for us.
This knowledge makes us more strategic and effective language learners.
eg: We compare how polite requests are formed in English and our language.
We notice differences in how emotions are expressed verbally across languages.
We identify similar and different sounds between English and our language.
We compare how both languages handle formal versus informal registers.
We discuss differences in conversational turn-taking across cultures.
We compare how English and our language use stress to change meaning.

VC2EALC2L13.w: We are learning to notice and fix aspects of our spoken English that prevent clear communication.
y: Self-monitoring is a sign of an advanced, self-directed language learner.
Fixing errors that impede communication has the biggest impact on clarity.
This skill helps us improve steadily without relying entirely on teacher correction.
eg: We notice when our pronunciation of a word causes confusion and adjust.
We rephrase a sentence when we see the listener doesn't understand.
We self-correct grammar errors that change the meaning of our message.
We adjust our vocabulary choice when a word doesn't convey our intended meaning.
We slow down or speak more clearly when we notice someone is struggling to follow.
We reflect on recurring errors and make a plan to practise them.

VC2EALC2L14.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to strengthen our English listening and speaking.
y: Our home language provides a deep well of linguistic and cultural knowledge.
Strategic use of both languages enhances all aspects of our English.
Advanced bilingual strategies are a hallmark of effective multilingual learners.
eg: We use our knowledge of complex concepts in our language to access English academic content.
We draw on our cultural storytelling traditions when presenting in English.
We compare rhetorical strategies in our language and English to enhance our persuasion skills.
We use our understanding of formal register in our language to inform our English academic speaking.
We translate complex ideas internally to support comprehension of spoken English.
We use cognates and borrowed words between our languages to expand our English vocabulary.

VC2EALC2L15.w: We are learning to interact with classmates effectively during regular pair and group-work activities.
y: Collaborative work is central to learning in secondary school.
Effective group interaction builds both our English and our teamwork skills.
These skills prepare us for the workplace and further education.
eg: We share our ideas and listen to others during a group research task.
We negotiate roles and responsibilities within a group project.
We provide constructive feedback to a partner on their work.
We participate in peer-assessment activities using appropriate language.
We manage disagreements in a group by discussing options respectfully.
We contribute equally to group presentations and written tasks.

VC2EALC2R01.w: We are learning to identify the main idea in simple texts, with teacher support.
y: Identifying main ideas is the foundation of reading comprehension across all subjects.
Teacher support helps us develop strategies we can eventually use independently.
This skill is essential for summarising, note-taking and academic study.
eg: We read a text and state the main idea in our own words with teacher prompting.
We distinguish main ideas from supporting details in an information text.
We identify the key argument in a short persuasive text.
We find the main theme of a short story or poem.
We use topic sentences to help locate the main idea in each paragraph.
We compare the main ideas of two texts on a related topic.

VC2EALC2R02.w: We are learning to find specific information in both printed and digital texts, with teacher support.
y: Information retrieval is a critical skill for research and study.
Working with both print and digital texts prepares us for modern learning.
Teacher support helps us develop efficient search and scan strategies.
eg: We scan a website for specific information the teacher asks about.
We locate facts in a printed textbook chapter using headings and index.
We find answers to comprehension questions by re-reading relevant sections.
We search a digital text using keywords to find specific information.
We extract data from an online source for a research task.
We locate specific quotes in a text to support our answers.

VC2EALC2R03.w: We are learning to find specific information from subject-related diagrams, graphs and charts, with teacher support.
y: Visual data is used extensively in science, maths, geography and other subjects.
Reading these visuals accurately is essential for academic success.
Teacher support helps us learn the conventions of different visual formats.
eg: We read a climate graph and identify the hottest month and the wettest month.
We extract data from a science results table.
We interpret a flow diagram showing a biological or chemical process.
We read a population graph and identify trends over time.
We use a map legend to interpret geographical data.
We compare data across different columns of a table.

VC2EALC2R04.w: We are learning to use clues from the context to predict the meaning of unfamiliar words and ideas.
y: Context prediction is one of the most powerful vocabulary-building strategies.
It allows us to read complex texts without stopping at every unknown word.
This skill grows naturally as our English reading experience increases.
eg: We use the words around an unfamiliar term to work out its meaning.
We predict the meaning of a word from the overall topic of the text.
We use our knowledge of word parts (prefixes and suffixes) alongside context.
We test our prediction by substituting the guessed meaning into the sentence.
We use images or diagrams near the text to support our prediction.
We compare our predicted meaning with a dictionary definition to check accuracy.

VC2EALC2R05.w: We are learning to identify different types of texts we encounter in our reading.
y: Text type identification helps us read with appropriate expectations and strategies.
Different types serve different purposes and are structured differently.
This knowledge is essential for both reading comprehension and writing.
eg: We distinguish a persuasive essay from an information report.
We identify a procedure by its step-by-step structure.
We recognise a narrative by its story elements: character, setting, plot.
We identify a discussion text by its presentation of multiple viewpoints.
We recognise a review by its evaluative language and personal opinion.
We identify a biography by its focus on a person's life and achievements.

VC2EALC2R06.w: We are learning how connecting words and features like pronouns organise ideas within a text.
y: Understanding cohesion helps us follow the logical flow of texts.
It shows us how ideas build on each other across sentences and paragraphs.
This awareness improves both our comprehension and our own writing.
eg: We track how 'however' signals a shift in argument.
We identify what 'this process' refers to in a science text.
We follow pronoun references across several sentences to track who is being discussed.
We see how 'therefore' introduces a conclusion based on earlier evidence.
We notice how repeated key terms create connections throughout a text.
We identify how 'in contrast' organises a comparison between two ideas.

VC2EALC2R07.w: We are learning to read and understand sentences with two or three parts joined together.
y: Compound and complex sentences are standard in academic texts.
Understanding multi-clause sentences is essential for year-level reading comprehension.
This builds our capacity to handle increasingly complex English texts.
eg: We read 'The scientist measured the temperature and recorded the results' and understand both actions.
We follow 'Although the evidence was limited, the researchers drew conclusions'.
We understand 'She succeeded because she practised every day after school'.
We interpret 'The river, which flows through three states, is one of the longest in Australia'.
We read 'When the experiment was complete, we analysed the data carefully'.
We follow compound-complex sentences in textbook passages.

VC2EALC2R08.w: We are learning to tell the difference between different verb tenses when we read.
y: Recognising tense changes helps us understand timelines and cause-effect relationships.
Authors shift tenses deliberately — understanding why improves our comprehension.
This grammar awareness is essential for interpreting both narratives and academic texts.
eg: We distinguish present tense from past tense in a text and understand the time shift.
We recognise future tense markers like 'will' and 'going to'.
We identify present perfect ('has studied') and understand its difference from simple past.
We notice when an author shifts from past to present tense and understand the effect.
We recognise continuous tenses and understand they show ongoing actions.
We identify conditional tenses and understand they express hypothetical situations.

VC2EALC2R09.w: We are learning to read and understand different kinds of descriptive language in texts.
y: Descriptive language creates meaning beyond basic facts.
Understanding it helps us appreciate how authors craft atmosphere and character.
This skill is important for both literary analysis and understanding informative texts.
eg: We understand how adjectives and adverbs add detail: 'the remarkably resilient ecosystem'.
We interpret similes: 'The city sparkled like a constellation of earthbound stars.'
We understand metaphors: 'The classroom was a beehive of activity.'
We recognise how sensory descriptions create mood in narratives.
We interpret technical descriptions in scientific texts.
We understand how emotive language influences our response to persuasive texts.

VC2EALC2R10.w: We are learning to recognise common everyday vocabulary and subject-specific words we have studied.
y: Strong vocabulary recognition is the gateway to fluent, confident reading.
Subject vocabulary connects our reading to our learning across the curriculum.
Building recognition of these words accelerates our academic progress.
eg: We recognise academic words like 'analyse', 'evaluate', 'justify' and 'investigate'.
We read subject vocabulary from science, maths, humanities and English.
We identify newly taught topic words in assessment tasks.
We recognise words in new contexts that we first learned in another subject.
We connect everyday meanings of words to their subject-specific definitions.
We use our vocabulary knowledge to read unfamiliar texts with greater understanding.

VC2EALC2R11.w: We are learning to use our knowledge of sounds and letter patterns to work out how to pronounce unfamiliar words.
y: Pronunciation skills help us read aloud fluently and connect reading to speaking.
Decoding pronunciation makes new vocabulary more memorable.
This skill supports our independent reading across all subjects.
eg: We apply known letter patterns to pronounce unfamiliar scientific terms.
We break multi-syllable words into parts to work out pronunciation.
We use analogy with known words to attempt new ones.
We apply knowledge of word stress rules to read new vocabulary aloud.
We recognise Greek and Latin word parts to help with academic vocabulary pronunciation.
We self-correct when we realise our pronunciation doesn't match a word we know orally.

VC2EALC2R12.w: We are learning what different punctuation marks do and how they affect meaning when we read.
y: Understanding punctuation helps us read with correct phrasing and comprehension.
Different marks serve different purposes — knowing them all improves our reading.
This knowledge helps us interpret complex sentences accurately.
eg: We understand commas separate items in a list and clauses in a sentence.
We interpret colons as introducing an explanation or list.
We recognise semicolons as linking closely related ideas.
We understand that dashes can add emphasis or extra information.
We interpret quotation marks as signalling speech, titles or irony.
We understand how parentheses (brackets) enclose additional information.

VC2EALC2R13.w: We are learning to compare and contrast English texts with texts in our home language.
y: Cross-linguistic comparison deepens our understanding of how texts work in both languages.
It builds appreciation for different cultural approaches to writing.
This comparative awareness makes us more thoughtful, critical readers.
eg: We compare how essays are structured in English versus our home language.
We notice differences in how formality is conveyed in written texts across languages.
We compare storytelling conventions in English with those from our culture.
We discuss how the same genre (e.g., news article) differs across languages.
We compare punctuation rules and conventions between the two languages.
We contrast how persuasive writing works in English and our language.

VC2EALC2R14.w: We are learning to select and read texts that are accessible but challenging enough to help us grow, with teacher support.
y: Reading at the right level of challenge accelerates our development.
Slightly challenging texts push us beyond our comfort zone productively.
Teacher support ensures we can access texts that stretch our skills.
eg: We choose a text that requires us to use some new vocabulary but is mostly understandable.
We select a book from a higher reading level with the teacher's encouragement.
We read a text on a new topic and use teacher support for the trickiest parts.
We try a longer or more complex text than we usually read.
We select a text from a different genre to broaden our reading experience.
We read a text independently and seek teacher help only for the most difficult sections.

VC2EALC2R15.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to support our English reading.
y: Our home language literacy is a powerful resource for English reading comprehension.
Drawing on both languages simultaneously produces deeper understanding.
This is a strategic advantage that we should actively cultivate.
eg: We use our knowledge of a concept in our language to understand a complex English text.
We compare text structures in our language and English to deepen comprehension.
We translate challenging passages to verify our understanding.
We read about the same topic in both languages for fuller understanding.
We use reading strategies developed in our first language with English texts.
We discuss difficult English texts with a peer who shares our language.

VC2EALC2R16.w: We are learning to participate in shared reading activities and express our personal views about what we read.
y: Expressing personal views develops our critical thinking about texts.
Shared reading gives us a community with whom to discuss ideas and interpretations.
Voicing our opinions in English builds both our language and our confidence.
eg: We share our opinion about a character's decision during class discussion.
We explain which part of a text we found most interesting or surprising.
We discuss themes in a shared text and relate them to our own experience.
We express agreement or disagreement with a text's argument.
We compare our interpretation with classmates' and consider different viewpoints.
We recommend a shared text to others and explain why.

VC2EALC2W01.w: We are learning to write texts for a narrow range of social purposes, with teacher support.
y: Social writing skills are immediately practical for real-world communication.
Developing these with teacher guidance ensures we learn appropriate conventions.
This builds our ability to handle diverse writing situations independently.
eg: We write a formal email to a teacher requesting information.
We write an apology or explanation for a school-related issue.
We write a short letter of application for a school leadership role.
We write a message to a classmate about a group project.
We draft a thank-you letter after a school excursion or event.
We write a note requesting permission for a specific activity.

VC2EALC2W02.w: We are learning to write information texts for school use, following models and patterns we have studied.
y: Information writing is the most common form of academic writing in secondary school.
Following modelled language provides scaffolding while our skills develop.
This builds towards independent academic writing across all subjects.
eg: We write a science report following a modelled structure.
We compose a geography description using language patterns from a class example.
We write a historical account based on a model provided by the teacher.
We create a subject summary using vocabulary and structures from class texts.
We write a comparative text following a template.
We produce an information text for a school display using modelled academic language.

VC2EALC2W03.w: We are learning to write imaginative and personal texts, with support.
y: Creative and personal writing helps us develop our unique voice in English.
It allows us to express our ideas, experiences and imagination.
This type of writing builds skills that transfer to all other writing.
eg: We write a short story inspired by a narrative we studied in class.
We compose a personal reflection on a meaningful experience.
We write a poem exploring a theme or emotion.
We create a diary entry from a character's perspective.
We write a personal narrative about our journey to Australia.
We compose a short creative piece for a school writing competition.

VC2EALC2W04.w: We are learning to include a variety of visual elements like diagrams, charts and images in our writing.
y: Visual elements enhance written texts by presenting information in different ways.
Combining text and visuals is expected in academic and professional writing.
This multimodal skill is increasingly important in our digital world.
eg: We include a data table in our science investigation report.
We add a labelled diagram to a technology design brief.
We create a graph from collected data and embed it in our report.
We include annotated photographs in a geography field study.
We add a timeline to a history essay.
We create an infographic that combines text and visual data.

VC2EALC2W05.w: We are learning to organise our writing using the correct structure for the text type, matching genre expectations.
y: Appropriate structure is essential for effective academic writing.
Meeting genre expectations shows we understand how different texts work.
Well-structured writing communicates ideas clearly and professionally.
eg: We organise a persuasive essay with thesis, arguments and conclusion.
We structure a laboratory report with aim, method, results and conclusion.
We write a narrative with a clear orientation, complication and resolution.
We organise a discussion text with introduction, arguments, counter-arguments and summary.
We structure a recount chronologically with a clear beginning, middle and end.
We format a formal letter with appropriate greeting, body and closing.

VC2EALC2W06.w: We are learning to link our ideas using a range of basic connecting words and devices in our writing.
y: Cohesive writing flows smoothly and is easier for readers to follow.
Using a variety of connectives shows growing sophistication.
This skill is essential for producing coherent academic texts.
eg: We use 'furthermore' and 'in addition' to add supporting points.
We employ 'however' and 'on the other hand' for contrasts.
We use 'consequently' and 'as a result' to show outcomes.
We use 'for instance' and 'specifically' to provide examples.
We employ pronouns and synonym substitution to maintain flow.
We use 'in summary' and 'to conclude' to signal endings.

VC2EALC2W07.w: We are learning to write compound and complex sentences to develop our ideas more fully.
y: Compound and complex sentences allow us to express more nuanced ideas.
They are expected in academic writing at secondary school level.
Writing longer, well-structured sentences shows growing English maturity.
eg: We write compound sentences: 'The data was collected, and the results were analysed.'
We write complex sentences: 'Although the hypothesis was incorrect, the experiment was valuable.'
We use relative clauses: 'The scientist, who discovered the element, won a Nobel Prize.'
We combine ideas: 'Because the population is growing, resources are becoming scarce.'
We write with embedded clauses: 'The essay, which took three days to complete, received high marks.'
We vary sentence length by mixing simple, compound and complex sentences.

VC2EALC2W08.w: We are learning to write using a range of tenses, including simple and continuous present and past tense, with increasing accuracy.
y: Tense control is one of the most important grammar skills in writing.
Using different tenses accurately lets us write about time, process and change.
Improving tense consistency makes our writing significantly clearer.
eg: We write present tense descriptions: 'The Earth orbits the Sun.'
We use past continuous: 'The students were working when the bell rang.'
We write present continuous: 'The population is growing rapidly.'
We maintain consistent past tense in recounts and narratives.
We use simple present for general facts and habitual actions.
We check and correct tense errors during our editing process.

VC2EALC2W09.w: We are learning to use adverbs and prepositions to add simple details about how, when, where and why things happen.
y: Adverbs and prepositions add important circumstantial details to our writing.
They help us provide the context readers need to understand our ideas fully.
Using them accurately shows growing control of English expression.
eg: We write 'The reaction occurred rapidly' using an adverb of manner.
We use prepositions of place: 'The experiment was conducted in the laboratory.'
We write 'She carefully measured the liquid' with an adverb before the verb.
We use time prepositions: 'The event took place during the nineteenth century.'
We add detail: 'The water flowed slowly through the narrow channel.'
We use 'because of', 'due to' and 'as a result of' to show cause.

VC2EALC2W10.w: We are learning to use varied and appropriate vocabulary across our writing.
y: Varied vocabulary prevents our writing from being repetitive.
Appropriate word choice shows understanding of context and audience.
A rich vocabulary is one of the strongest indicators of writing quality.
eg: We use synonyms to avoid repeating the same word: 'said/stated/explained/argued'.
We choose subject-appropriate vocabulary for different school tasks.
We use formal academic vocabulary in essays and reports.
We select vivid, precise words for creative writing.
We use evaluative vocabulary in reviews and analyses.
We draw on our expanding word knowledge to express ideas with precision.

VC2EALC2W11.w: We are learning to spell most words we encounter correctly, using strategies like sound patterns, word parts and context.
y: Accurate spelling makes our writing professional and credible.
Using multiple strategies gives us tools for any spelling challenge.
Consistent spelling accuracy allows readers to focus on our ideas.
eg: We use phonics knowledge to spell words with predictable letter patterns.
We break words into meaningful parts to spell: 'un-employ-ment'.
We use context to distinguish homophones: 'effect' vs 'affect'.
We apply rules for adding suffixes: dropping the 'e' before '-ing'.
We use our home language knowledge of word origins to help with English spelling.
We proofread specifically for spelling and use a dictionary for words we are unsure of.

VC2EALC2W12.w: We are learning to use a range of punctuation marks consistently and correctly throughout our writing.
y: Consistent, correct punctuation is a hallmark of skilled writing.
Each punctuation mark serves a specific purpose that aids reader comprehension.
Mastering punctuation elevates the quality of all our written work.
eg: We use commas, full stops and question marks correctly throughout a text.
We employ apostrophes for possession and contractions accurately.
We use quotation marks for direct speech and titles.
We begin to use colons and semicolons in appropriate places.
We use commas to separate clauses in complex sentences.
We proofread our work for punctuation consistency and accuracy.

VC2EALC2W13.w: We are learning to choose the right type of text for our purpose and audience, and to discuss why it is suitable.
y: Choosing the right text type is a strategic writing decision.
Discussing appropriateness shows we understand how form serves function.
This awareness helps us write effectively for any situation.
eg: We choose a letter format for a formal request and explain why.
We select a report structure for presenting research findings.
We decide to write a narrative to share a personal experience creatively.
We discuss why an essay is more appropriate than a list for presenting an argument.
We choose between a poster and an essay based on the intended audience.
We explain why we chose a particular text type for a school assessment.

VC2EALC2W14.w: We are learning to revise and edit our writing at the word, sentence and whole-text level, using teacher or peer feedback.
y: Multi-level editing produces significantly better writing.
Feedback from others helps us see our work from a reader's perspective.
Developing strong editing skills is essential for academic writing success.
eg: We replace weak words with stronger alternatives throughout a text.
We restructure sentences for clarity based on peer feedback.
We reorganise paragraphs to improve the overall flow of a text.
We check and correct grammar, spelling and punctuation at the sentence level.
We ensure our introduction and conclusion align with the body of our text.
We use teacher annotations to make specific improvements at each level.

VC2EALC2W15.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to support our English writing.
y: Our home language provides sophisticated ideas and strategies for English writing.
Bilingual writers can draw on a wider pool of knowledge and expression.
Using our first language strategically leads to stronger English writing outcomes.
eg: We plan complex arguments in our language before writing them in English.
We use our home language understanding of genre to structure English texts.
We translate key ideas from our language to express them precisely in English.
We draw on rhetorical traditions from our culture in English academic writing.
We use bilingual research to gather information for English writing tasks.
We discuss writing strategies with bilingual peers to improve our approach.

VC2EALC2W16.w: We are learning to participate in shared writing activities with our classmates.
y: Collaborative writing exposes us to different approaches and ideas.
Working with peers gives us practice in negotiating language choices.
Shared writing builds our skills in a supported, social context.
eg: We co-write a text with a partner, taking turns to compose sentences.
We contribute to a group research report, writing specific sections.
We participate in peer editing, improving each other's work.
We collaborate on a creative writing project with classmates.
We work together to plan and draft a persuasive text.
We jointly compose a text for a class display or publication.

VC2EALC3L01.w: We are learning to identify the main idea in spoken texts, with some support from the context around us.
y: Identifying main ideas in spoken texts is critical for following lessons and discussions.
Using contextual support strategically helps us manage increasingly complex listening.
This skill builds towards fully independent listening comprehension.
eg: We listen to a teacher's explanation and state the central point in our own words.
We use visual aids and the topic context to help identify the main idea of a talk.
We listen to a class debate and identify each speaker's core argument.
We watch a documentary segment and summarise its main message.
We identify the thesis of a guest speaker's presentation.
We listen to a podcast on a current issue and explain the main viewpoint.

VC2EALC3L02.w: We are learning to find specific information in spoken texts by using questioning, previewing and prediction strategies, with some support.
y: Active listening strategies help us target the information we need.
Previewing and predicting prepare our mind for what we are about to hear.
These skills are essential for academic note-taking and research.
eg: We preview the topic before listening and predict what key information to expect.
We formulate questions before a video and listen specifically for the answers.
We use a KWL chart to focus our listening on what we want to learn.
We predict what a speaker will say next based on the structure of their talk.
We listen for specific data points in a spoken report using pre-set questions.
We review our notes after listening to check if our predictions were accurate.

VC2EALC3L03.w: We are learning to participate in both informal and formal spoken interactions in familiar classroom and school settings.
y: Switching between formal and informal English is a key social and academic skill.
Participating across different contexts shows growing communicative competence.
This flexibility prepares us for diverse real-world communication situations.
eg: We participate in formal classroom discussions using academic language.
We chat informally with friends at lunchtime using everyday English.
We contribute to a structured debate using formal argumentation language.
We navigate conversations with different adults at school — teachers, office staff, visiting speakers.
We adjust our register when moving from a casual group chat to a class presentation.
We participate in school meetings or assemblies using appropriate formal language.

VC2EALC3L04.w: We are learning to prepare and present a formal talk with some visual or contextual support.
y: Formal presentations are a standard assessment task in secondary school.
Preparation and practice help us deliver clear, well-structured talks.
This skill builds confidence for public speaking throughout our lives.
eg: We prepare a researched presentation using a slideshow for visual support.
We deliver a formal speech on a topic using cue cards and practised timing.
We present an analytical talk on a text we studied, referencing key evidence.
We give a formal report on a group project, using visual displays.
We present our perspective on a current issue with a prepared argument structure.
We use props, diagrams or multimedia to support a formal talk.

VC2EALC3L05.w: We are learning to produce a range of well-organised spoken texts that suit our audience and purpose.
y: Different audiences and purposes require different approaches to speaking.
Coherent spoken texts show we can plan and organise our ideas in English.
This versatility is essential for success in secondary school and beyond.
eg: We give an informative explanation tailored to our audience's knowledge level.
We produce a persuasive speech aimed at convincing classmates of our position.
We deliver a recount to the class using appropriate structure and detail.
We give instructions to a group clearly enough for them to complete a task.
We present an analysis of a text using academic language appropriate for our teacher.
We adjust our spoken text based on whether we are speaking to peers, teachers or younger students.

VC2EALC3L06.w: We are learning to use a range of connecting words and devices to link our ideas in longer spoken texts.
y: Cohesive devices make extended speech easier for listeners to follow.
They signal relationships between ideas — addition, contrast, cause, sequence.
Using them well is a mark of fluent, sophisticated English speaking.
eg: We use 'moreover', 'furthermore' and 'in addition' to build on points.
We employ 'nevertheless', 'despite this' and 'conversely' for contrasts.
We use 'consequently', 'hence' and 'as a result' to show outcomes.
We signal examples with 'for instance', 'to illustrate' and 'such as'.
We summarise using 'in essence', 'overall' and 'to sum up'.
We transition between sections of a talk using phrases like 'turning now to' and 'moving on'.

VC2EALC3L07.w: We are learning to use more complex verb forms accurately in our spoken English.
y: Complex verb forms allow us to express precise meanings about time, possibility and condition.
Accuracy with these forms makes our spoken English sound natural and sophisticated.
This is a key indicator of advancing English proficiency.
eg: We use present perfect accurately: 'We have completed the first phase of the project.'
We employ conditional forms: 'If the results had been different, we would have changed our approach.'
We use passive voice naturally: 'The data was collected over a three-week period.'
We use modal verbs precisely: 'This could indicate a shift in public opinion.'
We employ past perfect: 'By the time we arrived, the presentation had already started.'
We use reported speech accurately: 'The researcher stated that the findings were significant.'

VC2EALC3L08.w: We are learning to use longer, more detailed describing phrases when we speak.
y: Extended descriptive phrases allow us to communicate complex ideas with precision.
They make our spoken English more vivid, engaging and academically appropriate.
This skill distinguishes fluent speakers from intermediate learners.
eg: We describe a process as 'a highly regulated chemical reaction that occurs under specific temperature conditions'.
We characterise a historical period as 'an era of unprecedented social upheaval driven by rapid industrialisation'.
We use embedded relative clauses: 'the community, which had been displaced by the conflict, rebuilt their lives'.
We describe a text's impact: 'a profoundly moving narrative that challenges conventional perspectives on identity'.
We use adverbial phrases: 'speaking with remarkable composure despite the difficult circumstances'.
We characterise an argument as 'a persuasive but ultimately flawed interpretation of the evidence'.

VC2EALC3L09.w: We are learning to use vocabulary from our school subjects accurately in spoken and multimodal texts.
y: Subject-specific vocabulary is essential for demonstrating knowledge in each learning area.
Using these words accurately in speech shows we truly understand the concepts.
This prepares us for academic discussions and oral assessments across the curriculum.
eg: We use science vocabulary like 'hypothesis', 'variable' and 'photosynthesis' in oral reports.
We employ maths terms like 'coefficient', 'quadratic' and 'proportion' in explanations.
We use humanities vocabulary like 'sovereignty', 'globalisation' and 'urbanisation' in discussions.
We use literary terms like 'metaphor', 'symbolism' and 'narrative perspective' in English.
We employ technology vocabulary like 'algorithm', 'iteration' and 'sustainability' in presentations.
We use terms from health and PE like 'cardiovascular', 'biomechanics' and 'tactics' accurately.

VC2EALC3L10.w: We are learning to use pronunciation strategically to make our communication clearer and more effective.
y: Strategic pronunciation goes beyond being understood — it enhances our message.
Clear pronunciation builds listener confidence and engagement.
This level of control shows we are becoming proficient English speakers.
eg: We emphasise key terms clearly when presenting academic content.
We adjust our pronunciation for clarity when speaking to unfamiliar audiences.
We pronounce technical and academic vocabulary with confidence.
We use clear articulation to distinguish between similar-sounding words.
We speak at an appropriate pace, ensuring every important word is clearly pronounced.
We self-monitor and adjust pronunciation mid-speech when needed.

VC2EALC3L11.w: We are learning to use a wider range of stress, intonation and body language skills to enhance our speaking.
y: Advanced prosodic skills make us compelling and persuasive speakers.
They convey meaning, emotion and emphasis that words alone cannot achieve.
Mastering these features is essential for effective academic and professional communication.
eg: We use emphatic stress to highlight the most important word in a key sentence.
We employ varied intonation patterns to maintain listener interest during a long talk.
We use purposeful pausing for dramatic effect or to allow ideas to be absorbed.
We gesture naturally to reinforce and clarify our spoken ideas.
We modulate our volume and pace to create engagement during presentations.
We use eye contact and open body posture to connect with our audience.

VC2EALC3L12.w: We are learning to discuss cultural differences in how people communicate through speech.
y: Understanding cultural communication differences builds intercultural competence.
It helps us avoid misunderstandings and communicate respectfully across cultures.
This awareness is increasingly important in our diverse, globalised world.
eg: We discuss how directness and indirectness in speech vary across cultures.
We explore how humour and irony are used differently in different languages.
We compare how turn-taking conventions differ across cultures.
We discuss how personal space and physical contact during conversation vary culturally.
We talk about how formality and respect are expressed differently in spoken English and our language.
We share examples from our own cultural backgrounds to illustrate communication differences.

VC2EALC3L13.w: We are learning to assess our own spoken English and actively work on improving areas that affect our communication.
y: Self-assessment drives independent improvement in our English.
Identifying specific areas to work on makes our practice more efficient.
This metacognitive skill supports lifelong language development.
eg: We record ourselves speaking and analyse our strengths and weaknesses.
We set specific spoken English goals and track our progress.
We identify recurring errors and practise the correct forms.
We seek targeted feedback from teachers and peers on specific aspects of our speech.
We compare our speaking with proficient English speakers and note differences.
We reflect after each presentation on what went well and what to improve.

VC2EALC3L14.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to strengthen all aspects of our English listening and speaking.
y: Our home language is an invaluable resource for sophisticated English communication.
Drawing deeply on both languages produces richer, more nuanced expression.
Advanced bilingual strategies are a hallmark of effective multilingual communicators.
eg: We use complex concepts from our language to access advanced English academic content.
We draw on our cultural knowledge to contribute unique perspectives to English discussions.
We use our home language's rhetorical strategies to enhance our English argumentation.
We translate complex abstract ideas to find the best English expression.
We compare formal speaking conventions across our languages to refine our English register.
We use our bilingual competence to mediate communication in multicultural settings.

VC2EALC3L15.w: We are learning to contribute effectively and meaningfully to collaborative group work.
y: Effective collaboration is essential for learning and for future workplaces.
Contributing meaningfully shows we can use English for productive teamwork.
Strong collaborative skills help us learn from others while sharing our own knowledge.
eg: We take initiative in suggesting ideas and directions for group tasks.
We build on others' contributions by adding supporting evidence or alternative perspectives.
We manage group dynamics by mediating disagreements and keeping discussions productive.
We take responsibility for specific parts of a group task and deliver them well.
We provide constructive feedback on group members' ideas and work.
We synthesise the group's ideas into a coherent summary or plan.

VC2EALC3R01.w: We are learning to skim a range of texts quickly to get the general meaning, with some contextual support.
y: Skimming is a critical reading strategy for managing the volume of text in secondary school.
It helps us decide which texts are relevant and worth reading in detail.
This efficiency skill is essential for research and study across all subjects.
eg: We skim a chapter to get an overview before reading it in detail.
We quickly read the headings, introduction and conclusion to grasp the general meaning.
We skim several articles to find which one is most relevant to our research question.
We run our eyes over a text and identify the general topic and tone.
We skim a long text and write a one-sentence summary of the overall content.
We skim an exam question text to understand the general situation before reading closely.

VC2EALC3R02.w: We are learning to scan printed texts to find specific detailed information, with some support.
y: Scanning lets us find specific facts quickly without reading every word.
This is an essential research and study skill for secondary school.
Efficient scanning saves time and helps us manage complex reading tasks.
eg: We scan a text for a specific date, name or statistic.
We use headings and key words to locate the relevant section of a long text.
We scan an index to find the page with the information we need.
We look through a text to find a quote that supports our argument.
We scan a news article for specific details about an event.
We search through a chapter to find the definition of a key term.

VC2EALC3R03.w: We are learning to extract information from a range of visual representations, including tables, graphs and diagrams.
y: Visual data literacy is essential across science, maths, humanities and other subjects.
Many assessment tasks require us to interpret and analyse visual information.
This skill prepares us for data-rich environments in higher education and careers.
eg: We extract trends from a line graph showing population change over time.
We compare data across categories using a grouped bar chart.
We interpret a Venn diagram showing shared and unique characteristics.
We read a flow diagram illustrating a scientific or industrial process.
We extract precise values from a data table in a science report.
We interpret a cross-section diagram showing geological or biological layers.

VC2EALC3R04.w: We are learning to use clues from the context to work out implied or hidden meanings in texts.
y: Inferring meaning goes beyond literal comprehension to deeper understanding.
Authors often imply ideas rather than stating them directly — we need to read between the lines.
This is a crucial skill for literary analysis and critical reading.
eg: We infer a character's motivation from their actions and dialogue.
We use context to determine the figurative meaning of an idiomatic expression.
We infer the author's purpose from the language choices and text structure.
We work out the tone of a text from the vocabulary and sentence structure used.
We infer a speaker's bias from the way they present information.
We use context to understand sarcasm, irony or understatement in a text.

VC2EALC3R05.w: We are learning to compare how different types of texts are structured and organised.
y: Comparing text structures deepens our understanding of how texts achieve their purpose.
It helps us make better choices when we write our own texts.
This analytical skill is valued in English and across all subjects.
eg: We compare the structure of a newspaper article with a scientific report.
We analyse how a persuasive essay is organised differently from a discussion text.
We compare the narrative structure of a short story with a film adaptation.
We examine how a biography and an autobiography organise the same life events differently.
We compare how information is structured in a textbook chapter versus a website.
We analyse the structural differences between a formal essay and a blog post.

VC2EALC3R06.w: We are learning to identify the role that connecting words and cohesive markers play in holding a text together.
y: Understanding cohesion helps us see how authors build logical, flowing texts.
It improves our comprehension of complex arguments and explanations.
This analytical awareness directly improves our own writing.
eg: We identify how 'consequently' signals a cause-effect relationship in an argument.
We recognise how pronoun references track participants across a text.
We analyse how 'furthermore' builds an accumulating argument.
We identify how lexical chains (repeated related words) create cohesion around a theme.
We recognise how 'in contrast' and 'similarly' organise comparative texts.
We analyse how 'thus' and 'hence' draw logical conclusions in academic writing.

VC2EALC3R07.w: We are learning to read texts containing a range of sentence types, including complex sentences with multiple clauses.
y: Academic texts rely heavily on complex, multi-clause sentences.
Reading them fluently is essential for comprehension at year level.
This skill prepares us for the demanding reading required in senior secondary school.
eg: We read and understand sentences with multiple subordinate clauses.
We follow sentences with relative clauses embedded in the middle.
We comprehend sentences that begin with adverbial clauses of time, reason or condition.
We read sentences with participial phrases: 'Having completed the experiment, we analysed the results.'
We understand sentences that use apposition: 'Darwin, the renowned naturalist, proposed the theory.'
We read passages where simple, compound and complex sentences are mixed.

VC2EALC3R08.w: We are learning to identify who did what and what resulted in sentences that use the passive voice.
y: Passive voice is common in academic and scientific writing.
Understanding who is acting and what is affected is crucial for accurate interpretation.
This grammar awareness prevents misreading of complex academic texts.
eg: We identify the agent in 'The samples were analysed by the research team.'
We recognise that in 'The decision was made', the agent is not stated.
We understand the consequence in 'The habitat was destroyed, causing species decline.'
We interpret 'The law was passed in 1967' and identify what happened and when.
We recognise agentless passive in science: 'The solution was heated to 100°C.'
We convert passive sentences to active to clarify meaning: 'X was discovered by Y' → 'Y discovered X.'

VC2EALC3R09.w: We are learning to read and understand sentences containing a wide range of descriptive language.
y: Rich descriptive language creates meaning, atmosphere and nuance in texts.
Understanding it is essential for literary analysis and for appreciating skilled writing.
This capability opens up more sophisticated texts across all genres.
eg: We interpret extended metaphors and understand their contribution to a text's meaning.
We understand how sensory imagery creates atmosphere in a narrative.
We interpret technical descriptions in scientific and geographical texts.
We understand how an author uses personification to bring abstract ideas to life.
We appreciate how carefully chosen adverbs and adjectives shape our perception of events.
We interpret the emotional impact of descriptive language in poetry and prose.

VC2EALC3R10.w: We are learning to identify groups of related words in a text that are connected by theme.
y: Thematic word groups reveal the key topics and concepts in a text.
Recognising them helps us understand the text's focus and build topic vocabulary.
This skill deepens our comprehension and expands our English vocabulary.
eg: We identify a cluster of words related to 'conflict' in a history text: 'battle', 'dispute', 'resistance', 'alliance'.
We recognise environment-related vocabulary in a geography text: 'ecosystem', 'biodiversity', 'conservation', 'sustainability'.
We notice emotion words grouping in a narrative: 'anxious', 'trembling', 'dread', 'relief'.
We identify scientific process words in a report: 'hypothesis', 'method', 'analysis', 'conclusion'.
We spot economic vocabulary in a news article: 'inflation', 'deficit', 'growth', 'investment'.
We use thematic groupings to build our own topic-specific vocabulary lists.

VC2EALC3R11.w: We are learning to use a range of strategies to decode words and phrases with increasing accuracy.
y: Multiple decoding strategies give us tools for any unfamiliar word we encounter.
Increasing accuracy shows our reading skills are maturing.
This independence is essential for confident reading across all subjects.
eg: We use morphemic analysis (prefixes, roots, suffixes) to decode unfamiliar words.
We apply knowledge of word origins (Greek, Latin) to work out meaning and pronunciation.
We use context and sentence structure together to decode phrases.
We break compound words into their component parts for meaning.
We use our knowledge of common spelling patterns to read new words accurately.
We employ multiple strategies simultaneously when a single approach isn't enough.

VC2EALC3R12.w: We are learning to interpret a wider range of punctuation marks and understand how they affect meaning.
y: Advanced punctuation carries subtle meaning that affects interpretation.
Understanding these marks helps us read complex academic and literary texts accurately.
This skill supports both our reading comprehension and our own writing.
eg: We interpret ellipsis (...) as indicating omitted text or trailing thought.
We understand how dashes create emphasis or insert additional information.
We interpret colons as introducing explanations, lists or elaborations.
We understand semicolons as linking closely related independent ideas.
We interpret the use of italics for emphasis, titles or foreign words.
We understand how parentheses provide supplementary information without disrupting flow.

VC2EALC3R13.w: We are learning to respond thoughtfully to different cultural values and attitudes we encounter in texts.
y: Texts reflect the cultural perspectives of their authors and contexts.
Responding to diverse values builds our intercultural understanding and empathy.
Critical engagement with cultural perspectives is essential for thoughtful citizenship.
eg: We identify and discuss cultural assumptions in a text set in a different time or place.
We respond to a text that presents values different from our own with curiosity and respect.
We compare how a text's cultural context shapes its messages and themes.
We discuss how our own cultural perspective influences our reading of a text.
We identify stereotypes or biases in texts and discuss their impact.
We respond to texts from diverse cultures by finding both commonalities and differences with our own experience.

VC2EALC3R14.w: We are learning to select and read long, complex texts with teacher support.
y: Reading long, complex texts is essential for senior secondary school and further education.
Developing stamina for extended reading builds our capacity for independent study.
Teacher support helps us develop the strategies needed for challenging texts.
eg: We read a full novel for a class study with teacher-guided comprehension support.
We work through a long non-fiction text, using teacher check-ins to maintain understanding.
We read extended academic articles with support for difficult vocabulary and concepts.
We tackle a complex primary source document with teacher-guided analysis.
We read a full play script and discuss themes and language with teacher guidance.
We manage reading a long text by breaking it into sections with teacher-set milestones.

VC2EALC3R15.w: We are learning to draw extensively on our home language to support all aspects of our English reading.
y: Our home language provides deep conceptual and analytical resources for English reading.
Advanced cross-linguistic strategies produce richer comprehension.
Drawing on our full linguistic repertoire is the hallmark of an effective multilingual reader.
eg: We use our understanding of literary techniques from our language to analyse English texts.
We draw on our cultural knowledge to interpret texts about unfamiliar contexts.
We compare how arguments are structured in our language and English to deepen analysis.
We use our first language to process complex ideas before discussing them in English.
We read parallel texts in both languages to build complete understanding.
We draw on our bilingual competence to engage with multilingual or translated texts.

VC2EALC3R16.w: We are learning to contribute our personal responses and interpretations during shared reading activities.
y: Personal responses demonstrate engagement and independent thinking about texts.
Sharing interpretations in English develops our academic speaking and analytical skills.
Hearing others' responses enriches our own understanding.
eg: We share our interpretation of a poem's meaning during class discussion.
We offer a personal response to a novel's themes based on our own experience.
We compare our reaction to a text with classmates' responses and discuss differences.
We explain what we found most powerful or challenging about a shared text.
We contribute an alternative interpretation that the class hadn't considered.
We connect a shared text to a current issue and share our perspective.

VC2EALC3W01.w: We are learning to write a simple argument or discussion text that presents and supports a position.
y: Argumentative writing is a cornerstone of academic communication.
Being able to write a clear argument helps us succeed in assessments across subjects.
This skill prepares us for persuasive writing, debate and critical thinking.
eg: We write a persuasive essay arguing for or against a school policy change.
We compose a discussion text presenting multiple perspectives on a social issue.
We write an argument supporting our interpretation of a text we studied.
We draft a letter to the editor expressing our view on a current issue.
We write a short essay defending our position on an ethical question.
We compose an argument using evidence from class materials to support our claims.

VC2EALC3W02.w: We are learning to write information texts with awareness of appropriate structure, purpose and organisation.
y: Well-structured information writing is the foundation of academic success.
Understanding structure and purpose helps us communicate facts and ideas clearly.
This skill is used in every subject across the curriculum.
eg: We write a science report with the correct sections: aim, method, results, conclusion.
We compose a geography explanation using cause-and-effect structure.
We write a historical account organised chronologically with relevant evidence.
We create an information text with clear headings, topic sentences and supporting details.
We write a comparison text using an appropriate organisational framework.
We compose a summary that captures the key points of a longer source text.

VC2EALC3W03.w: We are learning to write imaginative texts that show awareness of story elements like structure, character and setting.
y: Understanding story elements helps us craft more engaging and effective narratives.
Imaginative writing develops our creativity and our control of English expression.
This awareness of craft applies to both creating and analysing texts.
eg: We write a narrative with a well-developed orientation, complication and resolution.
We create believable characters with distinct voices and motivations.
We write a story with a vivid, well-described setting that contributes to the mood.
We experiment with narrative perspective — first person, third person, unreliable narrator.
We use techniques like foreshadowing, flashback or suspense in our stories.
We write a story that explores a theme through the characters' experiences.

VC2EALC3W04.w: We are learning to use visual elements like images, charts and diagrams to convey information in our writing.
y: Visual information can communicate complex data more efficiently than words.
Integrating visuals shows sophisticated, multimodal communication skills.
This is standard practice in academic and professional writing.
eg: We include a graph showing our experimental results in a science report.
We add a diagram illustrating a process described in our explanation text.
We create a comparison table to present data clearly in an essay.
We use photographs with captions to support a geographical description.
We include a timeline in a historical writing piece.
We design an infographic to communicate key findings from our research.

VC2EALC3W05.w: We are learning to plan and sequence information for a specific text type, using a model text as a guide.
y: Planning before writing produces better-organised, more coherent texts.
Model texts show us how experts structure their writing.
Sequencing information logically is essential for clear communication.
eg: We analyse a model essay and use its structure to plan our own.
We create a detailed outline before writing, following the model's organisation.
We use a model report to plan the sections and sequence of our own report.
We identify how a model text sequences arguments and replicate that pattern.
We plan a narrative using a story map modelled on a published short story.
We use a mentor text to decide the best order for presenting our information.

VC2EALC3W06.w: We are learning to write cohesive texts for a range of different purposes.
y: Cohesive writing holds together as a unified, connected piece.
Writing cohesively for different purposes shows adaptable writing skills.
This is a key quality that distinguishes competent writing from disconnected sentences.
eg: We write a persuasive text where each paragraph builds on the previous argument.
We compose a narrative where events are logically connected through cause and effect.
We write an information text where cohesive devices link paragraphs smoothly.
